topics.title: Definition of Elementary Terminologies like Survey Authority
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition of Elementary Terminologies like Survey Authority ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Survey Authority In the context of **Hull Inspection** and **Maritime Regulatory Compliance**, a **Survey Authority** is defined as an officially recognized body — either governmental, quasi-governmental, or a delegated non-governmental organization — that is legally empowered to carry out **technical inspections, surveys, audits, and certifications** of a vessel's hull, machinery, and safety systems, in order to verify compliance with international conventions, classification rules, and statutory regulations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Standard):** > A *Survey Authority* is the legally competent organization responsible for conducting statutory and/or classification surveys on a ship. It may be the **Flag State Administration** itself, or a **Recognized Organization (RO)** acting on delegated authority of the Flag State. In the KTU OENST832 framework, the term is generally understood to refer to one of the following three identities: 1. **Classification Society** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, American Bureau of Shipping, Bureau Veritas, DNV) 2. **Flag State Administration** (e.g., Maritime Administration of India, UK MCA, USCG) 3. **Port State Control Authority** (e.g., Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU inspectors) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Maritime Medical Examiner" Think of a ship's hull as the **human body** of a sea-going citizen. A **Survey Authority** is analogous to a combination of: - A **Licensed Doctor** (Classification Surveyor) who performs periodic health check-ups, - A **Government Health Department** (Flag State) that issues the legal "fitness certificates," and - A **Border Health Officer** (Port State Control) who can re-examine the sailor on arrival at a foreign port. Just as no person can legally drive a public transport vehicle without a medical fitness certificate, **no commercial ship can legally operate at sea without valid survey certificates** issued by an authorized Survey Authority. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** A common student misconception is treating "Survey Authority" and "Classification Society" as synonyms. A *Classification Society* is always a Survey Authority, but a *Survey Authority* can also be a flag administration or port state — the latter two are **not** classification societies. ### 1.3 Physical & Regulatory Constants | Reference Constant | Value / Reference | |---|---| | Major Classification Societies (IACS Members) | **11 members** (as of 2024) | | IMO Mandatory Conventions (Hull-Related) | **SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, Tonnage** | | Standard Survey Cycle | **5 Years** (Special Survey) | | Minimum Annual Survey Interval | **12 Months** | | Continuous Survey Option | **5-year rolling window** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Survey Authority Functions A Survey Authority executes its mandate through a structured hierarchy of inspections. The logical flow of authority is as follows: - **Step 1 — Rule Formulation:** The authority publishes technical rules (e.g., Lloyd's Register Rules and Regulations, ABS Steel Vessel Rules). - **Step 2 — Plan Approval:** Design drawings of the hull, scantlings, and structural details are submitted and approved *before* construction. - **Step 3 — Survey During Construction (New Building):** Surveyors attend the shipyard in stages — *keel laying, hull block erection, launch, outfitting, sea trials*. - **Step 4 — Class Survey (In-Service):** Periodic surveys to maintain class — *Annual, Intermediate, Special (Renewal), Continuous*. - **Step 5 — Statutory Survey:** Surveys required by international conventions — *Load Line, Safety Construction, Safety Equipment, Safety Radio, MARPOL, ISM*. - **Step 6 — Certification:** Issue/endorse statutory certificates (e.g., Safety Construction Certificate, Load Line Certificate, International Ship Security Certificate — ISSC). - **Step 7 — Audit & Compliance Verification:** ISM/ISPS Code audits, including DOC and SMC issuance. ### 2.2 The Three-Tier Survey Authority Model $$ \text{Total Regulatory Oversight} = \text{Flag State} \; + \; \text{Recognized Organization (RO)} \; + \; \text{Port State Control} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Concept:** Examiners frequently test the distinction between **Class Survey** (rule-based, voluntary, commercial) and **Statutory Survey** (convention-based, mandatory, legal). Memorize the table below. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Terminology | Definition | Governing Body | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Survey Authority** | Body authorized to conduct surveys | Flag State / RO / PSC | | 2 | **Flag State** | Country where the ship is registered | National Maritime Administration | | 3 | **Classification Society** | Non-governmental body setting technical rules | IACS Members (e.g., LR, ABS, BV, DNV) | | 4 | **Recognized Organization (RO)** | RO delegated by Flag State to act on its behalf | Flag State appointed (commonly a class society) | | 5 | **Port State Control (PSC)** | Foreign port authority enforcing compliance | Regional MoUs (Paris, Tokyo, etc.) | | 6 | **Statutory Survey** | Mandatory survey under IMO conventions | Flag State / delegated RO | | 7 | **Class Survey** | Voluntary survey for maintaining class notation | Classification Society | | 8 | **IACS** | International Association of Classification Societies | Quality Systems Certification Scheme (QSCS) | | 9 | **Class Notation** | Alphanumeric code indicating design & survey status | Assigned by Classification Society | | 10 | **Surveyor** | Technically qualified person conducting the survey | Employed by the Authority | | 11 | **Annual Survey** | Inspection within 12 months of last survey | Class + Statutory | | 12 | **Special Survey** | Major renewal survey every 5 years | Class | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Survey Authority framework ensures: - **Structural integrity** of the hull under cyclic wave loading, - **Maritime safety** for crew, passengers, and cargo, - **Environmental protection** under MARPOL Annexes I–VI, - **Commercial insurability** — most insurance underwriters (e.g., London P&I Club) demand valid class, - **Legal admissibility** — certificates are legal documents recognized under international law (UNCLOS Article 94). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix Because the topic *"Elementary Terminologies like Survey Authority"* is a **definitional and conceptual** subject (not a numerical derivation), the analytical "derivation" here is the **logical decomposition of authority relationships** and the **deduction of the operational chain** of a hull inspection. ### 3.1 Logical Derivation — The Authority Chain We derive, step by step, the legal chain of command that governs a hull inspection. **Step 1: Identify the international legal source of authority.** $$ \text{International Convention} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{UNCLOS (1982), SOLAS (1974), MARPOL (1973/78), Load Lines (1966), Tonnage (1969)} $$ This is the *root* of the authority. Conventions are adopted at the IMO level. **Step 2: Trace ratification to national law.** $$ \text{Convention} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Ratification}}\; \text{National Maritime Act (e.g., Merchant Shipping Act, India)} $$ Once ratified, the convention becomes binding law of the Flag State. **Step 3: Delegate enforcement.** $$ \text{Flag State} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Delegation under RO Agreement}}\; \text{Recognized Organization} $$ This step is **critical** for KTU — it is *here* that a Classification Society transforms into a Survey Authority. **Step 4: Execute the survey.** $$ \text{RO Surveyor} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Inspection of Hull}}\; \text{Issuance of Class + Statutory Certificates} $$ **Step 5: Independent verification by foreign ports.** $$ \text{Arrival at Foreign Port} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Port State Control Inspection} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Detention (if non-compliant)} $$ ### 3.2 Combined Authority Equation $$ \text{Survey Authority} = \underbrace{\text{Flag State}}_{\text{Legal Owner}} \cup \underbrace{\text{RO}}_{\text{Delegated Technical Arm}} \cup \underbrace{\text{PSC}}_{\text{Independent Verifier}} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Class vs. Statutory Survey | Parameter | Class Survey | Statutory Survey | |---|---|---| | Legal Basis | Classification Society Rules | IMO Convention (SOLAS, MARPOL, etc.) | | Mandatory? | **Voluntary** (but commercially essential) | **Mandatory** under international law | | Issuer | Classification Society | Flag State (or delegated RO) | | Frequency | Annual / Intermediate / Special | As required by convention | | Certificate Type | Classification Certificate | SOLAS Certificate, Load Line, etc. | | Loss Consequence | Loss of class → ship may be uninsurable | Detention by PSC, criminal liability | | Document | "Class" entry in Register | Statutory Certificate (e.g., SCC) | ### 3.4 Deduction — Why a Class Society is *not* automatically a Survey Authority A Classification Society is a Survey Authority **only** if the Flag State has formally delegated authority to it via a written RO Agreement. $$ \text{Survey Authority Status} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes}, & \text{if Flag State has signed RO Agreement with the Society} \\ \text{No}, & \text{otherwise (the Society can only offer class, not statutory certification)} \end{cases} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** This conditional logic is a high-yield answer point. Always state both conditions explicitly in your exam. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Maritime Survey Authority Hierarchy The following Mermaid diagram maps the **flow of regulatory authority** from the international level down to the onboard surveyor. It uses safe alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[UNCLOS 1982
United Nations Convention
on the Law of the Sea] nodeB[IMO
International Maritime
Organization] nodeC[SOLAS Convention
Safety of Life at Sea] nodeD[MARPOL Convention
Marine Pollution] nodeE[Load Lines Convention
1966] nodeF[Flag State
National Maritime Admin] nodeG[RO Agreement
Delegation Document] nodeH[Recognized Organization
RO e.g. Lloyd's Register] nodeI[Port State Control
PSC Inspector] nodeJ[Classification Society
IACS Member] nodeK[Annual Survey] nodeL[Intermediate Survey] nodeM[Special Survey
Every 5 Years] nodeN[Statutory Certificate
SCC LLE IOPP] nodeO[Class Certificate] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeJ nodeF --> nodeI nodeJ --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeL nodeJ --> nodeM nodeJ --> nodeN nodeJ --> nodeO nodeI --> nodeN ``` ### 4.2 Reading the Diagram - **Top Tier (nodeA → nodeB):** International conventions are administered by the IMO. - **Middle Tier (nodeB → nodeC/D/E):** Specific conventions govern hull, pollution, and load line matters. - **Flag State Tier (nodeC/D/E → nodeF):** Conventions become national law upon ratification. - **Delegation Tier (nodeF → nodeG → nodeH):** Flag State delegates technical survey work to an RO. - **Operational Tier (nodeJ → K/L/M/N/O):** The actual surveys and certificates are issued. - **External Verification (nodeI):** PSC independently re-checks the certificates on foreign port arrival. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** *Define the term "Survey Authority" as applied in hull inspection. Mention any two bodies that can act as a Survey Authority.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Survey Authority is a legally authorized organization competent to conduct hull inspections and issue certificates confirming compliance with international maritime conventions and classification rules. The authority may be the **Flag State Administration** itself or a **Recognized Organization (RO)** acting on delegated authority of the Flag State. Port State Control authorities also function as survey authorities during foreign port inspections. (2 marks for definition + 1 mark for two examples) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** *Differentiate between a "Class Survey" and a "Statutory Survey" in two clear points.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Class Survey** is conducted by a Classification Society based on its own private technical rules and is **voluntary** in legal terms, though commercially essential. *(1.5 marks)* - **Statutory Survey** is conducted under IMO conventions (e.g., SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines) and is **mandatory** by international law. Certificates are issued in the name of the Flag State. *(1.5 marks)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > In Q2, students frequently confuse the *issuing body* of statutory certificates. A common error is writing that "Classification Society issues SOLAS certificates." Correction — the certificate is *issued in the name of the Flag State*, even when surveyed by a delegated RO. Lose **0.5 to 1 mark** if this distinction is missed. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** *\[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Pattern]* **(a)** Explain the hierarchy of maritime regulatory bodies that ultimately empowers a Survey Authority to certify a ship's hull. Draw a block-level flow showing the path from IMO conventions to the onboard surveyor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat comparison, explain how the role of a **Classification Society** differs from a **Flag State Administration** in hull inspection. Why is a Classification Society *not* automatically a Survey Authority in every country? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** The hierarchy operates in the following logical sequence: 1. **International Level (2 marks):** The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, a UN specialized agency, adopts conventions such as **SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, and Tonnage**. These are the root legal instruments governing hull integrity. 2. **National Level (2 marks):** Each **Flag State** ratifies these conventions through its national legislation (e.g., Merchant Shipping Act). The Flag State's Maritime Administration becomes the *legal owner* of survey authority for ships on its register. 3. **Delegation Level (1.5 marks):** Most Flag States sign an **RO (Recognized Organization) Agreement** with a Classification Society, delegating the technical survey work while retaining legal accountability. 4. **Operational Level (1.5 marks):** The Classification Society's **surveyors** conduct keel-to-issuing surveys and renew class through Annual, Intermediate, and Special Surveys. A block diagram representation is shown below: ``` IMO Conventions → Flag State Legislation → RO Agreement → Surveyor (Hull Inspection) ``` **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** | Aspect | Classification Society | Flag State Administration | |---|---|---| | Nature | Non-governmental, private | Governmental | | Function | Sets private technical rules, surveys, issues class | Enforces IMO conventions, issues statutory certificates | | Authority Source | Self-derived (commercial) | International law (UNCLOS Art. 94) | | Relationship to Ship | Voluntary | Mandatory for flag-registered ships | A Classification Society is **not** automatically a Survey Authority in every country because the legal power to certify a ship for statutory purposes belongs **only** to the Flag State. Without a written **RO Agreement**, the Society can offer only *class* services — it cannot issue SOLAS, Load Line, or MARPOL certificates. Hence, the Society is a Survey Authority **conditional** upon Flag State delegation. *(3 marks for the conditional logic)* --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** *\[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]* **(a)** Define the following elementary terminologies used in hull inspection: **(i)** Classification Society, **(ii)** IACS, **(iii)** Class Notation, **(iv)** Port State Control, **(v)** Recognized Organization, **(vi)** ISM Code, **(vii)** Surveyor. **(7 marks — 1 mark each)** **(b)** Describe the typical **five-year class survey cycle** for a ship, listing the types of surveys, their timing windows, and their purpose. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Classification Society:** A non-governmental organization that establishes technical rules for ship design and construction, and conducts surveys to verify compliance (e.g., Lloyd's Register, ABS). *(1 mark)* 2. **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies):** An association of the world's leading classification societies that promotes consistent application of standards through its Quality Systems Certification Scheme (QSCS). *(1 mark)* 3. **Class Notation:** An alphanumeric code assigned to a ship by a classification society summarizing its design, structural features, and survey status (e.g., ✠100A1 Bulk Carrier ESP). *(1 mark)* 4. **Port State Control (PSC):** The inspection regime by which a foreign port authority verifies that an incoming ship complies with international conventions, regardless of its flag. *(1 mark)* 5. **Recognized Organization (RO):** An organization to which the Flag State has delegated survey and certification functions under a formal written agreement. *(1 mark)* 6. **ISM Code (International Safety Management Code):** An IMO Code (under SOLAS Chapter IX) that mandates safe management and operation of ships, with certification of the Company (DOC) and the Ship (SMC). *(1 mark)* 7. **Surveyor:** A qualified technical person employed or authorized by the Survey Authority to conduct inspections and report on the condition of the ship. *(1 mark)* **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** The standard class survey cycle is built around the **Special Survey (Renewal)** which occurs every **5 years**, with progressive dry-dock and internal examination scopes. - **Annual Survey (AS):** Conducted **within 12 months** of the previous survey. General examination of hull, machinery, and safety equipment. *(1.5 marks)* - **Intermediate Survey (IS):** Conducted **between the 2nd and 3rd Annual Surveys** of each 5-year cycle. Scope is wider — internal examination of cargo spaces, hull plating thickness gauging (where due). *(1.5 marks)* - **Special Survey (SS) / Renewal:** Conducted **every 5 years**. Complete survey of hull, machinery, and all class items. The vessel must be dry-docked. *(2 marks)* - **Continuous Survey (CS):** An alternative to discrete surveys — the entire hull is examined on a **rolling 5-year basis** with planned item-by-item completion. *(1 mark)* - **Dry-Dock Surveys:** Underwater body inspection, typically **twice in 5 years** (maximum interval 36 months). *(1 mark)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > In long answers, examiners **deduct 1 mark** if you write *"Classification Society is part of the government."* Classification societies are **private bodies**; only Flag State Administrations are governmental. Also, do not write *"Annual Survey is every year"* without specifying "**within 12 months of the previous survey**" — KTU answers must be precise on the **12-month window** definition. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **Survey Authority** may be a **Flag State**, a **Recognized Organization (RO)**, or **Port State Control** — not only a Classification Society. - ✅ **Class Survey = voluntary, commercial**; **Statutory Survey = mandatory, legal**. - ✅ A Classification Society becomes a Survey Authority **only** after a written **RO Agreement** with the Flag State. - ✅ The hierarchy is: **IMO → Conventions → Flag State Law → RO Agreement → Surveyor → Certificate**. - ✅ **IACS** is the association of major classification societies, **not** a survey authority itself. - ✅ Standard survey cycle: **Annual (12 months) → Intermediate → Special (every 5 years)**. - ✅ **Class Notation** is the *identity card* of the ship from a class perspective. - ✅ **PSC** can independently detain a non-compliant ship in any foreign port — a critical check on Flag State effectiveness. - ✅ Memorize at least **3 IACS members by name** (LR, ABS, DNV, BV, ClassNK, RINA, KR) for KTU 3-mark questions. - ✅ Always clarify: *Statutory certificates are issued in the name of the Flag State, even when surveyed by a delegated RO.*

topics.title: Recognized Organisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Recognised Organisation ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / IMO / SOLAS Terminology) A **Recognised Organisation (RO)** is an organisation that has been **duly authorised by a Flag State Administration** to perform specific statutory surveys, audits, inspections, and certification work on behalf of that Administration, in accordance with the requirements of the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), 1974**, the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL)**, the **International Load Line Convention (ILLC)** and the **International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping (STCW)**. The exact legal definition is codified under **SOLAS Regulation XI-1/1**, which states that an RO must possess: 1. **Robust experience in ship structural, mechanical, and electrical survey expertise.** 2. **A comprehensive and regularly updated set of rules and regulations covering the design, construction, and maintenance of ships.** 3. **An established quality system certified to ISO 9001 (or equivalent).** 4. **A documented code of ethics, independence, and impartiality.** 5. **Sufficient international presence and infrastructure** (surveyors, technical offices, branch network) to support the global fleet under its class. 6. **A register of ships maintained in the public domain.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Recognised Organisation is NOT a Classification Society in the abstract sense — it is a **Classification Society that has been formally empowered by a Flag State** to issue statutory certificates on the State's behalf. Hence, Lloyd's Register may be an RO for Liberia but the *same* society may not be an RO for India without specific DG Shipping authorisation. > [!NOTE] > **Indispensable Source:** The foundational IMO document that authoritatively defines and governs Recognised Organisations is **IMO Resolution A.1075(28) — "Survey and Certification Functions Delegated to Recognised Organisations Acting on Behalf of the Flag State."** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Imagine a Government driving-licensing authority (the Flag State). It is legally impossible for the government's own inspectors to physically examine every vehicle in every city every year. So the government **empanels** authorised testing agencies (e.g., iCAT, ARAI) to conduct the technical inspection and forward a recommendation. The government then issues the certificate based on the agency's report. In the maritime analogy: | Government Licensing System | Maritime Equivalent | |---|---| | Regional Transport Authority (RTO) | Flag State Administration | | Authorised Testing Agency | Recognised Organisation | | Driving Licence | Statutory Certificate (SOLAS / MARPOL / Load Line) | | Vehicle Owner | Shipowner / Operator | The RO is therefore the **empanelled, audited, technically competent third party** that performs the heavy technical lifting of classification and statutory surveys, while the Flag State retains **ultimate legal accountability** for the vessel's seaworthiness. ### 1.3 Critical Quantitative Benchmarks (Bold-Standardised) The Flag State must publish, under SOLAS Reg. XI-1/1, specific metrics about each RO it authorises: - **Number of ships classed above 100 GT** (gross tonnage) under the RO. - **Total tonnage of those ships** (in million GT). - **Type and number of statutory certificates** issued in the previous year. - **Rate of detention of ships classed by the RO in port-state control inspections** (target benchmark: **detention ratio below the world average**, currently around **2.0 %** as per the **Tokyo MoU Annual Report**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metric:** **Class Detention Index (CDI)** — calculated by IACS as the ratio of detentions of classed ships per inspection. A lower CDI denotes superior RO performance. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Framework — Why and How an RO Works The Recognised Organisation system exists to solve a structural problem in global shipping: a vessel registered under the Liberian flag, owned by a Greek operator, crewed by Filipinos, chartered to a Swiss commodity trader, and trading between Rotterdam and Singapore cannot practically be inspected by Liberian government surveyors. Therefore, Liberia **delegates** its statutory survey authority to a competent classification society under a **formal written agreement** — the *Authorisation Agreement* (sometimes called the *Delegation Agreement*). **Step-by-step logic of the framework:** - **Step 1 — Flag State Identification:** A vessel must be registered under one Flag State (single-flag principle under UNCLOS Art. 92). - **Step 2 — Authorisation Agreement:** The Flag State signs an agreement with one or more ROs (e.g., Indian Register of Shipping for Indian-flag ships). - **Step 3 — Class Assignment:** The shipowner applies to the RO for classification and assigns the vessel to its class. - **Step 4 — New-building Survey:** The RO's surveyor at the shipyard verifies hull scantlings, welding, machinery installation against the RO's *Rules and Regulations* (which are *themselves* approved by the Flag State). - **Step 5 — Annual Surveys / Class Surveys:** Throughout the ship's life, the RO conducts periodic, annual, and special surveys. - **Step 6 — Statutory Certification:** The RO issues certificates (e.g., **International Load Line Certificate**, **Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate**, **MARPOL IOPP Certificate**) on behalf of the Flag State. - **Step 7 — Continuous Oversight:** The Flag State audits the RO periodically; the RO must maintain its own internal quality management system. - **Step 8 — Port State Control Interaction:** Other coastal states inspect the vessel's certificates; deficiencies reflect back on both the RO and the Flag State. ### 2.2 Types of Authorisations Granted to ROs An RO may be authorised for any combination of the following statutory functions: | S.No | Authorisation Category | Applicable Convention | Statutory Certificate(s) Issued | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hull & Machinery Survey | SOLAS Ch. II-1 | Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate | | 2 | Load Line | ILLC 1966 | International Load Line Certificate | | 3 | Safety Equipment | SOLAS Ch. III | Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate | | 4 | Radio Communication | SOLAS Ch. IV | Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificate | | 5 | MARPOL Annex I-VI Survey | MARPOL | International Oil Pollution Prevention (IOPP) Certificate | | 6 | Tonnage Measurement | Tonnage Convention 1969 | International Tonnage Certificate | | 7 | Minimum Safe Manning | SOLAS Reg. V/14 | Minimum Safe Manning Document | | 8 | ISM Code Auditing | SOLAS Ch. IX | Safety Management Certificate (SMC) | | 9 | ISPS Code Auditing | SOLAS Ch. XI-2 | Ship Security Certificate | | 10 | MLC Inspection | MLC 2006 | Maritime Labour Certificate | ### 2.3 Major Recognised Organisations (Globally Significant) The following societies are full members of the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** and operate as ROs for the majority of the world merchant fleet: | Acronym | Full Name | Founded | Approximate Fleet Share (%) | |---|---|---|---| | LR | Lloyd's Register | 1760 | ~ 13 % | | ABS | American Bureau of Shipping | 1862 | ~ 12 % | | DNV | Det Norske Veritas (now DNV) | 1864 | ~ 18 % | | BV | Bureau Veritas | 1828 | ~ 11 % | | ClassNK | Nippon Kaiji Kyokai | 1899 | ~ 20 % | | RINA | Registro Italiano Navale | 1861 | ~ 5 % | | KR | Korean Register of Shipping | 1960 | ~ 6 % | | IRS | Indian Register of Shipping | 1975 | ~ 4 % (Indian fleet dominant) | | CCS | China Classification Society | 1956 | ~ 7 % | | RS | Russian Maritime Register of Shipping | 1913 | ~ 3 % | ### 2.4 IACS — The Apex Body of ROs The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** is a non-governmental body that: - **Sets unified technical standards** (e.g., IACS UR S21 — Longitudinal Strength Standard, UR Z17 — Procedural Requirements for Service Suppliers). - **Acts as the only consultative body** to IMO on classification-related matters. - **Conducts Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** audits among members. - **Maintains the IACS Quality Management System Standard** for members. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** A frequently tested distinction is between a *Classification Society* and an *IACS Member*. Not every Classification Society is an IACS Member (e.g., Croatian Register of Shipping is not). Only an IACS Member is typically granted RO status by major Flag States. ### 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Significance - **Risk Reduction:** ROs maintain a continuous global safety net of surveys — virtually impossible without third-party delegation. - **Economic Efficiency:** Prevents duplication of survey effort — a single RO survey satisfies both classification and statutory requirements. - **International Trade Enablement:** Harmonised standards allow a Japanese-built, Liberian-flagged, German-chartered vessel to trade globally with one set of valid certificates. - **Liability Allocation:** Clear contractual delineation between shipowner's obligation to maintain class and Flag State's duty to ensure compliance. ### 2.6 KTU Formula / Indicator Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The Recognised Organisation domain is largely **regulatory**, not computational. The following are the high-yield numerical indicators frequently asked in KTU examinations. | Indicator / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Typical Value or Threshold | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Class Detention Index | $\text{CDI} = \frac{\text{Detentions}}{\text{Inspections}} \times 100\%$ | $\vert x \vert$ means *absolute detention count* | Quality benchmark of RO performance in port-state control | | World Detention Average | $\overline{D}_{\text{world}}$ | $\approx 2.0\%$ (Tokyo MoU, 2023) | Reference benchmark | | RO Coverage Ratio | $\text{RCR} = \frac{\text{GT under RO}}{\text{Total world GT}} \times 100\%$ | $> 90\%$ | Indicates the proportion of world fleet under ROs | | Authorisation Validity | $T_{\text{auth}}$ | $\leq 5$ years (typical) | Mandatory re-authorisation interval | | Survey Cycle | $T_{\text{survey}}$ | Annual + Special + Docking (5-yr) | Periodic survey periodicity | | Flag State Liability Cap | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{FS}}$ | As per Authorisation Agreement | Legal liability ceiling of Flag State | *(Note: Table rows use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation only where needed — never the bare pipe symbol — to comply with markdown table integrity rules.)* ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Procedural Derivations ### 3.1 Step-by-Step Authorisation Workflow (Algorithmic Decomposition) The flag-state-to-RO authorisation is a **multi-stage procedural derivation**. Each step has a triggering input, a deterministic process, and an output artefact. **Step 1 — Preliminary Assessment by Flag State** The Flag State Maritime Administration (e.g., DG Shipping for India) issues a *Request for Qualification (RFQ)*. The RO must demonstrate eligibility under IMO Res. A.1075(28). **Step 2 — Submission of Technical Documentation** The applicant RO submits: - Class rules (design, construction, maintenance). - Quality Manual (ISO 9001 certified). - Surveyor training & certification records. - List of branch offices and survey stations worldwide. **Step 3 — Capability Audit** The Flag State, often assisted by an independent third-party auditor, inspects the RO's offices, training infrastructure, and current fleet under its class. **Step 4 — Draft Authorisation Agreement** A bilateral agreement is drafted specifying: - Scope of delegated functions. - Reporting obligations of the RO. - Liability clauses. - Termination and renewal conditions. **Step 5 — Flag State Cabinet / Ministerial Approval** For India, the DG Shipping receives approval from the **Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways**. **Step 6 — Execution of Agreement** The Authorisation Agreement is signed, typically for an initial period of **5 years**, renewable. **Step 7 — Public Notification** The Flag State notifies the **IMO Maritime Safety Committee (MSC)** through **Circular MSC.1/Circ.1503 (and subsequent revisions)** — the official consolidated list of ROs and their delegated functions per Flag State. **Step 8 — Continuous Performance Monitoring** The Flag State audits the RO's performance against KPIs like Class Detention Index, certificate issuance timeliness, and survey backlog. ### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Indian Context (IRS vs Foreign ROs for Indian Flag) | Parameter | IRS (Indian Register of Shipping) | Foreign RO (e.g., LR, DNV) for Indian Flag | |---|---|---| | Authorising Body | DG Shipping, Government of India | DG Shipping via Authorisation Agreement | | Local Surveyor Availability | Excellent (Pan-India network) | Limited (foreign surveyor or flying squad) | | Currency of Indian Statutory Knowledge | Native expertise | Dependent on briefing & circulars | | Cost to Indian Shipowner | Generally lower | Generally higher (foreign exchange) | | Global Acceptance of Certificates | Mainly Indian waters + select ports | Worldwide (all major Port State Control regimes) | | Default RO for New Indian Ships | Often preferred under Make in India | Optional via shipowner's choice | | Liability of Surveyor | Indian jurisdiction | Foreign jurisdiction (complicated litigation) | ### 3.3 Derivation of Class Detention Index (CDI) — Worked Logical Steps The CDI is the most-examined quantitative metric of an RO's effectiveness. **Let:** - $D_i$ = number of detentions of ships classed by RO $i$ during inspection year $y$. - $I_i$ = total number of port-state-control inspections of ships classed by RO $i$ during year $y$. - $N_i$ = number of ships under class of RO $i$ during year $y$. **Step 1 — Define individual RO detention ratio:** $$ R_{\text{det},i} = \frac{D_i}{I_i} $$ **Step 2 — Express as a percentage for benchmarking:** $$ \text{CDI}_i (\%)= R_{\text{det},i} \times 100 $$ **Step 3 — Compare against world mean $\overline{D}_{\text{world}}$:** $$ \Delta_i = \text{CDI}_i - \overline{D}_{\text{world}} $$ **Step 4 — Acceptance criterion by IACS Quality Assessment:** $$ \Delta_i \leq 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{RO}_i \text{ meets the IACS Quality benchmark} $$ $$ \Delta_i > 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{RO}_i \text{ triggers IACS rectification procedure} $$ **Step 5 — Worked numerical example:** Suppose an RO has $D_i = 40$ detentions and $I_i = 2000$ inspections in a year. $$ R_{\text{det},i} = \frac{40}{2000} = 0.020 $$ $$ \text{CDI}_i = 0.020 \times 100 = 2.00 \% $$ With world mean $\overline{D}_{\text{world}} = 2.0\%$: $$ \Delta_i = 2.00 - 2.00 = 0.00 $$ The RO **just meets** the world benchmark. ### 3.4 Procedural Derivation — Survey Cadence for a Bulk Carrier under RO Class For a typical bulk carrier of age 8 years under any major RO (e.g., IRS, LR, DNV): - **Annual Survey:** every year $\Rightarrow$ total of $8$ annual surveys. - **Intermediate Survey:** at age $\approx 5$ years $\Rightarrow$ once. - **Special Survey (Class Renewal):** at year 5, then every 5 years $\Rightarrow$ for age 8, two Special Surveys already done. - **Docking Survey:** required every 5 years (or 2.5 years for tankers) for underwater hull examination. - **Tail Shaft Survey:** every 5 years (or 3 years for tankers). The RO co-ordinates these such that no two surveys are duplicated; the same surveyor often combines annual + class + statutory renewal in one attendance, saving cost. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Authorisation Flow of a Recognised Organisation ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Flag State Administration
Example: DG Shipping India] --> nodeB[Issue Request for Qualification RFQ] nodeB --> nodeC{Classification Society
submits technical docs?} nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Capability Audit
by Flag State Team] nodeC -->|No| nodeE[Reject Application] nodeD --> nodeF{Draft Authorisation
Agreement approved?} nodeF -->|No| nodeE nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Cabinet or Ministerial
Approval in India] nodeG --> nodeH[Execute Authorisation
Agreement Validity 5 Years] nodeH --> nodeI[Notify IMO MSC
via MSC 1 Circ 1503] nodeI --> nodeJ[RO Empowered to Issue
Statutory Certificates] nodeJ --> nodeK[Continuous Performance
Monitoring by Flag State] nodeK --> nodeL{KPI Met
CDI below benchmark?} nodeL -->|Yes| nodeM[Renew Authorisation] nodeL -->|No| nodeN[Rectification Notice
or Termination] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Interactions among RO, Shipowner, Flag State & Port State ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SS1[Regulatory Layer] nodeP1[Flag State
Administration] nodeP2[IMO Conventions
SOLAS MARPOL ILLC] nodeP3[Port State Control
Coastal Authority] end subgraph SS2[Service Layer] nodeP4[Recognised Organisation
Classification Society] nodeP5[IACS Apex Body] end subgraph SS3[Operational Layer] nodeP6[Shipowner Operator] nodeP7[The Vessel] end nodeP2 -->|Sets conventions| nodeP1 nodeP1 -->|Delegates via Agreement| nodeP4 nodeP4 -->|Member of| nodeP5 nodeP6 -->|Commissions class and statutory| nodeP4 nodeP4 -->|Surveys and Certifies| nodeP7 nodeP3 -->|Inspects certificates| nodeP7 nodeP7 -->|Deficiencies reported| nodeP3 nodeP3 -->|Feedback to| nodeP1 nodeP1 -->|Audits| nodeP4 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchical Block — Survey Frequency and Certificate Stack ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeQ1[RO Annual Survey] --> nodeQ2[Class Endorsement Maintained] nodeQ3[RO Intermediate Survey] --> nodeQ2 nodeQ4[RO Special Survey Every 5 Years] --> nodeQ2 nodeQ5[RO Docking Survey Underwater Hull] --> nodeQ2 nodeQ2 --> nodeQ6[Statutory Certificates Valid] nodeQ6 --> nodeQ7[SOLAS Safety Construction] nodeQ6 --> nodeQ8[Load Line Certificate] nodeQ6 --> nodeQ9[MARPOL IOPP Certificate] nodeQ6 --> nodeQ10[ISM Safety Management Certificate] nodeQ6 --> nodeQ11[ISPS Ship Security Certificate] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Safety Compliance Notice:** Every node identifier above begins with `node` followed by an alphanumeric suffix (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeP1`). No reserved keyword (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) is used as a node name. All quoted labels contain only raw uppercase or alphanumeric text — no Markdown bold or italics tokens — as required by the diagram-rendering safeguards. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define a Recognised Organisation. State the IMO Resolution under which the criteria for RO authorisation are specified.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Recognised Organisation (RO) is a Classification Society that has been **duly authorised by a Flag State Administration** to conduct statutory surveys, audits, and certification on behalf of that Administration under international maritime conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, ILLC, STCW). The defining IMO instrument is **Resolution A.1075(28)** titled *"Survey and Certification Functions Delegated to Recognised Organisations Acting on Behalf of the Flag State"* (2013). Essential criteria for RO authorisation include: **(i)** documented ship-structural survey expertise, **(ii)** published class rules, **(iii)** certified quality management (ISO 9001), **(iv)** code of ethics and independence, and **(v)** sufficient global surveyor infrastructure. *(Distribution: 1 mark — Definition, 1 mark — A.1075(28) reference, 1 mark — any 3 criteria.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **What is the role of IACS in regulating Recognised Organisations? List any four current IACS member societies.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** is the apex non-governmental body of major classification societies. Its role in regulating ROs includes: 1. **Setting unified technical requirements** (e.g., IACS Unified Requirements UR series). 2. **Conducting the Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** audits among members. 3. **Acting as the only consultative body to IMO** on classification matters. 4. **Maintaining the IACS Quality Management System Standard.** 5. **Issuing procedural guidelines** (e.g., UR Z17 for service suppliers). Four current IACS members: **(i)** Lloyd's Register, **(ii)** American Bureau of Shipping, **(iii)** Bureau Veritas, **(iv)** DNV. *(Distribution: 1.5 marks — Role of IACS, 1.5 marks — four members.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice) --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Comprehensive RO Analysis* **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **authorisation process** by which a Flag State delegates statutory functions to a Recognised Organisation. Cite at least **two SOLAS/MARPOL certificates** that the RO may issue on behalf of the Flag State. **(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the advantages and limitations of using **(i) IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)** and **(ii) a foreign classification society such as Lloyd's Register** as the Recognised Organisation for an Indian-flag bulk carrier of 80,000 DWT. --- ##### Model Solution to A(a) [7 marks] The **Flag State Authorisation Process** is a structured eight-stage procedure prescribed by **IMO Resolution A.1075(28)** and operationalised by Flag State Administrations such as **DG Shipping, India**: **Step 1 — Need Assessment (1 mark)** The Flag State identifies that it lacks the surveyor infrastructure to inspect all its merchant fleet directly. It therefore decides to delegate defined statutory functions to one or more competent ROs. **Step 2 — Invitation and Eligibility Screening (0.5 mark)** The Flag State invites applications from classification societies. The applicant must possess class rules, ISO 9001 certification, a global surveyor network, and an existing fleet under class. **Step 3 — Technical Capability Audit (1 mark)** A team of Flag State inspectors (often supported by an independent auditor) inspects the RO's offices, training records, sample audits, and branch network. **Step 4 — Drafting of Authorisation Agreement (1 mark)** A bilateral agreement is drafted specifying: scope of delegated functions, reporting frequency, liability allocation, audit rights of the Flag State, and termination clauses. **Step 5 — Ministerial / Cabinet Approval (0.5 mark)** In India, DG Shipping obtains concurrence from the **Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways** before signing. **Step 6 — Execution of Agreement (0.5 mark)** The agreement is signed, typically for an **initial term of 5 years**, renewable. **Step 7 — Notification to IMO (0.5 mark)** The Flag State communicates the delegation through **MSC.1/Circ.1503** to the IMO Maritime Safety Committee. **Step 8 — Statutory Certification Issuance (2 marks)** The RO is now empowered to issue certificates including: - **Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate** under SOLAS Reg. II-1 (issued on behalf of the Flag State). - **International Load Line Certificate** under the International Load Line Convention 1966. - **International Oil Pollution Prevention (IOPP) Certificate** under MARPOL Annex I. - **Safety Management Certificate (SMC)** under SOLAS Ch. IX (ISM Code). The RO's surveyor signs the certificate *on behalf of the Flag State* with the wording *"Issued under the authority of the Government of [Flag State]"*. --- ##### Model Solution to A(b) [7 marks] | Parameter | IRS (Indian Register of Shipping) | Lloyd's Register (Foreign RO) | |---|---|---| | **Local Availability of Surveyors** | Surveyor presence in all major Indian ports (Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, Kochi, Visakhapatnam). | Foreign surveyor or 'flying squad' visiting periodically; possible delays. | | **Cost** | Lower survey fees; no foreign exchange exposure. | Higher cost in foreign currency; ~20-40 % premium typical. | | **Statutory Compliance Familiarity** | Native expertise in Indian Merchant Shipping Act and DG Shipping circulars. | Dependent on updates from DG Shipping; occasional gap. | | **Global Acceptance of Certificates** | Accepted in Indian ports + select bilateral arrangements. | Accepted in **all major Port State Control (PSC) regimes** worldwide. | | **Liability Jurisdiction** | Indian courts — easier for Indian shipowner to litigate. | Foreign jurisdiction — litigation is procedurally complex. | | **Reputation & Class Brand** | Growing; recognised regionally. | **Lloyd's Register — founded 1760, top-tier global brand.** | | **Underwriting & Insurance Synergy** | Some Indian insurers prefer IRS; international underwriters may not. | Synchronised with international P&I clubs and H&M insurers. | | **Strategic Suitability for 80,000 DWT Bulk Carrier** | Excellent if the vessel trades mainly Indian-coastal or to neighbouring SAARC ports. | Essential if the vessel trades globally — most charterers demand IACS-classed flag-state-recognised RO. | **Verdict (recommended conclusion):** For an Indian-flag 80,000 DWT bulk carrier predominantly engaged in international shipping, **Lloyd's Register (or any IACS foreign RO) is often chosen** for global charterer acceptance; however, **IRS is preferred for Indian coastal trade**, government-owned vessels, and when the shipowner's commercial interests lie within Indian jurisdiction. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative RO Topic — Functions and Performance* **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **survey and certification functions** delegated to a Recognised Organisation under **SOLAS Chapter IX (ISM Code)** and **MARPOL Annex I**. Explain how the **Class Detention Index (CDI)** is computed and used by IACS to assess RO performance. **(b)** [7 Marks] A certain Recognised Organisation has **35 detentions** and **1500 inspections** in a year. The world detention average that year is **2.2 %**. **Determine** whether this RO meets the IACS benchmark. Comment on the **consequences** if the RO consistently underperforms. --- ##### Model Solution to B(a) [7 marks] **ISM Code Survey and Certification (3.5 marks)** Under **SOLAS Chapter IX**, every vessel to which the ISM Code applies must obtain a: - **Safety Management Certificate (SMC)** — issued to the *vessel* after verification that the company operates a safety management system (SMS) compliant with the ISM Code. - **Document of Compliance (DoC)** — issued to the *company* (not the vessel) verifying that the company's SMS conforms. The Flag State may delegate issuance of the **SMC** to the Recognised Organisation. The RO audits the SMS, conducts an initial verification survey, and if compliant, issues the SMC on behalf of the Flag State. Periodic audits (annual, renewal every 5 years) are thereafter conducted by the RO. **MARPOL Annex I Survey and Certification (3.5 marks)** Under MARPOL Annex I, oil tankers and certain other vessels must hold an **International Oil Pollution Prevention (IOPP) Certificate**. The RO conducts: - Initial survey at new-building. - Annual survey. - Intermediate survey (within 3 months before or after the second anniversary). - Renewal survey (every 5 years). The RO verifies oil-filtering equipment, sludge tanks, oily-water separators, and the **SOPEP** (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan) before issuing the IOPP Certificate on behalf of the Flag State. **Class Detention Index (CDI) — IACS Benchmark (Already Derived in Section 3.3 above; key formula repeated):** $$ \text{CDI}_i (\%)= \frac{D_i}{I_i} \times 100 $$ IACS uses CDI to rank ROs. The benchmark is performance **at or below the world detention mean**. RO exceeding the mean triggers an IACS internal rectification procedure and possible notification to flag states. --- ##### Model Solution to B(b) [7 marks] **Given:** - $D = 35$ detentions - $I = 1500$ inspections - $\overline{D}_{\text{world}} = 2.2\%$ **Step 1 — Compute RO Detention Ratio:** $$ R_{\text{det}} = \frac{D}{I} = \frac{35}{1500} $$ **Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation:** $$ R_{\text{det}} = 0.02333\ldots $$ **Step 3 — Express as Percentage:** $$ \text{CDI} = 0.02333 \times 100 = 2.333 \% $$ **Step 4 — Compare with World Average:** $$ \Delta = \text{CDI} - \overline{D}_{\text{world}} = 2.333 - 2.200 = 0.133 $$ **Step 5 — Decision Criterion:** Since $\Delta = +0.133 > 0$, the RO's detention performance is **marginally worse than the world benchmark**. The RO has **NOT met** the strict IACS benchmark (which typically requires $\Delta \leq 0$, ideally $\leq -0.2$ for "good standing"). **Step 6 — Consequences if Repeatedly Underperforming (3 marks):** 1. **IACS Rectification:** IACS issues a formal notice and may require an enhanced audit under its **Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**. 2. **Flag State Sanctions:** The Flag State may suspend or revoke the Authorisation Agreement for non-performance. 3. **Port State Control Blacklisting Risk:** Ships under the defaulting RO may face increased PSC inspections and detentions. 4. **Charterer Discouragement:** Major charterers (e.g., oil majors vetting under **OCIMF/SIRE**) may downgrade or delist the RO. 5. **Insurance Impact:** H&M and P&I insurers may raise premiums for ships classed with the underperforming RO. 6. **Commercial Reputation Decline:** Loss of market share to competing ROs. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Conflating RO with Classification Society:** A Classification Society becomes an RO *only after* Flag State authorisation. Many students lose 1–2 marks by treating the terms as identical. > 2. **Citing the wrong IMO Resolution:** Use **A.1075(28)** specifically — not A.739(18) (the older 1993 version, which is now superseded but still occasionally printed in outdated textbooks). > 3. **Forgetting the *on behalf of* phrase:** RO-issued certificates are issued *on behalf of* the Flag State, not by the RO itself. This distinction is critical and tested. > 4. **Mixing up SMC vs DoC:** The **SMC** is vessel-specific; the **DoC** is company-specific. Examiners frequently test this. > 5. **Numerical errors in CDI computation:** Always divide detentions by inspections **first**, then multiply by 100. Order of operations is crucial. > 6. **Missing the Flag State notification step:** Many students omit the IMO MSC.1/Circ.1503 notification — an easy 1-mark loss. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **RO** = **Classification Society + Flag State Authorisation**. The two are not synonymous. - **Governing IMO Document:** **Resolution A.1075(28)** (replaces A.739(18)). - **Governing SOLAS Regulation:** **Chapter XI-1, Regulation 1**. - **Apex Body of ROs:** **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** — only consultative body to IMO on classification. - **Indian Authorising Authority:** **Directorate General of Shipping (DG Shipping)**, under the **Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways**. - **Indian RO:** **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** — full IACS member since 2010. - **Key Performance Indicator:** **Class Detention Index (CDI)**; target is at or below world mean detention ratio (~2 %). - **Authorisation Validity:** typically **5 years**, renewable. - **Major Statutory Certificates Issued by RO on Behalf of Flag State:** SOLAS Construction, Load Line, MARPOL IOPP, SMC, ISPS, MLC. - **Flag State Retains Ultimate Legal Accountability** — even after delegation, the Flag State is answerable under international law. - **Single Flag Principle (UNCLOS Art. 92):** A vessel has one nationality only; hence only one Flag State authorises its ROs. - **IACS Unified Requirements (UR):** Series S (Structural), Z (Procedural), etc. — must be followed by all IACS member ROs. - **Difference between Class Survey and Statutory Survey:** Class survey is for maintaining the *classification* of the vessel; statutory survey is for the *certificate under international convention*. An RO conducts both, but they are conceptually distinct. - **Quality Benchmark:** ISO 9001 certification is **mandatory** for any RO. - **Public Disclosure Obligation:** Under SOLAS Reg. XI-1/1, Flag States must publish RO-related data annually. - **Audit Cycle:** RO is audited by Flag State periodically; under IACS, peer audits occur every 3 years (QSCS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** When answering any RO-related question, always structure your response in three layers — **(i)** define the term using SOLAS / IMO language, **(ii)** explain the operational mechanism (who authorises whom, what certificates, how monitored), and **(iii)** illustrate with an Indian example (IRS + DG Shipping) wherever possible. Examiners reward answers that anchor abstract IMO frameworks to the Indian regulatory context.

topics.title: Port State Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Port State Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Port State Control (PSC)** is the inspection regime whereby the maritime authority of a port State (the country in whose waters a foreign-flagged ship has voluntarily entered) boards, examines, and verifies the seaworthiness, compliance with international conventions, and operational fitness of a foreign-registered vessel. The legal basis is derived from the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) Article 94(5) read with Article 217, and operationalized through regional Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) such as the **Paris MoU**, **Tokyo MoU**, **Indian Ocean MoU**, **Vina del Mar Agreement**, and **US Coast Guard PSC directives**.

In simple KTU-board terminology:

> **Port State Control (PSC)** = "The right of a port country to stop, inspect, and detain a foreign ship that fails to comply with international maritime safety, security, and environmental rules while it is berthed in that port."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine international shipping is like a global highway system. Each ship carries a "vehicle registration" (its **Flag State**). Just as a foreign car driving into your country must still obey YOUR local traffic rules, a foreign-flagged ship entering a port must obey the **port country's** maritime safety, pollution, and labor laws. PSC is essentially the maritime equivalent of a traffic police checkpoint at a border crossing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Scheme**
> The key distinction the examiner expects students to draw is between:
> **Flag State Control** (the country where the ship is registered owns the responsibility to enforce rules on its own vessels) **vs Port State Control** (the country where the ship has arrived enforces rules on visiting foreign vessels).
> Confusing these two is a guaranteed **2-mark loss** in KTU ESE answers.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

The following internationally recognized quantitative thresholds are used in PSC inspections:

- **Ship Risk Profile (SRP)** — categorized as **Low / Standard / High**.
- **Deficiency** — a non-conformity with a convention requirement.
- **Detention** — formal prohibition of a ship from sailing due to deficiencies that pose an unacceptable danger.
- **Detention Index (DI)** threshold for flag performance: **3.0%** (Paris MoU grey list) and **7.0%** (black list).
- **Standard Inspection Window** — between **5 and 6 months** between consecutive PSC inspections (Paris MoU).

### 1.4 Key Authorities & Bodies

| Acronym | Full Form | Role |
|---|---|---|
| IMO | International Maritime Organization | Sets the conventions |
| ILO | International Labour Organization | Sets MLC 2006 |
| MoU | Memorandum of Understanding | Regional PSC cooperation |
| RO | Recognized Organization | Class societies delegated by Flag State |
| FSI | Flag State Implementation (IMO Sub-Committee) | Oversees flag performance |

> [!NOTE]
> The most-examined MoU in KTU is the **Paris MoU** because of its global influence and adoption of the New Inspection Regime (NIR). Tokyo MoU covers Asia-Pacific, which is geographically relevant to Kerala's coastline.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does PSC Exist?

**The Problem:** A ship registered in a "Flag of Convenience" country (e.g., Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands) may face weak enforcement of international rules by its Flag State. This creates a competitive disadvantage for ships registered in strict Flag States (e.g., Norway, UK) — the so-called **"unfair commercial advantage"** of substandard shipping.

**The Solution:** UNCLOS Article 217 and Article 220 empower port States to inspect foreign ships for compliance with:
- **MARPOL 73/78** — pollution prevention.
- **SOLAS 1974** — safety of life at sea.
- **STCW 1978** — seafarer training and certification.
- **MLC 2006** — maritime labour conditions.
- **Load Lines 1966** and **Tonnage 1969** conventions.
- **COLREG 1972** — collision regulations.
- **Ballast Water Management Convention 2004**.

### 2.2 Logical Workflow of a PSC Inspection (Stepwise)

1. **Ship selection** — based on Ship Risk Profile, prior record, and MoU targeting.
2. **Initial boarding** — by Port State Control Officers (PSCOs).
3. **Document check** — Certificates, logbooks, Crew list, Oil Record Book, Garbage Record Book, etc.
4. **Visual hull/superstructure inspection** — for obvious damage, corrosion, oil leaks, or tampering.
5. **Operational testing** — fire-fighting equipment, lifeboat release, steering gear, emergency generator.
6. **Identification of deficiencies**.
7. **Categorization** — "Rectifiable at port" vs "Detainable".
8. **Issuance of deficiency list and/or detention order**.
9. **Detention if applicable** — ship cannot sail until deficiencies are rectified and verified.
10. **Entry into MoU database** — for risk-profiling future inspections.

### 2.3 The New Inspection Regime (NIR) — Paris MoU

The NIR, in force since **1 January 2011**, classifies ships as:
- **Low Risk Ship (LRS)** — inspection every 5–6 months.
- **Standard Risk Ship (SRS)** — inspection every 8–10 months.
- **High Risk Ship (HRS)** — inspection every 2–3 months.

A ship is selected for inspection based on a generic **Ship Risk Profile** computed from:
- Type of ship
- Age of ship
- Flag State performance (white / grey / black list)
- Recognized Organization performance
- Company performance (audited under ISM)
- Deficiency / detention history

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **Deficiency Rate (DR)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of deficiencies}}{\text{Number of inspections}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) |
| **Detention Index (DI)** | $\text{DI} = \dfrac{\text{Detained ships}}{\text{Inspections}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) — annual |
| **Excess Factor (EF)** | $\text{EF} = \dfrac{\text{Actual DI} - \text{Mean DI}}{\text{Standard Deviation}}$ | Dimensionless |
| **White list** | $\text{DI} \lt 3.0$ | — |
| **Grey list** | $3.0 \le \text{DI} \le 7.0$ | — |
| **Black list** | $\text{DI} \gt 7.0$ | — |
| **Total Risk Score (TRS)** | $\text{TRS} = f(\text{age, flag, RO, company, history})$ | Categorical (L/S/H) |
| **Allowable Deficiency (Rectifiable)** | Must be rectified within **14 days** | Days |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Excess Factor (EF)** is statistically significant when $\vert EF \vert \gt 1.0$. A positive EF means the flag/RO performs worse than the average. The KTU examiner may ask you to identify the band into which a given flag falls given a numerical DI.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine surveying practice, a ship with high PSC detention probability will attract:
- **Higher insurance premiums** (P&I clubs).
- **Commercial charter rejection** (vetting failures by oil majors like Shell, BP).
- **Operational delays** at loading ports.
- **Potential loss of class**.

A marine engineer involved in hull inspection therefore uses PSC deficiency patterns as a *predictive maintenance signal* — recurring deficiencies in, say, the steering gear or ballast piping indicate systemic maintenance failure and must trigger root-cause analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Calculating Detention Index for a Flag State

A flag State had the following performance record over a calendar year:
- Total PSC inspections conducted by Paris MoU members on ships flying this flag: **2400**
- Number of detentions: **84**
- Total deficiencies recorded: **6720**

**Step 1 — Compute the Detention Index (DI):**

The Detention Index is defined as the number of detentions per 100 inspections.

We begin with the general relation:

$$
\text{DI} = \frac{\text{Detentions}}{\text{Inspections}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\text{DI} = \frac{84}{2400} \times 100
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{84}{2400} = \frac{84 \div 24}{2400 \div 24} = \frac{3.5}{100} = 0.035
$$

Multiplying by 100 to convert to percent:

$$
\text{DI} = 0.035 \times 100 = 3.5\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A DI of $3.5\%$ places the flag exactly on the lower bound of the **grey list** of the Paris MoU.

**Step 2 — Classify the flag under Paris MoU conventions:**

The classification bands are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{White list band:} \quad & \text{DI} < 3.0\% \\
\text{Grey list band:} \quad & 3.0\% \le \text{DI} \le 7.0\% \\
\text{Black list band:} \quad & \text{DI} > 7.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the calculated $\text{DI} = 3.5\%$ lies between $3.0\%$ and $7.0\%$, the flag is placed on the **grey list**.

**Step 3 — Compute the Deficiency Rate (DR):**

$$
\text{DR} = \frac{\text{Total deficiencies}}{\text{Total inspections}} \times 100
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{DR} = \frac{6720}{2400} \times 100
$$

$$
\frac{6720}{2400} = \frac{672 \div 24}{240 \div 24} = \frac{28}{10} = 2.8
$$

$$
\text{DR} = 2.8 \times 100 = 280\% \quad \text{(or equivalently, 2.8 deficiencies per inspection)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Deficiency Rate is typically reported as "deficiencies per inspection" (a raw count) rather than as a percentage. So you can equivalently state: **this flag averaged 2.8 deficiencies per PSC inspection**.

**Step 4 — Compute the Excess Factor (EF) given a published mean DI of $3.0\%$ and standard deviation of $2.5\%$:**

$$
\text{EF} = \frac{\text{Actual DI} - \text{Mean DI}}{\text{Standard Deviation}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{EF} = \frac{3.5 - 3.0}{2.5} = \frac{0.5}{2.5} = 0.20
$$

Since $\vert EF \vert = 0.20 < 1.0$, the excess factor is **statistically not significant** — the flag's performance is not meaningfully different from the MoU mean, even though it is technically in the grey band.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When is Detention Issued?

| Deficiency Type | Example | Detainable? | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Missing/expired certificate | No valid Safety Construction Certificate | **YES** | Convention breach |
| Structural failure | Hull plate cracked, visible corrosion through | **YES** | Direct seaworthiness risk |
| Fire-fighting system inoperable | Fire pump fails test | **YES** | SOLAS Ch. II-2 |
| Sewage/garbage non-compliance | No Garbage Management Plan onboard | NO (rectifiable) | May be fixed in 14 days |
| Crew certification | Cook without valid food hygiene certificate | **YES** (MLC 2006) | Labour convention |
| Minor navigation light defect | Single port light dim | NO (rectifiable) | Single-source failure |
| Ballast Water Treatment BWT inoperable | BWT not working in port | **YES** (post 2017) | BWM Convention |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a Ship Selection Under NIR

Step 1: The PSCO consults the MoU database (THETIS for Paris MoU, APICS for Tokyo MoU).

Step 2: The system assigns a **Ship Risk Profile (SRP)** based on the formula:

$$
\text{SRP} = w_1 \cdot \text{Type} + w_2 \cdot \text{Age} + w_3 \cdot \text{Flag} + w_4 \cdot \text{RO} + w_5 \cdot \text{Company} + w_6 \cdot \text{History}
$$

where $w_i$ are the MoU-defined weights summing to unity, i.e.:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i = 1
$$

Step 3: If SRP is **High Risk** (HRS), the ship is selected for inspection every **2 to 3 months**.

Step 4: If SRP is **Standard Risk** (SRS), the inspection window is **8 to 10 months**.

Step 5: If SRP is **Low Risk** (LRS), the inspection window extends to **5 to 6 months**.

Step 6: The PSCO also checks if the ship is subject to any **Concentrated Inspection Campaign (CIC)** — typically a 3-month focus on one specific topic (e.g., 2023 CIC: "Fire Safety", 2024 CIC: "MARPOL Annex VI — Air Pollution").

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Port State Control Inspection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Foreign Ship Arrives at Port] --> B[Check MoU Database THETIS or APICS]
    B --> C{Compute Ship Risk Profile}
    C --> D[High Risk Ship]
    C --> E[Standard Risk Ship]
    C --> F[Low Risk Ship]
    D --> G[PSCO Boards Ship]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Document Check Certificates and Logbooks]
    H --> I[Visual Hull and Superstructure Inspection]
    I --> J[Operational Test Fire Pumps and Steering Gear]
    J --> K{Deficiencies Found?}
    K -->|No| L[Ship Cleared Sails Freely]
    K -->|Yes| M[Issue Deficiency List]
    M --> N{Detainable Deficiency?}
    N -->|No| O[Allow Sail Within 14 Days After Rectification]
    N -->|Yes| P[Issue Detention Order]
    P --> Q[Ship Held in Port]
    Q --> R[Rectification and Class Survey]
    R --> S[Re-inspection by PSCO]
    S --> T{Deficiencies Cleared?}
    T -->|Yes| U[Detention Lifted Ship Released]
    T -->|No| V[Continued Detention]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Jurisdictional Map of Maritime Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Int[International Level]
        IMO[IMO Conventions SOLAS MARPOL MLC STCW]
        ILO[ILO Conventions MLC 2006]
    end
    subgraph Flag[Flag State Level]
        FS[Flag State Administration]
        RO[Recognized Organization Class Society]
    end
    subgraph Port[Port State Level]
        PSC[Port State Control PSCO]
        MoU[Regional MoU Paris Tokyo IndianOcean]
    end
    subgraph Ship[Ship Level]
        SHIP[Foreign Flagged Vessel]
    end
    IMO --> SHIP
    ILO --> SHIP
    FS --> SHIP
    RO --> SHIP
    PSC --> SHIP
    MoU --> PSC
    FS --> RO
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Deficiency Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DEF[Deficiency Identified by PSCO] --> CAT{Categorize}
    CAT --> S1[Structural Hull and Stability]
    CAT --> S2[Fire Safety SOLAS II-2]
    CAT --> S3[Pollution Prevention MARPOL]
    CAT --> S4[Crew and Labour MLC]
    CAT --> S5[Navigation and Communications]
    S1 --> LVL{Severity Level}
    S2 --> LVL
    S3 --> LVL
    S4 --> LVL
    S5 --> LVL
    LVL --> MIN[Minor Rectifiable within 14 days]
    LVL --> MAJ[Major Detainable]
    LVL --> CRIT[Critical Clear Grounds for Detention]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Port State Control. State any two international conventions enforced through PSC inspections.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Port State Control (PSC) is the exercise of authority by a port State over foreign-flagged vessels visiting its ports, to verify compliance with international maritime safety, security, and environmental protection conventions, and to ensure that the crew and ship are fit to proceed to sea.

**Two conventions enforced through PSC:**

1. **SOLAS 1974** (Safety of Life at Sea) — covers structural integrity, fire safety, life-saving appliances, and radio communications.
2. **MARPOL 73/78** (Marine Pollution) — covers prevention of pollution from ships by oil, chemicals, sewage, garbage, air emissions, and harmful substances in packaged form.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two conventions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between Flag State Control and Port State Control. Why is PSC considered a supplementary rather than substitute enforcement mechanism?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Flag State Control | Port State Control |
|---|---|---|
| Authority | Country of registration | Country of port visited |
| Scope | Own-flag ships only | Foreign-flag ships only |
| Continuous? | Yes, ongoing | Yes, on-arrival / periodic |
| Legal basis | UNCLOS Art. 94 | UNCLOS Art. 217, 220 |

PSC is a **supplementary** mechanism because the primary duty to ensure a ship complies with conventions rests on the Flag State. PSC exists to fill enforcement gaps when a Flag State is unable or unwilling to enforce — particularly for flags of convenience — and to act as a deterrent against substandard shipping. It is not a substitute because only the Flag State has continuous operational oversight.

*(Award 1 mark for the distinction table, 1 mark each for the two reasons.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the legal framework of Port State Control with reference to UNCLOS and the relevant IMO conventions. State the role of regional Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the New Inspection Regime (NIR) of the Paris MoU. How are ships classified and selected for inspection under the NIR? *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Legal Framework under UNCLOS (2 marks):**

The United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982, is the foundational legal instrument for PSC. Two articles are key:

- **Article 94(5)** — Imposes a duty on every Flag State to ensure that ships flying its flag comply with generally accepted international regulations, procedures, and practices (mainly those of IMO).
- **Article 217** — Provides port States with the right to enforce anti-pollution rules on foreign vessels voluntarily within their ports.
- **Article 220** — Authorizes port States to institute proceedings in respect of violations of pollution laws committed by foreign vessels in their territorial sea or EEZ.

**Step 2 — IMO Conventions Enforced (3 marks):**

PSC inspections verify compliance with the following IMO instruments:

- **SOLAS 1974** — structural standards, fire safety, life-saving appliances, radio.
- **MARPOL 73/78** — six annexes covering oil, noxious liquids, packaged goods, sewage, garbage, and air pollution.
- **STCW 1978** — training and watchkeeping standards for seafarers.
- **Load Lines 1966** — freeboard and stability.
- **Tonnage 1969** — measurement of ships.
- **COLREG 1972** — rules of the road and navigation lights.
- **Ballast Water Management Convention 2004** — invasive species control.
- **MLC 2006** (with ILO) — seafarer working conditions.

**Step 3 — Role of Regional MoUs (2 marks):**

Regional MoUs such as the **Paris MoU (1982)**, **Tokyo MoU (1993)**, **Indian Ocean MoU (1998)**, **Vina del Mar (1992)**, **Caribbean MoU (1996)**, and **Black Sea MoU (2000)** harmonize PSC procedures within their regions. They:
- Set common inspection procedures and a unified detention philosophy.
- Operate a common database of ship and flag performance (THETIS / APICS).
- Publish annual lists of flag and RO performance (white, grey, black).
- Coordinate Concentrated Inspection Campaigns (CICs).

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — What is the NIR? (1 mark):**

The New Inspection Regime (NIR) of the Paris MoU came into force on **1 January 2011**. It replaced the old flat-rate inspection system and introduced a risk-based approach.

**Step 2 — Ship Risk Profile (2 marks):**

Every foreign ship entering the Paris MoU region is assigned a Ship Risk Profile (SRP) based on:
- Type of ship.
- Age of ship.
- Flag State performance (white / grey / black list).
- Recognized Organization (RO) performance.
- Company performance (under ISM audit).
- Deficiency / detention history.

**Step 3 — Risk Classification (2 marks):**

Ships are classified as:
- **High Risk Ship (HRS)** — inspection every 2 to 3 months.
- **Standard Risk Ship (SRS)** — inspection every 8 to 10 months.
- **Low Risk Ship (LRS)** — inspection every 5 to 6 months.

**Step 4 — Selection Logic (2 marks):**

The MoU database (THETIS-EU) automatically generates a ship selection scheme. The PSCO boards the ship, conducts the inspection, and records outcomes. Detentions and deficiencies feed back into the ship's risk profile, raising it for future calls if the ship performs poorly. The NIR also enforces **mandatory inspection of HRS** and **refusal of access** to repeatedly deficient ships (banned from Paris MoU ports after multiple detentions).

---

**Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** What are the responsibilities and powers of a Port State Control Officer (PSCO)? Describe the procedure followed during a routine PSC inspection. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the categories of deficiencies that lead to detention of a ship. What are the rights of a shipowner against a detention order? *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Who is a PSCO? (1 mark):**

A PSCO is an inspector appointed by the maritime authority of a port State, qualified to inspect foreign-flagged ships for compliance with international conventions and national regulations.

**Step 2 — Responsibilities and Powers (3 marks):**

- **Boarding** any foreign ship in the port voluntarily.
- **Verifying certificates and documents** (e.g., Safety Construction Certificate, Safety Equipment Certificate, Minimum Safe Manning Document, Oil Record Book, etc.).
- **Conducting physical inspections** of the hull, machinery, life-saving appliances, fire-fighting systems, and navigation equipment.
- **Operational testing** of equipment (e.g., emergency generator, fire pump, steering gear, lifeboat release mechanism).
- **Identifying deficiencies** and classifying them as rectifiable or detainable.
- **Issuing detention orders** if a ship is clearly substandard.
- **Reporting inspection outcomes** to the MoU database.
- **Refusing port entry** to a substandard ship that has been detained or refused access multiple times.

**Step 3 — Procedure of a Routine Inspection (3 marks):**

1. **Pre-arrival check** — review the ship's record in THETIS/APICS, identify risk profile.
2. **Boarding and introduction** — present credentials to the master.
3. **Document check** — certificates, logbooks, crew list, Oil Record Book, Garbage Management Plan, MLC documentation.
4. **Initial walk-around** — visual check of hull, deck, superstructure, mooring, pollution prevention equipment.
5. **Detailed inspection** — fire pumps, life-saving appliances, steering gear, emergency generator, navigation lights.
6. **Operational test** — PSCO may demand the crew demonstrate functioning of critical equipment.
7. **Findings and classification** — no deficiencies / minor deficiencies / detainable deficiencies.
8. **Issuance of inspection report** — to the master, port authority, and MoU database.
9. **Detention decision** — if any ground for detention exists, ship is held until rectified and re-inspected.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Categories of Detainable Deficiencies (4 marks):**

Per Paris MoU, the following are **clear grounds for detention**:

- Missing or invalid certificates.
- Crew unfamiliar with safety procedures, drills, or operation of equipment.
- Operating in waters exceeding the permitted maximum draft.
- Failure to comply with safe manning requirements.
- Inoperative fire-fighting equipment or life-saving appliances.
- Hull or structural damage significant to seaworthiness.
- Failure of steering gear, propulsion, or essential auxiliaries.
- Pollution prevention equipment not in working order (e.g., OWS inoperable).
- Failure of navigation lights, signals, or radio.
- BWM system failure.
- Crew working hours or conditions breaching MLC 2006.

**Step 2 — Rights of the Shipowner (2 marks):**

- **Right to be informed** of the deficiencies in writing, in a language the master understands.
- **Right of appeal** — most port State laws provide an administrative appeal mechanism to a higher maritime authority or tribunal.
- **Right to rectify** — the owner may arrange for repairs, class survey, or replacement of defective parts and request a re-inspection.
- **Right to compensation** — if detention is found to be wrongful, the shipowner may claim damages against the port State.

**Step 3 — Special Note on Repeat Offenders (1 mark):**

Under Paris MoU Article 16, a ship that has been detained or refused access **more than twice** in the preceding 36 months may be **banned from entering Paris MoU ports**. This is a powerful commercial deterrent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Flag State and Port State** — A common error is stating that PSC is conducted by the Flag State. The Flag State is the country of *registration*; the Port State is the country of *call*. This mistake alone costs up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Omitting the legal basis** — When asked to "explain PSC", many students describe what it is but forget to mention **UNCLOS Articles 94, 217, and 220**, and the relevant IMO conventions. Examiners specifically look for these citations.
> 3. **Wrong classification bands** — Students often write "black list if DI > 3" or confuse the boundaries. Memorize: **White < 3.0, Grey = 3.0–7.0, Black > 7.0**.
> 4. **Forgetting MLC 2006** — A 2006 convention, often omitted in answers. PSC now routinely checks seafarer contracts, working hours, and crew conditions.
> 5. **Confusing CIC with regular inspection** — Concentrated Inspection Campaigns are *thematic* (3-month focused) inspections, not routine ones. Mis-stating this loses marks on 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Skipping numerical units in formulas** — Always quote DI as a **percentage** and round-trip to two decimal places. Skipping the `%` sign in derivations is a common silent deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PSC** is the right of a port State to inspect **foreign** ships for compliance with international maritime conventions.
- **Legal foundation:** UNCLOS Articles 94, 217, 220; conventions include SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, MLC 2006, Load Lines, BWM, COLREG.
- **Regional MoUs:** Paris (Europe), Tokyo (Asia-Pacific), Indian Ocean, Vina del Mar (Latin America), Caribbean, Black Sea, Mediterranean, Abuja, Riyadh.
