- **New Inspection Regime (NIR):** Risk-based; HRS inspected every 2–3 months, SRS every 8–10 months, LRS every 5–6 months.
- **Flag list bands (Paris MoU):** White (< 3.0% DI), Grey (3.0–7.0%), Black (> 7.0%).
- **Excess Factor (EF)** significant when $\vert EF \vert > 1.0$.
- **Detention** is the formal holding of a ship; **clear grounds** include missing certificates, structural failure, inoperative fire/safety systems, pollution non-compliance.
- **Right of appeal** is available to the shipowner against wrongful detention.
- **PSCOs** board, inspect, test operationally, classify deficiencies, and decide on detention.
- **MLC 2006** is enforced alongside IMO conventions; covers seafarer working and living conditions.
- **Banned ships:** Ships detained or refused access 3+ times in 36 months may be banned from Paris MoU ports.
- **CIC** (Concentrated Inspection Campaign) — 3-month thematic focus on a single topic each year.
- **Differentiate clearly** between **Flag State Control** (own ships) and **Port State Control** (foreign ships in port).
- **Numericals:** Always show the formula, substitute values, show division steps, and quote the result in **percent** with **two decimal places**.
- **Kerala connection:** Indian Ocean MoU applies to Indian ports (Cochin, Vizhinjam); Tokyo MoU covers East Asia. Many Kerala-based cadets sit for PSC inspector exams after sea service.
- **Cybersecurity** is an emerging PSC concern (IMO Resolution MSC.428(98) — cyber risk management in SMS, enforced via PSC from 2021 onwards).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flag State Control
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flag State Control

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flag State Control (FSC)** is the sovereign regulatory authority and legal jurisdiction exercised by a country (the Flag State) over all ships that are registered and entitled to fly its national flag, regardless of where in the world those ships are operating. Under this authority, the Flag State Administration is responsible for ensuring that every vessel under its flag complies with international maritime conventions, national laws, and safety, security, and environmental protection standards throughout the vessel's operational life.

In the context of **Hull Inspection**, Flag State Control establishes the **primary legal basis** for conducting structural surveys, approving repair procedures, issuing statutory certificates, and maintaining the seaworthiness of the hull and its integral machinery systems. The **Flag State Administration** is the governmental body (often a Maritime Authority, Coast Guard, or Transport Ministry department) that discharges these duties — either directly or through **Delegated Bodies** and **Recognized Organizations (ROs)** such as classification societies (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas, ABS).

The legal foundation is anchored in the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982 — Article 94**, which obliges every Flag State to:
- Maintain a register of ships
- Assume jurisdiction over ships flying its flag
- Ensure safety at sea regarding construction, equipment, and seaworthiness
- Ensure compliance with generally accepted international regulations, procedures, and practices (mainly IMO conventions)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Driving License Authority" Model**

Think of Flag State Control as a country's **Vehicle Registration & Licensing Authority** (like the RTO in India or DVLA in the UK), but applied to ships instead of cars.

- **Flag of the Ship = Number Plate of the Car.** Just as every car displays a registration number tied to a state, every ship must fly the flag of the country where it is registered.
- **Flag State = The State that Issued the Registration.** When a car is registered in Kerala, the Kerala RTO is responsible for ensuring it meets road-safety standards (brakes, tires, structural integrity) — even if the car later drives to Delhi, Tamil Nadu, or abroad. Similarly, once a ship is registered under the flag of, say, Panama, Liberia, the Marshall Islands, or India, that country is legally accountable for the ship's safety, structural soundness, and compliance with all international rules — no matter which ocean the ship sails in.
- **Annual Inspection by RTO = Hull Survey by Classification Society on Behalf of Flag State.** Just as cars undergo periodic fitness tests, ships undergo **Annual, Intermediate, and Special Surveys** of their hulls, machinery, and safety equipment, with the results certified back to the Flag State.
- **Pollution Under Control Certificate = Statutory Certificate (e.g., IOPP, Air Pollution Certificate).**

The "intuition check": If a ship registered under Flag A causes a major oil spill in international waters, the international community will **first ask Flag State A** — *"Why did you not enforce the MARPOL regulations on your ship?"* Flag State Control is therefore the **first and most fundamental layer of maritime safety enforcement**.

## 1.3 Why Flag State Control is Central to Hull Inspection

For a marine engineer tasked with **Hull Inspection**, understanding Flag State Control is non-negotiable because:

- Every hull survey you conduct must ultimately satisfy the **statutory requirements** set by the Flag State.
- Repair specifications, steel renewal limits, wastage allowances, and NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) protocols are all governed by the **Flag State's rules** (often delegated to a Classification Society acting as a Recognized Organization).
- The **statutory certificates** that follow a successful hull survey (e.g., Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate, Load Line Certificate) are **issued in the name of the Flag State**, not the shipowner or the class society alone.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Flag State** | The country whose flag a ship is entitled to fly. |
| **Flag State Administration** | The governmental authority of the Flag State responsible for maritime affairs. |
| **Port State Control (PSC)** | Inspection of a foreign ship by the country in whose port it is berthed. |
| **Coastal State** | The country exercising sovereignty over territorial waters (up to 12 nm). |
| **Recognized Organization (RO)** | An organization authorized by the Flag State to perform surveys and certification on its behalf. |
| **Classification Society** | A non-governmental body (e.g., LR, DNV, BV) that sets technical rules and conducts surveys. |
| **Statutory Survey** | A survey required by international/national law, distinct from classification surveys. |
| **Maritime Authority** | The national body enforcing Flag State obligations. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per KTU 2024 OENST832 Module 1, students must clearly distinguish **Flag State Control from Port State Control**, identify Flag State obligations under **UNCLOS Article 94**, and understand the **delegation framework** through Recognized Organizations. This is a frequent 3-mark and 14-mark examination topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Maritime Control

Maritime safety enforcement operates on **three jurisdictional layers**. Understanding how they interrelate is the foundation of hull inspection governance.

### 2.1.1 Flag State Control (FSC) — The Primary Layer

The **Flag State** holds the **supreme and continuous jurisdiction** over its ships. Its responsibilities are **continuous** — they begin at the ship's design approval stage and continue throughout its operational life, including:

1. **Approval of design and construction drawings** of the hull.
2. **Initial survey** before the ship enters service.
3. **Periodic and intermediate surveys** of hull, machinery, and equipment.
4. **Renewal surveys** at mandated intervals (typically every 5 years).
5. **Issuance, endorsement, and renewal of statutory certificates**.
6. **Investigation of casualties** and serious marine incidents involving the ship.
7. **Detention and prosecution** of ships/vessels for non-compliance.

### 2.1.2 Port State Control (PSC) — The Backstop Layer

When a ship enters a foreign port, the **Port State** has the right to inspect the vessel to verify it complies with international conventions. PSC is **not** a replacement for FSC — it is a **secondary enforcement mechanism** that catches substandard ships that may have slipped through their Flag State's oversight. Famous PSC regimes include the **Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian Ocean MoU, and US Coast Guard exams**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** Flag State Control is **mandatory and continuous** by the country of registration. Port State Control is **discretionary and triggered by port call** by the host country.

### 2.1.3 Coastal State Control — The Geographic Layer

In **territorial waters (up to 12 nautical miles)**, the Coastal State can exercise sovereignty similar to land territory, including policing safety and pollution rules. Beyond territorial waters in the **EEZ (up to 200 nm)**, the Coastal State's jurisdiction is more limited and primarily concerns resource and pollution control.

## 2.2 Legal Framework — The Hierarchy of Instruments

Flag State obligations cascade from international law down to operational checklists:

1. **UNCLOS (1982)** — Establishes the sovereign right of Flag States and their duties (Article 94).
2. **IMO Conventions** (the technical rules) — SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, STCW, MLC, Tonnage.
3. **Flag State National Legislation** (e.g., the Merchant Shipping Act in India).
4. **Flag State Regulations & Rules** (administered by the Maritime Administration).
5. **Delegated Rules via Recognized Organizations** (Classification Society rules).
6. **Company Safety Management System (ISM Code)** — internal layer.

## 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Flag State Control Summary

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Primary Legal Source** | UNCLOS 1982, Article 94 |
| **Core Conventions Imposed** | SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines (LL), STCW, MLC 2006, COLREGS, Tonnage 1969 |
| **Flag State Authority Body** | Maritime Administration / Coast Guard / Transport Ministry Department |
| **Delegated Bodies** | Classification Societies acting as Recognized Organizations (ROs) |
| **Major Classification Societies** | Lloyd's Register (LR), DNV, Bureau Veritas (BV), American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), ClassNK, Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) |
| **Statutory Surveys (SOLAS Ch. I)** | Initial, Annual, Intermediate, Renewal, Additional |
| **Hull Survey Cycle** | Annual → Intermediate (within 3 months of 2nd or 3rd Anniversary) → Special / Renewal (every 5 years) |
| **Key Statutory Certificates** | Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate, Safety Equipment Certificate, Load Line Certificate, IOPP Certificate, Minimum Safe Manning Document |
| **Flag State Powers** | Detain, prosecute, cancel registration, refuse port entry, delist from registry |
| **Open vs. Closed Registry** | Open (Panama, Liberia) vs. Closed (USA, India) — open registries have weaker FSC traditionally, triggering stronger PSC response |
| **FSC vs PSC** | FSC = own-flag ships, always. PSC = foreign-flag ships in your port. |
| **ISM Code Link** | Flag State ensures shipowner company has DOC, vessel has SMC |

## 2.4 Delegation to Recognized Organizations (ROs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> No single Flag State has the physical resources to survey every ship under its flag. Therefore, most Flag States **delegate** statutory survey and certification work to **Recognized Organizations (ROs)**, which are almost always **Classification Societies**.

The IMO **Resolution A.739(18)** and the **RO Code (Resolution MEPC.237(65) and MSC.349(92))** govern how this delegation works. Key requirements include:

- The RO must be **independent and impartial**.
- A formal **written agreement** must exist between the Flag State and the RO.
- The RO must operate a **quality system** certified to ISO 9001 or equivalent.
- The Flag State retains the **ultimate responsibility** — it cannot outsource accountability.

## 2.5 Survey Cycle in Practice (Hull Focus)

For a typical bulk carrier or oil tanker flying, say, the Indian flag, the Flag State (Directorate General of Shipping, India) — through the Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) as its RO — mandates the following hull survey cycle:

```
1. Keel Laying → Design Approval → Initial Survey (before commissioning)
2. Year 1 → Annual Survey (in-water or dry-dock)
3. Year 2 or 3 → Intermediate Survey (more extensive)
4. Year 5 → Special Survey (dry-dock, full hull NDT, wastage gauging)
5. Cycle repeats every 5 years
```

Every survey concludes with a **Report of Survey** submitted to the Flag State, and if satisfactory, the relevant **statutory certificate** is issued or endorsed.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- A marine engineer performing **hull thickness gauging** during a Special Survey is executing a **Flag State-mandated statutory task** under SOLAS Chapter II-1 and the IACS Unified Requirements.
- The **wastage allowances** applied (typically 75% of original scantlings for renewed plates) come from Classification Society rules authorized by the Flag State.
- **NDT procedures** (UT, MPI, DPI) on hull welds follow the Flag State's approved survey procedures manual.
- In a **casualty investigation** (e.g., hull fracture), the Flag State has both the right and duty to lead the technical inquiry.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

Because Flag State Control is a **regulatory and procedural** topic rather than a calculus- or physics-heavy one, the "derivation" here takes the form of a **structured logical derivation** of how a ship moves from concept to certified operating vessel under Flag State oversight. This mirrors how examiners expect you to articulate processes in 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 Logical Derivation — The Flag State Certification Workflow

**Step 1: Ship Design and Plan Approval**
- The shipyard (or design house) submits **lines plan, general arrangement, midship section, structural drawings, and stability booklet** to the Classification Society acting as the RO.
- The RO reviews the design against its rules and the **Flag State's regulatory requirements** (which often incorporate SOLAS, Load Lines, MARPOL).
- An **Approval of Plans Certificate** is issued, with conditions.

> *Engineering Logic:* This is the **first statutory gate**. No construction begins without design approval because corrections are cheapest at the drawing stage.

**Step 2: Keel Laying and Construction Survey**
- Surveyors attend the shipyard to witness key milestones: **keel laying, hull block erection, installation of major machinery, launching, and outfitting**.
- Survey is **continuous during construction** — the surveyor is essentially a Flag State representative on-site.

**Step 3: Initial Survey (Pre-Commissioning)**
- A comprehensive inspection of the completed ship, including:
  - Full hull inspection (in dry dock or on slipway).
  - Machinery trials, steering gear tests, lifeboat drills.
  - Verification of SOLAS equipment (firefighting, life-saving, navigation).
  - Load Line marking verification.
  - MARPOL equipment (oil filtering, OWS, sewage, garbage).
- Successful completion triggers issuance of the **statutory certificates**, valid for a defined period (typically 5 years for major certificates).

**Step 4: Continuous Compliance — Surveys During Service**

$$
T_{survey} = \{ \text{Annual}_{y=1,2,3,4}, \text{Intermediate}_{y \in \{2,3\}}, \text{Renewal}_{y=5} \}
$$

where $y$ is the year in the certification cycle. Each survey has a **scope envelope** defined by the Flag State rules:

| Survey Type | Typical Scope (Hull Focus) | Window from Anniversary Date |
|---|---|---|
| Annual | Visual hull above waterline, ballast tank spot checks | Within 3 months before or after anniversary |
| Intermediate | Internal examination of representative spaces, more tanks | Within 3 months of 2nd or 3rd anniversary |
| Renewal (Special) | Full hull in dry dock, complete NDT, thickness gauging | Within 5-year window |

**Step 5: Issuance and Endorsement of Certificates**
- The Flag State (or its RO) issues statutory certificates that are **internationally recognized**.
- For short voyages and minor port calls, certificates may be **endorsed** rather than reissued.
- A ship's **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)** maintained by the Flag State tracks its certification history.

**Step 6: Casualty, Detention, and Sanction Pathway**
- If a major hull defect, casualty, or PSC detention occurs, the **Flag State is informed immediately** under IMO reporting obligations (e.g., SOLAS Ch. I, Reg. 21).
- The Flag State may order the ship to a repair yard, conduct its own investigation, and ultimately **suspend or revoke certificates** if compliance is not restored.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Flag State vs. Port State vs. Classification Society

| Parameter | Flag State (Administration) | Port State (Coastal) | Classification Society (RO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jurisdiction Trigger | Ship is registered under the flag | Ship enters a foreign port | Contractual agreement with shipowner / delegation from Flag State |
| Authority Type | Sovereign, governmental | Sovereign, governmental | Non-governmental technical body |
| Scope | Own-flag ships, anywhere in the world | Any foreign ship calling at its port | Ships classed with it, regardless of flag |
| Main Function | Legislative, regulatory, certification, casualty investigation | Verification of foreign ship compliance | Technical rules, classification, design approval, surveys |
| Sanction Power | Detain own ship, prosecute, deregister | Detain foreign ship until deficiencies rectified, deny sailing | Withdraw class, suspend trading |
| Example | Panama Maritime Authority, DG Shipping (India) | USCG, Paris MoU inspectors | DNV, LR, BV, IRS |
| In Hull Inspection | Mandates rules, audits RO, certifies hull soundness | Verifies hull condition during port call, may call for dry-dock | Performs thickness gauging, NDT, wastage assessment |

## 3.3 Decision Logic — Who Inspects What?

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three-Layer Inspection Logic**

When a ship is operating, three different bodies may inspect the hull:

1. **Flag State (via RO) — Routine:** Conducted at the Flag State's behest on the schedule above.
2. **Classification Society — Continuous:** A class survey is *also* performed separately, because classification is a commercial contract between the owner and the society. Many surveys are **dual-purpose** — i.e., one visit satisfies both statutory and class requirements.
3. **Port State Control — Random/Targeted:** Triggered by the port state's targeting system (e.g., Ship Risk Profile in Paris MoU, targeting factor based on age, flag, class, history).

The decision matrix is:

$$
\text{Inspection Required} = f(\text{Time since last survey}, \text{Ship risk profile}, \text{Port call event}, \text{Casualty event})
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example — The Statutory Hull Survey Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Hypothetical:** A bulk carrier of age 12 years flying the Indian flag is due for survey in 2025. Compute the survey schedule and identify the certificates that need renewal.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Identify the last Special Survey (Renewal) date → Assume it was completed on 15 March 2020, with the Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate (CSSCC) valid for 5 years until 14 March 2025.

**Step 2:** Lay out the survey calendar:

| Anniversary | Survey Due | Window | Activity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 15 Mar 2021 | Annual Survey #1 | ± 3 months | Visual hull, ballast tanks spot check |
| 15 Mar 2022 | Annual Survey #2 | ± 3 months | Visual hull, sea valves |
| 15 Mar 2023 | **Intermediate Survey** | ± 3 months of anniversary | Internal examination of multiple ballast/cargo tanks |
| 15 Mar 2024 | Annual Survey #4 | ± 3 months | Visual hull |
| 15 Mar 2025 | **Renewal Survey (Special)** | ± 3 months (5-year window) | Full dry-dock, hull NDT, thickness gauging, CSSCC renewal |

**Step 3:** Identify certificates to renew at the 2025 Renewal Survey:
- Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate (CSSCC)
- Load Line Certificate
- International Oil Pollution Prevention (IOPP) Certificate
- International Air Pollution Prevention (IAPP) Certificate (if applicable)

**Step 4:** Submit reports to the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS)** via the **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** as the delegated RO.

**Step 5:** Upon satisfactory completion, the Flag State issues a **new CSSCC valid until 14 March 2030**, restarting the 5-year cycle.

This entire process is a textbook example of **Flag State Control in action** for hull inspection.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Flag State Control. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

Flag State Control refers to the **sovereign regulatory authority** exercised by a country over all merchant ships that are registered and entitled to fly its national flag, irrespective of the ship's location. The Flag State, through its **Maritime Administration**, is responsible for ensuring that every ship under its flag complies with applicable **international conventions** (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, STCW) and **national maritime laws** throughout the vessel's operational life. This includes approving ship designs, conducting surveys, issuing statutory certificates, investigating casualties, and prosecuting violations. The legal basis is enshrined in **UNCLOS 1982, Article 94**, which obliges Flag States to maintain effective jurisdiction and control over their vessels in administrative, technical, and social matters.

**[3 marks allocation: Definition 1m, Key responsibilities 1m, UNCLOS Article 94 reference 1m]**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Flag State Control and Port State Control. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flag State Control (FSC) | Port State Control (PSC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Authority** | The country where the ship is registered | The country in whose port the ship is berthed |
| **Scope** | Applies to ships flying its own flag | Applies to foreign-flag ships in its ports |
| **Nature** | Continuous, mandatory, primary | Discretionary, triggered, secondary |
| **Purpose** | Ensure ongoing compliance of own fleet | Verify that visiting foreign ships meet international standards |
| **Sanctions** | Detain own ship, prosecute, deregister | Detain foreign ship, deny sailing until defects rectified |
| **Examples** | Panama Maritime Authority inspecting a Panama-flagged ship anywhere | USCG inspecting a Liberia-flagged ship in New York |

In essence, **FSC is the primary and continuous layer of safety enforcement**, whereas **PSC is a backstop** that catches substandard ships which may have evaded proper oversight by their Flag State.

**[3 marks allocation: 3 clear differentiating points or a small table]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Choice Pattern:** The 14-mark question offers an internal choice. **Attempt either Option A or Option B.** Each option carries sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

### Question A: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) Explain in detail the legal obligations of a Flag State under UNCLOS Article 94, and discuss the role of the Flag State Administration in implementing these obligations for hull inspection. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Legal Foundation:**

Article 94 of the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982** is the **cornerstone of Flag State obligations**. It imposes ten core duties on every Flag State, which can be grouped into four clusters:

1. **Registration and Flagging Duties (Art. 94(1)–(2)):**
   - Maintain a register of ships flying its flag.
   - Assume jurisdiction over such ships on the high seas.
   - The flag must be genuinely linked to the State (the "Genuine Link" doctrine).

2. **Safety of Construction, Equipment, and Seaworthiness (Art. 94(3)–(5)):**
   - Each ship must be in charge of a properly qualified master and crew.
   - The crew must be trained in accordance with applicable international regulations (**STCW Convention**).
   - The ship must be **properly constructed, equipped, and seaworthy** for its intended service.

3. **Compliance with International Conventions (Art. 94(5)):**
   - Each ship must conform to **generally accepted international regulations, procedures, and practices** — these are the IMO conventions: SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, COLREGS, STCW, MLC, and Tonnage.

4. **Survey, Inspection, and Certification (Implicit & via SOLAS Reg. 6/7):**
   - The Flag State must ensure that ships are surveyed by qualified surveyors.
   - Appropriate charts, nautical publications, and safety equipment must be carried.

**Step 2 — The Role of the Flag State Administration in Hull Inspection:**

The **Flag State Administration** (e.g., the **Directorate General of Shipping** in India, the **Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore**, the **Panama Maritime Authority**) translates the legal duties above into operational reality. In the context of **Hull Inspection**, this involves:

1. **Design and Plan Approval** — Reviewing and approving structural drawings, scantlings, and material specifications before construction.
2. **Construction Survey** — Stationing surveyors at the shipyard to verify workmanship, welding quality, and material traceability.
3. **Initial Survey** — Conducting a complete inspection of the completed hull and machinery before the ship enters service.
4. **Periodic Surveys** — Scheduling and conducting Annual, Intermediate, and Special (Renewal) Surveys at the prescribed intervals, including dry-dock inspections.
5. **Issuance of Statutory Certificates** — Issuing certificates such as the Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate, Load Line Certificate, and IOPP Certificate, all of which are **flag state-issued and internationally recognized**.
6. **Continuous Monitoring** — Maintaining the ship's Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR) and ensuring that any modifications or repairs receive proper approval.
7. **Casualty Investigation** — Leading inquiries into hull failures, structural collapses, or major incidents.
8. **Audit of Recognized Organizations** — Periodically auditing the ROs that act on its behalf.

**Step 3 — Engineering Significance:**

For a marine engineer conducting hull inspection, the **Flag State Administration is the ultimate authority** that validates the seaworthiness certification. Every thickness measurement, every NDT result, every repair specification must ultimately satisfy Flag State requirements. The Flag State does not merely "set the rules" — it **enforces compliance through its certification and inspection regime**, ensuring that the ship can legally trade internationally.

**Conclusion:** UNCLOS Article 94 lays down the *what*, while the Flag State Administration operationalizes the *how*. Together, they form the **legal-engineering backbone** of international maritime safety and hull integrity assurance.

---

#### (b) Describe the statutory survey cycle for a merchant ship under SOLAS, with particular reference to hull surveys. Explain the role of Recognized Organizations (ROs) in conducting these surveys on behalf of the Flag State. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Statutory Survey Cycle (SOLAS Chapter I, Regulations 6–11):**

Under the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)**, every merchant ship is subject to a structured regime of statutory surveys throughout its operational life. The hull-focused survey cycle consists of:

1. **Initial Survey:**
   - Conducted **before the ship is put into service**.
   - Includes a complete inspection of the structure, machinery, and equipment to ensure full compliance with SOLAS requirements.
   - On satisfactory completion, the relevant statutory certificates are issued.

2. **Annual Survey:**
   - Conducted **within 3 months before or after each anniversary date** of the certificate.
   - Generally a visual inspection of accessible parts of the hull, sea valves, hull markings, and a spot check of ballast tanks.
   - Verifies that no unauthorized modifications have been made and that the hull remains in satisfactory condition.

3. **Intermediate Survey:**
   - Conducted **within 3 months of either the second or third anniversary date** of the certificate (at the Flag State's option).
   - More extensive than the annual survey, including **internal examination of representative ballast tanks, cargo holds, and structural members**.
   - Ensures that progressive wastage or corrosion has not compromised hull integrity.

4. **Renewal Survey (Special Survey):**
   - Conducted **every 5 years** to renew the statutory certificate.
   - The most comprehensive survey, typically requiring the ship to be **dry-docked**.
   - Includes complete **non-destructive testing (NDT)**, **ultrasonic thickness gauging**, inspection of underwater parts, sea cocks, propellers, rudder, and cathodic protection systems.
   - A full assessment of structural fitness and a **wastage allowance calculation** is performed; renewals are required if wastage exceeds permissible limits.

5. **Additional Survey (Occasional):**
   - Triggered by specific events: casualties, grounding, major repairs, or modifications.
   - May be required by the Flag State following a PSC detention or following a casualty investigation.

**Step 2 — The Role of Recognized Organizations (ROs):**

Because most Flag States — even major maritime nations — do not have the surveyor capacity to personally inspect every ship, the day-to-day survey work is **delegated to Recognized Organizations**, which are typically the **Classification Societies**.

**Definition of RO (per IMO Resolution A.739(18) and the RO Code):**
A Recognized Organization is an organization that has been **formally authorized in writing** by a Flag State Administration to perform statutory surveys and certification on its behalf.

**Functions Performed by ROs:**

1. **Design Review and Plan Approval** — Examining structural drawings against Flag State regulations and class rules.
2. **Construction Surveys** — Attending shipyards during build to verify compliance.
3. **Periodic Surveys** — Conducting Annual, Intermediate, and Special Surveys on behalf of the Flag State.
4. **Issuance of Certificates** — The RO may issue certificates **in the name of the Flag State**, often using pre-printed Flag State stationery.
5. **Wastage and Repair Assessment** — Making engineering judgments on renewal vs. repair during hull thickness gauging.
6. **Reporting to the Flag State** — Submitting detailed survey reports, which the Flag State reviews and audits periodically.
7. **Maintaining the Class Register** — Keeping records of every class survey, modification, and recommendation.

**Examples of Major ROs:**
Lloyd's Register (LR), DNV, Bureau Veritas (BV), American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), ClassNK (Japan), Indian Register of Shipping (IRS), Korean Register (KR), RINA (Italy).

**Step 3 — Conditions and Limits of RO Delegation:**

- The Flag State retains **ultimate responsibility** — it cannot delegate accountability.
- The RO must be **independent, impartial, and technically competent**.
- The RO Code (Resolutions MEPC.237(65) and MSC.349(92)) imposes **transparency and quality obligations**.
- The Flag State conducts **periodic audits** of the RO's performance.
- The RO's authority can be **withdrawn** if it fails to meet the Flag State's standards.

**Conclusion:** The statutory survey cycle, when combined with the delegation framework to Recognized Organizations, ensures that hull integrity is verified at every stage of a ship's life — from keel laying to recycling — under the ultimate authority and accountability of the Flag State.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Option) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) Discuss the relationship between the Flag State, Port State, and Classification Society in ensuring hull integrity. Use a suitable diagram in your explanation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Three-Way Relationship Overview:**

The safety and structural integrity of a merchant ship's hull is enforced through a **tripartite regulatory architecture** comprising the Flag State, the Port State, and the Classification Society. Although each has a distinct role, they are **complementary and interlocking** — no single body can ensure hull integrity in isolation.

**Step 2 — Role of Each Body:**

**A. Flag State (Continuous Authority):**
- Exercises **supreme and continuous jurisdiction** over ships flying its flag.
- Sets the legal framework, including national maritime laws and adoption of IMO conventions.
- Delegates day-to-day survey work to ROs (Classification Societies) but **retains ultimate accountability**.
- Issues statutory certificates that are recognized internationally.
- Investigates casualties and detains substandard ships.

**B. Classification Society / RO (Technical Operator):**
- Develops and maintains **technical rules** for hull design, construction, and survey.
- Performs surveys on the **Flag State's behalf** (statutory) and on its **own contractual basis** (classification).
- Issues class certificates and statutory certificates.
- Provides engineering judgment on **repair vs. renewal** based on thickness gauging and NDT results.
- Many surveys are **dual-purpose**, satisfying both class and statutory requirements simultaneously.

**C. Port State (Backstop Enforcement):**
- Inspects **foreign-flag ships** calling at its ports.
- Acts as a **safety net** that catches substandard ships that may have evaded proper Flag State or class oversight.
- Has the power to **detain** a ship, deny sailing, or require specific repairs before departure.
- Operates under MoU frameworks (Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian Ocean MoU, etc.).
- Uses a **targeting system** (e.g., Ship Risk Profile) to decide which ships to inspect.

**Step 3 — The Interlocking Workflow (Block Diagram in Text Form):**

```
[FLAG STATE: Sets rules, issues certificates, retains accountability]
           |
           | delegates via formal agreement
           v
[CLASSIFICATION SOCIETY / RO: Performs surveys, gauges hull, issues technical reports]
           |
           | statutory + class surveys
           v
[THE SHIP: Hull integrity verified continuously]
           ^
           |
           | verification during port call
[PORT STATE: Random or targeted inspection, detention power if needed]
```

**Step 4 — How They Interact in Practice:**

1. The Flag State writes the **rules of the game** and selects an RO to enforce them.
2. The RO performs the **surveys** (initial, annual, intermediate, special) on the ship, often using its own class rules that are stricter than the statutory minimum.
3. The ship operates, undergoes a class-led dry-dock Special Survey every 5 years, and is recertified.
4. When the ship enters a foreign port, the **Port State may inspect it**, focusing on hull condition, documentation, and safety equipment.
5. If the Port State finds deficiencies, it may detain the ship and **inform the Flag State**, which is then obliged to follow up.

**Step 5 — Engineering Significance:**

- **Without Flag State Control:** No sovereign authority ensures baseline compliance.
- **Without Classification Societies:** No technical expertise to translate rules into practical surveys.
- **Without Port State Control:** Substandard ships can escape detection indefinitely.

The three together form a **layered defense system** that, in theory, leaves no gap through which an unsafe ship can trade legally. Hull integrity is thus enforced continuously, globally, and with overlapping scrutiny.

**Conclusion:** The Flag State, Port State, and Classification Society form a **triad of complementary enforcement bodies**. The Flag State is the legislative and accountability anchor, the Classification Society is the technical executor, and the Port State is the external verifier. For a marine engineer, recognizing which body is inspecting the hull — and under what authority — is essential to understanding the legal and engineering obligations that apply.

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of Open and Closed Registries. Discuss their impact on Flag State Control effectiveness and the implications for hull inspection standards. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Registries:**

A **Ship Registry** (also called a Flag State Register) is the official record of ships entitled to fly a particular country's flag. Registries fall into two broad categories:

1. **Open Registry (Flag of Convenience):**
   - A country allows **foreign-owned ships** to register under its flag with **minimal conditions** — typically the payment of a fee and a small tonnage tax.
   - The ship's beneficial ownership may be in a completely different country (e.g., a Greek-owned ship registered in Panama).
   - Examples: **Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Bahamas, Malta, Cyprus, Antigua and Barbuda, Comoros, Togo**.

2. **Closed Registry (Traditional / National Registry):**
   - Ships may only be registered if they are **owned, manned, and operated by nationals or residents** of the Flag State.
   - The "genuine link" between the ship and the country is enforced strictly.
   - Examples: **United States, India, United Kingdom (historically), Germany, Norway, Japan, China, Brazil, Russia**.

**Step 2 — Impact on Flag State Control Effectiveness:**

**Open Registries — Strengths and Weaknesses:**

*Weaknesses (the traditional criticism):*
- The Flag State often has **limited physical infrastructure** — few surveyors, no domestic shipbuilding, weak maritime administration.
- **Delegation to ROs is extensive**, and the Flag State has **less direct oversight** of survey quality.
- Historically associated with **substandard ships**, poor manning conditions, and lower enforcement of environmental rules.
- Crew may be from low-wage countries with weak training infrastructure, sometimes with inadequate STCW compliance.

*Recent Improvements:*
- Major open registries (Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands) have modernized significantly.
- They now maintain large maritime administrations, conduct RO audits, and align with IMO instruments.
- Many are **white-listed on the Paris MoU and Tokyo MoU performance tables**.

**Closed Registries — Strengths and Weaknesses:**

*Strengths:*
- **Tighter regulatory control** — national ownership creates accountability and reputational interest.
- Direct surveyor presence in domestic shipyards.
- Stronger enforcement of safety, environmental, and labor standards.
- Better integration with national maritime policy.

*Weaknesses:*
- Ships may **re-flag to open registries** if domestic taxes, manning costs, or regulations are perceived as burdensome (the "flagging out" phenomenon).
- May lead to a **loss of national fleet** and associated economic and strategic benefits.

**Step 3 — Implications for Hull Inspection Standards:**

| Aspect | Open Registry | Closed Registry |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Surveyor Presence | Often absent; relies heavily on RO | Strong; direct Flag State surveyors common |
| RO Audit Frequency | Variable; improving in major open registries | Regular and rigorous |
| Dry-Dock Compliance | Sometimes delayed; PSC detention rates higher historically | Generally excellent |
| Wastage Assessment | Relies on RO's engineering judgment | Often involves Flag State review |
| Casualty Investigation | May be slower or more distant | Usually prompt and thorough |
| PSC Detention Likelihood | Historically higher (improving) | Generally lower |
| Hull Steel Renewal Standards | Aligned with IACS; may vary with RO | Aligned with IACS + national rules |

**Step 4 — The IACS Solution:**

To harmonize quality across registries, the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** was formed. IACS members must comply with **Unified Requirements (UR)** that go beyond IMO minimums. This means that a hull surveyed by an IACS member (e.g., DNV, LR) typically meets consistent standards regardless of whether the ship flies an open or closed flag.

**Step 5 — Practical Implications for the Hull Inspector:**

- A marine engineer inspecting a ship flying a **major open-registry flag** must recognize that the actual survey work is being done by a **RO acting under delegation**, and the Flag State itself may have **limited direct oversight**.
- A ship flying a **closed-registry flag** typically has a **stronger chain of accountability** from designer → builder → operator → surveyor → Flag State.
- In both cases, the **engineering quality of the hull inspection** depends more on the **competence of the RO and the surveyors** than on the Flag State label itself.

**Conclusion:** Open and closed registries represent two different models of Flag State Control — one driven by commercial openness, the other by national sovereignty. While modern open registries have largely closed the historical quality gap, closed registries retain inherent advantages in direct enforcement. For hull inspection, the practical lesson is that the **flag on the stern is only one indicator**; the **class society, the RO's track record, and the PSC performance history** are equally important signals of hull integrity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Flag State Control Answers (Read Before Writing Your Exam):**
>
> 1. **Conflating Flag State and Port State Control.** This is the #1 mistake. Many students describe PSC functions when asked about FSC. Always clarify: **FSC is over your own-flag ships, continuous; PSC is over foreign ships, port-triggered.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting UNCLOS Article 94.** Examiners specifically look for this reference. Omitting it costs easy marks.
>
> 3. **Treating Classification Society and Flag State as the Same Body.** They are not. The Class Society is a **non-governmental technical body** acting under delegation; the Flag State is the **sovereign authority** that retains ultimate responsibility. Many students blur this distinction.
>
> 4. **Missing the Survey Schedule.** For 14-mark questions, examiners expect the full cycle — Annual → Intermediate → Special/Renewal — with windows. A vague "ships are inspected regularly" loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the RO Code and IMO Resolution A.739(18).** Naming the framework that governs RO delegation is a high-value KTU expected point.
>
> 6. **Writing essays without structure.** Use headings, sub-points, and tables. KTU 2024 scheme rewards **organised answers** with clear CO (Course Outcome) mapping.
>
> 7. **Omitting examples.** Name a few Flag States (India, Panama, Liberia), ROs (DNV, LR, BV, IRS), and conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines). Examples double your marks-per-word ratio.
>
> 8. **Not concluding the answer.** Always close with a 1–2 line conclusion summarizing the topic. Examiners allocate marks for a coherent ending.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Flag State Control (Module 1):**

- ☐ **Flag State Control = Sovereign jurisdiction over own-flag ships, continuous, mandatory.**
- ☐ **Legal Basis: UNCLOS 1982, Article 94** (the "Genuine Link" doctrine).
- ☐ **Flag State vs Port State:** FSC = own ships, anywhere. PSC = foreign ships, in your port.
- ☐ **Flag State vs Coastal State:** Flag = nationality link. Coastal = geographic link (territorial waters).
- ☐ **Key Flag State Body = Maritime Administration** (e.g., DGS India, Panama Maritime Authority).
- ☐ **Day-to-day surveys are DELEGATED to Recognized Organizations (ROs)** — typically Classification Societies.
- ☐ **Delegation framework: IMO Resolution A.739(18) + RO Code (MEPC.237(65), MSC.349(92)).**
- ☐ **Flag State retains ULTIMATE responsibility** — accountability cannot be outsourced.
- ☐ **Major Classification Societies (ROs):** LR, DNV, BV, ABS, ClassNK, IRS, KR, RINA.
- ☐ **Statutory Survey Cycle: Initial → Annual → Intermediate → Special (Renewal every 5 years).**
- ☐ **Key Statutory Certificates:** CSSCC, Safety Equipment Cert., Load Line Cert., IOPP, IAPP, ISPP, MLC.
- ☐ **Open Registry (Flag of Convenience) =** Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Bahamas, Malta.
- ☐ **Closed Registry =** USA, India, UK (historically), Germany, Norway, Japan, China.
- ☐ **IACS = International Association of Classification Societies** — sets Unified Requirements.
- ☐ **Major Conventions imposed by Flag States:** SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, STCW, MLC, COLREGS, Tonnage.
- ☐ **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR) =** maintained by Flag State, tracks ship's certification history.
- ☐ **For hull inspection specifically: Flag State mandates structural surveys, approves repair specs, certifies seaworthiness, and investigates casualties.**
- ☐ **Detention Power: Flag State can detain its own ship; Port State can detain foreign ships in its port.**
- ☐ **Casualty Reporting: Flag State must be informed of major incidents (SOLAS Reg. 21).**
- ☐ **Connection to ISM Code: Flag State ensures the operating company holds a DOC and the ship holds an SMC.**

---

**End of Module 1 — Flag State Control Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme | OENST832 — Hull Inspection)**

topics.title: Various Types of Marine Surveys - Initial Survey
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Various Types of Marine Surveys - Initial Survey ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Marine Survey A **Marine Survey** is a systematic, methodical, and documented technical inspection of a vessel, its machinery, equipment, structural integrity, and compliance with statutory and classification requirements, conducted by qualified surveyors acting on behalf of a **Flag State Administration**, a **Classification Society**, or a **Recognized Organization (RO)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim from OENST832):** > A marine survey is a critical examination of a ship to ascertain its structural condition, operational fitness, and conformity with international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, COLREGS) and Classification Society rules, prior to issuance, continuation, or renewal of statutory certificates. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Health Checkup" Model Imagine a ship as a **human body**: - A **marine survey** is analogous to a **comprehensive medical check-up** conducted at different life stages. - The **Initial Survey** is the **first full-body examination** before a person is issued a "fitness certificate" to start a job (or a voyage). - The **Periodic Survey** is the **annual health check-up**. - The **Renewal Survey** is the **major 5-yearly health overhaul**. Just as a doctor checks the heart (engine), bones (hull), lungs (ventilation), and blood (fuel/oil), a marine surveyor inspects the hull, machinery, electrical systems, lifesaving appliances, and pollution prevention equipment. ### 1.3 Key Authorities and Regulatory Bodies | Authority | Full Form | Role | |---|---|---| | **IMO** | International Maritime Organization | Sets global maritime safety/pollution standards | | **SOLAS** | Safety of Life at Sea | Convention on ship construction & equipment | | **MARPOL** | Marine Pollution (73/78) | Convention on prevention of pollution | | **IACS** | International Association of Classification Societies | Umbrella body for classification societies | | **Class** | Classification Society (e.g., LR, DNV, ABS, BV) | Establishes technical rules for ship design/construction | | **Flag State** | Country of registration | Legally responsible for enforcing conventions | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statutory Surveys** are mandatory under IMO conventions and are enforced by the **Flag State**. > **Classification Surveys** are voluntary in the legal sense but commercially indispensable because they enable insurance cover, chartering, and port-of-call acceptance. ### 1.4 Categories of Marine Surveys — Master Classification Marine surveys are broadly classified along **two axes**: **Axis A — By Purpose (Statutory vs. Classification)** 1. Statutory Surveys (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, MLC) 2. Classification Surveys (Hull & Machinery rules) **Axis B — By Lifecycle Stage** 1. Initial Survey 2. Annual Survey 3. Intermediate / Periodic Survey 4. Renewal / Special Survey 5. Occasional / Additional Survey 6. Continuous Survey (alternative to special survey) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus (OENST832 — Hull Inspection, Module 1) requires the student to identify **each survey type**, state its **trigger event**, **scope**, and **issuing authority**, and explain its **relationship to certificate validity**. ]<]minimax[>[ ]<]minimax[>[ ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Master Matrix — Types of Marine Surveys | # | Survey Type | Trigger Event | Scope (Brief) | Issuing Authority | Certificate Type | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Initial Survey** | Before ship enters service / new build delivery | Full verification of compliance for first certificate issuance | Flag State (via RO) | All statutory certs (first-time) | | 2 | **Annual Survey** | Every 12 months from issuance | General visual check, confirm continued compliance | Flag State / RO | Short-term validation | | 3 | **Intermediate / Periodical Survey** | Between 2nd and 3rd anniversaries (or as per convention) | Detailed selected items inspection | Flag State / RO | Mid-term validation | | 4 | **Renewal Survey** | End of certificate validity period (typically 5 years) | Comprehensive inspection, equal to Initial Survey | Flag State / RO | Renewal of certs | | 5 | **Special Survey** | Every 5 years (Class rules) | Docking, thickness gauging, internal inspection of hull | Classification Society | Class endorsement | | 6 | **Occasional Survey** | Damage, change of use, lay-up/reactivation | Specific to event | Flag State / RO / Class | Supplementary | | 7 | **Continuous Survey** | Opted-in alternative to Special Survey | Year-round progressive survey in lieu of 5-yearly dry-dock | Class | Class endorsement | | 8 | **Lay-up / Reactivation Survey** | Ship going out of service / re-entering | Condition assessment pre/post lay-up | Class | Conditional | | 9 | **Pre-purchase / Condition Survey** | Buyer request before acquisition | Independent assessment of value and condition | Independent surveyor | Commercial report | | 10 | **Damage Survey** | After grounding, collision, fire, etc. | Extent of damage and repair recommendations | Class / Insurer | Repair scope | ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Survey Type — Hierarchical Logic The KTU examiner will award full marks **only** if the student explains **why** each survey exists, not merely **what** it is. The logic is: - **Why Initial Survey?** → A ship cannot legally trade without proof of compliance at the moment it is born (new build) or changes flag/use. The Initial Survey is the **gatekeeper** that opens the door to all statutory certificates. - **Why Annual Survey?** → Things deteriorate with time. A yearly check confirms nothing has broken or been removed since the last full check. - **Why Renewal Survey?** → Five years is the maximum credible interval over which hidden defects (corrosion, fatigue cracks) can develop unnoticed without docking. - **Why Special Survey (Class)?** → Classification Societies must independently verify the ship meets their *technical* rules (not just statutory minimums) at 5-year intervals to keep the Class symbol valid. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This survey matrix forms the foundation for shipyard planning, dry-dock scheduling, certificate management software, and Port State Control (PSC) readiness. Every ship's annual operating budget includes a "Survey & Dry-dock Reserve" line item. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Marine Surveys are primarily a **regulatory and procedural** discipline, not a numerate one. However, a few quantitative rules are examinable. $$ T_{\text{cert}} = T_{\text{issue}} + 5\,\text{years} \quad \text{(Statutory certs, e.g., Safety Construction, Load Line)} $$ $$ T_{\text{annual}} = T_{\text{prev}} + 12\,\text{months} \pm 3\,\text{months window} $$ $$ \text{Class renewal interval} = 5\,\text{years}, \quad \text{with intermediate survey at } 2.5\,\text{years (}\pm \text{window)} $$ **Docking interval rules (typical IACS unified requirement):** $$ T_{\text{dock, max}} \le 2.5\,\text{years for ESP vessels (bulk carriers, oil tankers)} $$ $$ T_{\text{dock, max}} \le 5\,\text{years for general cargo / container ships} $$ **Thickness measurement coverage (KTU high-yield):** $$ \%\,\text{coverage} = \frac{N_{\text{measured}}}{N_{\text{required}}} \times 100 $$ where $N_{\text{required}}$ is dictated by IACS UR Z7/Z10 based on vessel age and structural risk. ### 2.4 Boundary Conditions / Validity Windows (Mandatory KTU Knowledge) | Survey | Window of Conduct | Consequence if Missed | |---|---|---| | Annual | Within 3 months **before or after** anniversary date | Certificate becomes invalid | | Intermediate | Between 2nd and 3rd anniversary, with ±6 mo. window | Cannot defer past 3rd anniversary | | Renewal | Within 3 months **before** expiry date | Cert lapses → ship cannot trade | | Docking (ESP) | Within 2.5-year window | Class may suspend/withdraw | ]<]minimax[>[ ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis — The INITIAL SURVEY (Module 1 Focal Topic) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Initial Survey** is the survey conducted **before a ship is put into service** for the first time, or **when a ship is re-registered under a new flag**, or when it is **commissioned for a new service/role** for which the existing certificates are not valid. The purpose is to verify that the ship fully complies with all applicable international conventions and the Flag State's regulations. ### 3.2 Trigger Conditions for an Initial Survey (DERIVATION LOGIC) **Step 1 — Identify the legal event.** A new ship is about to be delivered from the shipyard, or an existing ship is changing its status (flag, service type, or major modification). **Step 2 — Identify the applicable convention matrix.** Based on the ship's: - Gross Tonnage ($GT$) - Length ($L$) - Type of service (passenger / cargo / tanker / fishing / special) - Keel-laid date - Trading area (international / domestic) the surveyor determines which IMO conventions apply. **Step 3 — Apply the Initial Survey obligation.** The conventions explicitly state that **no statutory certificate can be issued** without an Initial Survey having been satisfactorily completed. The logic chain is: $$ \text{Ship Ready} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Initial Survey} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Compliance Verified} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Certificate Issued} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Trade Permitted} $$ This is a **necessary and sufficient** condition: *necessary* because the survey is required, *sufficient* because upon its satisfactory completion, the cert must be issued. ### 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step Scope of an Initial Survey The Initial Survey must verify, where applicable: **A. Hull Structural Compliance** 1. Review of **approved design drawings** (midship section, shell plating, framing, brackets). 2. Verification of **material certificates** for all grade-A, B, D, E steel used. 3. Inspection of **welding procedure specifications (WPS)** and **welder qualification records (WPQ)**. 4. Examination of **weld joints** by NDT (radiography, ultrasonic, magnetic particle) on critical seams. 5. Verification of **corrosion protection** (coating, cathodic sacrificial anodes). 6. Inspection of **watertight bulkheads, decks, and integrity** (hose test, hydrostatic test). **B. Machinery and Engineering Compliance** 1. Inspection of main and auxiliary engines, boilers, pressure vessels. 2. Verification of steering gear, shaft alignment, propeller clearances. 3. Electrical installation: generators, switchboards, emergency power, earthing. 4. Fuel, lube oil, and bilge piping systems. 5. Automation and control systems. **C. Life-Saving Appliances (SOLAS Ch. III)** 1. Lifeboats, liferafts, rescue boats — quantity, capacity, stowage, launching gear. 2. Lifejackets, immersion suits, lifebuoys, line-throwing appliances. 3. Pyrotechnics, EPIRBs, SARTs, two-way VHF radios. **D. Fire Safety (SOLAS Ch. II-2)** 1. Structural fire protection — fire-rated divisions, insulation. 2. Fire detection and alarm systems. 3. Fixed and portable fire-extinguishing systems (CO₂, foam, water mist, dry chemical). 4. Fireman's outfit, breathing apparatus. **E. Navigation and Radio (SOLAS Ch. V & IV)** 1. Gyro/magnetic compass, radar, ARPA, ECDIS, GPS, AIS, echo sounder, speed log. 2. GMDSS radio installation, DSC, NAVTEX, INMARSAT/COSPAS. 3. Voyage Data Recorder (VDR) / Simplified VDR (S-VDR) for applicable ships. **F. Pollution Prevention (MARPOL)** 1. Oil filtering equipment, oil content meter, sludge tank, oily water separator. 2. Sewage treatment plant, holding tank. 3. Incinerator, garbage management plan. 4. IOPP Certificate prerequisites. **G. Load Line Compliance (LL Convention)** 1. Verification of freeboard assignment, deck line, load line marks. 2. Weathertight integrity of hatchways, ventilators, air pipes, doors, scuttles. **H. Crew and Operational Compliance** 1. Minimum Safe Manning Document. 2. Crew certification, STCW compliance. 3. ISM Code Safety Management Certificate (SMC) and Document of Compliance (DOC) for the company. 4. ISPS Code Ship Security Certificate. ### 3.4 The Certificate Issuance Logic After all checks pass, the surveyor signs a **Survey Report**. The Flag State (or its RO) then issues: $$ \{\text{Certificates}\} = f(\text{Convention}, GT, L, \text{Service Type}) $$ The base set typically includes: - **Safety Construction Certificate** (SOLAS Ch. II-1, II-2) - **Safety Equipment Certificate** (SOLAS Ch. III) - **Safety Radio Certificate** (SOLAS Ch. IV) - **Minimum Safe Manning Document** - **Load Line Certificate** - **International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate (IOPP)** - **International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPP)** - **International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificate (ISPP)** - **International Anti-Fouling Systems Certificate (IAFS)** - **International Energy Efficiency Certificate (IEE)** - **Ship Security Certificate (ISPS)** ### 3.5 The 5-Year Validity Cycle and Survey Continuity Once the Initial Survey is complete, the cycle begins. For each cert (e.g., Safety Construction): $$ T_{\text{issue}}^{(0)} = T_{\text{Initial}} $$ $$ T_{\text{Annual}}^{(k)} = T_{\text{issue}}^{(0)} + k \cdot 12\,\text{months}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, 4 $$ $$ T_{\text{Intermediate}} \in [T^{(2)} - 6\,\text{mo}, \; T^{(3)} + 6\,\text{mo}] $$ $$ T_{\text{Renewal}}^{(1)} = T_{\text{issue}}^{(0)} + 5\,\text{years} $$ If the Renewal Survey is not completed within the window, the certificate **lapses**, and the ship must undergo a **fresh Initial Survey** equivalent before it can resume trading — a costly penalty. > [!NOTE] > **Examinable Concept:** A student must be able to draw a Gantt-like timeline showing the Initial Survey, the 4 Annual Surveys, the 1 Intermediate Survey, and the Renewal Survey within a 5-year certificate window. KTU questions often ask: *"Sketch the 5-year statutory survey cycle for a Safety Construction Certificate."* ]<]minimax[>[ ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Master Classification of Marine Surveys ```mermaid graph TD A[Marine Surveys]:::root --> B[Statutory Surveys] A --> C[Classification Surveys] A --> D[Commercial / Other Surveys] B --> B1[Initial Survey] B --> B2[Annual Survey] B --> B3[Intermediate Survey] B --> B4[Renewal Survey] B --> B5[Occasional Survey] C --> C1[Class Initial Survey] C --> C2[Class Annual Survey] C --> C3[Class Special Survey 5 Yr] C --> C4[Class Intermediate Survey] C --> C5[Continuous Survey alt] C --> C6[Lay Up Reactivation] D --> D1[Pre Purchase Survey] D --> D2[Damage Survey] D --> D3[Insurance Condition Survey] D --> D4[P and I Club Survey] classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Flow Logic of the INITIAL SURVEY ```mermaid graph TD S1[Ship Delivery or New Registration] --> S2{Determine Applicable Conventions} S2 --> S3[SOLAS MARPOL LL MLC ISPS] S3 --> S4[Drawing and Design Approval Review] S4 --> S5[Material and Weld NDT Verification] S5 --> S6[On Board Survey Hull and Machinery] S6 --> S7[Life Saving Fire and Navigation Check] S7 --> S8[Pollution Prevention Equipment Check] S8 --> S9{All Items Satisfactory?} S9 -- No --> S10[Rectification Required] S10 --> S6 S9 -- Yes --> S11[Survey Report Signed] S11 --> S12[Statutory Certificates Issued] S12 --> S13[5 Year Validity Cycle Begins] ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — 5-Year Statutory Survey Timeline (High-Yield Schematic) ```mermaid graph LR Y0A[Year 0: Initial Survey] --> Y1A[Year 1: Annual] Y1A --> Y2A[Year 2: Annual plus Intermediate Window] Y2A --> Y3A[Year 3: Annual] Y3A --> Y4A[Year 4: Annual] Y4A --> Y5A[Year 5: Renewal Survey equals Initial Scope] Y5A --> N1[New 5 Year Cycle Starts] ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Surveyor Authority and Reporting Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD FS[Flag State Administration] --> RO[Recognized Organization RO] RO --> SS[Shipyard Surveyor at Build] RO --> AS[Attending Surveyor AS] FS --> PSC[Port State Control Inspector] AS --> CERT[Issues Recommendations to RO] RO --> FLAG[Flag State Signs Statutory Certificate] CLASS[Classification Society Surveyor] --> CLASSCERT[Issues Class Certificate] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Initial Survey Process (Fallback Schematic) ```mermaid graph TD INPUT[INPUT: New Ship or Status Change] --> PROC1[Phase 1: Design Document Review] PROC1 --> PROC2[Phase 2: Material and Weld Verification at Yard] PROC2 --> PROC3[Phase 3: Outfitting Inspection] PROC3 --> PROC4[Phase 4: Sea Trials if Applicable] PROC4 --> PROC5[Phase 5: Final On Board Survey] PROC5 --> DEC{Conformity Achieved?} DEC -- Yes --> OUT[OUTPUT: Statutory Certificates Issued] DEC -- No --> LOOP[Loop Back to Rectification] LOOP --> PROC3 OUT --> CYCLE[5 Year Survey Cycle Activated] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "explain the Initial Survey," the examiner expects a flow that begins with **drawing approval**, proceeds through **construction surveillance**, and ends with **certificate issuance**. A purely descriptive answer without this flow will be marked down. ]<]minimax[>[ ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** *Define a "Marine Survey" and list any four types of marine surveys as per the KTU syllabus classification.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for types):** A marine survey is a systematic inspection of a ship, its machinery and equipment to verify compliance with international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines) and Classification Society rules. It is conducted by qualified surveyors authorized by the Flag State, a Recognized Organization, or a Classification Society. (1½ marks) Four types of marine surveys are: (½ mark each, pick any 4) 1. Initial Survey 2. Annual Survey 3. Intermediate / Periodical Survey 4. Renewal Survey 5. Special Survey 6. Occasional Survey 7. Continuous Survey 8. Damage Survey > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "survey means checking the ship" — this is too vague and loses the definition mark. The phrase "**statutory compliance**" or "**conformity with conventions**" is mandatory in the definition. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Retest]** *State the purpose and trigger event for conducting an Initial Survey on a vessel.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Purpose:** The Initial Survey is conducted to verify that a new ship, or a ship entering a new service or under a new flag, fully complies with all applicable international conventions and Flag State regulations **before any statutory certificate is issued** for the first time. (1½ marks) **Trigger Events:** (1½ marks) - A new ship entering service for the first time. - A change of Flag State. - A change in the type of service (e.g., domestic to international). - A major modification that invalidates the existing certificates. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Essay / Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern: Module carries 14-mark questions with internal choice. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part (a) and (b) is for 7 marks. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1(a) Modified]** **(a)** With the help of a suitable flowchart, explain in detail the various types of marine surveys, clearly differentiating between **statutory surveys** and **classification surveys**. (7 marks) **(b)** Describe the scope, trigger conditions, and the certificate issuance logic of the **Initial Survey** as required under IMO conventions. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **(a) Differentiation and Flowchart (7 marks):** *Statutory Surveys* (4 marks): These are surveys mandated by international conventions ratified by the Flag State, such as: - **SOLAS** — Safety of Life at Sea - **MARPOL** — Prevention of Pollution - **Load Lines Convention** — Freeboard - **MLC** — Maritime Labour - **ISPS** — Ship and Port Facility Security Statutory survey types include Initial, Annual, Intermediate, Renewal, and Occasional. The corresponding certificates (Safety Construction, Safety Equipment, Safety Radio, IOPP, IAPP, Load Line, etc.) are issued by the **Flag State** or a **Recognized Organization (RO)** authorized by it. *Classification Surveys* (2 marks): These are surveys performed under the rules of a **Classification Society** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, ClassNK). They are technically more rigorous than statutory minimums and cover hull scantlings, machinery, electrical systems, and automation. The ship receives a **Class Notation** (e.g., ✠100A1) and a Class Certificate. *Flowchart (1 mark)* — see **Diagram 4.1** in Section 4 above for the full classification tree. **(b) Initial Survey Scope, Triggers, and Issuance (7 marks):** *Trigger Conditions* (2 marks): An Initial Survey is triggered by: - A new build entering service for the first time. - Change of flag (re-registration). - Change of trading area or service type that makes existing certificates invalid. - Major structural or machinery conversion. *Scope* (3 marks): The survey verifies, in a single comprehensive exercise, the ship's compliance with **all** applicable conventions covering: hull structural integrity, machinery, electrical systems, life-saving appliances, fire safety, navigation and radio, pollution prevention, load line, and crew certification. NDT, hydrostatic tests, and operational trials are conducted as needed. *Certificate Issuance Logic* (2 marks): Upon successful completion, the surveyor submits the report to the RO/Flag State, which then issues the full set of statutory certificates with a 5-year validity. The cycle of Annual, Intermediate, and Renewal surveys then commences. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Internal Choice]** **(a)** "The Initial Survey is the foundation upon which the entire 5-year statutory survey cycle rests." Justify this statement with reference to the certificates issued and the subsequent annual/intermediate/renewal surveys. (7 marks) **(b)** A bulk carrier of 35,000 DWT built in 2019 is sold and re-registered under a new flag in 2024. Discuss which type of survey is now mandatory, list the certificates that will be issued, and outline the 5-year survey plan that follows. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **(a) Justification (7 marks):** The Initial Survey is the **prima facie** evidence of compliance that allows a ship to trade legally. Without an Initial Survey, no statutory certificate can be issued, and the ship cannot sail commercially. (1 mark) Once the Initial Survey is completed, certificates such as **Safety Construction**, **Safety Equipment**, **Safety Radio**, and **Load Line** are issued with a **5-year validity**. (2 marks) Within this 5-year window, the cycle of: - **Annual Surveys** (years 1, 2, 3, 4) — quick visual confirmation of continued compliance, and - **Intermediate Survey** (between 2nd and 3rd anniversary) — detailed selected-item inspection must be completed to keep the certificates valid. (2 marks) At the end of the 5 years, the **Renewal Survey** must be conducted, with a scope equivalent to the Initial Survey. If the renewal window is missed, the certificates lapse and the ship must undergo a **fresh Initial Survey**. Thus, the Initial Survey is the *anchor event* — the foundation — for the entire 5-year cycle. (2 marks) **(b) Bulk Carrier Re-registration Scenario (7 marks):** *Survey Type Required* (2 marks): This is a clear case of a **fresh Initial Survey** because the ship is being registered under a new Flag State. The existing certificates issued by the previous Flag State are no longer valid. *Certificates to be Issued* (2 marks): Under the new flag, the ship will receive: - Safety Construction Certificate - Safety Equipment Certificate - Safety Radio Certificate - Load Line Certificate - Minimum Safe Manning Document - IOPP, IAPP, ISPP, IAFS Certificates - ISPS Ship Security Certificate - New Class Certificate (continuous from 2019, but re-validated under the new flag's RO agreement) *5-Year Survey Plan* (3 marks): The plan will be: | Year | Survey | |---|---| | 2024 | **Initial Survey** (full scope) — now | | 2025 | Annual Survey | | 2026 | Annual Survey + Intermediate Survey (window 2026–2027) | | 2027 | Annual Survey | | 2028 | Annual Survey | | 2029 | **Renewal Survey** (full scope, equal to Initial) — by cert expiry | Additionally, because the ship is a **bulk carrier > 500 GT**, ESP (Enhanced Survey Programme) applies: - Docking at intervals not exceeding **2.5 years**. - Thickness gauging as per IACS UR Z7. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse Class and Statutory surveys.** A ship can hold a valid Class certificate but have *lapsed statutory certificates* — in that case, she cannot trade even if her Class symbol is active. The reverse is also possible. > 2. **Always mention the issuing authority** — Flag State (or its RO) for statutory, Classification Society for class. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 3. **For Initial Survey answers, students often forget the operational checks** (sea trials, lifeboat launching drill, fire pump test). Including these boosts marks. > 4. **Docking intervals are often missed** in the 5-year plan — KTU frequently tests ESP vs. non-ESP vessel differences. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Marine Surveys / Initial Survey** - A **marine survey** is a documented, authorized inspection to verify compliance with conventions and rules. - **Two parallel regimes:** Statutory (Flag/RO) vs. Classification (Class Society). - **Statutory triggers:** SOLAS, MARPOL, LL, MLC, ISPS, AFS, BWM. - **Classification triggers:** Society rules (IACS unified requirements). - **Initial Survey** = first-time full-scope survey before a ship is put into service, or upon a status-changing event (new flag, new service, major conversion). - **Initial Survey scope** = complete verification: design, material, welding, hull, machinery, electrical, lifesaving, fire, navigation, radio, pollution, load line, crew. - **Certificates issued** = Safety Construction, Safety Equipment, Safety Radio, Load Line, IOPP, IAPP, ISPP, IAFS, IEE, ISPS, Manning Document, plus Class. - **Validity** = 5 years (typical), subject to Annual and Intermediate surveys. - **Annual Survey** = 12-month interval, ±3-month window, visual & operational checks. - **Intermediate Survey** = between 2nd and 3rd anniversary, ±6-month window. - **Renewal Survey** = 5-yearly, full scope equal to Initial. - **Special Survey (Class)** = 5-yearly, includes dry-dock, internal inspection, thickness gauging. - **Continuous Survey** = opt-in alternative to Special Survey; spread over 5 years. - **ESP vessels** (tankers, bulk carriers) have **2.5-year maximum docking interval** and IACS UR Z7/Z10 thickness gauging obligations. - **Missing a survey window** = certificate lapse → may need fresh Initial Survey → costly commercial penalty. - **Surveyors** are authorized by **Flag State** (or its RO) for statutory, and by the **Class Society** for classification work. - **Initial Survey triggers** the **5-year cycle** of Annual → Annual → Intermediate → Annual → Annual → Renewal. - **Port State Control (PSC)** can independently verify validity of all these certificates at any foreign port. - **KTU mantra:** "Without Initial Survey → No Certificate → No Trade." ---

topics.title: Annual Survey
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Annual Survey

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Annual Survey (AS)** is a mandatory, periodic class survey carried out by an authorized surveyor of a recognized Classification Society (e.g., Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, ABS, IRS) once every calendar year, to verify that the hull, machinery, and statutory equipment of a classed vessel remain in a condition consistent with the Society's Rules and applicable International Conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, MLC), between successive Special Surveys.

The survey is conducted while the vessel is in service, afloat, or at a suitable berth, and is the first level of the three-tier classification regime comprising **Annual → Intermediate → Special Surveys**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Basis:** The Annual Survey obligation arises from the vessel's Class contract with the Classification Society and indirectly from Flag State adoption of IMO Conventions. The Survey Certificate and Record of Annual Survey are valid only for **12 months** from completion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your car requires a **yearly fitness check-up** at an authorized garage, even though you maintain it daily. The mechanic doesn't dismantle the engine; instead, he inspects the visible parts, tests the brakes, checks tire wear, oil levels, lights, and emissions. Similarly:

| Car Fitness Test Analogy | Ship Annual Survey |
|---|---|
| Visual check of bodywork (rust, dents) | Visual check of hull plating, frames |
| Brake & steering functionality test | Steering gear, rudder, anchors |
| Light & horn check | Navigation lights, signals, GMDSS |
| Emission test (statutory) | MARPOL pollution prevention equipment |
| Odometer & service log review | Records of past surveys, thickness measurements |
| Annual sticker (validity 1 year) | Annual Survey Certificate (validity 12 months) |

> [!TIP]
> The Annual Survey is **NOT a complete overhaul**. It is a **"health check + sampling"** to confirm that the vessel has not degraded significantly between Special Surveys.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Class Surveyor:** A qualified inspector employed or authorized by the Classification Society.
- **Continuous Survey (CS):** An alternative scheme where surveys are spread across the 5-year cycle instead of being grouped as Annual Surveys.
- **Condition of Class (CoC):** A written requirement issued by the surveyor specifying a deficiency and a deadline for rectification.
- **Single Point of Failure (SPoF):** Critical items whose failure would cause major incident — these get extra attention in AS.
- **ESP (Enhanced Survey Programme):** Mandatory for oil tankers, bulk carriers under IACS rules.
- **Threshold for Steel Renewal:** Corrosion allowance exceeded, typically **75%** of original scantling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** An Annual Survey must be completed within **±3 months** of the anniversary date of the last Special Survey completion. The next Special Survey's 5-year clock starts from the last Special Survey, not from the last Annual Survey.

### 1.4 Scope of Annual Survey (High-Level)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope Summary:** Visual examination + function tests + sampling-based thickness measurement + statutory equipment verification.

The principal areas covered are:
1. Hull (above waterline, waterline, and limited below-waterline).
2. Machinery and electrical installations.
3. Navigation, life-saving, and fire-fighting appliances.
4. Mooring and anchoring equipment.
5. Pollution prevention equipment.
6. Statutory documentation review.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Annual Survey?

The underlying engineering rationale is based on **structural fatigue theory, corrosion kinetics, and progressive degradation models**. Steel hulls of ships are subjected to:

- **Cyclic wave loading** (≈10⁶ – 10⁸ cycles/year) → fatigue crack initiation.
- **Electrochemical corrosion** → thickness loss (typically 0.1 – 0.3 mm/year for ballast tanks).
- **Mechanical wear** (rudder, shaft brackets, stern tube area).
- **Operational damage** (cargo hold buckling, shell plate indentations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A ship is not a static structure; it is a **fatigue-driven, corrosion-driven system** whose residual strength decays with time. Annual Survey is the early-warning mechanism for this decay.

### 2.2 Breakdown of Operational Steps (Logic Flow)

**Step 1 — Pre-Survey Preparation**
- Surveyor reviews prior survey reports (Special Survey, last AS, Conditions of Class).
- Reviews the **Survey Planning Document (SPD)** if applicable.
- Verifies the **Classification Register** entry and statutory certificates are current.

**Step 2 — Documentation Check**
- Crew certificates, training records, operating manuals, logbooks.
- Thickness measurement reports from previous Special Survey.
- Maintenance records (planned maintenance system, PMS).

**Step 3 — Visual Examination (Hull)**
- Inspection of weather deck, hatch covers, bulwarks, freeing ports.
- Corrosion mapping of accessible areas.
- Check for cracks at stress-concentration zones (bracket toes, hatch corners, bilge strakes).

**Step 4 — Function Tests**
- Steering gear (with full rudder movement).
- Mooring winch brakes, anchor windlass.
- Emergency fire pump, bilge pump, OWS (Oily Water Separator).
- Lifeboat release mechanism (load test every 5 years — partial check annually).

**Step 5 — Thickness Measurement (Sampling)**
- A **sub-set** of points from the Special Survey gauging scheme is repeated.
- Comparison with baseline values to estimate **corrosion rate**.

**Step 6 — Issue of Survey Report / Endorsement**
