- If satisfactory → Class notation continues.
- If deficient → **Condition of Class** issued with a deadline.
- If critical → vessel may be detained.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for KTU 2024 Scheme numerical/analytical questions on hull inspection.

| Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion Rate | $CR = \dfrac{t_0 - t_1}{\Delta t}$ | Linear corrosion rate; $t_0$ = original thickness, $t_1$ = current thickness | mm/yr |
| Minimum Allowable Scantling | $t_{min} = t_{net} + t_{corr}$ | $t_{net}$ = net (rule) thickness, $t_{corr}$ = corrosion addition | mm |
| Renewal Threshold | $t_{actual} \le 0.75\, t_{original}$ | Trigger for steel renewal per IACS UR Z10 | mm |
| Residual Strength Factor (RSF) | $RSF = \dfrac{t_{actual}}{t_{original}}$ | RSF < 0.75 indicates significant degradation | dimensionless |
| Subdivision Load Line | $F_d = P_{waterline} - P_{damage}$ | Damage stability check | kN/m² |
| Submergence Percentage | $\% = \dfrac{\Delta_{d}}{\Delta_{total}} \times 100$ | Permeability check | % |
| Plate Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{6M}{t^2}$ | Bending stress in a plate strip of unit width | N/mm² |
| Shear Stress in Web | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A_{web}}$ | Shear in stiffener web | N/mm² |
| Plate Slenderness | $\beta = \dfrac{b}{t}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | Used in CSR/Common Structural Rules buckling check | dimensionless |
| Annual Survey Window | $W = \pm 3$ months | Window around anniversary date | months |
| Validity of Survey | $V = 12$ months | Maximum gap between consecutive AS | months |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never use the pipe character `\vert` symbol to denote absolute value. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ **only inside math mode**, and reserve plain `|` for column separators.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insurance & Chartering:** Banks, P&I Clubs, and charterers require a valid Class Certificate. Loss of class = loss of hire insurance.
- **Statutory Compliance:** Flag State recognizes Class surveys as evidence of compliance with IMO Conventions.
- **Risk Mitigation:** Early detection of cracks prevents catastrophic failures (e.g., Prestige, Erika tanker break-ups).
- **Asset Valuation:** A vessel with continuous Class records commands a **5–10%** higher resale price.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Corrosion Rate from Thickness Measurements

A common KTU numerical problem: Given original thickness, two subsequent thickness readings at known time intervals, determine the corrosion rate and predict the **Year of Next Mandatory Renewal**.

**Problem Setup:**

A plate has an original thickness $t_0 = 22.0$ mm. Thickness gauging shows $t_1 = 20.5$ mm at the last Special Survey (Year 0). At the 2nd Annual Survey (Year 2), $t_2 = 19.7$ mm. The renewal threshold is 75% of original. Determine the corrosion rate and remaining service life.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the net thickness loss between the two measurements.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{lost} &= t_1 - t_2 \\
&= 20.5 - 19.7 \\
&= 0.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This loss occurred over the time interval $\Delta T = 2$ years.

**Step 2 — Compute the linear corrosion rate $CR$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \dfrac{\Delta t_{lost}}{\Delta T} \\
&= \dfrac{0.8 \text{ mm}}{2 \text{ yr}} \\
&= 0.4 \text{ mm/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the renewal thickness threshold $t_{ren}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ren} &= 0.75 \times t_0 \\
&= 0.75 \times 22.0 \\
&= 16.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the remaining thickness to be lost before renewal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{rem} &= t_2 - t_{ren} \\
&= 19.7 - 16.5 \\
&= 3.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Predict the remaining service life $T_{rem}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rem} &= \dfrac{\Delta t_{rem}}{CR} \\
&= \dfrac{3.2 \text{ mm}}{0.4 \text{ mm/yr}} \\
&= 8.0 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the absolute renewal year.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{renewal} &= 2 + 8.0 \\
&= 10.0 \text{ years from last Special Survey}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since the next Special Survey occurs at 5 years, the plate **will not reach renewal threshold before the next Special Survey**, provided the corrosion rate remains linear. The condition should, however, be re-evaluated at each Annual Survey.

### 3.2 Derivation — Residual Strength Factor and Wastage Acceptance

A second classic problem: Determine whether a plate passes the IACS UR Z10 acceptance criteria given actual wastage and corrosion addition.

**Step 1 — Define the original scantling $t_{original} = 18.0$ mm.**

**Step 2 — Compute the observed wastage percentage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W\% &= \dfrac{t_{original} - t_{actual}}{t_{original}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{18.0 - 14.5}{18.0} \times 100 \\
&= 19.44\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to acceptance limits (IACS UR Z10).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Acceptance Range} &: 0\% \le W\% \le 25\% \\
\text{Observed} &: 19.44\% \le 25\% \quad \text{(PASS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Per IACS UR Z10, a **substantial corrosion** condition (wastage > 75%) requires steel renewal. **Moderate corrosion** (25–75%) requires increased gauging frequency. **Light corrosion** (< 25%) is acceptable with routine AS checks.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Annual Survey Outcomes

The Surveyor follows a decision tree based on findings. Below is a structured analytical matrix for KTU-style answer framing.

| Finding Severity | Condition Observed | Surveyor Action | Class Impact | Operational Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nil** | No defect, gauges within tolerance | Endorse survey | Class maintained | Continue trading |
| **Minor** | Surface corrosion, paint failure | Recommend (verbal/written) | No CoC | Continue with PMS |
| **Moderate** | Local wastage 25–75% | Issue CoC with deadline (≤ next AS) | Class maintained (conditional) | Reduced survey interval |
| **Major** | Substantial corrosion >75% | Issue CoC with ≤ 4 months deadline | Class suspended if overdue | Schedule repair before next voyage |
| **Critical** | Active cracks, fracture, buckling | Stop vessel immediately | Recommend suspension | Detain / No sail clearance |
| **Statutory Failure** | LSA/FSS/MARPOL equipment non-functional | Detain-able deficiency | Report to Flag State | Port State may detain |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Survey Window Calculation

A vessel's last Special Survey was completed on 15 March 2022. Determine the Annual Survey window for 2023, 2024, and the next Intermediate Survey date.

**Step 1 — Establish the anniversary date.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{anniv} &= 15 \text{ March each year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 2023 AS window (1st Annual Survey).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{2023} &= D_{anniv} \pm 3 \text{ months} \\
&= 15 \text{ Dec 2022} \;\; \text{to} \;\; 15 \text{ Jun 2023}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the same logic for 2024 and 2025.**

**Step 4 — Identify Intermediate Survey date (between 2.5 and 3 years from last SS).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{IS} &\in [15 \text{ Sep 2024}, \; 15 \text{ Mar 2025]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **Special Survey (5-year) anniversary** with the **Annual Survey anniversary**. The 5-year window is fixed from the *last* Special Survey, while the AS window follows a *rolling 12-month* cycle. Marks lost here are very common in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Annual Survey Process Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of an Annual Survey, from the initial class notification through the issuance of the survey endorsement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AS1: Class Notification to Owner] --> B[AS2: Owner Applies for AS]
    B --> C[AS3: Surveyor Reviews Prior Records]
    C --> D[AS4: Documentation Audit]
    D --> E[AS5: Visual Hull Examination]
    E --> F[AS6: Function Tests of Equipment]
    F --> G[AS7: Sampling Thickness Measurement]
    G --> H{AS8: All Items Satisfactory?}
    H -- Yes --> I[AS9: Endorse Class Register]
    H -- No --> J[AS10: Issue Condition of Class]
    J --> K{AS11: Rectification Within Deadline?}
    K -- Yes --> L[AS12: Follow-up Survey]
    L --> I
    K -- No --> M[AS13: Suspend / Withdraw Class]
    I --> N[AS14: Issue Annual Survey Certificate]
    N --> O[AS15: Valid 12 Months]
    M --> P[AS16: Vessel Cannot Trade]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Class Survey Regime

This diagram shows the relationship between Annual, Intermediate, Special, and Docking Surveys, helping students remember the survey pyramid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SS[Special Survey every 5 years] --> AS1[Annual 1]
    SS --> IS[Intermediate Survey 2.5-3 yrs]
    SS --> AS2[Annual 2]
    SS --> DS[Docking Survey 2.5-3 yrs]
    SS --> AS3[Annual 3]
    SS --> AS4[Annual 4]
    SS --> AS5[Annual 5]
    AS1 --> AS2
    AS2 --> AS3
    AS3 --> AS4
    AS4 --> AS5
    IS --> SSnext[Next Special Survey]
    DS --> SSnext
    SS --> SSnext
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Annual Survey Scope (Hierarchical Tree)

A functional architecture mapping the **inspection domains** covered during an Annual Survey.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Annual Survey Scope] --> HULL[Hull and Structural]
    ROOT --> MACH[Machinery and Electrical]
    ROOT --> STAT[Statutory Equipment]
    ROOT --> MOOR[Mooring and Anchoring]
    ROOT --> DOC[Documentation]

    HULL --> H1[Hull Plating and Frames Visual]
    HULL --> H2[Corrosion Mapping]
    HULL --> H3[Crack Inspection in High Stress Zones]
    HULL --> H4[Sampling Thickness Measurement]

    MACH --> M1[Steering Gear Test]
    MACH --> M2[Engine Room Walk-through]
    MACH --> M3[Bilge Pump Test]
    MACH --> M4[Electrical Insulation Check]
    MACH --> M5[Boilers and Pressure Vessels]

    STAT --> S1[Lifeboats and Life Rafts]
    STAT --> S2[Fire Pumps and Hydrants]
    STAT --> S3[FFFA and CO2 System]
    STAT --> S4[Navigational Lights and Signals]
    STAT --> S5[OWS and MARPOL Equipment]

    MOOR --> MO1[Anchor Windlass Brake Test]
    MOOR --> MO2[Mooring Winch Test]
    MOOR --> MO3[Mooring Lines Visual]

    DOC --> D1[Prior Survey Records]
    DOC --> D2[Thickness Reports]
    DOC --> D3[Crew Certifications]
    DOC --> D4[Oil Record Book]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Survey Decision Tree (Defect Severity)

This diagram provides a visual flow for the surveyor's decision logic, mapped directly to the analytical matrix in Section 3.3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Defect Found?] --> OBS[Observe and Document]
    OBS --> CAT{Categorize Severity}
    CAT -- Nil --> END1[Endorse - No Action]
    CAT -- Minor --> REC[Issue Recommendation]
    CAT -- Moderate --> COC[Issue Condition of Class]
    CAT -- Major --> COC2[CoC - Short Deadline]
    CAT -- Critical --> HALT[Halt Operations]
    REC --> END1
    COC --> FOLLOW[Follow-up at Next AS]
    COC2 --> REPAIR[Schedule Immediate Repair]
    HALT --> FLAG[Notify Flag State]
    REPAIR --> END1
    FOLLOW --> END1
    FLAG --> SUSPEND[Suspend Class]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define Annual Survey as applicable to classification of ships. List any four items included in its scope.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Annual Survey** is a yearly class survey carried out by a Classification Society surveyor to confirm that the hull, machinery, and statutory equipment of a vessel remain in compliance with the Society's Rules and applicable IMO Conventions between successive Special Surveys.

**Four items in scope:**
1. Visual examination of weather deck, hatch covers, and accessible hull plating.
2. Function test of steering gear, mooring winches, and anchor windlass.
3. Verification of life-saving, fire-fighting, and navigational equipment.
4. Sampling-based thickness measurement at locations specified in the Survey Planning Document.

---

**Q2. State the validity period of an Annual Survey Certificate and the allowed window for its completion.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Annual Survey Certificate is **valid for 12 months** from the date of completion.

The survey must be completed within **3 months before to 3 months after the anniversary date** of the last Special Survey, giving a **6-month total window**. If the survey is not completed within this window, the vessel's class may be suspended, leading to a possible detention by Port State Control.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Style, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q A(a)** Describe the **scope and objectives** of the Annual Survey for a classed vessel. Explain the role of the **Classification Society** and the **Condition of Class** mechanism. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition and Scope:**

The Annual Survey is a yearly, mandatory inspection conducted by a Classification Society surveyor to verify the continued seaworthiness of a vessel between Special Surveys. Its scope encompasses:

- **Hull:** Visual examination of accessible plating, frames, decks, hatch covers, and bulwarks. Corrosion mapping at critical zones (e.g., bilge strakes, bracket toes).
- **Machinery:** Functional tests of steering gear, bilge pumps, OWS, boilers, electrical insulation.
- **Statutory:** Verification of LSA (life-saving appliances), FSS (fire safety systems), navigation lights, and GMDSS.
- **Thickness Measurement:** Sampling of gauging points per the Survey Planning Document.
- **Documentation:** Review of prior survey reports, oil record book, and crew certifications.

**Role of Classification Society:**

The Classification Society is an independent, non-governmental organization that:
- Sets technical Rules for hull and machinery design.
- Verifies compliance during new construction.
- Conducts periodic class surveys to ensure Rule compliance throughout the vessel's service life.
- Maintains the **Class Register** of vessels that meet its standards.
- Acts as a **deemed surveyor** for many Flag States for statutory certification.

**Condition of Class (CoC):**

A **Condition of Class** is a written requirement issued by the surveyor when a defect is observed that does not warrant immediate detention but must be rectified within a specified deadline. Key aspects:

- Typical deadlines: **next Annual Survey** (moderate defects), **≤ 4 months** (major defects), or **next scheduled drydock** (structural).
- CoC is **class-specific** and does not in itself prohibit the vessel from sailing, but failure to clear it leads to **suspension of class**.
- CoC is a binding obligation that is **recorded in the Class Register** until cleared.
- It is the primary **risk-management tool** linking survey findings to vessel operation.

> [!NOTE]
> A CoC for a single minor deficiency is **not** equivalent to a Port State Control deficiency; the latter can result in detention, while CoC primarily affects class standing and insurance coverage.

---

**Q A(b)** A plate in a ballast tank has an original thickness of **20.0 mm**. Thickness gauging at the 1st Annual Survey (1 year after the Special Survey) shows **18.8 mm**, and at the 2nd Annual Survey (2 years after the Special Survey) shows **17.7 mm**. The renewal threshold is **75% of original**. Calculate the **corrosion rate** and predict the **remaining service life**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the corrosion rate between the two AS readings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= t_1 - t_2 = 18.8 - 17.7 = 1.1 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta T &= 1 \text{ year} \\
CR &= \dfrac{1.1}{1} = 1.1 \text{ mm/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the renewal threshold.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ren} &= 0.75 \times 20.0 = 15.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the remaining thickness to be lost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{rem} &= 17.7 - 15.0 = 2.7 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Predict the remaining service life.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rem} &= \dfrac{2.7}{1.1} \approx 2.45 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the absolute year of renewal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{renewal} &= 2 + 2.45 = 4.45 \text{ years from last Special Survey}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**

Since the next Special Survey is at 5 years, the plate is **likely to reach the renewal threshold before the next Special Survey** if the corrosion rate remains constant. The Surveyor should:

- Issue a **Condition of Class** with a deadline before the next Special Survey.
- Recommend **increased gauging frequency** (subsequent Annual Surveys).
- Consider **coating restoration** to reduce corrosion rate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q B(a)** Explain the **statutory and class significance** of the Annual Survey. Discuss how the Annual Survey differs from a **Special Survey** and an **Intermediate Survey** in terms of scope, frequency, and vessel condition. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Statutory Significance:**

- The Annual Survey is **not directly statutory** but is mandated by the **Class contract** between the owner and the Classification Society.
- However, Flag States (under SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line) rely on Class for many statutory surveys. Hence, a valid Class certificate implies statutory compliance.
- Port State Control authorities check the validity of class certificates; expired or suspended class can lead to **detention**.

**Class Significance:**

- It maintains the vessel's **Class Register** entry without interruption.
- It generates **Conditions of Class** that bind the owner to a specific rectification timeline.
- It provides early warning of **structural, mechanical, or statutory** deterioration.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Annual Survey | Intermediate Survey | Special Survey |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Every 12 months | Between 2.5 and 3 years from SS | Every 5 years |
| Vessel Condition | In service or afloat | In service + partial docking | Docking + extensive examination |
| Hull Examination | Visual, accessible parts | More detailed; selected tanks | All internal spaces; complete |
| Thickness Measurement | Sampling | Increased scope | Full survey of all zones |
| Machinery | Function tests | Detailed examination | Complete overhaul |
| Duration | 1–2 days | 3–5 days | 2–6 weeks |
| Statutory Linkage | Indirect | Indirect | Direct (e.g., load line re-issue) |
| Cost to Owner | Low | Moderate | High |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Intermediate Survey** is essentially an "advanced" Annual Survey performed halfway through the 5-year cycle, with expanded scope (e.g., inspection of at least one ballast tank for ESP vessels).

---

**Q B(b)** A vessel's last Special Survey was completed on **20 June 2021**. Determine the **due dates for the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th, and 5th Annual Surveys**, and identify the **window for the Intermediate Survey** and the **due date of the next Special Survey**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Establish the anniversary date.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{anniv} &= 20 \text{ June}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the 1st Annual Survey window.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= 20 \text{ Mar 2022} \;\; \text{to} \;\; 20 \text{ Sep 2022}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the same pattern for subsequent surveys.**

| Survey | Window Start | Window End |
|---|---|---|
| 1st AS | 20 Mar 2022 | 20 Sep 2022 |
| 2nd AS | 20 Mar 2023 | 20 Sep 2023 |
| 3rd AS | 20 Mar 2024 | 20 Sep 2024 |
| 4th AS | 20 Mar 2025 | 20 Sep 2025 |
| 5th AS | 20 Mar 2026 | 20 Sep 2026 |

**Step 4 — Identify the Intermediate Survey window.**

The Intermediate Survey is due between **2.5 and 3 years** from the last Special Survey, i.e., between:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{IS} &= 20 \text{ Dec 2023} \;\; \text{to} \;\; 20 \text{ Jun 2024}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the next Special Survey due date.**

The next Special Survey is due at the **5th anniversary** of the previous one:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{SS, next} &= 20 \text{ June 2026}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Some students add 5 years from the **last completed Annual Survey** instead of from the **last Special Survey**. This is a common error worth 2–3 marks. The 5-year cycle is anchored to the Special Survey, not the Annual Survey.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Survey Window Confusion** — Mixing up the **±3 month** AS window with the **5-year** SS cycle. (2 marks lost frequently.)
> 2. **Class vs Statutory** — Not clearly distinguishing between **Classification Survey** (class-driven) and **Statutory Survey** (Flag-State-driven). Always use the term "deemed surveyor" where applicable.
> 3. **CoC vs Detention** — A Condition of Class does **not** automatically detain a vessel. Detention is a Port State Control action, not a Class action.
> 4. **Unit Errors in Corrosion Calculations** — Mixing **mm/yr** with **inches/yr**. Always state the unit explicitly.
> 5. **IACS UR Z10 Thresholds** — Students often quote **75%** as the only threshold. There are actually three tiers: < 25% (light), 25–75% (moderate), > 75% (substantial).
> 6. **Continuous Survey (CS) vs Annual Survey** — Under the CS scheme, the vessel still undergoes annual inspections, but the scope is divided across the 5-year cycle. Do not equate CS with "no surveys."
> 7. **Forgetting the 12-month Validity** — A valid AS endorsement is required for trading; lapsing it by even a few days is a serious non-conformity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Annual Survey**

- **Definition:** Yearly Class survey by an authorized surveyor to confirm compliance between Special Surveys.
- **Frequency:** Every 12 months, within a **±3-month window** of the anniversary date.
- **Validity:** **12 months** from completion date.
- **Scope:** Visual hull + function tests + sampling thickness measurement + statutory equipment verification.
- **Three IACS Corrosion Tiers:** < 25% (light), 25–75% (moderate), > 75% (substantial — renewal required).
- **Renewal Threshold Formula:** $t_{ren} = 0.75 \times t_{original}$.
- **Corrosion Rate Formula:** $CR = \dfrac{\Delta t}{\Delta T}$ in **mm/yr**.
- **Class Document:** Annual Survey Certificate + entry in Class Register.
- **Condition of Class (CoC):** Written rectification requirement with deadline; failure = suspension of class.
- **Annual vs Intermediate vs Special:** Annual (12 mo, visual+sampling) → Intermediate (2.5–3 yr, expanded) → Special (5 yr, complete + dry-dock).
- **Hierarchical Documents:** SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, MLC, IACS UR Z10, IACS UR Z23 (Hull Surveys).
- **Continuous Survey (CS):** Alternative scheme where SS scope is spread across 5 years; AS still required.
- **Surveyor Authority:** Can detain (only if also Flag State inspector), can suspend class, can issue CoC.
- **Port State Control Linkage:** Valid Class certificate is a basic entry requirement at any foreign port.
- **Critical Items in AS:** Steering gear, anchor windlass, fire pumps, OWS, navigation lights, hatch covers, mooring winches.
- **Failure to Comply:** Suspension of class → potential P&I insurance void → detention by Port State.
- **Engineering Use:** Fatigue life tracking, corrosion rate trend analysis, asset residual strength estimation.

> **🎯 One-Line Takeaway:** *Annual Survey is the **vessel's yearly health check** — a sampling-based, function-driven inspection that keeps the Class alive and the ship insured, employable, and seaworthy between major Special Surveys.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Periodic Survey
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Periodic Survey ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Periodic Survey** in the context of ship hull inspection is a structured, time-bound, and rule-based examination of a vessel's hull structure, machinery installations, and safety systems, conducted at predetermined intervals throughout the ship's service life by an authorized **Classification Society Surveyor** (or a Flag State Administration surveyor) to verify continued compliance with the society's rules and the relevant international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines). Under the **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) Unified Requirements (UR)** framework—specifically **UR Z1, Z2, Z3, Z7, Z9, Z10, and Z17**—the periodic survey system replaces the older "special survey" methodology with a continuous, rolling survey regime. The periodicity and scope are functions of the **ship's age (t)** measured in years from the date of build, and the **vessel's structural category**. $$\text{Survey Interval} \propto f(t_{\text{age}}, \text{Structural Category}, \text{Flag State})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** > Periodic Survey forms the backbone of any hull inspection curriculum. Students must clearly distinguish between **Classification Surveys** (voluntary, rule-based) and **Statutory Surveys** (mandatory, convention-based). Both periodic regimes run in parallel. > [!NOTE] > **IACS Member Societies (recognized by KTU syllabus):** > ABS, DNV, LR (Lloyd's Register), BV (Bureau Veritas), ClassNK, KR (Korean Register), RINA, IRS, CCS, RS, PRS, and CRS. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you bought a 20-year-old house in Kerala's coastal belt. You would not just rely on the original builder's certificate; instead, you would: 1. **Annually** check the roof for leaks, 2. **Every 5 years** do a thorough electrical & plumbing audit, 3. **Every 10–15 years** open up the walls and inspect structural members for termite damage, 4. **After 30 years**, you would expect a complete renovation feasibility study. A ship's **Periodic Survey** is precisely this — a layered, time-staged inspection regime applied to a marine steel structure constantly subjected to: - **Corrosion** (electrochemical attack from seawater), - **Fatigue** (cyclic loading from waves), - **Slamming and green-water loading** (for bow region), - **Sloshing** (in partial-filled tanks). The classification society acts like a **certified structural doctor**, and the survey schedule is the **prescription protocol** that ensures the ship's "skeleton" (hull girder, frames, plates, tanks) remains fit for the punishing marine environment. ### 1.3 Key Constants / Standard Metrics - **Annual Survey Window:** Within **3 months before or after** the anniversary date of the previous survey (**+3 / -3 month rule**). - **Special Survey Cycle:** Every **5 years** for most merchant vessels. - **Standard Scantling Corrosion Allowance (t_c):** Typically **0.5 mm to 3.0 mm** depending on the structural member (e.g., bottom plating vs. superstructure). - **IACS UR Z10.1 Threshold Age:** Vessels **15 years and older** require enhanced close-up survey scope. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Periodic Surveys (IACS UR Z Scheme) The IACS Unified Requirement **UR Z** series defines a harmonized survey framework. Understanding the hierarchy is essential for KTU valuation: 1. **Annual Survey (AS):** - Conducted **once per year** within the survey window. - General examination of hull, machinery, and equipment. - Visual check for obvious damage, corrosion, or leaks. 2. **Intermediate Survey (IS):** - Conducted between the **2nd and 3rd Annual Surveys** of each Special Survey cycle. - Scope is roughly **halfway** between Annual and Special. - Includes internal examination of ballast tanks, peak tanks, and selected spaces. 3. **Special Survey (SS) / Renewal Survey:** - The most comprehensive survey, conducted every **5 years** (±6 months for Class). - Full hull examination, internal tank inspections, thickness gauging, NDT (UT, MPI, DPI). - "Renewal" of the Classification Certificate. 4. **Continuous Survey (CS):** - An alternative to the Special Survey cycle. - The ship's hull and machinery are surveyed on a **rolling/continuous basis**, with portions completed each year. - Requires surveyor's approval and an agreed schedule. 5. **Propeller Shaft and Boiler Surveys:** - Independent periodic surveys coupled with hull schedule. ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Tier - **Why Annual?** Early detection of progressive damage prevents costly special-survey surprises. - **Why Intermediate?** Captures damage that progresses over **2–3 years** (e.g., coating breakdown in ballast tanks). - **Why Special?** Comprehensive validation before renewal; statutory load-line renewal aligns here (every 5 years). - **Why Continuous?** Operational efficiency — the ship avoids long, costly dry-dock immobilization. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes | |---|---------|---------------|-------|-------| | 1 | Age of vessel | $t_{\text{age}} = T_{\text{survey}} - T_{\text{build}}$ | years | Drives survey scope | | 2 | Allowable diminution | $t_{\text{as-built}} - t_{\text{measured}} \leq t_{\text{c,allow}}$ | mm | Per IACS UR Z10 | | 3 | Renewal thickness criterion | $t_{\text{ren}} = t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{corr}}$ | mm | $t_{\text{net}}$ from rule tables | | 4 | Substantial corrosion limit | $t_{\text{wastage}} \geq 0.75 \cdot t_{\text{corr}}$ | mm | Triggers "substantial corrosion" | | 5 | Annual survey window | $[t_{\text{due}} - 3, \; t_{\text{due}} + 3]$ | months | IACS PR-1A rule | | 6 | Special survey window | $[t_{\text{due}} - 6, \; t_{\text{due}} + 6]$ | months | 5-year cycle | | 7 | Close-up scope % (age 5–10) | $\sim 25\%$ of tanks in each zone | % | UR Z10.1 | | 8 | Close-up scope % (age 10–15) | $\sim 50\%$ of tanks in each zone | % | UR Z10.1 | | 9 | Close-up scope % (age $\geq$ 15) | $100\%$ of ballast + critical tanks | % | UR Z10.1 | | 10 | Tank coating age (Epoxy) | Practical life $\sim 10$–15 years | years | Triggers re-coating scope | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Conversion Note (Anti-Markdown-Break):** > In formulas 2, 4, and 10, the symbol `\vert` is rendered as a vertical bar. To avoid breaking markdown table syntax, we have written inequalities using $\leq$ and $\geq$ instead of inline pipes $\vert \cdot \vert$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Periodic Survey system is the **operational backbone** of maritime safety worldwide. Without it: - Aging tankers (e.g., the **Erika 1999** and **Prestige 2002** catastrophes) would not have triggered timely intervention. - Insurance premiums (H&M and P&I) depend on a vessel's **Class status**. - Port State Control (PSC) detentions are heavily influenced by overdue surveys. In the Indian context, vessels registered under the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 (as amended)** must follow survey intervals prescribed by the Director General of Shipping (DGS) and classification societies. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Renewal Thickness Criterion for a Hull Plate The **renewal thickness ($t_{\text{ren}}$)** is the minimum acceptable as-built thickness required to retain class. The surveyor compares measured thickness ($t_{\text{measured}}$) against $t_{\text{ren}}$. **Step 1 — Identify Rule Minimum (Net Scantling):** The classification society rules (e.g., DNV Pt.3 Ch.4 or LR Pt.3 Ch.3) prescribe a minimum net plate thickness $t_{\text{net}}$ based on: - Plate location (bottom, side, deck, bulkhead), - Stiffener spacing $l$ (m), - Design pressure $p$ (kN/m²), - Plate yield stress $\sigma_y$ (N/mm²). Using the classical panel plate-buckling formula: $$t_{\text{net}} = s \cdot l \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k \cdot \sigma_y}}$$ Where: - $s$ = safety factor (typically 1.0–1.2 for class minimum), - $k$ = buckling coefficient (typically 4.0 for simply supported plates under uniform compression). **Step 2 — Add the Corrosion Allowance:** The design corrosion allowance $t_{\text{corr}}$ is added to obtain the renewal (as-built) thickness: $$\boxed{t_{\text{ren}} = t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{corr}}}$$ **Step 3 — Establish Renewal Decision:** If $t_{\text{measured}} < t_{\text{ren}}$, then the plate is **renewed** (replaced or built up by welding). **Step 4 — Substantial Corrosion Threshold:** A plate is flagged for "substantial corrosion" when wastage exceeds **75%** of the corrosion allowance: $$t_{\text{wastage, observed}} = t_{\text{as-built}} - t_{\text{measured}}$$ $$t_{\text{wastage, observed}} \geq 0.75 \cdot t_{\text{corr}} \implies \text{Substantial Corrosion}$$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:** A side shell plate has: - Design net thickness: $t_{\text{net}} = 12.0$ mm - Corrosion allowance: $t_{\text{corr}} = 2.0$ mm - As-built thickness: $t_{\text{as-built}} = t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{corr}} = 14.0$ mm - Measured thickness at survey: $t_{\text{measured}} = 12.4$ mm Compute wastage: $$t_{\text{wastage}} = 14.0 - 12.4 = 1.6 \; \text{mm}$$ Check substantial corrosion threshold: $$0.75 \cdot t_{\text{corr}} = 0.75 \cdot 2.0 = 1.5 \; \text{mm}$$ Since $1.6 \text{ mm} \geq 1.5 \text{ mm}$, the plate exhibits **substantial corrosion**. The surveyor must: 1. Measure adjacent plates within 1 m radius. 2. Apply protective coating (if not already coated). 3. Schedule replacement within next survey cycle. Check renewal: $$t_{\text{ren}} = 12.0 + 2.0 = 14.0 \; \text{mm}$$ Since $t_{\text{measured}} = 12.4 \; \text{mm} < t_{\text{ren}} = 14.0 \; \text{mm}$, the plate is **below renewal threshold** but flagged for **enhanced monitoring**. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Scope of Survey vs. Ship Age (UR Z10.1) | Vessel Age (years) | Annual Survey | Intermediate Survey | Special Survey Scope | Close-up Survey % | |--------------------|---------------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------------| | 0 – 5 | Visual, general | Selected tanks | Full survey + gauging | ~25% of critical tanks | | 5 – 10 | Visual + thickness trend | Ballast tanks internal | Full + extended gauging | ~50% of ballast tanks | | 10 – 15 | Visual + NDT | All ballast tanks | Full + extensive NDT | ~75% of all tanks | | $\geq$ 15 | Continuous monitoring | All spaces + UT | Full + 100% thickness | 100% of ballast & critical tanks | ### 3.3 Logic Flow for a Periodic Survey Decision (Textual Derivation) 1. **Initiate Survey:** Surveyor enters vessel; verifies identity (IMO No., Class register). 2. **Documentation Review:** Approved drawings, previous survey reports, operating profile. 3. **Visual Examination:** Hull exterior (in drydock), interior spaces, accessible areas. 4. **Thickness Measurement:** Ultrasonic Thickness Gauging (UTG) at grid points per class procedure. 5. **Compare to Renewal Criteria:** Apply $t_{\text{ren}}$ test and substantial corrosion test. 6. **Issue Conditions:** Memorandum, Recommendation, or Condition of Class (CoC). 7. **Continuous Survey Planning (if applicable):** Schedule next partial survey. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Periodic Survey Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Survey Trigger
Annual or Intermediate or Special] --> nodeB[Documentation Review
Approved Drawings and Class Records] nodeB --> nodeC{Vessel Age
less than 5 years?} nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Visual Hull Examination
General Scope] nodeC -- No --> nodeE{Vessel Age
5 to 15 years?} nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF[Enhanced Visual Plus
Thickness Gauging] nodeE -- No --> nodeG[Vessel Age over 15
Full Close-up plus 100 percent UTG] nodeD --> nodeH[Thickness Measurement
UTG at Grid Points] nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI{Compare Measured
to Renewal Thickness} nodeI -- Below Renewal --> nodeJ[Issue Condition of Class
Schedule Renewal] nodeI -- Within Limits --> nodeK[Issue No Condition
Continue Class] nodeJ --> nodeL[End of Survey
Update Class Register] nodeK --> nodeL ``` ### 4.2 Periodic Survey Cycle (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeY1[Year 1
Annual Survey 1] --> nodeY2[Year 2
Annual Survey 2] --> nodeY3[Year 3
Intermediate Survey] --> nodeY4[Year 4
Annual Survey 4] --> nodeY5[Year 5
Special Survey Renewal] nodeY5 -.-> nodeY6[Year 6
New Cycle Annual 1] ``` ### 4.3 Hull Zone Classification for Periodic Survey (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeZ1[Hull Structural Zones] --> nodeZ2[Zone 1
Bottom and Keel] nodeZ1 --> nodeZ3[Zone 2
Side Shell Lower] nodeZ1 --> nodeZ4[Zone 3
Side Shell Upper] nodeZ1 --> nodeZ5[Zone 4
Deck and Hatch Coaming] nodeZ1 --> nodeZ6[Zone 5
Transverse Bulkheads] nodeZ2 --> nodeZ2A[Critical for Grounding and Slamming] nodeZ3 --> nodeZ3A[Critical for Side Impact and Corrosion] nodeZ4 --> nodeZ4A[Critical for Fatigue and Sloshing] nodeZ5 --> nodeZ5A[Critical for Shear Stress] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note (KTU 2024 Adaptation):** > Per the V10 protocol, complex physical drawings of the ship hull are abstracted into **functional zone-block diagrams**. Each zone carries a unique IACS UR Z10.1 designation used by surveyors during close-up scope selection. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Periodic Survey" as applied to ship hull inspection. List any three types of periodic surveys conducted by classification societies.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Periodic Survey is a systematic, rule-based inspection of a ship's hull, machinery, and equipment carried out at predetermined intervals by a Classification Society Surveyor to verify continued compliance with class rules and statutory requirements. Three types of periodic surveys (1 mark each): 1. **Annual Survey** – conducted every year within a ±3 month window. 2. **Intermediate Survey** – conducted between 2nd and 3rd annual surveys of each cycle. 3. **Special Survey** – the most comprehensive survey conducted every 5 years for class renewal. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *What is the role of IACS in standardizing periodic surveys across classification societies?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** develops **Unified Requirements (UR)** — most notably the **UR Z-series (Z1, Z2, Z3, Z7, Z9, Z10)** — which harmonize survey procedures, intervals, and scope across all member societies. This ensures that a vessel classed by ABS receives an equivalent safety standard as one classed by DNV or LR, facilitating global maritime trade and uniform Port State Control acceptance (1.5 marks). IACS also issues Procedural Requirements (PR) and Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS) audits to ensure consistent surveyor performance (1.5 marks). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[INTERNAL CHOICE — KTU ESE Pattern: Answer ANY ONE full question]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various types of periodic surveys carried out under the IACS Unified Requirements. Discuss the survey windows, scope, and documentation requirements for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bottom plate of a bulk carrier has the following data: - Design net thickness, $t_{\text{net}} = 14.0$ mm - Corrosion allowance, $t_{\text{corr}} = 2.5$ mm - As-built thickness = 16.5 mm - Ultrasonic measured thickness at survey = 13.2 mm Determine: 1. The renewal thickness. 2. Whether the plate exhibits "substantial corrosion." 3. The surveyor's recommendation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** The IACS UR Z series defines the following periodic survey types: 1. **Annual Survey (UR Z1):** Conducted yearly within ±3 months of the anniversary date. Scope includes general visual examination of hull, machinery spaces, weather decks, hatch covers, and emergency equipment. Documentation reviewed: classification records, operating manual, previous survey reports. 2. **Intermediate Survey (UR Z7 / Z9):** Conducted between the 2nd and 3rd annual surveys of each 5-year Special Survey cycle. Includes internal examination of selected ballast tanks, peak tanks, cofferdams, and double-bottom spaces. Thickness measurements are performed on representative plates. Documentation: Survey Planning Document, previous thickness reports. 3. **Special Survey (UR Z3 / Z10):** Conducted every 5 years, within ±6 months of the due date. Most comprehensive — full hull examination in drydock, internal inspection of all tanks, comprehensive thickness gauging, NDT (UT, MPI, DPI) of critical welds. Renews the Class Certificate. Documentation: complete as-built drawings, full thickness database, Coating Technical File (CTF). 4. **Continuous Survey (UR Z11):** Alternative regime where hull and machinery are surveyed on a rolling basis. Requires an approved Continuous Survey Schedule agreed between Owner and Society. Each item surveyed at intervals not exceeding those prescribed for Special Survey. **Marking:** Each survey type ~1.5 marks; windows and documentation ~0.5 mark each. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Renewal Thickness:** $$t_{\text{ren}} = t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{corr}} = 14.0 + 2.5 = 16.5 \; \text{mm}$$ **Step 2 — Wastage Observed:** $$t_{\text{wastage}} = t_{\text{as-built}} - t_{\text{measured}} = 16.5 - 13.2 = 3.3 \; \text{mm}$$ **Step 3 — Substantial Corrosion Check:** $$0.75 \cdot t_{\text{corr}} = 0.75 \cdot 2.5 = 1.875 \; \text{mm}$$ Since $3.3 \; \text{mm} \geq 1.875 \; \text{mm}$, **substantial corrosion is established**. **Step 4 — Renewal Decision:** Since $t_{\text{measured}} = 13.2 \; \text{mm} < t_{\text{ren}} = 16.5 \; \text{mm}$, the plate is **below the renewal threshold**. **Step 5 — Surveyor's Recommendation:** 1. **Renew the plate** by inserting a new plate or doubler (insert plate). 2. Apply protective coating after renewal. 3. Investigate adjacent plates within 1 m radius. 4. Update the vessel's Thickness Database and report to Class. 5. Issue a **Condition of Class** with a due date within the next survey window. **Marking:** Renewal thickness (2 marks), substantial corrosion logic (2 marks), renewal decision (1 mark), recommendation (2 marks). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Differentiate between Classification Surveys and Statutory Surveys. Explain why both regimes operate in parallel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bulk carrier is 18 years old. The surveyor is preparing the scope for the Special Survey. 1. Determine the close-up survey scope per IACS UR Z10.1. 2. List the testing methods (NDT) typically required for a vessel of this age. 3. Explain the "substantial corrosion" criterion with its numerical threshold. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** | Aspect | Classification Survey | Statutory Survey | |--------|----------------------|------------------| | Authority | Classification Society (e.g., DNV) | Flag State Administration (e.g., DG Shipping, India) | | Legal basis | Voluntary (commercial contract) | Mandatory (international convention / national law) | | Standards | Society rules (e.g., DNV Pt.3) | SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, MLC | | Cert. issued | Class Certificate | Statutory Certificates (e.g., Safety Construction, Load Line) | | Frequency | Per UR Z schedule (Annual, Special) | Per convention (5 years, with annual endorsement) | | Surveyor | Class Surveyor | Flag State Inspector or "Recognized Organization" | **Why both operate in parallel (3 marks):** - **Different objectives:** Class focuses on structural and mechanical fitness (technical standards). Statutory focuses on safety, environmental, and labor compliance. - **Risk-based separation:** Class is owner-driven (insurance/H&M); Statutory is public-driven (PSC, flag control). - **Coordinated timing:** Both schedules are aligned (e.g., Load Line Renewal = Special Survey) to minimize vessel downtime. - **Recognized Organization (RO) framework:** Class societies are authorized by flag states to conduct statutory surveys on their behalf (per SOLAS Reg. II-1, etc.). --- **Model Solution (b):** **1. Close-up Survey Scope (UR Z10.1):** For a vessel **18 years old (≥ 15 years)**, the close-up survey must cover **100% of all ballast tanks and peak tanks**, plus critical structural areas in the side shell, deck, and transverse bulkheads in way of the engine room. **2. NDT Methods (3 marks):** - **Ultrasonic Testing (UT / UTG)** – thickness gauging. - **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** – surface crack detection on welds. - **Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)** – surface defects on non-magnetic materials. - **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** – for non-ferrous components. - **Radiographic Testing (RT)** – for critical butt welds (optional). **3. Substantial Corrosion Criterion:** Substantial corrosion is defined as **observed wastage ≥ 75% of the allowable corrosion allowance** ($t_{\text{corr}}$). When this threshold is exceeded, the surveyor must extend the thickness measurement scope, flag adjacent plates, and consider the structural member as a candidate for renewal even if the absolute measured thickness is still above the rule minimum. **Marking:** (a) Table 4 marks + parallel-operation 3 marks; (b) close-up 2 marks, NDT 3 marks, substantial corrosion 2 marks. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):** > 1. **Confusing "Annual" with "Special":** Annual is general visual + selected items; Special is full + drydock + UTG. Many students blur this — **lose 1–2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance formula:** $t_{\text{ren}} = t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{corr}}$. Writing only $t_{\text{net}}$ instead is a frequent error — **lose 2 marks**. > 3. **75% threshold missing:** Students often say "corrosion is bad" without quantifying. **Always quote 0.75 × $t_{\text{corr}}$** — **lose 2 marks**. > 4. **Survey window errors:** Annual = ±3 months; Special = ±6 months. Mixing them up is a Class-action mistake. > 5. **No mention of IACS UR Z-series:** Examiners expect at least one UR reference (Z10, Z7, etc.) for full marks in Part B (a). > 6. **Skipping surveyor's recommendation:** In numerical Part B (b), the final 2 marks require an engineering recommendation (CoC, renewal scope) — not just a number. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - A **Periodic Survey** is rule-based, time-bound, and conducted by a **Class Surveyor** to ensure ongoing hull fitness. - **Three main types:** Annual (±3 months), Intermediate (between 2nd & 3rd Annual), Special (±6 months, every 5 years). - **Continuous Survey** is an alternative rolling regime under UR Z11. - **IACS UR Z10.1** governs hull structural surveys; **Z1** annual, **Z7** machinery, **Z17** ESP (Enhanced Survey Programme) for tankers. - **Renewal thickness:** $t_{\text{ren}} = t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{corr}}$. - **Substantial corrosion threshold:** wastage $\geq 0.75 \cdot t_{\text{corr}}$. - **Close-up scope increases with age:** 25% (0–5 yrs) → 50% (5–10 yrs) → 75% (10–15 yrs) → 100% (≥ 15 yrs). - **NDT methods for old vessels:** UT (gauging), MPI (cracks), DPI (surface), ET, RT. - **Classification ≠ Statutory:** Class is commercial; Statutory is mandatory under conventions. - **Survey windows matter:** Late surveys trigger Class suspension, insurance void, and PSC detention. - **Always issue a recommendation** in numerical problems — never end with just a number. - **Recognize IACS societies** — ABS, DNV, LR, BV, ClassNK, KR, RINA, IRS (India), RS, CCS, PRS, CRS. - **Indian flag vessels** are governed by the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** and surveyed by **IRS** or IACS societies recognized by **DG Shipping**.

topics.title: Intermediate Survey
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intermediate Survey

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intermediate Survey (IS)** is a periodical hull and machinery survey, conducted at the midway point between two consecutive **Special Surveys (SS)**, mandated by Classification Societies operating under the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** Unified Requirements (UR Z7 / Z7.1 / Z10). It is designed to verify that the structural integrity, watertight integrity, and machinery condition of the vessel remain within acceptable limits during the 5-year Special Survey cycle.

The Intermediate Survey is **not** a full Special Survey, but a representative sampling survey. Its scope is determined by:
- **Vessel age** (measured from the date of build)
- **Vessel type** (Bulk Carrier, Oil Tanker, Chemical Tanker, Container Ship, General Cargo)
- **Structural criticality** (CSR - Common Structural Rules applicability)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Human Health Check-up Model:**
> Think of a ship's hull like a human body. A **Special Survey** is like a complete annual master health check-up involving full body scans, blood tests, and deep diagnostics. The **Intermediate Survey** is the "mid-cycle review" — your doctor doesn't re-run every test, but he listens to your heart (machinery), checks visible moles/skin (hull plating from outside), runs specific blood panels (thickness measurements), and asks about symptoms reported by you (crew reports). It catches problems that develop *between* the major check-ups.

### 1.3 Survey Position in the IACS Cycle

In the standard IACS 5-year cycle, the Intermediate Survey falls in this hierarchy:

- **Annual Survey (AS)** — every year
- **Intermediate Survey (IS)** — at years **2.5 to 3** (typically aligned with the 2nd or 3rd Annual Survey)
- **Special Survey (SS)** — every **5 years** (with possible Continuous Survey - CS - extension)

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Special Survey Interval:** **5 years** (for most steel-hulled vessels)
> - **Intermediate Survey Window:** between **2.5 and 3 years** from last SS
> - **Minimum Dry-docking Period:** as per Class rules (typically 5–14 days depending on vessel size)
> - **Annual Survey Thickness Measurement Threshold (IACS UR Z7.1):** when wastage exceeds **75%** of the original build margin, additional measurements are required.
> - **Steel Density used for weight calculation:** **7.85 t/m³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scope Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Intermediate Survey

The Intermediate Survey exists because the Special Survey interval (5 years) is too long to be left unchecked. Corrosion, fatigue, and wastage progress continuously. The IS provides:

1. **Early detection** of progressing structural wastage before it reaches critical limits.
2. **Verification** that the vessel has not suffered unforeseen damage between major surveys.
3. **Compliance continuity** with Class and Flag State requirements (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line).
4. **Risk-based sampling** — Class societies tailor scope using vessel age and IACS UR Z7.1.

### 2.2 Breakdown of Survey Operations (Logic Steps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Documentation Review (Pre-Survey)**
> Examine previous survey reports, Thickness Measurement (TM) files, Condition Evaluation Reports (CER), and any damage history.

> **Step 2 — Internal Hull Examination**
> Inspect all cargo holds (bulkers), tanks (tankers), ballast tanks, and cofferdams. Focus on areas with historically high wastage.

> **Step 3 — External Hull Examination (In Water or Dry)**
> Underwater hull inspection by divers or ROV. Inspectors look for corrosion, propeller/shaft damage, sea-cocks, and hull appendages.

> **Step 4 — Selected Thickness Measurements**
> Take gauging readings at representative points (transverse sections, critical structural locations). Compare with original build thickness.

> **Step 5 — Machinery Spaces & Electrical Survey**
> Limited scope — main propulsion, auxiliaries, steering gear, boilers.

> **Step 6 — Reporting and Condition Coding**
> Findings coded as **C1 (minor)**, **C2 (recommendation)**, **C3 (condition)**, or **C4 (critical)** per IACS procedural guidelines.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|-----------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| Remaining Thickness Margin | $\text{RTM} = t_{\text{actual}} - t_{\text{min}}$ | mm | Where $t_{\text{min}}$ is the rule minimum allowable thickness |
| Wastage Percentage | $W\% = \dfrac{t_{\text{original}} - t_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{original}}} \times 100$ | % | Triggers closer survey if $W\% > 75\%$ |
| Section Modulus (for buckling check) | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\text{max}}}$ | cm³ | Used in CSR scantling verification |
| Annual Wastage Rate | $w_{\text{annual}} = \dfrac{W\%}{\text{Years since last SS}}$ | %/yr | Used for predicting next SS acceptance |
| Total Weight of Steel Renewed | $W_{\text{steel}} = \rho_{\text{steel}} \times \sum (A_i \times t_i)$ | tonnes | $\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7.85$ t/m³ |
| Corrosion Allowance | $t_{\text{design}} = t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{corr}}$ | mm | Typically **0.5 mm to 2.0 mm** per side depending on compartment |
| Transverse Section Spacing | $L_s / N_{\text{sections}}$ | m | IACS UR Z7.1 requires a minimum distribution |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

In modern shipping, the Intermediate Survey is being **digitally transformed**. Classification Societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ABS, ClassNK) now use:
- **Digital twin hull monitoring** (real-time strain and corrosion sensors)
- **Remote Inspection Techniques (RIT)** — ROVs and drone-based close-up surveys
- **Predictive analytics** — extrapolating wastage curves using $\text{RTM}$ data

This reduces the **dry-docking time** from 14 days down to 7 days in many cases, saving shipowners approximately **USD 50,000–150,000 per day** in off-hire costs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Determining Renewal Threshold from Wastage Data

**Problem Context:** A bulk carrier's hopper tank plating has an original thickness of **18.0 mm**. During the Intermediate Survey at year 2.5, the gauged thickness reads **15.2 mm**. The Class rule minimum allowable thickness (after accounting for corrosion addition) is **12.0 mm**. Determine whether renewal is required NOW, and predict the wastage rate.

**Step 1 — Compute Current Wastage Percentage**

We define the current wastage $W\%$ as the loss in thickness expressed as a percentage of the original thickness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W\% &= \frac{t_{\text{original}} - t_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{original}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{18.0 - 15.2}{18.0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2.8}{18.0} \times 100 \\
&= 15.555\ldots \%
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding to two decimal places, we obtain **$W\% = 15.56\%$**.

**Step 2 — Compute Remaining Thickness Margin (RTM)**

The RTM is the difference between the actual measured thickness and the rule minimum allowable thickness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTM} &= t_{\text{actual}} - t_{\text{min}} \\
&= 15.2 - 12.0 \\
&= 3.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision Logic (IACS UR Z7.1 trigger)**

The wastage observed is **15.56%**, which is significantly below the **75%** trigger threshold. No immediate renewal is mandated. The plate still has a comfortable **3.2 mm** margin above the minimum.

**Step 4 — Compute Annual Wastage Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{annual}} &= \frac{W\%}{\text{Years since last SS}} \\
&= \frac{15.56}{2.5} \\
&= 6.224 \text{ \%/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Projected Wastage at Next Special Survey (Year 5)**

Assuming linear continuation of wastage, the projected wastage at the next SS (2.5 years from now) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W\%_{\text{projected, SS}} &= W\%_{\text{current}} + (w_{\text{annual}} \times \Delta t) \\
&= 15.56 + (6.224 \times 2.5) \\
&= 15.56 + 15.56 \\
&= 31.12 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

The projected thickness at the next SS would be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{projected}} &= t_{\text{original}} \times \left(1 - \frac{W\%_{\text{projected,SS}}}{100}\right) \\
&= 18.0 \times (1 - 0.3112) \\
&= 18.0 \times 0.6888 \\
&= 12.40 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict**

Since $12.40 \text{ mm} > t_{\text{min}} = 12.0 \text{ mm}$, the plate is **expected to pass** the next Special Survey without renewal, provided the corrosion rate remains linear and no localized pitting is observed.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Thickness Measurement Sampling (IACS UR Z7.1)

The table below summarizes the minimum TM scope for an Intermediate Survey on a **bulk carrier** older than 10 years.

| Structural Component | Sampling Requirement | Transverse Sections | Reason |
|----------------------|----------------------|---------------------|--------|
| Deck Plating | Every plate in each selected section | 3 sections | Deck is a primary load-bearing surface |
| Bottom Plating | Every plate in each selected section | 3 sections | High exposure to corrosion |
| Side Shell Plating | Every 4th plate | 3 sections | Moderate sampling |
| Hopper Tank Plates | Every plate | 3 sections (incl. hopper knuckles) | Known high-wastage area |
| Transverse Bulkheads | Every 4th plate per bulkhead | All bulkheads in selected sections | Critical for watertight integrity |
| Brackets and Gussets | Selected critical locations | As per drawing | Stress concentration zones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about TM scope, always cite the **vessel type**, **vessel age**, and **specific IACS UR** as your authority. Generic answers lose marks.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Survey Types

| Survey Type | Frequency | Dry-dock Required? | TM Scope | Internal Exam Scope |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------|----------|---------------------|
| Annual Survey (AS) | Yearly | Optional | Trend monitoring only | Selected spaces |
| Intermediate Survey (IS) | Once between SS | **Yes (in water or dry)** | Substantial — per UR Z7.1 | All accessible spaces |
| Special Survey (SS) | Every 5 years | **Yes (full dry-dock)** | Comprehensive — 100% | 100% of all spaces |
| Continuous Survey (CS) | Rolling programme | Linked to specific items | Component-based | Component-based |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Intermediate Survey Workflow (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Survey Notification to Owner] --> B1[Pre-Survey Documentation Review]
    B1 --> C1[Survey Planning and Scope Definition]
    C1 --> D1[Internal Hull Examination]
    C1 --> D2[External Hull Underwater Inspection]
    C1 --> D3[Thickness Measurement TM Campaign]
    D1 --> E1[Findings Documentation]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1{Wastage within limits?}
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[Survey Concluded with No Conditions]
    F1 -- No --> H1[Issue C2 C3 or C4 Condition]
    H1 --> I1[Repair and Re-inspection]
    I1 --> J1[Issue Interim Class Certificate]
    G1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Record in Class Database]
```

### 4.2 IACS Survey Cycle Position Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Year 0 Special Survey] --> nodeB[Year 1 Annual]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Year 2 Annual]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Year 2.5 to 3 Intermediate Survey]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Year 4 Annual]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Year 5 Special Survey]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Year 6 Annual]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Year 7 Annual]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Year 7.5 to 8 Intermediate Survey]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Intermediate Survey Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    compA[Documentation Module] --> compB[Survey Execution Module]
    compB --> compC[Internal Exam]
    compB --> compD[External Exam]
    compB --> compE[Thickness Measurement]
    compC --> compF[Evaluation Engine]
    compD --> compF
    compE --> compF
    compF --> compG[Condition Coding C1 C2 C3 C4]
    compG --> compH[Report Generation]
    compH --> compI[Class Database Update]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define Intermediate Survey. When is it conducted during a vessel's survey cycle?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Intermediate Survey is a periodical survey conducted at the midway point between two consecutive Special Surveys, typically between the **2nd and 3rd Annual Survey** of the 5-year Class cycle. Its purpose is to verify the continued structural integrity and seaworthiness of the vessel through selected internal and external examinations, together with thickness measurements as required by IACS Unified Requirements. It is mandated by Class rules and serves as a check on wastage progression between Special Surveys.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *State the IACS wastage trigger percentage for additional thickness measurements during an Intermediate Survey.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
According to IACS UR Z7.1, when the wastage of any structural member is found to have reached **75%** of the original build margin during an Intermediate Survey, additional thickness measurements shall be carried out on that member and adjacent structural areas to confirm the extent of wastage. This trigger ensures that progressively corroded areas receive closer scrutiny and that appropriate remedial action can be planned before the next Special Survey.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** *During an Intermediate Survey of a bulk carrier aged 12 years, the following readings were obtained on the deck plating of section 3:*

| Location | Original Thickness (mm) | Actual Thickness (mm) |
|----------|-------------------------|------------------------|
| Plate P-1 | 20.0 | 17.2 |
| Plate P-2 | 20.0 | 14.8 |
| Plate P-3 | 20.0 | 16.5 |

*The rule minimum allowable thickness for this deck plating is 13.0 mm.*

**(a)** Calculate the wastage percentage and remaining thickness margin for each plate. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Identify whether any plate requires immediate renewal and predict whether the deck plating will pass the next Special Survey assuming linear corrosion progression and 2.5 years remaining in the cycle. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** For each plate, compute $W\% = \frac{t_{\text{orig}} - t_{\text{act}}}{t_{\text{orig}}} \times 100$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plate P-1: } W\% &= \frac{20.0 - 17.2}{20.0} \times 100 = 14.00\% \\
\text{RTM}_1 &= 17.2 - 13.0 = 4.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plate P-2: } W\% &= \frac{20.0 - 14.8}{20.0} \times 100 = 26.00\% \\
\text{RTM}_2 &= 14.8 - 13.0 = 1.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plate P-3: } W\% &= \frac{20.0 - 16.5}{20.0} \times 100 = 17.50\% \\
\text{RTM}_3 &= 16.5 - 13.0 = 3.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b):** Renewal Decision Logic:

Plate P-2 has $W\% = 26\% < 75\%$ trigger, and $\text{RTM}_2 = 1.8 \text{ mm} > 0$. **No immediate renewal mandated by IS trigger.** However, Plate P-2 is approaching the critical limit, so a **C3 condition (recommend renew at next SS)** should be issued.

Predicting the next SS (2.5 years away):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{annual, P-2}} &= \frac{26.00}{2.5} = 10.40 \text{ \%/yr} \\
W\%_{\text{projected, SS}} &= 26.00 + (10.40 \times 2.5) = 52.00\% \\
t_{\text{projected, P-2}} &= 20.0 \times (1 - 0.52) = 9.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $9.6 \text{ mm} < t_{\text{min}} = 13.0 \text{ mm}$, **Plate P-2 will FAIL the next Special Survey** and must be renewed before or at the next SS. Plates P-1 and P-3 will comfortably pass.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *(Alternative choice)*

**(a)** Explain the scope of an Intermediate Survey for a hull, listing the major areas examined internally and externally. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the difference between an Intermediate Survey and a Special Survey in terms of frequency, dry-docking requirement, and thickness measurement coverage. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** The Intermediate Survey scope includes:

**Internal Examination:**
- All cargo holds (bulk carriers), cargo tanks (tankers), and pump rooms.
- Ballast tanks, including double-bottom tanks, wing tanks, and forepeak/aftpeak tanks.
- Cofferdams, void spaces, and tunnels.
- Engine rooms and auxiliary machinery spaces (limited).

**External Examination (in water or dry-dock):**
- Underwater hull plating, including bottom, side shell, and bilge keels.
- Propeller, rudder, shaft brackets, and stern frame.
- Sea-cocks, overboard discharges, and hull penetrations.
- Cathodic protection (sacrificial anodes / ICCP system) condition.

**Selected Thickness Measurements:** Conducted on representative transverse sections per IACS UR Z7.1, focusing on areas known for high wastage rates.

**Part (b):**

| Aspect | Intermediate Survey | Special Survey |
|--------|---------------------|----------------|
| Frequency | Once between two SS (~2.5 to 3 years) | Every **5 years** |
| Dry-docking | Often done in water (divers) but dry-dock preferred for older vessels | **Mandatory full dry-dock** |
| TM Coverage | Selected sections (sampling) per UR Z7.1 | **100% comprehensive** |
| Internal Exam | Selected spaces | All spaces (100%) |
| Class Certificate Issued | Interim / Continuing | Full renewal |
| Cost to Owner | Lower | Significantly higher |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often confuse the **Intermediate Survey** with the **Annual Survey**. Marks are lost if you state that the Intermediate Survey is conducted yearly. The correct position is **between the 2nd and 3rd Annual Survey**, i.e., around **2.5 to 3 years** from the last Special Survey. Also, do **not** claim that the Intermediate Survey requires full dry-docking — it can be conducted in-water for certain vessel ages, although dry-docking is preferred for vessels above **15 years**. Always cite **IACS UR Z7 / Z7.1** in your answers to earn the "authority" marks examiners award.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Intermediate Survey** is positioned at the **midpoint** of the 5-year Special Survey cycle.
- Governed by **IACS Unified Requirements UR Z7 and UR Z7.1**.
- Scope covers: documentation review, internal exam, external exam (in-water or dry), selected **thickness measurements**.
- **Trigger threshold for additional TM:** **75%** of original build margin wastage.
- Condition codes follow the system: **C1, C2, C3, C4** (per IACS Procedural Requirements).
- The **75% trigger** is a key KTU exam point — memorize it.
- Key formulas:
  - $W\% = \frac{t_{\text{orig}} - t_{\text{act}}}{t_{\text{orig}}} \times 100$
  - $\text{RTM} = t_{\text{act}} - t_{\text{min}}$
  - $w_{\text{annual}} = W\% / \text{Years since last SS}$
- **Steel density** for weight calculations: **$\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7.85$ t/m³**.
- For vessels over **10 years**, the IS scope expands considerably.
- For vessels over **15 years**, dry-docking during IS is often mandatory.
- The Intermediate Survey does **not** issue a fresh Class Certificate — it issues an **Interim/Continuing recommendation**.
- Modern trend: **Remote Inspection Techniques (RIT)** are increasingly accepted as equivalent to physical surveys.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Renewal Survey
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Renewal Survey

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Renewal Survey (RS)** is a comprehensive, systematic, and class-mandated examination of a ship's hull, machinery, and equipment, conducted at prescribed intervals (typically aligned with the **Special Survey cycle**) to verify continued fitness for purpose, ensure compliance with **Classification Society Rules**, and to renew or repair components that have reached the limits of their **renewal thickness** or service life.

According to the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** framework adopted by major societies such as **Lloyd's Register (LR)**, **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)**, **Bureau Veritas (BV)**, **DNV**, and **ClassNK**, a Renewal Survey is the most extensive periodic survey in a vessel's operational life cycle. It is the engineering equivalent of a "complete structural and machinery audit" of the ship.

In **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology for *OENST832 – Hull Inspection*, the Renewal Survey falls under the broader category of **Class Surveys** and acts as the "milestone event" around which other surveys (Annual, Intermediate, Docking) are organised.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Comprehensive vs. Routine Medical Check-up**

Imagine your car (or your body):
- An **Annual Survey** is like a *yearly health check-up* — quick vitals, oil level, brakes check.
- An **Intermediate Survey** is like an *18-month detailed review* — additional blood tests, scans.
- A **Renewal Survey** is like a *complete hospital full-body overhaul* — every organ scanned, every joint X-rayed, every system dismantled and inspected, worn parts replaced.

When a ship reaches the **5-year Special Survey (SS)** mark, the classification society cannot simply trust the past 5 years of records. They must physically open, measure, test, and verify *every critical component*. Any steel plate whose thickness has reduced below the **renewal thickness** must be renewed (replaced). This is the essence of a Renewal Survey.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!WARNING] > **Key Numerical Anchors (per IACS S21 / UR Z17):**
> - **Renewal Survey Cycle (Special Survey):** every **5 years**
> - **Minimum Renewal Thickness:** generally **75 %** of the original **as-built thickness** (varies by structural member)
> - **Substantial Corrosion Limit:** wastage exceeding **75 %** of original scantlings
> - **Annual Rate of Wastage (ARW):** typically **0.10 mm – 0.30 mm per year** for ballast tanks
> - **Marginal plating renewal trigger:** thickness $\leq t_{renewal}$ where $t_{renewal} = 0.75 \times t_{as-built}$

---

**Key Classification Society Bodies:**

| Body | Full Name | Survey Notation |
|---|---|---|
| IACS | International Association of Classification Societies | UR Z-series |
| LR | Lloyd's Register | Special Survey (SS) |
| ABS | American Bureau of Shipping | Continuous / Special |
| DNV | Det Norske Veritas | Survey after construction |
| BV | Bureau Veritas | Special Survey (S) |
| ClassNK | Nippon Kaiji Kyokai | Special Survey (SS) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Anatomy of a Renewal Survey

A Renewal Survey is executed in **three coordinated streams**, each governed by separate IACS Unified Requirements (URs):

### Stream A — Hull Structural Renewal Survey
- Covers **shell plating, deck plating, bulkheads, frames, girders, brackets**
- **Thickness measurements (TM)** of all primary and secondary structural members
- Comparison of measured thickness ($t_{measured}$) against:
  - **As-built thickness** ($t_{as-built}$)
  - **Renewal thickness** ($t_{renewal}$)
  - **Allowable wastage** ($\Delta t_{allow}$)
- **Close-up inspection** of critical areas (e.g., side shell amidships, hopper corners, bilge keels)
- **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing):** ultrasonic thickness gauging (UT), MPI (Magnetic Particle Inspection), dye penetrant test

### Stream B — Machinery Renewal Survey
- Disassembly and inspection of **main engine, auxiliary engines, boilers, shafting, propellers**
- **Crankshaft deflection check**, **bearing inspection**, **cylinder liner gauging**
- Testing of **safety devices**, **alarms**, **automation systems**
- Renewal of components reaching fatigue or wear life limits

### Stream C — Electrical & Automation Renewal Survey
- **Insulation resistance testing** (megger testing) of all circuits
- Function testing of **emergency generator, steering gear, fire pumps**
- Verification of **emergency shutdowns (ESD)** and **integrated automation system (IAS)**

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rationale:**
> A ship operates in a **highly corrosive marine environment** with **cyclic wave loading**, **thermal stress**, and **mechanical wear**. Over 5 years:
> - Wastage reduces plate thickness → loss of structural strength
> - Fatigue cracks initiate at stress-concentration zones
> - Corrosion pits act as crack initiation sites
>
> The Renewal Survey is the **engineering safeguard** that quantifies these degradations and mandates remediation *before* failure.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU ESE papers.**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $t_{renewal} = 0.75 \times t_{as-built}$ | Renewal thickness (standard 25 % wastage rule) | mm |
| $\Delta t_{wastage} = t_{as-built} - t_{measured}$ | Total wastage to date | mm |
| $\% \text{Wastage} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{wastage}}{t_{as-built}} \times 100$ | Percentage wastage | % |
| $ARW = \dfrac{\Delta t_{wastage}}{Age_{years}}$ | Annual Rate of Wastage | mm/yr |
| $t_{remaining} = t_{measured} - t_{renewal}$ | Margin before mandatory renewal | mm |
| $Y_{remaining} = \dfrac{t_{measured} - t_{renewal}}{ARW}$ | Remaining years of fitness (forecast) | years |
| $P_{margin} = \dfrac{t_{measured}}{t_{renewal}} \times 100$ | Percentage margin over renewal threshold | % |
| $L_{corrosion\,addition} = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | Time-dependent corrosion wastage (k = corrosion factor) | mm |

> **NOTE on absolute values:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ in formulas, use $\vert \text{x} \vert$ or $\mid \text{x} \mid$ to avoid markdown table conflicts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Renewal Survey framework is the **backbone of maritime safety regulation**. Without it:
- Aging tankers would suffer catastrophic structural failure
- Bulk carriers would collapse (e.g., **MV Derbyshire** disaster — 1980 — was a key driver for stricter renewal surveys)
- Insurance premiums would become unquantifiable

It is the **single most important economic event** in a vessel's 25–30 year operational life, often costing **USD 1–5 million** and taking 1–3 months in dry dock.

---

## 2.5 Survey Interval Philosophy

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{RS} &= n \times 5 \text{ years} \quad \text{where } n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \\
T_{Special} &= \text{(Schedule-based, every 5 years)} \\
T_{Annual} &= 12 \text{ months} \\
T_{Intermediate} &= \text{Within } 2.5 \text{ years of Special Survey (or as per class rules)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Where:**
> - $T_{RS}$ = Renewal Survey interval
> - The **1st Renewal Survey** occurs at age 5 years
> - The **2nd Renewal Survey** at age 10 years
> - Subsequent surveys every **5 years** up to scrapping
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Renewal Thickness Determination

### Problem Setup
A ship's bottom shell plating was originally built with a thickness $t_{as-built} = 22 \text{ mm}$. After 5 years of service, the measured thickness is $t_{measured} = 17.8 \text{ mm}$. Determine:
1. The **renewal thickness** threshold
2. The **percentage wastage**
3. The **margin over renewal**
4. The **annual rate of wastage**
5. The **predicted remaining service years** if ARW continues

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1 — Renewal Thickness Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{renewal} &= 0.75 \times t_{as-built} \\
t_{renewal} &= 0.75 \times 22 \text{ mm} \\
t_{renewal} &= 16.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The 25 % wastage rule is the IACS standard maximum permitted reduction before renewal is mandatory.

#### Step 2 — Total Wastage to Date

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{wastage} &= t_{as-built} - t_{measured} \\
\Delta t_{wastage} &= 22.0 - 17.8 \\
\Delta t_{wastage} &= 4.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Percentage Wastage

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Wastage} &= \frac{\Delta t_{wastage}}{t_{as-built}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4.2}{22.0} \times 100 \\
&= 19.09 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* 19.09 % wastage is **below** the 25 % renewal threshold → plate passes.

#### Step 4 — Margin Over Renewal

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{margin} &= t_{measured} - t_{renewal} \\
&= 17.8 - 16.5 \\
&= 1.3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Annual Rate of Wastage

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARW &= \frac{\Delta t_{wastage}}{Age_{years}} \\
&= \frac{4.2 \text{ mm}}{5 \text{ years}} \\
&= 0.84 \text{ mm/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Remaining Years of Fitness

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{remaining} &= \frac{t_{margin}}{ARW} \\
&= \frac{1.3 \text{ mm}}{0.84 \text{ mm/yr}} \\
&= 1.548 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Engineering Verdict:* Plate has only **~1.55 years of safe margin** before renewal becomes mandatory. **Recommend renewal at next docking.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Substantial Corrosion Threshold

### Problem
A hopper tank side shell plate has $t_{as-built} = 18 \text{ mm}$. At a 5-year Special Survey, wastage is found to be **4.8 mm**. Determine if "substantial corrosion" is triggered.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Wastage} &= \frac{4.8}{18} \times 100 = 26.67 \text{ \%} \\
\text{Since } 26.67 \text{ \%} > 25 \text{ \% threshold} &\rightarrow \text{Substantial Corrosion} \\
t_{measured} &= 18 - 4.8 = 13.2 \text{ mm} \\
t_{renewal} &= 0.75 \times 18 = 13.5 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Since } t_{measured} = 13.2 \text{ mm} < t_{renewal} = 13.5 \text{ mm} &\rightarrow \text{RENEWAL MANDATORY}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Measured Thickness ($t_{measured}$) | Action |
|---|---|
| $t_{measured} > t_{as-built} - 0.20 \cdot t_{as-built}$ | Acceptable — minor wastage |
| $t_{as-built} - 0.25 \cdot t_{as-built} < t_{measured} \leq t_{as-built} - 0.20 \cdot t_{as-built}$ | Moderate wastage — closer TM interval |
| $t_{measured} \leq t_{as-built} - 0.25 \cdot t_{as-built}$ | **RENEW** — substantial corrosion limit reached |
| $t_{measured} < t_{as-built} - 0.30 \cdot t_{as-built}$ | **IMMEDIATE** renewal before sailing |

---

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example — Continuous Survey Conversion

A vessel owner wants to convert from **Cycle Survey (every 5 years)** to **Continuous Survey** scheme. The hull structure is divided into **5 zones** for sequential annual inspection.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 5 \text{ years} \\
N_{zones} &= 5 \quad \text{(e.g., Fwd, Mid-Fwd, Midship, Mid-Aft, Aft)} \\
T_{zone\,interval} &= \frac{T_{cycle}}{N_{zones}} = \frac{5}{5} = 1 \text{ year/zone}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Each year, **one zone** undergoes complete Renewal Survey, and within any 5-year cycle the entire hull is examined. This balances ship downtime and safety verification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
