# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "Renewal Survey" as applied to a merchant vessel. State its typical interval and the percentage wastage that triggers mandatory renewal.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Renewal Survey** is a comprehensive class survey conducted at intervals of **5 years**, aligned with the **Special Survey cycle**, during which the entire hull, machinery, and electrical systems of the vessel are thoroughly inspected, measured, and tested to verify continued compliance with classification society rules.

The typical interval is **every 5 years**, and the **mandatory renewal threshold** is reached when the wastage exceeds **25 %** of the original as-built thickness, i.e., when the measured thickness falls to or below **75 %** of the as-built value.

*(Marks: Definition 1, Interval 1, Threshold 1)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List four major components examined during the hull structural part of a Renewal Survey.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major components examined are:

1. **Shell plating** (bottom, side, and deck)
2. **Internal structural members** (frames, floors, girders, brackets)
3. **Watertight and oiltight bulkheads**
4. **Critical structural connections** (hopper corners, bilge keels, bracket toes)

*(Marks: 1 per component, 0.75 each if explained)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Detailed Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **scope and objectives** of a Renewal Survey for the hull of a bulk carrier, with reference to the relevant **IACS Unified Requirements**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A side shell plate of an oil tanker has the following data:
- As-built thickness, $t_{as-built} = 24$ mm
- Measured thickness after 8 years, $t_{measured} = 19.5$ mm
- Vessel age = 8 years

Calculate: **(i)** Renewal thickness, **(ii)** Total wastage, **(iii)** Percentage wastage, **(iv)** Annual rate of wastage, and **(v)** Remaining years before mandatory renewal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Scope and Objectives of Renewal Survey (7 marks)**

The **Renewal Survey** is the most comprehensive periodic class survey. Under **IACS UR Z17** (Hull Surveys for Oil Tankers) and **UR Z23** (Hull Surveys for Bulk Carriers), the objectives are:

| S.No. | Objective | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Verify structural integrity | Confirm hull fitness for continued service |
| 2 | Quantify wastage | Measure thickness reduction in all primary members |
| 3 | Detect defects | Identify cracks, corrosion, deformations |
| 4 | Mandate renewals | Replace members reaching renewal thickness |
| 5 | Update records | Maintain thickness measurement database |
| 6 | Issue/endorse class | Confirm vessel maintains class status |

**Scope includes:**
- **Overall survey** of all hull structures
- **Close-up inspection** at critical areas (side shell amidships, hopper corners, transverse bulkheads)
- **Thickness measurement** of representative plates per zone
- **NDT testing** (ultrasonic, MPI, dye penetrant)
- **Ballast tank coating assessment**
- **Review of operating manuals** for corrosion prevention

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Renewal Thickness:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{renewal} &= 0.75 \times t_{as-built} = 0.75 \times 24 = 18 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Wastage:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{wastage} &= 24 - 19.5 = 4.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage Wastage:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% W &= \frac{4.5}{24} \times 100 = 18.75 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Annual Rate of Wastage:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
ARW &= \frac{4.5}{8} = 0.5625 \text{ mm/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Remaining Years:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{margin} &= 19.5 - 18.0 = 1.5 \text{ mm} \\
Y_{remaining} &= \frac{1.5}{0.5625} = 2.667 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** Plate has ~2.67 years margin. **Plan renewal at next docking survey.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Cycle Survey**, **Continuous Survey**, and **Special Survey**. Discuss the **advantages of Continuous Survey** over the conventional 5-year cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's deck plate has the following readings taken over three consecutive surveys:

| Survey Year | Year | Measured Thickness (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1st Special | 0 (As-built) | 20.0 |
| 2nd Special | 5 | 17.6 |
| 3rd Special | 10 | 14.8 |

Calculate: **(i)** Renewal thickness, **(ii)** % wastage at each survey, **(iii)** ARW in each 5-year period, and **(iv)** Predict the **renewal year** if current trend continues. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Survey Type Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Cycle Survey | Continuous Survey | Special Survey |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Every 5 years | Annual (rotational) | Every 5 years (full hull) |
| Scope | Complete hull | One zone/year | Complete hull |
| Surveyor | Class surveyor | Class surveyor + crew tasks | Class surveyor |
| Documentation | Single report | Per-zone report | Single comprehensive report |
| Downtime | 1–3 months concentrated | Spread across years | 1–3 months concentrated |

**Advantages of Continuous Survey:**
1. **Reduced dock time** — vessel only dry-docks for ~2–3 weeks per year
2. **Better cargo scheduling** — shorter off-hire periods
3. **Early defect detection** — annual zone inspections catch issues earlier
4. **Lower insurance premiums** (in some cases)
5. **No single massive overhaul event**

**(b) Numerical Analysis (7 marks)**

**(i) Renewal Thickness:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{renewal} &= 0.75 \times 20.0 = 15.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Wastage:**

*At Year 5:*
$$
\% W_5 = \frac{20.0 - 17.6}{20.0} \times 100 = 12.0 \text{ \%}
$$

*At Year 10:*
$$
\% W_{10} = \frac{20.0 - 14.8}{20.0} \times 100 = 26.0 \text{ \%}
$$

**(iii) ARW per 5-year interval:**

*Y0 to Y5:*
$$
ARW_1 = \frac{20.0 - 17.6}{5} = 0.48 \text{ mm/yr}
$$

*Y5 to Y10:*
$$
ARW_2 = \frac{17.6 - 14.8}{5} = 0.56 \text{ mm/yr}
$$

**(iv) Predicting Renewal Year:**

At Year 10: $t_{measured} = 14.8 \text{ mm}$ which is **already below** $t_{renewal} = 15.0 \text{ mm}$.

$$
\boxed{\text{RENEWAL MANDATED IMMEDIATELY}}
$$

**Critical observation:** The average ARW has increased from 0.48 to 0.56 mm/yr, indicating accelerating corrosion — likely due to coating breakdown. The next 5-year Special Survey (Year 15) would require extensive renewals if mitigation is not undertaken.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in Renewal Survey questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.75 factor** — writing renewal thickness as $t_{as-built} - 25$ instead of $0.75 \times t_{as-built}$
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — writing 25 instead of 25 % or 0.75 in calculations
> 3. **Confusing Cycle vs. Continuous Survey** — examiners specifically test this
> 4. **Missing IACS UR references** — always cite UR Z17/Z23/Z7 when relevant
> 5. **Skipping the conclusion/verdict** in numerical problems — always state "Renew/Accept"
> 6. **Not drawing/labeling diagrams** — partial credit for labelled sketches in 14-mark questions

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Renewal Survey**

- **Definition:** Comprehensive 5-yearly class survey of hull, machinery, and electrical systems
- **Cycle Interval:** every **5 years** (aligned with Special Survey)
- **Renewal Threshold:** **25 %** wastage $\Rightarrow$ measured thickness $\leq$ **75 %** of as-built
- **Key Formula:** $t_{renewal} = 0.75 \times t_{as-built}$
- **Wastage Formula:** $\Delta t = t_{as-built} - t_{measured}$
- **ARW Formula:** $ARW = \Delta t / Age_{years}$
- **Remaining Years Formula:** $Y_{remaining} = (t_{measured} - t_{renewal}) / ARW$
- **Three Streams:** Hull, Machinery, Electrical
- **Three Survey Modes:** Cycle (5-yr), Continuous (rotational annual), Special (intensive 5-yr)
- **Key Documents:** IACS UR Z17, UR Z23, UR Z7, Classification Society Rules
- **Critical Areas:** Hopper corners, bilge keels, side shell amidships, transverse bulkhead connections
- **Standard ARW Range:** 0.10 – 0.30 mm/yr (sea chests/ballast tanks may exceed)
- **Documentation:** Thickness Measurement Report (TMR), Survey Status Report, Class Endorsement Certificate
- **Engineering Insight:** Increasing ARW over time = coating breakdown → mitigate immediately
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Additional Survey** in the context of hull inspection refers to any non-routine, event-driven, or situation-specific structural and operational assessment of a vessel's hull, machinery, and associated systems that falls outside the scope of the regular classification cycle (Annual, Intermediate, and Special Surveys conducted per the IACS/Classification Society rules).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** An Additional Survey is a supplementary, purpose-driven technical inspection carried out by a classification society surveyor, an appointed marine surveyor, or a flag-state inspector, triggered by a specific operational event, contractual obligation, regulatory requirement, or commercial transaction. It supplements—but does not replace—the periodic classification survey regime.

### Standard Classification Survey Hierarchy (for context)

| Survey Type | Trigger | Frequency | Primary Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| Annual Survey | Calendar | Every 12 months | General condition, safety systems |
| Intermediate Survey | Calendar | Within 2.5–3 years | Underwater parts, more detailed |
| Special Survey | Calendar | Every 5 years (Renewal) | Comprehensive structural integrity |
| **Additional Survey** | **Event / Contract** | **As required** | **Specific scope based on trigger** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Car Inspection Parallel:**
> Think of your car. Every year you take it for a regular service (oil change, brakes, general check-up). That is the *Annual Survey*. Every few years, you do a deep mechanical overhaul—that is the *Special Survey*. But sometimes:
> - You are about to buy the car → you call a mechanic for a one-time detailed check (**Pre-Purchase Survey**).
> - You had a small accident → the insurance company sends an assessor (**Damage Survey**).
> - You want to convert your petrol car to CNG → a specialist inspects before and after (**Conversion Survey**).
> - Your car was idle in the garage for 2 years and you want to drive it again → an expert inspects everything (**Re-commissioning Survey**).
>
> **All these one-off, situation-specific inspections are "Additional Surveys" in ship terminology.** They are not part of the regular service cycle, but they are equally critical for safety, compliance, and commercial decisions.

## 1.3 Categories of Additional Surveys (Overview)

The principal types encountered in KTU-aligned hull inspection curricula are:

1. **Condition Survey** — Evaluation of a vessel's present technical state for a prospective buyer, charterer, or financier.
2. **Damage Survey** — Post-incident assessment (collision, grounding, fire, flooding, structural failure).
3. **Conversion / Alteration Survey** — Before, during, and after modifications to hull form, machinery, or systems.
4. **Occasional / Extra Survey** — Requested by the owner, charterer, or insurer outside the regular cycle.
5. **Pre-Voyage Survey** — Conducted before undertaking a special voyage (e.g., heavy-lift, extended route, polar waters).
6. **Lay-Up and Re-Commissioning Survey** — For vessels entering or leaving prolonged idle/storage status.
7. **Towage Survey** — Assessment of a vessel's fitness to be towed (dead-ship tow).
8. **Insurance / Loss Survey** — For underwriting, claims adjustment, or average adjustment.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Hull Inspection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics (Bold):**
> - **Lightweight (LWT):** Mass of the empty vessel (no cargo, fuel, stores, or crew). Units: **tonnes (t)**.
> - **Deadweight (DWT):** Total weight the vessel can carry (cargo + fuel + stores + water + crew). Units: **tonnes (t)**.
> - **Gross Tonnage (GT):** Volumetric measure of total enclosed space. **Unitless index.**
> - **Net Tonnage (NT):** Volumetric measure of cargo-earning space. **Unitless index.**
> - **Safety Life Factor (SLF):** Ratio of actual hull girder section modulus to the minimum required by the rule. **Dimensionless, ≥ 1.0**.
> - **Corrosion Allowance (CA):** Extra plate thickness added at new-building stage to allow for wastage over service life. Units: **mm** (typically **2.0 mm** for hull plating, **3.0 mm** for high-stress zones).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of an Additional Survey — Step-by-Step

Every Additional Survey, irrespective of its specific trigger, follows a structured operational sequence:

### Step 1 — Trigger Identification & Scope Definition
- **Why:** Establishes the legal, commercial, or technical basis for the survey.
- **How:** Trigger may originate from:
  - A casualty report (e.g., grounding).
  - A contractual clause (e.g., sale and purchase agreement).
  - A flag-state administration order (e.g., after detention).
  - A class condition of class (e.g., temporary repairs requiring permanent rectification).

### Step 2 — Surveyor Appointment & Credentials Verification
- **Why:** Only a recognized surveyor (classification society, flag-state inspector, or independent accredited marine surveyor) can issue a valid report.
- **How:** Verify IACS membership, flag-state authorization (e.g., IMO-recognized organization), and surveyor license validity.

### Step 3 — Document Review (Pre-Physical Survey)
- **Why:** History reveals patterns of weakness.
- **How:** Inspect:
  - Ship's certificates (Safety Construction, Safety Equipment, Load Line, etc.).
  - Class records and previous survey reports.
  - Stability booklet, loading manual, trim and stability information.
  - Maintenance logs, defect lists, and the Chief Engineer's records.

### Step 4 — Physical Inspection Plan
- **Why:** An unfocused inspection wastes time and may miss critical defects.
- **How:** Develop a checklist based on scope, e.g., for a **Damage Survey** focus on:
  - Plating deformation (using straightedge, template, or 3D laser scan).
  - Fracture surfaces and crack propagation direction.
  - Watertight integrity (doors, hatches, ventilators).

### Step 5 — In-Situ Examination & Measurement
- **Why:** Quantitative data supports a defensible report.
- **How:**
  - **Ultrasonic Thickness Measurement (UTM):** Measures remaining plate thickness.
  - **Close-Up Visual Inspection (CVI):** For critical structural details.
  - **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** MPI (Magnetic Particle Inspection), DPI (Dye Penetrant Inspection), Eddy Current.

### Step 6 — Defect Mapping & Classification
- **Why:** Categorisation guides the repair specification.
- **How:** Each defect is classified as:
  - **Recommend (R):** Repair at next scheduled survey.
  - **Condition of Class (CC):** Repair within a defined period (e.g., 3 months).
  - **Immediate Repair (IR):** Must be rectified before the vessel sails.

### Step 7 — Report Compilation & Certification
- **Why:** Documented evidence for legal, commercial, and regulatory use.
- **How:** Issue a formal report with photographs, measurement tables, defect sketches, and recommendations.

### Step 8 — Follow-Up & Verification
- **Why:** A report without verification is incomplete.
- **How:** Re-survey after repairs, confirm defect rectification, update class records.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Engineering Mathematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are routinely applied during Additional Surveys, especially Damage, Pre-Purchase, and Condition Surveys.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion wastage percentage | $\%W = \dfrac{T_{original} - T_{measured}}{T_{original}} \times 100$ | Determines if plate is below **corrosion allowance**; triggers replacement if > **75% wastage** in critical zones. |
| 2 | Minimum acceptable thickness | $T_{min} = T_{as-built} - CA + S_{min}$ | $S_{min}$ = rule minimum (e.g., 70% of as-built for ordinary hull plating per IACS). |
| 3 | Hull girder section modulus reduction | $Z_{actual} = Z_{as-built} \times \dfrac{I_{actual}}{I_{as-built}}$ | Used to check if hull is still compliant with the rule minimum section modulus. |
| 4 | Plate bending stress (simplified) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress due to wave/hogging-sagging moments; **M** in N·m, **Z** in m³. |
| 5 | Shear stress in plate | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A_{shear}}$ | V = shear force at the section, A = effective shear area. |
| 6 | Buckling strength check | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | Euler-type plate buckling; **k** = buckling coefficient, **E** = Young's modulus, **ν** = Poisson's ratio, **t** = thickness, **b** = panel width. |
| 7 | Lightweight verification (inclining experiment) | $\Delta = \dfrac{W \cdot x}{GG'}$ | Used during re-commissioning surveys to confirm LWT after conversions. |
| 8 | Mean draft change to displacement | $\Delta T = \dfrac{W_{added/removed}}{TPC}$ | Tracks weight changes after conversion or repair; **TPC** = Tonnes Per Centimetre immersion. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note on Tables:** Use of vertical pipes inside table rows breaks markdown rendering. All set-builder or absolute-value notation in the formulas above uses $\vert$ or $\mid$ for clean rendering. Always cross-check symbol units before substitution in numerical problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Additional Surveys are the **backbone of maritime risk management**:
- **For P&I Clubs and Insurers:** They underpin claims settlement (General Average, Particular Average).
- **For Banks and Financiers:** They inform the lending value against the vessel as collateral.
- **For Charterers:** They verify vessel condition before signing a time-charter party.
- **For Flag States and Port State Control:** They verify continued compliance after incidents.
- **For Shipyards:** They scope conversion works and post-conformance inspections.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation — Hull Plate Renewal Trigger (Corrosion Wastage)

This is a foundational calculation in any Additional Survey involving thickness gauging. Let us derive the condition under which a hull plate must be renewed.

### Given
- $T_{as-built}$ = As-built plate thickness from the shipyard plan (mm).
- $CA$ = Corrosion allowance (mm).
- $T_{measured}$ = In-situ ultrasonic thickness measurement (mm).
- $S_{min}$ = Rule minimum acceptable thickness fraction (e.g., 0.70 or 70% of as-built, per IACS UR Z7).

### Step 1 — Define the "Renewal Threshold"
The classification society requires that at any time during service life, the remaining thickness of a hull plate shall not be less than the rule minimum. The plate is **acceptable** only if:

$$
T_{measured} \;\geq\; T_{renewal}
$$

where $T_{renewal}$ is the threshold below which the plate must be renewed.

### Step 2 — Express $T_{renewal}$ in Terms of $T_{as-built}$ and $S_{min}$
The rule minimum thickness (without considering wastage history) is:

$$
T_{rule} \;=\; S_{min} \times T_{as-built}
$$

The plate must be renewed when measured thickness falls to or below this value:

$$
T_{renewal} \;=\; S_{min} \times T_{as-built}
$$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Percentage Wastage

We define percentage wastage $\%W$ as the fraction of original thickness lost to corrosion:

$$
\%W \;=\; \frac{T_{as-built} - T_{measured}}{T_{as-built}} \times 100
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Rearrange
Substitute $T_{measured}$ from the wastage formula:

$$
T_{measured} \;=\; T_{as-built} \left(1 - \frac{\%W}{100}\right)
$$

Setting $T_{measured} = T_{renewal}$ gives the critical percentage wastage $\%W_{crit}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{as-built}\left(1 - \frac{\%W_{crit}}{100}\right) &= S_{min} \times T_{as-built} \\
1 - \frac{\%W_{crit}}{100} &= S_{min} \\
\frac{\%W_{crit}}{100} &= 1 - S_{min} \\
\%W_{crit} &= (1 - S_{min}) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
For example, with $S_{min} = 0.75$ (i.e., 75% of as-built):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%W_{crit} &= (1 - 0.75) \times 100 \\
&= 0.25 \times 100 \\
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The plate must be renewed when more than **25%** of its original thickness has been lost to corrosion (under the simplified rule minimum of 75%).

### Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** $T_{as-built} = 22$ mm, $CA = 3$ mm, $T_{measured} = 17.5$ mm, $S_{min} = 0.75$.

**Step A — Compute percentage wastage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%W &= \frac{T_{as-built} - T_{measured}}{T_{as-built}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{22 - 17.5}{22} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4.5}{22} \times 100 \\
&= 20.45\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute the renewal threshold:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{renewal} &= S_{min} \times T_{as-built} \\
&= 0.75 \times 22 \\
&= 16.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compare:**

$$
T_{measured} = 17.5 \text{ mm} \;>\; T_{renewal} = 16.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** The plate is **acceptable** (no renewal required), but a close-up inspection and re-measurement at the next annual survey should be scheduled because wastage is approaching the threshold.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Derivation — Plate Buckling Critical Stress

For a simply supported rectangular plate under uniaxial compression, the critical buckling stress per classical plate theory:

### Given
- $E$ = Young's modulus of steel = **205,000 N/mm²**.
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio of steel = **0.30**.
- $t$ = plate thickness.
- $b$ = plate width (smaller panel dimension).
- $k$ = buckling coefficient (depends on loading and boundary condition; for uniaxial compression with simply supported edges, $k = 4.0$).

### Derivation (Summary Path)
- The differential equation of a buckled plate yields the characteristic value.
- Applying boundary conditions (zero deflection and zero bending moment at simply supported edges) gives a half-wave solution.
- Substituting back into the strain-energy balance yields:

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2
$$

### Numerical Example
Take $t = 14$ mm, $b = 600$ mm, $k = 4.0$, $E = 205{,}000$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.30$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{cr} &= \frac{4.0 \times \pi^2 \times 205{,}000}{12 \times (1 - 0.30^2)} \times \left(\frac{14}{600}\right)^2 \\
&= \frac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 205{,}000}{12 \times 0.91} \times (0.02333)^2 \\
&= \frac{8{,}093{,}876}{10.92} \times 0.0005444 \\
&= 741{,}196 \times 0.0005444 \\
&\approx 403.5 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Critical buckling stress is **403.5 N/mm²**, comfortably above typical steel yield stress (~235 N/mm²). Buckling is not a concern at as-built condition, but if thickness reduces by 30% (say, $t = 9.8$ mm), $\sigma_{cr}$ drops sharply (proportional to $t^2$):

$$
\sigma_{cr,\,reduced} = 403.5 \times \left(\frac{9.8}{14}\right)^2 = 403.5 \times 0.490 = 197.7 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

This is below the yield stress and signals **buckling risk** → plate renewal or stiffener retrofit required.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Trigger an Additional Survey

| Trigger Event | Applicable Additional Survey Type | Priority | Typical Time Frame |
|---|---|---|---|
| Collision with another vessel | Damage Survey | **Critical** | Within 24–48 hours |
| Grounding / contact with seabed | Damage Survey | **Critical** | Within 24 hours |
| Fire / explosion in any compartment | Damage Survey + Safety Survey | **Critical** | After safety permit |
| Hull deformation observed at sea | Damage / Condition Survey | High | At next port call |
| Sale of vessel (S&P agreement signed) | Pre-Purchase / Condition Survey | High | During delivery period |
| Major machinery or hull modification | Conversion / Alteration Survey | High | Before, during, after work |
| Vessel idle > 3 months | Lay-Up Survey | Medium | Before lay-up starts |
| Reactivation from lay-up | Re-Commissioning Survey | Medium | Before sailing |
| Tug-towing of disabled vessel | Towage Survey | High | Before tow commences |
| Detention by Port State Control | Follow-Up Survey | Critical | Before PSC release |
| Insurance claim filed | Insurance / Loss Survey | High | As per insurer instruction |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Flow — Additional Survey Workflow

```mermaid

    A[SurveyTriggerEvent] --> B{TriggerClassification}
    B -->|Casualty| C[DamageSurvey]

```

    Defect[IdentifiedDefect] --> Meas[MeasureAgainstRuleLimits]
    Meas --> Check1{IsItBelowRenewalThreshold}
    Check1 -->|Yes| Sev1[SeverityImmediateRepair]
    Check1 -->|No| Check2{IsItBelowCorrosionAllowance}
    Check2 -->|Yes| Sev2[SeverityConditionOfClass]
    Check2 -->|No| Check3{IsItMinorCosmetic}

```

        Z5[SuperstructureAndAccommodation]
        Z6[ForeAndAftPeakTanks]
    end

    Triggers[CommonDamageTriggers] --> Z1
    Triggers --> Z2
    Triggers --> Z3
    Triggers --> Z4
    Triggers --> Z5

    Z1 --> M1[MeasurePlateThicknessUTM]
    Z1 --> M2[CheckBottomShellForDeformation]

```
