# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Additional Survey" in the context of hull inspection and state two situations that necessitate it.**

**Model Answer:**
An Additional Survey is a non-routine, event- or purpose-driven inspection of a vessel's hull and associated systems conducted outside the regular classification survey cycle (Annual / Intermediate / Special).

**Two situations that necessitate it:**

1. **Damage Survey after a casualty** such as collision, grounding, fire, or explosion.
2. **Pre-Purchase (Condition) Survey** before a vessel changes ownership or is taken on a long-term charter.

*(A third valid answer: Conversion/Alteration Survey when major structural or machinery modifications are carried out.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between a Condition Survey and a Damage Survey. Mention the trigger for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Condition Survey | Damage Survey |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Commercial transaction (sale, purchase, financing, charter) | Physical event (collision, grounding, fire, structural failure) |
| Purpose | Document the present state for a buyer / lender / charterer | Identify the extent of structural damage and define repair scope |
| Output | A condition report with valuation and recommendations | A damage report with repair specification and class conditions |

The surveyor in both cases must hold **IACS-recognized** or **flag-state-authorized** credentials for the report to be valid.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain in detail the different types of Additional Surveys conducted during the service life of a vessel. Describe their scope, triggering events, and reporting requirements.**

#### Part (a) — Scope and Types of Additional Surveys (7 Marks)

The following categories of Additional Surveys are commonly encountered:

1. **Condition (Pre-Purchase) Survey**
   - Trigger: Sale and Purchase Agreement, financing, or charter party due-diligence.
   - Scope: General arrangement review, hull and machinery condition, NDT of critical zones, stability verification.
   - Reporting: Detailed report with photograph log, defect list, valuation, and recommendations.

2. **Damage Survey**
   - Trigger: Collision, grounding, fire, heavy weather damage, structural failure.
   - Scope: Full damage extent assessment, fracture analysis, watertight integrity, repair feasibility.
   - Reporting: Damage report with sketches, photographic evidence, classification society endorsements.

3. **Conversion / Alteration Survey**
   - Trigger: Modification of hull form, addition of a deck, lengthening, engine change, retrofit of ballast-water treatment.
   - Scope: Pre-conversion baseline, in-process verification, post-conformance to original rule set.
   - Reporting: Pre, during, and post-conversion reports.

4. **Lay-Up and Re-Commissioning Survey**
   - Trigger: Vessel entering / leaving a period of inactivity.
   - Scope: Corrosion monitoring, dehumidification system check, sea-valve sealing, reactivation trial.
   - Reporting: Lay-up readiness certificate and re-commissioning report.

5. **Towage Survey**
   - Trigger: Disabled vessel requires a tow.
   - Scope: Hull fitness for tow, stability under tow, towing gear, emergency tow arrangement.
   - Reporting: Towage approval certificate.

#### Part (b) — Triggering Events and Reporting Requirements (7 Marks)

**Triggering Events Summary:**

| Event Type | Examples | Typical Survey Type |
|---|---|---|
| Casualty | Collision, grounding, fire, sinking | Damage Survey |
| Commercial | Sale, refinancing, long-term charter | Condition Survey |
| Modification | Major conversion, retrofit | Conversion Survey |
| Operational | Lay-up, towing, special voyage | Lay-Up / Towage Survey |
| Regulatory | Flag-state directive, PSC detention | Follow-Up / Condition Survey |

**Reporting Requirements:**

- Reports must follow the **classification society or flag-state template**.
- Must include the surveyor's name, credentials, date, vessel particulars, IMO number.
- Must contain: photographs, defect sketches, NDT readings (UTM tables), recommendations.
- Must clearly state the **class status** (with or without conditions, or with recommendations only).
- Must be filed in the vessel's class record book and a copy retained by the owner.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students write about "annual surveys" or "special surveys" as if they were "additional surveys". This is a **direct conceptual error** and will attract zero marks. Also, simply listing survey types without stating **triggering events** and **reporting requirements** forfeits at least **3 marks** out of 14. Always structure your answer in three parts: **scope, trigger, and reporting**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A bulk carrier of DWT 50,000 tonnes has as-built bottom plate thickness of 22 mm with a corrosion allowance of 3 mm. During an Additional Survey (Damage Survey after grounding), the surveyor records the following ultrasonic thickness measurements at five points: 18.5 mm, 17.0 mm, 16.2 mm, 15.8 mm, and 14.5 mm. Take rule minimum as 75% of as-built thickness.**

#### Part (a) — Calculate the percentage wastage at each point and identify the points requiring immediate plate renewal. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

The rule minimum is:

$$
T_{rule} = 0.75 \times 22 = 16.5 \text{ mm}
$$

A plate requires renewal when its measured thickness is **less than** $T_{rule} = 16.5$ mm.

**Calculation Table (model answer layout):**

| Point | Measured $T_{measured}$ (mm) | Calculation $\%W = \frac{22 - T_m}{22} \times 100$ | Wastage (%) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 18.5 | $\frac{22-18.5}{22}\times 100$ | 15.91 | Acceptable (above 16.5 mm) |
| 2 | 17.0 | $\frac{22-17.0}{22}\times 100$ | 22.73 | Acceptable (above 16.5 mm) |
| 3 | 16.2 | $\frac{22-16.2}{22}\times 100$ | 26.36 | **Renew** (below 16.5 mm) |
| 4 | 15.8 | $\frac{22-15.8}{22}\times 100$ | 28.18 | **Renew** (below 16.5 mm) |
| 5 | 14.5 | $\frac{22-14.5}{22}\times 100$ | 34.09 | **Renew — Critical** |

**Verdict:** Points 3, 4, and 5 require **immediate plate renewal**. Point 5 is in the most severe condition and may trigger an underwater inspection or emergency dry-docking.

#### Part (b) — Calculate the average percentage wastage across the five points and recommend the surveyor's classification of the damage. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Average wastage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%W_{avg} &= \frac{15.91 + 22.73 + 26.36 + 28.18 + 34.09}{5} \\
&= \frac{127.27}{5} \\
&= 25.45\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**

- Average wastage of **25.45%** is just above the **critical 25% threshold** derived earlier for $S_{min} = 0.75$.
- More importantly, **3 out of 5** gauged points fall below the rule minimum.
- This indicates **localized but severe bottom-shell wastage**, likely caused by abrasion during grounding combined with pre-existing corrosion.

**Surveyor's Classification (Model Recommendation):**

| Severity | Defect | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Immediate Repair | Plates at points 3, 4, 5 (T < 16.5 mm) | Crop out and renew before sailing |
| Condition of Class | Adjacent plating within 1 m of renewed plates | Close-up + UTM at next dry-dock |
| Recommendation | Points 1 and 2 (T just above 16.5 mm) | Continue thickness monitoring at annual intervals |

**Overall Recommendation:** Issue a **Condition of Class** with a **3-month window** for permanent repair and re-survey. The vessel should not proceed to load heavy cargo until the bottom plates are renewed at a suitable repair yard.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Many students forget to multiply the rule minimum fraction by $T_{as-built}$ before comparing. This is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 2. Some forget to express wastage as a percentage. KTU expects the value in **% to two decimal places**.
> 3. Failure to state the surveyor's classification (R / CC / IR) in part (b) attracts a **2-mark penalty**, even if the numerical calculation is correct.
> 4. **Never skip the engineering interpretation** — KTU rewards answers that link numbers to physical actions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Additional Surveys are non-routine, event-driven inspections outside the standard Annual / Intermediate / Special Survey cycle.
- **Principal Types:** Condition, Damage, Conversion, Lay-Up, Re-Commissioning, Towage, Insurance, Pre-Voyage, Occasional.
- **Surveyor Credentials:** Must be IACS-recognized or flag-state-authorized for the report to be legally and commercially valid.
- **Inspection Methods:** Visual, Close-Up Visual Inspection (CVI), Ultrasonic Thickness Measurement (UTM), Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI), Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI), Eddy Current.
- **Defect Classification:** Recommend (R) / Condition of Class (CC) / Immediate Repair (IR).
- **Critical Renewal Rule:** Plate must be renewed when measured thickness falls below $S_{min} \times T_{as-built}$.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Wastage: $\%W = \dfrac{T_{as-built} - T_{measured}}{T_{as-built}} \times 100$
  - Rule minimum: $T_{rule} = S_{min} \times T_{as-built}$
  - Plate buckling: $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$
  - Lightweight: $\Delta = \dfrac{W \cdot x}{GG'}$
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing Additional Survey with Annual or Special Survey — they are categorically distinct.
- **Documentation:** Always retain the report in the vessel's class record and provide a copy to the owner, flag-state, and P&I club (if applicable).
- **Real-World Link:** Every major maritime casualty (Exxon Valdez, Costa Concordia, Rena) triggered an Additional (Damage) Survey before any repair decision was made.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Survey During Transfer of Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Survey During Transfer of Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Survey During Transfer of Ships — Module 1 | OENST832

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Survey During Transfer of Ships** (also called **Condition Survey**, **Sale-and-Purchase (S&P) Survey**, or **Pre-Transfer Survey**) is a systematic, documented, and technically rigorous inspection of the vessel's hull, machinery, and structural integrity conducted at the point of legal and operational transfer of ownership, flag, or classification society. It establishes the **baseline condition of class**, identifies deferred maintenance, validates remaining scantlings, and protects the financial and safety interests of both the *buyer* and the *seller* (or *transferor* and *transferee*).

In KTU 2024 OENST832 terminology, it is a *pre-disposition survey* in which the **Classification Society Surveyor**, **Owner's Superintendent**, and **Buyer's Representative** jointly verify the vessel against:
1. Classification Rules (e.g., IACS members — LR, DNV, BV, ABS, KR)
2. Statutory requirements (SOLAS, MARPOL, ILO)
3. The **Memorandum of Agreement (MoA)** conditions
4. The **Condition of Class** (CoC) records

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The survey is *not* a routine class survey. It is a **transaction-linked** inspection whose findings directly affect **sale price, indemnities, and warranties** in the Ship Sale & Purchase agreement.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Used Car Inspection, But For a 300-Metre Steel Giant"

Imagine buying a **10-year-old used car**. Before signing the cheque, you take it to an independent garage. They put it on a lift, check the chassis for rust, measure brake-pad thickness, scan the engine for fault codes, and produce a **report**. That report determines the price, the warranty, and whether you even buy the car.

A **Survey During Ship Transfer** is the maritime equivalent — except:
- The "car" weighs **30,000–300,000 tonnes**
- The "garage" is a **drydock or alongside berth**
- The "inspectors" use **ultrasonic thickness gauges, hull crawlers, ROVs, and diver teams**
- The "report" is a legally binding document called the **S&P Survey Report** or **Condition Survey Report**
- The "warranty" is the **seller's period of liability under MoA clauses 11–13**

> [!NOTE]
> The ship may be sold *with its class* (continuous class) or *without class* (re-classing required). The transfer survey approach differs significantly in each case.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Reference |
|---|---|
| Survey Window | **Within 30 days prior to delivery** of vessel to buyer |
| Renewal Threshold for Plates | **75%–80% of original as-built thickness** |
| Acceptance Criteria (Corrosion) | Within **corrosion allowance** (typically 0.5 mm–3.0 mm) |
| Report Issue Time | Usually **3–5 working days** post-survey |
| Class Entry Standard | **IACS Recommendation 71** (Z17.1 for surveys) |
| Diver Inspection Depth | Full underwater hull, including **boot-top** and **bilge keels** |
| Documentation Standard | **IACS UR Z23** for survey reporting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorise for KTU exams:**
> - Plate renewal is mandatory when measured thickness < `(t_{as-built} - corrosion\ allowance)$`
> - The **75% rule** is the universally accepted threshold for major structural items.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of a Transfer Survey — The Five Pillars

A transfer survey must satisfy **five independent objectives**:

1. **Verification of Class Status** — Confirm whether the vessel holds a *clean* or *overdue* condition of class.
2. **Structural Fitness** — Quantify hull thickness reduction, identify wastage areas, and project remaining useful life.
3. **Operational Verification** — Confirm machinery, navigation, and safety systems function per statutory requirements.
4. **Commercial Documentation** — Provide a basis for **purchase price negotiation**, **retention money**, and **indemnity claims**.
5. **Legal Compliance** — Certify compliance with SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, and flag-state regulations for smooth *change of flag* or *change of class*.

---

### 2.2 Types of Surveys Conducted During Transfer

| Survey Type | Trigger | Scope | Outcome Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Condition Survey** | Pre-purchase inspection | Hull + machinery + equipment | S&P Report |
| **Pre-Delivery Survey** | Just before handing over | Operational testing of all systems | Protocol of Delivery |
| **In-Water Survey (IWS)** | No dry-dock available | Underwater hull by divers/ROV | Diver Report |
| **Class Transfer Survey** | Change of classification society | Equivalent to Special Survey (partial) | Class Transfer Record |
| **Flag Transfer Survey** | Re-registration under new flag | Statutory compliance verification | Flag Endorsement |
| **Lay-up / Reactivation Survey** | Vessel kept idle before sale | Hull preservation, machinery | Reactivation Report |

---

### 2.3 Survey Procedure — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 — Pre-Survey Preparation**
- Obtain **General Arrangement (GA) plan**, **trunk section**, **shell expansion**, and **previous survey reports**
- Cross-reference with **Classification Society records**
- Confirm survey scope with **MoA clauses** and **letter of intent**

**Step 2 — Documentation Review (Desk Study)**
- Valid class certificate (expired/valid?)
- Continuous Survey Records (CSR) status
- Outstanding **Conditions of Class (CoC)**
- Memoranda/extensions granted
- Operating profile (trading pattern affects wear zones)

**Step 3 — On-Board Visual & Functional Inspection**
- Hull external (above waterline): boot-top, freeboard, deck
- Hull internal (in cargo holds, ballast tanks, cofferdams)
- Machinery spaces: main engine, auxiliaries, steering gear
- Bridge equipment and safety systems
- Accommodation and life-saving appliances

**Step 4 — Thickness Measurement (TM) Survey**
- As per **IACS UR Z7 / Z7.1** or class-specific TM requirements
- **UT gauge** readings at grid points defined by class rules
- Compare with **original build thickness** and **corrosion allowance**

**Step 5 — Underwater Hull Inspection**
- Conducted in drydock (preferred) or by **divers / ROV**
- Propeller, rudder, sea chests, anodes, anti-fouling condition

**Step 6 — Reporting & Recommendations**
- Categorise findings: **A (Immediate), B (Within 3 months), C (Next survey)**
- Issue **survey report** with photographs, thickness tables, and drawings

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Hull Deterioration & Renewal Criteria

| Concept | Formula / Criterion | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Plate Renewal Criterion** | $t_{measured} < (t_{as-built} - t_{corrosion\ allowance})$ | $t$ in **mm** |
| **Percentage Wastage** | $\%W = \dfrac{t_{as-built} - t_{measured}}{t_{as-built}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless |
| **75% Rule Threshold** | Renew if $\%W > 25\%$ | — |
| **Substantial Corrosion Limit** | $t_{substantial} = 0.75 \times t_{as-built}$ | mm |
| **Original Thickness (typical)** | $t_{as-built} = 16\text{–}32\ \text{mm}$ (side shell, ocean vessel) | mm |
| **Corrosion Allowance** | $t_{ca} = 0.5\text{–}3.0\ \text{mm}$ | mm |
| **Annual Corrosion Rate (typical)** | $0.10\text{–}0.30\ \text{mm/year}$ | mm/yr |
| **TM Grid Spacing (Bottom Plate)** | $1\ \text{frame\ spacing}$ or approx. **600 mm** | mm |
| **Renewal of Frames (typical)** | Renewal if $\%W > 30\%$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The **75% rule** is applied by class surveyors worldwide. For a 20 mm plate, $0.75 \times 20 = 15\ \text{mm}$. If UT gauge reads **14.8 mm**, the plate is **acceptable** (wastage = 26%, but within the $t_{measured} > t_{as-built} - t_{ca}$ criterion when $t_{ca} = 2\ \text{mm}$).

---

### 2.5 Critical Structural Areas Inspected

| Zone | Critical Items |
|---|---|
| **Shell Plating** | Side, bottom, bilge, keel |
| **Deck Plating** | Strength deck, stringer plate, sheer strake |
| **Internal Structures** | Transverse frames, web frames, stringers, brackets |
| **Cargo Holds** | Hatch coamings, hatch covers, hold frames (especially tankers/bulk carriers) |
| **Tanks** | Ballast, cargo, bunker, freshwater — focus on **corrosion hotspots** |
| **Aft Body** | Rudder, propeller, stern frame, shaft bracket |
| **Fore Body** | Bow thruster, anchor pockets, hawse pipes |

---

### 2.6 Engineering Significance

- **Insurance P&I Clubs** rely on transfer survey reports to assess risk premium.
- **Banks** financing the ship purchase require the report before releasing funds.
- **Flag state authorities** use it to confirm compliance before issuing new registry.
- **Charterers** may demand a clean transfer survey before time-chartering.
- **Crew safety** — undetected wastage can lead to catastrophic hull failure (e.g., **MV MOL Comfort** 2013, **MV Wakashio** 2020).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Plate Renewal Decision Using the 75% Rule

**Problem Statement:** A ship's side shell plate has an original as-built thickness of **22 mm**. The plate has a corrosion allowance of **2.5 mm**. During the transfer survey, the UT gauge reads **17.0 mm** at a critical location. Determine:
1. The percentage wastage.
2. Whether the plate requires immediate renewal.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

$$t_{as-built} = 22\ \text{mm}, \quad t_{measured} = 17.0\ \text{mm}, \quad t_{ca} = 2.5\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the percentage wastage.**

The percentage wastage is defined as the ratio of thickness lost to the original as-built thickness, expressed as a percentage.

$$\%W = \dfrac{t_{as-built} - t_{measured}}{t_{as-built}} \times 100$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\%W = \dfrac{22 - 17.0}{22} \times 100$$

$$\%W = \dfrac{5.0}{22} \times 100$$

$$\%W = 0.22727 \times 100$$

$$\%W \approx 22.73\%$$

**Conclusion of Step 2:** The plate has lost approximately **22.73%** of its original thickness.

---

**Step 3 — Apply the renewal criterion.**

The plate must be renewed if the measured thickness falls below the allowable minimum. The allowable minimum is defined as the original as-built thickness minus the corrosion allowance.

$$t_{min} = t_{as-built} - t_{ca}$$

$$t_{min} = 22 - 2.5$$

$$t_{min} = 19.5\ \text{mm}$$

**Comparison with measured value:**

$$t_{measured} = 17.0\ \text{mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad t_{min} = 19.5\ \text{mm}$$

Since $17.0 < 19.5$, the plate has **exceeded** the corrosion allowance.

$$t_{measured} - t_{min} = 17.0 - 19.5 = -2.5\ \text{mm}$$

The plate is **2.5 mm** thinner than the allowable minimum.

---

**Step 4 — Apply the 75% rule threshold.**

The substantial corrosion limit is defined as 75% of the original as-built thickness.

$$t_{substantial} = 0.75 \times t_{as-built}$$

$$t_{substantial} = 0.75 \times 22$$

$$t_{substantial} = 16.5\ \text{mm}$$

**Comparison:**

$$t_{measured} = 17.0\ \text{mm} > t_{substantial} = 16.5\ \text{mm}$$

The measured thickness is **above** the substantial corrosion threshold by **0.5 mm**.

---

**Step 5 — Final Engineering Decision.**

| Criterion | Threshold | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wastage percentage | $\%W > 25\%$ | $22.73\%$ | **Acceptable** |
| Corrosion allowance | $t_{measured} < t_{min}$ | $17.0 < 19.5$ | **Exceeded** |
| 75% substantial rule | $t_{measured} < t_{substantial}$ | $17.0 > 16.5$ | **Acceptable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision Logic:** Although the plate has not crossed the 75% substantial threshold, it has **exceeded the corrosion allowance**. Under most classification society rules (DNV, LR, ABS), this requires:
> - **Local renewals** (insert plate) **OR**
> - **Increased TM frequency** in subsequent surveys **OR**
> - **Engineering assessment** based on stress level.

For an exam answer, the conclusion is: **Renewal required or engineering review mandatory.**

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Renewal

The decision flow for determining whether a structural item needs renewal is summarised in the following logical cascade:

$$\text{Renew} \iff \left[ (t_{measured} < t_{min}) \ \lor \ (\%W > 25\%) \right]$$

For secondary stiffeners and frames:

$$\text{Renew frame} \iff \left[ (\%W > 30\%) \ \lor \ (\text{visible cracking}) \right]$$

The **equivalent remaining life** estimation (for pro-active planning) is:

$$t_{remaining} = \dfrac{t_{measured} - t_{min}}{\text{Corrosion Rate (mm/yr)}}$$

**Worked Example:** If $t_{measured} = 17.0\ \text{mm}$, $t_{min} = 19.5\ \text{mm}$, and corrosion rate = $0.20\ \text{mm/year}$:

$$t_{remaining} = \dfrac{17.0 - 19.5}{0.20} = \dfrac{-2.5}{0.20} = -12.5\ \text{years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative remaining life** confirms that the structural item has *already* fallen below safe limits — renewal is **non-negotiable** under IACS UR Z23.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Survey During Ship Transfer — Process Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the end-to-end workflow of a transfer survey, from the initiation of the Ship Sale Agreement to the issuance of the final class status for the buyer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initiation of Ship Sale Agreement MoA Signed] --> B[Nomination of Independent Surveyor]
    B --> C[Pre-Survey Desk Study GA Plan, TM Records, Class Status]
    C --> D{Q1 Is Vessel Due for Drydocking?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Drydock Survey Full Hull Inspection + UT Measurement]
    D -- No --> F[In-Water Survey Diver or ROV + Internal Inspection]
    E --> G[Thickness Measurement UT Gauge Readings at Grid Points]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compare Readings with As-Built and Corrosion Allowance]
    H --> I{Q2 Any Plate Below Allowable Minimum?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Flag Plate for Renewal or Insert]
    I -- No --> K[Acceptable Note in Report]
    J --> L[Compile Findings into S and P Survey Report]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Q3 Outstanding Conditions of Class}
    M -- Yes --> N[List CoC with Deadlines for Buyer]
    M -- No --> O[Clean Status Confirmed]
    N --> P[Negotiation of Price Adjustments and Indemnities]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Protocol of Delivery and Acceptance Signed]
    Q --> R[Class Endorsement to New Owner or Transfer to New Society]
    R --> S[End of Transfer Survey Process]

    step1A[Initiator]:::role
    step1B[Surveyor]:::role
    step1C[Surveyor]:::role
    step1D[Surveyor]:::role
    step1E[Surveyor]:::role
    step1F[Surveyor]:::role
    step1G[Surveyor]:::role

    classDef role fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Survey Findings Classification

The findings of a transfer survey are classified into three priority buckets, which directly drive the buyer's commercial decision and the seller's repair obligations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Survey Findings]:::input --> CAT{Categorisation by Surveyor}:::decision

    CAT -- A. Immediate Threat --> A_BLOCK[Category A: Critical Defects]:::crit
    A_BLOCK --> A1[Examples: Cracks in strength deck, active corrosion pits]
    A_BLOCK --> A2[Action: Rectify before delivery]
    A_BLOCK --> A3[Effect: Withhold 100 percent of purchase price until fixed]

    CAT -- B. Within 3 Months --> B_BLOCK[Category B: Serious Defects]:::warn
    B_BLOCK --> B1[Examples: Plate below 75 percent substantial]
    B_BLOCK --> B2[Action: Repair within 90 days post-delivery]
    B_BLOCK --> B3[Effect: Retention money held by buyer]

    CAT -- C. Next Survey --> C_BLOCK[Category C: Advisory]:::info
    C_BLOCK --> C1[Examples: Coating breakdown, minor wastage]
    C_BLOCK --> C2[Action: Monitor at next scheduled survey]
    C_BLOCK --> C3[Effect: Documented but no price impact]

    classDef input fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef decision fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#9333EA,stroke-width:2px,color:#581C87
    classDef crit fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef warn fill:#FFEDD5,stroke:#C2410C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7C2D12
    classDef info fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
```

---

### 4.3 Architecture — Hull Zones Mapped for Transfer Survey Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SHIP[Ship Hull Structure]:::main --> Z1[Fore Body]:::zone
    SHIP --> Z2[Midship Body]:::zone
    SHIP --> Z3[ Aft Body]:::zone
    SHIP --> Z4[Deck and Superstructure]:::zone
    SHIP --> Z5[Double Bottom and Tanks]:::zone

    Z1 --> I1[Hawse pipes, anchor pockets]:::item
    Z1 --> I2[Bow thruster tunnel, stem plate]:::item

    Z2 --> I3[Side shell, bottom plating, bilge]:::item
    Z2 --> I4[Transverse bulkheads, web frames]:::item
    Z2 --> I5[Cargo holds, hatch covers, coamings]:::item

    Z3 --> I6[Rudder, stern frame, propeller]:::item
    Z3 --> I7[Shaft bracket, sea chest]:::item

    Z4 --> I8[Strength deck, sheer strake]:::item
    Z4 --> I9[Mooring equipment, bulwarks]:::item

    Z5 --> I10[Double bottom plates, floors]:::item
    Z5 --> I11[Ballast tanks, bunker tanks]:::item
    Z5 --> I12[Cofferdams, duct keels]:::item

    classDef main fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#0C4A6E,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef zone fill:#3B82F6,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef item fill:#BFDBFE,stroke:#2563EB,stroke-width:1px,color:#1E3A8A
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
Define the term **"Survey During Transfer of Ships"**. State any four objectives of conducting such a survey.

**Model Answer:**

A Survey During Transfer of Ships is a systematic inspection of a vessel's hull, machinery, and statutory equipment conducted when ownership, flag, or classification society is being transferred. It establishes the baseline condition of the vessel and protects both buyer and seller.

**Four objectives:**
1. **Verification of class status** — to confirm whether the vessel holds a clean, conditional, or overdue class.
2. **Structural fitness assessment** — to quantify wastage in hull plating and identify zones requiring renewal.
3. **Commercial documentation** — to support sale-price negotiation and indemnity clauses.
4. **Statutory compliance** — to confirm adherence to SOLAS, MARPOL, and Load Lines before transfer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Explain the **75% rule** for plate renewal during a transfer survey with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

The 75% rule states that a structural plate must be renewed when its measured thickness falls below **75% of the original as-built thickness**.

**Example:** If $t_{as-built} = 20\ \text{mm}$, then renewal is triggered when $t_{measured} < 0.75 \times 20 = 15\ \text{mm}$.

The rule acts as a screening criterion for substantial corrosion and triggers further engineering assessment before class approval is granted.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **procedure and scope** of a typical survey conducted during the transfer of a bulk carrier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A side shell plate of a bulk carrier has an original thickness of **25 mm** and a corrosion allowance of **3 mm**. During the transfer survey, the UT gauge reads **18.0 mm**. Calculate the percentage wastage and determine whether the plate requires renewal under the 75% rule and the corrosion allowance criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The procedure and scope of a transfer survey for a bulk carrier comprises the following sequential stages:

1. **Pre-survey Documentation Review (1 mark)**
   - Review General Arrangement (GA) plan, midship section, shell expansion.
   - Examine the latest Class Survey Status Report, Continuous Survey Records (CSR), and outstanding Conditions of Class (CoC).
   - Verify the Memorandum of Agreement (MoA) clauses relating to condition of delivery.

2. **Hull Structural Inspection (2 marks)**
   - **Cargo holds:** Check hatch coamings, hatch covers, hold frames, bilge wells, and tank-top plating for wastage and deformation.
   - **Side shell plating:** Inspect for corrosion, dents, and fractures, with focus on the **midbody parallel zone**.
   - **Double bottom:** Inspect floors, girders, and stiffeners; check for wastage at intersections.
   - **Ballast tanks (topside, hopper, double side):** Close visual and UT thickness gauging; assess coating condition.

3. **Thickness Measurement (TM) Survey (2 marks)**
   - Conducted in accordance with **IACS UR Z7.1** or class-specific TM schedule.
   - UT readings taken at **grid points** defined by the classification society.
   - Compare with **as-built thickness** minus **corrosion allowance**.
   - Identify plates/stiffeners exceeding the **25% wastage threshold**.

4. **Machinery and Statutory Survey (1 mark)**
   - Main engine, auxiliaries, steering gear, boilers tested for functionality.
   - Lifeboats, lifejackets, fire-fighting equipment verified.
   - Load Line, SOLAS, MARPOL certificates checked for validity.

5. **Underwater Hull Inspection (1 mark)**
   - Either in drydock or via divers / ROV in-water survey.
   - Propeller, rudder, sea chests, anodes, anti-fouling coating inspected.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$t_{as-built} = 25\ \text{mm}, \quad t_{measured} = 18.0\ \text{mm}, \quad t_{ca} = 3\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 1 — Compute percentage wastage:**

$$\%W = \dfrac{t_{as-built} - t_{measured}}{t_{as-built}} \times 100$$

$$\%W = \dfrac{25 - 18.0}{25} \times 100 = \dfrac{7.0}{25} \times 100 = 28\%$$

**Step 2 — Determine substantial corrosion threshold:**

$$t_{substantial} = 0.75 \times 25 = 18.75\ \text{mm}$$

Since $t_{measured} = 18.0\ \text{mm} < t_{substantial} = 18.75\ \text{mm}$, the plate has crossed the 75% threshold.

$$\Delta t = 18.75 - 18.0 = 0.75\ \text{mm below threshold}$$

**Step 3 — Check corrosion allowance criterion:**

$$t_{min} = t_{as-built} - t_{ca} = 25 - 3 = 22\ \text{mm}$$

$$t_{measured} = 18.0\ \text{mm} < t_{min} = 22\ \text{mm}$$

The plate is **4 mm** below the corrosion allowance.

**Conclusion:**

| Criterion | Threshold | Result | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wastage percentage | $>25\%$ | $28\%$ | Exceeded |
| 75% rule | $t_{measured} < 18.75\ \text{mm}$ | $18.0 < 18.75$ | Exceeded |
| Corrosion allowance | $t_{measured} < 22\ \text{mm}$ | $18.0 < 22$ | Exceeded |

**Final Verdict:** The plate **must be renewed (insert plate)** before the vessel is delivered to the buyer. Alternatively, an engineering assessment demonstrating adequate residual strength may be accepted by the classification society, subject to increased TM frequency in the next survey.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **types of surveys** that may be triggered during the transfer of a ship. Mention the documentation involved in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship has been laid up for **3 years** prior to sale. List the **specific survey items** that must be verified before re-activating the vessel for trading. Justify each item. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The transfer of a ship can trigger any of the following six types of surveys:

| Survey Type | Trigger | Documentation |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Condition / S&P Survey** | Pre-purchase inspection by buyer | S&P Survey Report, photo log, UT tables |
| **2. Pre-Delivery Survey** | Just before delivery of vessel | Protocol of Delivery and Acceptance |
| **3. Class Transfer Survey** | Change of classification society (e.g., LR → DNV) | Class Transfer Record, status of CSR |
| **4. Flag Transfer Survey** | Re-registration under new flag (e.g., Panama → Marshall Islands) | Flag Endorsement Certificate, statutory compliance |
| **5. Lay-up / Reactivation Survey** | Vessel kept idle before transfer | Reactivation Report, preservation records |
| **6. In-Water Survey (if no drydock)** | Time constraints or budget | Diver / ROV report, photographs, UT logs |

The most commonly encountered combination during a **commercial sale** is the **Condition Survey + Class Transfer Survey**, supported by a **Pre-Delivery Survey** on the actual day of handover.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

A vessel laid up for **3 years** requires a **Reactivation Survey** before re-enting service. The following specific items must be verified:

1. **Hull preservation (justification):** During lay-up, hull anti-fouling degrades, marine growth attaches, and cathodic protection may be depleted. Hull must be cleaned, anodes replaced, and new coating applied.

2. **Cathodic protection system (justification):** Sacrificial zinc/aluminium anodes deteriorate over time. Their consumption over 3 years can be significant. Replace and verify ICCP (if fitted).

3. **Ballast tanks and void spaces (justification):** Tanks may have been drained and dried. Inspect for corrosion hotspots, check integrity of coatings, and verify tank tightness.

4. **Main engine and auxiliaries (justification):** Engines require turning gear operation, lubricating oil analysis, fuel system priming, and sea trials. Cylinder liners, bearings, and seals may have degraded.

5. **Steering gear and bow thruster (justification):** Hydraulic seals harden during lay-up. Verify functioning, leak test, and replace seals as preventive maintenance.

6. **Safety equipment and statutory certificates (justification):** Lifeboats, fire extinguishers, lifejackets, EPIRBs — all have expiry dates. Lifeboat release mechanisms must be load-tested.

7. **Navigation equipment (justification):** ECDIS charts, GPS, gyrocompass, radar — many require software updates after 3 years; recalibrate before trading.

8. **Mooring and anchoring equipment (justification):** Mooring ropes degrade. Anchors and chains must be gauged, range-tested, and certified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In Part (a), students **commonly lose marks** by listing survey types without explaining the **documentation** — examiners expect both. (Lose ~2 marks)
> - In Part (b), students list items but **fail to justify** them. Each item must have a one-line engineering reason. (Lose ~2 marks)
> - Always mention **statutory** and **classification** aspects separately in answers worth > 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Survey During Transfer of Ships** is a **transaction-linked inspection** — not a routine class survey.
- The survey window is typically **within 30 days** of vessel delivery.
- The **75% rule** is the universal threshold: renew if $t_{measured} < 0.75 \times t_{as-built}$.
- **Corrosion allowance** check: renew if $t_{measured} < (t_{as-built} - t_{ca})$.
- Six types of surveys may be triggered: **S&P, Pre-Delivery, Class Transfer, Flag Transfer, Lay-up/Reactivation, In-Water**.
- Three priority categories of findings: **A (Immediate), B (Within 3 months), C (Next survey)**.
- **IACS UR Z7.1** governs thickness measurement; **IACS UR Z23** governs survey reporting.
- **Documentation** to review: GA plan, trunk section, class records, CoC, MoA, statutory certificates.
- **Critical inspection zones:** Cargo holds, ballast tanks, double bottom, side shell, hatch coamings, bow/stern appendages.
- **Typical corrosion rates:** 0.10–0.30 mm/year — useful for **remaining life estimation**.
- **Categorisation of findings** drives commercial outcomes: retention money, indemnity claims, price adjustments.
- **Lay-up reactivation** is a unique transfer scenario requiring verification of preservation, cathodic protection, machinery, and statutory equipment.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mnemonic for KTU viva:** *"A transfer survey protects the BUYER, the SELLER, the BANK, and the SHIP itself — in that order of urgency!"*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Surveying Agencies - Roles and Responsibilities of Classification Societies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marine Surveying Agencies - Roles and Responsibilities of Classification Societies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Marine Surveying Agencies** are specialized, internationally recognized technical organizations that conduct independent technical inspections, audits, surveys, and certifications of ships and marine structures to verify compliance with established rules, standards, and statutory regulations.

**Classification Societies** are non-governmental, self-regulating organizations that establish and apply technical rules and standards for the design, construction, and operational maintenance of ships and offshore structures. They assign and maintain a vessel's "Class" — a certification of structural and mechanical fitness for the intended service — by performing systematic surveys throughout the ship's operational life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Classification Societies form the **third pillar** of maritime safety alongside (1) Flag State Administrations and (2) Port State Control. For a marine engineer dealing with hull inspection, understanding Class is non-negotiable.

The principal regulatory framework governing classification work is the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**, founded in **1968**, which currently comprises **12 member societies** representing approximately **90% of the world's gross tonnage** of merchant shipping.

### 1.2 The Major IACS Member Societies

| Society | Country of Origin | Founding Year | Common Abbreviation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lloyd's Register | United Kingdom | 1760 | LR |
| Bureau Veritas | France | 1828 | BV |
| DNV (Det Norske Veritas) | Norway | 1864 | DNV |
| American Bureau of Shipping | USA | 1862 | ABS |
| Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (ClassNK) | Japan | 1899 | NK |
| Registro Italiano Navale | Italy | 1861 | RINA |
| Korean Register of Shipping | South Korea | 1960 | KR |
| China Classification Society | China | 1956 | CCS |
| Indian Register of Shipping | India | 1975 | IRS |
| Russian Maritime Register of Shipping | Russia | 1913 | RS |
| Polish Register of Shipping | Poland | 1936 | PRS |
| Croatian Register of Shipping | Croatia | 1949 | CRS |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship's Medical & Engineering Fitness Authority"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Ship as a Patient**
>
> Imagine a ship as a **human body**:
> - The **Hull** is the skeleton
> - The **Machinery** is the heart and lungs
> - The **Cargo spaces** are the stomach
> - The **Classification Society** acts as the **annual health inspector + certifying doctor**
> - The **Class Certificate** is the medical fitness certificate
> - **Surveys** are the periodic health check-ups
>
> Just as a doctor certifies a pilot fit to fly by conducting systematic tests, a Classification Society certifies a ship fit to sail by conducting systematic surveys.

### 1.4 Why Classification Societies Exist — The Historical Context

Before classification societies, the seas were notoriously dangerous. Insurance underwriters in **17th-century London coffee houses** (notably Edward Lloyd's Coffee House, founded **1686**) needed a way to assess the seaworthiness of vessels they insured. This led to the registration of ships into "classes" based on hull condition — thus was born **Lloyd's Register of Shipping (1760)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory SeaSense:** Classification is **NOT** a statutory requirement under international maritime law (SOLAS/MARPOL), BUT in practice, **virtually every commercial vessel** in the world trades with class, because:
> - Insurers will not provide Hull & Machinery (H&M) cover without valid class
> - Charterers and cargo underwriters (P&I Clubs) demand it
> - Flag States delegate statutory surveys to Recognized Organizations (ROs) — which are almost always Class Societies
> - Port State Control inspectors can detain an unclassed ship

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Hierarchy of Maritime Oversight

A hull inspector must clearly distinguish between these three distinct authorities:

1. **Flag State Administration (Maritime Authority of the ship's registry country)**
   - Examples: MCA (UK), USCG (USA), DG Shipping (India), Panama Maritime Authority
   - Issues statutory certificates (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, etc.)
   - **Delegates** survey work to Classification Societies as "Recognized Organizations (ROs)"

2. **Classification Society (Technical body)**
   - Sets **class rules** (own standards, often exceeding statutory minimums)
   - Issues **Class Certificates**
   - Conducts **classification surveys**

3. **Port State Control (Coastal state's inspection authority)**
   - Examples: Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian Ocean MoU
   - Inspects foreign ships calling at their ports
   - Can detain substandard vessels

### 2.2 Core Roles of Classification Societies (Structured Breakdown)

#### **Role 1: Development and Maintenance of Technical Rules**
- Each society publishes comprehensive **Rules and Regulations** for:
  - Ship hull structural design (longitudinal strength, plate thickness, frame spacing)
  - Machinery installations (main engines, auxiliaries, boilers)
  - Electrical systems
  - Materials and welding standards
  - Special service craft (tankers, gas carriers, bulk carriers, container ships)
- **IACS Unified Requirements (URs)** — common technical standards that all members must adopt, e.g., **UR S21** for longitudinal strength standard, **UR Z17** for hull surveys

#### **Role 2: Plan Approval (Design Review)**
- Review and approval of all technical drawings and calculations **before** construction begins
- Hull girder scantlings, structural arrangements, buckling checks, fatigue analysis
- Machinery arrangements, piping systems, electrical one-line diagrams
- **Deliverable:** Approved plan set with Class stamp

#### **Role 3: Survey During Construction (New Building)**
- Continuous on-site supervision at the shipyard
- Stage-by-stage inspections:
  - Steel mill approval and material certification
  - Welding procedure qualification
  - Plate and profile inspection before cutting
  - Block and sub-block dimensional checks
  - Hull erection alignment
  - Launching survey
  - Sea trials
- **Deliverable:** Class Certificate (initial)

#### **Role 4: Classification Surveys (In-Service Period)**
- Systematic periodic surveys to maintain class
- The cornerstone of hull inspection work

#### **Role 5: Statutory Certification (Delegated Functions)**
- Acts as a **Recognized Organization (RO)** on behalf of Flag States
- Conducts surveys and issues statutory certificates on behalf of the Administration:
  - International Load Line Certificate
  - SOLAS-related safety construction certificates
  - MARPOL certificates (IOPP, ISPP, IAPP, IEE)
  - International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)
  - Minimum Safe Manning Document (sometimes)

#### **Role 6: Technical Advisory and Consultancy**
- Condition assessment of older vessels
- Independent calculations and finite element analysis
- Forensic investigation of casualties
- Conversion and modification approvals
- Sustainability and decarbonization advisory

### 2.3 The Survey Cycle — Heart of Hull Inspection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Year Class Cycle** is the most critical concept in this module for KTU exams.

A new vessel enters service with a Class Certificate valid for **5 years**. The Class cycle consists of:

| Survey Type | When Conducted | Scope | Marks Weightage Hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Annual Survey (AS)** | Within 3 months before/after each anniversary date | General examination, hull externally (above waterline), machinery spaces, fire-fighting equipment | 3-mark concept |
| **Intermediate Survey (IS)** | Between 2nd and 3rd Annual Survey (i.e., at or near 3rd anniversary) | More detailed than AS; internal hull examination via tanks/cofferdams; thickness measurements in suspect areas | 14-mark essay favorite |
| **Special Survey (SS) / Renewal Survey** | Every 5 years — for class renewal | Most comprehensive survey; complete internal and external hull; full thickness measurement (gauge) of all major structural items; machinery open-up; tailshaft withdrawal | 14-mark essay favorite |
| **Continuous Survey (CS)** | Alternative to SS — survey spread continuously over 5-year cycle, with yearly quota (e.g., 20% of hull) | Hull is divided into zones; each zone surveyed every year on a rolling basis | Concept question |
| **Docking Survey** | Twice within any 5-year period (with maximum intervals) | Hull inspection in dry-dock; bottom plating, propellers, sea valves, rudder | Important for hull inspectors |
| **Propeller Shaft & Stern Tube Survey** | Every 5 years (or as per class rules) | Shaft withdrawal, bearing inspection | 3-mark concept |
| **Boiler Survey** | Periodic (2-3 years depending on type) | Internal inspection, hydrostatic test | Less focus for hull module |

### 2.4 IACS Unified Requirements (URs) — The Common Standards

IACS has developed **Unified Requirements (URs)** that all member societies must implement. Some key ones for hull inspection:

- **UR Z7** — Hull surveys for oil tankers
- **UR Z9** — Hull surveys for bulk carriers (ESP — Enhanced Survey Programme)
- **UR Z10** — Hull surveys for ships other than oil tankers and bulk carriers
- **UR Z17** — Procedural requirements for service craft
- **UR S21** — Longitudinal strength standard
- **UR S22** — Direct strength assessment
- **UR S23** — Buckling assessment
- **UR Z23** — Thickness measurement for hull structural members

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

For a marine engineer engaged in hull inspection:
- Knowing which Class your vessel is registered with dictates the **exact survey scope and thickness measurement criteria** you must follow
- Different societies have slightly different rules, though IACS harmonization has narrowed differences
- Classification society surveyors are the **client's technical representative** alongside the owner
- Survey reports become **legal documents** used in insurance claims, casualties, and P&I disputes

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Process Matrix

### 3.1 The Complete Classification Process — Step-by-Step Workflow

**Step 1: Owner Applies for Class**
The shipowner (or newbuilding yard) selects a Classification Society and submits the **Application for Classification** along with the **Contract Specification** and **General Arrangement Plan**.

**Step 2: Plan Approval Phase**
The Class society's Plan Approval Office (often called "Technical Office" or "Engineering Centre") reviews:
- Midship section drawing
- Shell expansion plan
- Deck and bottom plate arrangements
- Transverse section modulus calculations
- Local strength calculations for openings
- Material specifications
- Welding procedure specifications

**Step 3: Survey During New Construction**
The Class surveyor assigned to the yard (called the **"Surveyor-in-Charge"** or **"Resident Surveyor"**) conducts:
- Material mill certificate verification
- Steel plate and section identification (Class stamps)
- Welding procedure qualification tests (witness)
- Fabrication stage surveys (cutting, forming, fit-up, welding)
- Erection surveys
- Launching survey
- Mooring and sea trial attendance

**Step 4: Issuance of Initial Class Certificate**
Upon satisfactory completion of all newbuilding surveys and sea trials, the Class Certificate is issued. The vessel is now "in class."

**Step 5: Operational Survey Cycle Commences**
The vessel enters the 5-year class cycle. Surveys are conducted at intervals as described in the Class cycle table.

**Step 6: Class Renewal / Special Survey**
At the **5th anniversary**, a Special Survey (SS) is performed. If satisfactory, the Class Certificate is renewed for another 5 years, and the cycle repeats.

**Step 7: Suspension / Withdrawal of Class (If Applicable)**
If surveys are not completed within the time limits or if the vessel is found unfit, Class may be:
- **Suspended** (temporary, with time-bound conditions to rectify)
- **Withdrawn** (vessel loses Class entirely)
- **Reinstated** (after satisfactory rectification and re-survey)

### 3.2 The 5-Year Class Cycle — Mathematical Expression

Let $T_0$ be the date of initial classification (keel laying or delivery date, depending on society rules). Then:

$$\text{Annual Survey Year: } T_{AS}(n) = T_0 + n \cdot (1 \text{ year}), \quad n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$$

$$\text{Intermediate Survey Year: } T_{IS} = T_0 + (2 \text{ to } 3) \text{ years}$$

$$\text{Special Survey (Renewal): } T_{SS}(k) = T_0 + k \cdot (5 \text{ years}), \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Window concept** — Class surveys have **tolerance windows** rather than exact dates:

$$\text{Survey Window} = T_{\text{scheduled}} \pm \Delta t$$

Where $\Delta t$ is typically **3 months** for Annual Surveys and **6 months** for Special Surveys (as per IACS UR Z1.1).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Class vs. Statutory — Clear Distinction

| Parameter | Classification Survey | Statutory Survey |
|---|---|---|
| **Authority Issuing** | Classification Society (IACS member) | Flag State Administration (or delegated RO) |
| **Legal Basis** | Society's own Rules (private contract) | IMO Conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, etc.) |
| **Mandatory?** | Not by international law, but by market/insurance | Mandatory under international conventions ratified by flag state |
| **Frequency** | 5-year cycle (AS, IS, SS) | Varies by convention (1-5 years) |
| **Scope** | Hull, machinery, electrical, automation | Safety, pollution prevention, load line, tonnage |
| **Certificate Type** | Class Certificate | Statutory Certificate (e.g., IOPP, IAPP) |
| **Consequence of Failure** | Loss of class → uninsurable → may be commercially scrapped | Detention by Port State Control, criminal liability |

### 3.4 Thickness Measurement — The Quantitative Backbone of Hull Surveys

During Special Surveys, **Close-Up Surveys (CUS)** and **Thickness Measurements (TM)** are mandatory. The procedure:

**Step 1:** Define the survey scope based on ship type (e.g., ESP for bulk carriers, Tanker ESP for oil tankers).

**Step 2:** Identify "critical structural areas" — locations with high stress concentration:
- Bilge strakes
- Sheer strakes
- Deck plating at hatch corners
- Transverse bulkheads
- Bracket toes
- Rudder horn and boss

**Step 3:** Mark the **gauge points** on the hull (e.g., ABS/Class rules specify minimum number of points per structural member).

**Step 4:** Take **ultrasonic thickness (UT) measurements** at each point using calibrated thickness gauges.

**Step 5:** Compare actual thickness $t_{actual}$ with **allowable minimum thickness** $t_{allowable}$ from Class rules:

$$\text{Acceptance Criterion: } t_{actual} \geq t_{allowable}$$

Where:

$$t_{allowable} = f(\text{Design scantling}, \text{Corrosion addition}, \text{Material grade}, \text{Frame spacing})$$

**Step 6:** Calculate **substantial corrosion** (typically $\geq 75\%$ of corrosion addition consumed) and **localized substantial corrosion** (isolated pitting/edge corrosion).

**Step 7:** Recommend **renewal (steel replacement)** or **repair (insert plate, doubler plate)** as appropriate.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
