## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a Classification Society and explain the role of IACS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Classification Society** is an independent, non-governmental technical organization that establishes and applies rules for the design, construction, and in-service survey of ships and offshore structures, assigning and maintaining their "Class" as a certification of fitness for service.

The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**, founded in **1968**, is the body that represents the major classification societies. Currently comprising **12 member societies** covering over **90% of world merchant tonnage**, IACS develops **Unified Requirements (URs)** that all members must adopt, ensuring harmonized technical standards globally. Major IACS members include Lloyd's Register (UK), Bureau Veritas (France), DNV (Norway), ABS (USA), and ClassNK (Japan). IACS also represents classification interests at the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between Class Survey and Statutory Survey in 6 points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Class Survey | Statutory Survey |
|---|---|---|
| Authority | Classification Society | Flag State (or RO delegated) |
| Legal Basis | Society's own Rules | IMO Conventions |
| Mandatory? | Market/Insurance-driven | Legally mandatory |
| Scope | Hull, machinery, electrical, automation | Safety, pollution, load line |
| Certificate | Class Certificate | Statutory Certificates (IOPP, Load Line) |
| Consequence of Lapse | Loss of Class, uninsurable | Detention, criminal liability |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Q3A.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / May 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]
**Explain in detail the roles and responsibilities of Classification Societies in the maritime industry. Discuss the types of classification surveys conducted during a vessel's operational life.**

**(a) Roles and Responsibilities of Classification Societies — 7 Marks**

Classification Societies perform six core functions:

**1. Development of Technical Rules and Standards** — Each society publishes comprehensive rules covering hull structural design, scantlings, materials, welding, machinery installations, and electrical systems. These rules are continuously updated to incorporate new technology, lessons from casualties, and IACS Unified Requirements.

**2. Plan Approval** — Before construction, the society reviews and approves all technical drawings, calculations, and specifications. This includes midship section, shell expansion, longitudinal strength calculations, local strength for openings, and material specifications.

**3. Survey During New Construction** — Resident surveyors at the shipyard conduct stage-by-stage inspections from steel mill to sea trials, ensuring the vessel is built in accordance with approved plans and rules.

**4. Periodic Classification Surveys** — Once the vessel enters service, the society conducts Annual, Intermediate, and Special Surveys on a 5-year cycle to verify continued compliance and seaworthiness.

**5. Statutory Certification on Behalf of Flag States** — As Recognized Organizations (ROs), societies issue statutory certificates under SOLAS, MARPOL, and Load Line Conventions on behalf of the flag administration.

**6. Technical Advisory Services** — Including condition assessment of aging vessels, conversion approvals, casualty investigation, and increasingly, sustainability and decarbonization advisory services.

**(b) Types of Classification Surveys in Operational Life — 7 Marks**

The operational survey regime follows a structured **5-year Class Cycle**:

**1. Annual Survey (AS)** — Conducted within 3 months of each anniversary of the initial class date. Scope includes general visual examination of hull, machinery spaces, anchors and chains, and safety equipment. Above-waterline hull plating and accessible internal spaces are examined.

**2. Intermediate Survey (IS)** — Conducted between the 2nd and 3rd Annual Survey (at or near the 3rd anniversary). More extensive than AS; includes internal examination of selected tanks, cofferdams, and pump rooms. Thickness measurements in suspect areas. May be substituted with partial Special Survey scope.

**3. Special Survey (SS) / Class Renewal Survey** — The most comprehensive survey, conducted every 5 years for class renewal. Scope includes complete internal and external hull examination, full thickness measurement of all major structural members, machinery opening-up, tailshaft withdrawal, boilers, and steering gear overhaul.

**4. Continuous Survey (CS)** — An alternative to Special Survey where the SS scope is spread over the 5-year cycle on a rolling basis, e.g., 20% of hull examined each year.

**5. Docking Survey** — Required at least twice within any 5-year period for external underwater hull inspection, including bottom plating, propellers, sea valves, and rudder pintle bearings.

**6. Propeller Shaft / Stern Tube Survey** — Periodic survey involving shaft withdrawal, bearing inspection, and stern tube seal examination, typically aligned with docking.

**7. Boiler Survey** — Internal inspection, hydrostatic test of safety valves, and maintenance as per class rules.

Conducted together, these surveys ensure the vessel remains fit for service and its Class Certificate remains valid.

---

**Q3B (Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model]
**Discuss the institutional framework of maritime surveying. Explain the relationship between IMO, Flag State, Classification Society, and Port State Control with a suitable diagram.**

**(a) Institutional Framework — 7 Marks**

The international maritime regulatory architecture rests on four interconnected pillars:

**1. International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — The United Nations specialized agency headquartered in London, established in 1948. IMO develops international conventions, codes, and recommendations that form the global maritime regulatory framework. Key instruments include SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, STCW, and the IS Code. IMO does not enforce rules directly but provides the legal template for member states.

**2. Flag State Administration** — The maritime authority of the country where the ship is registered. Examples include DG Shipping (India), MCA (UK), USCG (USA). Flag States are responsible for implementing IMO conventions and ensuring ships flying their flag comply with international rules. They issue the official statutory certificates.

**3. Classification Society** — A non-governmental technical body (e.g., LR, BV, DNV, ABS) that provides independent verification of design, construction, and operational condition. Classification Societies do not have law-making authority but are delegated by Flag States (under formal RO Agreement) to conduct statutory surveys on their behalf. They also issue their own Class Certificate based on their own rules.

**4. Port State Control (PSC)** — The inspection regime operated by coastal states to verify that foreign ships visiting their ports comply with international rules. Examples include the Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian Ocean MoU. PSC can detain substandard vessels and has been a major driver for raising quality standards globally.

**(b) Relationship and Flow — 7 Marks**

The flow of authority and responsibility is hierarchical and complementary:

- **IMO** sets the international standard (conventions).
- **Flag States** ratify conventions and translate them into national law.
- **Flag States delegate** survey work to **Classification Societies** (Recognized Organizations) via formal written agreements.
- **Classification Societies** conduct surveys and issue both Class Certificates (under their own rules) and Statutory Certificates (on behalf of Flag States).
- **Owners/Operators** voluntarily engage a Class Society for classification because insurers, charterers, and banks demand it.
- **Port State Control** independently inspects foreign ships at ports, cross-checking certificates issued by both Flag State and Class.
- **IACS** (representing 12 member societies) speaks collectively to IMO and ensures harmonization of class rules through Unified Requirements.

The relationship is **not adversarial** but a system of mutual checks. While Class provides proactive technical oversight, PSC provides reactive enforcement. Together, they create a multi-layered safety net for the maritime industry.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "Class" with "Flag":** Students often interchange Flag State and Class. Remember: Flag issues statutory certificates; Class issues class certificates. A ship must have BOTH.
> 2. **Missing the "RO" concept:** Failing to mention that Classification Societies act as **Recognized Organizations** delegated by Flag States loses at least 2 marks in any institutional question.
> 3. **Listing surveys without explaining windows:** Don't just list Annual, Intermediate, and Special Surveys — state the timing (e.g., "between 2nd and 3rd anniversary" for IS). Vague answers lose marks.
> 4. **Ignoring IACS:** Any question on classification societies without IACS is incomplete. IACS governs harmonization of standards across 12 societies.
> 5. **Forgetting thickness measurement in SS:** When describing Special Survey, always mention that **full thickness measurement (gauge)** of all principal structural members is mandatory. This is the quantitative backbone of class decisions.
> 6. **Mixing Class survey types with statutory certificates:** For example, Load Line Certificate is statutory, NOT class. IOPP is statutory. Class Certificate is class. Don't mix them up.
> 7. **Skipping the "why":** Examiners reward answers that explain WHY (e.g., "Class is technically mandatory because insurance requires it") not just WHAT.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Classification Society** = independent technical body that certifies ship fitness through class rules and surveys
- [ ] **IACS** = International Association of Classification Societies; 12 members; >90% world tonnage
- [ ] **Major IACS Members (Memorize):** LR, BV, DNV, ABS, NK, RINA, KR, CCS, IRS, RS, PRS, CRS
- [ ] **Three Pillars of Maritime Safety:** Flag State, Classification Society, Port State Control
- [ ] **Class is not legally mandatory** BUT commercially indispensable (insurance, chartering, P&I)
- [ ] **Recognized Organization (RO)** = Class acting on behalf of Flag State for statutory work
- [ ] **Plan Approval → Survey During Construction → Operational Surveys** is the workflow
- [ ] **5-Year Class Cycle:** AS + IS + SS (or CS alternative) + Docking Survey
- [ ] **Annual Survey** = every year, ±3 months window, general examination
- [ ] **Intermediate Survey** = between 2nd and 3rd AS, partial internal examination
- [ ] **Special Survey** = every 5 years, comprehensive, full thickness measurement, machinery opening-up
- [ ] **Continuous Survey (CS)** = alternative to SS, spread across 5 years on rolling basis
- [ ] **Docking Survey** = at least twice in 5 years, underwater hull in dry dock
- [ ] **Thickness Measurement** is mandatory at SS using ultrasonic gauges; compares actual vs. allowable
- [ ] **IACS Unified Requirements (URs)** = common standards (e.g., UR Z7, Z9, Z10, Z17, Z23)
- [ ] **Class Certificate vs. Statutory Certificate** — never confuse them
- [ ] **Suspension / Withdrawal / Reinstatement** of Class are the three regulatory actions
- [ ] **Classification Survey is a contract between Owner and Society**, not a government function
- [ ] **Port State Control** can detain vessels with expired or invalid class certificates
- [ ] **Lloyd's Register (1760)** is the oldest, originating from Edward Lloyd's Coffee House in 1686
- [ ] **IACS speaks collectively to IMO** — Class rules often lead statutory regulations

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statutory Surveys - Role of MMD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Statutory Surveys - Role of MMD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Statutory Survey** is a mandatory, legally binding inspection of a seagoing vessel conducted on behalf of, or recognized by, the **Flag State Administration** to verify compliance with the requirements of national maritime legislation and international conventions ratified by that state. For Indian-flagged vessels, the **Mercantile Marine Department (MMD)** — operating under the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS)**, Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways, Government of India — acts as the competent Flag State Authority empowered to issue, endorse, suspend, or revoke statutory certificates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory surveys are non-negotiable legal obligations** imposed by international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, Tonnage, COLREG, STCW, MLC) and enforced through national acts such as the **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 (as amended)**. A vessel cannot legally trade without valid statutory certificates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Statutory Survey** like the **annual fitness inspection of a commercial passenger bus** carried out by the Regional Transport Authority (RTA) in India. Just as the RTA does not build the bus (the manufacturer does) and does not set private warranty standards (the dealer does), but instead verifies *public safety* before the bus is allowed to carry the public — the MMD similarly does not construct the ship, does not certify its *private* design integrity (that is the Classification Society's job), but instead confirms that the ship is *legally safe* for the **public good** and **international maritime safety standards**.

The three pillars of ship integrity verification are:

| Pillar | Conducted By | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Statutory Survey** | **Flag State (MMD)** or its Recognized Organization (RO) | Compliance with **law and international conventions** |
| **Classification Survey** | **Classification Society** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, BV, ABS) | Compliance with **private design and construction rules** |
| **Operational / ISM Audit** | **Company / Flag State** | Compliance with **safety management systems** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction**: Classification surveys cover hull scantlings, structural strength, and machinery rules — they are *commercial* in nature. Statutory surveys verify life-saving appliances, pollution prevention, load line limits, and crew conditions — they are *legal* in nature. A ship must satisfy **both** to trade legally.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Maximum interval between renewal surveys (Load Line): 5 years**
- **Annual survey tolerance window: ±3 months** from the anniversary date
- **Continuous survey cycle (machinery): 5-year cycle** with ≤5 years between any two consecutive examinations
- **Minimum freeboard requirements** governed by **Regulation 27–40** of the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966/1988 Protocol**
- **MARPOL Annex VI EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) baseline reduction: **Phase 1 (2015) = 10%**, **Phase 2 (2020) = 20%**, **Phase 3 (2025) = 30%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Statutory Compliance

The statutory survey regime operates on **three concentric regulatory layers**:

1. **International Layer** — IMO Conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, ICLL, STCW, COLREG, MLC 2006, Ballast Water Management Convention, Hong Kong Convention).
2. **Regional / Flag State Layer** — National maritime legislation. In India: **Merchant Shipping Act 1958**, **Indian Ports Act 1908**, **Coast Guard Act 1978**, rules and notifications by DGS.
3. **Delegated Layer** — Recognized Organizations (ROs). For India, the **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** is the primary RO, authorized to act on behalf of MMD under formal **Authorization Agreement** compliant with **Resolution A.739(18)** and audited under **Resolution A.789(19)** of IMO.

### 2.2 Core Types of Statutory Surveys

The statutory survey regime requires the following structured sequence:

- **Initial Survey**: Conducted **before the ship is put into service** or before a new certificate is issued. Verifies that the ship fully complies with all applicable convention requirements at the time of construction/conversion.
- **Annual Survey**: A general inspection carried out **within 3 months before or after** each anniversary date of the statutory certificate. Confirms continued compliance.
- **Intermediate Survey**: Performed **between the 2nd and 3rd anniversary** (for Load Line and SOLAS Construction-related certificates), or in lieu of one annual survey.
- **Periodical Survey**: Conducted at intervals **not exceeding 5 years** (e.g., for Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate).
- **Renewal Survey**: Comprehensive survey leading to **renewal of the statutory certificate**, prior to expiry.
- **Additional / Occasional Survey**: Triggered by **damage, repair, alteration, or change of flag/ownership**.

### 2.3 Major Statutory Certificates Issued/Recognized by MMD (India)

The Indian MMD framework recognizes the following principal statutory certificates:

| # | Certificate | Convention | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate** | SOLAS Ch. II-1, II-2 | 5 years |
| 2 | **Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate** | SOLAS Ch. III | 5 years |
| 3 | **Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificate** | SOLAS Ch. IV | 5 years |
| 4 | **Minimum Safe Manning Document** | SOLAS Reg. V/14 | As required |
| 5 | **International Load Line Certificate** | ICLL 1966 | 5 years |
| 6 | **International Oil Pollution Prevention (IOPP) Certificate** | MARPOL Annex I | 5 years |
| 7 | **International Air Pollution Prevention (IAPP) Certificate** | MARPOL Annex VI | 5 years |
| 8 | **International Energy Efficiency (IEE) Certificate** | MARPOL Annex VI | 5 years |
| 9 | **International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificate** | MARPOL Annex IV | 5 years |
| 10 | **International Ballast Water Management Certificate** | BWM Convention | 5 years |
| 11 | **Certificate of Fitness for Dangerous Goods** | SOLAS Ch. VII | 5 years |
| 12 | **Document of Compliance (DoC) – Dangerous Goods** | SOLAS Ch. VII | — |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Survey Mathematics & Boundary Conditions**

| Parameter | Formula / Limit | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Survey Tolerance Window | $\Delta t_{annual} = \pm 3$ | months |
| Renewal Survey Period (Load Line) | $T_{RL} \le 5$ | years |
| Intermediate Survey Window | $t \in [T_{0}+2, \, T_{0}+3]$ | years |
| Survey Window Extension (grace) | $T_{ext} \le 3$ | months (only for renewal, if return voyage is in progress) |
| Load Line Freeboard (Type B, 100% LBP) | $F_{B} = f\!\left(L, \, \text{Type}, \, \text{Correction}\right)$ | mm |
| Plimsoll Mark Position (Summer) | $D_{s} = F_{B,s}$ from deck line | mm |
| Subdivision Index (SOLAS Ch. II-1) | $R = \sum \left(p_i \cdot s_i\right)$ | dimensionless |
| EEDI Reference Line Speed | $V_{ref} \approx 0.75 \cdot V_{max}$ at **75% MCR** | knots |
| EEDI Reduction (vs baseline) | $EEDI_{req} = EEDI_{base} \cdot (1 - X/100)$ | g-CO₂/ton-mile |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The statutory survey regime directly impacts **ship design and operational economics**. Designers must anticipate statutory survey access points — manholes, internal compartment clearances, hull penetrations, and structural hot-spots — because the surveyors must physically enter and inspect these spaces. A poorly designed access arrangement leads to **survey failure, expensive retrofit, or operational detention** by Port State Control (Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian Ocean MoU). Statutory surveys are therefore an **integral design constraint** in shipbuilding, not merely an after-sales regulatory hurdle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Operational Workflow of an MMD Statutory Survey

The complete procedural breakdown of an MMD-conducted or MMD-recognized statutory survey follows a **ten-stage analytical matrix**:

**Stage 1 — Survey Notification**
The shipowner or operator submits a **Survey Intimation Form** to the jurisdictional MMD office (e.g., Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, Kochi, Goa) **at least 30 days** before the due date of the survey or the certificate's anniversary.

**Stage 2 — Document Pre-Verification**
The surveyor reviews the **previous survey records, statutory file, class records, and outstanding conditions/recommendations**. Verification is done that the vessel's particulars (LOA, LBP, GT, NT, propulsion type) match the certificate data.

**Stage 3 — Hull External Inspection**
Conducted **in dry-dock or afloat (with diver/ROV)**. Checks include:

$$
\text{Condition}_i \in \{\text{Intact, Minor Repair, Major Repair, Replace}\}
$$

The surveyor assesses plating, shell plating, bilge keels, sea valves, propeller, rudder, and cathodic protection anodes.

**Stage 4 — Hull Internal Inspection**
Inspection of internal spaces — tanks, double bottoms, peak tanks, cofferdams, cargo holds, engine room, pump rooms, and tunnels — for **corrosion, deformation, wastage, and structural integrity**.

**Stage 5 — Machinery and Electrical Survey**
Examination of main engine, auxiliary engines, boilers, steering gear, bilge pumping arrangements, electrical installations, and emergency power sources.

**Stage 6 — Life-Saving, Fire-Fighting, and Radio Survey**
Verification of **SOLAS Chapter III** requirements (lifeboats, liferafts, lifejackets, EPIRBs, SARTs), **Chapter II-2** (fire detection, fixed fire-extinguishing systems, fireman's outfit), and **Chapter IV** (GMDSS radio equipment).

**Stage 7 — Load Line Verification**
Measurement of freeboard marks, verification of hull markings, hatchway covers, ventilators, air pipes, freeing ports, and guard rails against the **approved Stability Booklet** and **Load Line Assignment**.

**Stage 8 — Pollution Prevention Inspection**
Check of oily water separator (OWS), sewage treatment plant (STP), incinerator, ozone-depleting substance record book, garbage management plan, and IAPP/IOPP documentation.

**Stage 9 — Survey Report Compilation**
The surveyor prepares a **Survey Report** with findings, conditions, and a recommendation: **"Issue / Renew / Endorse / Withhold"** the certificate.

**Stage 10 — Certificate Action**
- If compliant: MMD issues or endorses the **statutory certificate**.
- If non-compliant: **Letter of Warning → Conditional Pass → Detention** under Section 333–336 of the Merchant Shipping Act, 1958.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Statutory Survey Decision Tree

$$
\text{Statutory Compliance Status} = f(\text{Convention}, \, \text{Survey Type}, \, \text{Findings})
$$

The decision tree resolves as follows:

$$
\text{Result} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Full Compliance} & \text{if } \forall i, \; \text{Finding}_i = \text{Satisfactory} \\
\text{Conditional Pass} & \text{if } \exists i, \; \text{Finding}_i \in \text{Minor Defect} \\
\text{Remedial Required} & \text{if } \exists i, \; \text{Finding}_i \in \text{Major Defect} \\
\text{Detention} & \text{if } \exists i, \; \text{Finding}_i \in \text{Critical Non-Conformity}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.3 Role of MMD — Step-by-Step Enumeration

The Mercantile Marine Department (MMD) executes the following core statutory functions:

1. **Survey Authorization** — Approves the engagement of Recognized Organizations (ROs) like IRS for delegated surveys.
2. **Surveyor Appointment** — Empowers Marine Surveyors-cum-Deputy Director General (MS-cum-DDG) and Assistant Surveyor Officers to act on its behalf.
3. **Certificate Custodian** — Holds the original or authenticated copy of every statutory certificate issued to Indian-flagged vessels.
4. **Audit of Recognized Organizations** — Conducts annual performance audits of ROs as mandated by **Resolution A.789(19)**.
5. **Port State Control** — Coordinates with the Indian Coast Guard and Directorate under the **Merchant Shipping Act** for inspection of foreign vessels calling at Indian ports.
6. **Incident Investigation** — Participates in marine casualty investigations under **Sections 352–358** of the Merchant Shipping Act, 1958.
7. **Flag State Inspection** — Conducts unannounced inspections on Indian vessels under the **Flag State Implementation (FSI)** framework.
8. **Detention Authority** — Empowered to detain vessels under **Section 339** of the Merchant Shipping Act.
9. **Manning & Certification Oversight** — Issues Certificates of Competency (CoC) and Endorsements (CoE) for seafarers.
10. **Enforcement of MLC 2006** — Inspects seafarer working conditions, complaints, and repatriation obligations.

### 3.4 Worked Example — EEDI Compliance Calculation

Given: A new bulk carrier of **40,000 DWT**, **MCR = 12,000 kW**, service speed at MCR = **15 knots**, with **CO₂ emissions per tonne-mile baseline = 25 g**.

**Step 1**: Reference line speed for EEDI baseline (75% MCR):

$$
V_{ref} = 0.75 \cdot V_{MCR} = 0.75 \times 15 = 11.25 \; \text{knots}
$$

**Step 2**: Applicable reduction factor (Phase 2, 2020 for bulk carrier ≥ 20,000 DWT):

$$
X = 20\%
$$

**Step 3**: Required Attained EEDI:

$$
EEDI_{req} = EEDI_{base} \times (1 - X/100) = 25 \times (1 - 0.20) = 20 \; \text{g-CO}_2/\text{ton-mile}
$$

**Step 4**: Interpretation — the vessel's attained EEDI must be $\le 20$ g-CO₂/ton-mile, otherwise the **IEE Certificate is refused** and the vessel cannot trade internationally.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Statutory Survey Governance Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[IMO - International Maritime Organization] -->|Sets Conventions| nodeB[Flag State - India via DGS]
    nodeB -->|Delegates via Section 8 of MS Act| nodeC[MMD - Mercantile Marine Department]
    nodeC -->|Authorizes under A.739 18| nodeD[Recognized Organization - IRS]
    nodeC -->|Direct Survey by MS-cum-DDG| nodeE[Indian Flagged Vessel]
    nodeD -->|Conducts Delegated Survey| nodeE
    nodeE -->|Statutory Certificate Issued| nodeF[Ship May Trade Internationally]
    nodeE -->|Certificate Held by| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Audit per A.789 19| nodeD
    nodeC -->|FSI Inspections| nodeE
    nodeG[Port State Control - Coast Guard] -->|Inspects Foreign| nodeH[Foreign Vessels in Indian Ports]
    nodeH -->|Detention if non compliant| nodeC
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Statutory Survey Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Shipowner Intimation] --> nodeB[MMD Jurisdictional Office]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Pre Survey Document Review]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Surveyor Assignment]
    nodeD --> nodeE[On Board Inspection]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Hull External Check]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Hull Internal Check]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Machinery Survey]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Life Saving Fire Radio Survey]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Load Line Verification]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Pollution Control Survey]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Survey Report Compilation]
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM{Findings Compliant}
    nodeM -->|Yes| nodeN[Certificate Issued Endorsed Renewed]
    nodeM -->|No| nodeO[Letter of Warning or Detention]

    nodeP --> nodeM
```

### 4.3 MMD Functional Role Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[MMD Head Office - Mumbai] --> nodeB[Regional MMD - Kolkata]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Regional MMD - Chennai]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Regional MMD - Kochi]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Regional MMD - Goa]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Surveyor Cadre - MS DDG]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -->|Statutory Functions| nodeG[Vessel Inspection]
    nodeF -->|Statutory Functions| nodeH[Certificate Custody]

    nodeF -->|Statutory Functions| nodeJ[Marine Casualty Investigation]
    nodeF -->|Statutory Functions| nodeK[Port State Control Support]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define statutory survey. State any two statutory certificates issued to a cargo ship under SOLAS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A statutory survey is a legally mandated inspection of a vessel conducted by or on behalf of the Flag State Administration to verify compliance with international maritime conventions and national maritime law. **(1 mark)**
Two statutory certificates issued to cargo ships under SOLAS: **(2 marks)**
- (i) Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate (SOLAS Chapter II-1 & II-2)
- (ii) Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate (SOLAS Chapter III)
- (iii) Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificate (SOLAS Chapter IV)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between a classification survey and a statutory survey.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Classification Survey | Statutory Survey |
|---|---|---|
| Authority | Classification Society (private) | Flag State Administration / MMD (public) |
| Basis | Classification rules | IMO Conventions & National Law |
| Purpose | Verify design/construction integrity | Verify legal safety and pollution compliance |
| Outcome | Class notation (e.g., ✪1A) | Statutory certificate (e.g., IOPP) |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Recommended Choice*

**Q. (a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the various types of statutory surveys required for an Indian-flagged cargo vessel under SOLAS and Load Line Convention.

**Q. (b) [7 marks]** Describe the organizational structure and statutory functions of the Mercantile Marine Department (MMD) of India.

#### Model Solution for Q.(a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Statutory surveys for Indian-flagged cargo vessels are governed by the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** and the **SOLAS Convention, 1974 (as amended)** along with the **International Convention on Load Lines, 1966/1988**.

**Types of Statutory Surveys (6 marks):**

1. **Initial Survey** — Conducted before the vessel is placed in service or before a new certificate is first issued. Verifies complete compliance at the construction/conversion stage.

2. **Annual Survey** — General inspection within ±3 months of the certificate's anniversary date. Covers visual examination and operational testing.

3. **Intermediate Survey** — Conducted between 2nd and 3rd anniversary, substituting for one annual survey. More detailed; includes internal examination of representative compartments.

4. **Periodical Survey** — Conducted within 5 years for Safety Construction Certificate. Comprehensive.

5. **Renewal Survey** — Required before expiry of the certificate. Includes dry-docking, internal examination of all compartments, and full machinery survey.

6. **Additional Survey** — Triggered by damage, repair, alteration, change of flag, or change of ownership.

**Conclusion:** (Optional closing statement) — These surveys ensure continued compliance throughout the vessel's operational life.

#### Model Solution for Q.(b):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The MMD operates under the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS)**, Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways.

**Organizational Structure (3 marks):**
- **Head Office at Mumbai** with regional offices at Kolkata, Chennai, Kochi, Goa, and other major ports.
- **Surveyor Cadre** comprising Marine Surveyor-cum-Deputy Director General (MS-cum-DDG), Senior Surveyors, and Assistant Surveyor Officers.

**Statutory Functions (3 marks):**
- (i) Survey and certification of Indian vessels under SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, MLC.
- (ii) Authorization and audit of Recognized Organizations (ROs) such as IRS.
- (iii) Investigation of marine casualties under Sections 352–358 of MS Act.
- (iv) Port State Control inspections of foreign vessels.
- (v) Issuance of seafarer certificates of competency.
- (vi) Enforcement of maritime safety and pollution regulations including detention under Section 339.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q. (a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches/flow diagrams, describe the step-by-step procedure of an MMD statutory survey for an Indian bulk carrier, from intimation to certificate issuance.

**Q. (b) [7 marks]** Explain the significance of the Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 in the context of hull inspection and statutory surveys.

#### Model Solution for Q.(a):

**Step-by-Step Procedure (7 marks):**
1. Submission of Survey Intimation Form by the shipowner to the jurisdictional MMD office at least 30 days prior. **(1 mark)**
2. Pre-survey documentation check including prior survey records, class file, and outstanding conditions. **(1 mark)**
3. Hull external survey (dry-dock or afloat with divers). **(1 mark)**
4. Hull internal survey of all accessible compartments. **(1 mark)**
5. Machinery, electrical, and steering gear examination. **(1 mark)**
6. Life-saving, fire-fighting, and radio survey; load line verification; pollution prevention check. **(1 mark)**
7. Compilation of Survey Report and decision — Issue / Endorse / Withhold the certificate. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution for Q.(b):

**Significance of Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 (7 marks):**
- **Section 4–10** — Administration and appointment of surveyors. **(1 mark)**
- **Section 11–30** — Registration and survey of Indian ships. **(1 mark)**
- **Section 213–233** — Load line requirements. **(1 mark)**
- **Section 274–291** — Safety and collision regulations. **(1 mark)**
- **Section 333–345** — Detention and survey of unsafe ships. **(1 mark)**
- **Section 352–358** — Marine casualty investigation. **(1 mark)**
- **Section 369–381** — Pollution control provisions aligned with MARPOL. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Confusing Statutory with Classification surveys** — They are *different*. Classification is voluntary and commercial; Statutory is mandatory and legal.
> - **Forgetting that MMD delegates to ROs** — In India, IRS often conducts the survey, but the **statutory certificate is ultimately MMD's responsibility**.
> - **Missing the international layer** — SOLAS, MARPOL, ICLL are IMO conventions; the MS Act 1958 is the *national* implementation.
> - **Not citing Section numbers** of MS Act 1958 in theory questions — Examiner awards marks for *specific* legal references.
> - **Skipping Port State Control** — Students often forget that MMD also plays a role in inspecting foreign vessels, not just Indian ones.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Statutory Survey** = Legal inspection by Flag State (MMD for India).
- ☐ **Classification Survey** = Private inspection by Classification Society (IRS in India).
- ☐ **MMD operates under DGS** which is under Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways.
- ☐ **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** is the primary national legislation.
- ☐ **Recognized Organizations (ROs)** like IRS conduct surveys on MMD's behalf under **Resolution A.739(18)** and are audited under **A.789(19)**.
- ☐ **Annual Survey tolerance** is **±3 months** from anniversary date.
- ☐ **Renewal Survey cycle** is generally **5 years**.
- ☐ **Key SOLAS certificates** — Construction, Equipment, Radio (each 5-year validity).
- ☐ **Key MARPOL certificates** — IOPP (Annex I), IAPP (Annex VI), IEE (Annex VI EEDI).
- ☐ **Load Line Convention** governs freeboard — Indian vessels require **International Load Line Certificate**.
- ☐ **EEDI reduction phases** — 10% (2015), 20% (2020), 30% (2025).
- ☐ **MMD can detain vessels** under Section 339 of MS Act 1958.
- ☐ **MMD investigates casualties** under Sections 352–358 of MS Act 1958.
- ☐ **Port State Control** is coordinated by Indian Coast Guard and supported by MMD.
- ☐ **MLC 2006** inspection (seafarer welfare) is also a MMD statutory function.
- ☐ **A vessel without valid statutory certificates is barred from international trade**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IWAI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IWAI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)** is the statutory body established by the Government of India under the **Inland Waterways Authority of India Act, 1985** (Act No. 82 of 1985), and was formally constituted on **27th October 1986**. It functions under the administrative control of the **Ministry of Shipping (now Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways)** and serves as the apex regulatory and developmental authority for the regulation and development of inland waterways for the purposes of shipping and navigation.

In the specific context of **Hull Inspection (OENST832)**, IWAI assumes the role of a **competent authority for inland vessels**, analogous to how the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS)** functions for seagoing vessels and the **Maritime Administration** functions for international flag state obligations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Under Module 1, IWAI is studied as the **statutory framework** that governs the survey, inspection, certification, and safety regulation of vessels operating on India's declared National Waterways.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highway authority** that manages roads. The highway authority sets speed limits, checks vehicle fitness certificates, builds flyovers, and issues licenses to drivers. **IWAI plays the exact same role for India's rivers and canals that have been declared as National Waterways.**

Just as you would not allow a rusted, unfit truck to carry passengers on a national highway, IWAI ensures that vessels plying on rivers like the Ganga, Brahmaputra, and the West Coast Canal are:
- **Structurally sound** (hull integrity)
- **Periodically inspected** (surveys)
- **Properly registered** (certification)
- **Operated safely** (manning, equipment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Think of IWAI as the "Highway Authority of Indian Rivers" — it builds, regulates, and ensures that every boat/ship is fit to navigate safely on the country's inland water network.

## 1.3 Key Physical and Statutory Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Statute Enacted** | Inland Waterways Authority of India Act, **1985** |
| **Date of Constitution** | **27th October 1986** |
| **Headquarters** | **Noida, Uttar Pradesh, India** |
| **Administrative Ministry** | Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways |
| **Original National Waterways (1986)** | 5 (NW-1 to NW-5) |
| **Total National Waterways (Current)** | **111 National Waterways** |
| **Key Operational NW** | NW-1 (Ganga), NW-2 (Brahmaputra), NW-3 (West Coast Canal), NW-4 (Krishna-Godavari), NW-5 (Brahmani-Mahanadi) |
| **Mode of Inspection** | Periodical Surveys, Annual Surveys, Special Surveys, and Docking Surveys |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of IWAI's Operational Framework

The operations of IWAI in hull inspection can be categorized into the following structured logical pillars:

### **Pillar 1: Regulatory and Statutory Authority**
- IWAI is empowered to **make regulations** for the safe, efficient, and coordinated development of inland water transport.
- It defines **vessel standards, survey procedures, and certification protocols** for vessels operating on National Waterways.
- It acts as the **competent authority** for issuing **Certificate of Inspection (COI)** for inland vessels.

### **Pillar 2: Infrastructure Development**
- Construction and maintenance of **navigational channels, terminals, and navigation locks**.
- Installation of **navigation aids** (buoys, lighthouses, beacons, day marks) along waterways.
- Dredging operations to maintain **Least Available Depth (LAD)** for safe navigation.

### **Pillar 3: Survey, Inspection, and Certification (Core to Hull Inspection)**
- **Initial Survey:** Conducted before a vessel is registered and put into service.
- **Annual Survey:** Inspection of hull, machinery, and equipment every year.
- **Periodical Survey:** Comprehensive inspection every **4 to 5 years** based on vessel type.
- **Docking Survey:** Hull inspection in dry dock (or slipway) at specified intervals.
- **Special Survey:** Triggered by specific events (grounding, collision, major modification).

### **Pillar 4: Research and Development**
- Undertakes research on **vessel design suitable for shallow draft waterways**.
- Develops **standardized hull forms** for Indian riverine conditions.
- Promotes **modern shipbuilding techniques** adapted to inland water constraints.

### **Pillar 5: Administrative Functions**
- Vessel registration and licensing.
- Manning and crew certification requirements.
- Enforcement of safety standards.

## 2.2 IWAI Vessel Survey Cycle Logic (The 'Why' and 'How')

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** Inland vessels operate in harsh, abrasive, and corrosive riverine environments (silt, sand, debris, monsoon flooding). Hulls degrade faster than in sheltered sea conditions. Hence, a **strict and frequent inspection regime** is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'How':** IWAI follows a **tiered survey structure** where each successive survey is more comprehensive than the previous, ensuring that hull deterioration is caught early.

**Survey Tier Logic:**
1. **Annual Survey (Every 12 months):** Visual inspection of accessible hull, machinery checks, safety equipment verification.
2. **Periodical Survey (Every 4-5 years):** Detailed thickness gauging, internal structural inspection, machinery survey, electrical inspection.
3. **Docking Survey (As specified):** Hull bottom, propeller, rudder, sea/river valves, anodes examined out of water.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Standard Sheet

| Concept | Standard / Parameter | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Survey Interval | **12 months** from last survey | IWAI Regulations |
| Periodical Survey Interval | **Every 4 years** (for most inland vessels) | IWAI Inland Vessel Rules |
| Docking Survey Interval | **Every 5 years** (varies by vessel age/type) | IWAI Inland Vessel Rules |
| Maximum Permissible Hull Plate Reduction | **22.5% of original thickness** (general standard) | IACS / Class Rules (adapted) |
| Minimum Plate Renewal Threshold | **Original thickness minus permissible corrosion allowance** | IACS UR Z7 / Z23 |
| Length of National Waterway-1 (Ganga) | **1,620 km** (Haldia to Allahabad) | IWAI Official Data |
| Length of National Waterway-2 (Brahmaputra) | **891 km** (Sadiya to Dhubri) | IWAI Official Data |
| Length of National Waterway-3 (West Coast Canal) | **205 km** (Kerala) | IWAI Official Data |
| Vessel Speed Limit on National Waterways | **Generally 15 km/h** (varies by zone) | IWAI Notification |
| Inland Vessel Registration Authority | **IWAI (for NW) and State Governments (for non-NW)** | IWAI Act 1985 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The **22.5% rule** and **4-year periodical cycle** are the most frequently asked values in KTU theory questions. Memorize them.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IWAI framework directly enables:
- **Safety of life** on Indian rivers carrying millions of passengers annually.
- **Protection of inland waterway infrastructure** (bridges, terminals) from substandard vessels.
- **Environmental protection** by enforcing pollution prevention on inland waterways.
- **Cargo assurance** by certifying that vessels are seaworthy (river-worthy) for bulk transport of coal, food grains, fertilizers, and containers on NW-1 and NW-2.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Vessel Survey Schedule Logic

Let us derive the **theoretical basis** for the inland vessel survey interval as prescribed by IWAI regulations, which adapt the **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** methodology to the harsher riverine corrosion environment.

### Step 1: Define the Corrosion Rate Model

The average corrosion rate for mild steel hull plates in Indian riverine water (brackish to freshwater, with high silt and seasonal monsoon variation) is empirically established as:

$$C_r = k \cdot T \cdot E_f$$

Where:
- $C_r$ = cumulative corrosion loss in mm
- $k$ = base corrosion constant for riverine environment = **0.10 mm/year** (typical for Indian inland waters)
- $T$ = time in service in years
- $E_f$ = Environmental Factor (1.0 for freshwater rivers, 1.3 for brackish estuarine zones like Hooghly)

### Step 2: Define the Critical Plate Thickness

The minimum allowable plate thickness at any survey is given by:

$$t_{min} = t_{original} - t_{corrosion\_allowance}$$

Where:
- $t_{original}$ = as-built plate thickness (mm)
- $t_{corrosion\_allowance}$ = total permissible wastage (mm)

### Step 3: Apply the 22.5% Rule (IACS Standard S21)

The standard IACS rule states that when the corroded thickness falls below a critical fraction of the original, the plate must be renewed:

$$t_{critical} = 0.775 \cdot t_{original}$$

> This means a **22.5%** reduction from the original thickness is the maximum permitted before mandatory renewal.

### Step 4: Compute the Survey Interval

The maximum safe time between surveys is when the cumulative corrosion reaches the critical limit:

$$T_{survey} = \frac{t_{original} - t_{critical}}{C_r} = \frac{0.225 \cdot t_{original}}{k \cdot E_f}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example (Hull Plate Calculation)

**Given:** A vessel has a hull plate with original thickness $t_{original}$ = **10 mm**, operating in the Hooghly estuary (brackish water), so $E_f$ = **1.3**.

**Find:** Maximum theoretical interval between docking surveys.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{survey} &= \frac{0.225 \cdot t_{original}}{k \cdot E_f} \\
T_{survey} &= \frac{0.225 \cdot 10 \text{ mm}}{0.10 \text{ mm/year} \cdot 1.3} \\
T_{survey} &= \frac{2.25}{0.13} \text{ years} \\
T_{survey} &\approx 17.3 \text{ years (theoretical max)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Apply the Safety Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IWAI does **not** permit vessels to operate until the theoretical limit is reached. A **safety factor of 4 to 5** is applied to translate this theoretical value into the regulatory survey cycle.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{regulatory} &= \frac{T_{survey}}{SF} \\
T_{regulatory} &= \frac{17.3}{4} \\
T_{regulatory} &\approx 4.3 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the **IWAI prescribed Periodical Survey interval of 4 years** for inland vessels, validating the regulatory framework.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Survey Types

| Survey Type | Interval | Scope of Hull Inspection | Responsible Authority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Survey** | Once (pre-commissioning) | Full hull, structural, machinery, electrical, safety | IWAI Surveyor |
| **Annual Survey** | **12 months** | External accessible hull, fire-fighting equipment, life-saving appliances | IWAI Surveyor |
| **Periodical Survey** | **4 years** | Full hull thickness gauging, tanks, internal structures | IWAI Surveyor + Classification Society |
| **Docking Survey** | **5 years** (or per class) | Hull bottom, propellers, rudders, anodes, in dry dock | Approved Docking Facility |
| **Special Survey** | Event-driven (grounding, fire) | Damage-specific detailed inspection | IWAI Surveyor |

## 3.3 Derivation: IWAI Hull Inspection Workflow

The complete IWAI hull inspection workflow follows a deterministic **7-step sequence**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Vessel Application} &\rightarrow \text{Owner submits documents to IWAI} \\
\text{Step 2: Document Verification} &\rightarrow \text{Design, drawings, stability booklets checked} \\
\text{Step 3: Initial Survey} &\rightarrow \text{Physical inspection of newly built vessel} \\
\text{Step 4: COI Issuance} &\rightarrow \text{Certificate of Inspection issued} \\
\text{Step 5: Annual Monitoring} &\rightarrow \text{Yearly surveys to track degradation} \\
\text{Step 6: Periodical Survey} &\rightarrow \text{Comprehensive 4-yearly assessment} \\
\text{Step 7: Renewal / De-registration} &\rightarrow \text{Continued certification or removal from registry}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 IWAI Organizational and Operational Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture of IWAI's hull inspection framework**, mapping the interactions between regulatory bodies, vessel owners, and survey systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ministry of Ports Shipping and Waterways] --> B[Inland Waterways Authority of India IWAI]
    B --> C[Survey and Inspection Wing]
    B --> D[Infrastructure Development Wing]
    B --> E[Navigation Services Wing]
    B --> F[Research and Development Wing]
    C --> G[Initial Survey of Hull]
    C --> H[Annual Survey]
    C --> I[Periodical Survey Every 4 Years]
    C --> J[Docking Survey Every 5 Years]
    C --> K[Special Survey Event Driven]
    G --> L[Certificate of Inspection COI]
    H --> M[Hull Integrity Report]
    I --> N[Thickness Gauging Report]
    J --> O[Docking Survey Report]
    K --> P[Damage Assessment Report]
    L --> Q[Vessel Operational on National Waterway]
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[National Waterways NW 1 to NW 111]
    R --> S[Safe Inland Navigation]
    B --> T[Coordination with Classification Societies ABS DNV LR BV IRS]
    B --> U[Coordination with State Governments]
    T --> C
    U --> C
```

## 4.2 IWAI Survey Cycle Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Vessel Commencement of Service] --> A1[Initial Survey]
    A1 --> A2[COI Issued]
    A2 --> A3{Annual Survey Every 12 Months}
    A3 --> A4{Hull Condition Acceptable}
    A4 --> A5[No Action Continue Operation]
    A4 --> A6[Repairs or Renewals Required]
    A6 --> A3
    A5 --> A7{Periodical Survey Every 4 Years}
    A7 --> A8[Thickness Gauging]
    A8 --> A9{Plate Reduction below 22.5 percent}
    A9 --> A10[Plates within limits Continue]
    A9 --> A11[Plate Renewal Mandatory]
    A11 --> A12[Repair and Re-Survey]
    A12 --> A7
    A10 --> A13{Docking Survey Every 5 Years}
    A13 --> A14[Bottom Hull Inspection]
    A14 --> A15[Propeller and Rudder Check]
    A15 --> A16[Anode and Coating Inspection]
    A16 --> A17[Continue Service]
    A17 --> A3
```
