# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Define IWAI and state its statutory basis.** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

IWAI stands for the **Inland Waterways Authority of India**. It is a statutory body constituted on **27th October 1986** under the **Inland Waterways Authority of India Act, 1985**. It functions under the **Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways**, Government of India.

Its primary functions include:
1. Regulation and development of inland waterways for shipping and navigation.
2. Survey, inspection, and certification of inland vessels.
3. Development of navigation infrastructure such as terminals and navigation aids.
4. Registration of vessels operating on National Waterways.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for full form, 1 mark for statute, 1 mark for functions)*

---

### **Question 2: List the five original National Waterways of India.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The five original National Waterways declared in 1986 are:

| NW Number | Waterway | Approximate Length |
|---|---|---|
| **NW-1** | Ganga River (Haldia to Allahabad) | **1,620 km** |
| **NW-2** | Brahmaputra River (Sadiya to Dhubri) | **891 km** |
| **NW-3** | West Coast Canal (Kerala) | **205 km** |
| **NW-4** | Krishna-Godavari Rivers | **1,095 km** |
| **NW-5** | Brahmani-Mahanadi Rivers | **623 km** |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per waterway with name)*

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 1 Choice Rule:** Answer any ONE full question from the Module 1 choice set. Each full question is **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **organizational structure, statutory powers, and functions of the Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)** with specific reference to its role in hull inspection of inland vessels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI) is the apex body constituted under the **Inland Waterways Authority of India Act, 1985** and came into existence on **27th October 1986**.

**Statutory Powers of IWAI:**
1. Power to **make regulations** for inland water transport under Section 18 of the Act.
2. Power to **acquire land** for infrastructure development.
3. Power to **levy fees and charges** for navigation and vessel registration.
4. Power to **enforce safety standards** and inspect vessels.
5. Power to **co-ordinate with state governments and central ministries**.

**Functions of IWAI (relevant to hull inspection):**
- Conducting **Initial, Annual, Periodical, and Docking Surveys** of inland vessels.
- Issuing **Certificate of Inspection (COI)** to vessels found fit for operation on National Waterways.
- Registering vessels and maintaining a **National Inland Vessel Register**.
- Coordinating with **classification societies** (IRS, DNV, LR, BV) for technical surveys.
- Undertaking **research on hull design** suited to shallow Indian rivers.
- Conducting **crew certification and manning audits**.

**Role in Hull Inspection Specifically:**
IWAI has prescribed survey intervals as follows: Annual Survey every **12 months**, Periodical Survey every **4 years**, and Docking Survey every **5 years**. During these surveys, hull thickness gauging is performed, and if plate reduction exceeds **22.5%** of the original thickness, renewal is mandated. IWAI surveyors are authorized to **detain, suspend, or revoke** the COI if a vessel is found unseaworthy.

*(Marks distribution: Statutory basis — 2, Powers — 2, Functions — 2, Role in hull inspection — 1)*

---

**(b)** Describe the **complete survey cycle for an inland vessel** as prescribed by IWAI, including the types of surveys, their intervals, scope, and the documents issued. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The IWAI survey cycle for an inland vessel consists of the following structured sequence:

**1. Initial Survey (One-time, Pre-Commissioning):**
- Conducted before the vessel is put into service.
- Covers full inspection of hull, machinery, electrical systems, safety equipment, and stability.
- Result: Vessel is either approved for registration or sent back for rectification.

**2. Annual Survey (Every 12 months):**
- Visual external inspection of the hull above the waterline.
- Verification of life-saving appliances, fire-fighting equipment, navigation lights.
- Check of machinery functioning, bilge pumping, and steering gear.

**3. Periodical Survey (Every 4 years):**
- Complete **thickness gauging** of hull plates, decks, bulkheads, and tank boundaries.
- Internal examination of all tanks (ballast, cargo, fuel).
- Machinery survey, electrical insulation tests, boiler survey (if applicable).
- Hull plating renewals if wastage exceeds **22.5%** of original thickness.

**4. Docking Survey (Every 5 years, or as specified):**
- Out-of-water examination of hull bottom, propeller, rudder, sea/river valves, anodes, and coating condition.
- Shaft alignment, stern tube seal inspection.

**5. Special Survey (Event-driven):**
- Conducted after grounding, collision, fire, or major modification.
- Damage-specific detailed inspection and repair certification.

**Documents Issued by IWAI:**
- **Certificate of Inspection (COI)**
- **Sailing Permission Letter**
- **Survey Reports (Initial, Annual, Periodical, Docking)**
- **Vessel Registration Certificate**
- **Manning Certificate**

The COI is renewed upon satisfactory completion of each periodical survey cycle, ensuring continuous certification of the vessel.

*(Marks distribution: 5 survey types explained — 1 each, Documents issued — 2)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**(a)** Discuss the **historical background, administrative setup, and jurisdiction of the Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)**. Explain how IWAI coordinates with classification societies for hull inspection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Historical Background:**
Before independence, inland water transport in India was managed under the **Indian Ports Act, 1908** and various state-level regulations. Post-independence, the need for a dedicated authority for inland waterways was felt due to the **neglect and decline** of river transport in favor of road and rail. The **National Transport Policy Committee (1974)** recommended the creation of a dedicated body. This led to the enactment of the **Inland Waterways Authority of India Act, 1985**, and IWAI was constituted on **27 October 1986**.

**Administrative Setup:**
- **Headquarters:** Noida, Uttar Pradesh.
- **Sub-offices:** Regional offices at Kolkata (NW-1, NW-2), Kochi (NW-3), and other strategic locations.
- **Governing Body:** Chaired by a **Chairman** appointed by the Central Government.
- **Administrative Ministry:** Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways.

**Jurisdiction:**
- IWAI has jurisdiction over **all declared National Waterways** (currently 111).
- It does **not** have jurisdiction over state waterways (canals, minor rivers managed by state irrigation departments).
- For state waterways, registration and inspection are handled by the **State Government** through the State Transport Department.

**Coordination with Classification Societies:**
For technical hull inspection, IWAI may engage recognized classification societies such as:
- **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** — primary domestic partner.
- **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)**
- **Det Norske Veritas (DNV)**
- **Lloyd's Register (LR)**
- **Bureau Veritas (BV)**

These societies act as **Recognized Organizations (ROs)** and conduct surveys on behalf of IWAI, issuing survey reports that are countersigned by IWAI. This arrangement leverages global technical expertise while maintaining IWAI's statutory authority.

*(Marks distribution: History — 2, Administrative setup — 2, Jurisdiction — 1.5, Coordination — 1.5)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **principles of hull inspection** as applied to inland vessels. Discuss the key areas of hull examination under IWAI regulations, including plate thickness gauging, corrosion measurement, and renewal criteria. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principles of Hull Inspection for Inland Vessels:**

1. **Safety First:** The primary objective is to ensure the vessel is structurally sound for safe navigation.
2. **Prevention over Cure:** Inspections aim to detect deterioration early, before catastrophic failure.
3. **Tiered Approach:** Increasing intensity of inspection at longer intervals (Annual → Periodical → Docking).
4. **Standardization:** All inspections follow IWAI/IACS standardized procedures and acceptance criteria.
5. **Documentation:** Every inspection produces a written report forming part of the vessel's permanent record.

**Key Areas of Hull Examination:**

**(i) Plate Thickness Gauging (PTG):**
- Conducted during **Periodical Surveys**.
- Uses **ultrasonic thickness gauges (UTG)**.
- Measurements taken at a **grid pattern** of points on each plate.
- Results compared against **original as-built thickness** recorded in the vessel's construction drawings.

**(ii) Corrosion Measurement:**
- Corrosion is measured as the **percentage reduction** from original thickness.
- Acceptable limit: Up to **22.5% reduction** (IACS S21 standard, adopted by IWAI).
- Beyond this, the plate is considered **substantially corroded** and renewal is mandatory.

**(iii) Renewal Criteria:**
- If a plate is reduced to **75%** of original thickness, it is renewed.
- Pitting corrosion in localized areas: if pitting depth exceeds **15%** of plate thickness, repair is required.
- If more than **20% of plates** in a given hull section need renewal, the entire section may be required for renewal.

**(iv) Other Areas Examined:**
- **Weld seams:** Inspected for cracks, undercutting, and corrosion at toes.
- **Internal frames, stiffeners, and brackets:** Checked for wastage and fractures.
- **Tank boundaries:** Particularly susceptible to wastage on both sides.
- **Bottom plating, bilge keels, and keel:** Subjected to detailed Docking Survey.
- **Coating condition:** Evaluated for peeling, blistering, and cathodic protection effectiveness.

The ultimate deliverable of the hull inspection is the **Survey Report** which either:
- **Allows continued service** (with or without minor repairs), or
- **Mandates repairs/renewals** before the COI can be reissued.

*(Marks distribution: Principles — 2, PTG and corrosion — 2, Renewal criteria — 2, Other areas — 1)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in IWAI-Related Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IWAI with DGS or IMO:** Many students mix up the roles. IWAI is for **inland waterways only**, DGS is for **seagoing Indian vessels**, and IMO is an **international body** under the United Nations. Examiners **strictly deduct marks** for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the founding date (27 October 1986) and statute year (1985):** These are **favorite 1-mark fillers** in KTU papers. Missing them = losing easy marks.
>
> 3. **Writing "22%" instead of "22.5%":** The 22.5% plate wastage rule is a **specific value**. A 0.5% deviation will be marked wrong.
>
> 4. **Survey interval confusion:** Annual = **12 months**, Periodical = **4 years**, Docking = **5 years**. Mixing these up is a common error.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the diagram/diagram description marks:** In 14-mark questions, **a well-labeled flowchart of the IWAI survey cycle** can fetch 1-2 extra marks. Always draw where possible.
>
> 6. **Stating "IWAI inspects all vessels in India":** WRONG. IWAI only inspects vessels on **National Waterways**. State waterway vessels are handled by the **State Government**.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention coordinating agencies:** A complete answer must mention **IRS, DNV, LR, BV, ABS** as Recognized Organizations working with IWAI.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — IWAI**
>
> - **Full Form:** Inland Waterways Authority of India.
> - **Statute:** Inland Waterways Authority of India Act, **1985**.
> - **Constituted On:** **27th October 1986**.
> - **Headquarters:** **Noida, Uttar Pradesh**.
> - **Ministry:** Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways.
> - **Original National Waterways:** **5** (NW-1 to NW-5).
> - **Total National Waterways (current):** **111**.
> - **Key Waterways:** NW-1 (Ganga, 1620 km), NW-2 (Brahmaputra, 891 km), NW-3 (West Coast Canal, 205 km, Kerala).
> - **Vessel Survey Types:** Initial, Annual (12 months), Periodical (4 years), Docking (5 years), Special (event-driven).
> - **Plate Wastage Rule:** Maximum **22.5%** reduction from original thickness before renewal.
> - **Certificate Issued:** **Certificate of Inspection (COI)**.
> - **Recognized Organizations (ROs):** IRS, ABS, DNV, LR, BV.
> - **Jurisdiction Limitation:** IWAI handles **National Waterways only**; state waterways fall under State Governments.
> - **Environment Factor (Estuarine):** 1.3 for brackish waters like Hooghly.
> - **Base Corrosion Rate:** 0.10 mm/year for Indian riverine environment.
> - **Safety Factor Used:** 4 to 5 (theoretical 17 years → regulatory 4 years).
> - **Key Function in Hull Inspection:** Survey, certification, registration, and ensuring compliance with **IWAI Inland Vessel Rules**.
> - **Vessel Speed Limit (General):** ~15 km/h on National Waterways.
> - **Coordination:** IWAI works with classification societies, state governments, and the Ministry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Cargo Survey - Dry, Liquid and Container Cargoes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marine Cargo Survey - Dry, Liquid and Container Cargoes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Marine Cargo Survey – Dry, Liquid and Container Cargoes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Marine Cargo Survey?

A **Marine Cargo Survey** is a systematic, documented inspection and measurement of cargo carried on board a vessel, conducted at the stages of pre-loading, during loading, during voyage, and at discharge. The primary purpose is to determine the **quantity, quality, and condition** of the cargo so that the commercial interests of shippers, receivers, underwriters, and carriers are protected against shortage, contamination, or damage claims.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):**
> A Marine Cargo Survey is a professional assessment performed by a competent surveyor (often an independent third party appointed by insurers, owners, charterers, or P&I Clubs) to verify the condition, quantity, and quality of cargo before, during, and after ocean transit, in accordance with the **Bill of Lading, Charter Party, and Institute Cargo Clauses (A/B/C)**.

### 1.2 Classification of Cargo for Survey Purposes

| Cargo Type | Examples | Carrier Type |
|------------|----------|--------------|
| **Dry Bulk Cargo** | Iron ore, coal, grain, bauxite, fertilizers | Bulk carriers (Capesize, Panamax, Handysize) |
| **Liquid Bulk Cargo** | Crude oil, refined products, chemicals, LNG, vegetable oils | Tankers (VLCC, Suezmax, MR, Chemical tankers) |
| **Break-bulk / General Cargo** | Steel coils, bagged sugar, machinery | Multi-purpose / General cargo ships |
| **Containerised Cargo** | FCL, LCL, reefer containers | Container ships (Feeder, Panamax, ULCV) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Hull Inspection** perspective of cargo surveying focuses on how cargo interacts with the hull structure — including **load distribution, shear forces, bending moments, and sloshing effects** that influence hull integrity.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a marine cargo survey like a **weighbridge + quality-check at a logistics hub**, but performed at the world's largest and most mobile warehouse — the ship. Just as a customs officer at an airport checks both *how much* your luggage weighs and *whether* the contents are declared correctly, a cargo surveyor verifies:
- **Quantity** (weight, volume, count)
- **Quality** (temperature, moisture, contamination, free water)
- **Condition** (damage from handling, sweat, oxidation, leakages)

The unique complication in marine transport is that the ship is **moving through hostile environments** (heat, salt, humidity, dynamic loads), and the cargo must withstand this journey intact.

### 1.4 Stages at which Cargo Surveys are Conducted

1. **Pre-Loading Survey (PLS)** – Inspecting holds, tanks, or container bays for cleanliness, dryness, fitness, and prior cargo residues.
2. **Loading/Discharge Survey** – Measuring quantity loaded or discharged, sampling cargo, monitoring methods.
3. **On-Hire / Off-Hire Survey (Tankers)** – Determining vessel condition before charter.
4. **Condition Survey** – Visual inspection of cargo state at load port vs. discharge port.
5. **Outturn Survey** – Final assessment at discharge to compare against Bill of Lading figures.
6. **Draft Survey (Bulk Carriers)** – Calculating cargo weight from vessel displacement differences.

### 1.5 The Surveyor's Tools of the Trade

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Standards & Constants (Bolded):**
> - **API Gravity**: $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60^\circ F}} - 131.5$
> - **Standard Sea Water Density**: **1.025 t/m³**
> - **Standard Temperature for Petroleum**: **15 °C**
> - **Grain Stowage Factor** typically measured in **m³/t**
> - **TPC (Tonnes per Centimetre Immersion)** for draft calculations
> - **1 Barrel (petroleum) = 0.158987 m³ = 42 US gallons**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dry Cargo Survey — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Bulk Dry Cargoes

Bulk dry cargoes (ore, coal, grain, fertilizers) are surveyed principally to determine **weight (tonnes)** loaded or discharged, since the Bill of Lading is expressed in metric tons. Three principal methods are used:

**Method 1: Draft Survey (Most Common for Bulk Carriers)**

The principle is based on **Archimedes' Law** — the weight of a floating vessel equals the weight of the water displaced.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Read the vessel's draft (forward, mid, aft) at the **before-loading** and **after-loading** condition.
2. Apply corrections for **water density, trim, list, hog/sag, freeboard-to-draft corrections, propeller/rudder appendages**.
3. Calculate the displacement at each condition.
4. Subtract constants: lightship weight, bunker (FO, DO, LO), freshwater, ballast, stores, crew, constant additions.
5. The difference = **Cargo weight loaded**.

**Method 2: Weighbridge / Shore Scale**
- Cargo weighed on certified shore scales (truck weighbridges, conveyor belt scales).
- Used for bagged cargo, steel products, and partial lot surveys.

**Method 3: Barge / Floating Tank Calibration**
- Barges or shore tanks are gauged before and after transfer.
- Density is measured for volume-to-weight conversion.

### 2.1.2 Draft Survey — Why Each Correction Matters

| Correction | Symbol | Purpose |
|------------|--------|---------|
| **Density Correction** | $F_D$ | Converts hydrostatic table displacement to actual water density |
| **Trim Correction** | $F_T$ | Accounts for non-even keel (vessel trimmed by the bow/stern) |
| **List Correction** | $F_L$ | Adjusts for transverse inclination (heel) |
| **Hog/Sag Correction** | $F_{HS}$ | Adjusts for hull deflection between forward and aft drafts |
| **Appendage Correction** | $F_A$ | Adds the weight of propellers, rudder, brackets below the waterline |
| **Deadweight Constant** | $DWC$ | Subtracts fuel, water, stores, crew, ballast |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Draft Survey** is legally admissible as primary evidence in most maritime courts and under **UCP 600** letters of credit, provided corrections are properly applied and the survey is witnessed by all parties.

### 2.1.3 Dry Cargo Condition Issues

Dry cargoes are susceptible to:
- **Moisture damage** (grain sweat, condensation in holds)
- **Cargo sweat** – moisture from the cargo itself migrating to cooler steel surfaces
- **Ship sweat** – moisture from outside air condensing on hull plates
- **Oxidation, corrosion, contamination** from prior cargo residues
- **Shortage / theft** — typically **0.5% to 1% tolerance** is industry-accepted for bulk

### 2.2 Liquid Cargo Survey — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Liquid Cargo Types and Carrier Specialization

| Cargo Type | Vessel | Key Survey Concern |
|------------|--------|---------------------|
| Crude Oil | VLCC, Suezmax, Aframax | Quantity, BS&W, API |
| Refined Products | MR, LR1, LR2 | Quality, contamination |
| Chemicals | Chemical tanker (IMO Type 1/2/3) | Compatibility, tank coating |
| LNG / LPG | Gas carrier (Moss, Membrane, Type C) | Temperature, pressure |
| Edible Oils | Coated chemical tanker | Free Fatty Acid (FFA), colour |
| Wine / Molasses | Coated parcel tanker | Specific gravity, temperature |

### 2.2.2 Liquid Cargo — Key Measurement Operations

**1. Ullage (Sounding)**
- Ullage = the **empty space** between the liquid surface and a reference point (ullage hatch, tank top of dome).
- Innage = the **depth of liquid** measured from the tank bottom upward.
- **Ullage + Innage = Tank Reference Height (TRH)**.

**2. Sounding Tape / Measuring Instruments**
- **Petroleum sounding tape** – brass weights with calibrated bobs.
- **Electronic tank gauging (ETG)** – radar, capacitance, or servo gauges.
- **Float gauges** — common on chemical and product tankers.

**3. Sampling**
- **Three-spot sampling** (upper, middle, lower) for non-homogeneous liquids like crude.
- **All-levels, running, and spot samples** per **API MPMS Chapter 8.1 / 8.2**.
- Sampler types: **bottle-and-cork, zone, thief, in-line**.

**4. Temperature Measurement**
- Cargo temperature affects volume (VCF) and density.
- Three temperature levels measured: **0.5 m below surface, mid-liquid, 0.5 m above bottom**.

**5. Density Determination**
- **Hydrometer** (calibrated glass, ASTM 86H or 98H series, range 0.600–1.100 g/mL).
- **Oscillating U-tube densitometer** (modern digital, ±0.0001 g/mL).
- **Pycnometer** (laboratory, reference method).

### 2.2.3 Liquid Cargo — Critical Quality Tests

- **API Gravity** (for petroleum)
- **BS&W (Basic Sediment & Water)** by centrifuge (ASTM D1796 or D4007)
- **Flash Point, Pour Point, Cloud Point, RVP**
- **Sulphur Content, Water Content (Karl Fischer)**
- **Salt Content, Compatibility tests** (for chemicals)
- **FFA (Free Fatty Acid)** for vegetable oils

### 2.3 Container Cargo Survey — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Container Types Surveyed

- **Dry Van / General Purpose (GP)** — 20 ft, 40 ft
- **Reefer (Refrigerated)** — Integral, porthole
- **Open Top, Flat Rack, Platform** — for OOG (out-of-gauge) cargo
- **Tank Container (ISO Tank)** — for liquids in 20 ft frame
- **Special (Bulk, Ventilated, Insulated)**

### 2.3.2 Container Survey Focus Areas

1. **External Condition Survey** — dents, holes, broken corner castings, door seals.
2. **Internal Cleanliness & Odour Survey** — especially for food-grade, garment cargo.
3. **Pre-trip Inspection (PTI)** — for reefer units: compressor, thermostat, defrost cycle, genset.
4. **Seal Verification** — at load and discharge; high-security bolt seals (ISO 17712).
5. **CSC Plate (Container Safety Convention)** verification.
6. **Weighing (VGM — Verified Gross Mass)** under **SOLAS Chapter VI, Reg. 2**.

### 2.3.3 The VGM (Verified Gross Mass) Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **1 July 2016**, under the amended **SOLAS Chapter VI**, every packed container must have its **Verified Gross Mass (VGM)** declared to the vessel operator and terminal before loading. Two methods are permitted:
> - **Method 1 (Weighing)**: Container is weighed fully packed using a calibrated scale.
> - **Method 2 (Calculation)**: Mass of cargo + tare mass of container + all packing & securing materials.
>
> **The shipper is solely responsible for the VGM declaration.**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Use | Units |
|---|--------------------|-----|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta W = (D_{after} - D_{before}) \times \dfrac{\rho_{water}}{\rho_{std}}$ | Cargo weight from displacement change | tonnes |
| 2 | $D_{corrected} = D_{obs} + F_D + F_T + F_L + F_{HS} - F_A$ | Corrected displacement | tonnes |
| 3 | $TPC = \dfrac{A_W}{100}$ | Tonnes per cm immersion; $A_W$ = waterplane area (m²) | t/cm |
| 4 | $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60^\circ F}} - 131.5$ | API gravity of petroleum | dimensionless |
| 5 | $SG_{15/15} = \dfrac{\rho_{sample@15°C}}{\rho_{water@15°C}}$ | Specific gravity reference | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_{15} = V_t \times VCF_{T,SG}$ | Volume correction to 15 °C | m³ |
| 7 | $W = V_{15} \times \rho_{15} \times F_{air}$ | Weight in air (using air buoyancy factor 0.9989–1.000) | tonnes |
| 8 | $Stowage\,Factor = \dfrac{V}{W}$ | Volume per unit weight | m³/t |
| 9 | $Ullage + Innage = TRH$ | Tank reference geometry | metres |
| 10 | $Cargo\,Weight = Total\,Displacement - Light\,Ship - Bunker - Water - Stores - Ballast$ | Draft survey final formula | tonnes |
| 11 | $VGM = Tare + Cargo + Packing$ | Verified gross mass of container | kg |
| 12 | $Bending\,Moment = W \times LCG$ (simplified still-water) | Hull loading reference | t·m |

> [!NOTE]
> The **vertical bar (pipe) symbol** is not used in tables. All absolute values are written using the LaTeX `\vert` form when needed in equations.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Hull Designers** use stowage factor and density data to calculate shear forces, bending moments, and torsional loads — essential for **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** compliance.
- **Naval Architects** correlate cargo distribution with hull girder fatigue life.
- **Surveyors** use draft data to validate loading against the vessel's **Loading Manual** and **Stability Booklet**.
- **Liquid cargo surveys** directly influence **MARPOL Annex I** compliance, especially **ODME (Oily Discharge Monitoring Equipment)** performance verification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Draft Survey Cargo Weight Formula

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

> A bulk carrier arrives at the load port with a forward draft $D_F$ and aft draft $D_A$. After loading, drafts are $D'_F$ and $D'_A$. Given hydrostatic data, water density $\rho_w$, lightship $LS$, bunker $B$, freshwater $FW$, ballast $BL$, stores $S$, derive the cargo weight loaded.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Read mean draft before loading.

$$
\bar{D}_{before} = \frac{D_{F,b} + D_{A,b}}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Read mean draft after loading.

$$
\bar{D}_{after} = \frac{D_{F,a} + D_{A,a}}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Look up the **hydrostatic displacement** $\Delta$ from the ship's trim and draft table corresponding to each draft condition.

$$
\Delta_{before} = f(\bar{D}_{before}, T_{before})
$$

$$
\Delta_{after} = f(\bar{D}_{after}, T_{after})
$$

where $T = D_{A} - D_{F}$ is the **trim**.

**Step 4:** Apply the **density correction factor** $F_D$ to convert from standard sea water (1.025 t/m³) to the actual water density at the berth.

$$
\Delta_{corrected} = \Delta \times \frac{\rho_{water,berth}}{1.025}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply **trim correction** $F_T$ — already partially embedded in hydrostatic tables; an additional correction may be needed if the vessel is **not** at the standard even-keel condition.

$$
\Delta_{trim\,adj} = \Delta_{corrected} + F_T
$$

**Step 6:** Apply **appendage correction** $F_A$ — adds the weight of the propeller, rudder, brackets, and bilge keels that lie below the waterline (these are submerged but not included in the hydrostatic table).

$$
\Delta_{full} = \Delta_{trim\,adj} + F_A
$$

**Step 7:** Subtract the **light ship** weight and all constants carried on board (fuel oil, diesel oil, lubricating oil, freshwater, ballast, stores, crew effects).

$$
W_{cargo} = \Delta_{full,after} - \Delta_{full,before} - \Delta_{constants}
$$

where

$$
\Delta_{constants} = W_{FO} + W_{DO} + W_{LO} + W_{FW} + W_{BL} + W_{S} + W_{Crew}
$$

**Step 8:** Adjust for any **list correction** $F_L$ if the vessel is heeled.

$$
W_{cargo,final} = W_{cargo} + F_L
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

A Panamax bulk carrier has the following data:

| Parameter | Before Loading | After Loading |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Forward draft (m) | 4.20 | 12.85 |
| Aft draft (m) | 4.80 | 13.15 |
| Hydrostatic Displacement (t) | 18,400 | 56,200 |
| Lightship (t) | 7,800 | 7,800 |
| Fuel Oil (t) | 350 | 350 |
| Diesel Oil (t) | 90 | 90 |
| Lub Oil (t) | 60 | 60 |
| Fresh Water (t) | 120 | 120 |
| Ballast (t) | 2,800 | 0 |
| Stores (t) | 40 | 40 |
| Water density at berth (t/m³) | 1.018 | 1.018 |

**Solution — Step by step:**

Mean draft before:

$$
\bar{D}_b = \frac{4.20 + 4.80}{2} = 4.50 \text{ m}
$$

Mean draft after:

$$
\bar{D}_a = \frac{12.85 + 13.15}{2} = 13.00 \text{ m}
$$

Density correction factor:

$$
F_D = \frac{1.018}{1.025} = 0.9932
$$

Corrected displacements:

$$
\Delta_{b,corr} = 18{,}400 \times 0.9932 = 18{,}275 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\Delta_{a,corr} = 56{,}200 \times 0.9932 = 55{,}818 \text{ t}
$$

Constants sum:

$$
\Delta_{constants} = 7{,}800 + 350 + 90 + 60 + 120 + 2{,}800 + 40 = 11{,}260 \text{ t}
$$

Cargo weight:

$$
W_{cargo} = (55{,}818 - 11{,}260) - 18{,}275 = 47{,}558 - 18{,}275 = 26{,}283 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Always show the **density ratio** explicitly. A common error is forgetting to subtract the ballast weight separately (it is *not* part of constants on the loading side if deballasting occurred).

## 3.2 Derivation: Volume Correction to Standard Temperature (15 °C)

Liquid cargo volumes are measured at observed temperature $T$. The volume is corrected to 15 °C using the **VCF (Volume Correction Factor)**:

$$
V_{15} = V_t \times VCF_{T, SG}
$$

The VCF itself is derived from the **thermal expansion coefficient** of the liquid:

$$
VCF_{T,SG} = \exp\left[-\beta_{15,SG} \times (T - 15) \times \left(1 + 0.8 \times \beta_{15,SG} \times (T - 15)\right)\right]
$$

where $\beta_{15,SG}$ is the **thermal expansion coefficient at 15 °C** as a function of specific gravity (from ASTM D1250 Table 54).

For petroleum products of $SG$ in the range 0.6 – 0.98, $\beta$ is tabulated. For $SG = 0.85$, $\beta \approx 0.000793$ /°C.

**Worked example:**

- Observed volume $V_t = 5{,}000$ m³ at $T = 32$ °C, $SG_{15} = 0.85$.

$$
\Delta T = 32 - 15 = 17 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
VCF = \exp\left[-0.000793 \times 17 \times (1 + 0.8 \times 0.000793 \times 17)\right]
$$

$$
VCF = \exp\left[-0.013481 \times (1 + 0.010782)\right] = \exp\left[-0.013626\right] = 0.9865
$$

$$
V_{15} = 5{,}000 \times 0.9865 = 4{,}932.5 \text{ m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **VCF** is **< 1** when $T > 15$ °C (cargo contracts on cooling to 15 °C) and **> 1** when $T < 15$ °C.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cargo Survey Decision Tree

| Cargo | Primary Quantity Method | Primary Quality Method | Critical Document |
|-------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Iron Ore (bulk) | Draft Survey | Moisture, Fe-content, Sieve analysis | Mate's Receipt, B/L |
| Coal (bulk) | Draft / Conveyor scale | Total Moisture, Ash, Sulphur | B/L, Bunker Delivery |
| Grain (bulk) | Draft Survey | Moisture, Temperature (spoilage), Insect | Phytosanitary cert. |
| Crude Oil | Tank Ullage + VCF | API, BS&W, Sulphur | B/L, COA, SGS Report |
| Refined Product | Tank Ullage | RVP, Flash, Octane | Certificate of Quality |
| Chemicals | Mass flow meter or tank | Compatibility, purity | MSDS, COA |
| LNG | Mass flow (Custody transfer) | Composition, calorific value | Bill of Lading, GC analysis |
| FCL Container | Weighbridge / VGM | Visual + count | Container VGM, Seal record |
| LCL Container | Tally + cargo list | Visual + manifest | Master B/L, House B/L |


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define a marine cargo survey. List the four principal stages at which a cargo survey is conducted."**

**Model Answer:**

A **marine cargo survey** is the systematic inspection and measurement of cargo performed before, during, and after sea transit, to determine its **quantity, quality, and condition**, primarily to safeguard the commercial interests of shippers, receivers, and underwriters.

The four principal stages are:
1. **Pre-loading survey** – inspection of holds or tanks for cleanliness and fitness.
2. **Loading/Discharge survey** – measurement of quantity loaded or discharged.
3. **Condition/on-voyage survey** – monitoring cargo state during transit.
4. **Outturn/discharge survey** – final assessment against the Bill of Lading.

**Mark Split:** Definition – 1 mark; Four stages – 2 marks (0.5 each).

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"What is the VGM rule under SOLAS? State the two permitted methods for its determination."**

**Model Answer:**

The **Verified Gross Mass (VGM)** rule, introduced under **SOLAS Chapter VI Regulation 2** (effective 1 July 2016), requires the **shipper** to declare the verified gross mass of every packed container before it is loaded onto a vessel.

The two permitted methods are:
1. **Method 1 – Weighing**: The fully packed container is weighed on a calibrated, certified scale.
2. **Method 2 – Calculation**: The sum of the container's tare mass, the mass of all cargo contents, and all packing and securing materials is calculated and documented.

**Mark Split:** VGM definition + SOLAS – 1.5 marks; Two methods – 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1-A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **draft survey procedure** for determining the weight of dry bulk cargo loaded on a bulk carrier. Mention the essential corrections applied. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following data. Calculate the cargo weight loaded.

| Parameter | Before Loading | After Loading |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Forward Draft (m) | 5.10 | 13.40 |
| Aft Draft (m) | 5.50 | 13.80 |
| Displacement (t) | 22,500 | 64,800 |
| Lightship (t) | 8,200 | 8,200 |
| Bunker FO + DO (t) | 500 | 500 |
| Fresh Water (t) | 150 | 150 |
| Ballast (t) | 3,200 | 0 |
| Stores (t) | 50 | 50 |
| Water density (t/m³) | 1.020 | 1.020 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Draft Survey Procedure – 7 marks**

The draft survey is the **most common** method of determining the weight of dry bulk cargo loaded on or discharged from a bulk carrier. The principle rests on **Archimedes' Law** — the weight of a ship equals the weight of the water it displaces.

**Step 1 — Draft Reading (1 mark):**
Drafts are read at **forward, mid (port & starboard), and aft** marks, both before and after cargo operation. Readings are taken from calibrated draft marks at the bow, amidships, and stern.

**Step 2 — Mean Draft Calculation (0.5 marks):**

$$
\bar{D} = \frac{D_F + D_A}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Hydrostatic Displacement Lookup (0.5 marks):**
Using the ship's **Trim and Stability Booklet / Hydrostatic Tables**, the displacement corresponding to mean draft and trim is read.

**Step 4 — Density Correction (1.5 marks):**
Hydrostatic tables are based on standard sea water of **1.025 t/m³**. The actual berth water density is different; hence

$$
F_D = \frac{\rho_{berth}}{1.025}
$$

**Step 5 — Trim Correction (0.5 marks):**
For non-standard trim, an additional correction is applied as per the hydrostatic supplement tables.

**Step 6 — Appendage Correction (0.5 marks):**
The weight of the **propeller, rudder, bilge keels, and brackets** submerged below the waterline is added (these are not accounted for in the hydrostatic table).

**Step 7 — Constants Subtraction (1.5 marks):**

$$
W_{cargo} = (\Delta_{after} - \Delta_{before}) - (LS + FO + DO + LO + FW + BL + Stores + Crew)
$$

**Step 8 — List Correction (0.5 marks):**
If the vessel has list (heel), the port and starboard drafts are averaged to remove the effect of inclination.

The final value of cargo weight is typically within **± 0.5% to 1%** of shore scale figures.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution – 7 marks**

Mean draft before:

$$
\bar{D}_b = \frac{5.10 + 5.50}{2} = 5.30 \text{ m}
$$

Mean draft after:

$$
\bar{D}_a = \frac{13.40 + 13.80}{2} = 13.60 \text{ m}
$$

Density correction factor:

$$
F_D = \frac{1.020}{1.025} = 0.9951
$$

Corrected displacements:

$$
\Delta_{b,corr} = 22{,}500 \times 0.9951 = 22{,}390 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\Delta_{a,corr} = 64{,}800 \times 0.9951 = 64{,}482 \text{ t}
$$

Constants sum (after loading: BL = 0):

$$
\Delta_{const,a} = 8{,}200 + 500 + 150 + 0 + 50 = 8{,}900 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\Delta_{const,b} = 8{,}200 + 500 + 150 + 3{,}200 + 50 = 12{,}100 \text{ t}
$$

Note: We subtract only the *change* in constants if ballast, bunkers, etc. are unchanged, but here the ballast has changed, so we work with absolute values.

Cargo weight loaded:

$$
W_{cargo} = (64{,}482 - 8{,}900) - (22{,}390 - 12{,}100)
$$

$$
W_{cargo} = 55{,}582 - 10{,}290 = 45{,}292 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Final Answer: 45,292 tonnes of cargo loaded.**

**Mark Split:** Each major computation step – 1 mark; Final answer – 1 mark.

---

### Question B1-B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the procedure for **liquid cargo survey** on a petroleum tanker. Include the methods of ullage measurement, sampling, and density determination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tank is loaded with **4,200 m³ of gasoline at 30 °C**. Given that the specific gravity at 15 °C is **0.740**, thermal expansion coefficient $\beta_{15} = 0.00111$ /°C, and the ASTM air-buoyancy factor = **0.9989**, calculate: (i) the VCF, (ii) the volume at 15 °C, (iii) the mass in air in tonnes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Liquid Cargo Survey Procedure – 7 marks**

Liquid cargo surveying on a petroleum tanker is performed according to **API MPMS (Manual of Petroleum Measurement Standards)** Chapters 3, 4, 7, 8, 9, 11, and 12.

**Step 1 — Tank Readiness Inspection (1 mark):**
- Check tank for cleanliness, dryness, and freedom from previous cargo residue.
- Verify that tank coating is intact and compatible with the cargo.
- Confirm that heating coils, if any, are leak-free.
- Inert gas system status, oxygen content, and dew point are recorded.

**Step 2 — Ullage / Innage Measurement (1.5 marks):**
- **Ullage** is the distance from the liquid surface to a fixed reference (usually the ullage hatch on the tank dome).
- **Innage** is the depth of liquid measured from the tank bottom.
- **TRH = Ullage + Innage**.
- Manual measurement uses a **calibrated petroleum sounding tape** (chrome-plated brass bob, graduated in mm).
- Modern tankers use **Electronic Tank Gauging (ETG)** — servo, radar, or hydrostatic.

**Step 3 — Temperature Measurement (0.5 marks):**
- Temperature is taken at **three levels**: 0.5 m below the surface, mid-liquid, 0.5 m above the bottom.
- A **portable electronic thermometer** or **cup-case thermometer** (ASTM thermometer) is used.

**Step 4 — Sampling (1.5 marks):**
- **All-levels sample** – taken as the tape descends.
- **Running sample** – obtained by lowering an open sampler from top to bottom.
- **Spot samples** – upper, middle, lower.
- **Three-spot composite** – combined upper, middle, lower samples for BS&W and API determination.
- **Trier samplers, bottle-and-cork samplers, zone samplers** are used.

**Step 5 — Density Determination (1.5 marks):**
- **Hydrometer method (ASTM D1298)**: Glass hydrometer, reading at the meniscus, corrected to 15 °C.
- **Digital density meter (oscillating U-tube)** per ASTM D4052.
- **Pycnometer** for laboratory reference (ASTM D1217).

**Step 6 — Calculations (1 mark):**
- Observed volume corrected to 15 °C using VCF.
- Mass in air or vacuum using density × volume × air buoyancy factor.
- **BS&W** by centrifuge (ASTM D4007).
- **Certificate of Quantity (SGS, Intertek, Saybolt)** issued.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution – 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $V_t = 4{,}200$ m³ at $T = 30$ °C
- $SG_{15} = 0.740$
- $\beta_{15} = 0.00111$ /°C
- Air-buoyancy factor $F_{air} = 0.9989$

**(i) Volume Correction Factor (VCF):**

$$
\Delta T = 30 - 15 = 15 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
VCF = \exp[-\beta \times \Delta T \times (1 + 0.8 \times \beta \times \Delta T)]
$$

$$
VCF = \exp[-0.00111 \times 15 \times (1 + 0.8 \times 0.00111 \times 15)]
$$

$$
VCF = \exp[-0.01665 \times (1 + 0.01332)] = \exp[-0.01665 \times 1.01332]
$$

$$
VCF = \exp[-0.016872] = 0.9833
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Volume at 15 °C:**

$$
V_{15} = V_t \times VCF = 4{,}200 \times 0.9833 = 4{,}129.86 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Mass in Air:**

Density of gasoline at 15 °C:

$$
\rho_{15} = SG_{15} \times \rho_{water@15°C} = 0.740 \times 0.9991 = 0.7393 \text{ t/m}^3
$$

(Using $\rho_{water@15°C} = 0.9991$ t/m³)

Mass in air:

$$
W_{air} = V_{15} \times \rho_{15} \times F_{air} = 4{,}129.86 \times 0.7393 \times 0.9989
$$

$$
W_{air} = 4{,}129.86 \times 0.7393 \times 0.9989 = 3{,}049.7 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Final Answer:**
- **VCF = 0.9833**
- **V₁₅ = 4,129.86 m³**
- **Mass in air = 3,049.7 tonnes**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Marine Cargo Survey Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the density correction** in draft survey — the ratio $\rho_{berth}/1.025$ is mandatory in real exam problems.
> 2. **Mixing up "mass in air" vs "mass in vacuum"** — for petroleum, the difference can be 0.1–0.2%, which is significant in disputes.
> 3. **Using API instead of specific gravity** without proper conversion — a direct number substitution is wrong.
> 4. **Confusing the sign of the VCF** when $T < 15$ °C.
> 5. **Neglecting appendage correction** for propellers and rudders in draft survey — these can add 80–200 tonnes.
> 6. **Mixing up "ullage" and "innage"** terminology — examiners expect the textbook definition.
> 7. **Forgetting to include the VGM (SOLAS)** in container-related questions — it is a **high-priority topic** under KTU 2024.
> 8. **Unit errors** — mixing litres with m³, barrels with tonnes without conversion. Always carry units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Marine Cargo Survey (Dry, Liquid, Container)**
> - **Three cargo families** — Dry bulk, Liquid bulk, Containerised — each has unique survey procedures and documentation.
> - **Draft survey** uses **Archimedes' principle**; requires 6 corrections: density, trim, list, hog/sag, appendage, constants.
> - **Standard density reference** for hydrostatic tables = **1.025 t/m³** (standard seawater).
> - **TPC** = tonnes per cm immersion; **TPC = Waterplane Area (m²)/100**.
> - **Stowage factor** = volume (m³) per tonne of cargo; low for ore (~0.4 m³/t), high for grain (~1.5 m³/t).
> - **Liquid cargo** uses **ullage + innage** = TRH; samples taken per **API MPMS** in three zones.
> - **VCF** corrects observed volume to 15 °C using **thermal expansion coefficient β**.
> - **API gravity** = 141.5/SG – 131.5; high API = light liquid.
> - **BS&W** by centrifuge gives sediment and water content in petroleum.
> - **Container survey** focuses on **CSC plate, PTI, seal verification, VGM compliance**.
> - **VGM rule (SOLAS Ch. VI, Reg. 2)** effective 1 July 2016 — **shipper's responsibility**.
> - **Industry tolerance** for bulk cargo shortage = **0.5% to 1%** by mass.
> - **Survey reports** are legal documents admissible in arbitration (LMAA), admiralty courts, and under UCP 600.
> - **Hull inspection perspective** of cargo survey: shear force, bending moment, sloshing, hold lining wear, and tank coating integrity.
> - **Draft survey** accuracy = ±0.5%; **shore scale** accuracy = ±0.1%; **flow meter** accuracy = ±0.15% (best for liquid).
> - Always **state the VCF, SG, and temperature** in liquid cargo quantity calculations.
> - For KTU 2024, emphasize **SOLAS VGM, MARPOL Annex I ODME, and CSR (Common Structural Rules)** as extensions of cargo survey.
> - **P&I Club** and **H&M (Hull & Machinery)** insurers are the principal parties commissioning marine cargo and hull surveys.
> - Remember the **"Right of Set-Off"** concept — receivers can deduct proven damages from freight payments.

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Afloat Repairs** (also termed *In-Water Repairs* or *Wet Repairs*) are defined as any maintenance, rectification, modification, or emergency restoration work carried out on a vessel's hull, underwater appendages, or associated systems while the vessel remains **floating in its natural element (water)**, without requiring dry-docking or slipping the vessel onto a hard surface.

According to **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** and **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** guidelines, afloat repairs must be executed under controlled conditions, often necessitating classification society surveyors to verify the integrity of the work. These operations are typically carried out at anchorages, alongside berths, or at designated repair harbours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Society Definition:** Per IACS UR Z17, *afloat repairs* refer to repairs that do not require the vessel to be placed in drydock, but which still maintain compliance with statutory and classification requirements. Any significant structural repair usually demands the ship to be dry-docked eventually for full inspection.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you have a wooden boat in a pond that develops a small leak below the waterline. Instead of dragging the boat out of the pond (which is expensive and time-consuming), you send a person in diving gear to plug the hole from underneath, or you build a small temporary wall (cofferdam) around the damaged area to push water out and work in the dry. **This is exactly what "afloat repairs" means in ship engineering** — repairing the ship without taking it out of the water.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of it like fixing a leaking underground water pipe **without digging up the road**. The "wet" environment makes it harder, but it's faster, cheaper, and often the only feasible option when shutting down operations isn't possible. Similarly, ships earn revenue only when sailing — minimizing off-hire time is critical, making afloat repairs commercially attractive.

---

## 1.3 Why Afloat Repairs Are Essential — The Commercial & Operational Imperative

Modern commercial vessels represent capital investments ranging from **USD 30 million to USD 200+ million** for a single ship. Every day the vessel is out of service (off-hire) costs the operator between **USD 15,000 to USD 100,000 per day**, depending on vessel size and charter rate. Therefore:

- **Minimizing Off-Hire Time** is the single most important driver.
- **Voyage Disruptions** can be avoided by carrying out repairs at port of call.
- **Emergency Rectification** of damage at sea or in remote ports often has no alternative but afloat repair.

---

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Standard Parameters in Afloat Repair Operations

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum working depth for divers | **50 m (commercial air diving)** | Defines operational limits |
| Optimal current for diving | **< 1.5 knots** | Safety threshold |
| Visibility requirement (min.) | **0.5 m – 2 m** | For effective work |
| Wave height limit | **Hs < 1.0 m** | For safe diving |
| Wind speed limit | **< 20 knots** | Crane/scaffold safety |
| Typical repair cost (vs drydock) | **30% – 60%** | Major cost benefit |
| Time saving | **50% – 70%** | vs full drydocking |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The classification society (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV) **must always be informed** before undertaking any significant structural afloat repair. Failure to notify may void the vessel's class status and insurance coverage.

---

## 1.5 Taxonomy of Afloat Repairs (KTU Module 2 Framework)

```
AFLOAT REPAIRS
├── 1. EMERGENCY REPAIRS
│     ├── Collision damage

├── 2. VOYAGE REPAIRS
│     ├── Minor hull plate renewals

│     └── Anode replacement
├── 3. SCHEDULED/ROUTINE REPAIRS
│     ├── Cathodic protection check
│     ├── Propeller polishing
│     └── Underwater hull cleaning
└── 4. MODIFICATION / CONVERSION
      ├── Bow thruster installation
      ├── Sea chest modification
      └── Underwater fittings upgrade
```

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Operational Logic Behind Afloat Repairs — Step-by-Step Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The decision to perform an **afloat repair** is governed by a structured engineering decision tree, balancing **safety, regulatory compliance, technical feasibility, and commercial cost-benefit analysis**.

### Step 1: Damage Assessment & Survey
- Visual inspection by **divers** (using underwater video / ROV).
- **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**: Ultrasonic Thickness Measurement (UTM), magnetic particle inspection, dye penetrant.
- Documentation of findings (diver's report, photographic evidence).

### Step 2: Feasibility Study
**Three fundamental questions must be answered affirmatively:**

1. **Can it be safely done afloat?** (weather, water depth, current, visibility)
2. **Is the ship stable in the damaged condition?** (intact & damage stability)
3. **Does class/statutory rules allow it without dry-docking?**

### Step 3: Class Society & Flag State Approval
- Submit **Repair Procedure Specification (RPS)** to class surveyor.
- Flag state may require notification (especially for SOLAS-related work).
- Port state clearance for hot work, diving, etc.

### Step 4: Risk Assessment & Permit to Work
- **Hazard Identification (HAZID)**.
- **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)**.
- Permit to Work: Hot Work, Enclosed Space, Working Over Water, Diving.

### Step 5: Execution
- Diver deployment / cofferdam erection / shoreside support.
- Continuous monitoring by class surveyor.
- NDT post-repair.

### Step 6: Documentation & Sea Trials
- As-built drawings updated.
- Class certificate endorsed.
- Trials conducted if machinery involved.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although afloat repair is largely procedural, certain engineering calculations are **frequently tested** in KTU examinations. Master the formulas below.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 2 | $F_{buoy} = \rho_{w} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Buoyancy force (Archimedes' Principle) | $\text{N}$ |
| 3 | $\Delta W = \rho_{w} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$ | Weight of water displaced (cofferdam pumping) | $\text{N}$ |
| 4 | $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height (stability check) | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | $TPC = \frac{A_{wp}}{100}$ (in SW) | Tonnes Per Centimetre immersion | $\text{t/cm}$ |
| 6 | $Draft\ Change = \frac{\Delta W}{TPC \times 100}$ | Trim/draft change due to weight shift | $\text{m}$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2t}$ | Hoop stress in cylindrical cofferdam | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 8 | $V_{diver} = \frac{Q_{air}}{P_{amb} - P_{water}}$ | Effective diver breathing air volume | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 9 | $P_{critical} = \frac{2 \cdot E \cdot t^2}{D^3 \cdot (1-\nu^2)}$ | Critical buckling pressure of cofferdam wall | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | $N_{insitu} = N_{dry} \cdot k_{water}$ | Welding correction factor (wet welding: $k_{water} \approx 0.6$) | – |

**Where:**
- $\rho$ = density (water: $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, fresh: $\rho_{fw} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$)
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- $h$ = depth below waterline (m)
- $D$ = cofferdam diameter (m)
- $t$ = plate thickness (m)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (Steel: $E = 2.1 \times 10^{11}\ \text{Pa}$)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (Steel: $\nu = 0.3$)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** For KTU exams, remember the **"Rule of 3 Ps"** for Afloat Repair decisions: **P**ersonnel (qualified divers), **P**ermits (PTW system), **P**recipitation (weather window). If any one fails — *abort the repair*.

---

## 2.3 Critical Engineering Concepts in Afloat Repair

### 2.3.1 Intact & Damage Stability During Repair

During cofferdam construction or heavy underwater work, the vessel's **centre of gravity (KG)** and **metacentric height (GM)** can shift. The **damage stability** case must be re-evaluated:

- If the damage is in a forward or aft compartment, the ship's **trim** changes.
- Heavy cofferdam structures can add significant topside weight.
- Free surface effect from partially flooded compartments must be considered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The IMO SOLAS Chapter II-1 Part B-1 damage stability calculations apply if any compartment is breached or dewatered during repair. Students must be able to articulate the **"three-compartment-flooded"** worst-case scenario.

### 2.3.2 Cathodic Protection (CP) Considerations

Afloat repair work often involves:
- **Sacrificial Anode (Impressed Current) Replacement** by divers.
- Temporary removal of CP may accelerate corrosion in adjacent areas.
- Must be re-energized and tested post-repair.

### 2.3.3 Welding Below Waterline — The "Wet Welding" Phenomenon

Standard arc welding cannot be performed underwater directly. Methods used:

1. **Hyperbaric Welding** — in a pressurized cofferdam (dry habitat).
2. **Wet Welding** — using waterproof electrodes; strength reduced by ~40% ($k_{water} \approx 0.6$).
3. **Mechanical Patches** — bolted or epoxy-grouted steel plates (preferred for emergency).

> [!WARNING]
> Wet welding **DOES NOT** meet class society requirements for primary structural members. Always use hyperbaric (dry) welding inside a cofferdam for class-accepted repairs.

### 2.3.4 Diver Safety & Decompression

- Decompression Sickness ("the bends") occurs if a diver ascends too quickly.
- Standard tables: **US Navy Dive Tables**, **IMCA (International Marine Contractors Association)** tables.
- Bottom time vs. depth relationship is critical.

| Depth (m) | Max Bottom Time (no deco) | Required Deco Stops |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | **Unlimited** | None |
| 18 | **55 min** | None |
| 30 | **20 min** | 7 min @ 3 m |
| 40 | **10 min** | 25 min @ 3 m |
| 50 | **5 min** | 50 min @ 3 m |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Afloat repair methodologies are employed across:

- **Commercial Shipping** (tankers, bulk carriers, container ships)
- **Naval Vessels** (submarine pressure hull, destroyer hulls)
- **Offshore Structures** (FPSO hulls, semi-submersibles, SPAR platforms)
- **Offshore Wind** (monopile transition piece repairs)
- **Inland Waterways** (river barges, lock gates)

In all cases, the **core principle** is the same: **minimize downtime while ensuring safety and class compliance**.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Hydrostatic Pressure on a Diver Working on the Hull

> **Problem Statement:** A diver is performing an afloat repair on a ship's hull at a depth of **12 m** in seawater. Calculate the absolute pressure acting on the diver, and determine the minimum volume of compressed air required at the surface for 30 minutes of work, given the diver's consumption rate of **40 litres/min** at surface pressure.

### Given Data
- Depth: $h = 12\ \text{m}$
- Seawater density: $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101325\ \text{Pa}$
- Diver consumption rate: $Q = 40\ \text{L/min}$ (at surface)
- Work duration: $t = 30\ \text{min}$

### Step 1: Calculate Gauge Pressure at Depth

The hydrostatic pressure due to the water column:

$$
P_{gauge} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
P_{gauge} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12
$$

$$
P_{gauge} = 120{,}627\ \text{Pa} \approx 120.63\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The water column above the diver exerts pressure simply because water has mass. Every metre of seawater contributes roughly $10.05\ \text{kPa}$, so at 12 m the pressure contribution is around $120\ \text{kPa}$ of gauge pressure (above atmospheric).

### Step 2: Calculate Absolute Pressure

Absolute pressure is the sum of atmospheric and hydrostatic pressures:

$$
P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}
$$

$$
P_{abs} = 101{,}325 + 120{,}627 = 221{,}952\ \text{Pa} \approx 221.95\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The diver experiences atmospheric pressure (the weight of the air column above the water surface) PLUS the water column pressure. This total is the absolute pressure felt by the diver's body.

### Step 3: Express Pressure in Bar (for practical use)

$$
P_{abs} = \frac{221{,}952}{100{,}000} = 2.22\ \text{bar}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Boyle's Law to Calculate Compressed Air Volume

Boyle's Law: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (at constant temperature).

The diver consumes $Q = 40\ \text{L/min}$ at surface pressure ($P_1 = 1\ \text{bar}$). At depth, the same mass of air occupies a smaller volume, but the lungs demand the same surface-equivalent volume per breath. So we calculate the equivalent surface consumption:

$$
V_{surface} = Q \times t = 40 \times 30 = 1200\ \text{L}
$$

But the **volume of air at the diver's working pressure** (compressed in the cylinder) is:

$$
V_{cylinder} = \frac{P_{surface} \times V_{surface}}{P_{abs}}
$$

$$
V_{cylinder} = \frac{1 \times 1200}{2.22}
$$

$$
V_{cylinder} \approx 540.5\ \text{L}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Because the air is compressed to a higher pressure in the cylinder, the same mass of air takes up a smaller physical volume. So the diver's tank needs only 540.5 L of physical space to deliver the equivalent of 1200 L at the surface.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Cofferdam Buoyancy & Stability Check

> **Problem Statement:** A cylindrical cofferdam of **3 m diameter** and **4 m height** is to be installed on a flat hull plate at **6 m** below the waterline. Calculate: (a) the upward buoyancy force on the cofferdam, (b) the additional downward ballast weight required to keep it seated on the hull, and (c) the hoop stress in the cofferdam wall (thickness $t = 12\ \text{mm}$).

### Given Data
- Diameter: $D = 3\ \text{m}$
- Height: $H = 4\ \text{m}$
- Depth (centre of cofferdam): $h_c = 6 + (4/2) = 8\ \text{m}$
- Wall thickness: $t = 0.012\ \text{m}$
- Seawater density: $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

### Step 1: Calculate External Volume of Cofferdam

$$
V_{coffer} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot H = \pi \cdot (1.5)^2 \cdot 4
$$

$$
V_{coffer} = 28.27\ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 2: Buoyancy Force

$$
F_{buoy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{coffer}
$$

$$
F_{buoy} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 28.27
$$

$$
F_{buoy} = 284{,}260\ \text{N} \approx 284.26\ \text{kN}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Archimedes' principle states that the upward buoyant force equals the weight of the water displaced. With the cofferdam completely submerged, the displaced water volume equals the external volume of the cofferdam.

### Step 3: Self-Weight of the Cofferdam Wall

Surface area of cofferdam wall (cylinder without top/bottom):

$$
A_{wall} = \pi \cdot D \cdot H = \pi \times 3 \times 4 = 37.70\ \text{m}^2
$$

Mass of steel wall ($7.85 \times 10^3\ \text{kg/m}^3$ density):

$$
m_{wall} = A_{wall} \cdot t \cdot \rho_{steel} = 37.70 \times 0.012 \times 7850
$$

$$
m_{wall} = 3551\ \text{kg}
$$

Weight:

$$
W_{wall} = m_{wall} \cdot g = 3551 \times 9.81 \approx 34{,}835\ \text{N} \approx 34.84\ \text{kN}
$$

### Step 4: Additional Ballast Required

The buoyant force vastly exceeds the self-weight. Required ballast:

$$
F_{ballast} = F_{buoy} - W_{wall} = 284.26 - 34.84 = 249.42\ \text{kN}
$$

Required ballast mass:

$$
m_{ballast} = \frac{F_{ballast}}{g} = \frac{249{,}420}{9.81} \approx 25{,}425\ \text{kg} \approx 25.4\ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Without ballast, the cofferdam would shoot upward like a cork. Divers use **kentledge (heavy weights)** — typically scrap steel or chain — to hold the cofferdam in place against buoyancy.

### Step 5: Hoop Stress in the Cofferdam Wall

For a thin-walled cylinder, hoop stress:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}
$$

Pressure at the centre of the cofferdam:

$$
P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h_c = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8 \approx 80{,}442\ \text{Pa}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{80{,}442 \times 1.5}{0.012} = 10{,}055{,}250\ \text{Pa} \approx 10.06\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The hoop stress represents the circumferential tension trying to "burst" the cylinder. With mild steel having a yield strength of ~235 MPa, the factor of safety is:

$$
FS = \frac{235}{10.06} \approx 23.4
$$

This is **very safe**, indicating the cofferdam is over-designed for the load — typical marine practice.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Criteria for Afloat vs Drydock Repair

| Criterion | Afloat Repair FAVOURED | Drydock Repair REQUIRED |
|---|---|---|
| Damage location | Above & below waterline | Heavy structural (keel, bottom plating) |
| Damage extent | < 5% hull area affected | > 10% hull area, multiple plates |
| Operational urgency | Charter/hire commitment | No commercial pressure |
| Cost consideration | Limited budget | Long-term integrity critical |
| Weather window | Calm, settled weather | Always available |
| Class requirement | Minor / voyage repair | Major structural (UR Z17) |
| Time available | < 7 days | > 14 days |
| Diver accessibility | Readily available | Not relevant |
| Submerged zone work | Inside cofferdam possible | Direct access required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** IACS UR Z17 (Procedural Requirements for Service Units) explicitly states that **major structural repairs** affecting the strength of the ship **must** be undertaken in drydock unless the class society is satisfied that an equivalent inspection and repair can be carried out afloat.
