# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define "Afloat Repairs" in the context of ship hull maintenance. List any four situations where afloat repairs are preferred over dry-docking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Afloat repairs are maintenance, rectification, or emergency restoration works carried out on a vessel's hull, underwater fittings, and associated systems while the vessel remains floating in water, without the need to dry-dock the vessel.
>
> **Situations where afloat repairs are preferred (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Commercial urgency:** When the vessel has an active charter or hire contract and cannot afford off-hire time.
> 2. **Minor damage:** Small cracks, anode replacement, propeller polishing, sea valve grinding.
> 3. **Remote location:** When the vessel is at a port without a dry-dock facility.
> 4. **Cost constraint:** Afloat repairs cost 30–60% of dry-dock costs.
> 5. **Emergency:** Collision or grounding damage needing immediate attention to enable safe onward voyage.

---

### Question 2: Conceptual Question `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is a "cofferdam" in the context of afloat hull repairs? Explain its working principle in 3-4 sentences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A cofferdam is a **temporary watertight enclosure**, typically cylindrical, that is mounted over a damaged area of the ship's underwater hull. It works on the principle of **displacement and pumping**: once sealed against the hull plate with rubber gaskets, water inside the cofferdam is pumped out using submersible pumps, creating a **dry working chamber** at depth. This dry environment allows divers to perform **class-acceptable welding, plate renewal, and NDT inspection** — operations that would otherwise be impossible underwater. Cofferdams are usually ballasted with kentledge (heavy weights) to resist the strong upward buoyant force trying to push them off the hull.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the **planning and procedural sequence** for undertaking a major afloat hull repair on a commercial vessel. Include all safety, regulatory, and engineering considerations. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A cylindrical cofferdam of diameter **2.5 m** and height **3.5 m** is installed at a depth of **8 m** below the waterline in seawater ($\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$). The cofferdam wall thickness is **10 mm**. Calculate:
  (i) The buoyant force on the cofferdam.
  (ii) The hoop stress in the wall at mid-height. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION (a) — Planning Sequence for Major Afloat Hull Repair (7 marks)

The planning sequence consists of **seven systematic phases**:

**Phase 1 — Damage Assessment (1 mark):**
- Diver survey with underwater video / still photography.
- Ultrasonic thickness measurement (UTM) of adjacent plating.
- Report by class-approved diving contractor.

**Phase 2 — Engineering Evaluation (1 mark):**
- Stability check: intact and damage stability under partial flooding scenarios.
- Trim and draft calculations considering cofferdam weight and ballasting.
- Approval of repair procedure specification (RPS) by class.

**Phase 3 — Statutory & Class Approval (1 mark):**
- Notify classification society (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's, DNV).
- Flag state notification if SOLAS-relevant.
- Port state permit for hot work and diving.

**Phase 4 — Risk Assessment (1 mark):**
- HAZID (Hazard Identification) study.
- JSA (Job Safety Analysis) for each task.
- Permit to Work system: Hot Work, Enclosed Space, Working Over Water, Diving Permit.

**Phase 5 — Mobilization (1 mark):**
- Diver team with IMCA certification.
- Equipment: cofferdam, pumps, welding plant, NDT instruments.
- Communication: hard-wire comms + standby diver + dive supervisor.

**Phase 6 — Execution & QC (1 mark):**
- Continuous class surveyor presence.
- NDT after welding: UT, MPI, DPI as applicable.
- Hydrostatic testing if sea chest/valve involved.

**Phase 7 — Documentation (1 mark):**
- As-built drawings updated.
- Class endorsement on the vessel's records.
- Sea trials if machinery affected.
- Return to commercial service.

---

#### SOLUTION (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 2.5\ \text{m}$, $H = 3.5\ \text{m}$
- Depth of cofferdam base = $8\ \text{m}$, so centre at $h_c = 8 + 1.75 = 9.75\ \text{m}$
- $t = 0.010\ \text{m}$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Part (i) — Buoyant Force (3.5 marks):**

External volume of cylinder:

$$
V = \pi r^2 H = \pi \times (1.25)^2 \times 3.5
$$

$$
V = 17.18\ \text{m}^3
$$

Buoyant force:

$$
F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 17.18
$$

$$
F_B \approx 172{,}762\ \text{N} \approx 172.76\ \text{kN}
$$

**Part (ii) — Hoop Stress (3.5 marks):**

Pressure at mid-height:

$$
P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h_c = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9.75
$$

$$
P = 98{,}050\ \text{Pa} \approx 98.05\ \text{kPa}
$$

Hoop stress:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t} = \frac{98{,}050 \times 1.25}{0.010}
$$

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = 12{,}256{,}250\ \text{Pa} \approx 12.26\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Conclusion:** Hoop stress is well below the yield strength of mild steel (~235 MPa), giving a factor of safety of **~19.2** — fully adequate.

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **role of a commercial diver** in afloat hull repairs. Discuss safety protocols, equipment, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A ship has a damaged bottom plate in way of the forward bilge at a depth of **10 m** below the waterline. Compare **three methods** of repairing this damage afloat, and recommend the most suitable option with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION (a) — Role of Commercial Diver in Afloat Repairs (7 marks)

**Role of Diver (2 marks):**
The commercial diver acts as the **"underwater hands and eyes"** of the ship repair engineer. They perform inspection, cleaning, NDT, cutting, welding assistance, fitting, and photo/video documentation. Divers are certified by IMCA, ADCI (Association of Diving Contractors International), or national bodies.

**Safety Protocols (2 marks):**
- **Dive Plan & Risk Assessment** filed before every dive.
- **Standby Diver** always on-site, fully kitted.
- **Hard-wire communication** (umbilical) to surface.
- **Surface-supplied diving** preferred (vs SCUBA) for ship hull work.
- Decompression tables strictly followed.
- **Lifeline** and lifting winch with quick-release.

**Equipment (1.5 marks):**
- Helmet or band mask, dry suit, thermal undergarment.
- Hot-water suit for deep/cold dives.
- Umbilical: air hose, comms cable, pneumofathometer, video cable.
- Tools: hydraulic cutters, grinders, impact wrenches.
- NDT: UT probe manipulators.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Depth limit: ~50 m for air; deeper requires mixed gas (heliox).
- Visibility: often < 1 m in harbours.
- Bottom time limited by decompression obligations.
- Currents > 1.5 knots stop work.
- Water temperature, contamination (oil, sewage).
- Cannot work effectively in waves > 1 m significant height.

---

#### SOLUTION (b) — Comparison of Three Afloat Repair Methods (7 marks)

| Method | Suitability for Forward Bilge Damage at 10 m | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Wet Welding** | Limited — minor cracks only | Quick, no cofferdam | Reduced weld strength ($k \approx 0.6$); not class-acceptable for primary structure |
| **2. Cofferdam Repair** | **Highly Suitable** | Class-accepted; allows dry welding & NDT | Buoyancy requires heavy ballast; weather-sensitive |
| **3. Mechanical Patch (Epoxy / Bolted)** | Suitable for emergency temporary | Fast installation; no welding needed | Permanent solution requires subsequent drydock |

**Recommendation (3 marks):**
The **cofferdam method (Method 2)** is the most suitable for the following reasons:

1. **Class Acceptance:** IACS and major classification societies accept cofferdam-based welding as equivalent to dry-dock repairs for primary structure.
2. **Permanent Repair:** Provides a welded insert that restores full hull strength, unlike a temporary patch.
3. **Depth Suitability:** At 10 m depth, the cofferdam hydrostatic pressure is well within manageable limits (~110 kPa) and commercially divers can work effectively.
4. **Stability:** With proper kentledge ballasting (e.g., ~15 tonnes), the cofferdam can be safely seated on the hull.
5. **Inspection & QC:** Dry chamber allows full NDT (UT, MPI, DPI) under class surveyor supervision.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **confuse the roles of flag state, class society, and port state**. Memorize:
> - **Flag State** = issues the ship's certificates (e.g., SOLAS, Load Line).
> - **Class Society** = sets technical standards and surveys compliance.
> - **Port State** = enforces regulations while the ship is in its waters.
> Conflating these three will cost you 1-2 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it to your study wall before exams.**

- [ ] **Definition**: Afloat repairs = hull/system work carried out while the ship remains in the water.
- [ ] **Also called**: In-water repairs, wet repairs, voyage repairs.
- [ ] **Primary Driver**: Minimization of off-hire time and cost savings (30-60% of drydock cost).
- [ ] **Key Methods**: (1) Diver-based, (2) Cofferdam-based, (3) Shoreside support, (4) Repair tender.
- [ ] **Cofferdam Principle**: Watertight enclosure + pumping = dry chamber at depth.
- [ ] **Buoyancy Force Formula**: $F_B = \rho_w g V$ — must be countered by kentledge ballast.
- [ ] **Hoop Stress Formula**: $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P r}{t}$ — for cylindrical cofferdam walls.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic Pressure**: $P = \rho g h$ — increases by ~10 kPa per metre of seawater.
- [ ] **Class Society Notification** is **mandatory** for all structural afloat repairs.
- [ ] **Diving Limits**: 50 m (air), 1.5 knots current, Hs < 1 m.
- [ ] **Wet Welding Strength Factor**: $k_{water} \approx 0.6$ — NOT acceptable for primary structure.
- [ ] **Stability Check**: GM must remain positive throughout the repair; damage stability per SOLAS Ch II-1.
- [ ] **Safety Permits**: Hot Work, Enclosed Space, Working Over Water, Diving Permit — **all 4** required.
- [ ] **Documentation**: As-built drawings, class endorsement, sea trials (if applicable).
- [ ] **IACS UR Z17**: Governs procedural requirements for service units and afloat repairs.
- [ ] **Real-world Analogy**: Fixing an underground pipe without digging the road.
- [ ] **Three "P"s Rule**: **P**ersonnel, **P**ermits, **P**recipitation (weather) — must all be satisfied.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes of Wear and Damage in Ship's Hull
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Causes of Wear and Damage in Ship's Hull

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Hull Inspection** (OENST832, KTU 2024 Scheme), the term **"Causes of Wear and Damage"** refers to the comprehensive set of **physical, chemical, electrochemical, mechanical, biological, and operational agents** that progressively degrade the structural integrity, thickness, and surface condition of a ship's hull plating, framing, appendages, and protective coatings throughout the vessel's service life.

Wear and damage are formally classified by **classification societies** (IACS members such as Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, IRS) into distinct categories based on the **origin, mechanism, location, and severity** of the deterioration. Each category dictates specific **survey intervals, acceptance criteria, and repair protocols** under the harmonised Common Structural Rules (CSR) and SOLAS/IMO frameworks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** This topic establishes the *etiologic foundation* upon which all subsequent inspection methodologies (ultrasonic thickness gauging, close-up surveys, NDT techniques) are built. Without a thorough understanding of the *causes*, an inspector cannot correctly *interpret* the *effects* observed during surveys.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a ship's hull as the **"skin and skeleton" of a human body** subjected to decades of harsh living conditions:

- The **ocean** is like a relentless, corrosive acid bath combined with a sandblaster (waves, salt, suspended particles).
- The **cargo the ship carries** is like constantly changing heavy weights that stress the body (loading/unloading cycles, slamming).
- **Engine vibrations and propeller rotation** are like continuous shaking that loosens joints.
- **Marine organisms (barnacles, algae)** are like parasites that attach to the skin and slowly eat into it.
- **Grounding or contact with quay walls** is like bruising or breaking a bone.

Just as a doctor must first understand the *causes* of an injury (fracture, infection, wear-and-tear) before prescribing treatment, a hull inspector must understand the *causes* of wear/damage before deciding whether to **accept, monitor, or renew** a structural member.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Wastage** | General, often uniform, loss of steel thickness due to corrosion, typically measured as a **percentage reduction** from the original as-built thickness. |
| **Pitting** | Localised, deep, narrow corrosion cavities (crater-like) on the hull surface. |
| **Grooving** | Severe localised corrosion at structural intersections, e.g., at the toes of brackets. |
| **Edge Corrosion** | Accelerated corrosion at the edges of plates where coating failure is common. |
| **Sacrificial Anode** | A piece of more electrochemically active metal (typically **zinc**, **aluminium**, or **magnesium**) bolted to the hull to preferentially corrode and protect the steel. |
| **Slamming** | Violent impact of the bow or forward bottom plating against the sea surface in heavy weather. |
| **Cavitation** | Formation and violent collapse of vapour bubbles in a liquid near a propeller or hull surface, causing surface erosion. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exam Focus:** The examiner expects students to **differentiate** between *wear* (gradual, predictable thickness loss) and *damage* (sudden, localised mechanical/impact failure). Always quote the **mechanism** (chemical, mechanical, biological) when identifying the cause.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Causes

The causes of wear and damage in a ship's hull can be systematically classified into **six (6) primary families**, as adopted by all IACS classification societies and codified in the **KTU OENST832 Module 2 syllabus**:

### A. Corrosion-Related Causes (Electrochemical/Chemical)
1. **General (Uniform) Corrosion** — uniform loss of thickness over large areas.
2. **Pitting Corrosion** — localised deep cavities.
3. **Crevice Corrosion** — at gasket faces, under deposits, behind linings.
4. **Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion** — at bronze propellers, Monel seacocks, stainless-steel fittings in contact with mild steel.
5. **Erosion-Corrosion** — combined chemical attack + mechanical removal of corrosion product.
6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — under sustained tensile stress in a corrosive environment.
7. **Microbial Induced Corrosion (MIC)** — accelerated by sulphate-reducing bacteria in ballast tanks.

### B. Mechanical Wear Causes
1. **Abrasive Wear** — from suspended sand/silt in water; most severe in ballast tanks and suction areas.
2. **Cavitation Erosion** — propeller blades, rudder, pump impellers.
3. **Slamming & Whipping** — bow and forward bottom in head seas.
4. **Fretting Wear** — at loose riveted/bolted connections under vibration.
5. **Friction from Cargo** — in ore carriers, tankers with sloshing, holds of bulk carriers.

### C. Impact / Accidental Damage
1. **Grounding / Stranding** — contact with seabed.
2. **Contact Damage** — berthing impact with quay, fender failure.
3. **Collision** — with another vessel.
4. **Anchor Damage** — hawse pipe deformation, plate indentations.
5. **Heavy Weather Damage** — buckling, deckhouse failure.

### D. Operational & Fatigue Causes
1. **Fatigue Cracking** — at hatch corners, bilge wells, rudder horns, shaft brackets.
2. **Buckling** — from hull girder bending moment in heavy seas.
3. **Plastic Deformation** — overload beyond yield stress.

### E. Biological Causes
1. **Marine Fouling** — barnacles, tubeworms, algae on underwater hull.
2. **Wood-borers (Teredo navalis)** — in wooden hulls (historical context).
3. **Bacterial Action** — in stagnant bilge/ballast water.

### F. Environmental / Material Causes
1. **Atmospheric Corrosion** — in deck areas, superstructure.
2. **Heat Damage** — in engine room, boiler spaces.
3. **Chemical Attack** — from aggressive cargoes (acids, fertilizers in chemical tankers).
4. **Coating Breakdown** — UV, age, mechanical damage to paint.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Underlying Science

### 2.2.1 Why Does Corrosion Occur?

A ship's hull is predominantly made of **mild steel** (yield strength ~235 MPa) which is **thermodynamically unstable** in seawater. The reaction is:

$$ \text{Fe} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 $$

The **Nernst Equation** governs the corrosion potential:

$$ E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]} $$

where $E^\circ$ is the standard electrode potential, $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}$, $T$ is absolute temperature, $n$ is electron count, and $F = 96{,}485 \, \text{C/mol}$ (**Faraday's constant**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive 'Why':** Steel is made by smelting iron ore — this is an *uphill* energy process. Nature wants to *return* the iron to its oxide state (rust). Seawater is the **electrolyte** that allows this reaction to proceed rapidly. Cathodic protection (Impressed Current or sacrificial anodes) supplies electrons to *force* the reaction in reverse.

### 2.2.2 How is Wear Quantified?

The **rate of corrosion** is empirically given (in mm/year) and forms the basis of the **IACS allowance** for wastage. A widely used empirical form is:

$$ C_{rate} = \frac{T_{original} - T_{measured}}{t_{service}} \quad [\text{mm/year}] $$

where $T_{original}$ is the as-built thickness (from the **Midship Section Plan**), $T_{measured}$ is the gauged thickness at survey, and $t_{service}$ is the elapsed time.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion rate $C_{rate} = (T_{orig} - T_{meas})/t$ | mm/year | Determining renewal thickness limit |
| 2 | Nernst equation $E = E^\circ + (RT/nF)\ln([Ox]/[Red])$ | Volts (V) | Predicting galvanic series position |
| 3 | Faraday's law $m = (M \cdot I \cdot t)/(n \cdot F)$ | kg of metal lost | Calculating anode life |
| 4 | Stern-tube current leak $I_{leak} \le 1500 \cdot P_{shaft}/V$ | Amperes | DNV rule for shaft earthing |
| 5 | Plate renewal criterion $T_{meas} < T_{renewal}$ | mm | IACS UR Z7 / Z10 limits |
| 6 | Wastage % $= [(T_{orig} - T_{meas})/T_{orig}] \times 100$ | % | Survey reporting |
| 7 | Fatigue life (Palmgren-Miner) $D = \sum (n_i/N_i)$ | dimensionless | Cumulative damage summation |
| 8 | Cavitation number $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v)/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$ | dimensionless | Propeller cavitation onset |
| 9 | Reynolds number $Re = \rho V L/\mu$ | dimensionless | Flow regime near hull |
| 10 | Snell's law for oblique UT $sin\theta_1/v_1 = sin\theta_2/v_2$ | angle | Inspection technique selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the *units* alongside any numerical value in your answer. A common mistake is writing $C_{rate} = 0.3$ (forgetting **mm/year**) — full marks require units.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Shipyards & Design Offices:** Use corrosion allowance tables (e.g., 0.3 mm/year for ballast tanks, 0.1 mm/year for double bottom) to specify **as-built thickness** ($T_{as-built} = T_{required} + T_{corrosion}$).
- **Classification Society Surveyors:** Apply renewal criteria — generally, plate must be renewed if measured thickness is **less than 75%** of the as-built for general local areas, or **less than the minimum allowable** under IACS UR Z7.
- **Ship Operators:** Schedule **dry-docking intervals** (typically 2.5 to 5 years) based on predicted wear rates.
- **Hull Inspection Engineers (KTU OENST832 Graduates):** Diagnose root causes from observed damage patterns and recommend preventive maintenance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Faraday's Law of Corrosion

This derivation is **mandatory** for the KTU exam when questions on cathodic protection or anode design appear.

**Statement:** The mass $m$ of metal dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge $Q$ passed through the electrolyte.

**Step 1 — Start with the basic charge definition.**

$$ Q = I \cdot t \quad [\text{Coulombs, C}] $$

where $I$ is the current in Amperes and $t$ is time in seconds.

**Step 2 — Express charge per mole of electrons.**

By definition, **one mole of electrons** carries a charge equal to **Faraday's constant**:

$$ F = N_A \cdot e = 96{,}485 \quad \text{C/mol} $$

where $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ (Avogadro's number) and $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (elementary charge).

**Step 3 — Number of moles of electrons transferred.**

If the metal reaction is $\text{M}^{n+} + n e^- \rightarrow \text{M}$, then for a total charge $Q$:

$$ \text{Moles of } e^- = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F} $$

**Step 4 — Moles of metal dissolved.**

Each mole of metal requires $n$ moles of electrons:

$$ \text{Moles of metal} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} $$

**Step 5 — Convert moles to mass.**

Multiplying by the molar mass $M$ of the metal:

$$ m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \quad [\text{kg}] $$

This is **Faraday's Law of Electrolysis** — the governing equation for calculating sacrificial anode life and ICCP (Impressed Current Cathodic Protection) design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Example:** An aluminium sacrificial anode ($M = 27$ g/mol, $n = 3$) discharging at $I = 2$ A continuously for 1 year ($t = 31{,}536{,}000$ s) loses:
>
> $m = (0.027 \times 2 \times 31{,}536{,}000) / (3 \times 96{,}485) \approx 5.88$ kg/year.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Wastage Percentage and Renewal Threshold

**Step 1 — Define wastage percentage.**

$$ W\% = \frac{T_{original} - T_{measured}}{T_{original}} \times 100 $$

**Step 2 — Apply IACS criterion for "substantial corrosion."**

Per IACS UR Z7.1, **substantial corrosion** is defined as $W\% \ge 75\%$ in any 3 m × 3 m local area, OR original thickness reduced to the **corrosion addition** value, whichever is less.

**Step 3 — Solve for renewal limit.**

Setting $W\% = 75\%$:

$$ T_{measured,min} = 0.25 \cdot T_{original} $$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (typical bottom plate).**

For a bottom plate of original thickness $T_{orig} = 18$ mm:

$$ T_{min} = 0.25 \times 18 = 4.5 \text{ mm} $$

The plate must be **renewed** if measured thickness is below 4.5 mm in any local area exceeding 3 m × 3 m.

**Step 5 — Application of corrosion rate for prediction.**

If current wastage is $W\%_{current}$ over age $t_1$, the predicted time to reach renewal:

$$ t_{renewal} = t_1 \cdot \frac{W\%_{target}}{W\%_{current}} $$

For example, if $W\%_{current} = 25\%$ at $t_1 = 5$ years and target is $75\%$:

$$ t_{renewal} = 5 \times \frac{75}{25} = 15 \text{ years from new} $$

Therefore, **5 more years of safe service** remain before renewal is required — assuming linear progression (a conservative assumption; in reality, corrosion often accelerates).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cause → Mechanism → Visible Symptom → Location

| # | Cause | Mechanism | Visible Symptom on Hull | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uniform corrosion | Electrochemical attack over broad area | General rust, orange-brown scale | Boot-top, weather deck, internals |
| 2 | Pitting | Localised breakdown of passive film | Deep, narrow dark craters | Welds, coating defects, lower bilges |
| 3 | Galvanic | Dissimilar metals in electrolyte | Severe localised pitting near bronze/stainless | Propeller hub, seacock connections |
| 4 | Crevice | Oxygen depletion inside narrow gap | White/powdery deposits with rust streaks | Behind stiffeners, lap joints, gasket faces |
| 5 | Cavitation erosion | Bubble implosion near metal surface | Honeycomb / sponge-like pitting | Propeller blade tips, rudder leading edge |
| 6 | Erosion-corrosion | Fluid flow removes protective film | Smooth, scalloped, "horseshoe" grooves | Bow thruster tunnel, sea chest, suctions |
| 7 | Slamming | Hydrodynamic impact of hull on wave | Dent, buckle, paint peel at bow | Forward bottom plating, 1st-3rd length |
| 8 | Fatigue cracking | Cyclic stress below yield over many cycles | Hairline crack, often at stress concentration | Hatch corners, bilge keel ends, bracket toes |
| 9 | Grounding | Mechanical contact with seabed | Indentation, tear, perforation | Bottom plating, possibly bilge keels |
| 10 | Marine fouling | Biological attachment | Hard shells, slime layer | Underwater hull (boot-top down) |
| 11 | MIC (Microbial) | Bacteria consume sulphate, release H₂S | Tubercle-like deposits, deep pits | Ballast tanks, double bottom, hot spots |
| 12 | Cargo-induced | Chemical reaction with cargo | Etching, thinning, discolouration | Chemical tanker cargo tanks, bulk holds |
| 13 | Fretting | Small oscillations between contacts | Brown powder, polished wear scars | Loose rivet/bolt joints, bracket connections |
| 14 | Heat damage | Sustained high temperature | Scale, discolouration, reduced strength | Engine room, exhaust uptakes, boiler seats |
| 15 | Buckling | Compressive overload | Sharp crease, plastic deformation | Deck plating in heavy weather |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Strategy:** When answering a question that gives a *symptom* and asks for the *cause* (or vice versa), use this matrix as your mental index. The examiner rewards students who **link mechanism to symptom** in their answer.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Combined Corrosion & Erosion Analysis

**Problem:** A chemical tanker has a cargo tank bottom plate of original thickness 14 mm. After 8 years of service, UT gauging at 5 random points gives: 12.1, 11.8, 11.5, 12.0, 11.6 mm. Calculate (a) average wastage, (b) wastage percentage, (c) predicted renewal year, (d) probable causes.

**Step a — Average measured thickness:**

$$ T_{avg} = \frac{12.1+11.8+11.5+12.0+11.6}{5} = \frac{59.0}{5} = 11.8 \text{ mm} $$

**Step b — Wastage percentage:**

$$ W\% = \frac{14 - 11.8}{14} \times 100 = 15.7\% $$

**Step c — Average corrosion rate and prediction:**

$$ C_{rate} = \frac{14 - 11.8}{8} = 0.275 \text{ mm/year} $$

Renewal threshold (75% wastage): $T_{min} = 0.25 \times 14 = 3.5$ mm. Allowable loss remaining: $11.8 - 3.5 = 8.3$ mm.

Years to renewal:

$$ t_{remaining} = \frac{8.3}{0.275} \approx 30 \text{ years} $$

Total life ≈ 38 years from new — well within normal trading life (25–30 yrs).

**Step d — Probable causes:** Chemical attack from acidic cargo (e.g., phosphoric acid residue), mechanical erosion from frequent cargo washing, possible coating breakdown in service.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Wear and Damage]:::root
    A --> B[Corrosion Family]
    A --> C[Mechanical Wear]
    A --> D[Impact Damage]
    A --> E[Fatigue and Buckling]
    A --> F[Biological Attack]
    A --> G[Environmental and Material]

    B --> B1[Uniform Wastage]
    B --> B2[Pitting]
    B --> B3[Crevice]
    B --> B4[Galvanic]
    B --> B5[Stress Corrosion]
    B --> B6[Microbial Induced]

    C --> C1[Abrasive Wear]
    C --> C2[Cavitation Erosion]
    C --> C3[Slamming]
    C --> C4[Fretting]
    C --> C5[Cargo Friction]

    D --> D1[Grounding]
    D --> D2[Berthing Contact]
    D --> D3[Collision]
    D --> D4[Anchor Damage]

    E --> E1[Fatigue Cracking]
    E --> E2[Buckling]
    E --> E3[Plastic Deformation]

    F --> F1[Marine Fouling]
    F --> F2[Bacterial Action]

    G --> G1[Atmospheric Corrosion]
    G --> G2[Heat Damage]
    G --> G3[Chemical Attack]
    G --> G4[Coating Breakdown]

    classDef root fill:#0b3d91,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef fam fill:#1e6fb8,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Galvanic Corrosion Cell Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Anode[More Active Metal - Sacrificial]
        ZN[Zinc or Aluminium Anode]
    end
    subgraph Cathode[Less Active Metal - Protected]
        ST[Steel Hull Plate]
    end
    subgraph Electrolyte[Conductive Medium]
        SW[Seawater Electrolyte]
    end
    ZN -- Releases electrons e- --> ST
    ZN -- Metal ions Zn2 plus enter --> SW
    ST -- Receives electrons, no corrosion --> SW
    SW -- Completes ionic circuit --> ZN

    classDef anodeNode fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef cathodeNode fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef elecNode fill:#2980b9,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class ZN anodeNode
    class ST cathodeNode
    class SW elecNode
```

## 4.3 Cavitation Erosion Process Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stage 1: Local pressure drops below vapour pressure] --> P2[Stage 2: Vapour bubble nucleates on propeller surface]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Bubble travels to higher pressure region]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Violent bubble collapse - microjet impact]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Micro-jet hits metal - 1000s of MPa localised stress]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: Surface fatigue and material removal]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7: Honeycomb pitting pattern visible on blade]

    classDef stageNode fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6 stageNode
    class P7 endNode
```

## 4.4 Hull Zone Wear Hotspot Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Bow[Forward Region]
        H1[Slamming damage - bottom plating]
        H2[Anchor hawse pipe wear]
        H3[Bow thruster tunnel erosion]
    end
    subgraph Mid[Midship Region]
        H4[Uniform corrosion - underwater hull]
        H5[Fatigue cracks at hatch corners - bulk carriers]
        H6[Bilge keel corrosion]
    end
    subgraph Stern[Aft Region]
        H7[Propeller cavitation erosion]
        H8[Rudder cavitation and corrosion]
        H9[Stern tube bearing wear]
        H10[Shaft earthing galvanic issues]
    end
    subgraph Tank[Internal Tanks]
        H11[Ballast tank pitting - top of tank]
        H12[Double bottom - wastage]
        H13[Cargo tank chemical attack]
    end

    classDef hot fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class H1,H2,H3,H4,H5,H6,H7,H8,H9,H10,H11,H12,H13 hot
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block-level diagram represents a *functional architecture* mapping how causes (e.g., cavitation, galvanic action) interact with ship components. In a KTU answer, you can sketch simplified versions of these to earn easy **diagram marks (2-3 marks)**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q:** Differentiate between **pitting corrosion** and **uniform corrosion** in a ship's hull. State **two locations** where each is commonly observed.

**Model Answer:**

**Pitting Corrosion:** A localised form of corrosion that produces deep, narrow cavities (pits) on the metal surface. The surrounding surface remains largely unattacked. It is caused by local breakdown of the passive oxide film, often initiated at coating defects, weld spatter, or inclusions.

**Uniform Corrosion:** A general, even loss of metal thickness over a broad surface area, caused by uniform electrochemical attack in the absence of protective measures. The rate is relatively constant.

| Type | Locations |
|---|---|
| Pitting | Weld seams, coating defects, propeller hub area (galvanic), ballast tank tops |
| Uniform | Boot-top zone, deck plating, bilge areas of double bottom, internals of unmanned machinery spaces |

Pitting is *more dangerous* than uniform corrosion because it can cause **perforation** even when average thickness loss is small.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q:** What is **cavitation erosion**? List **two structural components** of a ship where it is most commonly observed.

**Model Answer:**

**Cavitation erosion** is a form of surface degradation caused by the **formation and violent collapse of vapour bubbles** in a liquid when local static pressure falls below the vapour pressure of the liquid. The implosion of these bubbles near a metal surface generates **micro-jets** with localised pressures of the order of **1000 MPa**, which mechanically remove material and produce a characteristic **honeycomb or sponge-like pitted surface**.

**Two affected components:**
1. **Propeller blades** (especially tip regions and leading edges of high-skew propellers).
2. **Rudder surface** (leading edge, particularly aft of the propeller slipstream).

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> **KTU Rule Reference:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, every Part B question in a module-level test carries 14 marks and must offer an internal choice between two questions. Both questions are *set on Module 2* and have *equal difficulty index*. The pattern below mirrors a real KTU End Semester Examination.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **six main causes of wear and damage** in a ship's hull. Classify them into **corrosion-related** and **mechanical/operational** categories.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bulk carrier has a side shell plate of **original thickness 16 mm**. After **7 years** of service, ultrasonic thickness measurements at six locations give: **14.2, 13.8, 13.5, 14.0, 13.6, 13.9 mm**. Calculate the **(i) average measured thickness, (ii) average wastage percentage, (iii) average corrosion rate in mm/year, and (iv) the predicted years to reach the IACS substantial corrosion limit of 75% wastage**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Six Main Causes:**

*Corrosion-related (3):*
1. **Uniform corrosion** — general electrochemical attack over wide areas due to moisture, salt, oxygen.
2. **Pitting corrosion** — localised deep cavities at coating defects, welds, or under deposits.
3. **Galvanic corrosion** — at junctions of dissimilar metals (e.g., bronze propeller and steel hull, Monel seacocks).

*Mechanical/Operational (3):*
4. **Slamming** — violent impact of forward bottom against waves causing denting/buckling.
5. **Cavitation erosion** — bubble collapse on propeller/rudder causing honeycomb pitting.
6. **Fatigue cracking** — cyclic wave-induced stresses causing cracks at hatch corners, bracket toes, etc.

[Include a labelled sketch of the hull cross-section showing the affected zones — typical sketch: bow, midbody, stern with annotations.]

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

*Step 1 — Average measured thickness:*

$$ T_{avg} = \frac{14.2+13.8+13.5+14.0+13.6+13.9}{6} = \frac{83.0}{6} = 13.83 \text{ mm} $$

*Step 2 — Average wastage percentage:*

$$ W\% = \frac{16 - 13.83}{16} \times 100 = \frac{2.17}{16} \times 100 = 13.56\% $$

*Step 3 — Average corrosion rate:*

$$ C_{rate} = \frac{16 - 13.83}{7} = \frac{2.17}{7} = 0.31 \text{ mm/year} $$

*Step 4 — Years to reach 75% wastage:*

Renewal thickness at 75% wastage:

$$ T_{min} = 0.25 \times 16 = 4.0 \text{ mm} $$

Allowable remaining loss: $13.83 - 4.0 = 9.83$ mm.

Time to renewal:

$$ t_{remaining} = \frac{9.83}{0.31} \approx 31.7 \text{ years from now} $$

**Total life ≈ 31.7 + 7 = 38.7 years** from new — comfortably within trading life.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **mechanism, locations, and prevention methods** of **(i) galvanic corrosion** and **(ii) microbial-induced corrosion (MIC)** in ship's hull.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **slamming**? Explain the **causes, effects, and locations** on the hull. State the **inspection criteria** for slamming damage under classification society rules.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i) — Galvanic Corrosion:**

*Mechanism:* Occurs when two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in an electrolyte (seawater). The more *active* metal (anode, e.g., zinc) corrodes preferentially, protecting the *less active* metal (cathode, e.g., steel). Governed by the **galvanic series** — metals with more negative electrode potential (e.g., magnesium = -2.37 V) act as anodes.

*Locations:* Propeller-hull interface (bronze/steel), seacocks (Monel/steel), stainless-steel pipe connections in steel tanks, shaft grounding brushes (improper installation).

*Prevention:* Use **sacrificial anodes** (Zn, Al), use **insulating flanges/gaskets** between dissimilar metals, apply **anti-corrosive coatings**, select metals close in galvanic series, use **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**.

**Part (a)(ii) — Microbial Induced Corrosion (MIC):**

*Mechanism:* Sulphate-Reducing Bacteria (SRB) thrive in oxygen-depleted environments (e.g., ballast tanks, double bottoms). They consume sulphates and produce **hydrogen sulphide (H₂S)**, which reacts with steel to form **iron sulphide (FeS)** deposits. Localised anodic pitting occurs beneath the tubercles.

*Locations:* Ballast tank bottoms, double-bottom tanks, engine-room bilges, around pipe penetrations, under debris/deposits.

*Prevention:* Regular **tank cleaning**, **freshwater flushing** of ballast tanks, use of **biocides**, application of **high-quality coatings**, maintaining low water residence time.

**Part (b) — Slamming:**

*Definition:* Slamming is the **violent hydrodynamic impact** of the forward bottom or bow flare plating against the sea surface when the ship pitches heavily in head or bow seas.

*Causes:* High forward speed in head seas, insufficient bow flare, light load condition (reduced draft), resonance of pitch and wave encounter periods.

*Effects:* Localised **denting, paint peeling, buckling, weld cracking**, and in severe cases **fatigue cracking** of bottom plating and longitudinals.

*Locations:* **0.0L to 0.25L from FP** (forward perpendicular) on the **bottom plating** and **lower bilge area**.

*Inspection criteria (IACS UR Z7 / Z10):*
- Visual + UT gauging for thickness loss.
- Close-up survey of suspect areas.
- Renewal if permanent deformation exceeds **5% of plate breadth** or as-built thickness reduced below renewal limit.
- Buckled plates to be **cropped and renewed**, not hammered back.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing wear vs. damage:** Wear = *gradual, thickness loss* (corrosion, erosion). Damage = *sudden, mechanical* (impact, buckling). Examiners deduct 1 mark if not distinguished.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** "Corrosion rate is 0.3" is *incomplete* — must be **0.3 mm/year**.
> 3. **Skipping the IACS reference:** Always quote the relevant IACS Unified Requirement (UR Z7, Z10, S21 etc.) when discussing renewal criteria.
> 4. **Treating linear corrosion rate as exact:** In reality, corrosion often *accelerates* with coating degradation. Linear extrapolation is a *conservative lower bound*. Examiners reward students who mention this caveat.
> 5. **Ignoring prevention:** A *complete* KTU answer mentions *both* the cause and the *preventive* measure. Always close with a one-line "prevention" remark.
> 6. **Missing the diagram:** Part B questions (especially 7-mark sub-parts) carry an implicit **2-3 marks for diagrams/sketch**. A simple labelled sketch dramatically improves your score.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Six families of causes:** Corrosion, Mechanical, Impact, Fatigue/Buckling, Biological, Environmental/Material. *Memorise this master classification first.*
- **Galvanic corrosion** = dissimilar metals + electrolyte. Governed by **galvanic series**. Prevention: sacrificial anodes (Zn, Al, Mg), ICCP, insulating joints.
- **Pitting corrosion** = local deep cavities; *more dangerous* than uniform because of perforation risk.
- **Cavitation erosion** = vapour bubble collapse on propeller/rudder. Cavitation number $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v)/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$ indicates onset.
- **Slamming** = forward bottom impact in head seas. Affects 0–0.25L from FP.
- **Fatigue** = cyclic stress, governs hatch corners, bilge keel ends, rudder horns.
- **MIC** = bacterial action in stagnant/seawater-filled spaces. Prevention: cleaning + biocides.
- **Faraday's Law** $m = (M \cdot I \cdot t)/(n \cdot F)$ — used for anode life calculation.
- **Wastage % formula** $W\% = [(T_{orig} - T_{meas})/T_{orig}] \times 100$ — applied to renewal decision.
- **IACS renewal limit** = **75% wastage** in any 3 m × 3 m local area (substantial corrosion criterion).
- **Wastage hotspots:** Boot-top, double bottom, ballast tank top, propeller blades, rudder, bow bottom, hatch corners.
- **Coating breakdown** is often the *initiating event* for almost all corrosion types — a key KTU-favourite link.
- **Always state units** (mm/year, %, V, etc.) and **reference IACS UR** in exam answers.
- **Cavitation erosion produces honeycomb pattern; pitting produces discrete deep craters** — examiners love this distinction.
- **Bulk carriers** are most prone to hatch-corner fatigue; **tankers** to corrosion (cargo + ballast); **container ships** to hull girder fatigue and slamming.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "C-M-I-F-B-E":**
> **C**orrosion, **M**echanical wear, **I**mpact damage, **F**atigue & Buckling, **B**iological attack, **E**nvironmental/Material causes. *Six letters, six families, full syllabus coverage in 5 seconds.*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison Between Different Types of Repair Activities (Afloat, Berthed etc.)
Testing for Water Tightness and Hull Continuity
Repair of Hull and Other Parts While Afloat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Repair of Hull and Other Parts While Afloat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Afloat Repair (Definition as per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Afloat repair refers to the execution of structural, mechanical, or auxiliary rectification works on the hull, appendages, and underwater fittings of a vessel **without the benefit of dry-docking**, i.e., while the ship remains floating in water, either at a berth, anchorage, or at sea.*

In the context of the **Hull Inspection** course, afloat repair forms the third and most operationally critical response level in the maintenance hierarchy:

$$\text{Inspection} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Defect Diagnosis} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Repair Decision} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Afloat Repair}$$

The governing principle is **continuity of service**. When defects are detected during routine in-service inspection (typically under Class survey intervals), the surveyor must decide whether the defect can be safely:

1. Allowed to remain operational until the next scheduled dry-dock (rare for major findings).
2. Rectified afloat using diver-assisted or shore-supported methods (subject of this module).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Bedside Surgery" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a human patient with a leaking heart valve. Ideally, the surgeon opens the chest cavity (this is the **dry-dock**). But if the patient is too unstable to be moved to the operating theatre, the cardiologist performs a catheter-based repair through the groin artery while the patient remains awake in bed. This bedside, minimally-invasive procedure is **afloat repair**.

| Element in Surgery | Equivalent in Ship Repair |
|---|---|
| Patient (Human Body) | Hull of the Vessel |
| Operating Theatre | Dry Dock |
| Catheter / Endoscope | Diver, ROV, Cofferdam |
| Bedside Surgery | **Afloat Repair** |
| Surgical Suture | Soft Patch, Cement Box, Weld |
| Recovery Period | Sea Trial, Dock Trial |

## 1.3 Necessity of Afloat Repair

A vessel may be forced into an afloat repair scenario for the following reasons:

- **Unavailability of dry-dock slots** in the region (commercial reality; can cost **USD 50,000 to 200,000 per day** for a Capesize bulker).
- **Operational urgency**: charter commitments, naval deployment, wartime readiness.
- **Minor defects** that do not warrant the cost of a full docking (e.g., a small shell plate patch above the waterline).
- **Emergency damage** at sea (collision, grounding) before reaching a port of refuge.
- **Salvage situations** where refloating is required without the option of immediate dry-docking.

## 1.4 Classification of Afloat Repairs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Tier Repair Classification:**
> 1. **Emergency / Temporary Afloat Repair** — Done to make the vessel seaworthy enough to reach a port. Examples: collision mat, wooden plug, underwater epoxy.
> 2. **Voyage Repair** — Temporary repair sufficient for one round voyage to a repair yard.
> 3. **Permanent Afloat Repair** — A Class-acceptable repair intended to remain effective until the next scheduled docking. Examples: insert plate renewal above waterline using shoreside welding crew.

## 1.5 Typical Defects Addressed Afloat

- **Hull plating** wastage, pitting corrosion, fractures (above and below waterline).
- **Appendage damage**: rudder, propeller, A-bracket, rope guard.
- **Sea connections**: sea chest grating, overboard discharge valves.
- **Stern tube / tail shaft** bearing wear surveys.
- **Bow thruster tunnel** cracks.
- **Shell doors and side ports** (ferries / offshore vessels).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Afloat Repair Methods

The decision tree for choosing an afloat repair method depends on four engineering factors:

1. **Location of defect** (above WL / below WL / load line zone).
2. **Severity of defect** (wastage percentage vs. renewal thickness).
3. **Class society tolerance** (IACS UR Z-series implications).
4. **Availability of resources** (divers, welding crew, cofferdam, graving dock).

$$\text{Repair Method Selection} = f(\text{Location, Severity, Class Rules, Resources})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Rule:**
> Any repair that reduces the **scantlings below the rule minimum** must be compensated by insert plating, not by simple doubler plates. The renewal thickness $t_{new}$ must satisfy:
> $$t_{new} \;\geq\; t_{rule} + t_{corrosion\_allowance}$$

## 2.2 Standard Afloat Repair Techniques — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Wooden Plug Repair (Temporary)

- Used for small hole leaks in shell plating (typically < 300 mm diameter).
- A tapered hardwood (teak or oak) cone is driven into the hole from **outside** by a diver.
- Backing by a wooden frame or canvas bag filled with oakum from inside.
- **Pressure rating**: up to approximately **0.3 MPa (≈ 3 bar / 30 m water head)**.
- Temporary; Class will mark as "Temporary Repair — Permanent repair required at next docking."

### 2.2.2 Cement Box Repair

- A wooden or steel box is built around the defective area (inside the vessel) and packed with quick-setting cement.
- Used for **small fractures, pitted plates, wasted seam butts** in **freshwater or non-corrosive compartments** (e.g., forepeak tanks with regular fresh water flushing).
- **Setting time**: 24 to 48 hours depending on cement grade.
- **Cement-to-water ratio**: typically **0.4 to 0.5 by mass**.
- Compression strength: **≥ 30 MPa** after 28 days.

### 2.2.3 Soft Patch (Welding Doubler Plate Above Waterline)

- A steel doubler plate welded directly over the defective area.
- Only permissible **above the load waterline** in non-critical areas.
- Class society restrictions apply — IACS UR Z17.1 generally **prohibits** soft patches on:
  - Strength decks
  - Sheer strakes
  - Bottom plating within amidships 0.4 L
- **Doubler thickness**: $t_{doubler} \approx 0.75 \times t_{original}$.

### 2.2.4 Insert Plate Renewal (Permanent Afloat Repair)

- The defective plate is **cut out** and a new plate is **inserted** in its place, welded into position.
- This is the only Class-acceptable permanent repair afloat (above WL only).
- Requires:
  - **Hot work permit** and gas-free environment.
  - Shore-based or yard-based welding crew.
  - Temporary shores / strong-backs to maintain hull stiffness.

### 2.2.5 Collision Mat (Emergency)

- A heavy tarred canvas or rubber mat lowered over the side and weighted to cover the damaged area.
- Used **only as an emergency measure** to reduce flooding rate.
- Cannot be considered a permanent or even voyage repair.
- **Limitation**: Only effective at low speeds; vulnerable to wave action and current.

### 2.2.6 Cofferdam Repair

- A watertight temporary enclosure built around the damaged area and pumped dry.
- Allows conventional welding / riveting work below the waterline.
- **Most expensive** afloat repair method (cost comparable to a short dry-dock).
- Used for **rudder trunk leaks, stern tube seal failures, bow thruster seal damage**.

### 2.2.7 Underwater Welding (Hyperbaric)

- Performed by commercial divers in a habitat at ambient pressure.
- Two main processes:
  - **Wet welding** — direct in water; limited quality, used only for emergency.
  - **Dry hyperbaric welding** — in a chamber welded to the hull; Class acceptable for some repairs.
- **Typical depth limit**: 50 m for wet welding; 300+ m for dry.

## 2.3 Repair of Specific Appendages While Afloat

### 2.3.1 Propeller Repair

- Minor blade tip cracks: ground out by diver using pneumatic tools.
- Major blade loss: **emergency drophammer loosening** of remaining blades (not afloat) — usually requires dry-dock.
- Propeller pitch deviation: corrected only after shaft withdrawal.

### 2.3.2 Rudder Repair

- Pintle bearing clearance check via feeler gauge by diver.
- Cracks in rudder plating above waterline: insert plate renewal.
- Rudder stock corrosion: ultrasonic thickness measurement afloat, full repair only in dry-dock.

### 2.3.3 Sea Chest / Overboard Valves

- Grating cleaning: diver task.
- Plugging overboard discharge: wooden cone + cement box from inside.
- Valve seat grinding: only afloat if a Class-approved in-situ lapping tool is available.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the exam-ready reference for this topic. Memorize the formulas and boundary conditions.**

| # | Concept / Formula | Symbol / Expression | Boundary / Limit | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Renewal Thickness Rule | $t_{new} \geq t_{rule} + t_{CA}$ | $t_{CA} \approx 1\text{–}3$ mm | mm |
| 2 | Corrosion Allowance Factor | $f_c = \dfrac{t_{as-built} - t_{measured}}{t_{as-built}}$ | $f_c > 25\% \Rightarrow$ plating renewal | % |
| 3 | Plug Pressure Resistance | $P_{plug} \approx \sigma_{wood} \cdot \dfrac{d^2}{d_{hole}}$ | $\sigma_{teak} \approx 80$ MPa | Pa |
| 4 | Doubler Thickness | $t_{doubler} \approx 0.75 \cdot t_{orig}$ | Not on strength deck | mm |
| 5 | Cement Box Setting Time | $t_{set} = \dfrac{24 \text{ to } 48}{T_{ambient}}$ | $T_{ambient} \geq 5^{\circ}$C | hours |
| 6 | Cofferdam Head (m of water) | $h_{cd} \leq \dfrac{P_{pump}}{\rho_w \cdot g}$ | $P_{pump} \leq 1$ bar typical | m |
| 7 | Hyperbaric Depth-Pressure | $P_{depth} = P_{atm} + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot d$ | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa | Pa |
| 8 | Wooden Plug Hole Limit | $d_{hole} \leq 300$ mm | Temporary only | mm |
| 9 | Scantling Reduction Limit | $\dfrac{t_{measured}}{t_{rule}} \geq 75\%$ | Else immediate repair | % |
| 10 | Soft Patch Prohibited Zone | $0.4L$ amidships bottom | IACS UR Z17.1 | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The decision to repair afloat vs. dry-dock has direct **commercial and safety implications**:

- A Capesize bulk carrier losing **USD 30,000–50,000/day** in charter hire.
- A naval warship must be battle-ready at all times — *afloat repair capability is a force multiplier*.
- Offshore platforms and FPSOs (Floating Production Storage Offloading) often cannot be docked for 15–20 years of operation — **afloat inspection and repair is the only feasible maintenance path**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The Hull Inspector must balance **Class compliance**, **operational continuity**, and **safety of life**. A correctly certified afloat repair is just as legally valid as a dry-dock repair, provided it is documented and surveyed by an attending surveyor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Wasmentage Percentage

The Hull Surveyor's first task upon finding reduced plate thickness is to calculate the **percentage wastage** relative to the as-built thickness. This determines whether the plate may remain in service, requires local renewal, or requires complete renewal.

**Step 1 — Define as-built thickness.**

$$t_{as\text{-}built} = t_0$$

**Step 2 — Measure the current thickness at the most-corroded point** (using UT gauge, average of 9-point grid reading).

$$t_{measured} = t_m$$

**Step 3 — Compute absolute wastage.**

$$\Delta t = t_0 - t_m$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage wastage.**

$$f_c = \dfrac{t_0 - t_m}{t_0} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Apply the KTU decision rule** (based on IACS UR Z10.1 harmonized rules).

$$\boxed{\;\begin{aligned}
f_c \leq 25\% &\Rightarrow \text{Acceptable, no renewal} \\
25\% < f_c \leq 75\% &\Rightarrow \text{Conditionally acceptable, increased scrutiny} \\
f_c > 75\% &\Rightarrow \text{Renewal mandatory} \\
f_c > 90\% &\Rightarrow \text{Critical; emergency repair}
\end{aligned}\;}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A bottom plate has an as-built thickness of $t_0 = 22$ mm. UT survey gives $t_m = 17$ mm.

$$\Delta t = 22 - 17 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

$$f_c = \dfrac{5}{22} \times 100\% = 22.7\%$$

Since $f_c \leq 25\%$, the plate may remain in service until the next scheduled docking, but should be recorded as a **noteworthy finding** in the survey report.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wooden Plug Pressure Resistance

For a temporary wooden plug inserted into a circular hole of diameter $d_{hole}$ in the shell plating, the resisting force comes from the **lateral expansion** of the tapered plug against the hole wall.

**Step 1 — Define the friction-resisting force.**

$$F_{resist} = \mu \cdot N$$

where $\mu$ is the friction coefficient between wood and steel ($\mu \approx 0.4$ for wet teak on rusted steel) and $N$ is the normal force.

**Step 2 — Express the normal force from the taper geometry.**

If the cone has half-angle $\alpha$, then driving force $F_{drive}$ produces:

$$N = \dfrac{F_{drive}}{\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha} \cdot \cos\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Balance with hydrostatic pressure.**

The hydrostatic force pushing the plug outward is:

$$F_{water} = P_{water} \cdot A_{hole} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_{hole}^2}{4}$$

where $d$ is the depth of the hole below the waterline.

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum depth at which the plug holds.**

Setting $F_{resist} = F_{water}$ and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;d_{max} = \dfrac{4 \mu \sigma_{wood} \cos\alpha}{\pi \rho_w g d_{hole} (\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)}\;}$$

**Numerical Evaluation:**

Given $d_{hole} = 0.2$ m, $\mu = 0.4$, $\sigma_{wood} = 80 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\alpha = 5^{\circ}$, $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m$^3$, $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$:

$$d_{max} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.4 \times 80 \times 10^6 \times \cos 5^{\circ}}{\pi \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \times (\sin 5^{\circ} + 0.4 \cos 5^{\circ})}$$

Computing numerator:

$$\text{Num} = 4 \times 0.4 \times 80 \times 10^6 \times 0.9962 \approx 1.275 \times 10^8$$

Computing denominator:

$$\text{Den} = \pi \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \times (0.0872 + 0.4 \times 0.9962)$$
$$\text{Den} = \pi \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \times (0.0872 + 0.3985)$$
$$\text{Den} = \pi \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \times 0.4857$$
$$\text{Den} \approx 3067 \text{ N/m}$$

Final:

$$d_{max} \approx \dfrac{1.275 \times 10^8}{3067} \approx 41{,}572 \text{ m}$$

This absurdly high value indicates that the dominant failure mode is not friction but **plug pull-out due to water-jet erosion** and **plug corrosion**. The empirical **300 mm hole limit** is therefore set by *Class experience*, not by this idealized formula.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cement Box Compressive Strength Check

For a cement box installed to seal a defect, the load is the hydrostatic head from the outside.

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic load per unit area on the box wall.**

$$P = \rho_w g d$$

**Step 2 — Required compressive strength of cured cement.**

The cement must withstand $P$ without crushing:

$$\sigma_{cement} \geq P = \rho_w g d$$

**Step 3 — Convert to minimum cement grade.**

Standard cement grades and their 28-day compressive strengths:

| Cement Grade | 28-Day Strength (MPa) |
|---|---|
| Ordinary Portland (OPC 33) | 33 |
| OPC 43 | 43 |
| OPC 53 | 53 |
| High-Alumina | $\geq 40$ |

**Step 4 — Maximum water depth per grade.**

For OPC 33 grade:

$$d_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{cement}}{\rho_w g} = \dfrac{33 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81} \approx 3{,}283 \text{ m}$$

Again, the formula gives an unrealistically large depth. The actual limit is set by **bond strength between cement and steel plate**, typically **0.5 to 1.5 MPa**, giving:

$$d_{max, practical} = \dfrac{\sigma_{bond}}{\rho_w g} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81} \approx 99 \text{ m}$$

This is why cement boxes are permitted in **deep tanks and ballast compartments** of most vessels without depth restriction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Repair Method Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for KTU Examination Use:**

| Defect Location | Defect Severity | Recommended Repair | Class Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shell plate, above WL | Pitting, wastage ≤ 25% | Soft patch / doubler | Temporary acceptable |
| Shell plate, above WL | Crack / fracture | Insert plate renewal | Permanent (Class approved) |
| Shell plate, below WL | Small hole (< 300 mm) | Wooden plug + cement box | Voyage repair only |
| Shell plate, below WL | Large fracture | Cofferdam + insert plate | Permanent (expensive) |
| Strength deck | Any defect | No soft patch; insert renewal mandatory | Permanent |
| Sea chest | Grating missing | Diver replacement | Permanent |
| Rudder | Pintle clearance > 2 mm | Diver measurement; repair in dock | Survey only |
| Propeller | Blade tip crack | Diver grinding | Temporary |
| Bow thruster tunnel | Crack in plating | Cofferdam | Permanent |
| Stern tube seal | Leakage | Cofferdam + seal replacement | Permanent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Afloat Repair Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Hull Defect Detected] --> node2{Defect Below WL?}
    node2 -- Yes --> node3{Severity Critical?}
    node2 -- No --> node4{Above WL Zone}
    node3 -- Yes --> node5[Emergency Measures]
    node5 --> node6[Collision Mat]
    node5 --> node7[Wooden Plug]
    node5 --> node8[Cement Box]
    node3 -- No --> node9[Voyage Repair]
    node9 --> node10[Diver Cleaning]
    node9 --> node11[UT Thickness Survey]
    node4 --> node12{Zone Critical?}
    node12 -- Yes --> node13[Insert Plate Renewal]
    node12 -- No --> node14{Class Allows Soft Patch?}
    node14 -- Yes --> node15[Soft Patch Doubler]
    node14 -- No --> node13
    node13 --> node16[Hot Work Permit]
    node13 --> node17[Shores Strong Backs]
    node15 --> node18[Temporary Acceptance]
    node15 --> node19[Schedule Permanent Repair]
    node6 --> node20[Reach Port of Refuge]
    node7 --> node20
    node8 --> node20
    node20 --> node21[Class Surveyor Inspection]
    node21 --> node22[Permanent Repair Decision]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of an Afloat Repair Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Shoreside Coordinator] --> nodeB[Attending Class Surveyor]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Repair Yard Project Manager]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Diving Contractor]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Welding Crew]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Scope Approval]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Method Statement Review]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Hot Work Required?}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[Gas Free Certificate]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ[Direct Execution]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Fire Watch Posted]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Repair Execution]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeL
    nodeE --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Visual Inspection]
    nodeM --> nodeN[NDT UT MPI as Required]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Class Surveyor Final Approval]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Repair Documentation]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Ship Resume Operations]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Section View of a Cofferdam Repair

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX1[Sea Water Outside Hull] --> nodeX2[Damaged Shell Plate]
    nodeX2 --> nodeX3[Cofferdam Steel Shell Welded to Hull]
    nodeX3 --> nodeX4[Reinforcement Stiffeners]
    nodeX3 --> nodeX5[Drain Pump Suction]
    nodeX3 --> nodeX6[Welder Access Hatch]
    nodeX6 --> nodeX7[Dry Working Chamber]
    nodeX7 --> nodeX8[New Insert Plate]
    nodeX7 --> nodeX9[Welder Position]
    nodeX9 --> nodeX10[Cut Out Old Defective Plate]
    nodeX10 --> nodeX11[Fit New Plate]
    nodeX11 --> nodeX12[Welding All Around]
    nodeX12 --> nodeX13[NDT Inspection]
    nodeX13 --> nodeX14[Remove Cofferdam]
    nodeX14 --> nodeX15[Grind Flush and Paint]
```

```
         │                     │
```
