<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define afloat repair. State any three situations in which afloat repair becomes necessary for a vessel.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Afloat Repair** is the execution of structural, mechanical, or auxiliary rectification works on a vessel's hull and underwater fittings **without dry-docking**, i.e., while the ship remains floating.

Three situations where afloat repair becomes necessary:

1. **Unavailability of dry-dock slots** in the region, leading to unacceptable commercial loss of **USD 50,000+ per day**.
2. **Emergency damage at sea** (collision, grounding) where the vessel must be made seaworthy to reach a port of refuge.
3. **Operational urgency** — naval deployment, charter commitments, or continuous production requirement for FPSOs.
4. *(Alternate)* **Minor defects** that do not warrant the cost of a full docking.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "voyage repair" with "permanent afloat repair." Voyage repair is temporary and valid only until the next port of call; permanent afloat repair is Class-approved and remains in service until next scheduled docking.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any four methods of temporary afloat repair of shell plating with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Method | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wooden Plug** | Tapered hardwood cone driven into the hole by a diver from outside. |
| 2 | **Cement Box** | Wooden box packed with quick-setting cement, built inside the vessel over the defect. |
| 3 | **Soft Patch** | Steel doubler plate welded over the defect above the waterline only. |
| 4 | **Collision Mat** | Heavy tarred canvas or rubber mat lowered over the side and weighted down. |
| 5 | *(Bonus)* **Cofferdam** | Watertight enclosure built around the defect and pumped dry for welding. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe in detail the procedure of carrying out a **cement box repair** for a corroded bottom plate inside a double-bottom tank. State the limitations of this method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure for **underwater (hyperbaric) welding** as a permanent afloat repair method. State the depth limitations and Class society acceptability. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Cement Box Repair

**Step 1 — Pre-Repair Survey:**

- Drain the double-bottom tank, gas-free the space, and obtain entry permit.
- Conduct **UT thickness survey** at 200 mm grid points to map the corroded area.
- Mark the renewal boundary on the plate using chalk.

**Step 2 — Surface Preparation:**

- Wire-brush / grit-blast the corroded surface to **Sa 2.5 (ISO 8501-1)** standard.
- Wipe clean with acetone to remove oil and grease.

**Step 3 — Build the Cement Box:**

- Construct a wooden or steel frame around the defective area extending **at least 50 mm beyond** the corroded boundary on all sides.
- The frame height should be **at least 100 mm** above the plate surface.

**Step 4 — Mix and Pour Cement:**

- Use **OPC 43 grade cement** with water-cement ratio of **0.45**.
- Add bonding agent (e.g., epoxy emulsion) at 5% by mass of cement for improved adhesion.
- Pour into the box and compact with a vibrator to remove air pockets.

**Step 5 — Curing:**

- Allow **at least 48 hours** of curing before re-flooding the tank.
- Maintain humidity by covering with wet hessian cloth.

**Step 6 — Post-Repair Survey:**

- Visual inspection of the cement surface for cracks.
- Hammer-tap test for hollow sounds indicating poor bond.
- Class surveyor attends and endorses the repair in the repair log.

**Limitations of Cement Box Repair:**

1. Only suitable for **non-structural** or **secondary boundary** defects.
2. Cannot be used in compartments carrying **oil, chemicals, or potable water** (contamination risk).
3. **Not Class acceptable** as a permanent repair for primary strength members.
4. Bond strength degrades over time in **vibration-prone** areas.
5. Setting time is critical — low temperatures $\;<\;5^{\circ}$C delay curing.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Underwater Hyperbaric Welding

**Step 1 — Habitat Docking:**

- A prefabricated **welding habitat** (steel chamber) is lowered over the defect site by a crane barge.
- The habitat is sealed against the hull using inflatable seals.

**Step 2 — Water Displacement:**

- Compressed air is pumped into the habitat, displacing water until the working chamber is dry.
- Internal pressure is maintained at **ambient hydrostatic pressure** of the depth (otherwise habitat collapses).

**Step 3 — Pre-Weld Preparation:**

- Surface grinding by diver inside the habitat.
- Preheating using resistance heating pads.

**Step 4 — Welding Process:**

- Use **shielded metal arc welding (SMAW)** with **cellulosic or basic low-hydrogen electrodes**.
- Voltage: 22–28 V, Current: 100–180 A depending on plate thickness.
- Welder qualification: must hold **diver-welder certification** per AWS D3.6M.

**Step 5 — Post-Weld NDT:**

- **Magnetic particle inspection (MPI)** is performed before habitat removal.
- **Ultrasonic testing** from inside the vessel to confirm full penetration.

**Step 6 — Documentation:**

- Class surveyor issues a "Welding Repair Afloat" endorsement.

**Depth Limitations:**

- **Wet welding**: up to **50 m** practical depth, with rapid cooling causing **hardness > 350 HV** (cracking risk).
- **Dry hyperbaric**: up to **300+ m** with proper pressure chambers.

**Class Society Acceptability:**

- **IACS UR Z17.1** generally permits hyperbaric welding **only for non-critical repairs** below waterline and subject to special survey.
- **ABS, DNV, LR** have specific notation for "Underwater Repair Certified" vessels.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the procedure for **insert plate renewal** as a permanent afloat repair. Explain when this method is preferred over soft patching. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a labelled diagram, describe the **cofferdam repair** method for a shell plate fracture below the waterline. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Insert Plate Renewal Afloat

**Step 1 — Defect Mapping:**

- Mark the boundaries of the defective plate area, extending to **sound metal at least 50 mm** beyond the crack tip or corroded zone.

**Step 2 — Hot Work Preparation:**

- Obtain **hot work permit** with gas-free certificate.
- Set up **fire watch** and fire-fighting equipment.
- Provide adequate ventilation inside the compartment.

**Step 3 — Plate Cutting:**

- Cut out the defective plate using **oxy-fuel cutting** or **plasma arc**.
- Leave a **4–5 mm gap** on each side for welding access.

**Step 4 — Edge Preparation:**

- Grind the cut edges to a **double-V or single-V bevel** with a root gap of **2–3 mm** and included angle of **60° ± 5°**.

**Step 5 — New Plate Fitting:**

- Cut the new plate to size with **shrinkage allowance of 1.5–2 mm per metre**.
- Fit into position using dogs and wedges.
- Tack weld at 200 mm spacing.

**Step 6 — Welding:**

- Weld using **approved WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)**.
- Sequence: **vertical-up progression, alternating sides** to minimize distortion.

**Step 7 — NDT:**

- **100% MPI or DPI** of butt welds.
- **UT** for thicker plates (> 20 mm).

**Step 8 — Class Survey:**

- Surveyor attends, witnesses NDT, and endorses the repair.

**Why Preferred Over Soft Patching:**

| Criterion | Insert Plate | Soft Patch |
|---|---|---|
| Permanent classification | Yes | No (temporary) |
| Used on strength deck | Yes | **Not allowed** |
| Restores original strength | Yes | Reduces net section efficiency |
| Class acceptability | Full | Conditional |
| Cost and time | Higher | Lower |

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Cofferdam Repair

**Step 1 — Design the Cofferdam:**

- Steel plate and stiffener construction.
- Sized to encompass the defect with **300 mm clearance** on all sides.
- Designed to withstand **full hydrostatic pressure** at the repair depth.

**Step 2 — Welding to Hull:**

- The cofferdam base is welded to the **sound shell plating** around the defect using a **fillet weld**.
- Weld tested by **dye-penetrant inspection** before flooding.

**Step 3 — Draining:**

- A pump is connected to a drain port; cofferdam is pumped dry.

**Step 4 — Internal Repair:**

- Working in dry conditions, the defect is cut out and a new insert plate is fitted and welded.

**Step 5 — NDT and Testing:**

- MPI / UT of the new weld.
- **Hydrostatic leak test** by re-flooding the cofferdam and checking for seepage.

**Step 6 — Removal:**

- Cofferdam is cut free, residual weld ground flush, and the shell plate is repainted.

**Advantages:**

1. Allows **permanent quality** repairs below the waterline.
2. Welding is done in **dry conditions** — full Class acceptability.
3. No dry-dock needed.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Very expensive** — often costs more than a short docking.
2. Requires **specialized diving and welding contractors**.
3. **Weather sensitive** — sea state limits crane operations.
4. Risk of **cofferdam weld failure** leading to catastrophic flooding of the compartment.
5. Time-consuming: **2–6 weeks** for installation and removal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing soft patch with insert plate.** Soft patch = doubler over existing plate (temporary). Insert plate = cut-out and renewal (permanent).
> 2. **Forgetting Class society rules.** Many students describe what *can* be done without stating what *Class allows*. Always reference **IACS UR Z-series**.
> 3. **Skipping the surveyor's role.** Every repair method must end with surveyor attendance, NDT, and documentation. Marks are specifically awarded for this step.
> 4. **Ignoring safety.** Hot work permits, gas-free certificates, fire watch — these are mandatory in any afloat repair answer for full marks.
> 5. **No sketch in Part B (a) or (b).** A labelled diagram is worth **2–3 marks by itself** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Repair of Hull and Other Parts While Afloat**

- **Definition:** Afloat repair = rectification work without dry-docking.
- **Three Tiers:** Emergency → Voyage → Permanent.
- **Wooden Plug:** Hole ≤ 300 mm, temporary, diver-driven from outside.
- **Cement Box:** Built inside the hull over the defect; OPC 43 cement; 48 h cure.
- **Soft Patch:** Doubler plate welded above WL only; **NOT allowed on strength deck or sheer strake**.
- **Insert Plate Renewal:** Cut out + new plate welded in; **only permanent afloat method above WL** (Class accepted).
- **Collision Mat:** Emergency only; canvas/rubber weighted over the side.
- **Cofferdam:** Watertight shell + dry welding below WL; most expensive; permanent quality.
- **Hyperbaric Welding:** Wet up to 50 m; dry habitat up to 300+ m; AWS D3.6M qualified.
- **Appendages:** Rudder pintle ≥ 2 mm clearance requires dock survey; propeller major damage always in dock.
- **Decision Rule:** $t_{measured}/t_{rule} \geq 0.75$ for continued service.
- **Class Reference:** IACS UR Z10.1 (Hull Surveys), Z17.1 (Repair).
- **Key Formulae:**
  - Wastage %: $f_c = (t_0 - t_m)/t_0 \times 100\%$
  - Cement bond limit depth: $d_{max} = \sigma_{bond}/(\rho_w g)$
  - Plug hole size limit: $d_{hole} \leq 300$ mm.
- **Surveyor's Triad:** (1) Method statement review → (2) NDT witnessing → (3) Documentation.
- **Cost Reality:** Afloat cofferdam ≈ **80–120%** of equivalent docking cost; only justified when docking slot unavailable.
- **Safety:** Hot work permit + gas-free certificate + fire watch + entry permit are mandatory for every permanent repair.
- **Documentation:** Repair entry in **Ship's Repair Logbook** + **Class survey report** + **drawing amendment** if plate renewal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Underwater Welding - Welding Equipment, Quality Control and Standards
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Underwater Welding - Welding Equipment, Quality Control and Standards

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underwater Welding** is defined by **AWS D3.6M:2017 (Underwater Welding Code)** as a welding process where at least part of the weldment is performed while the welder or the welding operation is submerged in a liquid environment, typically seawater or fresh water, at ambient hydrostatic pressure.

In the context of **Hull Inspection**, underwater welding is critical for in-service repair of ship hulls, offshore platforms, subsea pipelines, and underwater structural components. The 2024 KTU syllabus categorizes the operational domain into three principal techniques:

1. **Wet Welding** – Performed directly in the aquatic environment without exclusion of water.
2. **Dry Welding (Habitat Welding)** – Conducted inside a pressurized chamber that excludes water from the weld zone.
3. **Hyperbaric Welding** – Performed at elevated ambient pressure, usually inside a dry habitat pressurised to balance local hydrostatic pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hull Inspection Relevance:** Underwater welding is the **primary in-situ remedial action** after a hull survey reveals cracks, severe wastage (corrosion), or plate perforations detected during in-water survey (IWS) or dry-dock inspection.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

**Think of underwater welding like performing surgery in a flooded operating room:**
- A surgeon underwater (wet welding) must work quickly with limited visibility and unstable conditions.
- A surgeon inside a sealed, pressurised bubble (dry/habitat welding) has near-surface conditions but must manage complex equipment.

In both cases, the **quality of the weld is your "patient's recovery"** — you cannot afford contamination, porosity, or lack of fusion because the structure will continue to bear cyclic loads (slamming, wave-induced bending, hull girder shear).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

The following standards-bound metrics govern underwater welding performance evaluation:

| Parameter | Standard Reference Value |
|---|---|
| **Minimum Hydrostatic Pressure Gradient** | **0.101 MPa per 10 m depth** (seawater) |
| **Maximum Allowable Wet Weld Depth (Class A)** | **≈ 100 m** (AWS D3.6M limit) |
| **Cooling Rate (Wet, FCAW)** | **> 100 °C/s** near fusion line |
| **Operator Qualification Re-test Interval** | **6 months** (AWS D3.6M) |
| **Visual Acceptance – Undercut Limit** | **0.8 mm max** (Class A welds) |
| **Porosity Acceptance (Radiographic)** | **2% max area** (Class A) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Underwater Welding Process

The operation can be decomposed into a **6-stage functional chain**:

1. **Environmental Isolation Decision** – Choose wet / habitat / cofferdam based on water depth, accessibility, and class requirement.
2. **Surface Preparation** – Hydroblasting, grit-blasting, mechanical cleaning to remove marine growth, rust, and salt deposits. **Acceptable chloride threshold** typically $< 50 \text{ mg/m}^2$.
3. **Power Source Delivery** – Transmission of welding current from the surface through watertight cables.
4. **Arc Ignition & Stability Maintenance** – Specialised electrodes (e.g., **rutile-cellulosic**, **basic low-hydrogen**) compensate for rapid cooling.
5. **Solidification & Microstructure Formation** – Rapid quenching produces predominantly **acicular ferrite + martensite** mixtures.
6. **Post-Weld Quality Verification** – Visual + NDT (MT, PT, UT, RT as applicable).

## 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Is Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| Isolation choice | Wet welds have ~30–40% lower tensile strength vs. surface welds | Habitat or one-atmosphere chambers |
| Surface prep | Salt + moisture = hydrogen-induced cracking | Hydro-jetting + immediate welding |
| Power source | DC required for stability; AC unsafe underwater | Surface DC rectifiers (constant voltage) |
| Electrode coating | Dissociates water vapour; produces CO shield gas | Cellulosic / rutile coatings |
| Cooling control | High cooling rate → martensite → brittleness | Heat input tuning via travel speed |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulae below are syllabus-aligned and examinable in KTU 2024 ESE.

### **Table 2.1 — Underwater Welding Governing Equations**

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_h = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure ($\rho$ = density of seawater $\approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $h$ = depth in m) |
| 2 | $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | Heat input (J/mm), where $\eta$ = arc efficiency, $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $v$ = travel speed |
| 3 | $\sigma_t^{\text{wet}} \approx 0.85 \sigma_t^{\text{dry}}$ | Empirical tensile strength reduction factor (AWS D3.6M derived) |
| 4 | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{HI}{2\pi \lambda}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_0 - T_8} - \dfrac{1}{T_0 - T_5}\right)^{-1}$ | Cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C — critical for HAZ toughness |
| 5 | $R_a = \dfrac{V}{60 \cdot S}$ | Arc travel feed rate (mm/s) where $V$ = feed and $S$ = stickout |
| 6 | $CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent (IIW formula) — governs weldability and crack susceptibility |
| 7 | $P_{allow} = \dfrac{P_m \cdot A_{\text{joint}}}{N_{\text{design}}}$ | Allowable joint pressure in habitat chamber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooling Time ($t_{8/5}$) is the single most important KTU examinable concept** — it determines the HAZ microstructure. Wet welds typically show $t_{8/5} < 5$ seconds, producing brittle martensite.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure Acting on a Habitat Chamber

A hyperbaric welding habitat is lowered to a depth $h$ below the waterline. The internal pressure must balance the external hydrostatic head to prevent structural collapse of the chamber.

**Given Data:**
- Depth $h = 25 \text{ m}$
- Seawater density $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 0.1013 \text{ MPa}$

**Derivation Steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define hydrostatic pressure at depth } h \\
P_h &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h \\
P_h &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 25 \quad \text{(substituting values)} \\
P_h &= 251{,}381.25 \text{ Pa} \\
P_h &\approx 0.2514 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Add atmospheric pressure for absolute chamber pressure} \\
P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_h \\
P_{abs} &= 0.1013 + 0.2514 \\
P_{abs} &= 0.3527 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(gauge + atmospheric)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Express as bar (common in diving engineering)} \\
P_{abs} &= 0.3527 \times 10 \approx 3.53 \text{ bar} \\
\text{Gauge Pressure} &= P_{abs} - 1.013 \text{ bar} \approx 2.52 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The habitat must be pressurised to **3.53 bar absolute** to maintain structural neutrality. This corresponds to diving on a **mixed-gas (Heliox)** schedule rather than compressed air due to nitrogen narcosis limits.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Time ($t_{8/5}$) for a Wet FCAW Weld

**Given:**
- Heat input $HI = 1.2 \text{ kJ/mm} = 1200 \text{ J/mm}$
- Plate thickness $d = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Initial (preheat) temperature $T_0 = 25 \text{ °C}$
- Thermal conductivity $\lambda = 0.025 \text{ W/(mm·K)}$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{8/5} &= \dfrac{HI}{2\pi \lambda}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_0 - T_5} - \dfrac{1}{T_0 - T_8}\right) \\
\text{where } T_5 = 500 \text{ °C}, \; T_8 = 800 \text{ °C} \\
t_{8/5} &= \dfrac{1200}{2\pi \times 0.025}\left(\dfrac{1}{25 - 500} - \dfrac{1}{25 - 800}\right) \\
t_{8/5} &= \dfrac{1200}{0.1571}\left(\dfrac{1}{-475} - \dfrac{1}{-775}\right) \\
t_{8/5} &= 7639.7 \times \left(-0.002105 + 0.001290\right) \\
t_{8/5} &= 7639.7 \times (-0.000815) \quad \text{(wait, units miscalculated - sign convention)} \\
\text{Re-expressing with magnitudes:} \\
t_{8/5} &= \dfrac{1200}{2\pi \times 0.025}\left(\dfrac{1}{475} - \dfrac{1}{775}\right) \\
t_{8/5} &= 7639.7 \times (0.002105 - 0.001290) \\
t_{8/5} &= 7639.7 \times 0.000815 \\
t_{8/5} &\approx 6.23 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $t_{8/5} \approx 6.2 \text{ s}$ is **borderline-acceptable** for low-carbon steel HAZ. Wet welds typically fall below 4 s — indicating martensite formation and reduced toughness.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Welding Method Selection

| Selection Criterion | Wet Welding | Habitat (Dry) | Cofferdam |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Practical Depth** | 100 m | 300+ m | Surface / shallow |
| **Cost Index** | 1× (baseline) | 8–12× | 4–6× |
| **Weld Quality (vs. surface)** | 60–80% | 95–100% | 95–100% |
| **Setup Time** | Hours | Days | Weeks |
| **Class Applicability (IACS)** | Class B/C only | Class A accepted | Class A accepted |
| **Hull Repair Suitability** | Emergency patch | Permanent structural | Permanent structural |
| **Inspection Difficulty** | Very high | Moderate | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Underwater Welding Equipment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface Power Source - DC Constant Voltage Rectifier 400-600A] --> B[Armoured Watertight Cable Umbilical]
    B --> C[Waterproof Junction Box at Splash Zone]
    C --> D[Diver Electrode Holder with Safety Switch]
    D --> E[Waterproof Electrode - Cellulosic or Rutile Coating]
    E --> F[Workpiece - Hull Plate or Subsea Structure]
    F --> G[Return Current Path via Ground Cable]
    G --> A
    H[Diving Bell or Habitat Chamber] -.Optional.-> D
    I[Topside Welding Supervisor Console] -.Monitors.-> A
    J[NDT Inspection Unit - UT MT PT] -.Post-Weld.-> F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Quality Control Workflow (AWS D3.6M Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WP1[Welding Procedure Specification - WPS Drafted] --> WP2[Procedure Qualification Record - PQR Tested]
    WP2 --> WP3[Welder Performance Qualification - WPQ]
    WP3 --> QA1[Pre-Weld Visual Inspection]
    QA1 --> QA2[In-Process Monitoring - Voltage Current Travel Speed]
    QA2 --> QA3[Post-Weld Visual - 100% Coverage]
    QA3 --> ND1[NDT Selection Based on Class]
    ND1 --> ND2[MT - Magnetic Particle for Surface Cracks]
    ND1 --> ND3[UT - Ultrasonic for Volumetric Flaws]
    ND1 --> ND4[RT - Radiographic for Internal Defects]
    ND2 --> QA4[Acceptance per AWS D3.6M Class A B C]
    ND3 --> QA4
    ND4 --> QA4
    QA4 --> QA5[Final Documentation and Traceability]
```

## 4.3 Process Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{Is hull accessible from outside and depth < 100 m?}
    S -- Yes --> W{Wet Welding economically viable?}
    S -- No --> H{Is depth > 100 m and critical joint?}
    W -- Yes, emergency --> A[Use Wet Welding - Class B or C]
    W -- No, critical joint --> D[Deploy Habitat Chamber]
    H -- Yes --> P[Hyperbaric Chamber Welding]
    H -- No --> C[Consider Cofferdam if depth permits]
```

## 4.4 Standards Cross-Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AWS D3.6M - Underwater Welding Code] --> B[Class A - Hyperbaric Structural]
    A --> C[Class B - Wet Production Welding]
    A --> D[Class C - Wet Emergency Repair]
    E[ASME Section IX] --> B
    F[API 1104] --> P[Pipeline Welding]
    G[ISO 15614] --> B
    H[DNV GL Rules for Ships] --> R[Hull Repair Acceptance]
    I[ABS Rules - Hull Inspection] --> R
    J[IACS UR Z17 Z23] --> R
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between wet welding and dry (habitat) underwater welding in terms of weld quality, cost, and depth capability.**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines expected):**

| Parameter | Wet Welding | Dry (Habitat) Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Environment | Open water, no exclusion of water | Pressurised chamber, water excluded |
| Weld Quality | Lower (porosity, brittleness) | Comparable to surface welds |
| Cost | Low | Very high (8–12×) |
| Depth Limit | ~100 m | 300+ m |
| Cooling Rate | Very high → martensitic HAZ | Controlled → tougher HAZ |

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**State the role of the AWS D3.6M code in underwater hull repair.**

**Model Answer:**
The **AWS D3.6M** is the principal international standard governing underwater welding. It classifies welds into **Class A (structural hyperbaric), Class B (wet production), and Class C (wet emergency)**. The code mandates **WPS, PQR, WPQ documentation**, defines **NDT acceptance criteria**, specifies **welder qualification re-test intervals (6 months)**, and forms the acceptance basis for **IACS member classification societies** including ABS, DNV, and Lloyd's Register during hull repair approvals.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **AWS Class A / B / C** with **IACS class notation**. Underwater weld classes refer to **weld quality grades**, NOT ship classification society tiers. Writing "Class A means Lloyd's Class A ship" will fetch **zero marks** for that section.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] With neat sketches, explain the components of a hyperbaric underwater welding habitat system.**

**Model Solution:**

A hyperbaric habitat consists of:

1. **Habitat Chamber (Diving Bell)** — Steel pressure vessel rated to depth equivalent + 10% safety margin. Pressure rating = $P_{atm} + \rho g h$ absolute.
2. **Access Shaft / Open Bottom Design** – Open-bottom chambers match ambient pressure; closed-top require trunking.
3. **Gas Supply System** – Heliox or Hydreliox mixture (avoids nitrogen narcosis beyond 30 m).
4. **Life Support Module** – CO₂ scrubbing, O₂ monitoring, temperature control, humidity management.
5. **Welding Equipment Port** – Specially sealed gland entries for electrode and power cables.
6. **Viewing Ports / CCTV** – Acrylic viewports rated to chamber pressure.
7. **Blow-down & Emergency Vent Systems** – For fire or gas contamination emergencies.

**Pressure Calculation Example:**
At 30 m depth, $P_{abs} = 0.1013 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 30 / 10^6 = 0.403 \text{ MPa} \approx 4 \text{ bar}$.

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Describe the procedure qualification and welder qualification requirements as per AWS D3.6M.**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure Qualification (WPS/PQR):**
- A **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** must be drafted listing: process, position, base metal, electrode classification, voltage, current, travel speed, polarity, gas shielding.
- A **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)** is generated by test welding on a representative sample. Mandatory tests include tensile, bend, impact (Charpy V-notch at service temperature), and hardness survey (max **350 HV10** typically).
- For wet welding, PQR tests must be performed **at the actual depth or simulated equivalent** within **12 months** of production welding.

**Welder Performance Qualification (WPQ):**
- Welder must produce a test coupon evaluated by **visual + radiographic + bend tests**.
- Qualification valid for **6 months** in the qualified process/position.
- Re-qualification required if welder lapses >6 months or changes process.
- Qualification ranges are strictly limited (e.g., a 3G welder cannot weld 5G without supplementary test).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the non-destructive testing methods applicable to underwater welded joints with their acceptance criteria per AWS D3.6M.**

**Model Solution:**

| NDT Method | Defect Detected | Acceptance Criteria (Class A) |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual Testing (VT)** | Surface cracks, undercut, profile | Undercut ≤ 0.8 mm, crater cracks not permitted |
| **Magnetic Particle (MT)** | Surface & near-surface cracks | No linear indications > 1.5 mm |
| **Dye Penetrant (PT)** | Surface open defects | No relevant indications |
| **Ultrasonic (UT)** | Vollametric flaws, lack of fusion | Per ASME V Article 4 acceptance |
| **Radiographic (RT)** | Internal porosity, slag | Porosity ≤ 2%, no slag > 3 mm |

**Practical Limitations Underwater:**
- RT is rarely used underwater due to radiation safety logistics.
- MT and PT are most commonly applied topside after cofferdam dewatering.
- UT with specially designed probes is the **preferred in-situ volumetric method**.

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the quality control challenges in underwater wet welding and how they are mitigated.**

**Model Solution:**

**Challenges:**
1. **Rapid Cooling** – Produces brittle martensitic HAZ → mitigated by using austenitic electrodes (e.g., E309L) for buffer layers.
2. **Hydrogen Embrittlement** – Water dissociates → atomic H → cold cracking → mitigated by **basic low-hydrogen electrodes** (e.g., E7018 modified).
3. **Porosity** – Entrapped steam and gas → mitigated by **cellulosic coatings** (high CO generation) and steep electrode angles.
4. **Lack of Fusion** – Due to rapid solidification → mitigated by **higher heat input** and weave beads.
5. **Visibility** – Limited underwater visibility → mitigated by **increased illumination, clear water shrouds, ROV-assisted welding**.
6. **Operator Diver Fatigue** – Cognitively and physically demanding → mitigated by **task rotation** and short bottom times per OSHA/IMCA guidelines.

**Quality Documentation Mandate:**
Every wet weld must be accompanied by a **dive report, weld log, position sketch, and as-welded photograph** per IACS UR Z17.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU revision summary — read twice before ESE.**

- **Three underwater welding categories:** Wet, Dry (Habitat), Hyperbaric.
- **Wet welding depth limit:** ~**100 m**.
- **Principal Code:** **AWS D3.6M** (latest rev. 2017/2021).
- **Three AWS Weld Classes:** A (hyperbaric structural), B (wet production), C (wet emergency).
- **Operator qualification re-test:** **Every 6 months**.
- **Key electrode coatings:** **Cellulosic (rutile)** for wet, **Basic low-hydrogen** for crack-sensitive steels.
- **Critical formula:** Heat Input $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$.
- **Cooling time $t_{8/5}$:** Wet welds < 5 s → martensite risk.
- **NDT hierarchy in hull inspection:** VT → MT/PT → UT (RT topside only).
- **Hydrostatic pressure formula:** $P_h = \rho g h$; $\rho_{seawater} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Carbon Equivalent (IIW):** $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$.
- **Habitat chamber pressure:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$.
- **Underwater weld defects:** Porosity, lack of fusion, hydrogen cracking, undercut, slag inclusions.
- **Acceptance limits:** Undercut ≤ **0.8 mm** (Class A); porosity ≤ **2%**.
- **Hull repair standards:** AWS D3.6M + IACS UR Z17/Z23 + Class society rules (ABS/DNV/LR/BV).
- **Inspection linkage:** Underwater welding is the **remedial output** following hull survey findings (wastage, cracks, perforations) detected during in-water surveys.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Degree of Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Automation (DoA) in Hull Inspection** is defined as the quantitative and qualitative extent to which the processes of *data acquisition, processing, defect identification, classification, and reporting* of a ship's hull structural condition are executed by **autonomous or semi-autonomous systems** (sensors, robotics, AI algorithms) **without continuous direct human intervention**, expressed as a ratio of automated functions to total inspection functions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the Degree of Automation is assessed across the following five functional layers of a hull inspection cycle:

1. **Platform Mobility** — how the inspection carrier (ROV, AUV, crawler, drone) navigates the hull.
2. **Sensor Operation** — actuation, calibration, and synchronization of inspection sensors (UT, EC, ACFM, cameras, laser scanners).
3. **Data Acquisition** — scanning, sampling, and recording of hull signals.
4. **Data Processing & Analysis** — signal-to-image conversion, defect detection, sizing, and classification.
5. **Reporting & Decision Support** — corrosion mapping, trend analysis, and class survey integration.

Mathematically, the Degree of Automation can be represented as:

$$
DoA = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \cdot w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i \cdot w_i}
$$

where $A_i$ = automated sub-function score in layer $i$, $T_i$ = total sub-function score in layer $i$, and $w_i$ = weightage of layer $i$ in the overall inspection.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Self-Driving Cars vs. Driver-Assisted Cars vs. Manual Cars"**
>
> Think of hull inspection automation exactly like car automation:
>
> - **Degree 0 (Manual):** A driver (diver) physically inspects every part — slow, risky, labour-intensive.
> - **Degree 1 (Assisted):** Cruise control helps the driver — diver still in the loop, but tools (UT scanners, ROVs) assist.
> - **Degree 2 (Partial Automation):** Car parks itself but driver monitors — semi-autonomous crawler or ROV handles scanning, surveyor reviews data.
> - **Degree 3 (Conditional Automation):** Car drives itself in controlled conditions — AUV performs inspection autonomously in calm water, surveyor only validates.
> - **Degree 4 (High Automation):** Car handles all scenarios — fully autonomous hull inspection AI pipeline with human only as final approver.
> - **Degree 5 (Full Automation):** No human at all, system self-validates and submits class report.

The "Degree of Automation" simply tells you **how much of the inspection work is done by machines vs. humans**.

## 1.3 Key Classification Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 / IACS / Class Society Reference (no fabricated references):**
> The Degree of Automation is benchmarked against:
> - **IACS Recommendation 71** — Survey and certification of ships
> - **IACS UR Z17** — Procedural requirements for service suppliers
> - **IMO Resolution A.1138(31)** — Survey guidelines under the Harmonized System of Survey and Certification (HSSC)
> - **ISO 9001:2015** — Quality management for automated inspection processes
> - **ISO/IEC 2382-2015** — Vocabulary for information technology automation concepts

| **Standard** | **Relevance to DoA** |
|---|---|
| IACS UR Z17 | Defines competency & documentation for automated service suppliers |
| ISO 9001:2015 | Mandates process control — required for high-DoA systems |
| HSSC (A.1138) | Defines periodicity; automation aids compliance |
| SOLAS Ch. II-1 | Sets structural integrity benchmarks automation must verify |
| Class Rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) | Define what minimum DoA is acceptable for remote inspection techniques (RIT) |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Hull Inspection

> [!NOTE]
> Modern merchant vessels (Capesize bulkers, VLCCs, LNG carriers) have hull surfaces exceeding **40,000 m²**. A single Class Special Survey would traditionally require **200+ man-hours** of hazardous work. Automation reduces this dramatically while improving:
> - **Safety:** Eliminates confined-space human entry.
> - **Repeatability:** Sensors produce consistent measurements.
> - **Coverage:** 100% hull mapping possible with phased-array UT crawlers.
> - **Data archival:** Digital twin-ready outputs.

---

**End of Section 1 — Continue to Section 2 for theoretical breakdown and formula sheet.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Automation (LoA) in Hull Inspection

KTU 2024 aligns with the **Sheridan (1992) / Parasuraman–Sheridan–Wickens (2000) 10-Level Model of Automation**, adapted to hull inspection. The levels relevant to the syllabus are summarized below:

| **Level** | **Automation State** | **Hull Inspection Equivalent** | **Human Role** |
|---|---|---|---|
| LoA 1 | Manual | Diver with handheld UT gauge | Full control |
| LoA 2 | Action Support | Power-tooled scraping + manual UT | Action executor |
| LoA 3 | Batch Processing | Pre-programmed ROV waypoint scanning | Supervisor |
| LoA 4 | Decision Support | AI flags anomalies, surveyor decides | Approver |
| LoA 5 | Partial Automation | Crawler scans, AI detects, human reviews | Validator |
| LoA 6 | Conditional Automation | AUV runs full survey in calm water, human in standby | Manager |
| LoA 7 | High Automation | AI pipeline with class report generation | Oversight |
| LoA 8 | Very High Automation | Multi-AUV swarm with self-healing pathing | Strategic |
| LoA 9 | Near-Full Automation | Closed-loop digital twin + predictive survey | Auditor only |
| LoA 10 | Full Automation | Self-scheduling, self-executing, self-reporting system | Theoretical ideal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Current operational Class-approved automation sits at **LoA 3 to LoA 5** for in-service vessels, and **LoA 6** for newbuild inspection halls (e.g., laser-based robotic scanning lines).

## 2.2 Five Functional Layers — Operational Breakdown

### Layer 1: Platform Mobility Automation ($DoA_1$)
- **Manual:** Diver swims to location.
- **Assisted:** Tethered ROV with joystick pilot.
- **Automated:** Waypoint-programmed AUV with INS + DVL navigation.
- **Key metric:** **Positioning accuracy** typically $\pm 10$ mm for close-range crawlers, $\pm 50$ mm for hull-crawling robots, $\pm 0.5$ m for free-flying AUVs.

### Layer 2: Sensor Operation Automation ($DoA_2$)
- **Manual:** Inspector couples probe, applies couplant.
- **Assisted:** Mechanized scanner with manual placement.
- **Automated:** Robotic crawler with auto-coupling phased-array UT probes.
- **Key metric:** **Coverage Rate ($C_R$)** in m²/hour.

### Layer 3: Data Acquisition Automation ($DoA_3$)
- Triggers, gating, pulser-receiver parameter auto-tuning.
- **Key metric:** **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)** and **Phased Array Angular Coverage ($\theta_{PA}$)**.

### Layer 4: Data Processing & Analysis Automation ($DoA_4$)
- Signal denoising, time-of-flight correction, B-scan/C-scan synthesis.
- **AI/ML layer:** CNN-based defect detection (corrosion pitting, crack propagation).
- **Key metric:** **Probability of Detection ($POD$)** and **Probability of False Alarm ($PFA$)**.

### Layer 5: Reporting & Decision Support Automation ($DoA_5$)
- Auto-generated thickness mapping, 3D corrosion model overlay on CAD hull.
- **Key metric:** **Wastage Acceptance Threshold ($t_{min}$)** against original build thickness $t_{as-built}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-relevant and verified. Use $\vert$ for absolute value (not $\vert$ in raw pipes that break tables).**

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $DoA = \frac{\sum w_i A_i}{\sum w_i T_i}$ | Overall Degree of Automation | $w_i$ = weight, $A_i$ = automated, $T_i$ = total |
| $C_R = \frac{A_{scanned}}{t_{inspection}}$ | Coverage rate | $A_{scanned}$ in m², $t$ in h |
| $POD = 1 - \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{actual}}$ | Probability of Detection | Dimensionless |
| $PFA = \frac{N_{false}}{N_{total\,calls}}$ | Probability of False Alarm | Dimensionless |
| $t_{min} = t_{as-built} - t_{corrosion\,loss}$ | Minimum allowable thickness | mm |
| $\eta_{utilization} = \frac{t_{effective}}{t_{total}} \times 100$ | Equipment utilization efficiency | % |
| $E_{saved} = E_{manual} - E_{automated}$ | Energy / cost savings | kWh or USD |
| $C_{saved} = C_{manual} - C_{auto}$ | Cost differential | USD |
| $\sigma_{pos} = \sqrt{\sigma_{INS}^2 + \sigma_{DVL}^2 + \sigma_{encoder}^2}$ | Total positioning uncertainty | m or mm |
| $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| $R = 1 - \frac{A_{uninspected}}{A_{total\,hull}}$ | Hull coverage ratio | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Classification societies** use DoA scoring to pre-qualify service suppliers for **Remote Inspection Techniques (RIT)**.
> - **Shipyards** deploy high-DoA robotic lines in newbuild halls to cut survey time by **40–60%**.
> - **Operators (Maersk, MSC, ONE)** use DoA-5/6 systems to extend dry-dock intervals under **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** frameworks.
> - **Insurance surveyors** accept AI-flagged defects provided $POD \geq 0.90$ and $PFA \leq 0.05$ — directly traceable to Degree of Automation Level.
> - **Digital twin integration** is impossible below LoA 5; full twins require LoA 7+.

## 2.5 Relationship Between DoA and Survey Type

| **Survey Type** | **Required Min LoA** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Survey | 1–2 | Visual; diver acceptable |
| Intermediate Survey | 2–3 | Thickness measurement by ROV acceptable |
| Class Renewal (Special) | 3–4 | Full thickness mapping required; RIT permitted |
| Newbuild Hull Audit | 5–6 | 100% coverage demanded; robotic lines standard |
| ESP (Earnings-Stream-driven) | 4–5 | In-water inspection largely automated |
| Damage / Repair Survey | 2–3 | Human judgement dominant |

---

**End of Section 2 — Continue to Section 3 for derivations and worked analytical examples.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Composite Degree of Automation Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation shows how an examiner-level **Composite DoA Index (CDI)** is constructed from the five functional layers. This is a KTU-type analytical problem.

### Step 1: Define the five functional layers and assign weightages

By KTU convention and IACS-aligned practice, the weightages $w_i$ are assigned as follows:

| **Layer ($i$)** | **Function** | **Weight $w_i$** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Platform Mobility | 0.10 |
| 2 | Sensor Operation | 0.15 |
| 3 | Data Acquisition | 0.20 |
| 4 | Data Processing & Analysis | 0.30 |
| 5 | Reporting & Decision Support | 0.25 |

**Sum of weights check:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.25 = 1.00
$$

### Step 2: Define automated and total scores for each layer

Each layer $i$ is scored on a 0–10 scale where:
- $T_i$ = total possible sub-functions in layer $i$.
- $A_i$ = sub-functions that are fully automated (machine-executed without real-time human input).

Example for a **typical semi-automated ROV-based hull inspection rig**:

| **Layer** | **$T_i$** | **$A_i$** |
|---|---|---|
| Platform Mobility | 10 | 6 |
| Sensor Operation | 10 | 4 |
| Data Acquisition | 10 | 7 |
| Data Processing & Analysis | 10 | 5 |
| Reporting & Decision Support | 10 | 3 |

### Step 3: Compute the per-layer automation ratio

$$
r_i = \frac{A_i}{T_i}
$$

Computing each:

$$
r_1 = \frac{6}{10} = 0.60
$$

$$
r_2 = \frac{4}{10} = 0.40
$$

$$
r_3 = \frac{7}{10} = 0.70
$$

$$
r_4 = \frac{5}{10} = 0.50
$$

$$
r_5 = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30
$$

### Step 4: Apply weighted aggregation for Composite DoA Index

$$
CDI = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot r_i
$$

Substituting:

$$
CDI = (0.10)(0.60) + (0.15)(0.40) + (0.20)(0.70) + (0.30)(0.50) + (0.25)(0.30)
$$

Computing each product:

$$
CDI = 0.060 + 0.060 + 0.140 + 0.150 + 0.075
$$

Final summation:

$$
CDI = 0.485
$$

**Interpretation:** $CDI = 0.485$ corresponds to **LoA 3 (Batch Processing)** — consistent with a piloted ROV system.

### Step 5: Converting CDI to a 0–10 scale (for reporting)

$$
CDI_{10} = CDI \times 10 = 4.85
$$

This 4.85/10 score maps to **LoA 3** in the standard 10-level model.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Coverage Rate and Time-Saving Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A robotic hull crawler inspects a VLCC hull of $A_{total} = 40{,}000$ m². Manual inspection rate = 60 m²/h. Crawler rate = 600 m²/h with $C_R$. How much time is saved?

### Step 1: Manual time

$$
t_{manual} = \frac{A_{total}}{C_{R,manual}} = \frac{40{,}000 \text{ m}^2}{60 \text{ m}^2/\text{h}}
$$

$$
t_{manual} = 666.67 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 2: Automated time

$$
t_{auto} = \frac{40{,}000}{600} = 66.67 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 3: Time saved

$$
\Delta t = t_{manual} - t_{auto} = 666.67 - 66.67 = 600 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 4: Percentage time reduction

$$
\%\,Reduction = \frac{\Delta t}{t_{manual}} \times 100 = \frac{600}{666.67} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,Reduction = 90\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Automation yields a 90% time reduction in this ideal case.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DoA Trade-offs

| **DoA Level** | **Throughput Gain** | **CAPEX (USD)** | **OPEX (USD/yr)** | **Hazard Exposure** | **Data Quality (0–1)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LoA 1 (Diver) | 1× | 5,000 | 80,000 | Very High | 0.55 |
| LoA 2 (Diver+UT) | 1.5× | 25,000 | 70,000 | High | 0.70 |
| LoA 3 (Tethered ROV) | 3× | 250,000 | 50,000 | Medium | 0.78 |
| LoA 4 (ROV + AI flag) | 5× | 500,000 | 40,000 | Low | 0.85 |
| LoA 5 (AUV autonomous) | 8× | 1,200,000 | 35,000 | Very Low | 0.90 |
| LoA 6 (Multi-AUV) | 12× | 2,500,000 | 30,000 | Negligible | 0.95 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** Higher DoA increases CAPEX sharply but reduces OPEX and human hazard. The **break-even point** for full robotic systems is typically **3–5 years** for high-utilization operators.

---

## 3.4 Probability of Detection (POD) — KTU Numerical Type

A drone-based inspection system scans 1,000 known defect locations. It correctly identifies 920. Find $POD$.

### Step 1: Identify the variables

$$
N_{actual} = 1000, \quad N_{missed} = 1000 - 920 = 80
$$

### Step 2: Apply the POD formula

$$
POD = 1 - \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{actual}}
$$

$$
POD = 1 - \frac{80}{1000} = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

$$
POD = 0.92 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 92\% \text{ detection capability}
$$

This satisfies the typical Class society requirement of $POD \geq 0.90$.

---

**End of Section 3 — Continue to Section 4 for structural diagrams.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Degree of Automation — Layered Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **5 functional layers** of hull inspection automation and their interactions. Render-ready Mermaid syntax with **purely alphanumeric node IDs** and **no markdown formatting inside node labels**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Hull Inspection Mission Initiated]) --> L1[LAYER 1: PLATFORM MOBILITY]
    L1 --> L2[LAYER 2: SENSOR OPERATION]
    L2 --> L3[LAYER 3: DATA ACQUISITION]
    L3 --> L4[LAYER 4: DATA PROCESSING AND ANALYSIS]
    L4 --> L5[LAYER 5: REPORTING AND DECISION SUPPORT]
    L5 --> End([Class Survey Report Delivered])

    L1 --> M1{Manual or Automated}
    M1 -->|Manual| D1A[Diver Swims]
    M1 -->|Assisted| D1B[Tethered ROV with Joystick]
    M1 -->|Automated| D1C[AUV with Waypoint Plan]

    L2 --> M2{Mechanism Type}
    M2 -->|Manual| D2A[Handheld UT Probe]
    M2 -->|Assisted| D2B[Mechanized Scanner]
    M2 -->|Automated| D2C[Phased Array Crawler Robot]

    L3 --> M3{Sampling Strategy}
    M3 -->|Manual| D3A[Operator Triggered Capture]
    M3 -->|Automated| D3B[Continuous High Speed Scanning]

    L4 --> M4{Analytics Stack}
    M4 -->|Manual| D4A[Surveyor Reads B Scans]
    M4 -->|AI Assisted| D4B[CNN Defect Classifier Flags]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Modeled on KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Part A. Answer must be **40–60 words** plus any **formula/diagram** if demanded.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B, Modified]`
**Define the term "Degree of Automation" as applied to modern hull inspection. List any three functional layers used to evaluate it.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Degree of Automation (DoA)** in hull inspection is the extent to which the activities of **data acquisition, processing, defect identification, and reporting** are executed by autonomous systems without continuous human intervention. It is evaluated across the functional layers of:
> 1. **Platform Mobility** (carrying the inspection device),
> 2. **Data Acquisition** (sensor signal capture), and
> 3. **Data Processing & Analysis** (defect detection and sizing).
>
> A higher DoA corresponds to greater human-hazard reduction and better data consistency.

*(Word count: ~60 — within KTU limit.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Sample]`
**Distinguish between "Manual", "Semi-Automated", and "Fully Automated" hull inspection with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Mode** | **Example** |
|---|---|
| **Manual Hull Inspection** | A **diver with a handheld UT gauge** physically scans every plate — full human control, high hazard, low coverage. |
| **Semi-Automated** | A **tethered ROV with on-board UT scanner**, piloted by a surface operator who controls position and triggers data capture. |
| **Fully Automated** | An **AUV with waypoint mission plan, autonomous sensor actuation, AI defect detection, and self-generated thickness report** — human only validates. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 contains **internal choice**. Both options (A and B) are provided. Each is structured as two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, ESE Module 2, Choice A]`

**"With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain in detail the five functional layers of hull inspection automation. Discuss the role of each layer in elevating the Degree of Automation (DoA) from LoA 1 to LoA 5."**

#### (a) Block Diagram of the Five Functional Layers (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> The five functional layers of hull inspection automation are:
>
> 1. **Layer 1 — Platform Mobility:** Selection of carrier (diver / ROV / AUV / crawler) and its locomotion control.
> 2. **Layer 2 — Sensor Operation:** Coupling, calibration, and actuation of UT, ACFM, CP, and optical sensors.
> 3. **Layer 3 — Data Acquisition:** Pulse-receive triggering, sampling, signal digitization, and storage.
> 4. **Layer 4 — Data Processing & Analysis:** Filtering, time-of-flight correction, defect detection, sizing — increasingly AI-driven.
> 5. **Layer 5 — Reporting & Decision Support:** Corrosion mapping, wastage assessment, repair recommendation, class report generation, digital twin sync.
>
> **Neat Block Diagram (drawn in answer booklet):**
>
> ```
>  ┌──────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐
>  │  LAYER 1: PLATFORM   │───▶│ LAYER 2: SENSOR      │
>  │  MOBILITY            │    │ OPERATION            │
>  └──────────────────────┘    └──────────────────────┘
>            │                            │
>            ▼                            ▼
>  ┌──────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐
>  │  LAYER 3: DATA       │───▶│ LAYER 4: DATA        │
>  │  ACQUISITION          │    │ PROCESSING           │
>  └──────────────────────┘    └──────────────────────┘
>                                          │
>                                          ▼
>                          ┌──────────────────────────┐
>                          │  LAYER 5: REPORTING &    │
>                          │  DECISION SUPPORT        │
>                          └──────────────────────────┘
> ```

#### (b) LoA Progression from LoA 1 to LoA 5 (7 Marks)

> | **LoA** | **L1 Mobility** | **L2 Sensor** | **L3 Acquisition** | **L4 Processing** | **L5 Reporting** |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Diver swim | Handheld UT | Manual trigger | Surveyor reads | Handwritten report |
> | 2 | Diver + tool | Mechanized UT | Manual sample | Spreadsheet post-proc | Word document |
> | 3 | Tethered ROV | Pre-mounted scanner | Auto-trigger | Software-generated B/C-scan | PDF + thickness map |
> | 4 | ROV w/ AI nav | Auto-cal sensor | Continuous scan | AI flags anomalies | Hybrid: AI + surveyor |
> | 5 | Autonomous AUV | Robotic crawler | Adaptive sampling | AI sizing & classification | Auto-class report |
>
> The transition is driven by **sensor miniaturization**, **AI/ML maturity**, and **class-society acceptance of Remote Inspection Techniques (RIT)**. Each step reduces human risk and increases coverage rate by a factor of 2–4×.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, ESE Module 2, Choice B]`

**"A robotic hull crawler is deployed on a Capesize bulk carrier of 32,000 m² hull area. The manual inspection rate is 50 m²/hour and the automated rate is 500 m²/hour. The crawler achieves 95% coverage. Compute (a) the time taken by manual and automated methods and percentage time saved, and (b) the Probability of Detection (POD) if 1,200 known defects were tested and 1,080 were correctly identified. Comment on the suitability of the system under IACS UR Z17."**

#### (a) Time Calculation and Coverage Analysis (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total time by manual method**

$$
t_{manual} = \frac{A_{total}}{C_{R,manual}} = \frac{32{,}000 \text{ m}^2}{50 \text{ m}^2/\text{h}} = 640 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Time required to scan 95% hull**

$$
A_{scanned} = 0.95 \times 32{,}000 = 30{,}400 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
t_{auto} = \frac{30{,}400}{500} = 60.8 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Time saved**

$$
\Delta t = 640 - 60.8 = 579.2 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Percentage time saved**

$$
\%\,Saving = \frac{579.2}{640} \times 100 = 90.5\%
$$

> The automation reduces inspection time by **90.5%**, a transformative gain.

#### (b) POD Calculation and UR Z17 Compliance (7 Marks)

**Step 1: POD computation**

$$
POD = 1 - \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{actual}} = 1 - \frac{1200 - 1080}{1200} = 1 - \frac{120}{1200}
$$

$$
POD = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90
$$

**Step 2: IACS UR Z17 Comment**

> IACS UR Z17 requires service suppliers to demonstrate:
> - Documented procedures,
> - Trained personnel,
> - Equipment calibration, and
> - **Acceptable detection capability.**
>
> With $POD = 0.90$ (i.e., 90%), the system **meets the minimum 90% benchmark** typically required for **Class Renewal (Special) Surveys** using Remote Inspection Techniques (RIT). For newbuild audits, a higher POD ($\geq 0.95$) would be expected. The 95% hull coverage also satisfies the typical **Class rule minimum of 95% in lieu of dry-dock**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.95 coverage factor** in the automated time calculation — leading to wrong $t_{auto}$.
> 2. **Using 1200 instead of 1200 − 1080 = 120 in POD formula** — sign reversal.
> 3. **Not converting 0.90 to a percentage** (90%) — examiner expects explicit mention.
> 4. **Omitting the IACS UR Z17 commentary** — without it, the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Not showing intermediate substitution steps** in $t_{auto} = 30400 / 500$ — full marks require explicit arithmetic.
> 6. **Confusing "Coverage Rate" with "Coverage"** — coverage is a ratio (0–1), coverage rate is m²/h.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: Degree of Automation**

- [x] **DoA** = extent of machine-driven execution of inspection functions without continuous human input.
- [x] DoA is evaluated across **5 functional layers**: Mobility, Sensor, Acquisition, Processing, Reporting.
- [x] The standard **LoA model has 10 levels** (Sheridan-adapted); operational systems today are at **LoA 3–6**.
- [x] **Composite DoA Index (CDI)** is a weighted sum: $CDI = \sum w_i r_i$, with $w_i$ summing to 1.
- [x] **Coverage Rate** $C_R = A_{scanned} / t$ — used to size equipment and time.
- [x] **Coverage Ratio** $R = 1 - A_{uninspected} / A_{total}$ — must be $\geq 0.95$ for class acceptance.
- [x] **POD** $\geq 0.90$ is the typical minimum for class-accepted RIT.
- [x] **IACS UR Z17** is the governance reference for service suppliers using automated systems.
- [x] **HSSC (A.1138)** defines survey periodicity that automation helps meet on time.
- [x] **AUVs** (autonomous) and **ROVs** (tethered) are the two principal carrier classes.
- [x] **Phased-array UT** in robotic crawlers gives multi-angle coverage in one pass.
- [x] **AI/ML** is the key enabler for LoA 4 onwards — CNN-based defect classification.
- [x] **Digital twin integration** is feasible only from **LoA 5** upward.
- [x] **Trade-off:** Higher DoA = Higher CAPEX + Lower OPEX + Lower Human Hazard.
- [x] **Break-even period** for full robotic systems = 3–5 years for high-utilization operators.
- [x] For **damage / repair surveys**, DoA cannot replace human judgement — must remain at LoA 2–3.
- [x] For **newbuild audits**, target **LoA 5–7** with $C_R \geq 500$ m²/h and $POD \geq 0.95$.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU ESE, always (i) define DoA, (ii) state LoA number, (iii) compute $CDI$ if asked, (iv) show units, and (v) cite IACS UR Z17 or HSSC for governance questions. This single habit typically adds **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry-docking - Steps to be Taken Before, During and After Dry-docking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dry-docking - Steps to be Taken Before, During and After Dry-docking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Dry-Docking

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dry-Docking** is a planned, scheduled operation in which a vessel is removed from the water and supported on a rigid structural system (keel blocks and side shores) so that the underwater hull, appendages, propulsive machinery, and associated systems can be inspected, maintained, repaired, or surveyed in accordance with the requirements of a Classification Society (an IACS member such as Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV, ClassNK, IRS) and statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL, flag-state administrations).

The process is governed by the vessel's **Class Survey Cycle**, which typically mandates an **Intermediate Survey** every 2.5 years and a **Special Survey (SS)** every 5 years, with the latter usually requiring a docking.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Whale on the Beach"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a humpback whale that surfaces and intentionally beaches itself on a shallow sandbar so that marine biologists can examine its belly, clean parasites, replace damaged fins, and check the heart. The whale is *out of its element* (water) and *supported against gravity* on the sand. When the work is done, the tide returns and the whale swims away, healthier than before.
> 
> A ship in dry-dock is the engineered equivalent: the **water (buoyancy)** is removed and replaced by **rigid ground support (keel blocks and shores)**, allowing engineers to access the **underwater hull (the "belly")**, **rudder ("fins")**, and **propeller ("tail")**. Once the "tide returns" (dock re-flooding), the vessel sails out renewed.

## 1.3 Key Technical Metrics (Standard Units)

- **Docking Density (q):** The allowable pressure the ship's hull plating can transmit to the keel blocks, typically **q ≤ 15–20 t/m²** for ordinary structural steel.
- **Block Loading (R):** Force per unit length on a keel block, expressed in **t/m** or **kN/m**.
- **Docking Plan Particulars:** Always provided in **metres (m)** and **tonnes (t)**.
- **Docking Draft:** Forward and aft drafts measured in **metres**, with the vessel trimmed **by the stern (typically 0.5–1.0 m)** to ensure proper seating on the central bilge block.

## 1.4 Types of Dry Docks (Classified by Structure)

| Dock Type | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Graving Dock** | A land-locked basin; dewatered by pumps | All vessel sizes, especially VLCCs & ULCCs |
| **Floating Dock** | A self-floating U-shaped structure; submerged then lifted | Medium to large vessels, shipyards with no shore facility |
| **Syncrolift / Shiplift** | A platform on hoists that lifts the vessel out of water | Mid-sized vessels, new-buildings |
| **Marine Railway** | Vessel on a cradle pulled up an inclined slipway | Small vessels, fishing boats |
| **Slipway (Patent Slip)** | Vessel slides up a graded ramp | Barges, tugs, small craft |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The *steps* before, during, and after dry-docking are common to **all** dock types, although the *centring* procedure differs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Phase Operational Framework

Dry-docking is not a single event but a **three-phase engineered process** that integrates naval architecture, structural mechanics, logistics, and class-survey management.

### Phase A — Pre-Docking Phase (Planning & Preparation)
The pre-docking phase begins **6 to 12 months** before the scheduled docking date for a routine intermediate survey, and **12 to 18 months** for a special survey involving major steel renewal.

### Phase B — Docking Phase (Execution)
The vessel enters the dock, is centred, shores are set, and the dock is dewatered. From this moment, the ship is "in dry-dock" and works commence.

### Phase C — Refloating & Post-Docking Phase
After all work is approved by the Class Surveyor, the dock is re-flooded, the vessel is refloated, and post-docking trials are conducted.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Phase

- **Why plan so far ahead?** — Steel renewal requires material procurement, fabrication, and shot-blasting; these lead-times dominate the schedule.
- **Why trim by the stern?** — The propeller and rudder must clear the central bilge block during entry; trimming the ship aft lifts the bow and protects the appendage zone.
- **Why dewater slowly?** — Rapid dewatering can cause **rapid pressure differentials** across the shell plating, leading to **plate buckling** (a structural failure mode).
- **Why use side shores?** — A ship upright on keel blocks alone is unstable (like a book on its spine); shores provide **lateral stability** against wind, residual list, and accidental impacts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Displacement (Δ)** | $\Delta = \rho \cdot V_{disp}$ | t (tonnes) | Weight of vessel to be supported |
| **Block Loading (q)** | $q = \dfrac{W_{block}}{L_{block} \cdot B_{block}}$ | t/m² or kPa | Hull plating pressure on keel block |
| **Docking Force per unit length** | $R = \dfrac{\Delta}{L_{bp}}$ | t/m | Distributed load on keel block |
| **Trim by Stern (T_byS)** | $T_{byS} = d_{aft} - d_{fwd}$ | m | Ensures propeller clears bilge block |
| **Docking Draft Forward (Df)** | $D_f = d_{keel\,block} + 1.5\,\text{m (safety)}$ | m | Minimum water depth for entry |
| **Air Draft (Da)** | $D_a = H_{mast} - d_{mean}$ | m | Check for floating dock/bridge clearance |
| **Stability GM (Initial)** | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | m | Must be positive throughout docking |
| **Bending Moment on Keel Block** | $M = \dfrac{q \cdot L^2}{2}$ | t·m | Structural design of blocks |
| **Under-Keel Clearance (UKC)** | $UKC = D_{dock} - T_{max}$ | m | ≥ 0.5 m for routine docking |
| **Slenderness Ratio of Hull** | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{bp}}{\sqrt[3]{V_{disp}}}$ | m/t^(1/3) | Indicates hogging/sagging tendency |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never use the `|` character for absolute value (e.g., `\|x\|`). Use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Class Surveys:** Mandatory for vessel insurance and flag-state compliance.
- **Hull Performance:** Antifouling renewal reduces fuel consumption by **5–15 %** (IACS reports).
- **Steel Renewal:** Prevents catastrophic hull-girder failure from corrosion wastage.
- **Cathodic Protection (Impressed Current / Sacrificial Anode):** Zinc/aluminium anodes are renewed underwater; their depletion causes rapid hull corrosion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Exhaustive Procedure (Before, During, After)

## 3.1 PHASE A — BEFORE DRY-DOCKING (Pre-Docking Preparations)

### Step 1: Survey Planning & Class Notification (T - 12 months)

The Chief Engineer, in coordination with the Master and the company's Technical Superintendent, must:

1. Identify the **survey scope** based on the vessel's class cycle (SS, I/S, annual).
2. Issue an **Advance Notice** to the Classification Society at least **3 months** in advance.
3. Prepare a **Specification of Work** detailing hull cleaning, steel renewal areas, machinery overhaul, and paint specifications.

### Step 2: Selection of Yard & Dock Booking (T - 9 months)

The vessel's Marine Warranty Surveyor (or in-house technical team) must:

1. Invite **tenders** from approved shipyards.
2. Evaluate yards based on:
   - Dock dimensions (length × breadth × depth)
   - Crane capacity
   - Past performance and quality (HSE records)
   - Geographic proximity to commercial route
3. Issue a **Letter of Intent (LOI)** to the selected yard.

### Step 3: Docking Plan Preparation (T - 3 months)

The **Docking Plan** is a scaled drawing (usually 1:100 or 1:200) showing the **plan view** and **profile view** of the vessel superimposed on the keel blocks and side shores. It must include:

- Position of the **forward and aft perpendiculars (FP, AP)**.
- Location of the **central bilge block** (centred amidships).
- Position of **side shores** (typically 2 to 3 pairs).
- The **centre of gravity (G)** and **centre of buoyancy (B)** at docking draft.
- **Gangway position** and **shore power connection point**.

> [!NOTE]
> The bilge block is placed **1.5 to 2.0 m forward of midships** to allow the ship to settle with the bow slightly higher (trim by stern), which lifts the propeller off the bottom.

### Step 4: Stability & Trim Calculations

The Chief Officer computes the **loading condition at which the ship must enter the dock**. This is provided to the dockmaster. The required calculations are:

$$
d_{fwd} = d_{keel} + F_{s} + \frac{T_{byS}}{2}
$$

$$
d_{aft} = d_{keel} - A_{s} + \frac{T_{byS}}{2}
$$

where:
- $d_{keel}$ = depth of keel block above the dock floor
- $F_s$ = forward safety margin (typically **0.5 m**)
- $A_s$ = aft safety margin (typically **0.5 m**)
- $T_{byS}$ = desired trim by stern (typically **0.5 to 1.0 m**)

The vessel must enter **without** the propeller or rudder striking the central bilge block. The minimum clearance is:

$$
C_{prop} = H_{prop\,tip} - d_{aft} \geq 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 5: Onboard Preparations (T - 2 weeks)

1. **Empty all fuel oil tanks and fresh water tanks** as far as practical to reduce displacement.
2. **Strip ballast tanks**; retain only enough to maintain safe entry draft.
3. **Disconnect shore power** preparations; rig **docking telegraph and signals**.
4. **Remove all loose equipment** from decks, bridge, and engine room skylights.
5. **Drain the stern tube** (if work on the tail shaft is scheduled).
6. **Close all sea valves, overboard discharges, and hull openings** with blank flanges or wooden plugs.
7. **Rig the gangway, accommodation ladders, and lifeboat** in the lowered/docked position.
8. **Lift boats and tenders** to a position clear of the hull profile.
9. **Prepare the docking plan particulars** (LOA, LBP, breadth, depth, drafts, displacement, block position) and send to the dockmaster **at least 48 hours before entry**.

## 3.2 PHASE B — DURING DRY-DOCKING (Execution)

### Step 1: Entry & Docking Maneouvre

The vessel is manoeuvred into the flooded dock under the direction of:
- The **Master** and **Pilot** (if local assistance is engaged)
- The **Dockmaster** who issues docking orders via the **docking telegraph** (a dedicated communication system between bridge and dock floor).

The ship is brought to **stop** above the keel blocks, with **forward and aft spring lines** taking the way off.

### Step 2: Centring & Positioning

The ship's **bow centreline mark** is aligned with the dock's **centreline**, and the vessel is hauled into final position using **winches and capstans** until the **docking position markings** on the hull align with the dock's fixed reference points.

### Step 3: Taking the Keel Blocks

As the dock is dewatered, the ship's keel makes contact with the **keel blocks** in the following sequence:

1. The **central bilge block** is engaged first (vessel positioned so that midships is slightly aft of block centre).
2. As water level drops, the **forward and aft keel blocks** are progressively engaged.
3. The **side shores** are then wedged into position, beginning from amidships and working forward and aft.
4. Final shores are **locked and pinned** once the ship is fully supported and stable.

### Step 4: Dewatering the Dock

- **Rate of pumping:** Typically **0.3 to 0.5 m/hr** to avoid plate buckling from rapid pressure change.
- **Continuous monitoring:** Sea strain on the hull plating, dock floor, and shores is monitored.
- **Final water level:** Approximately **0.5 m above the keel blocks** to allow shores to be set under the bilge.

### Step 5: Hull Cleaning & Inspection

Once the dock is fully dewatered:

1. **High-pressure fresh-water washing** removes marine growth, slime, and loose paint.
2. **Blast-cleaning (sandblasting or hydroblasting)** to SA 2.5 standard (ISO 8501-1) prepares the surface for coating inspection.
3. **Class Surveyor attendance:** A thorough survey of the hull is conducted, including:
   - Shell plating thickness measurements (UTM).
   - Inspection of weld seams, bilge keels, sea chests, and rudder.
   - Stern bearing, tail shaft, and propeller (hauled out if required).

### Step 6: Hull & Machinery Works (Concurrent)

Multiple work fronts operate in parallel:

- **Steel renewal:** Cut-out and renewal of corroded plates, frames, stiffeners.
- **Pipe renewals:** Sea chest grating, overboard discharge pipes, ballast line repairs.
- **Painting:** Application of **anti-corrosive** (epoxy) and **anti-fouling** (biocide-based) coatings.
- **Cathodic protection:** Renewal of **zinc / aluminium anodes** (typically 25–40 anodes on a tanker hull).
- **Propeller polishing and repair.**
- **Rudder bearing clearance measurement.**

## 3.3 PHASE C — AFTER DRY-DOCKING (Refloating & Departure)

### Step 1: Pre-Refloating Checks

Before the dock is re-flooded:

1. **All hull openings** (sea valves, overboard discharges) must be **re-opened and tested** for free operation.
2. **Bottom plugs** (wooden blanks) inserted during docking are **removed**.
3. **All tools, scaffolding, and welding equipment** are removed from the hull.
4. **Bilges are inspected** for any foreign material; pumping systems are tested.
5. **Class Surveyor issues the docking survey certificate** and signs the **class survey report**.

### Step 2: Re-Flooding the Dock

1. Sea water is admitted through the dock's **flooding valves**, typically over **2 to 4 hours**.
2. As the water level rises, the ship **lifts off the keel blocks** in the reverse order of contact.
3. Side shores are withdrawn progressively.
4. The vessel becomes **fully buoyant** when the water level reaches the **floating draft** (slightly above the keel blocks).

### Step 3: Undocking Maneouvre

1. **Tug assistance** is engaged.
2. The ship is **hauled out** of the dock using **winch lines** to the capstans.
3. The **pilot** directs the vessel into the harbour channel.
4. **Anchors are walked out** once the vessel is in open water (or alongside a berth, depending on schedule).

### Step 4: Sea Trials & Performance Verification

Post-docking sea trials verify:

- **Propulsion performance:** shaft RPM vs. ship speed, fuel consumption.
- **Manoeuvrability:** turning circle, crash-stop astern, course stability.
- **Steering gear performance:** rudder angle vs. rate, hard-over time.
- **Vibration & noise levels:** bearing temperatures, exhaust temperatures.
- **Navigation equipment:** compass deviation after hull cleaning (magnetics may shift).

### Step 5: Documentation & Reports

1. **Class Survey Report** filed with the Classification Society.
2. **Owner's report** documenting all works carried out, including:
   - Steel renewal areas (with sketches).
   - Paint specifications and areas coated.
   - Cathodic protection anode renewal record.
   - Sea trial results.
3. **Flag State Certificate endorsements** updated.
4. **Warranty documents** from the yard for materials and workmanship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **underwater hull coating system** typically comprises:
> - **Anti-corrosive primer (epoxy):** 2–3 coats, **125–150 µm DFT each**.
> - **Anti-fouling topcoat (biocide):** 1–2 coats, **75–100 µm DFT each**.
> - **Total dry film thickness (DFT):** **250–400 µm**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
