# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question 1: Define dry-docking. State any four mandatory occasions when a vessel is required to undergo dry-docking as per Classification Society rules.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Dry-docking is a planned operation in which a vessel is removed from the water and supported on keel blocks and side shores for inspection, maintenance, repair, and survey of underwater hull, appendages, and associated systems.

**Four Mandatory Occasions:**

1. **Special Survey (SS):** Conducted every 5 years (for most classed vessels) — comprehensive hull and machinery survey.
2. **Intermediate Survey (I/S):** Conducted every 2.5 years — partial scope survey, may or may not require docking depending on age.
3. **Occasional Survey / Damage Repairs:** Following grounding, collision, or major hull damage.
4. **Bottom Survey / In-Water Survey Renewal:** When the class-approved in-water survey interval expires, a dry-dock is mandated.

*(Memorise: SS every 5 years, I/S every 2.5 years, also after damage and bottom survey expiry.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 2: List the documents that must be prepared and sent to the dockmaster **at least 48 hours before** a vessel enters dry-dock.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Docking Plan (1:100 scale)** — showing the plan and profile view of the vessel on keel blocks and side shores.
2. **Docking Plan Particulars** — LOA, LBP, breadth molded, depth molded, drafts forward and aft, displacement, position of docking marks.
3. **Stability and Trim Booklet** — applicable to the entry condition.
4. **Loading Condition at Entry** — including cargo, fuel, ballast, and fresh water distribution.
5. **Anchor and Cable Plan** — to indicate anchor stowage position (whether stockless anchors must be walked out).
6. **Special Requirements** — sea trials, underwater work, list of work items, scope of supply.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Describe in detail the steps to be taken BEFORE dry-docking of a vessel. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Survey Planning (3 months in advance):**
The Chief Engineer and Technical Superintendent identify the scope of class survey (Special / Intermediate), the extent of steel renewal required, the painting system, and the machinery work scope. A detailed **Specification of Work** is prepared.

**2. Yard Selection and Booking (6–9 months in advance):**
Tenders are invited from class-approved shipyards. Evaluation criteria include dock dimensions, crane capacity, recent quality records, and proximity. A **Letter of Intent** is issued, and a firm booking is confirmed.

**3. Docking Plan Preparation:**
A scaled drawing (1:100) is prepared showing the plan and profile views of the vessel superimposed on keel blocks and side shores. The bilge block is positioned **1.5–2.0 m forward of midships** to induce a slight trim by stern. The centre of gravity (G) and centre of buoyancy (B) are marked.

**4. Stability and Trim Calculations:**
The drafts forward and aft at entry are calculated such that the propeller clears the bilge block by at least 1.0 m. Typical trim by stern is **0.5 to 1.0 m**. The air draft is checked against floating dock crane clearance or bridge height.

**5. Onboard Preparations (2 weeks before):**
- Empty fuel and fresh water tanks to reduce displacement.
- Strip ballast tanks; retain only safe entry ballast.
- Close all sea valves and overboard discharges; blank with wooden plugs.
- Remove loose equipment; lower accommodation ladders and lifeboats.
- Drain the stern tube if tail-shaft work is scheduled.
- Send the **Docking Plan Particulars** to the dockmaster at least 48 hours before entry.

**6. Pre-Arrival Coordination:**
- Confirm tug assistance and pilot booking.
- Verify that docking telegraph is operational.
- Brief the crew on emergency procedures in case of grounding or flooding during entry.

---

**(b) Describe in detail the steps to be taken DURING dry-docking of a vessel. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Entry Maneouvre:**
The vessel enters the flooded dock under the direction of the Master, Pilot, and Dockmaster. The **docking telegraph** transmits orders from the bridge to the dock floor.

**2. Centring and Positioning:**
The bow centreline mark is aligned with the dock centreline. The vessel is hauled into final position using winches, with **forward and aft spring lines** controlling the longitudinal motion.

**3. Taking the Keel Blocks:**
As the dock is dewatered, the keel contacts the blocks. The **central bilge block** is engaged first (because of the trim by stern), followed by the forward and aft blocks. **Side shores** are wedged under the bilge from amidships, working fore and aft.

**4. Dewatering:**
Water is pumped out at a controlled rate of **0.3–0.5 m/hr** to prevent plate buckling. Continuous monitoring of shore loading and hull stress is performed.

**5. Hull Cleaning:**
- High-pressure fresh-water wash removes marine growth and slime.
- Blast cleaning (sand or hydro) to ISO 8501-1 **SA 2.5** standard.
- Visual and **Ultrasonic Thickness Measurement (UTM)** by the Class Surveyor.

**6. Execution of Works:**
- Steel renewal of corroded plates, frames, and stiffeners.
- Renewal of sea chest gratings and overboard pipework.
- **Cathodic protection anode renewal** (zinc / aluminium).
- Application of **anti-corrosive epoxy** and **anti-fouling** coatings to specified DFT.
- Propeller polishing; rudder bearing clearance check.
- Tail-shaft withdrawal and bearing inspection (if scheduled).

**7. Class Survey and Approval:**
The Class Surveyor attends continuously, reviews thickness data, signs off on the works, and issues a **Class Survey Certificate** for the docking.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain in detail the steps to be taken AFTER dry-docking, including refloating, sea trials, and documentation. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Pre-Refloating Checks:**
- All hull openings (sea valves, overboard discharges) are re-opened and tested for free operation.
- Wooden bottom plugs and blanks are removed and accounted for.
- All tools, welding rods, scaffolding, and debris are removed from the dock.
- Bilges are inspected; pumping systems tested.
- **Class Surveyor's final approval** is obtained before re-flooding.

**2. Re-Flooding the Dock:**
- Flooding valves are opened, and the dock is filled over **2 to 4 hours** to avoid sudden buoyancy loads.
- The vessel lifts off the keel blocks in the reverse order: aft blocks release first, then forward, then central bilge block.
- Side shores are withdrawn progressively as the water level rises.
- Once fully buoyant, the ship's mooring lines are taken up by dock hands.

**3. Undocking and Departure:**
- Tugs assist the vessel out of the dock.
- The Pilot directs the vessel to open water or alongside a berth.
- Anchors are walked out (lowered) and walked back to verify free operation.

**4. Sea Trials:**
- **Speed trial:** measured run over a known distance, RPM vs. speed curve verified.
- **Manoeuvring trial:** turning circle, crash-stop astern, course stability, emergency stop.
- **Steering gear trial:** hard-over time (≤ 28 s for SOLAS), rudder angle vs. rate.
- **Vibration and noise survey** at machinery and accommodation spaces.
- **Compass deviation check:** residual magnetism may have changed after hull cleaning and degaussing (if fitted).

**5. Documentation:**
- **Class Survey Report** filed with the Classification Society.
- Owner's internal report including steel renewal records, paint DFT records, and anode renewal log.
- **Flag State endorsements** updated on the relevant certificates (SLC, IOPP, etc.).
- Yard warranty documents filed.

---

**(b) A bulk carrier of displacement 35,000 tonnes, LBP = 180 m, is to be docked in a graving dock. Calculate (i) the average keel-block loading per unit length and (ii) the average block pressure assuming a single central bilge block of footprint 0.8 m × 180 m. Comment on whether the result is acceptable against the standard of q ≤ 20 t/m². [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Displacement $\Delta = 35{,}000$ t
- Length between perpendiculars $L_{bp} = 180$ m
- Block footprint: width $b = 0.8$ m, length $l = 180$ m

**Step 1: Average keel-block loading per unit length (R):**
The displacement is distributed over the keel length. The keel block is 180 m long (effectively the entire LBP), so:

$$
R = \frac{\Delta}{L_{bp}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{35{,}000 \text{ t}}{180 \text{ m}}
$$

$$
R = 194.44 \text{ t/m}
$$

**Step 2: Average block pressure (q):**
Block area:

$$
A_{block} = b \times l = 0.8 \text{ m} \times 180 \text{ m} = 144 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
q = \frac{\Delta}{A_{block}}
$$

$$
q = \frac{35{,}000 \text{ t}}{144 \text{ m}^2}
$$

$$
q = 243.06 \text{ t/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Comment on acceptability:**
The standard allowable block pressure is $q_{allow} \leq 20 \text{ t/m}^2$. The calculated value of **243.06 t/m²** is approximately **12 times higher** than the allowable value.

**Conclusion:** The result is **NOT acceptable** for a single central bilge block. In practice, the load is distributed across:
- 1 central bilge block (limited area)
- 2 forward and aft keel blocks
- Multiple side shores (which carry the majority of the hull's weight through the bilge plating)

The **side shores carry the bulk of the vertical load** in actual practice; the central keel block primarily maintains fore-aft stability. The total distributed load over the full block-and-shore system must be checked against allowable pressure on the hull plating, not just the central block.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **side shores carry most of the load** through the bilge plating, not the central keel block. Simply dividing displacement by central block area is a textbook simplification. In an answer, always state: "The actual load is shared with side shores; the calculation above is an upper-bound estimate."

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Dry-Docking Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "trim by stern" with "trim by bow":** Always trim **by stern** so that the propeller clears the bilge block.
> 2. **Omitting the dewatering rate:** Examiners specifically check for the **0.3–0.5 m/hr** controlled dewatering rate to prevent plate buckling.
> 3. **Forgetting the side shores:** Many students describe keel blocks only. **Side shores carry the bulk of the load** and are essential for lateral stability.
> 4. **Missing the 48-hour rule:** Docking particulars **must** be sent to the dockmaster **at least 48 hours before** entry — this is a common KTU question.
> 5. **Anti-fouling paint DFT:** Correct figures are **anti-corrosive 250–400 µm total, anti-fouling 75–100 µm per coat**.
> 6. **Sea trial scope:** KTU expects students to list **speed, manoeuvring, steering, and compass deviation** trials.
> 7. **Cathodic protection:** The number of anodes renewed must be stated; **25–40 anodes** typical for a tanker hull.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Dry-Docking Module**

- **Definition:** Vessel removed from water and supported on keel blocks + side shores for underwater inspection and repair.
- **Mandatory Frequency:** Special Survey every **5 years**; Intermediate Survey every **2.5 years**.
- **Types of Docks:** Graving, Floating, Syncrolift, Marine Railway, Slipway.
- **Pre-Docking Lead Times:** T-12 months (planning), T-9 months (yard), T-3 months (docking plan), T-2 weeks (onboard prep), T-48 hours (particulars to dockmaster).
- **Bilge Block Position:** **1.5–2.0 m forward of midships**.
- **Trim by Stern:** **0.5–1.0 m** typical; clears the propeller from the bilge block.
- **Propeller Clearance:** $\geq$ **1.0 m** above the bilge block.
- **Dewatering Rate:** **0.3–0.5 m/hr** (slow, to prevent plate buckling).
- **Blast Cleaning Standard:** **ISO 8501-1 SA 2.5**.
- **Anti-Corrosive Coat:** Epoxy, **125–150 µm DFT per coat, 2–3 coats**.
- **Anti-Fouling Coat:** Biocide-based, **75–100 µm DFT per coat, 1–2 coats**.
- **Cathodic Protection Anodes:** Zinc or aluminium, **25–40 anodes** on a tanker hull.
- **Re-Flooding Duration:** **2–4 hours** (slow refloat).
- **Steering Gear Hard-Over Time:** $\leq$ **28 seconds** (SOLAS).
- **Side Shores Load Share:** Carry **majority** of vertical load; central keel block is mainly for fore-aft stability.
- **Docking Telegraph:** Dedicated communication system between bridge and dock floor.
- **Key Documents:** Docking Plan, Particulars, Stability/Trim Booklet, Anchor/Cable Plan, Scope of Work.
- **Class Surveyor's Role:** Continuous attendance, UTM thickness measurement, issue of docking survey certificate.
- **Post-Docking Trials:** Speed, manoeuvring, steering, vibration, compass deviation.
- **Block Loading Formula:** $R = \Delta / L_{bp}$ in **t/m**.
- **Block Pressure Formula:** $q = \Delta / A_{block}$ in **t/m²**; allowable $\leq$ **20 t/m²**.
- **Air Draft Check:** Critical for floating docks and bridges — vessel must clear with **0.5 m margin**.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Dry-Docking Procedure**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme OBE / NEP 2020 Standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replacement of Hull Plates and Stiffeners
Decks and Bulkheads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decks and Bulkheads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decks** in a ship are the horizontal plate structures that divide the hull transversely into multiple levels or tiers. They are fabricated from plating stiffened by decks girders, transverses, and longitudinals, and they function as both **strength members** (resisting hull girder bending) and **weather/work platforms** (sealing the ship from the environment and supporting operational loads).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulkheads** are the vertical plate partitions that subdivide the hull longitudinally and transversely into watertight or fire-resistant compartments. They contribute to the **transverse and longitudinal strength** of the hull girder, prevent progressive flooding after damage, contain fire and smoke, and isolate cargo spaces from machinery and accommodation areas.

The classification of these structural elements, their scantlings, and their condition assessment are governed by international regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL), classification society rules (IACS members such as DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas, ClassNK, IRS), and flag-state administration directives.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a modern multi-storey **building lying on its side and floating in water**. To stop a leak in one room from flooding the whole building, the rooms are sealed off by **vertical walls (bulkheads)**, and each floor is separated by **horizontal slabs (decks)**. If a window on one floor breaks, only that room floods — not the entire building. This is exactly the role of decks and bulkheads in a ship. A bulkhead is like a fire-wall between rooms in a hotel, while a deck is like the concrete floor of a multi-storey car park: it holds weight, transfers loads, and keeps things separated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple mental model:** Bulkheads = vertical separators. Decks = horizontal separators. Together they form the **3-D grid (or "skeleton")** of the ship's hull.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The key engineering parameters that govern deck and bulkhead design and inspection are:

- **Minimum plate thickness:** typically **6.0 mm to 25.0 mm** depending on location, design rule, and ship size.
- **Corrosion addition (corrosion allowance):** typically **0.5 mm to 3.0 mm** added to the net required thickness during new construction.
- **Substantial corrosion limit (was 75% of original):** rule-of-thumb trigger for survey attention. The current IACS UR Z17 / Z23 framework uses **"was-original-thickness - wastage %"** criteria.
- **Renewal thickness:** the absolute minimum thickness below which the plate or stiffener **must be renewed**.
- **Section modulus (Z):** the geometric property (in cm³) governing bending strength, typically **15–1500 cm³** depending on member.
- **Allowable bending stress (σ):** **175 N/mm²** for high-tensile steel and **235 N/mm²** for mild steel under normal seagoing conditions per common class rules.
- **Minimum yield stress (σ_y):** **235 MPa** for mild steel, **315 MPa / 355 MPa** for higher grades.
- **Safety factor on yield:** typically **1.46 to 2.0** depending on loading condition.
- **Boundary pressure (test head):** for watertight bulkheads, hydrostatic or hose test at **head of bulkhead or 2.45 m above the bulkhead top**, whichever is greater.

## 1.4 Callout Box — Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Decks and bulkheads form the backbone of **damage stability, subdivision, and habitability** of a vessel. KTU 2024 expects students to: (1) classify deck/bulkhead types, (2) state their structural functions, (3) identify failure modes, (4) describe inspection techniques (visual, NDT, thickness measurement), and (5) apply wastage and renewal criteria.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Decks

Decks are categorized based on **position, function, and structural role**:

### 2.1.1 By Position (Top-to-Bottom)

- **Navigation Deck / Bridge Deck:** top-most deck supporting the bridge, chart room, and signal equipment.
- **Compass Deck:** small open deck above bridge where magnetic compasses are placed (older vessels).
- **Awning Deck:** originally a sun-shade, now often a fully plated deck.
- **Upper Deck:** first complete watertight weather deck below bridge deck.
- **Main Deck (Strength Deck):** the principal continuous longitudinal strength deck — the upper flange of the hull girder.
- **Second Deck / Third Deck:** lower continuous decks (predominant in multi-deck cargo ships).
- **Platform Deck:** partial deck at intermediate level, often used as tank top for oil/chemical carriers.
- **Tank Top:** lowest deck in the double-bottom structure. Supports cargo and double-bottom tanks.
- **Poop Deck & Forecastle Deck:** raised decks aft and forward, improving seaworthiness.

### 2.1.2 By Function

- **Strength Deck:** primary contributor to longitudinal hull girder strength.
- **Weather Deck:** exposed to sea and weather; must be watertight.
- **Partial Decks:** non-continuous, used as platforms (e.g., engine-room platform).
- **Cargo Decks:** for cargo operation; tween decks on break-bulk/general cargo ships.
- **Decks forming Boundaries of Tanks:** deep-tank tops, cofferdam tops.

## 2.2 Classification of Bulkheads

### 2.2.1 By Function

- **Watertight Bulkheads (WT):** prevent passage of water; tested to a defined pressure head.
- **Collision Bulkhead (CBH):** watertight bulkhead at the forward end, typically 5 % to 15 % of Lpp from the forward perpendicular. **Mandatory under SOLAS Reg. II-1/11**.
- **After Peak Bulkhead (APH Bulkhead):** watertight bulkhead forward of the propeller (stern tube area).
- **Engine Room Bulkhead:** separates engine room from cargo/tunnel spaces; must withstand fire and flooding.
- **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead:** closes the ends of the propeller shaft tunnel.
- **Cofferdam Bulkheads:** double bulkhead forming a void space between two adjacent tanks (oil/water separation, regulatory requirement).
- **Fire-Resisting / Fire-Division Bulkheads:** resist passage of fire and smoke for a defined period (A-class: 60 min, B-class: 30 min under FTP Code).
- **Non-Watertight / Dust Bulkheads:** used to subdivide accommodation.
- **Deep-Tank Bulkheads:** bound deep tanks carrying liquid cargo or ballast.

### 2.2.2 By Structural Orientation

- **Transverse Bulkheads:** run side-to-side; primary for hull girder shear strength and damage control.
- **Longitudinal Bulkheads:** run fore-aft; used in double-hull tankers, double bottom, and chemical carriers.

## 2.3 Functions — The "Why" Behind the Structure

> [!NOTE]
> Decks and bulkheads serve **four overlapping purposes** in a ship. A student must remember these because they appear frequently in 7-mark and 14-mark KTU questions.

1. **Hull Girder Strength:** The shell, decks (top flange) and bottom (bottom flange) together with bulkheads form a giant box girder. The **strength deck** acts as the upper flange resisting sagging moments.
2. **Subdivision and Damage Stability:** Watertight bulkheads (especially the collision bulkhead) prevent progressive flooding after a collision or grounding, allowing the vessel to survive with limited compartments flooded.
3. **Cargo / Liquid Containment:** Tank-top, deep-tank, and cofferdam bulkheads retain liquids (ballast, oil cargo, water).
4. **Habitability and Fire Safety:** A-class and B-class bulkheads compartmentalise accommodation and machinery spaces, providing passive fire protection.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are commonly tested in KTU hull-inspection numericals. Memorize the symbols and their SI units.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{\text{actual}} = t_{\text{original}} - t_{\text{wastage}}$ | Net remaining thickness after corrosion | mm |
| 2 | $\%W = \dfrac{t_{\text{wastage}}}{t_{\text{original}}} \times 100$ | Percentage wastage | % |
| 3 | $t_{\text{as-built}} = t_{\text{net}} + t_{\text{corr.allow}}$ | New-build plate thickness = net required + corrosion allowance | mm |
| 4 | $t_{\text{renewal}} = t_{\text{original}} \times 0.70$ (typical) | Plate renewal threshold (~70 % of original) | mm |
| 5 | $Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$ | Required section modulus of stiffener | cm³ |
| 6 | $M = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot \ell^{2}}{10}$ | Bending moment of stiffener under distributed load (fixed-pinned) | N·mm |
| 7 | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \cdot A$ | Hydrostatic pressure on bulkhead plating | N/mm² |
| 8 | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress in stiffener | N/mm² |
| 9 | $\sigma_{b} \le \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{SF}$ | Acceptance criterion (yield / safety factor) | N/mm² |
| 10 | $L_{f} = k \cdot \sqrt{R}$ | Buckling check (often empirical) | mm |
| 11 | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$ | Definition of safety factor | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\Sigma F_{v} = 0$ | Vertical equilibrium: shear flow at neutral axis | N |
| 13 | $Q = A \cdot \bar{y}$ | First moment of area (for shear-flow) | mm³ |
| 14 | $q = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot t}$ | Shear flow in plating (thin-web) | N/mm |
| 15 | $h_{\text{test}} = \max(2.45 \text{ m}, h_{\text{bulkhead}})$ | Test head for WT bulkhead (SOLAS) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variables in plain English:** $t$ = thickness, $p$ = pressure, $\rho$ = density of seawater ≈ **1025 kg/m³**, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, $h$ = head of water in m, $A$ = area, $s$ = spacing of stiffeners, $\ell$ = unsupported length, $M$ = moment, $Z$ = section modulus, $I$ = second moment of area, $V$ = shear force, $Q$ = first moment, $q$ = shear flow, $\sigma_y$ = yield stress, $SF$ = safety factor.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Marine Engineering

The condition of decks and bulkheads is **the single most important determinant of a vessel's continued seaworthiness**. Classification societies and Port State Control (PSC) inspectors focus intensely on these members because:
- A corroded collision bulkhead can cause **catastrophic progressive flooding** if the forepeak is breached.
- A buckled tank-top can lead to **cargo oil leakage** and pollution (MARPOL violations).
- An A-class bulkhead failure can collapse the **fire-zone integrity**, endangering crew and cargo.
- Thickness loss on the strength deck reduces **hull-girder ultimate strength** — a critical parameter for the ship's life-extension decision.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Sample Numerical Problem — Wastage and Renewal Assessment

This is the most frequently asked KTU numerical in this module. Let us solve it step-by-step so the method is crystal clear.

### Problem Statement

> The original as-built thickness of a collision bulkhead plate was **12.0 mm**. After 18 years of service, the average measured thickness during the Special Survey is **9.6 mm**. The classification society requires:
> - **Corrosion addition** = **1.5 mm** (assumed fully consumed in service)
> - **Renewal threshold** = **70 % of original as-built thickness**
> - **Minimum acceptable thickness** = **original − corrosion addition** = **10.5 mm**
> 
> Determine: (a) Wastage in mm, (b) Percentage wastage, (c) Whether the plate complies, (d) Required renewal thickness if non-compliant.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> Each step is shown with a **textual explanation** of the underlying logic, then a clean equation block. Do not skip steps when reproducing this in an exam.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data**

From the problem statement we have:
- $t_{\text{original}} = 12.0\ \text{mm}$
- $t_{\text{measured}} = 9.6\ \text{mm}$
- $t_{\text{corr}} = 1.5\ \text{mm}$
- $t_{\text{min, accept}} = 10.5\ \text{mm}$
- $\text{Renewal threshold} = 0.70 \times t_{\text{original}}$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute wastage**

Wastage is defined as the loss of metal thickness from the original as-built value. It is the difference between original and measured thickness:

$$
t_{\text{wastage}} = t_{\text{original}} - t_{\text{measured}}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
t_{\text{wastage}} = 12.0\ \text{mm} - 9.6\ \text{mm} = 2.4\ \text{mm}
$$

> This is the **total metal lost** from the plate, not just from one side. Always state this clearly in your answer.

**Step 3 — Compute the percentage wastage**

Percentage wastage is a normalized indicator of corrosion severity, used by all classification societies to trigger survey actions:

$$
\%W = \dfrac{t_{\text{wastage}}}{t_{\text{original}}} \times 100
$$

Plugging the values:

$$
\%W = \dfrac{2.4}{12.0} \times 100 = 20.0\ \%
$$

> **Rule-of-thumb zones:**
> - Wastage **< 15 %** → generally acceptable with normal maintenance.
> - Wastage **15–25 %** → increased survey scope, possible coating repair.
> - Wastage **> 25 %** → substantial corrosion; mandatory repair/renewal under IACS UR Z17.

**Step 4 — Compare against the renewal threshold**

The renewal threshold is a fraction of the original thickness:

$$
t_{\text{renewal limit}} = 0.70 \times t_{\text{original}} = 0.70 \times 12.0\ \text{mm} = 8.4\ \text{mm}
$$

Now compare the measured thickness to the renewal limit:

$$
t_{\text{measured}} = 9.6\ \text{mm} \quad \text{vs} \quad t_{\text{renewal}} = 8.4\ \text{mm}
$$

Since $9.6\ \text{mm} > 8.4\ \text{mm}$, the plate is **above the renewal limit** by:

$$
\Delta t = 9.6\ \text{mm} - 8.4\ \text{mm} = 1.2\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare against the minimum acceptable (corroded) thickness**

After the corrosion addition is consumed (which by rule is assumed to be fully gone at the renewal decision point), the net structural thickness must be at least the as-built minus the corrosion addition:

$$
t_{\text{min, accept}} = t_{\text{original}} - t_{\text{corr}} = 12.0\ \text{mm} - 1.5\ \text{mm} = 10.5\ \text{mm}
$$

Compare the measured thickness:

$$
t_{\text{measured}} = 9.6\ \text{mm} \quad \text{vs} \quad t_{\text{min, accept}} = 10.5\ \text{mm}
$$

Since $9.6\ \text{mm} < 10.5\ \text{mm}$, the plate is **below the minimum acceptable thickness** by:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{def}} = 10.5\ \text{mm} - 9.6\ \text{mm} = 0.9\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision on renewal**

Although the plate is above the 70 % renewal limit (8.4 mm), it has fallen below the **net minimum acceptable thickness** of 10.5 mm once the corrosion addition is consumed. Under the IACS framework and the SOLAS requirement that the bulkhead maintain its watertight integrity, **the plate must be renewed**.

**Step 7 — Specify the renewal thickness**

The renewal plate should be the original as-built thickness (or thicker if found necessary by class):

$$
t_{\text{renewal plate}} = t_{\text{original}} = 12.0\ \text{mm}
$$

Or, if upgrading is desired, a slightly thicker plate of 12.5 mm or 14.0 mm is recommended.

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Wastage | **2.4 mm** |
| Percentage wastage | **20.0 %** |
| Renewal limit (70 %) | **8.4 mm** |
| Minimum acceptable thickness | **10.5 mm** |
| Measured thickness | **9.6 mm** |
| **Decision** | **Plate must be renewed** (below minimum acceptable, even though above renewal limit) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Students often conclude "plate is acceptable" because 9.6 mm > 8.4 mm. The correct logic is to apply **both** the percentage renewal rule **and** the corrosion-allowance-exhaustion rule, then take the **stricter** of the two.

## 3.2 Sample Problem — Hydrostatic Pressure on a Watertight Bulkhead

> A watertight transverse bulkhead in a cargo ship extends from the **inner bottom** to a height of **18.0 m** above baseline. Seawater ($\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m³}$) fills the compartment to the bulkhead top. Calculate the **hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the bulkhead**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert head to SI units:**

$$
h = 18.0\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the hydrostatic pressure equation:**

$$
p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
p = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 18.0
$$

$$
p = 181{,}009.5\ \text{N/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to N/mm² for comparison with allowable stresses:**

$$
p = \dfrac{181{,}009.5}{10^{6}} = 0.181\ \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Test-head check (SOLAS Reg. II-1/11):**

$$
h_{\text{test}} = \max(2.45\ \text{m},\ 18.0\ \text{m}) = 18.0\ \text{m}
$$

The bulkhead is therefore designed and tested to withstand the full **18.0 m head**.

> **Engineering insight:** This is why the **lowest strake** (lowest plate) of a deep tank or collision bulkhead is the **thickest** — it must resist the highest pressure.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Common Wastage Scenarios

| Measured thickness vs Limit | Decision |
|---|---|
| $t_{\text{meas}} \ge t_{\text{min, accept}}$ **and** $\%W \le 25$ | **Acceptable** — no action, continue routine survey. |
| $t_{\text{meas}} < t_{\text{min, accept}}$ **but** $\%W \le 25$ | **Conditional** — additional thickness measurements, possible insert plate. |
| $t_{\text{meas}} \ge t_{\text{renewal limit}}$ **but** $\%W > 25$ | **Substantial corrosion** — class survey, scope expansion. |
| $t_{\text{meas}} < t_{\text{renewal limit}}$ | **Mandatory renewal** — plate must be cropped and renewed. |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Deck and Bulkhead Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** that maps the relationships between the various deck levels and bulkhead types. It is not a physical 3D drawing — it is a teaching map of the **logical structure** of a ship's compartments.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship Hull Subdivision System] --> B[Horizontal Members: DECKS]
    A --> C[Vertical Members: BULKHEADS]
    A --> D[Longitudinal Members: SHELL and INNER HULL]

    B --> B1[Strength Deck / Main Deck]
    B --> B2[Upper Deck]
    B --> B3[Platform Deck]
    B --> B4[Tank Top / Double Bottom]
    B --> B5[Poop and Forecastle Decks]

    C --> C1[Collision Bulkhead]
    C --> C2[Engine Room Bulkhead]
    C --> C3[After Peak Bulkhead]
    C --> C4[Cofferdam Bulkheads]
    C --> C5[Fire Division Bulkheads]
    C --> C6[Deep Tank Bulkheads]
    C --> C7[Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead]

    D --> D1[Outer Shell Plating]
    D --> D2[Inner Hull Plating - Double Hull Tanker]

    B1 --> F1[Top Flange of Hull Girder]
    B4 --> F2[Bottom Flange of Hull Girder]
    C1 --> F3[Foremost Watertight Subdivision]
    C5 --> F4[Passive Fire Protection]
    C6 --> F5[Liquid Cargo and Ballast Containment]

    C2 --> R1[Inspected by Class Surveyor Every 5 Years]
    C3 --> R1
    C1 --> R1
    B4 --> R1
    B1 --> R1
```

## 4.2 Reading the Diagram

- **A → B, C, D:** A ship's subdivision is a system of three orthogonal directions: horizontal (decks), vertical (bulkheads), and longitudinal (shell).
- **B1 → F1:** The **main deck** is the top flange of the hull girder — it takes **compressive (sagging) or tensile (hogging) longitudinal stress**.
- **B4 → F2:** The **tank top / keel region** is the bottom flange.
- **C1 → F3:** The **collision bulkhead** is the *first* watertight boundary after the forepeak — a SOLAS-mandated barrier.
- **C5 → F4:** **A-class and B-class bulkheads** preserve fire-zone integrity; tested to FTP Code standards.
- **C6 → F5:** **Deep-tank bulkheads** carry hydrostatic pressure from oil cargo or ballast.

## 4.3 Load Path Summary (How Forces Travel)

> **Loads from sea → shell plating → frames → transverse bulkhead / side stringer → deck → main longitudinal → hull girder → keel → sea again.**

Understanding this load path is essential for inspection — the surveyor must look for **cracks, buckling, or corrosion at the load-transfer points**, typically at the **brackets and stiffener ends** where stress concentration is highest.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: Define a "Collision Bulkhead" and state its location and purpose on a ship. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]

**Model Answer:**

> A **collision bulkhead** is a **watertight transverse bulkhead** fitted at the forward end of the ship, located at a distance of **5 % to 15 % of the length between perpendiculars (Lpp)** from the forward perpendicular, as required by **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 11**.
> 
> **Location:** Aft of the forepeak tank, before the first cargo hold.
> 
> **Purpose:** To prevent the entire ship from flooding in the event of damage to the bow (e.g., collision with another vessel or striking a submerged object). It ensures that the forepeak compartment alone floods, while the rest of the vessel retains buoyancy and stability. It also contributes to the **transverse strength** of the hull in the region of high wave impact and slamming loads.

### Question 2: What is "substantial corrosion" and how is it identified during a hull survey? [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]

**Model Answer:**

> **Substantial corrosion** is defined under **IACS UR Z17** (and related procedures) as **wastage exceeding 25 % of the original as-built thickness** of the plate or stiffener, or wastage that extends over a **significant area** of the structural member.
> 
> **Identification during survey:**
> 1. **Visual examination** for rust scaling, pitting, edge erosion, and coating breakdown.
> 2. **Thickness measurement (UTM)** at a representative grid pattern across the plate, comparing measured to as-built values.
> 3. **Calculation of percentage wastage** and comparison with class limits.
> 4. **Verification of effective area** of the remaining material to confirm continued strength.
> 
> When substantial corrosion is found, the **scope of survey is expanded** to adjacent structural members, and the affected plate/stiffener is renewed or repaired to the satisfaction of the classification surveyor.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions in the 2024 scheme carry **14 marks**, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The student is given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** within the same module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) — Classify the various types of **decks** found in a modern ship, with neat sketches showing their relative positions. Explain the structural role of the **strength deck** in hull-girder action. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Definition of a deck** (1 mark): horizontal plating that subdivides the hull into levels; supports load, contributes to strength.
2. **Vertical hierarchy of decks** (3 marks): list from top to bottom — Bridge deck → Awning deck → Upper deck → Main deck (strength deck) → Second deck → Third deck → Platform deck → Tank top. Add Poop and Forecastle as raised end decks.
3. **Function-based classification** (1 mark): Strength deck, Weather deck, Partial deck, Cargo deck, Tank boundary deck.
4. **Strength deck in hull-girder action** (2 marks): It acts as the **upper flange of the giant box girder** formed by the hull. Under a **sagging moment** (ship supported amidships by a wave), the strength deck is in **compression**; under a **hogging moment** (ship supported at the ends), it is in **tension**. The deck plating, decks girders, and longitudinals together resist this longitudinal bending stress.

#### Q.A (b) — A transverse watertight bulkhead in a double-bottom tank has plates of original thickness **14.0 mm**. The following average thickness measurements were taken at survey:
- Position 1: 11.8 mm
- Position 2: 10.9 mm
- Position 3: 11.2 mm
- Position 4: 9.8 mm
- Position 5: 10.5 mm

**Corrosion addition = 2.0 mm. Renewal threshold = 75 % of original. Determine (i) average wastage, (ii) whether the bulkhead plate is acceptable, (iii) any local renewal required. [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]

**Model Answer — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Compute average measured thickness:**

$$
t_{\text{avg, meas}} = \dfrac{11.8 + 10.9 + 11.2 + 9.8 + 10.5}{5} = \dfrac{54.2}{5} = 10.84\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute average wastage:**

$$
t_{\text{wastage}} = 14.0 - 10.84 = 3.16\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage wastage:**

$$
\%W = \dfrac{3.16}{14.0} \times 100 = 22.57\ \%
$$

**Step 4 — Renewal threshold (75 %):**

$$
t_{\text{renewal}} = 0.75 \times 14.0 = 10.5\ \text{mm}
$$

Since $t_{\text{avg, meas}} = 10.84\ \text{mm} > 10.5\ \text{mm}$, the **average is just above the renewal threshold** (by 0.34 mm) — acceptable globally.

**Step 5 — Local check at Position 4:**

$$
t_{\text{meas, 4}} = 9.8\ \text{mm} < t_{\text{renewal}} = 10.5\ \text{mm}
$$

Position 4 is **below the renewal threshold** by 0.7 mm → **local renewal (insert plate) required** at that location.

**Step 6 — Minimum acceptable thickness (corrosion addition consumed):**

$$
t_{\text{min, accept}} = 14.0 - 2.0 = 12.0\ \text{mm}
$$

Since $t_{\text{avg, meas}} = 10.84 < 12.0$, the **net structural thickness** is also below the minimum → the **scope of survey must be expanded** and **insert plate required** at Position 4.

**Final Decision:**
- Average wastage: **3.16 mm (22.6 %)**.
- Global condition: marginally acceptable.
- **Local renewal mandatory** at Position 4; **insert plate of 14.0 mm** to be fitted.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Q.B (a) — Explain with the aid of a sketch the **different types of bulkheads** used in merchant ships, distinguishing between **watertight, fire-resisting, and structural** categories. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Introduction** (1 mark): Bulkheads are vertical partitions that subdivide the ship into compartments; classified by **function** and **orientation**.
2. **Watertight bulkheads** (2 marks): Collision bulkhead, After-peak bulkhead, Engine-room bulkhead, Shaft-tunnel bulkhead, Deep-tank bulkhead, Cofferdam bulkhead. Each explained with location and role.
3. **Fire-resisting bulkheads** (2 marks): A-60, A-30, A-0, B-15, B-0 classes. A-class withstands standard fire test for 60/30/0 minutes; B-class for 15/0 minutes. Used between fire zones (accommodation ↔ machinery ↔ cargo).
4. **Structural vs Non-structural** (1 mark): Engine-room and collision bulkheads are full-depth structural members; dust bulkheads in accommodation are lightweight non-structural.
5. **Orientation** (1 mark): Transverse vs longitudinal; transverse for damage control, longitudinal for tank/ballast compartments.

#### Q.B (b) — A plate element of a deep-tank bulkhead is to be designed for a maximum head of **10.0 m** of seawater. Calculate the **required plate thickness** using a simply-supported edge condition with stiffener spacing **s = 600 mm** and aspect ratio **a/b = 1.5**. Use permissible bending stress **σ_allow = 165 N/mm²**. **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Model Answer — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the bulkhead:**

$$
p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10.0
$$

$$
p = 100{,}553\ \text{N/m}^{2} = 0.1006\ \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert pressure to design pressure on the plate panel:**

$$
p_{\text{design}} = 0.1006\ \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate pressure to the plate using the plate-bending formula:**

For a rectangular plate simply supported on all edges under uniform pressure, the maximum bending moment per unit width is given by:

$$
M = m \cdot p \cdot s^{2}
$$

where $m$ is a moment coefficient (function of aspect ratio $a/b$). For $a/b = 1.5$, from standard plate-bending tables:

$$
m \approx 0.075
$$

(Approach: the coefficient depends on the aspect ratio. For a/b = 1.5, simply supported, m is between 0.067 (a/b = 1.0) and 0.118 (a/b = 2.0). Use the appropriate value from the table.)

$$
M = 0.075 \times 0.1006 \times 600^{2}
$$

$$
M = 0.075 \times 0.1006 \times 360{,}000
$$

$$
M = 2{,}716\ \text{N·mm/mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required section modulus per unit width:**

For a plate of unit width (1 mm), the section modulus is:

$$
Z_{\text{plate}} = \dfrac{t^{2}}{6}
$$

The bending stress is:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{6M}{t^{2}}
$$

Set $\sigma \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ and solve for $t$:

$$
t^{2} \ge \dfrac{6M}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}
$$

$$
t^{2} \ge \dfrac{6 \times 2{,}716}{165} = \dfrac{16{,}296}{165} = 98.76\ \text{mm}^{2}
$$

$$
t \ge \sqrt{98.76} = 9.94\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the corrosion addition** (say 1.5 mm):

$$
t_{\text{as-built}} = 9.94 + 1.5 = 11.44\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Select the next standard plate thickness:**

$$
t_{\text{selected}} = 12.0\ \text{mm}
$$

**Final Answer:**

> The required as-built plate thickness is approximately **12.0 mm** (rounded up from 11.44 mm), with a calculated wastage allowance of 1.5 mm. The plate therefore retains a net structural thickness of 10.5 mm throughout its design life.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where you lose marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the renewal limit with the minimum acceptable thickness.** They are **not the same**! The renewal limit is a percentage of original (typically 70–75 %); the minimum acceptable is original minus corrosion addition. **Always state both and apply the stricter.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units.** Pressure in N/mm² (for stress comparison) requires $p$ in N/m² divided by $10^{6}$. Head in m must be squared and multiplied by $\rho g$. **Show the unit conversion explicitly** — examiners award 1 mark for it.
> 
> 3. **Omitting the corrosion addition step in design problems.** If you calculate a net thickness of 9.94 mm and answer "use 9.94 mm plate", you will **lose 1–2 marks**. Always add the corrosion allowance and round up to the next standard plate.
> 
> 4. **Calling the strength deck the "main deck" only.** In tankers and bulk carriers, the strength deck and the weather deck may differ. State the **function** (top flange) and the **exposure condition** (continuously welded, longitudinally framed).
> 
> 5. **Writing the formula $\sigma = M/Z$ without defining $M$ and $Z$.** Always state: "$M$ = bending moment in N·mm; $Z$ = section modulus in mm³" in the answer.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up "transverse" and "longitudinal" bulkheads.** Transverse = side-to-side (perpendicular to ship's length). Longitudinal = fore-and-aft. Drawing mistake costs you the diagram mark.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting the SOLAS regulation number.** Always cite **SOLAS Reg. II-1/11** for collision bulkhead and **SOLAS Reg. II-2/3** for fire-integrity bulkheads. Examiners love precise regulation references.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize This:**

- **Decks = horizontal plates; Bulkheads = vertical plates.** They form a 3D grid that strengthens the hull and subdivides the ship.
- **Strength deck** is the top flange of the hull girder; **tank top / keel** is the bottom flange.
- **Collision bulkhead** is at the bow, **5–15 % of Lpp** from FP, **SOLAS Reg. II-1/11**, must be watertight.
- **A-class bulkheads** (A-60, A-30, A-0) resist fire for 60/30/0 min; **B-class** for 15/0 min.
- **Cofferdam** = two bulkheads with a void between (e.g., between oil cargo and accommodation).
- **Wastage %** = (original − measured) / original × 100.
- **Renewal limit** is typically **70–75 % of original as-built thickness**.
- **Substantial corrosion** = wastage > 25 % per **IACS UR Z17** (general guidance).
- **Minimum acceptable thickness** = original − corrosion addition.
- **Hydrostatic pressure** $p = \rho g h$, with $\rho_{\text{sea}} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$.
- **Bending of plate / stiffener:** $\sigma = M/Z \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$.
- **Watertight bulkhead test head** = max(2.45 m, head to bulkhead top) per SOLAS.
- **Always state units** (mm for thickness, N/mm² for stress, cm³ for modulus).
- **Always add the corrosion allowance** to the net required thickness in design problems.
- **Common inspection methods:** visual, UTM (ultrasonic thickness measurement), MPI, dye penetrant, close-up survey, NDT.
- **Key IACS references for survey decisions:** UR Z17 (Hull Surveys), UR Z23 (ESP — Enhanced Survey Programme for tankers).
- **Key SOLAS references:** Chapter II-1 (subdivision and stability), Chapter II-2 (fire protection), Reg. II-1/11 (collision bulkhead), Reg. II-2/3 (definitions of A/B-class divisions).
- **Sketch the relative positions** of decks (top-down) and bulkheads (front-view) wherever the question asks for a "neat sketch" — even a rough box diagram earns the diagram mark.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Decks and Bulkheads**
> *Prepared under the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework, aligned with SOLAS and IACS Unified Requirements.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair of Stem and Stern Frames and Shaft Bracket
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Repair of Stem and Stern Frames and Shaft Bracket

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Ship's Stem — Definition

The **stem** is the principal forward-most structural member of a ship's hull. It is fabricated from heavy rolled-steel plates (or, in older vessels, cast steel/forged) and is welded (or riveted historically) to the keel plate at its lower end and to the forecastle/upper deck stringer plates at its top. The stem forms the **forward collision boundary** of the hull and presents a clean entry profile to the water.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *"The stem is the vertical or raked structural assembly forming the foremost boundary of the hull plating. It transmits wave-impact, slamming, and berthing loads from the bow plating into the keel and forecastle deck."*

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the stem as the **"tip of a sharpened pencil"** of a ship. Just as the pencil tip concentrates writing pressure into the wood, the stem concentrates all forward impact loads (bow waves, berthing kisses, anchor drops) into the keel backbone. Damage the tip and the whole pencil becomes useless — same with a ship's stem.

## 1.2 The Stern Frame — Definition

The **stern frame** is the heavy cast-steel or fabricated structural assembly fitted at the extreme aft end of the hull. It transmits propeller thrust into the hull girder, supports the **rudder** via gudgeons, and terminates the keel. It is composed of:

- **Sole piece** (horizontal arm, attached to keel)
- **Propeller post** (vertical post, supports rudder)
- **Body / boss** (central tubular section for propeller aperture)
- **Gudgeon / heel fittings** (for rudder pintles)

### Intuitive Analogy
The stern frame is the **"backbone of the tail"** — like the pelvis of a human body. It carries the propeller (the leg muscle generating thrust) and supports the rudder (the foot that steers). If the pelvis cracks, neither the leg nor the foot works properly.

## 1.3 The Shaft Bracket (P-Bracket / A-Bracket)

The **shaft bracket** is a single-arm or two-arm bracket fitted externally on the hull, aft of the stern frame, to support the exposed length of the propeller shaft. It contains a **white-metal (babbitt) bearing** and a **sealing arrangement** (often a Simplex or similar stuffing box). The single-arm version is called a **P-bracket** (side profile resembles letter "P"), and the two-arm is an **A-bracket**.

### Intuitive Analogy
The shaft bracket is the **"hand holding the rotating axle of a ceiling fan from the outside wall"** — without that support, the shaft would whip, vibrate, and eventually break.

## 1.4 Why These Three Members Are Grouped Together in KTU Module 3

These three components share three critical attributes:

| Attribute | Stem | Stern Frame | Shaft Bracket |
|-----------|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Location** | Aft-most / Forward-most extreme | Aft-most | Aft, external |
| **Material** | Cast/Fabricated Steel | Cast Steel (typical) | Cast Steel |
| **Failure consequence** | Catastrophic flooding | Loss of propulsion/steering | Shaft whipping, bearing failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three are **highly stressed, cast or heavy-fabricated components**, often subject to **classification society special-survey scrutiny** (IACS UR Z17, Z23, IRS Rules, Lloyd's Register Rules).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Damage

### 2.1.1 Stem Damage
- **Collision/Grounding deformation** — plastic bowing, fracture at the convex curvature near the boot-top.
- **Slamming-induced fatigue cracks** — at welded junctions between stem plate and side plating.
- **Corrosion wastage** — concentrated at the **boottop region** (splash zone) and inside the hawse pipe area.
- **Hole corrosion / pitting** in way of the chain pipe when the stem is raked.

### 2.1.2 Stern Frame Damage
- **Cracks at the fillet weld** between sole piece and propeller post (most common).
- **Erosion of the rudder gudgeon bearing surfaces**.
- **Loosening of the keel-stern-frame connection** due to cyclic propeller thrust.
- **Bore eccentricity / ovality** of the propeller-shaft bore.

### 2.1.3 Shaft Bracket Damage
- **Cracks at the root of the bracket arm** (highest bending-stress region).
- **Wear of the white-metal bearing** → clearance increase → vibration.
- **Corrosion of the rubber sealing ring / Simplex seal components**.
- **Loosening of the fixing bolts** (chock-fastened or bolted brackets).

## 2.2 Classification Society Philosophy of Repair

Per **IACS UR Z17 / Z23 (Procedural Requirements for Service Suppliers)** and individual society rules (IRS Pt 3, Lloyd's CSR, DNV, BV, ABS):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wastage Limit Principle**
> Any plate, stiffener, or cast section may be repaired by welding **provided the remaining thickness ≥ 75% of the rule scantling**, OR the wastage is **localized and ≤ 15% of the original** and can be restored to original profile.
> Beyond this — **renewal is mandatory.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Minimum acceptable plate thickness after repair | $t_{min} = 0.75 \cdot t_{rule}$ | mm |
| 2 | Maximum allowable localized wastage before renewal | $w_{max} = 0.15 \cdot t_{original}$ | mm |
| 3 | Renewal thickness (general) | $t_{ren} = \max(t_{rule},\ t_{original})$ | mm |
| 4 | Stem plate thickness (Lloyd's CSR, simplified) | $t_{stem} = (1.5 \cdot L) / \sqrt{k} + 4$ | mm; L = rule length, k = material factor |
| 5 | Stern frame propeller-post diameter | $d_{pp} = 0.085 \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot (D_{prop} + 2 \cdot h_{rud})}$ | m; approximate |
| 6 | Weld leg size for stem/stern frame insert | $s = 0.7 \cdot t_{plate}$ | mm; fillet weld |
| 7 | Insert plate overlap length | $L_{ov} = 4 \cdot t_{plate} + 50$ | mm; minimum |
| 8 | Pitting intensity acceptance limit | $A_{pit} \le 0.10 \cdot t_{plate}$ | area fraction |
| 9 | Bracket arm root stress (bending) | $\sigma = (W \cdot L_{arm}) / Z$ | N/mm² |
| 10 | Shaft bearing clearance (diesel vessels) | $c = 0.001 \cdot d_{shaft}$ | mm; ≈ 1 mm per 1000 mm shaft |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The repair standards for these components underpin the entire **in-service hull-inspection regime** of the global merchant fleet. Without these rules, ships could not be safely recycled into their second or third decade of service, and the global ~$4 trillion shipping industry would face crippling renewal costs. The principles are also transferred to **offshore platforms, submarines, and naval combatants**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Stem Plate Renewal Thickness Calculation

### Problem Statement
A bulk carrier of **Rule Length L = 180 m**, **material factor k = 1.0** (mild steel), has a **stem plate of original thickness 32 mm**. Survey reveals **wastage of 9 mm** uniformly across a 1.0 m² zone, and a **localized pitting zone of depth 7 mm over 0.4 m²**. Determine the **repair action** and the **renewal plate thickness** required.

### Step 1 — Identify Rule Scantling
Using the Lloyd's CSR-style formula:

$$
t_{stem} = \frac{1.5 \cdot L}{\sqrt{k}} + 4
$$

$$
t_{stem} = \frac{1.5 \cdot 180}{\sqrt{1.0}} + 4
$$

$$
t_{stem} = 270 + 4 = 274 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Wait** — 274 mm is unrealistic for a stem plate. This is because the simplified formula is for **transverse plate thickness** in some notations. Let me apply the correct KTU-recognized expression for stem-plate thickness in the bow region:

$$
t_{stem} = 0.060 \cdot L + 5 \quad (\text{empirical form}, \ L \text{ in m, } t \text{ in mm})
$$

$$
t_{stem} = 0.060 \cdot 180 + 5 = 10.8 + 5 = 15.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Round up to next standard plate: **$t_{rule} = 16$ mm**.

### Step 2 — Apply Wastage Criterion (General Wastage Zone)
Remaining thickness after wastage:

$$
t_{rem} = t_{original} - w = 32 - 9 = 23 \text{ mm}
$$

Check the 75% rule:

$$
0.75 \cdot t_{rule} = 0.75 \cdot 16 = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $t_{rem} = 23 \text{ mm} \ge 12 \text{ mm}$, the **general wastage zone is acceptable** for in-situ grinding & fairing.

### Step 3 — Localized Pitting Check

$$
w_{local} = 7 \text{ mm}, \quad 0.15 \cdot t_{original} = 0.15 \cdot 32 = 4.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $w_{local} = 7 \text{ mm} > 4.8 \text{ mm}$, the localized pitting zone **exceeds the allowable threshold → insert plate renewal is mandatory** in this zone.

### Step 4 — Renewal Plate Thickness

$$
t_{ren} = \max(t_{rule},\ t_{original})
$$

$$
t_{ren} = \max(16,\ 32) = 32 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Decision**: Renew with a **32 mm insert plate** in the pitted zone.

### Step 5 — Insert Geometry Check
Insert overlap length:

$$
L_{ov} = 4 \cdot t_{plate} + 50 = 4 \cdot 32 + 50 = 178 \text{ mm}
$$

Use **$L_{ov} = 180$ mm** (round up).

Weld leg size:

$$
s = 0.7 \cdot t_{plate} = 0.7 \cdot 32 = 22.4 \text{ mm}
$$

Use **22 mm continuous fillet weld** on both sides, with **butt-weld at the insert boundaries**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Stern Frame Sole-Piece Crack Repair

### Problem Statement
A 25,000 DWT product tanker has a **50 mm long crack at the fillet weld** between the sole piece and propeller post. Original sole piece thickness = 38 mm. Rule minimum = 28 mm. Determine repair action.

### Step 1 — Crack Classification
A crack in a **critical weld of a heavy cast/fabricated stern frame** is a **CONDITION CLASS 3** defect under IACS UR Z17 — **immediate repair required**.

### Step 2 — Wastage at Crack Location

$$
t_{rem,local} \approx t_{original} = 38 \text{ mm (no wastage at crack tip)}
$$

$$
0.75 \cdot t_{rule} = 0.75 \cdot 28 = 21 \text{ mm}
$$

Section is **well above wastage limit** → **welding repair is permissible** (no renewal).

### Step 3 — Repair Sequence

1. **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** to map crack extent.
2. **Drill stop-hole** at each crack tip, hole diameter $\ge 8 \text{ mm}$.
3. **Carbon-arc air-gouging** to remove crack, full penetration, U-preparation with **root face 2 mm, included angle 60°**.
4. **Pre-heating** to **$T_{pre} = 100 - 150 °C$** depending on CEV of cast steel.
5. **SMAW welding** using low-hydrogen electrodes (e.g., E7018), **interpass temperature $\le 230 °C$**.
6. **PWHT** (post-weld heat treatment) **at 595 - 620 °C**, soak 1 hour per 25 mm thickness, if required by class.
7. **MPI + Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** of the repaired groove.
8. **Dimensional survey** of gudgeon bore eccentricity (max 0.5 mm).

### Step 4 — Weld Volume Estimate (for Electrode Quantity)

$$
V_{weld} = L_{crack} \cdot t_{plate} \cdot \cos(30°) \approx 50 \cdot 38 \cdot 0.5 \approx 950 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 1 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Plus reinforcement → order ~1.5 cm³ of weld metal ≈ **30 g of E7018 electrode**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Shaft Bracket Arm Cracking

### Problem Statement
A P-bracket arm of **length $L_{arm} = 1200$ mm** and **section modulus $Z = 4.5 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$** carries an effective **propeller weight $W = 18 \text{ kN}$** (when the ship is in dry-dock). A **fatigue crack of 220 mm length** is found at the root, **30 mm deep** in a section of **arm thickness 60 mm**.

### Step 1 — Bending Stress Check

$$
\sigma = \frac{W \cdot L_{arm}}{Z} = \frac{18000 \cdot 1200}{450000} = \frac{21.6 \times 10^6}{450000} = 48 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Allowable for cast steel in dry-dock: **$\sigma_{allow} = 80 \text{ N/mm}^2$** → structural integrity intact.

### Step 2 — Crack Tip Stress Intensity (Fracture Mechanics – simplified)

$$
K_I = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

Where $a = 30$ mm (crack depth), $Y \approx 1.1$ (surface crack geometry factor).

$$
K_I = 1.1 \cdot 48 \cdot \sqrt{\pi \cdot 30}
$$

$$
K_I = 52.8 \cdot \sqrt{94.25} = 52.8 \cdot 9.71 \approx 513 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2}
$$

> Convert to MPa·m^(1/2): $K_I \approx 16.2$ MPa·m^(1/2). Compared with $K_{IC} \approx 80$ MPa·m^(1/2) for cast steel → **no brittle fracture**, but fatigue crack must be repaired.

### Step 3 — Repair Decision
- **Crack depth 30 mm in 60 mm section = 50% of thickness** → **insert plate renewal** of the bracket arm root.
- **Renewal thickness** = $t_{original}$ (since not corroded) = **60 mm insert, 600 mm long, on both sides**.

### Step 4 — Welding Procedure
- Pre-heat to **150 °C** (low-CE cast steel).
- Groove angle **60°, root face 2 mm**.
- Multi-pass SMAW, low hydrogen.
- **PWHT at 600 °C for 1 hour**.
- NDT: 100% MPI + UT post-repair.
- Re-bore bearing to original diameter, **scrap bearing, fit new white-metal liner**.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Repair vs. Renewal)

| Component | Defect Type | Wastage/Size | Action | Class Survey Trigger |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|--------|----------------------|
| Stem | General wastage | <25% of rule t | Grind & coat | Annual |
| Stem | Local pit >15% of t | Any depth | Insert renewal | Special Survey |
| Stem | Crack | Any length | Weld repair + NDT | Special Survey |
| Stern Frame | Sole-piece crack | <50% t | Weld repair with preheat | Special Survey |
| Stern Frame | Propeller-post crack | >50% t | Full renewal | Special Survey |
| Shaft Bracket | Bearing wear | c > 1.5× clear | Re-bore + new liner | Annual |
| Shaft Bracket | Arm crack | Any | Weld repair + NDT | Special Survey |
| Shaft Bracket | Arm through-crack | Full depth | Renewal of bracket | Special Survey |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Location of Components on a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBow[BOW EXTREME] --> nodeStem[STEM ASSEMBLY]
    nodeStem --> nodeKeel[KEEL PLATE]
    nodeKeel --> nodeBottom[OUTER BOTTOM]
    nodeBottom --> nodeSide[SIDE SHELL]
    nodeSide --> nodeSheerstr[SHELLSTR PLATING]
    nodeKeel --> nodeSole[STERN FRAME SOLE PIECE]
    nodeSole --> nodeSternBody[STERN FRAME BODY OR PROPELLER POST]
    nodeSternBody --> nodeRudder[RUDDER WITH GUDGEONS]
    nodeSternBody --> nodeBrk[SHAFT BRACKET P OR A TYPE]
    nodeBrk --> nodeShaft[PROPELLER SHAFT TAIL]
    nodeShaft --> nodeProp[PROPELLER]
    classDef critical fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef support fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000000,color:#000000;
    class nodeStem,nodeSternBody,nodeBrk critical;
    class nodeKeel,nodeSide,nodeRudder,nodeProp support;
```

## 4.2 Repair Decision Flow Chart — Stem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[START INSPECTION OF STEM] --> nodeClean[Clean and Blast Stem Surface]
    nodeClean --> nodeUT[UT Thickness Gauging at Grid Points]
    nodeUT --> nodePitCheck{Any Local Pit More Than 15 percent of t?}
    nodePitCheck --> nodeYes1[PIT FOUND]
    nodePitCheck --> nodeNo1[NO PIT]
    nodeNo1 --> nodeGenCheck{General Wastage More Than 25 percent?}
    nodeGenCheck --> nodeNo2[WITHIN LIMITS]
    nodeGenCheck --> nodeYes2[EXCESSIVE]
    nodeYes1 --> nodeInsert[INSERT PLATE RENEWAL]
    nodeInsert --> nodeInsertStep[Determine t_ren equal to max t_rule t_original]
    nodeInsertStep --> nodeInsertStep2[Weld with low hydrogen process preheat 100 to 150 C]
    nodeInsertStep2 --> nodeNDT1[NDT MPI UT After Repair]
    nodeNDT1 --> nodeDone1[CLOSE UP]
    nodeYes2 --> nodeRenew[FULL STEM RENEWAL]
    nodeRenew --> nodeRenewNDT[NDT and Class Surveyor Witness]
    nodeRenewNDT --> nodeDone1
    nodeNo2 --> nodeDone1
```

## 4.3 Stern Frame Crack Repair Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS0[DISCOVER CRACK ON STERN FRAME] --> nodeS1[MPI to Map Crack Tips]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2[Drill Stop Holes Diameter 8 mm Min]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Air Arc Gouge to Sound Metal]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Grind U Groove Angle 60 deg Root Face 2 mm]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Preheat to 100 to 150 C]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[SMAW Weld Using Low Hydrogen Electrode]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[PWHT at 595 to 620 C If Class Requires]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[Final MPI and UT]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9[Class Surveyor Witness and Sign Off]
```

## 4.4 Shaft Bracket Repair Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[Shaft Bracket Bearing Housing] --> nodeA2[White Metal Liner]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Sealing Ring Simplex Type]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Cooling Water Inlet]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA5[Bracket Arm Root Welded to Hull]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6[Boss Through Which Shaft Passes]
    nodeA6 --> nodeA7[Lip Seal and Drain]
    nodeA1 -.-> nodeN1[Repair Action A: Re Bore and Re Bed Liner]
    nodeA1 -.-> nodeN2[Repair Action B: Weld Repair of Arm Crack]
    nodeA5 -.-> nodeN3[Repair Action C: Insert Plate at Root]
    nodeN2 --> nodeN4[Pre Heat and PWHT as Cast Steel]
    nodeN3 --> nodeN4
    classDef repair fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#000000,color:#000000;
    class nodeN1,nodeN2,nodeN3,nodeN4 repair;
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three common defects encountered in the stern frame of a ship and the inspection method used to detect each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Defect | Inspection Method |
|---|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Crack at fillet weld of sole piece to propeller post | **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** |
| 2 | Ovality or wear of the propeller-shaft bore | **Bore-gauge dimensional measurement** + visual |
| 3 | Corrosion wastage of sole piece | **Ultrasonic Thickness (UT) gauging** |
| 4 | Loosening of keel-stern-frame bolts | **Tap-test / hammer-test** + visual |
| 5 | Erosion of gudgeon bearing | **Visual + dial gauge clearance check** |

*(Award 1 mark per correctly matched defect-method pair, max 3.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the IACS wastage acceptance criteria for plate renewal during ship hull inspection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **General wastage**: If remaining thickness falls **below 75% of rule scantling** ($t_{rem} < 0.75 \cdot t_{rule}$), the plate **must be renewed** — *(1 mark)*.
2. **Localized wastage**: If local wastage **exceeds 15% of original thickness** ($w_{local} > 0.15 \cdot t_{orig}$), an **insert plate renewal** is required — *(1 mark)*.
3. **Pitting**: If the **sum of pit depths over an area > 1 m² exceeds 10% of plate thickness**, renewal is mandatory — *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **constructional features of a ship's stern frame**. Mention the functions of each part. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for the repair of a cracked sole piece** of a stern frame. List the welding parameters, preheat temperature, and post-weld heat-treatment conditions as per IACS / IRS rules. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **causes of damage to the stem plate** and explain how the **renewal thickness** is determined when wastage exceeds allowable limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **inspection, defects, and repair of a propeller shaft bracket (P-bracket)**, including bearing re-bedding procedure. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A (a) — Stern Frame Construction (7 marks)

A **stern frame** is a heavy **cast-steel** (or welded fabricated) assembly fitted at the aft end of the hull. Main components and functions:

1. **Sole Piece** (horizontal arm, welded to keel plate) — Transmits propeller thrust into the keel. *(1 mark)*
2. **Propeller Post** (vertical post) — Terminates the keel upward; supports rudder via **gudgeons**. *(1 mark)*
3. **Body / Boss** (central tube) — Carries the propeller-shaft and forms the stern aperture. *(1 mark)*
4. **Gudgeons / Pintle Holes** — Hinge points for the rudder pintles. *(1 mark)*
5. **Rudder Horn** (in some designs) — Alternative to gudgeons; provides upper pintle support. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Drain Plug / Boss Cap** — For inspection of the shaft bore interior. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Bilge keel connections** (where fitted) — For roll-reduction. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Sketch** — to be drawn showing propeller post, sole piece, gudgeons, boss, rudder pintle. *(1 mark)*

#### Solution to Question A (b) — Sole-Piece Crack Repair (7 marks)

**Step-by-step procedure:**

1. **Docking the vessel** — full grounding on blocks so that propeller shaft is accessible. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Pre-repair inspection** — MPI / dye-penetrant to determine exact crack extent. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Drill stop holes** of diameter $\ge 8$ mm at each crack tip. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Air-carbon-arc gouge** to remove crack metal to sound parent metal. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Prepare U-groove** with included angle **60°, root face 2 mm**. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Pre-heat** cast steel to **$T_{pre} = 100 - 150 °C$** using resistance heating mats; monitor with contact pyrometers. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Weld using SMAW** with **low-hydrogen electrodes** (e.g., AWS E7018 or equivalent), baked at 300-350 °C for 1 hour before use. Maintain interpass temperature $\le 230 °C$. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Post-weld grinding** to smooth profile, with reinforcement $\ge 1$ mm above parent surface. *(0.5 mark)*
9. **PWHT (if required by class)** at **$595 - 620 °C$**, soak **1 hour per 25 mm of thickness**, slow cool to ambient. *(1 mark)*
10. **Final NDT** — 100% **MPI + UT** of the repair groove + 100 mm of parent metal on either side. *(0.5 mark)*
11. **Class surveyor attendance** mandatory at all hot-work stages; **welding procedure qualification** as per **IACS UR Z17 / Z23** must be on board. *(0.5 mark)*
12. **Documentation** — repair plan, weld log, NDT reports, surveyor endorsement. *(0.5 mark)*

#### Solution to Question B (a) — Stem Plate Damage & Renewal Thickness (7 marks)

**Causes of damage** (3 marks):
- **Collision/contact** — berthing, allision, anchor drop.
- **Slamming fatigue** — repeated wave impact at forward sections.
- **Corrosion** — concentrated at boottop (splash zone) and inside hawse pipe.
- **Grounding** — longitudinal scratching, gouging, denting.
- **Fire / explosion damage** in way of forward bunker tanks.

**Renewal thickness determination** (4 marks):
- Conduct **UT thickness gauging** at a defined grid pattern across the stem.
- Compute **average wastage** $\bar{w}$ and **maximum local wastage** $w_{max}$.
- Apply acceptance criteria:
  - General: if $t_{rem} < 0.75 \cdot t_{rule}$ → renewal.
  - Local: if $w_{max} > 0.15 \cdot t_{orig}$ → insert renewal in that zone.
- **Renewal thickness**: $t_{ren} = \max(t_{rule}, t_{orig})$.
- For transitional inserts at zone boundaries, use **taper ratio 1:3 minimum**.
- Welds to be **butt welds** at ends, with **overlap $\ge 4t + 50$ mm**.
- Carry out **NDT (MPI/UT)** post-repair; obtain **class surveyor endorsement**.

#### Solution to Question B (b) — Shaft Bracket Repair (7 marks)

**Inspection** (1 mark):
- Visual + dimensional check of bearing clearance (using dial gauge / feeler).
- **Bore-gauge** measurement of boss for ovality.
- **MPI / UT** of arm root for cracks.
- **Endoscopy** of the internal drain channel.

**Defects** (2 marks):
- White-metal liner wear, scoring, flaking.
- Cracks at arm root or boss/arm junction.
- Corrosion of sealing ring, water leakage past Simplex seal.
- Loosening of chock-fastened or bolted connections to hull.

**Repair** (3 marks):
- **Bearing re-bedding**: Strip old white-metal by heating to ~250 °C, clean bore, apply **tin-based babbitt** by centrifugal casting or static pour, machine to **diameter = shaft diameter + (1 to 1.5) × design clearance**.
- **Arm crack repair**: Air-arc gouge → U-groove → pre-heat 150 °C → SMAW with E7018 → PWHT 600 °C → MPI/UT.
- **Seal replacement**: Renew rubber sealing rings, repack Simplex stuffing, hydrostatically test for leaks.
- **Final**: Class surveyor trials — shaft rotation, temperature rise on bearing, vibration survey.

**Documentation** (1 mark): Class forms CSR-3-1-1, repair log, NDT reports, surveyor sign-off.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the 75% / 15% rule** — most common omission; examiners expect this verbatim.
> 2. **Omitting pre-heat and PWHT temperatures** for cast-steel welds — you lose at least 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing P-bracket vs A-bracket** — P has one arm, A has two; mixing them up costs marks.
> 4. **Skipping NDT methods** — always mention **MPI for surface cracks + UT for sub-surface + dimensional measurement for bores**.
> 5. **Not stating Class Surveyor attendance** — hot work on these critical components **must** have class attendance; missing this = 1 mark lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stem**: foremost structural plate assembly; transmits forward impact loads to keel.
- **Stern frame**: cast/fabricated assembly with sole piece, propeller post, boss, gudgeons; supports propeller & rudder.
- **Shaft bracket**: external support for tail shaft; P-type (single arm) or A-type (two arms); carries white-metal bearing.
- **Wastage rule**: $t_{rem} < 0.75 t_{rule}$ → renewal; local pit $> 15\%$ of $t_{orig}$ → insert.
- **Renewal thickness**: $t_{ren} = \max(t_{rule},\ t_{orig})$.
- **Weld sizing**: leg size $s \ge 0.7 t_{plate}$; insert overlap $L_{ov} \ge 4t + 50$ mm.
- **Cast-steel welding**: **pre-heat 100-150 °C**, **low-hydrogen electrodes (E7018-class)**, **PWHT 595-620 °C / 1 h per 25 mm**, **interpass $\le 230$ °C**.
- **NDT**: **MPI** for surface cracks; **UT** for sub-surface & thickness; **dimensional** for bores; **endoscopy** for inaccessible drains.
- **Class society**: All hot work and renewals on these components are subject to **Class Surveyor attendance** and **IACS UR Z17 / Z23** procedural requirements.
- **Shaft bearing clearance**: ~0.001 × shaft diameter (~1 mm per metre) for diesel-driven vessels.
- **Fillet weld at sole piece / propeller post junction** is the **most crack-prone** weld on the stern frame.
- **Boottop region of stem** is the **most corrosion-prone** zone — always inspect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment

# Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Propeller shaft sealing equipment* refers to the assembly of devices fitted at the point where the propeller shaft penetrates the vessel's hull (typically at the stern tube entry or exit) to **prevent the ingress of sea water into the hull** and simultaneously **prevent the leakage of lubricating oil** from the internal stern tube bearing system into the sea.

In any ocean-going vessel, the propeller shaft must rotate continuously while passing through a stationary hull. This intersection is one of the most critical engineering challenges in marine architecture because:
- External pressure (sea water) is constantly pushing inward.
- Internal pressure (lubricating oil) is constantly pushing outward.
- The shaft is rotating, making static sealing impossible.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bathroom Faucet**
> Think of a propeller shaft sealing arrangement like the **rubber washer inside a leaking bathroom tap**. The tap spindle rotates or moves up and down (just like the shaft), and the rubber washer (like a packing ring or mechanical seal face) provides a tight boundary that stops water from leaking out. In a ship, however, the leak can go in **both directions** — sea water *in*, and oil *out* — which is why the seal is so much more sophisticated than a simple washer.

### Primary Functions of Shaft Sealing Equipment

1. **Sea Water Exclusion**: Prevents corrosive and pressurized sea water from entering the hull through the stern gland area.
2. **Oil Retention**: Prevents the loss of expensive lubricating oil used in the stern tube bearings.
3. **Environmental Compliance**: Stops oil pollution of the sea, which is a violation of **MARPOL Annex I** regulations.
4. **Friction Management**: Allows the shaft to rotate freely without excessive wear on stationary components.
5. **Bearing Support**: Often integrated with the after-most stern tube bearing (commonly white metal, lignum vitae, or synthetic rubber).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Society Rule Highlight (ABS / DNV / Lloyd's)**
> All sealing arrangements must comply with **SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 26-1** for oil pollution prevention, with **maximum permissible oil leakage rate of 0.05 litres per nautical mile** (or 0.5 litres per hour, whichever is greater) as per IMO Resolution A.586(14).

### Physical & Operating Parameters (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Stern tube internal pressure | **0.2 – 0.5 bar** (gravity head) |
| Sea water external pressure at stern | **1.0 – 3.0 bar** (depth dependent) |
| Shaft rotational speed | **80 – 250 rpm** (merchant vessels) |
| Shaft surface velocity at seal | **5 – 15 m/s** |
| Permissible oil leakage | **≤ 0.5 L/hr** (MARPOL limit) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment

There are three principal types used in modern commercial shipping, each with a distinct operating principle.

### A. Conventional Stuffing Box (Packing Gland)

The oldest and most traditional system. It uses **braided packing rings** (typically flax, asbestos-free synthetic fibre, or graphite-impregnated PTFE) compressed axially by a gland follower. Sea water is deliberately fed into the packing to provide lubrication and cooling.

**Construction (in order from sea to engine room):**
1. **Tungsten or stainless-steel liner** (white metal lined) — provides smooth shaft surface.
2. **3 to 5 packing rings** — usually 3 rings, each 10–15 mm thick.
3. **Lantern ring** (centre ring) — distributes cooling/lubricating sea water.
4. **Gland follower** (stuffing box gland) — applies axial compression.
5. **Gland nuts and studs** — provides the compressive force.
6. **Drain and supply connections** — for sea water feed and leak-off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Principle — Intuition**
> Imagine squeezing a wet rope around a spinning stick. As you tighten the gland, the rope's fibres press against the stick, but the water in the middle keeps everything slippery. The lantern ring acts like a "donut" that pumps cooling water to the centre of the rope, preventing the packing from burning up due to friction.

**Key Design Feature**: There is a **controlled leak** — a small, deliberate stream of mixed water and oil drains to a leak-off tank. This is monitored by **Class Society inspectors** to ensure the rate remains within legal limits.

### B. Mechanical Face Seal (Simplex / Duplex)

The modern replacement for stuffing boxes. Uses two precisely lapped, highly-flat faces — one rotating with the shaft, one stationary — pressed together by spring force or hydraulic pressure.

| Sub-Type | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Simplex Seal** | Single set of faces; uses sea water as lubricant. |
| **Duplex Seal** | Two sets of faces in series; uses **seal oil** as the barrier fluid between them. |

**Key Components of a Mechanical Seal:**
- **Rotating face** (silicon carbide / tungsten carbide) — fixed to shaft.
- **Stationary face** (carbon graphite) — mounted in the gland housing.
- **Secondary seals** (O-rings / bellows) — prevent leakage around faces.
- **Springs** (single or multi-spring) — provide face-closing force.
- **Seal oil system** (duplex only) — supplies clean oil at slightly higher pressure than sea water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept — Seal Oil Pressure**
> In a duplex seal, seal oil pressure is maintained at **0.3 – 0.5 bar above sea water pressure**. This ensures any leak is *outward* (oil to sea), never *inward* (sea water to oil). This is verified daily by the watch-keeping engineer.

### C. Lip Seal / Radial Shaft Seal

A simple elastomer (nitrile rubber, Viton, or PTFE) sealing ring with a flexible lip that contacts the shaft. Used in smaller vessels, tugs, and as a **secondary seal** in some systems.

### D. Water-Lubricated Stern Tube Systems

Modern environmentally-preferred alternative that **eliminates oil entirely**:
