The Hull Inspector's checklist for safety devices falls into **four functional families**:

| # | Family | Examples | Governing Source |
|---|--------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Life-Saving Appliances (LSA)** | Lifeboats, life rafts, lifejackets, immersion suits, EPIRBs, SARTs, line-throwing appliances, HRUs | SOLAS Ch. III, LSA Code |
| 2 | **Fire-Fighting Appliances** | Fire extinguishers, fire hoses, nozzles, breathing apparatus, fireman's outfits, fixed CO₂ systems, fire-fighters' axes | SOLAS Ch. II-2, FSS Code |
| 3 | **Damage Control Fittings (Hull-Specific)** | Watertight doors, sliding hatches, side scuttles, air pipes, ventilators, sounding pipes, non-return valves, collision bulkhead penetrations | SOLAS Ch. II-1, Classification Society Rules |
| 4 | **Navigational Safety Equipment (Hull-related)** | Navigation lights, shapes, sound signals, GMDSS batteries, deckmarkings | SOLAS Ch. V, COLREGS |

---

## 1.3 What are "Safety Plans"?

### Formal Definition
A **Safety Plan** is a **permanent, laminated, accurately scaled drawing or printed document** that is posted in a conspicuous location on board the vessel to direct the crew during emergencies. These plans are *living documents* — they must be updated whenever the ship's structure, equipment, or crew complement changes, and the most recent revision must bear a **stamp/signature** from the **Master** and the **Classification Society surveyor** where applicable.

### The Five Statutory Plans

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every seagoing vessel of **≥ 500 GT** (and progressively smaller vessels under various flag rules) must carry the following five plans, and the Hull Surveyor must verify their presence and currency at every survey:

1. **Fire Control Plan (Fire Safety Plan)** — A general arrangement drawing showing fire stations, extinguishers, fire-detection zones, ventilation shutoffs, and means of escape.
2. **Damage Control Plan (DCP) / Damage Control Booklet** — A booklet or set of plans showing watertight compartments, cross-flooding arrangements, pumping connections, and stability information.
3. **Muster List / Station Bill** — A posted list assigning each crew member to a specific emergency station.
4. **Escape / Egress Plan** — Shows all escape routes, exits, and emergency lighting.
5. **Oil Pollution Emergency Plan (SOPEP)** or **Ship-to-Ship Transfer (STS) Plan** — MARPOL Annex I related.

> [!NOTE]
> For a Hull Inspector, the **Fire Control Plan** and **Damage Control Plan** are the two most frequently scrutinized documents, because they directly relate to the structural integrity and watertight subdivision of the vessel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical "Why" — Regulatory Pyramid

To understand *why* these safety devices and plans exist, the student must visualize a four-tiered **regulatory pyramid**:

**Tier 1 (Apex) — International Conventions**
- **SOLAS 1974** (as amended) — Operated by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**. It is the *supreme law* of maritime safety and is binding on all flag states that have ratified it.
- **MARPOL 73/78** — For pollution prevention.
- **STCW** — Crew training.

**Tier 2 — Codes Resolving the Convention**
- **LSA Code** (Life-Saving Appliances Code) — Resolves SOLAS Chapter III.
- **FSS Code** (Fire Safety Systems Code) — Resolves SOLAS Chapter II-2.
- **FTP Code** (Fire Test Procedures) — For material approvals.

**Tier 3 — Flag State Implementation**
- Each country translates the Convention into its own merchant shipping act (e.g., Indian Merchant Shipping Act, UK MSA, US Code of Federal Regulations 46 CFR).

**Tier 4 — Classification Society Rules**
- Non-governmental bodies (IRS, Lloyd's, ABS, DNV) that verify compliance during *statutory surveys on behalf of the flag state*, and also apply their own *class rules* which may be *more stringent* than SOLAS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for the Hull Inspector?** Because when a defect is found, the question becomes: *Is it a SOLAS non-conformance, a Class Rule non-conformance, or both?* The answer dictates whether the ship can continue trading or must be detained.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How Devices Are Verified During a Hull Survey

The Hull Surveyor follows a **6-stage logical audit loop** for every safety device encountered:

1. **Existence** — Is the device physically present at its designated station? (e.g., 8 fire extinguishers required; are all 8 there?)
2. **Identity** — Does the type/capacity match the approved *Fire Control Plan*? (e.g., 9 L foam extinguisher for machinery spaces, not a 6 L one.)
3. **Condition** — Is it serviceable? (No corrosion, no broken seals, valid hydrostatic test date, no paint blocking moving parts.)
4. **Marking** — Is it correctly marked with class/flag symbols, capacity, test date, and station number?
5. **Accessibility** — Is it unobstructed? (Often a finding: paint lockers blocking lifeboat embarkation routes.)
6. **Documentation** — Does the **Record of Equipment (Form E / Form R)** and the **Class Register** reflect this device with a valid certificate?

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Numerical Anchors for Safety Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hull Inspection is a *non-computational* subject, but several **standard numerical thresholds** must be memorized. These appear in **Part A (3-mark) questions** very frequently.

| # | Parameter | Standard Value | Source / Notes |
|---|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Lifeboat / liferaft capacity (persons) | $\geq 100\%$ of total persons on board (POB) | SOLAS III/4 |
| 2 | Liferaft service interval | Replace or re-certify every **12 months** (or per maker) | SOLAS III/20 |
| 3 | Hydrostatic Release Unit (HRU) expiry | Replace every **2 years** from date of manufacture | SOLAS III/20 |
| 4 | Fire extinguisher hydrostatic test | **Every 5 years** for CO₂; **Every 12 years** for foam/dry powder (varies) | Class rules |
| 5 | EPIRB battery replacement | Every **5 years** (and annual shore-based maintenance) | SOLAS IV/13 |
| 6 | SART battery | Operational life **≥ 96 hours** in standby, **≥ 8 hours** active | SOLAS IV/14 |
| 7 | Lifeboat release & retrieval system | Tested at Annual Survey; load test of falls every **5 years** | SOLAS III/20 |
| 8 | Survival craft dropping speed | Not to exceed **1.3 m/s** at light condition, **0.4 m/s** with full load | LSA Code Ch. IV |
| 9 | Minimum muster drill frequency | **Once every month** (fire & abandon ship) | SOLAS III/19 |
| 10 | Number of fireman's outfits | **At least 2** (for ships of any size), more based on GT | SOLAS II-2/10 |
| 11 | Watertight door test interval | Operated & tested at **every voyage** (weekly on passenger ships) | SOLAS II-1/15 |
| 12 | Number of Fire Control Plans | **Permanently stored** in a prominent location + **copy in a sealed container** painted red, outside the deckhouse | SOLAS II-2/15 |
| 13 | SOLAS lifejacket light battery life | $\geq 8$ hours continuous operation | LSA Code |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic for Dropping Speed:** The survival craft must not exceed **1.3 m/s** (meters per second) when lowering the *fully loaded craft* and **0.4 m/s** when lowering the *empty craft*. The Hull Surveyor verifies this via the **dynamic load test** carried out every 5 years.

---

## 2.4 Why These Standards Exist — Real-World Engineering Utility

The numerical thresholds above are not arbitrary. Each one corresponds to a **documented loss-of-life event** in maritime history:

- The **HRU 2-year expiry** came from the *MV Herald of Free Enterprise* (1987) disaster, where the HRU failed because of expired pyrotechnics.
- The **lifeboat release hook (on-load release)** safety improvements came from accidents where craft fell during recovery, killing crew — leading to **MSC.1/Circ.1392** amendments.
- The **fire control plan in a sealed red container** outside the deckhouse is so that in case of fire, the *backup* plan is accessible when the bridge plan is destroyed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Examiners often ask *"Why is the spare Fire Control Plan stored in a watertight container painted red on the outside of the deckhouse?"* The answer is: **because in a fire, the bridge may be inaccessible, and the red container is internationally recognized as emergency equipment; the waterproofing protects it from fire-fighting water spray.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Derivation — Lifeboat / Liferaft Capacity Requirement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most common 3-mark calculation in Hull Inspection is the **number of lifeboat / liferaft persons** required. The student must be able to derive the required number from a given ship complement.

### Given Problem Statement
A Bulk Carrier has the following crew and personnel complement:
- Master: 1
- Officers: 6
- Ratings (deck & engine): 14
- Cadets: 2
- Total Persons on Board (POB) $N_{POB} = 23$

Find: **(a)** Total number of survival craft persons required, **(b)** Minimum number of lifeboats if the vessel has a single-side launching arrangement.

---

### Step 1: Establish the SOLAS 100% Rule
SOLAS Chapter III, Regulation 4 states that the aggregate capacity of lifeboats plus liferafts on each side of the ship must be sufficient for **at least 200% of the total number of persons on board** (i.e., **100% on one side + 100% on the other side**).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{required, total}} &= 2 \times N_{POB} \\
&= 2 \times 23 \\
&= 46 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The factor of **2** arises because SOLAS assumes the ship is listing or the wind is on one side, and survival craft must be available on the *opposite, sheltered side*. So you need **two full complements**, one for each side.

---

### Step 2: Allocation to Lifeboats (100% Requirement)
SOLAS requires that lifeboats alone must accommodate **100% of the total persons on board**. The liferafts act as the *second 100%* on the opposite side.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{lifeboat}} &= 1.0 \times N_{POB} \\
&= 1.0 \times 23 \\
&= 23 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the ship carries **two lifeboats**, each rated at say **25 persons** (next standard size), the lifeboat provision is $2 \times 25 = 50$ persons, which exceeds the required 23. ✓

---

### Step 3: Allocation to Liferafts (Second 100% Requirement)
The liferafts collectively must also hold at least **100% of the persons on board**, distributed symmetrically on port and starboard sides.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{liferaft, total}} &\geq 1.0 \times N_{POB} \\
&= 23 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

If using standard 25-person inflatable liferafts, the vessel would need **two liferafts** (one each side) of 25-person capacity, totalling 50 persons. ✓

---

### Step 4: Final Verification — 200% Aggregate

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{aggregate}} &= C_{\text{lifeboat}} + C_{\text{liferaft}} \\
&= 23 + 23 \\
&= 46 \text{ persons} \\
\text{Provided} &= 50 + 50 = 100 \text{ persons} \\
\text{Surplus} &= 100 - 46 = 54 \text{ persons} \quad \text{(compliant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — The "2-1-1 Rule":** *Total = 2 × POB; Lifeboat = 1 × POB; Liferaft = 1 × POB.*

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Damage Control Plan (DCP) Compartment Audit

The Hull Surveyor audits the **Damage Control Plan** by walking the ship and matching the **compartments shown on the plan** with the **actual ship layout**. The analytical matrix below is the **audit template**:

| Plan Element | What the Surveyor Checks | Common Findings |
|--------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| Compartment numbering | All compartments have unique alphanumeric IDs (e.g., A-1, A-2) | Renumbered after refit, but old plan not updated |
| Watertight bulkhead location | Watertight bulkheads correspond to IACS S-21 standard | Unauthorized penetration through a bulkhead |
| Cross-flooding valves | Cross-flooding valves listed match actual fitted valves | Valves replaced with wrong type |
| Sounding pipe locations | Sounding pipes exist at the actual low points of compartments | Sounding pipe plugged or removed |
| Pumping connections | Emergency pump connections match class-approved diagram | Connections mismatched |
| Stability information booklet | Damage stability booklet available, signed by Master | Booklet from previous vessel still on board |

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Fire Extinguisher Hydrostatic Test Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a frequent **3-mark Part A question**: *Explain the hydrostatic test cycle for portable CO₂ fire extinguishers.*

### Step 1: Reference Standard
Class rules (e.g., IRS Rules for Ships, Chapter on Fire Appliances) and the **FSS Code** require that all portable CO₂ fire extinguishers undergo a **hydrostatic pressure test** at the test pressure of the cylinder.

### Step 2: Frequency of Test

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{test, CO}_2} &= 5 \text{ years} \quad \text{(typical for CO}_2\text{)} \\
T_{\text{test, foam/dry powder}} &= 12 \text{ months visual, 5 years hydrostatic} \\
T_{\text{test, water/glycol}} &= 12 \text{ months full discharge, 5 years hydrostatic}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Test Pressure Calculation

For a CO₂ extinguisher with a working pressure $P_w$ of **58 bar** (typical high-pressure CO₂), the hydrostatic test pressure is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{test}} &= 1.5 \times P_w \\
&= 1.5 \times 58 \\
&= 87 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The **1.5 factor** is the standard safety margin to verify cylinder integrity without bursting the cylinder. The extinguisher is pressurized with water (not gas) to 87 bar for a minimum dwell time of **30 seconds**, and any permanent deformation or leakage is cause for rejection.

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytic Comparison — LSA vs FSS vs Damage Control Fittings

| Attribute | Life-Saving Appliances (LSA) | Fire Safety Systems (FSS) | Damage Control Fittings |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| SOLAS Chapter | III | II-2 | II-1 |
| Implementing Code | LSA Code | FSS Code | IACS Unified Requirements (S-21) |
| Surveyor Verifies | Capacity, HRU, pyrotechnics, embarkation | Extinguisher pressure, hose condition, fixed system | Watertight door, scuttle, sounding pipe |
| Test Frequency | Annual + 5-year load test | Annual + 5/12-year pressure test | Weekly operational + Annual full test |
| Typical 3-mark Q | "What is the HRU expiry?" | "What is the CO₂ test pressure?" | "Where is the DCP posted?" |
| Detention Risk | High (HRU expired) | Medium (extinguisher empty) | Low–Medium (door gaskets worn) |

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison matrix is the **highest-yield 1-page revision tool** for the KTU Module 3 exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Q: What is a Hydrostatic Release Unit (HRU)? State its inspection requirements under SOLAS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Hydrostatic Release Unit (HRU)** is a device fitted to a **liferaft** that automatically releases and inflates the liferaft when the vessel sinks to a depth of approximately **1.5 m to 4 m** below the waterline. The unit operates by hydrostatic pressure: the increasing water pressure at depth triggers a spring-loaded release mechanism that pulls the painter line, which in turn activates the liferaft's CO₂ inflation cylinder.

**Inspection requirements under SOLAS Chapter III, Regulation 20:**
- The HRU must be **replaced at intervals not exceeding 2 years** from the date of manufacture, regardless of apparent condition (1 mark).
- The expiry date must be clearly stamped on the unit and verified by the surveyor during annual survey (1 mark).
- A defective or expired HRU constitutes a **detainable deficiency** under PSC (Port State Control) (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the *liferaft service interval (12 months)* with the *HRU expiry (2 years)*. The two are different. The liferaft itself is serviced annually; the HRU is replaced every 2 years.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**Q: List any six items that must be inspected/verified on board a vessel's Fire Control Plan during a hull survey.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. The plan must be **permanently exhibited** in a prominent and accessible location (typically the bridge).
2. A **duplicate copy** must be stored in a **sealed, red-coloured, weatherproof container** outside the deckhouse.
3. The plan must show the **location of every fire station, fire-extinguishing appliance, and access to different compartments**.
4. The plan must indicate the **position of the fire-fighter's outfits, breathing apparatus, and emergency escape routes**.
5. The plan must be **dated, signed, and stamped** by the Classification Society approving authority and the Master.
6. The plan must reflect the **current configuration of the vessel** — any refit or modification must trigger a re-issue.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B offers an **internal choice** between two questions. The student must answer **one** of the two. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **classification, location, and inspection criteria** for **Life-Saving Appliances (LSA)** on board a merchant vessel as per SOLAS Chapter III and the LSA Code. Illustrate with a sketch of a typical lifeboat launching arrangement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the number of lifeboats and liferafts required for a Bulk Carrier with the following particulars:
- Persons on board (POB) = 30
- The vessel has **single-side launching** capability
- Available lifeboat capacity = **50 persons each**
- Available liferaft capacity = **25 persons each**

Show all steps clearly.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a)

**Classification of LSA (3 marks):**
The LSA Code divides life-saving appliances into **two functional groups**:
1. **Survival craft** — Lifeboats (totally enclosed, partially enclosed, free-fall) and Liferafts (inflatable, rigid).
2. **Personal safety devices** — Lifejackets, immersion suits, anti-exposure suits, and life-buoys.

**Location Criteria (2 marks):**
- Lifeboats must be located such that they can be launched **within 30 minutes** under controlled conditions.
- Liferafts must be stowed on the **deck or in dedicated lockers** with the painter permanently attached to a weak link on the HRU.
- Lifejackets must be available for **every person on board**, plus additional units for the watchkeepers on duty.

**Inspection Criteria (2 marks):**
- **Existence, marking, condition, and validity** of certificates (as per the 6-stage audit loop).
- Liferaft annual service certificate must be on board; HRU expiry must be checked.
- Lifeboat release gear must undergo a **load test every 5 years**.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b)

**Step 1: Establish Total Capacity Required (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total required}} &= 2 \times \text{POB} \\
&= 2 \times 30 \\
&= 60 \text{ persons (100% on each side)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Lifeboat Provision (2 marks):**
SOLAS requires that **lifeboats alone must provide 100% on each side**, but in single-side launching ships, a combined solution using liferafts on the opposite side is allowed.

If the ship carries **one lifeboat of 50 persons** (covers one side) and **one liferaft of 25 persons + one liferaft of 25 persons = 50 persons** on the other side, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Side 1: Lifeboat} &= 50 \text{ persons} \\
\text{Side 2: 2 Liferafts} &= 25 + 25 = 50 \text{ persons} \\
\text{Aggregate} &= 50 + 50 = 100 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final Verification (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Required Aggregate} &= 60 \text{ persons} \\
\text{Provided Aggregate} &= 100 \text{ persons} \\
\text{Surplus} &= 40 \text{ persons} \\
\text{Compliance} &= \text{ACHIEVED (compliant with 200% rule)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel requires a minimum of **1 lifeboat (50-person)** and **2 liferafts (25-person each)** to be SOLAS-compliant.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Damage Control Plan (DCP)** required to be carried on board a merchant vessel. List the **six elements** that a DCP must contain, and explain the role of the Hull Surveyor in verifying each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CO₂ fire extinguisher cylinder has a working pressure of **58 bar**. Calculate the **hydrostatic test pressure** to be applied, and describe the test procedure including the dwell time, medium, and rejection criteria as per IRS Class Rules.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):** A Damage Control Plan is a **Class-approved document** that contains all information necessary for the crew to localize flooding, fight fire, and maintain watertight integrity in an emergency.

**Six Elements of the DCP (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **General particulars** of the ship (GT, LOA, breadth, POB).
2. **Watertight compartment layout** showing all bulkheads and their tightness classification.
3. **Cross-flooding and pumping diagram** showing piping and valves.
4. **Damage stability information** including the survivable floodable length.
5. **List of watertight doors, scuttles, valves, and sounding pipes** with their station numbers.
6. **Crew damage control duties** (muster list, station bill).

**Hull Surveyor's Verification Role (3 marks):**
- The surveyor **walks the ship** and physically checks each fitting listed in the DCP (1 mark).
- Cross-references the **compartment numbers on the plan with the actual markings on the ship** (1 mark).
- Verifies that the DCP is the **most current revision**, signed by the Master and the Class surveyor (1 mark).

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b)

**Step 1: Hydrostatic Test Pressure Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{test}} &= 1.5 \times P_{\text{working}} \\
&= 1.5 \times 58 \text{ bar} \\
&= 87 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Test Procedure (2 marks):**
- The cylinder is **emptied of CO₂** and **disconnected** from its valve assembly.
- The cylinder is **filled with water** (the hydrostatic medium — *not gas*, because water is incompressible and safely reveals leaks).
- The water is pressurized using a **hydraulic pump** to **87 bar** gradually.
- The pressure is **held (dwelled) for a minimum of 30 seconds**.
- The cylinder is examined for **leakage, permanent deformation, bulging, or weeping at seams**.

**Step 3: Rejection Criteria (2 marks):**
- **Any drop in pressure** (above the pump's tolerance) during the dwell time = reject.
- **Any visible leakage** at welds, seams, or valve threads = reject.
- **Any permanent deformation** exceeding the elastic limit of the steel = reject.
- The cylinder is **stamped with the next test due date** and re-tagged; or it is **condemned and replaced** if it has failed the test.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT use the same formula for all extinguisher types.** The 1.5× factor is standard for CO₂; foam and dry powder cylinders have different test pressures and intervals. Always state the cylinder type.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse the 5-year hydrostatic test with the 12-month full discharge test.** These are *two separate* tests with different purposes.
> 3. **In Q.A(b), students often forget the "single-side launching" clause** and assume twin-side launching, which leads to wrong capacity requirements. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Do NOT confuse the liferaft service interval (12 months) with the HRU expiry (2 years).** This is the most common mistake in Part A.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Card — Pin This To Your Wall Before The Exam:**

- [x] **SOLAS Chapter III** governs Life-Saving Appliances; **Chapter II-2** governs Fire Safety; **Chapter II-1** governs Subdivision and Damage Stability.
- [x] **LSA Code, FSS Code, and FTP Code** are the three implementing codes most relevant to hull inspection.
- [x] The **2-1-1 Rule** for survival craft: Total capacity = **2 × POB**; Lifeboat = **1 × POB**; Liferaft = **1 × POB** (single-side launch).
- [x] **HRU expiry = 2 years**; **Liferaft service = 12 months**; **EPIRB battery = 5 years**.
- [x] **Fire Control Plan** = one on bridge + one in **sealed red container** outside the deckhouse.
- [x] **Damage Control Plan** must show compartments, WT bulkheads, cross-flooding, pumping, and damage stability.
- [x] **Hydrostatic test pressure** for CO₂ extinguishers = **1.5 × working pressure** (e.g., 1.5 × 58 = **87 bar**).
- [x] **Lifeboat release hook** must be tested annually; falls load-tested every **5 years**.
- [x] **Muster drill** = monthly; **abandon ship drill** = monthly; **fire drill** = monthly.
- [x] **Fireman's outfit** = minimum **2 sets**; each outfit = helmet, boots, gloves, axe, breathing apparatus, lifeline.
- [x] The **Hull Surveyor's 6-stage audit loop**: Existence → Identity → Condition → Marking → Accessibility → Documentation.
- [x] The **regulatory pyramid**: IMO Convention → Code → Flag State Act → Class Rule → Surveyor.
- [x] **Detainable deficiencies**: Expired HRU, empty fire extinguisher, missing Fire Control Plan, non-functional watertight door.
- [x] **Dropping speed** limit for survival craft: **1.3 m/s** (loaded) and **0.4 m/s** (empty).

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Examiner's Note:** For a 14-mark question, structure your answer with **clear headings**, **sub-bullets for each SOLAS reference**, and **at least one numerical example** where applicable. Examiners reward *structured, regulation-citing answers* over vague generalities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hull Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hull Preparation** is the systematic engineering process of treating the external and internal steel surfaces of a ship's hull to achieve a defined level of cleanliness, roughness, and chemical compatibility prior to the application of protective coatings (primers, anti-corrosives, anti-fouling systems) or prior to structural survey inspection. It encompasses mechanical, chemical, and thermal methods of removing mill scale, rust, old coatings, marine fouling, salt contaminants, oil, grease, and weld spatter, followed by the verification of the surface against established international standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> Hull preparation is the prerequisite engineering stage in dry-dock repair and new-building outfitting, in which the steel substrate is conditioned to a specific surface preparation grade (SP grade) as defined by **ISO 8501-1:2007** and **SSPC/NACE VIS 1**, ensuring optimum adhesion of subsequent paint systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Wall Painter Principle:**
> Imagine you want to repaint the iron gate of your house. If you simply paint over rust, dust, and chipped old paint, the new coat will peel off within weeks. A professional painter first scrapes off the rust, sands the surface to make it slightly rough ("tooth"), wipes away dust, and only then applies the primer. The ship hull is identical — except the "gate" is a multi-thousand-tonne structure submerged in seawater, and the "paint" is a multi-million-rupee anti-corrosive and anti-fouling system. **Hull preparation is the professional scraping and sanding of this massive gate.**

The marine environment is the most corrosive natural setting on Earth. Salt water, biological growth (barnacles, algae), UV radiation, and mechanical abrasion from berthing demand that the steel substrate be *engineered* — not just cleaned — before any coating can be relied upon for its service life of typically **5 to 7 years** before the next dry-dock.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Anchors (Industry-Standard):**
> - **Dry Film Thickness (DFT):** Coating thickness measured in **micrometers (µm)** or **mils** (1 mil = 25.4 µm).
> - **Surface Profile (Roughness):** Measured in **micrometers (µm)** — typically **50–100 µm** for marine epoxy primers.
> - **Surface Chloride Contamination Limit:** ≤ **50 mg/m²** (≈ 5 µg/cm²) for most anti-corrosive systems (per **IMO PSPC MSC.215(82)**).
> - **Soluble Salt Limit (IMO PSPC):** ≤ **70 mg/m²** of sodium chloride equivalent.
> - **Dew Point Margin:** Steel temperature must be ≥ **3 °C above dew point** during blasting and coating.
> - **Relative Humidity (RH) Limit:** ≤ **85 %** during application; ≤ **50 %** preferred for blasting verification.
> - **Ambient Air Temperature:** Typically **10 °C to 35 °C** for most marine paint chemistries.

## 1.4 Significance in the KTU 2024 Scheme

In **OENST832 – Hull Inspection**, hull preparation is the *bridge* between raw fabrication and paint inspection. The Course Outcomes (COs) of this module require the student to:

| Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Hull Preparation Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4 – Apply** | Apply | Select correct surface preparation method for a given hull zone |
| **CO4 – Analyse** | Analyse | Compare blast profiles, interpret SSPC/NACE photographs |
| **CO5 – Evaluate** | Evaluate | Judge acceptance/rejection of a prepared surface against DFT & contamination limits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Hull Preparation

A successful hull preparation is judged on **three independent engineering parameters** — failing any one means coating failure:

### Pillar 1 — Surface Cleanliness (Visual Grade)
Removes: rust, mill scale, old paint, fouling, dirt, oil.
Governed by: **ISO 8501-1:2007** (visual assessment via reference photographs).

### Pillar 2 — Surface Profile / Roughness (Anchor Pattern)
Creates: microscopic peaks and valleys that mechanically "lock" the paint film.
Governed by: **ISO 8503** (comparator segments) and **ISO 19840** (DFT measurement).

### Pillar 3 — Surface Chemistry (Salt/Dust/Contamination)
Removes: invisible chlorides, sulfates, dust, oil.
Governed by: **IMO PSPC MSC.215(82)**, **ISO 8502**, and **ASTM D4940**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inspection Triangle:** Cleanliness, Profile, and Chemistry are *mutually independent*. A surface may be visually "white metal" (Sa 3) but chemically contaminated with salt — the coating will blister and fail.

## 2.2 Surface Preparation Grades (ISO 8501-1)

The ISO standard uses a two-character code:

$$\text{Grade} = \underbrace{\text{Letter}}_{\text{Method}} + \underbrace{\text{Digit}}_{\text{Visual finish}}$$

| Letter | Method |
|---|---|
| **Sa** | Abrasive blast cleaning |
| **St** | Hand/power tool cleaning |
| **Fl** | Flame cleaning |
| **Pi** | Pickling (chemical) |
| **Ma** | Machine abrading |
| **Wa** | Water jetting |

| Digit | Visual Finish (Reference: ISO 8501-1) |
|---|---|
| **Sa 1** | Light blast — only loose rust, mill scale, paint removed |
| **Sa 2** | Thorough blast — ≈ **80 %** of surface free from visible residue |
| **Sa 2½** | Commercial blast — ≈ **95 %** clean — **most common for hull** |
| **Sa 3** | White metal — **100 %** pure metallic finish — for cargo holds, ballast tanks |
| **St 2 / St 3** | Hand/power tool — for spot repair, not full re-blast |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas required for numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE on this topic are consolidated below.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression | Unit / Standard | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Anchor Pattern (Profile) Height** | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$ | µm (ISO 8503) | Specifies mean surface roughness for primer adhesion |
| 2 | **DFT – WFT Conversion** | $\text{DFT} = \dfrac{\text{WFT} \times \text{Vol.}\ \% \ \text{Solids}}{100}$ | µm | Wet film → Dry film equivalent |
| 3 | **Consumption Rate of Abrasive** | $C = k \times A \times \text{Grade Factor}$ | kg/m² | Abrasive quantity for blasting contract |
| 4 | **Dew Point Margin Check** | $T_{steel} \geq T_{dew} + 3\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | Prevents condensation under coating |
| 5 | **Soluble Salt Limit (PSPC)** | $\text{[NaCl]}_{eq} \leq 70\ \text{mg/m}^2$ | mg/m² | IMO PSPC compliance test |
| 6 | **Dust Quantity Limit** | $Q_{dust} \leq \text{Class 2 (ISO 8502-3)}$ | mg/m² | Tape-lift method |
| 7 | **Salt Spray Test (Lab Correlate)** | $t_{fail} \propto \dfrac{\text{DFT} \times \text{Adhesion}}{\sigma_{corrosion}}$ | hours | ASTM B117 |
| 8 | **Blasting Pressure Loss** | $P_{nozzle} = P_{tank} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{L_{hose}}{200}\right)$ | bar | Site planning of compressors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WARNING ON FORMULA 1:** Do not confuse the **Rₐ (arithmetical mean roughness)** used here with the **Rₘₐₓ (maximum peak-to-valley)** used in comparator-segment instruments. They are NOT the same number — Rₘₐₓ ≈ 4 to 6 × Rₐ for blast-cleaned steel.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hull preparation decisions affect:

1. **Dry-dock duration** — Re-blasting consumes 30–45 % of total dock time.
2. **Fuel efficiency** — A 1 mm increase in hull roughness ≈ **1 %** increase in frictional resistance ≈ ~1 % rise in fuel consumption.
3. **Coating service life** — PSPC-compliant preparation can extend ballast tank coating life from 5 → 15 years.
4. **Class society approvals** — IACS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS all require documented surface preparation evidence in the Coating Technical File (CTF).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — WFT to DFT Conversion (Used in Paint Inspection)

The paint manufacturer supplies a **Volume Solids (%)** value, $V_s$. A coating of wet film thickness (WFT) loses solvent and shrinks to a dry film thickness (DFT) given by the mass balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{dry} &= V_{wet} \times \dfrac{V_s}{100} \\[6pt]
\text{Area} \times \text{DFT} &= \text{Area} \times \text{WFT} \times \dfrac{V_s}{100} \\[6pt]
\therefore \text{DFT} &= \text{WFT} \times \dfrac{V_s}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Let the wet paint film have volume $V_{wet}$ per unit area = WFT.
2. The volatile solvent fraction evaporates, leaving only the **non-volatile solids** behind.
3. The volume of dried film = $V_{wet} \times (V_s / 100)$.
4. Dividing both sides by the coated area yields the linear relationship above.

**Sample Numerical (KTU typical 3-mark problem):**

> An epoxy primer is applied at WFT = **250 µm**. The datasheet specifies Volume Solids = **64 %**. Find the theoretical DFT.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFT} &= 250\ \mu\text{m} \times \dfrac{64}{100} \\[4pt]
\text{DFT} &= 250 \times 0.64 \\[4pt]
\text{DFT} &= 160\ \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Theoretical DFT = **160 µm** (Target DFT in the painting specification is typically 150–175 µm → pass).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dew Point Margin Determination

The **dew point** is the temperature at which air becomes saturated (RH = 100 %). For a given ambient temperature $T_a$ and relative humidity $\phi$, the dew point $T_{dew}$ is computed from the **Magnus approximation**:

$$
T_{dew} = \dfrac{b \cdot \gamma(T_a, \phi)}{a - \gamma(T_a, \phi)}
$$

where

$$
\gamma(T_a, \phi) = \ln(\phi) + \dfrac{a \cdot T_a}{b + T_a}
$$

with Magnus constants $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (valid for $0 \le T_a \le 60\ ^\circ\text{C}$, RH in decimal).

**Derivation logic (Magnus form):**

1. Start from the Clausius–Clapeyron saturation vapour pressure curve.
2. The ratio of actual to saturation vapour pressure is $\phi = e / e_s(T_{dew})$.
3. Apply the Magnus empirical fit $\ln(e/e_s) = a T / (b + T)$.
4. Rearrange to solve for $T_{dew}$ in closed form.

**Acceptance Criterion (Industry/KTU):**

$$
\boxed{T_{steel} \geq T_{dew} + 3\ ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

**Sample Numerical:**

> Ambient $T_a = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$, RH = **70 %**, Steel temperature $T_{steel} = 31\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Is blasting permissible?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \ln(0.70) + \dfrac{17.625 \times 30}{243.04 + 30} \\[4pt]
&= -0.3567 + \dfrac{528.75}{273.04} \\[4pt]
&= -0.3567 + 1.9362 \\[4pt]
&= 1.5795 \\[6pt]
T_{dew} &= \dfrac{243.04 \times 1.5795}{17.625 - 1.5795} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{383.93}{16.0455} \\[4pt]
&\approx 23.9\ ^\circ\text{C} \\[6pt]
T_{dew} + 3 &= 23.9 + 3 = 26.9\ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T_{steel} = 31\ ^\circ\text{C} \geq 26.9\ ^\circ\text{C}$, **blasting and coating ARE permissible**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Surface Coverage by Abrasive Blasting

Theoretical abrasive consumption $C$ (kg/m²) for a given cleanliness grade is given by:

$$
C = \rho_{abrasive} \times d_{penetration} \times f_{grade}
$$

where $f_{grade}$ is an empirical factor:

| Grade | $f_{grade}$ |
|---|---|
| Sa 1 | 1.0 |
| Sa 2 | 1.6 |
| Sa 2½ | 2.2 |
| Sa 3 | 3.0 |

**Derivation logic:**

1. A single abrasive particle removes a crater of depth $d_{penetration}$.
2. To remove all mill scale and rust from 1 m², one must remove a uniform depth $\approx d_{penetration} \times f_{grade}$.
3. Multiplying by abrasive density yields mass per unit area.

**Sample Numerical:**

> Garnet abrasive, $\rho = 4.0\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $d_{penetration} = 0.1\ \text{mm}$, target Sa 2½.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 4000\ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 0.0001\ \text{m} \times 2.2 \\[4pt]
&= 0.4 \times 2.2 \\[4pt]
&= 0.88\ \text{kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 10,000 m² hull underwater area → **~ 8.8 tonnes** of garnet required.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Method

| Method | Old Coating Removal | Productivity (m²/h) | Dust | Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sa 2½ (Dry abrasive blast)** | Excellent | 15–25 | High | Medium | New building, full re-blast |
| **Sa 2½ (Vacuum blast)** | Excellent | 8–15 | Very Low | High | Tank interiors, near-sensitive equipment |
| **UHP Water Jet (Wa 2½)** | Excellent | 25–50 | None | Very High | In-service maintenance, eco-zones |
| **Hand Tool (St 2)** | Poor | 1–3 | Low | Low | Spot repair only |
| **Power Tool (St 3)** | Moderate | 3–6 | Low | Low | Local repair, transitions |
| **Flame Cleaning (Fl)** | Moderate | 10–20 | None | Medium | Decommissioning, niche yards |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Recall:** "Sa 2½" is the **default** grade for underwater hull and boottop. "Sa 3" is for **ballast tanks, void spaces, and cargo holds** under PSPC. "St 2/3" is **never** acceptable for full re-coating of underwater hull.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hull Preparation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Steel Arrives at Dry Dock] --> B[Pre-Survey & De-Fouling]
    B --> C[Method Selection per ISO 8501-1]
    C --> D[Abrasive Blasting Sa 2.5]
    C --> E[UHP Water Jetting Wa 2.5]
    C --> F[Hand or Power Tool St 2 or St 3]
    D --> G[Visual Cleanliness Check]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Grade Achieved?}
    H -- No --> D
    H -- Yes --> I[Profile Measurement ISO 8503]
    I --> J[Contamination Test ISO 8502]
    J --> K{Salt and Dust OK?}
    K -- No --> L[Fresh Water Wash]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Dehumidify and Hold]
    M --> N[Primer Application within 4 to 6 hours]
    N --> O[DFT and Holiday Inspection]
    O --> P[Coating Technical File CTF Entry]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three Pillars of Inspection Acceptance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Pillar 1 - Cleanliness]
    C1[Visual reference ISO 8501-1]
    C2[SSPC VIS 1 NACE]
    C1 --> R1[Sa 2.5 Commercial]
    C2 --> R1
    end

    subgraph P2[Pillar 2 - Profile]
    P1A[ISO 8503-1 Comparator]
    P1B[Replica Putty]
    P1C[Stylus Profilometer]
    P1A --> R2[Target 50 to 100 micrometer]
    P1B --> R2
    P1C --> R2
    end

    subgraph P3[Pillar 3 - Chemistry]
    CH1[ISO 8502-6 Bresle Patch Salt]
    CH2[ISO 8502-3 Dust Tape]
    CH3[ASTM D4940 Oil]
    CH1 --> R3[NaCl less than 70 mg per sq m]
    CH2 --> R3
    CH3 --> R3
    end

    R1 --> ACCEPT[Surface Accepted for Coating]
    R2 --> ACCEPT
    R3 --> ACCEPT
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparative Hierarchy of Cleaning Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Hull Surface Preparation Methods]
    TOP --> M1[Mechanical Methods]
    TOP --> M2[Chemical Methods]
    TOP --> M3[Thermal Methods]
    TOP --> M4[Water Based Methods]

    M1 --> M1A[Hand Tool St 2]
    M1 --> M1B[Power Tool St 3]
    M1 --> M1C[Dry Abrasive Blast Sa 2.5]
    M1 --> M1D[Vacuum Blast Sa 2.5]

    M2 --> M2A[Acid Pickling Pi]
    M2 --> M2B[Alkaline Degreasing]

    M3 --> M3A[Oxy Acetylene Flame Fl]
    M3 --> M3B[Induction Heating]

    M4 --> M4A[Hydroblasting Wa 1]
    M4 --> M4B[UHP Water Jetting Wa 2.5]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Coating Tech File Documentation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Surface Condition at Intake] --> A2[Blast Log Sheet]
    A2 --> A3[Profile Reading Record]
    A3 --> A4[Bresle Salt Test Report]
    A4 --> A5[Dust Rating Photo]
    A5 --> A6[Primer Batch and DFT Log]
    A6 --> A7[CTF Compilation]
    A7 --> A8[Class Surveyor Endorsement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams:** The original V10 protocol permits block-level functional architecture flow when physical drawings are impractical. Hull preparation, being a *process*, is best represented as a workflow hierarchy rather than a perspective sketch — these diagrams precisely serve the KTU 2024 module objective of **"process identification and documentation"**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
**Define "Sa 2½" surface preparation as per ISO 8501-1. State two hull zones where this grade is mandatory.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Sa 2½ (Commercial Blast Cleaning) is defined by ISO 8501-1:2007 as a surface preparation grade in which the steel, when viewed without magnification, is **free from visible mill scale, rust, paint, and foreign matter**. At least **95 % of the surface area** must appear free from all residues, with the remaining 5 % limited to light discolouration, light staining, or traces of tightly adherent material distributed as patches.

**Two mandatory hull zones:**

1. **Underwater Hull (Boot-top to Light Load Line)** — All anti-fouling and anti-corrosive systems require this minimum grade.
2. **External Hull above Waterline** — For cosmetic and corrosion protection, also for areas to be coated with anti-fouling tie-coats.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's trick:** Students often confuse Sa 2 with Sa 2½. Remember: Sa 2 allows traces on ~20 % of the area; Sa 2½ allows traces on only ~5 %. The grade is the **most common commercial standard** for marine painting.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**List the three independent acceptance parameters (the "Inspection Triangle") of a properly prepared hull surface and state one test method for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Standard | Test Method (one example) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visual Cleanliness** | ISO 8501-1 | Comparison with SSPC-VIS 1 / NACE reference photographs |
| 2 | **Surface Profile (Roughness)** | ISO 8503-1 | Visual/tactile comparison with **ISO 8503-1 comparator segments** (or replica putty + micrometer) |
| 3 | **Surface Chemical Cleanliness** | ISO 8502 | **Bresle Test (ISO 8502-6)** for soluble salt; **Dust Tape (ISO 8502-3)** for dust |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "VPC":** **V**isual, **P**rofile, **C**hemistry — all three are mandatory; a single failure invalidates the surface for coating.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]**

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete workflow of hull surface preparation** in a modern dry dock. Discuss the role of dehumidification, dew-point control, and the **"Hold-Point"** between blasting and priming. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A shipowner specifies **Sa 2½ + Primer within 4 hours** for a 12,000 m² underwater hull. Calculate the (i) **minimum abrasive quantity** required using garnet (ρ = 4.0 g/cm³, penetration depth 0.1 mm, $f_{grade} = 2.2$), (ii) **theoretical DFT** if WFT = 300 µm and Volume Solids = 60 %, and (iii) check whether the conditions $T_a = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$, RH = 75 %, $T_{steel} = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$ are acceptable for blasting. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Workflow + Hold-Point (7 marks):**

The complete hull preparation workflow (as per ISO 8501 / IMO PSPC) is:

1. **Pre-Survey** (1 mark): Inspection of the hull in dry dock, photo-documentation of existing paint condition, identification of corroded zones and extent of fouling. This is recorded in the **Coating Technical File (CTF)**.
2. **Pre-Cleaning / De-fouling** (1 mark): High-pressure freshwater wash (typically 200–350 bar) to remove marine growth, slime, oil, and loose dirt. Marine fouling (barnacles) is scraped manually.
3. **Method Selection** (1 mark): Selection of Sa 2½ dry abrasive blast, UHP water jetting (Wa 2½), or combination based on grade requirement, area, and environmental regulation.
4. **Abrasive Blasting / Water Jetting** (1 mark): Performed by certified blasters using calibrated equipment. Compressed air at 7–10 bar, garnet/copper slag/steel grit. The nozzle is held 200–300 mm from the surface at 60–80° angle.
5. **Dew-Point and Hold-Point Control** (1 mark): After blasting, the surface is verified for:
   - $T_{steel} \geq T_{dew} + 3\ ^\circ\text{C}$
   - RH ≤ 85 %
   - Primer to be applied within **4 hours** (per the question) to prevent **flash-rusting** and **re-contamination**.
6. **Inspection Verification** (1 mark): Cleanliness (ISO 8501-1), Profile (ISO 8503), and Chemistry (ISO 8502 — Bresle patch, dust tape) are recorded and countersigned.
7. **Dehumidification and Priming** (1 mark): Dehumidifiers (DH) maintain RH ≤ 50 %; the surface is primed within the hold-point. The CTF is updated.

**Role of Dew-Point:** Prevents moisture condensation on the freshly blasted steel, which would cause **rust-back**, **osmotic blistering**, and **coating disbondment**.

**Hold-Point:** The contractual and procedural barrier where one trade (blasting) stops, verification is signed, and the next trade (priming) begins. In a 4-hour hold, the surface must remain "rust-free" (typically ≤ Sa 2½ standard before flash rust degree "F, M, L" of ISO 8501-4).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Abrasive Quantity (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \rho \times d_{penetration} \times f_{grade} \\[4pt]
&= 4000\ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} \times 2.2 \\[4pt]
&= 0.88\ \text{kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Total abrasive} = 0.88 \times 12{,}000 = 10{,}560\ \text{kg} \approx 10.56\ \text{tonnes}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical DFT (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFT} &= \text{WFT} \times \dfrac{V_s}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 300\ \mu\text{m} \times 0.60 \\[4pt]
&= 180\ \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Dew-Point Check (3 marks):**

Using Magnus formula with $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \ln(0.75) + \dfrac{17.625 \times 28}{243.04 + 28} \\[4pt]
&= -0.2877 + \dfrac{493.5}{271.04} \\[4pt]
&= -0.2877 + 1.8206 \\[4pt]
&= 1.5329 \\[6pt]
T_{dew} &= \dfrac{243.04 \times 1.5329}{17.625 - 1.5329} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{372.55}{16.092} \\[4pt]
&\approx 23.15\ ^\circ\text{C} \\[6pt]
T_{dew} + 3 &= 23.15 + 3 = 26.15\ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T_{steel} = 30\ ^\circ\text{C} \geq 26.15\ ^\circ\text{C}$, **blasting IS permissible** and all conditions are **accepted**.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**a)** Compare the **three primary methods of surface preparation** — Dry Abrasive Blasting, Ultra-High Pressure (UHP) Water Jetting, and Power Tool Cleaning — with respect to cleanliness grade achieved, productivity, dust generation, cost, and applicable hull zones. Use a tabulated comparison. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A ballast tank of a chemical tanker is to be prepared to **Sa 3** under the **IMO PSPC (MSC.215(82))** standard. Describe in detail the **specific requirements** of PSPC for ballast tank coatings, including: (i) the steel condition before blasting, (ii) the maximum allowable salt and dust limits, (iii) the surface profile range, and (iv) the mandatory "Coating Technical File" (CTF) contents. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Dry Abrasive Blast (Sa 2½) | UHP Water Jet (Wa 2½) | Power Tool (St 2 / St 3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cleanliness Grade** | Up to Sa 3 | Up to Wa 2½ (=Sa 2½) | St 2 (loose) / St 3 (firm) |
| **Productivity (m²/h)** | 15–25 | 25–50 (with recovery) | 3–6 |
| **Dust Generation** | High (controlled by vacuum) | None (water-based) | Low |
| **Cost per m²** | Medium | High (capital intensive) | Low |
| **Application** | New build, full re-blast | Maintenance, eco zones | Spot repair, transitions |
| **Underwater hull suitability** | Excellent | Excellent (preferred for in-service) | Not acceptable |
| **Ballast tank suitability** | Yes (with ventilation) | Yes (preferred) | No (PSPC requires Sa 2½ min) |
| **Health / Environmental** | Silicosis risk if silica sand | No dust; slurry disposal issue | Hand-arm vibration risk |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** "Power tool" is **never** the answer for underwater hull or ballast tanks. It is restricted to **localized repair** of less than **5 %** of the area, in accordance with paint manufacturer's repair procedure.

---

**(b) PSPC Requirements for Ballast Tanks (7 marks):**

**(i) Steel condition before blasting (2 marks):**
- Steel must be free of weld spatter, sharp edges, and laminations.
- All welds must be ground smooth — no undercuts, no porosity.
- Steel must be free of oil, grease, and visible contaminants.
- Class society-approved steel Mill Certificate must be available.
- Steel temperature ≥ dew point + 3 °C; RH ≤ 85 %.

**(ii) Salt and Dust Limits (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Limit | Test Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Soluble salt (NaCl eq.) | ≤ **50 mg/m²** (new build) / ≤ **70 mg/m²** (maintenance) | ISO 8502-6 (Bresle) |
| Dust | Class **2** or better | ISO 8502-3 (Tape + gravimetric) |
| Visible oil/grease | **Zero** | ASTM D4940 (or visual + UV) |

**(iii) Surface Profile (1.5 marks):**
- Profile range: **50–100 µm R₅ (Ry5)** as per ISO 8503-5.
- Measured using **replica putty** (Testex Press-O-Film) or **stylus profilometer**.
- Coarse profile (>100 µm) causes peaks to project through the primer film → rust points.

**(iv) Coating Technical File (CTF) — mandatory contents (1.5 marks):**
1. Steel Mill Certificate.
2. Surface preparation log (photos, blast grade).
3. Profile measurement records.
4. Bresle and dust test reports.
5. Paint batch numbers, technical datasheets.
6. DFT logs for each coat.
7. Holiday detection report (for tanks >1000 V).
8. Inspection and class endorsement signatures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing ISO 8501-1 grades** with SSPC grades (e.g., writing "SSPC-10" instead of "Sa 2½"). Always quote ISO first, SSPC equivalent in brackets.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting the 3 °C dew-point margin** — this is a single-line answer that costs 1 full mark if omitted.
> 3. ❌ **Stating "Sa 2½ is for ballast tanks"** — that is **wrong**. PSPC requires **Sa 2½ minimum**; many specifications demand **Sa 3 (white metal)** for ballast tanks.
> 4. ❌ **Ignoring salt limits** — students remember the grade but forget the chemistry pillar.
> 5. ❌ **WFT = DFT** in numerical problems — always apply the volume-solids correction.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Hull Preparation**

- [x] **Hull Preparation = Cleaning + Profiling + Chemistry (VPC triangle).**
- [x] **ISO 8501-1** governs visual grade; default hull grade = **Sa 2½**.
- [x] **Sa 3 (white metal)** required for **ballast tanks / cargo holds / void spaces** under PSPC.
- [x] **ISO 8503** governs surface profile; target **50–100 µm** for marine epoxies.
- [x] **ISO 8502** governs chemical cleanliness; key tests — **Bresle patch (salt), Dust tape, ASTM D4940 (oil)**.
- [x] **IMO PSPC MSC.215(82)** is the legally binding standard for ballast tank coating on new builds.
- [x] **Dew-point margin = 3 °C** (always check before blasting and before each coat).
- [x] **WFT to DFT:** $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times (V_s / 100)$ — Volume Solids is a percentage, not a ratio.
- [x] **Hold-Point** = contractual break between blasting and priming, usually **4–6 hours**.
- [x] **Abrasive consumption** scales with $f_{grade}$: Sa 1 = 1.0, Sa 2 = 1.6, Sa 2½ = 2.2, Sa 3 = 3.0.
- [x] **Hand/power tool (St 2/3)** is for **spot repair only** — never for underwater hull.
- [x] **UHP Water Jet (Wa 2½)** is the eco-friendly alternative to dry blasting.
- [x] **CTF (Coating Technical File)** is a **statutory** document — every entry is auditable by class surveyors.
- [x] **Silica sand** is **banned** in most countries due to silicosis — use **garnet, copper slag, or steel grit**.
- [x] **Flash rust** (ISO 8501-4 grades F, M, L) must be removed before priming — re-blast if heavy.
- [x] **Mnemonic for grades:** "**1 = Loose, 2 = Better, 2½ = Commercial, 3 = White Metal**".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull Painting
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hull Painting

---

# MODULE 4 — HULL PAINTING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hull Painting** is the systematic application of engineered coating systems (primers, intermediate coats, anti-corrosive layers, and anti-fouling layers) on the external and internal steel surfaces of a ship's hull to achieve **three simultaneous engineering objectives**: (1) **corrosion protection** of the base mild steel, (2) **biofouling prevention** on the underwater hull, and (3) **hydrodynamic efficiency** preservation by maintaining a smooth, low-drag surface profile.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with ISO 12944, IMO PSPC, and IACS UR Z17), hull painting is recognized as a **risk-based, life-cycle engineering discipline** rather than a simple cosmetic activity. It is a statutory requirement under **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 3-2** (corrosion protection of ballast tanks) and **IMO Resolution MEPC.104(49)** for ballast tank coatings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Sunscreen + Raincoat + Polish" Analogy for Hull Painting:**
>
> Imagine you have a brand-new iron bicycle that you want to protect for **25 years** in harsh marine conditions (sun, saltwater, barnacles). You would:
>
> 1. Apply a **primer (zinc-rich epoxy)** — like an undercoat that chemically "sacrifices" itself to protect the iron (this is called **cathodic / galvanic protection**).
> 2. Apply a **build coat (epoxy intermediate)** — like a thick raincoat that physically blocks saltwater from reaching the metal.
> 3. Apply an **anti-fouling topcoat** — like a slippery, biocide-releasing sunscreen that prevents barnacles (the "sunscreen enemies" of the marine world) from sticking.
> 4. Apply a **finish coat** on topsides — like a car polish that keeps the ship looking smart and reduces hydrodynamic drag.
>
> **The hull of a ship behaves exactly like this bicycle**, but on a far more aggressive scale. A single breach in the coating film can lead to pitting corrosion rates of **0.5–1.5 mm/year** in seawater, ultimately compromising structural integrity.

### 1.3 The Three Engineering Functions of Hull Paint

| Function | Mechanism | Typical Film Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| **Corrosion Protection** | Barrier + Cathodic (zinc primer) + Inhibitive (micaceous iron oxide) | Primer: 50–80 µm DFT, Build coat: 100–150 µm DFT |
| **Anti-Fouling** | Biocide leaching (cuprous oxide, copper acrylate) OR low-surface-energy release (silicone, fluoropolymer) | Anti-fouling: 75–150 µm DFT per coat |
| **Hydrodynamic / Cosmetic** | Smooth gloss finish reduces frictional drag; bright color aids visibility | Topcoat: 30–50 µm DFT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Films (SRF):** KTU examiners often ask students to remember that the **total hull paint system DFT** for a newbuild bulk carrier is typically **320–450 µm** (above waterline) and **400–600 µm** (below waterline, including anti-fouling).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Multi-Layer Coating Architecture

A modern marine hull coating is **never a single paint layer**. It is a meticulously engineered **layered composite system**, where each layer performs a discrete role. The architecture from base steel outward is:

**Steel Substrate → Surface Preparation → Primer → Tie Coat / Build Coat → Anti-Corrosive Coat → Anti-Fouling Coat (if underwater) → Finish / Topcoat (if topsides)**

#### 2.1.1 Layer-by-Layer Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 1 — Surface Preparation (NOT a paint, but the foundation):**
> - Achieved through **grit blasting (Sa 2½)** or **hydroblasting (WJ-2)**.
> - Removes mill scale, rust, salt contaminants, and old paint.
> - Surface profile (anchor pattern) must be **50–100 µm Rz** for proper adhesion.
> - Without proper preparation, **even the best paint will fail prematurely** — this is the #1 cause of paint failure reported by classification societies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 2 — Primer (Shop Primer / Anti-Corrosive Primer):**
> - **Zinc-rich epoxy primer** (zinc content ≥ 80% by weight in dry film): Provides **cathodic / galvanic protection**. Zinc acts as the sacrificial anode.
> - **Zinc silicate (inorganic) primer**: Used for ballast tanks under IMO PSPC; superior chemical resistance.
> - Typical DFT: **50–80 µm**.
> - **Shop primers** (e.g., zinc silicate prefabrication primers) are applied at the steel stockist before plate cutting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 3 — Intermediate / Build Coat:**
> - Usually **epoxy-based** with **micaceous iron oxide (MIO)** pigmentation.
> - MIO platelets align parallel to the substrate, creating a **labyrinth/tortuous path** that dramatically slows water and ion ingress.
> - Provides the bulk of the total film thickness (100–150 µm).
> - Acts as a **barrier coat**, sealing the primer from the external environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 4 — Anti-Fouling Coat (Underwater Hull Only):**
> - **Conventional (Contact Leaching) Anti-Fouling**: Contains **cuprous oxide (Cu₂O)** + booster biocides (e.g., zinc pyrithione, copper thiocyanate). Biocide diffuses/leaches from the paint matrix at a controlled rate.
> - **Self-Polishing / Ablative Anti-Fouling (SPC)**: Uses **copper acrylate** or **zinc acrylate** resin. The resin hydrolyses in seawater at a controlled rate (~5–15 µm/month), continuously exposing fresh biocide.
> - **Fouling Release Coatings (FRC)**: Silicone or fluoropolymer based. **Non-biocidal**. Marine organisms cannot firmly attach; they are washed off at speeds ≥ 15 knots ("self-cleaning").
> - **TBT (tributyltin) coatings**: **BANNED globally** since the IMO AFS Convention (2008).

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 5 — Finish / Topcoat (Topsides & Superstructure Only):**
> - **Polyurethane** or **acrylic polyurethane** finish coat.
> - Provides **UV resistance**, gloss retention, and color.
> - Epoxy degrades under UV — therefore, epoxy must NEVER be the outermost layer in topside.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE numerical problems.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theoretical Coverage Rate** | $C = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{DFT}$ | m²/L | $V_s$ = Volume Solids (%) |
| 2 | **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{V_s} \times 100$ | µm | Used in field to verify application |
| 3 | **Practical Coverage Rate** | $C_p = \dfrac{C \times E}{100}$ | m²/L | E = Transfer Efficiency (%) |
| 4 | **Paint Quantity Required** | $Q = \dfrac{A}{C_p}$ | Litres | A = Total Area to paint (m²) |
| 5 | **Number of Coats Required** | $N_c = \dfrac{Total\ DFT\ Required}{DFT\ per\ Coat}$ | (integer, round up) | Always round UP |
| 6 | **Volume of Paint per m²** | $V_m = \dfrac{DFT}{V_s \times 10}$ | L/m² | Useful for estimating |
| 7 | **Solids by Volume** | $V_s = \dfrac{DFT}{WFT} \times 100$ | % | Reverse calculation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute value symbol rule:** When writing the modulus of a value, always use $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\mid x \mid$ — this avoids breaking markdown table rendering.

### 2.3 Surface Preparation Standards (ISO 8501-1)

| Grade | Designation | Visual Appearance | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sa 1** | Light blast | Light visible cleaning | Not acceptable for hull |
| **Sa 2** | Thorough blast | Most rust, mill scale removed | Maintenance only |
| **Sa 2½** | Very thorough blast | 95% clean, slight shadows | **Standard for newbuild hull** |
| **Sa 3** | White metal | 100% clean, uniform metallic | Specialty applications |

| Grade | Designation | Method |
|---|---|---|
| **St 2** | Thorough hand/power tool | Wire brush, sander |
| **St 3** | Very thorough hand/power | Same tools, more rigorous |
| **WJ-2** | High-pressure water jet | Hydroblasting standard |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

The hull painting system directly determines:
- **Fuel consumption**: A 10 µm increase in hull roughness can increase fuel consumption by **~1%**.
- **Drydock interval**: Anti-fouling efficiency determines 2.5–5 year dry-docking schedule.
- **Steel wastage rate**: Effective coating reduces corrosion from **0.3–1.0 mm/year (bare)** to **< 0.05 mm/year (coated)**.
- **Compliance with Classification Society Rules** (DNV, LR, BV, ABS) and **IACS UR Z17 / Z23**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Paint Quantity Calculation (Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A shipowner wants to paint the underwater hull of a bulk carrier with **3 coats of self-polishing anti-fouling paint**. The hull area is **8,500 m²**. Each coat must achieve a **DFT of 100 µm**. The paint has a **volume solids of 60%**, and the application **transfer efficiency** (spray losses, overspray, etc.) is **70%**.
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Total paint quantity required in litres. (b) WFT for verification during application.

**Given Data:**
- Area: $A = 8{,}500\ \text{m}^2$
- Number of coats: $N_c = 3$
- DFT per coat: $DFT = 100\ \mu\text{m}$
- Volume solids: $V_s = 60\%$
- Transfer efficiency: $E = 70\%$

#### Step 1 — Theoretical Coverage Rate

Using $C = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{DFT}$:

$$
C = \dfrac{60 \times 10}{100}
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{600}{100} = 6.0\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Logic:** Each litre of paint, if 100% efficient, would cover 6 m² to a dry film of 100 µm.

#### Step 2 — Practical Coverage Rate (Account for Losses)

Using $C_p = \dfrac{C \times E}{100}$:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{6.0 \times 70}{100}
$$

$$
C_p = \dfrac{420}{100} = 4.2\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Logic:** Real-world spray losses reduce coverage from 6.0 to 4.2 m²/L.

#### Step 3 — Total Paint Quantity

Using $Q = \dfrac{A}{C_p}$:

$$
Q = \dfrac{8{,}500}{4.2}
$$

$$
Q = 2{,}023.81\ \text{L}
$$

**Logic:** This is for **one coat**. For 3 coats, multiply by $N_c = 3$.

#### Step 4 — Final Quantity for All Three Coats

$$
Q_{total} = Q \times N_c
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 2{,}023.81 \times 3 = 6{,}071.43\ \text{L}
$$

**Rounded with 10% safety margin for wastage, spillage, and touch-ups:**

$$
Q_{final} \approx 6{,}679\ \text{L} \approx 6.68\ \text{kL}
$$

#### Step 5 — Wet Film Thickness (Field Verification)

Using $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{V_s} \times 100$:

$$
WFT = \dfrac{100}{60} \times 100
$$

$$
WFT = 166.67\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Logic:** During application, the painter's wet film comb must read ~167 µm to guarantee a 100 µm DFT after solvent evaporation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coating Thickness Verification (Statistics)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** During dry-dock, the surveyor takes **20 DFT readings** on the hull coating. The specified DFT is **300 µm**. The readings (in µm) are:
>
> 285, 295, 310, 305, 290, 300, 315, 295, 305, 300, 290, 310, 295, 300, 305, 295, 300, 310, 295, 300.
>
> **Determine:** Mean DFT, Standard Deviation, and whether the coating passes the IMO PSPC acceptance criterion (Mean ≥ Specified DFT AND No individual reading < 90% of specified).

#### Step 1 — Sum of Readings

$$
\sum x_i = 285 + 295 + 310 + 305 + 290 + 300 + 315 + 295 + 305 + 300 + 290 + 310 + 295 + 300 + 305 + 295 + 300 + 310 + 295 + 300
$$

$$
\sum x_i = 6{,}000
$$

#### Step 2 — Mean DFT

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{6{,}000}{20} = 300\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Logic:** Mean exactly equals specification. ✓ Pass criterion 1.

#### Step 3 — Standard Deviation

Using $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$:

Each deviation squared:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(285 - 300)^2 &= (-15)^2 = 225 \\
(295 - 300)^2 &= (-5)^2 = 25 \\
(310 - 300)^2 &= (10)^2 = 100 \\
(305 - 300)^2 &= (5)^2 = 25 \\
(290 - 300)^2 &= (-10)^2 = 100 \\
(300 - 300)^2 &= (0)^2 = 0 \\
(315 - 300)^2 &= (15)^2 = 225 \\
(295 - 300)^2 &= 25 \\
(305 - 300)^2 &= 25 \\
(300 - 300)^2 &= 0 \\
(290 - 300)^2 &= 100 \\
(310 - 300)^2 &= 100 \\
(295 - 300)^2 &= 25 \\
(300 - 300)^2 &= 0 \\
(305 - 300)^2 &= 25 \\
(295 - 300)^2 &= 25 \\
(300 - 300)^2 &= 0 \\
(310 - 300)^2 &= 100 \\
(295 - 300)^2 &= 25 \\
(300 - 300)^2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 225 + 25 + 100 + 25 + 100 + 0 + 225 + 25 + 25 + 0 + 100 + 100 + 25 + 0 + 25 + 25 + 0 + 100 + 25 + 0
$$

$$
\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 1{,}150
$$

Standard deviation:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1{,}150}{20 - 1}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{60.526}
$$

$$
\sigma \approx 7.78\ \mu\text{m}
$$

#### Step 4 — Acceptance Check

**IMO PSPC Criteria:**

$$
\text{Criterion 1: Mean DFT} \geq \text{NDFT}
$$

$$
300\ \mu\text{m} \geq 300\ \mu\text{m}\quad ✓
$$

$$
\text{Criterion 2: All individual readings} \geq 0.9 \times \text{NDFT}
$$

$$
0.9 \times 300 = 270\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Lowest reading = 285 µm** ≥ 270 µm ✓

#### Step 5 — Result

**Result:** The coating **PASSES** both criteria. The mean DFT equals the specified value, and all individual readings are within tolerance. Coating accepted.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Coating Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Visual Symptom | Root Cause | KTU-Relevant Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blistering** | Dome-shaped bubbles | Osmotic pressure (solvent retention, soluble salts) | Often from poor surface prep |
| **Delamination** | Paint peels off in sheets | Adhesion failure, overcoat interval exceeded | Critical near weld seams |
| **Chalking** | Powdery surface on topcoat | UV degradation of binder | Epoxy exposed to UV |
| **Cracking (mud-cracking)** | Pattern of fine cracks | Excessive DFT in single coat | Over-application of MIO |
| **Rust Spotting / Scribe Failure** | Rust bleed at scratches | Coating breach + cathodic protection overwhelmed | Holiday detection needed |
| **Anti-fouling sloughing** | AF peels off underwater | Incompatible primer, wrong solvent | Check compatibility |
