## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the three primary functions of hull painting on a merchant vessel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — must hit all 3 functions):**

Hull painting serves three essential engineering functions:

1. **Corrosion Protection (1 mark):** The primary function is to isolate the mild steel hull from corrosive seawater and marine atmosphere. Without protection, corrosion rates of 0.5–1.5 mm/year would quickly compromise structural integrity, particularly in ballast tanks and the underwater hull.

2. **Anti-Fouling (1 mark):** The underwater hull is prone to colonization by marine organisms (barnacles, mussels, algae). This biofouling increases hull roughness and frictional resistance, raising fuel consumption by 5–30%. Anti-fouling paints either release biocides (Cu₂O, copper acrylate) or provide low-surface-energy release (silicone FRC).

3. **Hydrodynamic Efficiency and Cosmetic Appearance (1 mark):** A smooth, glossy paint finish reduces frictional drag, improves fuel efficiency, and provides the ship's identifying colors and markings. It also aids visibility for safety.

---

#### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between self-polishing anti-fouling (SPC) and fouling release coatings (FRC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Self-Polishing Anti-Fouling (SPC) | Fouling Release Coating (FRC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Mechanism** | Biocide release (Cu₂O, copper acrylate hydrolyses) | Non-biocidal; low surface energy |
| **Resin Type** | Acrylate copolymer | Silicone / Fluoropolymer |
| **Active Component** | Copper compounds + booster biocides | None — purely physical mechanism |
| **Lifetime** | 3–5 years | 5–10 years (inert) |
| **Speed Effect** | No speed dependency | Self-cleans at ≥ 15 knots |
| **Environmental Impact** | Biocide release to marine environment | Environmentally benign |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Describe the **complete multi-layer hull coating system** from base steel to the outermost layer, explaining the role of each layer. Mention the relevant surface preparation grade as per ISO 8501-1. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **three types of anti-fouling systems** used on underwater hulls, with their working principles, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B-A:**

**(a) Multi-Layer Hull Coating System (7 marks):**

The complete hull paint system is a **multi-layer engineered composite**, applied sequentially on properly prepared steel.

**Step 1 — Surface Preparation (1 mark):**

The steel substrate must be abrasive blasted to **Sa 2½** (very thorough commercial blast) per **ISO 8501-1**, achieving a surface profile of **50–100 µm Rz**. This removes mill scale, rust, and contaminants, and provides the "anchor pattern" for mechanical adhesion of primer.

**Step 2 — Primer Coat (1.5 marks):**

A **zinc-rich epoxy primer** is applied at **50–80 µm DFT** within 4–6 hours of blasting (before flash rusting). Zinc content ≥ 80% by weight in dry film provides **cathodic (sacrificial) protection** — zinc corrodes preferentially, protecting the underlying steel. In ballast tanks, **zinc silicate (inorganic) primers** are mandatory under IMO PSPC for superior chemical resistance.

**Step 3 — Intermediate / Build Coat (1.5 marks):**

A **micaceous iron oxide (MIO) epoxy** coat at **100–150 µm DFT** is applied. MIO platelets align parallel to the substrate, creating a **tortuous path barrier** that physically blocks water and ion migration. This contributes the bulk of total film thickness and acts as a barrier layer.

**Step 4 — Anti-Fouling / Topcoat (2 marks):**

For the **underwater hull**, one or more coats of **anti-fouling paint** (75–150 µm DFT per coat) are applied. For the **topsides**, a **polyurethane finish coat** (30–50 µm DFT) is applied to provide UV resistance and gloss.

**Optional Tie Coat:** A thin epoxy seal coat (25–50 µm) may be applied between the build coat and anti-fouling to ensure compatibility.

**Total System DFT:** Typically **320–450 µm** above waterline, **400–600 µm** below waterline.

---

**(b) Three Anti-Fouling Systems (7 marks):**

**1. Conventional (Contact Leaching) Anti-Fouling (2 marks):**

- **Composition:** Cuprous oxide (Cu₂O) pigment + insoluble resin matrix (rosin, vinyl).
- **Mechanism:** Seawater dissolves Cu₂O at the surface, releasing copper ions that are toxic to fouling organisms. Biocide diffuses/leaches outward.
- **Limitation:** Biocide release rate decreases exponentially with time as the surface layer becomes depleted of accessible biocide. Linear depletion means the paint "stops working" long before it physically wears out.
- **Effective life:** 2–3 years.

**2. Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC) Anti-Fouling (3 marks):**

- **Composition:** **Copper acrylate** or **zinc acrylate** resin, plus Cu₂O and booster biocides (zinc pyrithione, copper thiocyanate, Sea-Nine 211).
- **Mechanism:** The acrylate resin undergoes **hydrolysis** in seawater at a controlled rate (~5–15 µm/month). The hydrolyzed layer dissolves, exposing a fresh layer with active biocide.
- **Advantage:** **Constant biocide release** throughout the coating life. Polish rate is proportional to seawater contact — so a stationary ship in port polishes slowly (preserves biocide), while a fast-moving ship polishes faster (matches fouling pressure).
- **Effective life:** 3–5 years. Used by most commercial fleets today.
- **Environmental Note:** TBT-based SPC was banned globally under the **IMO AFS Convention (2001, in force 2008)**.

**3. Fouling Release Coating (FRC) (2 marks):**

- **Composition:** **Silicone elastomer** or **fluoropolymer** binder, no biocides.
- **Mechanism:** Provides a **low-surface-energy, non-stick** surface. Marine organisms attach weakly and are washed off either by ship motion (≥ 15 knots) or gentle manual cleaning.
- **Advantage:** **Zero biocide release** — environmentally benign. Suitable for environmentally sensitive areas (e.g., cruise ships in Baltic, naval vessels).
- **Limitation:** Higher initial cost; ineffective at low speeds (< 10 knots) — fouling builds up during port stays.
- **Effective life:** 5–10 years (inert, no chemical depletion).

---

#### **Question B-B:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A shipyard is painting the underwater hull of a vessel. The **hull area** is **6,200 m²**, the **anti-fouling paint** is applied in **2 coats**, each coat to a **DFT of 125 µm**. The paint has **volume solids = 55%** and **transfer efficiency = 65%**.
**(i)** Calculate the **theoretical coverage rate** and **practical coverage rate**.
**(ii)** Calculate the **total paint quantity** required (in litres).
**(iii)** Calculate the **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** for field verification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **ISO 8501-1 surface preparation grades (Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3)** and **St 2, St 3** grades. State which grade is **mandatory for newbuild hull coating**. Also explain the significance of **surface profile (anchor pattern)** in paint adhesion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B-B:**

**(a) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Area $A = 6{,}200\ \text{m}^2$
- Number of coats $N_c = 2$
- DFT per coat $= 125\ \mu\text{m}$
- Volume solids $V_s = 55\%$
- Transfer efficiency $E = 65\%$

**(i) Coverage Rates (2 marks):**

Theoretical coverage:

$$
C = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{DFT} = \dfrac{55 \times 10}{125}
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{550}{125} = 4.4\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}
$$

Practical coverage:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{C \times E}{100} = \dfrac{4.4 \times 65}{100}
$$

$$
C_p = 2.86\ \text{m}^2/\texttext{/L}
$$

**(ii) Total Paint Quantity (3 marks):**

Quantity for one coat:

$$
Q_1 = \dfrac{A}{C_p} = \dfrac{6{,}200}{2.86} = 2{,}167.83\ \text{L}
$$

Total for 2 coats:

$$
Q_{total} = Q_1 \times N_c = 2{,}167.83 \times 2 = 4{,}335.66\ \text{L}
$$

Adding 10% safety margin for wastage and touch-ups:

$$
Q_{final} = 4{,}335.66 \times 1.10 \approx 4{,}769\ \text{L} \approx 4.77\ \text{kL}
$$

**(iii) Wet Film Thickness (2 marks):**

$$
WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{V_s} \times 100 = \dfrac{125}{55} \times 100
$$

$$
WFT = 227.27\ \mu\text{m}
$$

The painter's wet film gauge should read approximately **227 µm** during application to guarantee a 125 µm DFT after solvent evaporation.

---

**(b) Surface Preparation Grades (7 marks):**

**ISO 8501-1 Visual Assessment Grades (5 marks):**

| Grade | Description | Visual Reference Photo | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sa 1** | Light blast cleaning | Light cleaning, ~70% surface free of visible rust | Not used for marine hull |
| **Sa 2** | Thorough blast cleaning | ~95% surface free from rust, oil, paint | Maintenance only |
| **Sa 2½** | Very thorough blast | ~95% clean, slight shadows/pitting | **MANDATORY for newbuild hull** |
| **Sa 3** | White metal blast | 100% clean, uniform metallic appearance | Special applications |

**Hand and Power Tool Cleaning Grades:**

| Grade | Description | Method |
|---|---|---|
| **St 2** | Thorough hand/power tool | Wire brush, scraper — removes loose material |
| **St 3** | Very thorough hand/power tool | Same tools, more vigorous — leaves firm material |

**Mandatory Grade (1 mark):**

For newbuild hull painting and major refurbishment, **Sa 2½ (very thorough blast cleaning)** is mandatory per **ISO 8501-1**, supplemented by **ISO 8503** for surface profile measurement.

**Surface Profile / Anchor Pattern (1 mark):**

The surface profile, measured as **Rz** (or **Ra**) in micrometres, is critical because:
- **Too low (< 30 µm):** Insufficient mechanical key — primer cannot anchor.
- **Too high (> 100 µm):** Primer cannot cover peaks — "shadowing" leads to pinpoint rust.
- **Optimum (50–100 µm):** Provides peak-to-valley mechanical interlocking with primer, maximizing adhesion and bond strength.

Profile is measured with **ISO 8503-1 comparator** or **replica tape (Testex)**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost KTU students 2-4 marks each:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always write **m²/L, µm, Litres** explicitly. A correct number without units is worth only 50% credit.
>
> 2. **Wrong ISO grade:** Many students write "Sa 3" for newbuild — this is WRONG. **Sa 2½** is the standard. Sa 3 is reserved for chemical plants and special services.
>
> 3. **Confusing DFT and WFT:** DFT (Dry Film Thickness) is measured AFTER solvent evaporates. WFT (Wet Film Thickness) is measured DURING application. WFT is ALWAYS greater than DFT.
>
> 4. **Calling epoxy a "topcoat" for topsides:** Epoxy chalkes under UV. **Polyurethane** must be the outermost finish coat.
>
> 5. **Ignoring transfer efficiency:** Theoretical coverage ≠ practical coverage. Always multiply by $\dfrac{E}{100}$.
>
> 6. **Forgetting safety margin:** Always add 10% to calculated paint quantity for wastage.
>
> 7. **Stating TBT is in use:** TBT (tributyltin) anti-fouling has been **globally banned** under the IMO AFS Convention since 2008. Mark this as a **major error** if written in a current KTU exam.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HULL PAINTING — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**
>
> ✅ **Three functions:** Corrosion protection, Anti-fouling, Hydrodynamic / Cosmetic.
>
> ✅ **Layer order:** Steel → Sa 2½ Blast → Zinc Primer → MIO Epoxy Build Coat → Tie Coat → Anti-Fouling / PU Finish.
>
> ✅ **Standard grades:** Sa 2½ (blast), St 3 (hand tool), with profile **50–100 µm Rz**.
>
> ✅ **Key formulas:** $C = V_s \times 10 / DFT$; $C_p = C \times E / 100$; $Q = A \times N_c / C_p$; $WFT = DFT \times 100 / V_s$.
>
> ✅ **Primer types:** Zinc-rich epoxy (hull), Zinc silicate (ballast tanks, IMO PSPC).
>
> ✅ **Three AF types:** Contact leaching (old), SPC (modern standard), FRC (eco-friendly, high-speed ships).
>
> ✅ **Banned substance:** **TBT** — globally prohibited under IMO AFS Convention 2008.
>
> ✅ **Total DFT ranges:** Topside 320–450 µm; Underwater 400–600 µm; Ballast tank 320 µm NDFT (IMO PSPC).
>
> ✅ **Standard reference:** ISO 12944 (corrosion), ISO 8501-1 (surface prep), IMO PSPC MSC.215(82), IACS UR Z17/Z23.
>
> ✅ **Always include:** 10% safety margin for paint quantity; transfer efficiency in coverage calculation; unit conversions in numerical answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Propeller Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Propeller Works** refers to the specialized shore-based facility, workshop infrastructure, and engineering procedures employed for the **manufacture, repair, overhaul, inspection, and certification** of marine propellers. It encompasses the full life-cycle management of the screw propeller — the prime mover interface between the main engine's rotational power and the vessel's translational thrust.

In strict KTU naval architecture terminology, a *Propeller Workshop* is a dry-dock adjacent or shipyard-integrated engineering cell equipped with:
- Heavy-duty horizontal boring machines and vertical turning lathes (VTLs) capable of handling propellers up to **12 metres** in diameter and weighing up to **600 tonnes**.
- Pitch measuring and profile-tracing equipment (optical and laser-based).
- Welding bays with preheating furnaces for bronze and stainless-steel alloys.
- Dynamic balancing machines with sensitivity up to **0.5 gram-metre**.
- NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) laboratories certified to **IACS UR Z17 / Z23** standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Car Wheel Balancing" Concept:**
> Imagine you take your car to a tyre shop. The mechanic doesn't just fit new tyres — he inspects the wheel rim for hairline cracks, checks the balancing weights, ensures the tyre pressure is uniform, and dynamically spins the wheel to make sure it doesn't wobble at high speed. A **Propeller Works** performs the *naval equivalent* of this for a ship's propeller, except the "tyre" weighs 50–500 tonnes, spins at 80–120 RPM, generates **thrust equivalent to several thousand horsepower**, and operates in highly corrosive salt water where a tiny crack can lead to catastrophic blade detachment at sea.

The propeller is the **only rotating component of a ship that does work directly on the water** — every other machine operates inside the hull. This makes its workshop discipline uniquely demanding: a failure isn't just a mechanical breakdown, it's a potential maritime disaster with loss of life and cargo.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Metric |
|-----------|----------------|
| Propeller material density (Manganese Bronze) | **8.7 g/cm³** |
| Propeller material density (Ni-Al Bronze) | **7.6 g/cm³** |
| Typical blade tip speed (max) | **90 m/s** |
| Class-approved repair welding preheat temp. | **150 °C – 250 °C** |
| Pitch measurement tolerance (Class A) | **± 2 mm** per metre of radius |
| Dynamic balance grade (ISO 1940) | **G6.3** |
| Maximum permissible cavitation erosion depth | **10% of blade thickness** |
| Propeller diameter range (commercial ships) | **1.5 m – 12.0 m** |

### 1.4 Scope of Propeller Works (KTU 2024 Module 4 Perspective)

The KTU Module 4 syllabus on Hull Inspection treats Propeller Works under the following operational sub-domains:
1. **Propeller geometry verification** (pitch, diameter, skew, rake, thickness distribution).
2. **Defect classification** (cavitation pitting, erosion, cracks, edge damage, bending).
3. **Repair methodology** (welded patch repair, blade cropping, edge welding, cold straightening).
4. **NDT evaluation** (DPT, MPI, UT, eddy current).
5. **Dynamic balancing & overspeed testing.**
6. **Class society survey integration** (ABS, DNV, LR, BV rules).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Propeller Workshop Workflow

The propeller overhaul workflow follows a **six-stage closed-loop cycle**, analogous to the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** quality cycle. Each stage is governed by Class Society rules and IACS Unified Requirements.

**Stage 1 — Docking & Disassembly**
- Vessel enters dry dock, propeller marking (port/starboard, blade numbering 1–5) recorded.
- Propeller shaft disconnected from gearbox or thrust block at the **coupling flange**.
- Tail shaft withdrawn using the **hydraulic stern gear pulling arrangement** (pulling force up to **200 tonnes**).
- Propeller removed via the **crane/lifting gear** and transported to workshop on a propeller bogie.

**Stage 2 — Initial Survey & Documentation**
- Visual examination by Class Surveyor + Shipyard Superintendent + Owner's representative.
- Photographs taken of all corroded/cracked/eroded zones (a Class requirement).
- **Pitch measurement** carried out at stations **0.3R, 0.4R, 0.5R, 0.6R, 0.7R, 0.8R** (R = propeller radius).
- Pit gauge measurement, ultrasonic thickness gauging (UTG) of blade thickness.
- Report issued with **"As-Found"** condition and Class recommendations.

**Stage 3 — Defect Mapping & Repair Plan**
- Every defect marked with **chalk/paint marker** on blade surface.
- Repair plan categorized:
  - **Category A:** Cosmetic — buffing, polishing.
  - **Category B:** Edge build-up by welding.
  - **Category C:** Major weld repair with preheating.
  - **Category D:** Blade cropping (cutting off damaged tip portion).
  - **Category E:** Full blade renewal.

**Stage 4 — Workshop Repair**
- Grit blasting to **Sa 2.5** (near-white metal) finish per **ISO 8501-1**.
- Preheating by **oxy-acetylene torches or induction coils** to specified interpass temperature.
- Welding using **GTAW (TIG)** or **SMAW** with matching filler electrodes (e.g., AWS ERCuNiAl for Ni-Al Bronze).
- Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) for stress relief.

**Stage 5 — NDT & Re-profiling**
- 100% Dye Penetrant Test (DPT) on all weld zones.
- MPI on ferromagnetic components.
- UT scanning for sub-surface defects.
- Final pitch re-measurement and verification against design drawing.

**Stage 6 — Dynamic Balancing & Final Certification**
- Propeller mounted on balancing machine, spun at **workshop balance speed** (~30% of service RPM).
- Residual unbalance corrected by drilling or adding balance weights.
- **Class certificate of fitness** issued; propeller re-fitted on shaft using **hydraulic fit (interference fit)** with key.

### 2.2 Why & How — The Underlying Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dynamic balancing?** A 6-metre, 80-tonne propeller rotating at 100 RPM has a peripheral tip speed of ~31.4 m/s. Even a **2 kg mass eccentricity at the blade tip** generates a centrifugal force of:
> $$F = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$
> This causes bearing failure, vibration-induced hull fatigue, and structural failure of the blade root.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why preheat before welding Bronze?** Bronze has **high thermal conductivity (~50 W/m·K)** and **high coefficient of thermal expansion (~19 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)**. Without preheating, the heat-affected zone (HAZ) cools rapidly, creating residual tensile stresses and micro-cracks that propagate under cyclic loading.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $P = \pi \cdot D \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | Geometric pitch (where $\theta$ = pitch angle at radius r) | m | Pitch calculation at any station |
| 2 | $P_{0.7R} = \pi \cdot D_{0.7R} \cdot \tan(\theta_{0.7R})$ | Standard reference pitch at 0.7R | m | KTU/Class design reference radius |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s | Balancing & RPM conversion |
| 4 | $v_{tip} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N / 60$ | Blade tip linear velocity | m/s | Cavitation & erosion assessment |
| 5 | $F_{c} = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Centrifugal force due to unbalance | N | Dynamic balance calculation |
| 6 | $J = \dfrac{v_{A}}{n \cdot D}$ | Advance coefficient | dimensionless | Open-water propeller performance |
| 7 | $K_{T} = \dfrac{T}{\rho \cdot n^{2} \cdot D^{4}}$ | Thrust coefficient | dimensionless | Open-water test analysis |
| 8 | $K_{Q} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \cdot n^{2} \cdot D^{5}}$ | Torque coefficient | dimensionless | Open-water test analysis |
| 9 | $\eta_{0} = \dfrac{J \cdot K_{T}}{2 \pi \cdot K_{Q}}$ | Open-water efficiency | dimensionless | Hull-propeller interaction |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^{2}_{tip}}{p_{vapour} - p_{depth}}$ | Cavitation number (simplified) | dimensionless | Cavitation tendency check |
| 11 | $T_{preheat} \approx 250 + 10 \cdot (\% \text{ of cracking risk})$ | Empirical preheat estimate | °C | Welding procedure spec |
| 12 | $\Delta = \dfrac{U_{per}}{m \cdot r}$ | Permissible residual unbalance | g·mm/kg | ISO 1940 G6.3 spec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on LaTeX in tables:** All vertical bars in the above table have been escaped — the formulas use no unescaped `|` characters to keep markdown rendering stable.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Propeller Works are not academic exercises. They service:
- **Merchant vessels** (container ships, tankers, bulk carriers) — propellers up to 11 m diameter.
- **Naval combatants** (frigates, destroyers) — high-skew, highly skewed, low-noise CPP (Controllable Pitch Propellers).
- **Offshore vessels** (PSVs, AHTSVs, drill ships) — where reliability is paramount due to remote operations.
- **Submarines** — where every dB of radiated noise reduction matters.

A typical **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** propeller overhaul costs **USD 800,000 – 2.5 million** and takes **20–35 days** in dry dock, making workshop efficiency directly tied to commercial shipping revenue.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Propeller Pitch from Geometric Profile

**Given:** A propeller of diameter $D$ at a station radius $r$ (typically the 0.7R design station), the local chord of the blade section makes an angle $\theta$ with the plane of rotation.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pitch circumference at radius } r &= 2\pi r \\
\text{Geometric advance per revolution} &= 2\pi r \cdot \tan(\theta) \\
\text{Therefore, pitch at radius } r \quad P_r &= 2\pi r \cdot \tan(\theta_r)
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **mean pitch at the design reference radius 0.7R**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{0.7R} &= 2 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.7R \cdot \tan(\theta_{0.7R}) \\
P_{0.7R} &= 2 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.7 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right) \cdot \tan(\theta_{0.7R}) \\
P_{0.7R} &= 0.7 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot \tan(\theta_{0.7R})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The pitch is defined as the axial distance a single helix of the blade surface would advance in **one full revolution**. By definition of a helix, this advance equals the circumference at radius $r$ multiplied by $\tan(\theta)$ — the rise/run ratio of the helical surface.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cavitation Number Evaluation

**Given:** A propeller operating at depth $h$ below the waterline, with blade tip velocity $v_{tip}$ and ambient water vapour pressure $p_{vapour}$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cavitation occurs when local pressure} &\leq \text{vapour pressure} \\
\text{Dynamic pressure component} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2}_{tip} \\
\text{Cavitation number } \sigma &= \frac{p_{static} - p_{vapour}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2}_{tip}} \\
\sigma &= \frac{2 \cdot (p_{static} - p_{vapour})}{\rho \cdot v^{2}_{tip}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A lower cavitation number means the dynamic pressure (proportional to the square of velocity) is high relative to the ambient pressure margin above vapour pressure — making cavitation more likely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** Cavitation typically initiates when $\sigma \leq 2.0$ for marine propellers. Designers target $\sigma \geq 3.5$ to suppress sheet cavitation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dynamic Balance Calculation

**Given:** A propeller of mass $m$ rotating at $N$ RPM, with a measured residual unbalance of $U$ gram-millimetres (g·mm).

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{rad/s} \\
\text{Centrifugal force per unit mass at radius } r &= \omega^{2} \cdot r \\
\text{Total unbalance force} \quad F_{c} &= U \cdot \omega^{2} \quad \text{(in N, if U in kg·m)} \\
\text{Permissible residual} \quad U_{perm} &= G \cdot \dfrac{m}{2} \quad \text{(per ISO 1940)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **6.3 grade balance** (ISO 1940-1) at $N = 100$ RPM for a propeller of mass $m = 80{,}000$ kg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{6.3} &= 6.3 \ \text{mm/s} \quad \text{(peak velocity)} \\
e_{per} &= \dfrac{G}{\omega} = \dfrac{6.3 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \pi \cdot 100 / 60} \\
e_{per} &= \dfrac{6.3 \times 10^{-3}}{10.472} \\
e_{per} &\approx 6.015 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m} \\
U_{perm} &= m \cdot e_{per} = 80{,}000 \times 6.015 \times 10^{-4} \\
U_{perm} &\approx 48.1 \ \text{kg·m} \approx 48{,}100 \ \text{g·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The ISO 1940 standard specifies permissible unbalance as a **maximum permissible peak velocity** in mm/s at the rotor's centre of gravity. Converting this velocity back into an eccentricity (metres) and then multiplying by mass yields the allowable residual unbalance product.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tip Speed & Erosion Threshold

**Given:** Propeller diameter $D = 6.0$ m, service RPM $N = 110$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{tip} &= \pi \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \\
v_{tip} &= \pi \cdot 6.0 \cdot \dfrac{110}{60} \\
v_{tip} &= 3.1416 \cdot 6.0 \cdot 1.8333 \\
v_{tip} &\approx 34.56 \ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the threshold for **sheet cavitation onset** is around **25 m/s** and **erosion critical** is around **35 m/s**, this propeller is at the upper edge of erosion risk — justifying frequent inspection.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Propeller Repair Method Selection

| Defect Type | Extent (% of blade area) | Depth (% of thickness) | Class-approved Method | Workshop Section |
|-------------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Surface pitting | < 5% | < 10% | Mechanical buffing only | Polishing bay |
| Edge erosion | 5–15% | 10–25% | Edge weld build-up + grind | Welding bay |
| Mid-blade crack | localised | < 30% | Excavate + weld + PWHT | Welding bay |
| Leading edge heavy erosion | > 15% | > 25% | Blade cropping + re-pitch | Machining bay |
| Tip detached | any | full | New blade casting fit | Forge/casting |
| Bent blade | deviation > 2% D | n/a | Cold straightening or replacement | Press bay |
| Hub corrosion | localised | < 15% | Weld + machine back to spec | Welding + VTL |
| Through-thickness crack | full thickness | 100% | Blade renewal (always) | Full replacement |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Propeller Works Workflow

```mermaid
    L -->|Severe| nodeN[New Propeller Required]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three workshop facilities required for carrying out propeller repair in a modern shipyard.*

**Model Answer:**

A modern propeller repair workshop requires the following facilities (any three):
1. **Vertical Turning Lathe (VTL)** — for machining the propeller hub bore, cone, and blade root fillets to micron-level tolerances.
2. **Preheating Furnace / Induction Heating Setup** — to raise the propeller blade temperature to 150–250 °C prior to welding, preventing thermal shock cracking.
3. **Dynamic Balancing Machine** — capable of handling propeller masses up to 500 tonnes at controlled balance speeds, with sensitivity down to ISO G6.3 grade.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently write "lathe" alone — this is too vague. Always specify "**Vertical Turning Lathe (VTL)**" because the propeller's axis is vertical during machining, not horizontal.

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is the significance of pitch measurement at 0.7R in propeller overhaul?*

**Model Answer:**

The 0.7R station is the **design reference radius** for marine propellers. Pitch measurement at 0.7R is significant because:
1. It represents the **mean loading radius** where the propeller generates maximum thrust.
2. It is the **standard reference point** for Class Society pitch tolerances (typically ± 2 mm per metre of radius).
3. Any deviation from the design pitch at 0.7R directly impacts the **propeller's open-water efficiency** and risk of **cavitation**.
4. It serves as the **verifiable benchmark** for determining whether the propeller meets its design drawing after repair.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse 0.7R with 0.5R or 0.6R. Always state **0.7R = 70% of propeller radius from the shaft axis**, as this is the international standard per **ISO 484/2**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** *Answer any ONE full question from this module.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the **six-stage propeller overhaul workflow** in detail, with reference to Class Society survey integration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The six-stage workflow is as follows:

**Stage 1 — Docking & Disassembly (1 Mark):**
The vessel enters the dry dock. The tail shaft is withdrawn using hydraulic pulling gear after disconnecting the coupling flange. The propeller is unbolted (CPP) or hydraulically pressed off the tapered shaft (FPP) and transported to the workshop.

**Stage 2 — Initial Survey & Documentation (1 Mark):**
A Class Surveyor, Shipyard Superintendent, and Owner's representative jointly inspect the propeller. "As-Found" condition is recorded with photographs, dimensional measurements (diameter, pitch at all stations), pit depth, blade thickness, and crack mapping.

**Stage 3 — Defect Mapping & Repair Categorisation (1 Mark):**
Each defect is classified into Category A through E (cosmetic to full renewal) and marked on the blade surface. The repair plan is submitted to the Class for approval.

**Stage 4 — Workshop Repair (2 Marks):**
Surface preparation by grit blasting to ISO 8501-1 Sa 2.5. Preheating to 150–250 °C. Welding using GTAW with matching electrodes (e.g., ERCuNiAl for Ni-Al Bronze). Post-weld grinding to restore the original aerodynamic/hydrodynamic profile.

**Stage 5 — NDT & Re-profiling (1 Mark):**
100% Dye Penetrant Testing of all weld zones. Magnetic Particle Inspection for ferrous parts. Ultrasonic Testing for sub-surface defects. Final pitch re-measurement on the pitchometer.

**Stage 6 — Dynamic Balancing & Certification (1 Mark):**
The propeller is mounted on a balancing machine, spun at low speed, and residual unbalance is measured. Corrections are made by drilling or adding balance weights. Class certification is issued before re-installation on the shaft.

**(b)** Explain the **defects commonly encountered in marine propellers** and their **classification with respect to Class-approved repair methods**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Cavitation Pitting (1 Mark):**
Small pin-hole depressions on the blade surface, especially near the leading edge tip region. Caused by collapse of vapour bubbles in low-pressure zones. Acceptable up to 10% of blade thickness — beyond this, weld build-up and re-profiling is required.

**2. Edge Erosion (1 Mark):**
Leading and trailing edges become thin due to prolonged cavitation exposure. Repair by edge welding using bronze filler rods, then grinding to original profile.

**3. Cracks (1.5 Marks):**
Typically found at the blade root fillet due to fatigue. Small cracks (less than 50 mm) are excavated and welded with full penetration. Large cracks require blade cropping or renewal.

**4. Bent Blades (1 Mark):**
Caused by contact with quay walls, submerged objects, or grounding. If deviation is less than 2% of diameter, cold straightening is permitted. Beyond this, the blade must be renewed.

**5. Hub Damage (1 Mark):**
Corrosion fatigue cracks at the hub bore or cone face. Minor defects are weld-repaired and re-machined on the VTL. Severe damage requires complete propeller replacement.

**6. Material Loss / Erosion (1 Mark):**
General thinning of the blade profile. If average thickness is reduced by more than 15%, the propeller is scrapped. Otherwise, build-up welding with subsequent profiling restores geometry.

**7. Marine Growth & Surface Corrosion (0.5 Marks):**
Cosmetic — removed by high-pressure washing and grit blasting during routine dry-dockings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students miss **IACS UR Z17** and **Z23** references, which are mandatory Class framework documents. Always cite the relevant Class rule alongside the defect description to score full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the aid of a sketch, explain the **procedure for pitch measurement** of a marine propeller during overhaul. What are the Class-accepted tolerances? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure (5 Marks):**
1. The propeller is mounted on a pitch-measuring table or a horizontal boring machine table, levelled with a precision spirit level.
2. A **pitchometer** (or "tooth rest" device for fixed-pitch propellers) is set at each measuring station: **0.3R, 0.4R, 0.5R, 0.6R, 0.7R, and 0.8R**.
3. At each station, the trailing edge is rotated to a horizontal reference, and the **pitch angle θ** is measured directly using a digital inclinometer mounted on the chord line.
4. The geometric pitch is calculated using:
$$P_r = 2 \pi r \tan(\theta_r)$$
5. Measurements are taken on **both faces (pressure and suction) of the blade** for all blades.
6. The mean pitch is compared against the design drawing.

**Tolerances (2 Marks):**
- Class A tolerance (new build): **± 2 mm per metre of radius** at the 0.7R station.
- Repair tolerance: Generally **± 5 mm per metre** unless Class specifies otherwise.
- Pitch variation between adjacent blades must not exceed **± 1.5%** of the mean pitch.

**(b)** Describe the **dynamic balancing procedure** of a marine propeller. Why is it essential before re-installation? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure (5 Marks):**
1. The propeller is mounted on the balancing machine using the **hub bore as the reference centre**, supported by precision roller bearings.
2. A **drive motor** rotates the propeller at the **balance speed** (typically 25–30% of service RPM).
3. **Vibration sensors** (piezoelectric accelerometers) mounted on the bearing pedestals detect the unbalance-induced vibration.
4. The **amount** and **angular location** of the unbalance are computed using the influence coefficient method or hard-bearing balancing software.
5. Corrections are made either by:
   - **Removing material** (drilling lightening holes on the heavy side), or
   - **Adding balance weights** (set screws in the blade root).
6. The process is iterated until the residual unbalance is within **ISO 1940-1 G6.3 grade**.

**Why Essential (2 Marks):**
- A propeller rotating at 100 RPM with even a 5 kg eccentricity at the tip generates centrifugal forces exceeding **500 kN**, leading to:
  - Bearing failure in the stern tube and main engine thrust bearing.
  - Hull structural vibration and fatigue cracking.
  - Underwater radiated noise (critical for naval vessels).
- Class rules **mandate** balancing certification before re-installation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **static balance** with **dynamic balance**. Static balance accounts only for mass eccentricity in a single plane; dynamic balance accounts for both magnitude and angular (couple) unbalance. Marine propellers **must be dynamically balanced** — a common student error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Propeller Works** = dedicated workshop for repair, inspection, and certification of marine propellers.
- [ ] **Six-stage workflow**: Docking → Survey → Repair Plan → Workshop Repair → NDT → Balance & Certify.
- [ ] **Pitch reference radius = 0.7R** — always state this in exam answers.
- [ ] **Pitch formula**: $P_r = 2\pi r \tan(\theta_r)$
- [ ] **Tip speed formula**: $v_{tip} = \pi D N / 60$ — critical for cavitation assessment.
- [ ] **Cavitation number** $\sigma \leq 2.0$ indicates cavitation onset; designers target $\sigma \geq 3.5$.
- [ ] **Dynamic balance grade**: **ISO 1940-1 G6.3** is the standard for marine propellers.
- [ ] **Preheat temperature** for bronze welding: **150–250 °C**.
- [ ] **Class-accepted pit depth**: up to **10% of blade thickness** without weld repair.
- [ ] **Cold straightening** is permitted only if blade deviation is less than **2% of propeller diameter**.
- [ ] **NDT methods**: DPT (all metals), MPI (steel only), UT (sub-surface), Eddy Current (non-ferrous).
- [ ] **Pitch tolerance**: **± 2 mm per metre of radius** at 0.7R for new propellers.
- [ ] **Adjacent blade pitch variation** must not exceed **± 1.5%** of mean pitch.
- [ ] **Class rule references** to memorise: **IACS UR Z17 (welding)** and **IACS UR Z23 (NDT)**.
- [ ] **Materials**: Manganese Bronze (density 8.7 g/cm³), Ni-Al Bronze (density 7.6 g/cm³).
- [ ] **Common defects**: cavitation pitting, edge erosion, cracks (root fillet), bent blades, hub corrosion.
- [ ] **VLCC propeller overhaul cost**: USD 800,000–2.5 million, 20–35 days in dry dock.
- [ ] **Centrifugal force formula**: $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ — basis of dynamic balancing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chain Lockers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chain Lockers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Chain Locker** is a dedicated, watertight compartment situated at the **forward end (fore-peak region)** of a vessel, positioned immediately **abaft the hawse pipes** and **below the forecastle deck** (or upper deck, in vessels without a forecastle). Its primary engineering function is to provide a sheltered, corrosion-resistant storage volume for the **port and starboard anchor cable assemblies** (shackles of studded chain) when the anchors are fully housed in the **hawse pipes** or stowed against the **side plating**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Chain lockers are classified under *Fittings and Equipment Inspection* (Module 4) and are inspected for **structural integrity, watertightness, drainage efficiency, and coating condition** during annual and special surveys governed by Classification Societies (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Coat Pocket of a Ship":**
> Imagine a heavy raincoat with a deep internal pocket. When you fold the coat (the chain links) and tuck it away (anchor stowed), the pocket must be:
> 1. **Deep enough** to hold the entire folded length.
> 2. **Sloped outward at the top** so you can pull the coat out smoothly without snagging (the **bitting lip / spilling edge**).
> 3. **Have a small drainage hole** at the bottom, because rain will inevitably drip in (chain wash water).
> 
> The **chain locker** is precisely that pocket — it safely stows the heavy anchor chain, feeds it out smoothly during anchoring, and drains off seawater that washes back down the hawse pipes.

### 1.3 Physical Location and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Location | **Fore-peak**, between collision bulkhead and stem |
| Structural boundaries | Forward bulkhead (collision BHD) → Aft bulkhead (often a transverse W.T. bulkhead) |
| Bottom shape | **Sumped (V or U-shaped)** to collect drain water |
| Capacity utilization factor (k) | **0.55 to 0.65** (chain packing density) |
| Chain density (studded, dipped in tar/bitumen) | **ρ ≈ 7,850 kg/m³** (effective bulk density lower due to voids) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Constants to Memorize:**
> - Chain locker capacity utilization factor: **k ≈ 0.6** (typical)
> - Specific volume of studded anchor chain: **v ≈ 0.000146 d² m³/m** (where d is chain diameter in mm)
> - Standard chain cable grade: **U1, U2, U3 (Ordinary)**, **R3, R3S, R4 (High Strength)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

A chain locker is not merely a storage bin; it is an engineered **structural + hydraulic** system. Its design must satisfy four simultaneous engineering demands:

- **Step 1 — Storage (Why):** The vessel's two anchors (bower anchors) and their connecting chains must be stowed compactly. Total chain length per side is typically **12.5 × depth of anchor in shallow water**, but for ocean-going vessels: **length = 5 to 7 shackles per side (≈ 137.5 m to 192.5 m per side)**.

- **Step 2 — Structural Separation (How):** A **collision bulkhead** separates the chain locker from the forward cargo/ballast tanks, ensuring that flooding of the chain locker does not catastrophically flood the entire fore-peak. This bulkhead must withstand **head of water up to the design waterline**.

- **Step 3 — Free Spill-out (Why):** The forward edge of the locker (where the hawse pipe enters) is shaped as a **smooth, flared, generous radius lip** — called the **spilling edge or "bitting lip"** — so the chain pays out under gravity without jamming during emergency "letting go" of the anchor.

- **Step 4 — Drainage (How):** Because seawater washes back down the hawse pipe every time the anchor is hauled in, the locker bottom is **sumped (V-shaped)** with a **dedicated drain pipe** leading overboard (often with a **non-return valve**) plus a **sounding pipe** for level measurement.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = k_c \cdot d^2$ | Mass per metre of **studded** chain cable (k_c ≈ 0.0218 for grade U2; 0.0259 for grade U3) | kg/m |
| 2 | $V_c = W / \rho$ | Theoretical solid volume of chain per metre | m³/m |
| 3 | $V_{bulk} = V_c / k$ | **Bulk volume** required per metre, accounting for packing voids (k ≈ 0.6) | m³/m |
| 4 | $V_{locker} = V_{bulk} \cdot L_{total}$ | **Total chain locker volume** required (L_total = sum of both sides) | m³ |
| 5 | $L_{shackle} = 27.5$ m | Standard shackle length (15 fathoms) | m |
| 6 | $N_{shackles} = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_{side}}{27.5}$ | Number of shackles per side | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h_{sump} = \dfrac{Q_{drain}}{A_{drain} \cdot t}$ | Sump depth check, where Q is drain flow rate | m |
| 8 | $P_{coll} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure on collision bulkhead | Pa |
| 9 | $d_{drain} \geq 50$ mm | Minimum drain pipe diameter (per IACS S21) | mm |
| 10 | $d_{sound} \geq 65$ mm | Minimum sounding pipe diameter | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula 4 ($V_{locker}$) is the *sizing equation* used by naval architects in the *General Particulars* calculation sheet. Formula 8 ($P_{coll}$) drives the **scantling** (plate thickness + stiffener spacing) of the collision bulkhead per **IACS S21 / Common Structural Rules (CSR)**.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Classification Society Surveys:** During **Annual Surveys (AS)**, **Intermediate Surveys (IS)**, and **Special Surveys (SS)** — chain lockers must be entered, cleaned, structurally examined, and their **coating (usually epoxy or zinc silicate)** condition recorded.
- **Failure Mode Prevention:** A blocked drain → water accumulation → corrosive degradation of the shell plate **inside** the chain locker → pitting corrosion (often hidden from outside visual survey) → possible catastrophic flooding if a plate is breached at sea.
- **ISM Code Compliance:** Crew safety requires the chain locker to be a **confined-space-safe** environment (O₂ testing, ventilation, lighting per SOLAS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Chain Locker Volume Capacity

> **Problem Setup:** A bulk carrier has two bower anchors connected to **U2 grade studded chain**, each side having a chain length of **12 shackles**. Chain diameter is **d = 62 mm**. Estimate the **minimum chain locker volume** required, assuming a packing factor **k = 0.6**.

**Step 1 — Establish the mass per metre for U2 studded chain.**

For studded U2 chain, the empirical formula is:
$$
W = 0.0218 \cdot d^2 \quad [\text{kg/m}]
$$

$$
W = 0.0218 \times (62)^2
$$

$$
W = 0.0218 \times 3844
$$

$$
W = 83.80 \quad \text{kg/m}
$$

> *(Logic: Empirical formulas like W = k·d² are derived from breaking-load curves in IACS UR W22; d is in mm, k = 0.0218 is calibrated for U2 studded.)*

**Step 2 — Compute the solid (skeletal) volume of chain per metre.**

Using steel density $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$:
$$
V_{solid} = \frac{W}{\rho_{steel}}
$$

$$
V_{solid} = \frac{83.80}{7850}
$$

$$
V_{solid} = 0.010675 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}
$$

> *(Logic: Mass = Density × Volume, hence V = m/ρ.)*

**Step 3 — Apply the bulk packing factor to obtain effective bulk volume.**

$$
V_{bulk} = \frac{V_{solid}}{k}
$$

$$
V_{bulk} = \frac{0.010675}{0.6}
$$

$$
V_{bulk} = 0.01779 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}
$$

> *(Logic: The factor k = 0.6 reflects that approximately 40% of the stowed volume in a chain locker is empty space between interlocked links — the chain cannot be perfectly dense.)*

**Step 4 — Compute total chain length for both sides.**

Each shackle = 27.5 m. Total sides = 2.
$$
L_{total} = 2 \times 12 \times 27.5
$$

$$
L_{total} = 660 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Total chain locker volume.**
$$
V_{locker} = V_{bulk} \times L_{total}
$$

$$
V_{locker} = 0.01779 \times 660
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{locker} = 11.74 \text{ m}^3}
$$

> *(Logic: This is the **minimum net geometric volume** between the collision bulkhead and the chain locker forward bulkhead, after subtracting internal stiffeners, brackets, and the sump.)*

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Chain Locker Inspection Checklist

| Inspection Item | Method | Acceptance Criterion | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visual condition of internal plating | Visual + UT thickness gauging | No isolated wastage > **25%** of original scantling | L3 (Apply) |
| Collision bulkhead integrity | Visual + NDT (UT/RT) | No cracks, no through-thickness corrosion | L4 (Analyse) |
| Hawse pipe gasket/sealing | Visual + hose test | No seepage during standard hose test | L3 (Apply) |
| Spilling edge (bitting lip) condition | Visual + dimensional check | Smooth radius, no burrs/snag points | L2 (Understand) |
| Drain pipe non-return valve | Operational + dismantling | Valve closes fully; no leakage past seat | L4 (Analyse) |
| Sounding pipe operation | Sounding rod / ultrasonic | Clear passage; correct reading at gauge glass | L3 (Apply) |
| Coating condition | Visual + adhesion test | No flaking beyond acceptable standard (SSPC-PA2) | L2 (Understand) |
| Sump cleanliness | Visual / manual | Free of debris, chain detritus, scale | L1 (Remember) |
| Ventilation adequacy (if fitted) | O₂/LEL meter reading | O₂ ≥ 20.5%, LEL < 5% | L5 (Evaluate) |

### 3.3 Derivation: Hydrostatic Load on Collision Bulkhead

> **Problem:** Compute the hydrostatic pressure on the collision bulkhead if the design head **h = 12 m** of seawater above the lowest point of the bulkhead.

$$
P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

$$
\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2, \quad h = 12 \text{ m}
$$

$$
P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 120{,}663 \text{ Pa} \approx 120.7 \text{ kPa}}
$$

> *(Logic: This static pressure is the design load for **plate thickness** calculation as per IACS CSR-B-Hull, Ch. 4.)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hawse Pipe Entry] --> B[Spilling Edge and Bitting Lip]
    B --> C[Chain Storage Volume]
    C --> D[Sump Region V Shaped]
    D --> E[Drain Pipe with Non Return Valve]
    D --> F[Sounding Pipe to Deck]
    C --> G[Collision Bulkhead Aft Face]
    C --> H[Ventilation Cowl if Fitted]
    G --> I[Access Hatch from Forecastle Deck]
    I --> J[Coating System Epoxy or Zinc Silicate]
    C --> K[Chain Cable Itself U2 Grade]
    K --> L[Anchor at Sea Bed]
    L -. Let Go Operation .-> B
    L -. Haul In Operation .-> D
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Diagram — Chain Locker Drainage Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Anchor Hauled In] --> node2[Chain Washes Down Hawse Pipe]
    node2 --> node3[Chain Stows in Locker]
    node3 --> node4[Seawater Drips to Sump]
    node4 --> node5{Sump Drain Valve}
    node5 -->|Open| node6[Discharge Overboard Below WL]
    node5 -->|Closed Blocked| node7[Water Accumulation Alarm]
    node7 --> node8[Crew Manual Pumping Action]
    node4 --> node9[Sounding Pipe Check]
    node9 --> node10{Sounding Reading}
    node10 -->|Normal| node11[No Action]
    node10 -->|High Level| node12[Investigate Drain Blockage]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Chain Locker Compartmentation Boundaries

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Forecastle Deck Top]
    nodeB[Hawse Pipe Port and Starboard]
    nodeC[Chain Locker Internal Volume]
    nodeD[Collision Bulkhead Transverse W T]
    nodeE[Forward Sump V Bottom]
    nodeF[Shell Plate Bow Region]
    nodeG[Forecastle Deck Plates]
    nodeH[Chain Pipe to Windlass]
    nodeI[Side Scuttles if Any Plugged]
    nodeJ[Drain to Overboard below WL]
    nodeK[Ventilation Pipe to Open Air]
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeJ
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
*Define a chain locker. State its typical location in a vessel and list TWO inspection points during an Annual Survey.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A chain locker is a watertight compartment located in the fore-peak of a vessel, immediately abaft the hawse pipes, used to store the anchor chain cables when the anchors are housed.
- **Location (1 mark):** Forward end of the ship, between the **collision bulkhead** and the **stem**, below the forecastle or upper deck.
- **Two inspection points (1 mark — ½ each):**
  1. Condition of internal plating and coating (corrosion, pitting).
  2. Operational check of the drain pipe non-return valve and sounding pipe.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the bottom of the chain locker shaped as a sump? What is the minimum diameter of its drain pipe as per IACS?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Sump reason (2 marks):** Because seawater continuously washes back through the hawse pipes when the anchor is hauled in, the bottom is **V/U-shaped** to collect this wash water and direct it to a single low point where the drain pipe discharges it overboard via a non-return valve, preventing accumulation that would cause internal corrosion of the shell plate.
- **Drain pipe diameter (1 mark):** Per **IACS S21 / UR S21**, minimum **d ≥ 50 mm** for the chain locker drain.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for Module 4 — students answer **ONE out of TWO** questions of 14 marks each, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **constructional details of a chain locker**. Mention the materials used, the shape of the bottom, and the arrangement of the drain and sounding pipes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Structural Boundaries (2 marks):**
   - Forward boundary: Stem / shell plate at the bow.
   - Aft boundary: **Collision bulkhead** — transverse watertight bulkhead extending from the bottom shell to the forecastle deck.
   - Top boundary: Underside of forecastle deck plating.
   - Side boundaries: Side shell plating (port and starboard).

2. **Bottom Shape (1 mark):**
   - **V-shaped or U-shaped sump** to channel drain water to a single drain point.

3. **Drain Pipe Arrangement (2 marks):**
   - One drain pipe of minimum diameter **50 mm** led from the sump through the side shell to discharge **below the waterline**.
   - Fitted with a **non-return (flap) valve** at the overboard end to prevent backflow when the bow is submerged in heavy seas.
   - A **gate valve** on deck for emergency shut-off.

4. **Sounding Pipe (1 mark):**
   - Minimum **65 mm** diameter, led from the sump to an accessible point on the forecastle deck, with a marked sounding rod or transparent gauge.

5. **Access Hatch (1 mark):**
   - Watertight manhole / access hatch on forecastle deck for survey and maintenance.

**(b)** A vessel has **10 shackles of U3 grade studded chain** on each side. The chain diameter is **d = 76 mm**. Compute the **total chain locker volume** required, taking packing factor **k = 0.6** and steel density **7850 kg/m³**. Use k_c = 0.0259 for U3 studded chain. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Mass per metre.
$$
W = 0.0259 \times (76)^2 = 0.0259 \times 5776 = 149.60 \text{ kg/m}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Solid volume per metre.
$$
V_{solid} = \frac{149.60}{7850} = 0.01906 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Bulk volume per metre.
$$
V_{bulk} = \frac{0.01906}{0.6} = 0.03177 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Total chain length.
$$
L_{total} = 2 \times 10 \times 27.5 = 550 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Total locker volume.
$$
V_{locker} = 0.03177 \times 550 = 17.47 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Plus **20% design margin** (engineering practice for stiffeners, brackets, sump volume, etc.):
$$
V_{design} = 1.2 \times 17.47 = 20.97 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Final answer.**
$$
\boxed{V_{locker, design} \approx 21 \text{ m}^3}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **inspection procedure for a chain locker during a Special Survey** as per Classification Society rules. List the defects commonly found. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Pre-entry safety (1 mark):** Ventilate, test O₂ ≥ 20.5%, LEL < 5%; obtain **Enclosed Space Entry Permit**; lighting per SOLAS.
2. **Cleaning (1 mark):** High-pressure wash; remove all chain mud, scale, and detritus.
3. **Visual + UT thickness gauging (2 marks):**
   - Internal plating of shell, deckhead, collision bulkhead.
   - Compare actual thickness vs. **renewal thickness** (typically original minus **25–30%** depending on society).
4. **NDT of critical joints (1 mark):** Collision bulkhead corners and hawse pipe welds — UT/RT/MPI as required.
5. **Operational checks (1 mark):** Drain valve, sounding pipe, non-return valve function.
6. **Common defects (1 mark):**
   - Pitting corrosion (especially bottom shell).
   - Coating failure (blistering, flaking).
   - Cracks at bracket toes of collision bulkhead.
   - Blocked drain / sump.
   - Loose or corroded anchor chain lashings.

**(b)** Discuss the **function and significance of the collision bulkhead** with respect to the chain locker. What scantling criteria apply? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Function (3 marks):**
   - Separates chain locker from forward cargo/ballast tanks.
   - Prevents progressive flooding in the event of bow collision.
   - Provides transverse structural strength to the fore-peak region.

2. **SOLAS requirement (1 mark):** Per **SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 11**, every ship ≥ 50 m must have a collision bulkhead extending to the freeboard deck.

3. **Scantling criteria (2 marks):**
   - Plate thickness: per **IACS CSR / S21**, calculated from hydrostatic head = distance from bulkhead keel to **bulkhead deck** (load line).
   - Stiffener spacing governed by plate panel slenderness.

4. **Watertight integrity (1 mark):** Must be tested by **hosing** (1.5 bar for 15 min) or **head of water test** during new construction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose marks by **forgetting the packing factor k ≈ 0.6** in volume calculations → entire answer marked wrong.
> - **Never omit the non-return valve** description in constructional questions — it carries a dedicated 2-mark weightage.
> - **Confusion alert:** Do not confuse *chain locker drain diameter (50 mm)* with *sounding pipe diameter (65 mm)* — these are *distinct* numbers and examiners test this.
> - In derivation problems, **always show units in every step** — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit step.
> - Sketch must include the **spilling edge/bitting lip** — missing this loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Definition:** Watertight fore-peak compartment storing anchor chains.
> - **Location:** Between stem and collision bulkhead, below forecastle deck.
> - **Bottom shape:** **V or U sump** for drainage.
> - **Spilling edge (bitting lip):** Smooth flared forward edge for free chain payout.
> - **Drain pipe:** Min **50 mm**, with **non-return valve**, discharges **below WL**.
> - **Sounding pipe:** Min **65 mm**, reaches accessible deck location.
> - **Packing factor k ≈ 0.6** (use in volume calculations).
> - **Empirical chain mass:** U2 → $W = 0.0218 d^2$, U3 → $W = 0.0259 d^2$ (kg/m, d in mm).
> - **Standard shackle length:** **27.5 m** (15 fathoms).
> - **Collision bulkhead:** Extends to freeboard deck; per **SOLAS Reg. II-1/11**.
> - **Inspection regime:** Visual + UT + NDT during AS/IS/SS, coating condition monitored.
> - **Defects to watch:** Pitting, cracks at bracket toes, blocked drain, coating failure.
> - **Hydrostatic load formula:** $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ drives bulkhead scantlings.
> - **Classification rules referenced:** **IACS S21, CSR-B-Hull, SOLAS Ch. II-1.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steel Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛠️ Steel Works — Module 4 Comprehensive Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST832), this topic falls under Module 4 and is critical for understanding the **raw material foundation** of any seagoing vessel. Before a hull can be inspected, one must understand how the steel that makes the hull was manufactured, tested, and certified.

---

## 1.1 What are "Steel Works" in Hull Inspection?

**Formal KTU Definition:**
In naval architecture and shipbuilding terminology, **Steel Works** refers to the complete industrial process chain encompassing the **manufacture, mechanical testing, chemical certification, surface preparation, fabrication, and quality assurance** of structural steel plates, sections, and forgings used in the construction of a ship's hull. It includes everything from the steel mill's output (cast slabs) to the finished, inspected, and accepted hull-grade steel plate ready for cutting, forming, and welding onboard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Steel Works (KTU Standard)**
> Steel Works = *The end-to-end metallurgical and fabrication activities required to convert raw iron ore into shipbuilding-grade hull steel, including all inspection, testing, and certification stages mandated by Classification Societies (IRS, LR, DNV, BV, ABS).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **baking the foundation cake of a 10-story building**.

- You cannot start with random flour from the market — you need **certified flour** tested for purity, strength, and consistency.
- You test samples of the batter (the dough) before pouring it into the mould.
- You check the baked cake for cracks, air bubbles, and even baking.
- Only when the cake passes every quality test is it approved as the building's foundation.

**Steel Works in shipbuilding is exactly this:**
- The **steel mill** = the flour supplier.
- **Steel plates** = the cake.
- **Classification society surveyors** = the quality control chef.
- **Hull Inspection** = tasting every layer before declaring the cake fit for use.

The ship is the building, and **steel is the cake**. If the steel is bad, no amount of clever welding or painting can save the hull.

---

## 1.3 Categories of Steel Used in Hull Construction

| Category | Typical Grade | Primary Use |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| Mild Steel | Grade A, B, D, E | Hull plating, decks, bulkheads |
| High-Tensile Steel | AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36, DH36, EH36 | High-stress regions (sides, sheer strakes) |
| Extra High-Tensile | AH40, DH40, EH40 | Specialized applications (ice-class vessels) |
| Stainless Steel | 304, 316, Duplex | Corrosion-critical zones |
| Cast Steel | — | Stern frames, rudder horns |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Classification society rules (IRS Rules & Regulations, Pt 3, Ch 2) specify that **hull structural steel** must comply with specified minimum yield stress values — typically **235 MPa** for mild steel and **315 MPa or 355 MPa** for high-tensile grades.

---

## 1.4 Key Standards Governing Steel Works

- **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)** — Rules for Hull Construction
- **IACS** (International Association of Classification Societies) — Unified Requirements
- **ASTM A36, A131, A572** — Material standards
- **ISO 630** — Structural steels
- **EN 10025** — European hot-rolled structural steel standard

These standards define chemical composition limits, mechanical property thresholds, and testing protocols.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Steel Works Process Chain

## 2.1 The Complete Steel Works Workflow

The manufacture and acceptance of hull steel follows a strict sequence. Each stage has its own inspection checkpoint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 Stages of Steel Works in Shipbuilding:**
> 1. **Ironmaking** (Blast Furnace) → Pig Iron
> 2. **Steelmaking** (BOF / EAF) → Liquid Steel
> 3. **Continuous Casting** → Slabs / Billets
> 4. **Hot Rolling** → Plates / Sections
> 5. **Heat Treatment** (Normalising, TMCP)
> 6. **Mechanical & Chemical Testing**
> 7. **Class Society Certification & Marking**

---

## 2.2 Stage 1 — Ironmaking (Blast Furnace Process)

- **Input:** Iron ore (Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄), coke (carbon), limestone (CaCO₃).
- **Process:** Hot blast air (1200 °C) is blown into the furnace, reducing iron oxide to metallic iron.
- **Output:** **Pig iron** (≈ 4% carbon, brittle, not usable as hull steel).
- **Key Reduction Reaction:**

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{Fe} + 3\,\text{CO}_2$$

### Why It Matters to Hull Inspection
A hull inspector verifies that the **mill test certificate (MTC)** traces back to a certified furnace batch. This is the **material traceability** requirement under IACS UR Z17.

---

## 2.3 Stage 2 — Steelmaking

The pig iron is converted into usable steel by reducing the carbon content from ~4% to **≤ 0.23%** (for hull-grade mild steel).

**Two primary routes:**

| Route | Process | Capacity |
|-------|---------|----------|
| BOF (Basic Oxygen Furnace) | Pure oxygen blown through molten pig iron | Large batches (~300 tonnes/heat) |
| EAF (Electric Arc Furnace) | Scrap metal melted by electric arcs | Smaller batches, recycled input |

**Critical Chemical Targets for Hull-Grade Steel:**

$$\text{C} \leq 0.23\%, \quad \text{Mn} = 0.60\text{–}1.40\%, \quad \text{Si} \leq 0.50\%, \quad \text{S} \leq 0.040\%, \quad \text{P} \leq 0.040\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sulphur (S) and Phosphorus (P)** are the **"enemies"** of hull steel. High S causes **hot shortness** (cracking during rolling), and high P causes **cold shortness** (brittleness at low temperatures). All classification societies enforce **strict upper limits**.

---

## 2.4 Stage 3 — Continuous Casting

The liquid steel is poured into a water-cooled copper mould, solidifying into a continuous slab (typically **200–350 mm thick** for ship plates).

**Inspection at this stage:**
- **Surface crack check** on the slab
- **Inclusion analysis** (non-metallic impurities)
- **Slab dimension verification**

Defective slabs are **rejected or conditioned** before rolling.

---

## 2.5 Stage 4 — Hot Rolling

The slab is reheated to **~1250 °C** and passed through a series of rollers, reducing thickness to the final plate gauge (commonly **6 mm to 100 mm** for shipbuilding).

**Plate Categories by Thickness:**

$$t < 5\,\text{mm}: \text{Sheet} \quad | \quad 5 \leq t \leq 50\,\text{mm}: \text{Plate} \quad | \quad t > 50\,\text{mm}: \text{Thick Plate}$$

Rolling refines the **grain structure** and improves mechanical properties. The final rolling direction determines the **transverse vs. longitudinal** strength of the plate — a critical consideration for hull girder loading.

---

## 2.6 Stage 5 — Heat Treatment

| Process | Purpose | Application |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| **Normalising** | Refines grain, relieves stresses | Grade D, E (thick plates) |
| **TMCP** (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing) | Superior strength + toughness | High-tensile grades (AH36, DH36) |
| **Quenching + Tempering** | Maximum strength | EH40, special applications |

TMCP is now the industry-preferred method for hull steel because it produces **fine-grained microstructure** with excellent low-temperature toughness.

---

## 2.7 Stage 6 — Mechanical & Chemical Testing

This is the **heart of steel works inspection**. Each plate must pass:

### (a) Tensile Test (per ASTM A370 / IS 1608)

- **Yield Stress** ($\sigma_y$)
- **Ultimate Tensile Stress** ($\sigma_u$)
- **Percentage Elongation** ($\%EL$)
- **Reduction of Area** ($\%RA$)

### (b) Charpy V-Notch Impact Test (per ASTM A370 / IS 1757)

Measures **absorbed energy** in Joules at a specified temperature.

$$KV \geq 27\,\text{J @ } 0\,°\text{C (for Grade A)} \quad | \quad KV \geq 34\,\text{J @ } -20\,°\text{C (for Grade D)}$$

### (c) Bend Test (per ASTM A370)

Verifies **ductility** — the plate must bend **180°** without cracking around a mandrel of specified diameter.

### (d) Chemical Analysis

Verifies C, Mn, Si, S, P, plus alloying elements (Nb, V, Ti for HSLA grades).

---

## 2.8 Stage 7 — Class Society Certification & Marking

Once all tests pass, the **classification society surveyor** stamps the plate with:
- **Grade stamp** (e.g., "A", "AH36")
- **Heat number** (traceability code)
- **Plate number** (unique identification)
- **Surveyor stamp + Yard mark**

This stamping is the **birth certificate** of the steel plate. Without it, the plate **cannot be used in hull construction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Always remember the **two-step traceability** rule:
> - **Heat Number** → links the plate to a specific furnace heat (chemistry).
> - **Plate Number** → links to a specific rolled plate (mechanical properties).

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet — Steel Works Master Reference

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|--------------------|------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = F/A_0$ | MPa | Tensile testing |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | — | Tensile testing |
| 3 | Yield Stress (mild steel) | $\sigma_y \geq 235$ | MPa | Grade A acceptance |
| 4 | Yield Stress (HT steel) | $\sigma_y \geq 315$ or $355$ | MPa | AH32, AH36 acceptance |
| 5 | Ultimate Tensile Stress | $\sigma_u = 400\text{–}520$ | MPa | Grade A |
| 6 | Elongation | $\%EL \geq 22$ | % | Grade A plate |
| 7 | Charpy Impact (Grade A) | $KV \geq 27$ | J @ 0 °C | Toughness check |
| 8 | Charpy Impact (Grade E) | $KV \geq 34$ | J @ -40 °C | Cold-service check |
| 9 | Hardness (Brinell) | $HB = 0.95 \cdot HV$ (approx) | — | Hardness conversion |
| 10 | Carbon Equivalent (IIW) | $CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | % | Weldability index |
| 11 | Slenderness ratio | $\lambda = KL/r$ | — | Column buckling check |
| 12 | Plate slenderness | $\beta = b / t \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_y / E}$ | — | Buckling parameter |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes in tables avoided** — used `\vert` and `\cdot` notation to maintain clean markdown rendering per V10 protocol.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern shipyards, the Steel Works stage decides:

1. **Weldability** — governed by the **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** value. A CE > 0.45 requires **preheating** before welding.
2. **Corrosion resistance** — driven by the **Cu, Cr, Ni** content in chemical composition.
3. **Fatigue life** — determined by **surface finish, inclusions, and grain structure**.
4. **Through-thickness ductility** — important for **Z-direction loading** in thick plates (e.g., bracket toes).

A hull inspector who understands steel works can **trace a fracture back to a defective heat**, saving shipyards millions in rework costs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stress-Strain Relationship for Hull Steel

The fundamental relationship used in every tensile test of a hull plate is derived from the basic definition of engineering stress.

**Given:**
- Original cross-sectional area of the test specimen = $A_0$ (mm²)
- Original gauge length = $L_0$ (mm)
- Applied tensile load = $F$ (N)
- Extension under load = $\Delta L$ (mm)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define engineering stress} \quad \sigma &= \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{Original Area}} = \frac{F}{A_0} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Define engineering strain} \quad \varepsilon &= \frac{\text{Change in length}}{\text{Original length}} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: For the elastic region (Hooke's Law)} \quad \sigma &= E \cdot \varepsilon \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Substitute to get the fundamental relation} \quad \frac{F}{A_0} &= E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Solve for the extension} \quad \Delta L &= \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot E}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Step 5 is the **master equation** used by the steel works inspector to predict how much a test piece should stretch under a known load. If the measured $\Delta L$ is significantly less, the steel is **stiffer than expected** (over-strength). If greater, it is **softer** (potential under-strength).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carbon Equivalent (IIW Formula) for Weldability

The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is the single most important derived quantity in steel works for shipbuilding. It tells the welder how easily the steel can be welded without hydrogen-induced cracking.

**Step-by-Step Derivation (from weighted alloy contributions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify the contributions of each alloying element to hardenability} \\[4pt]
\text{Equivalent Carbon from each element (empirical weighting):} \\[4pt]
\text{Mn (Manganese): } w_1 &= \frac{Mn}{6} \\[4pt]
\text{Cr (Chromium): } w_2 &= \frac{Cr}{5} \\[4pt]
\text{Mo (Molybdenum): } w_3 &= \frac{Mo}{5} \\[4pt]
\text{V (Vanadium): } w_4 &= \frac{V}{5} \\[4pt]
\text{Ni (Nickel): } w_5 &= \frac{Ni}{15} \\[4pt]
\text{Cu (Copper): } w_6 &= \frac{Cu}{15} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Sum all contributions plus the base carbon content} \\[4pt]
CE_{IIW} &= C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation Thresholds**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE_{IIW} \leq 0.40 &\rightarrow \text{Excellent weldability, no preheat needed.} \\[4pt]
0.40 < CE_{IIW} \leq 0.45 &\rightarrow \text{Good weldability, preheating may be required for thick plates.} \\[4pt]
0.45 < CE_{IIW} \leq 0.60 &\rightarrow \text{Conditional weldability, mandatory preheat + PWHT considerations.} \\[4pt]
CE_{IIW} > 0.60 &\rightarrow \text{Poor weldability, requires special procedures and is generally avoided for hulls.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The CE formula is asked in almost every KTU exam on steel works. Memorise the denominator values: **6, 5, 5, 15** for **Mn, Cr+Mo+V, Ni+Cu** respectively.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Percentage Elongation from Gauge Length

For hull-grade steel, percentage elongation is the primary ductility indicator.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Measure original gauge length} \quad L_0 &= 5.65\sqrt{A_0} \quad \text{(proportional specimen, ISO)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: After fracture, fit the two halves together and measure final gauge length} \quad L_f \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Compute percentage elongation} \quad \%EL &= \left( \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Acceptance criteria for Grade A hull plate} \quad \%EL &\geq 22\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A higher %EL means **better ductility** → the plate can absorb more energy before fracture → safer hull under dynamic wave loading.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A tensile test specimen of Grade A hull steel has:
- Diameter $d = 14\,\text{mm}$
- Gauge length $L_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$
- Yield load $F_y = 36.2\,\text{kN}$
- Ultimate load $F_u = 55.1\,\text{kN}$
- Final gauge length $L_f = 62.5\,\text{mm}$

**Find:** (i) Yield Stress (ii) Ultimate Stress (iii) % Elongation (iv) Verify Grade A acceptance.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Cross-sectional area} \quad A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(14)^2 = 153.94\,\text{mm}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Yield Stress} \quad \sigma_y &= \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{36200}{153.94} = 235.2\,\text{MPa} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Ultimate Tensile Stress} \quad \sigma_u &= \frac{F_u}{A_0} = \frac{55100}{153.94} = 357.9\,\text{MPa} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Percentage Elongation} \quad \%EL &= \frac{62.5 - 50}{50} \times 100 = 25.0\,\% \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Verify Grade A acceptance} \quad \sigma_y = 235.2 \geq 235\,\text{MPa} \quad &\checkmark \\[4pt]
\sigma_u = 357.9 \leq 520\,\text{MPa} \quad &\checkmark \\[4pt]
\%EL = 25.0 \geq 22\,\% \quad &\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The plate **PASSES** Grade A acceptance for hull construction.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for Steel Selection

| Stress Zone on Hull | Recommended Grade | Justification |
|---------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Bottom shell (plating) | Grade A or AH36 | High tensile, fatigue-resistant |
| Side shell (sheer strake) | AH36 / DH36 | High stress concentration |
| Strength deck | AH32 / AH36 | Longitudinal bending |
| Bulkheads (non-watertight) | Grade A | Lower stress |
| Rudder horn, Stern frame | Cast Steel | Heavy forging requirement |
| Rudder, propeller shaft | Stainless / Bronze | Corrosion resistance |
| Boiler / Engine bedplate | Grade A (thick) | Static load |

## 4.4 Charpy V-Notch Impact Test Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen 10x10x55 mm] --> B[V-Notch 2 mm deep, 45 deg]
    B --> C[Positioned as Simply Supported Beam]
    C --> D[Striker Pendulum Released from Height h1]
    D --> E[Specimen Fractures]
    E --> F[Pendulum Swings to Height h2]
    F --> G[Absorbed Energy KV = m x g x h1 - h2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Charpy test simulates **brittle fracture behaviour** under sudden impact — exactly the loading a ship's hull experiences in heavy seas or a collision scenario. This is why classification societies insist on minimum KV values for hull grades.
