# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define "Steel Works" as applicable to hull construction. List any four stages of the steel works process.**

**Model Answer:**
Steel Works in shipbuilding refers to the complete industrial chain from raw material preparation to certified hull-grade steel plate delivery. The four main stages are:

1. **Ironmaking** in the blast furnace (producing pig iron).
2. **Steelmaking** in BOF or EAF (controlling carbon content).
3. **Continuous Casting** (forming slabs/billets).
4. **Hot Rolling and Heat Treatment** (producing plates).
5. **Mechanical and Chemical Testing** (certifying properties).
6. **Class society marking and traceability stamping.**

*(Any four correctly named stages fetch full marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**What is the Carbon Equivalent (CE) of steel? State the formula and its significance in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**
Carbon Equivalent is a numerical value that expresses the combined effect of all alloying elements (other than carbon) on the hardenability and weldability of steel.

$$CE_{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

**Significance in shipbuilding:**
- Determines the **weldability** of hull steel.
- If CE ≤ 0.40 → excellent weldability (no preheat needed).
- If CE > 0.45 → preheating is mandatory to prevent hydrogen cracking.
- Used by hull inspectors to verify whether the welding procedure is suitable for the steel grade.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Two questions are provided below as alternatives. In the actual exam, students attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **manufacturing process of hull-grade steel plates**, starting from ironmaking to the final certified plate. Include a brief note on TMCP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tensile test specimen of Grade AH36 hull steel has a diameter of 20 mm and a gauge length of 100 mm. The yield load recorded is 99.0 kN, the ultimate load is 137.5 kN, and the final gauge length after fracture is 124 mm. Verify whether the plate meets Grade AH36 acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1: Ironmaking in the Blast Furnace.**
Iron ore (Fe₂O₃), coke (carbon source), and limestone (flux) are charged into a blast furnace. Hot air at ~1200 °C reduces the iron oxide to metallic iron. The product is **pig iron** containing ~4% carbon.

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{CO} \rightarrow 2\,\text{Fe} + 3\,\text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2: Steelmaking (BOF or EAF).**
Pig iron is converted to steel by blowing oxygen (BOF) or melting scrap (EAF). Carbon content is reduced to ≤ 0.23% for hull grades. Alloying elements (Mn, Nb, V) are added to achieve desired properties.

**Step 3: Continuous Casting.**
Liquid steel is poured into a water-cooled mould, forming a continuous slab of 200–350 mm thickness.

**Step 4: Hot Rolling.**
Slabs are reheated to 1250 °C and rolled into plates of 6–100 mm thickness. The rolling direction defines longitudinal vs. transverse mechanical properties.

**Step 5: Heat Treatment (with note on TMCP).**
**TMCP (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing)** combines controlled rolling with accelerated cooling to produce **fine-grained microstructure**. The result:
- Higher strength (≥ 355 MPa yield for AH36)
- Excellent low-temperature toughness (Charpy values)
- Reduced reliance on heavy alloying → better weldability

**Step 6: Testing and Certification.**
Tensile, Charpy, bend, and chemical tests are conducted. Classification society surveyors stamp the plate with grade, heat number, and acceptance marks.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $d = 20\,\text{mm}$, Gauge length $L_0 = 100\,\text{mm}$
- $F_y = 99.0\,\text{kN}$, $F_u = 137.5\,\text{kN}$
- $L_f = 124\,\text{mm}$

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(400) = 314.16\,\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Yield Stress**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{99000}{314.16} = 315.2\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Ultimate Tensile Stress**

$$\sigma_u = \frac{137500}{314.16} = 437.7\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Elongation**

$$\%EL = \frac{124 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 24.0\,\%$$

**Step 5: Verify Grade AH36 Acceptance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y \geq 315\,\text{MPa?} &\quad 315.2 \geq 315 \quad \checkmark \\[4pt]
\sigma_u \in [400, 530]\,\text{MPa?} &\quad 437.7 \in [400, 530] \quad \checkmark \\[4pt]
\%EL \geq 21\,\%? &\quad 24.0 \geq 21 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The plate **PASSES** Grade AH36 acceptance criteria. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Discuss the various **mechanical tests** performed on hull-grade steel plates. Explain the Charpy V-notch impact test with a neat sketch description. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The chemical composition of a hull steel plate is as follows: C = 0.18%, Mn = 1.10%, Cr = 0.05%, Mo = 0.02%, V = 0.01%, Ni = 0.08%, Cu = 0.10%. Calculate the Carbon Equivalent (CE) and comment on its weldability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**The Four Key Mechanical Tests on Hull Steel Plates:**

**1. Tensile Test (per ASTM A370 / IS 1608):**
- Provides **Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$)**, **Ultimate Tensile Stress ($\sigma_u$)**, **% Elongation**, and **% Reduction in Area**.
- A round or flat specimen is pulled in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) until fracture.

**2. Charpy V-Notch Impact Test (per ASTM A370 / IS 1757):**
- A standardised specimen (10 × 10 × 55 mm) with a 2 mm deep V-notch is struck by a swinging pendulum.
- The **absorbed energy** (in Joules) at the test temperature indicates the **toughness** of the steel.
- **Minimum Acceptance:**
  - Grade A: ≥ 27 J at 0 °C
  - Grade D: ≥ 34 J at -20 °C
  - Grade E: ≥ 34 J at -40 °C
- A **high Charpy value** indicates ductile behaviour; **low value** → brittle fracture risk.

**Sketch Description (functional):**
The specimen is supported as a **simply supported beam** between two anvils (span 40 mm). A pendulum hammer is released from a known height. The hammer strikes the side opposite the notch, fracturing the specimen. The energy absorbed is calculated from the difference in swing heights:

$$KV = m \cdot g \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$$

**3. Bend Test (per ASTM A370):**
- A plate specimen is bent 180° around a mandrel of specified diameter.
- No cracks on the outer surface → ductility confirmed.

**4. Hardness Test (Brinell / Vickers / Rockwell):**
- Provides an approximate check on tensile strength:
$$\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times HB \quad (\text{empirical, MPa})$$
- Used for forgings, castings, and rough checks.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** C = 0.18%, Mn = 1.10%, Cr = 0.05%, Mo = 0.02%, V = 0.01%, Ni = 0.08%, Cu = 0.10%

**Step 1: Apply the IIW Formula**

$$CE_{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE_{IIW} &= 0.18 + \frac{1.10}{6} + \frac{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.01}{5} + \frac{0.08 + 0.10}{15} \\[6pt]
&= 0.18 + 0.1833 + \frac{0.08}{5} + \frac{0.18}{15} \\[6pt]
&= 0.18 + 0.1833 + 0.0160 + 0.0120 \\[6pt]
CE_{IIW} &= 0.3913\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comment on Weldability**

Since **CE = 0.3913 < 0.40**, the steel has **excellent weldability**.
- No preheating is required for normal plate thicknesses.
- Standard hull welding procedures (SMAW, FCAW, GMAW) can be used.
- Low risk of **hydrogen-induced cracking** in the heat-affected zone (HAZ).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Hull Yard Tip:** Although CE < 0.40 generally allows no preheat, shipyard practice is to **always preheat thicker plates (>25 mm)** to remove moisture and ensure consistent weld quality, even when CE is low.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong CE formula** — students often write Mn/10 instead of Mn/6. This loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** for stress (MPa must be specified).
> 3. **Misreading the problem** — students sometimes compute $L_f - L_0$ as a fraction instead of percentage.
> 4. **Mixing Grade A and AH36** limits — A requires $\sigma_y \geq 235\,\text{MPa}$; AH36 requires $\sigma_y \geq 355\,\text{MPa}$.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** — always end with a clear ✅ / ❌ verification statement.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- 🔩 **Steel Works = Mill to Plate** — every step from blast furnace to stamped plate is part of "steel works" in hull context.
- ⚙️ **Grade A minimum yield stress = 235 MPa**; **AH36 minimum yield stress = 355 MPa** — memorise these two values.
- 🧪 **Carbon Equivalent formula** uses denominators **6, 5, 15** — Mn/6, (Cr+Mo+V)/5, (Ni+Cu)/15.
- ❄️ **Charpy Impact Test** measures toughness at low temperature — critical for cold-service vessels (ice-class, polar ships).
- 🔍 **Two-step traceability** = Heat Number (chemistry) + Plate Number (mechanical).
- 🏷️ **Classification Society stamping** is mandatory — without it, the steel cannot enter the hull.
- 📋 **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** is the steel's birth certificate — must be verified at receipt inspection.
- 🔥 **TMCP** is the modern preferred heat-treatment method — yields high strength + toughness + good weldability.
- ⚠️ **Sulphur ≤ 0.040%, Phosphorus ≤ 0.040%** are absolute upper limits in hull steel.
- 🛠️ **Weldability acceptance** = CE ≤ 0.45 (typical hull limit); CE > 0.60 → reject.
- 📐 **Bend Test** verifies ductility — 180° bend without cracking is the acceptance.
- 📏 **% Elongation** for Grade A plate ≥ 22% — a key ductility benchmark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — PLANNING CHARTS IN HULL INSPECTION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Planning Charts (Docking Plans / Hull Form Drawings)** are a set of pre-prepared, scale-accurate technical drawings used by naval architects, ship surveyors, and dry-dock supervisors to plan and safely execute a vessel's dry-docking, hull inspection, thickness measurement, and repair campaign. They integrate hull geometry, block positions, appendage locations, and critical structural zones into a single decision-support document.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a *Planning Chart* is **not just a drawing** — it is a coordinated bundle of:

- The **General Arrangement (GA) Plan**
- The **Hull Lines Plan** (Sheer, Body, and Half-Breadth)
- The **Shell Expansion / Shell Plate Development Plan**
- The **Docking Plan / Docking Drawing** (with block layout)
- The **Longitudinal Profile / Profile Plan**
- The **Thickness Measurement Plan (TMP)**
- The **Appendage Drawing** (rudder, propeller shaft, bilge keel, stabilizer fins)
- The **Corrosion / Wastage Allowance Chart**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Blueprint" for Ships

Imagine a critically ill patient being wheeled into an operation theatre. Before any surgeon touches the patient, the hospital staff has already prepared:

1. **An X-Ray / MRI map** of the patient (→ Hull Lines Plan — knows the body shape)
2. **A pre-op layout** showing exactly where to cut, where the support cushions go, and which organs to avoid (→ Docking Plan — knows where the keel blocks will land safely)
3. **A biopsy chart** indicating where tissue samples must be taken (→ Thickness Measurement Plan — knows where to gauge steel)
4. **A surgical checklist** of weak zones needing extra care (→ Corrosion / Wastage Chart)

A **Planning Chart** is exactly this — the **pre-operative blueprint** of the ship. Without it, the dock-master would be guessing where to place supports, risking **hogging, sagging, or even collapse of the hull girder** on the dock blocks. With it, the entire inspection campaign is executed like clockwork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric (IACS / Class Society):** The docking plan is typically drawn to a scale of **1:200** for the profile and **1:100** for block layouts, with all longitudinal positions referenced to **AP (After Perpendicular) or Midship (+/−) in millimetres** and transverse positions to **centerline (CL)**.

---

### 1.3 Why Planning Charts Are Indispensable

A ship in water is supported by hydrostatic pressure along its entire hull. The moment she is grounded on **dry-dock blocks**, that distributed support is replaced by **discrete point supports**. If these discrete supports are misplaced:

- **Local panel buckling** can occur (thin bottom plating)
- **Hull girder bending failure** can occur (improper trim)
- **Appendages** (sea-chest grating, propeller, bilge keel) can collide with the dock floor
- **Repair cost** skyrockets due to secondary damage

The Planning Chart **eliminates all of these risks** by mapping every contact point in advance.

---

### 1.4 Classes of Information Encoded in a Planning Chart

A complete planning chart pack communicates six classes of information:

| Class | Information Encoded | Used By |
|:------|:-------------------|:--------|
| Geometric | Hull form lines, offsets, station numbers | Naval Architect |
| Structural | Plate thickness, frame spacing, longitudinal locations | Surveyor |
| Operational | Draft forward/aft, trim angle, list angle | Dock Master |
| Safety | Sea chest locations, bilge keel edges, propeller tip clearance | Dock Master |
| Inspection | TMP zones, close-up areas, NDT locations | Class Surveyor |
| Repair | Renewal boundaries, doublings, insert plate dimensions | Shipyard Foreman |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Docking Plan

A **Docking Plan** (the single most important planning chart) is composed of:

**(a) Profile View (Side Elevation):** Shows the longitudinal profile of the hull above the keel. The keel baseline is drawn horizontally; the bottom curvature is plotted station-by-station. Critical features marked:

- Forward and aft drafts at the moment of touchdown
- Trim angle $\theta$
- Block heights at each station
- Position of appendages (echo-sounder dome, sea chests, bilge keel edges)
- Propeller tip-to-keel clearance
- Rudder hinge-to-keel clearance

**(b) Block Layout View (Plan View from Below):** Shows the transverse position of keel blocks and side blocks, drawn as if looking up at the hull from underneath. Each block is numbered (e.g., **B-01, B-02 … B-N**), and its longitudinal station is tabulated.

**(c) Aft and Forward End Views:** Show the transverse curvature and the location of side blocks on the bilge.

**(d) Block Loading Schedule (Table):** Lists, for each block, the maximum permissible reaction force in **tonnes** or **kN**.

---

## 2.2 Hull Lines Plan — The Three-View Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Hull Lines Plan** is the geometric "DNA" of the ship. From this plan, all other planning charts are derived.

The hull form is fully defined by **three orthographic projections** linked by common station lines:

1. **Sheer Plan (Profile View):** Side elevation showing waterlines, buttock lines, and station lines.
2. **Body Plan (Transverse Sections):** Aft body sections on the right, forward body sections on the left, with centreline as the mirror axis.
3. **Half-Breadth Plan (Horizontal Sections):** Plan view showing waterlines as horizontal slices of the hull.

The **Offsets Table** (numerical data behind the lines) gives, for each **station number** ($S = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 20$, where 0 is AP and 10 is midship), the **half-breadths** at each waterline and the **heights** at each buttock. These offsets feed directly into the docking plan.

---

## 2.3 Shell Expansion Plan

The **Shell Expansion** is the unrolled (developed) view of the entire outer hull plating. It is generated by:

- Taking each station's intersection with the shell
- "Unrolling" the curved surface into a flat plane
- Drawing the plate seams, butts, and frame lines onto the flat

**Why is this a planning chart?** Because:

- It shows every **plate by number** (P-101, P-102, …) and dimensions (length × breadth × thickness).
- It identifies **renewal zones** during dry-dock.
- It is used to order **insert plates** in advance.
- It is the master reference for **paint condition surveys** (zone A, B, C coatings).

---

## 2.4 Thickness Measurement Plan (TMP)

The TMP is a planning chart that overlays gauging locations onto the shell expansion or onto selected structural members. Each gauging point is assigned:

- A unique **Gauging Point ID** (e.g., **G-A1-P-S1** meaning: Zone A, frame 1, port side, station 1)
- A **Class-required minimum thickness** $t_{\min}$
- An **as-built thickness** $t_{\text{as-built}}$
- A **corrosion addition** $t_{\text{corr}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substantial Corrosion Threshold (IACS UR Z7.1 / Z7.2):** A plate or member is deemed substantially corroded when measured thickness $t_{\text{meas}} \leq t_{\min} + 0.5\,\text{mm}$.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Used For |
|:-:|:--------------------|:------:|:----------------------|:---------|
| 1 | Corrosion wastage | $w = t_{\text{as-built}} - t_{\text{meas}}$ | $\text{mm}$ | Calculating actual wastage |
| 2 | Remaining thickness percentage | $\%\text{RT} = (t_{\text{meas}} / t_{\text{as-built}}) \times 100$ | $\%$ | Comparing against class limits |
| 3 | Substantial corrosion criterion | $t_{\text{meas}} \leq t_{\min} + 0.5$ | $\text{mm}$ | Triggering close-up survey |
| 4 | Average wastage (area basis) | $\bar{w} = \dfrac{\sum w_i \cdot A_i}{\sum A_i}$ | $\text{mm}$ | Renewal zone decision |
| 5 | Block reaction force | $R_b = W_{\text{light}} / N_b$ | $\text{kN or t}$ | Per-block load (uniform case) |
| 6 | Trim angle | $\tan\theta = (T_{\text{AFT}} - T_{\text{FWD}}) / L_{\text{BP}}$ | radians / degrees | Docking geometry |
| 7 | Keel block height | $h_b = T_{\text{AFT}} - \tan\theta \cdot x_b$ | $\text{m}$ | Match hull profile |
| 8 | Hull girder section modulus (minimum) | $Z_{\min} = M / \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ | $\text{cm}^3$ | Docking check |
| 9 | Renewal boundary thickness | $t_{\text{ren}} \leq t_{\min}$ | $\text{mm}$ | Triggers plate renewal |
| 10 | Corrosion addition (class) | $t_c$ (typical: $0.5\text{–}2.0\,\text{mm}$) | $\text{mm}$ | Added to $t_{\min}$ for $t_{\text{as-built}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class Society Rule of Thumb:** Renewal is **mandatory** when $t_{\text{meas}} < t_{\min}$. **Conditional renewal / doublings** are allowed when $t_{\min} \leq t_{\text{meas}} \leq t_{\min} + 1.0\,\text{mm}$, subject to class approval.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Planning Charts

In commercial shipyards worldwide, the planning chart pack is the **single regulatory document** demanded by:

- **Classification Societies** (LR, DNV, BV, ABS, IRS) before survey
- **Flag State Inspectors** (Maritime Authority)
- **P&I Clubs** (insurance)
- **H&M Underwriters** (hull and machinery insurance)

The planning chart is also the **basis of the dry-dock specification**, which is the contract document between shipowner and shipyard. Without a properly prepared planning chart pack, a docking cannot lawfully proceed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Trim Angle on the Docking Blocks

When a vessel approaches the dry-dock, she usually has a **trim** (longitudinal inclination) due to loading or ballast condition. The dock blocks must be set to match this trim so that the keel lands cleanly on every block without shock loads.

**Given:**
- Draft aft: $T_{\text{AFT}}$ (in metres)
- Draft forward: $T_{\text{FWD}}$ (in metres)
- Length between perpendiculars: $L_{\text{BP}}$

**Step 1 — Define trim as the difference in drafts:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{AFT}} - T_{\text{FWD}}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate the trim difference to the trim angle $\theta$:**

By the geometry of similar triangles, over the length $L_{\text{BP}}$ the keel drops by $\Delta T$ from forward to aft (or vice-versa). Therefore:

$$
\tan\theta = \dfrac{\Delta T}{L_{\text{BP}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the trim angle:**

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{T_{\text{AFT}} - T_{\text{FWD}}}{L_{\text{BP}}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Express in degrees for engineering use:**

$$
\theta^{\circ} = \theta \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A bulk carrier has $T_{\text{AFT}} = 8.20\,\text{m}$, $T_{\text{FWD}} = 6.80\,\text{m}$, $L_{\text{BP}} = 180\,\text{m}$.

$$
\Delta T = 8.20 - 6.80 = 1.40\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\tan\theta = \dfrac{1.40}{180} = 0.00778
$$

$$
\theta = \arctan(0.00778) = 0.4454^{\circ}
$$

$$
\theta \approx 0.4454 \times 60 = 26.7\,\text{arc-minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **graving docks** (typical trim tolerance is $\pm 0.5^{\circ}$), this trim is **acceptable** and the keel blocks will be pre-set to a height profile that follows this slope.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Height of Each Keel Block

Each keel block must be set to a height such that the block top touches the keel underside. If the block is at a longitudinal distance $x_b$ forward of midship (or aft, taken negative):

**Step 1 — Establish a reference. Let the keel at midship have height zero relative to the baseline. The block heights follow the trim line.**

The height of the keel underside above the reference baseline at any point $x_b$ (positive forward of midship, negative aft) is:

$$
h_{\text{keel}}(x_b) = -\tan\theta \cdot x_b
$$

**Step 2 — The dock-floor is a horizontal plane (typical graving dock). The block top must reach the keel underside. Therefore, the block height measured from the dock floor is:**

$$
h_b(x_b) = h_{\text{reference}} - \tan\theta \cdot x_b
$$

where $h_{\text{reference}}$ is the height of the keel block at midship, set by the dock-master based on the docking draft.

**Step 3 — Compute for a specific block:**

For a block at station $x_b = +60\,\text{m}$ (forward of midship), with $h_{\text{reference}} = 1.20\,\text{m}$ and $\tan\theta = 0.00778$:

$$
h_b(+60) = 1.20 - (0.00778)(+60) = 1.20 - 0.467 = 0.733\,\text{m}
$$

For a block at station $x_b = -60\,\text{m}$ (aft of midship):

$$
h_b(-60) = 1.20 - (0.00778)(-60) = 1.20 + 0.467 = 1.667\,\text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **±0.467 m step** across blocks is critical: the yard must shim the blocks accurately to these heights, otherwise the keel will not land uniformly and **local bending stresses** will develop in the bottom plating.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Block Reaction Force (Uniform Distribution Assumption)

If the docking blocks are considered to share the vessel's lightship weight **uniformly**:

**Given:**
- Lightship weight (lightweight): $W_{\text{light}}$ (in tonnes or kN)
- Number of keel blocks: $N_b$

**Step 1 — Total downward force equals lightship weight:**

$$
F_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{light}} \cdot g \quad \text{(in kN, with } g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — If uniformly distributed, each block carries:**

$$
R_b = \dfrac{F_{\text{total}}}{N_b}
$$

**Step 3 — In practice, the load is **not** uniform because of the hull form curvature. The central blocks carry more load. The actual distribution follows a curve close to the displacement curve. Engineers apply a **load concentration factor** $k$:**

$$
R_{b,\max} = k \cdot \dfrac{F_{\text{total}}}{N_b}
$$

where typical $k = 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5$ for normal hull forms and $k$ can reach **2.0** for fine, slender vessels.

**Worked Example:**
$W_{\text{light}} = 12{,}000\,\text{t}$, $N_b = 80$, $k = 1.3$.

$$
F_{\text{total}} = 12{,}000 \times 9.81 = 117{,}720\,\text{kN}
$$

$$
R_b = \dfrac{117{,}720}{80} = 1{,}471.5\,\text{kN} \text{ (uniform assumption)}
$$

$$
R_{b,\max} = 1.3 \times 1{,}471.5 = 1{,}913.0\,\text{kN}
$$

Each central block is rated for at least **1,913 kN ≈ 195 tonnes** capacity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Corrosion Wastage and Renewal Decision

The planning chart's **Wastage Allowance Chart** quantifies how much steel has been lost to corrosion.

**Given:**
- As-built thickness: $t_{\text{as-built}} = 22.0\,\text{mm}$ (bottom plate, typical bulk carrier)
- Measured thickness today: $t_{\text{meas}} = 18.5\,\text{mm}$
- Class minimum thickness (from CSR/UR): $t_{\min} = 17.0\,\text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Calculate actual wastage:**

$$
w = t_{\text{as-built}} - t_{\text{meas}} = 22.0 - 18.5 = 3.5\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate percentage wastage:**

$$
\%w = \dfrac{w}{t_{\text{as-built}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.5}{22.0} \times 100 = 15.91\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compare against class substantial-corrosion criterion:**

$$
t_{\min} + 0.5 = 17.0 + 0.5 = 17.5\,\text{mm}
$$

Since $t_{\text{meas}} = 18.5\,\text{mm} > 17.5\,\text{mm}$, the plate is **NOT substantially corroded**.

**Step 4 — Calculate remaining strength margin:**

$$
\text{Margin} = t_{\text{meas}} - t_{\min} = 18.5 - 17.0 = 1.5\,\text{mm}
$$

Since margin $= 1.5\,\text{mm} \leq 2.0\,\text{mm}$, the surveyor would **flag the plate for close-up survey at the next docking** and **renewal at the following docking** if wastage rate continues.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow on a Planning Chart

| Observation | Class Threshold | Decision per Planning Chart |
|:------------|:----------------|:----------------------------|
| $t_{\text{meas}} > t_{\min} + 1.0\,\text{mm}$ | Acceptable | Routine survey next cycle |
| $t_{\min} + 0.5 < t_{\text{meas}} \leq t_{\min} + 1.0$ | Borderline | **Close-up survey** at next docking |
| $t_{\min} \leq t_{\text{meas}} \leq t_{\min} + 0.5$ | Substantial corrosion | **Renewal / Doubling** recommended |
| $t_{\text{meas}} < t_{\min}$ | Below minimum | **Immediate renewal mandatory** |
| Local pitting $> 75\%$ of local thickness | Pitting limit | **Insert plate** required |
| Two adjacent plates both renewed | Class scope | **Renew third plate** as well (continuity) |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Information Layers of a Planning Chart

| Layer | Drawing | Scale | Primary Reader |
|:------|:--------|:------|:---------------|
| L1 — Hull form | Lines plan | 1:100 | Naval Architect |
| L2 — Plate layout | Shell expansion | 1:50 | Shipyard |
| L3 — Block positions | Docking plan | 1:200 | Dock Master |
| L4 — TMP | Thickness plan | 1:100 | Surveyor |
| L5 — Wastage limits | Allowance chart | Tabular | Class Surveyor |
| L6 — Coating zones | Paint plan | 1:100 | Coating Inspector |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Planning Chart Preparation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Shipowner Receives Class Survey Notice] --> A1[Compile Vessel Hull Documentation]
    A1 --> A2[Extract Lines Plan and GA Plan]
    A2 --> A3[Identify Docking Drafts Forward and Aft]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Trim Angle]
    A4 --> A5[Design Docking Block Layout Profile]
    A5 --> A6[Prepare Docking Plan Drawing]
    A6 --> A7[Prepare Shell Expansion with Plate Numbers]
    A7 --> A8[Overlay Thickness Measurement Plan TMP]
    A8 --> A9[Calculate Corrosion Allowances per Class Rule]
    A9 --> A10[Generate Wastage Allowance Chart]
    A10 --> A11[Compile Final Planning Chart Pack]
    A11 --> A12{Class Approval Required?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Submit to Class Society]
    A12 -- No --> A14[Issue to Dock Master and Surveyor]
    A13 --> A14
    A14 --> A15[Dry Docking Commences]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Architecture of Planning Chart Pack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P0[Foundation Layer]
        P00[Hull Lines Plan]
        P01[Offsets Table]
        P02[GA Plan]
    end

    subgraph P1[Geometric Derivation Layer]
        P10[Shell Expansion]
        P11[Profile Plan]
        P12[Body Plan Sections]
        P13[Appendage Plan]
    end

    subgraph P2[Operational Planning Layer]
        P20[Docking Plan with Block Layout]
        P21[Block Loading Schedule]
        P22[Trim and List Settings]
        P23[Docking Drafts]
    end

    subgraph P3[Inspection Planning Layer]
        P30[Thickness Measurement Plan]
        P31[Wastage Allowance Chart]
        P32[Close Up Survey Zones]
        P33[Coating Condition Plan]
    end

    subgraph P4[Repair Planning Layer]
        P40[Renewal Boundary Plan]
        P41[Insert Plate Schedule]
        P42[Doubling Plate Schedule]
    end

    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P2 --> P4
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Logic for Renewal from Planning Chart Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Measured Thickness t_meas Obtained] --> B2{Is t_meas less than t_min?}
    B2 -- Yes --> B3[Mandatory Plate Renewal]
    B2 -- No --> B4{Is t_meas less than or equal to t_min plus 0.5 mm?}
    B4 -- Yes --> B5[Substantial Corrosion Flagged]
    B5 --> B6[Close Up Survey at This Docking]
    B6 --> B7{Doubling Acceptable?}
    B7 -- Yes --> B8[Apply Doubling Plate]
    B7 -- No --> B9[Schedule Renewal Next Docking]
    B4 -- No --> B10{Is t_meas less than or equal to t_min plus 1.0 mm?}
    B10 -- Yes --> B11[Increased Survey Frequency Recommended]
    B10 -- No --> B12[Routine Survey Acceptable]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Coordinate System Used in Planning Charts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O0[Origin at Midship Centreline at Keel] --> O1[X axis runs longitudinally Forward positive]
    O0 --> O2[Y axis runs transversely Starboard positive]
    O0 --> O3[Z axis runs vertically Upward positive]
    O1 --> O4[Stations numbered 0 at AP to 20 at FP]
    O2 --> O5[Transverse offsets in metres]
    O3 --> O6[Heights above keel baseline in metres]
    O4 --> O7[All block positions referenced to station numbers]
    O5 --> O7
    O6 --> O7
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Reading Sequence of a Docking Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Open Docking Plan Sheet] --> R2[Identify Sheet Number and Revision]
    R2 --> R3[Locate the Profile View at the Bottom of the Sheet]
    R3 --> R4[Identify Forward and Aft Docking Drafts]
    R4 --> R5[Locate the Plan View of Block Layout]
    R5 --> R6[Identify Number of Keel Blocks and Spacing]
    R6 --> R7[Read Block Loading Schedule Table]
    R7 --> R8[Check Critical Appendage Clearances]
    R8 --> R9[Cross Reference with TMP for Surveyor]
    R9 --> R10[Sign Off by Dock Master and Class Surveyor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a Docking Plan and list FOUR information items it must show.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Docking Plan** is a specialised planning chart prepared by the shipyard and approved by the Class Society before a vessel enters the dry-dock. It depicts the hull's underwater profile and the layout of supporting blocks.

Four information items it must show:
1. **Profile view** of the hull bottom with docking drafts forward and aft.
2. **Plan view** showing positions of keel blocks and side blocks with station numbers.
3. **Block loading schedule** giving permissible reaction force on each block.
4. **Clearances of appendages** — propeller tip, rudder, sea chests, bilge keel edges.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for the four items. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the purpose of a Shell Expansion Plan in hull inspection? Mention any TWO uses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Shell Expansion Plan** is a developed (unrolled) two-dimensional view of the entire external shell plating, showing every plate seam, butt joint, and frame line in true scale.

**Two uses:**
1. **Plate renewal identification** — each plate is numbered and dimensioned, allowing the yard to order correct insert plates before docking.
2. **Thickness gauging reference** — the TMP is overlaid on the shell expansion so that every gauging point has a unique location code linked to a specific plate.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark × 2 = 2 marks for uses. Total 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Q: With neat sketches explain the components of a Docking Plan. Discuss how the docking drafts and trim angle are used to set the heights of keel blocks. (14 marks)**

#### (a) Components of a Docking Plan — 7 marks

A complete Docking Plan consists of the following components:

1. **Profile View (Side Elevation):** Shows the longitudinal profile of the hull bottom, the baseline of the keel, the waterline at the moment of touchdown, and the top profile of the keel blocks as a stepped or contoured line.

2. **Plan View (Top View of Dock Floor):** Shows the transverse position of each keel block and side block as rectangles, with the centreline as the reference.

3. **End Views (Forward and Aft):** Show the transverse curvature at the ends of the vessel and the location of side blocks on the bilge.

4. **Block Loading Schedule:** A tabular list of every block with its station number, transverse offset, designed height, and permissible load.

5. **Notes and Legend:** Specify the scale (typically 1:200 for profile, 1:100 for plan), revision number, approval signatures, and special warnings (sea-chest gratings, bilge-keel edges, propeller-tip clearance).

6. **Critical Clearances Annotation:** Highlights of low-clearance zones (echo-sounder, sea-chest, stabilizer fin) where the dock-master must keep the dock floor clear of obstruction.

#### (b) Use of Docking Drafts and Trim Angle to Set Keel Block Heights — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Read the forward and aft drafts** ($T_{\text{FWD}}$, $T_{\text{AFT}}$) at the moment of touchdown. These are obtained from the docking plan.

**Step 2 — Compute the trim angle** $\theta$:

$$
\tan\theta = \dfrac{T_{\text{AFT}} - T_{\text{FWD}}}{L_{\text{BP}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the height of each keel block** at station $x_b$ measured from midship (positive forward, negative aft):

$$
h_b(x_b) = h_{\text{ref}} - \tan\theta \cdot x_b
$$

where $h_{\text{ref}}$ is the height of the midship keel block, set such that the deepest-drafted end just lands on its block with adequate clearance.

**Step 4 — Yard executes the shimming** — Each keel block is shimmed (using steel plates or wedges) to its computed height, verified by surveyor's level.

**Step 5 — Cross-check critical clearances** — Propeller tip, rudder hinge, and bilge keel clearances are verified against the dock floor depth.

**Worked Numerical Mini-Example (to be written in exam):**

Given: $T_{\text{FWD}} = 6.80\,\text{m}$, $T_{\text{AFT}} = 8.20\,\text{m}$, $L_{\text{BP}} = 180\,\text{m}$, midship block height $h_{\text{ref}} = 1.20\,\text{m}$.

$$
\tan\theta = (8.20 - 6.80) / 180 = 0.00778
$$

For block at $x_b = +60\,\text{m}$: $h_b = 1.20 - (0.00778)(60) = 0.733\,\text{m}$.

For block at $x_b = -60\,\text{m}$: $h_b = 1.20 - (0.00778)(-60) = 1.667\,\text{m}$.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for components list, 2 marks for sketch description, 2 marks for trim-angle derivation, 1 mark for block-height formula. Total 7+7 = 14 marks.)*

---

### ❓ QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**Q: Explain the Hull Lines Plan and its three projections. Describe the Shell Expansion Plan and its role in thickness measurement planning. (14 marks)**

#### (a) Hull Lines Plan and Three Projections — 7 marks

The **Hull Lines Plan** is the geometric master drawing of the ship. It is composed of three orthographic projections, all sharing common reference lines:

1. **Sheer Plan (Profile View):** Side elevation showing the waterlines (horizontal lines WL-0 to WL-n) intersected by station lines (vertical lines at every station from AP to FP) and buttock lines (vertical longitudinal sections). It defines the longitudinal curvature of the hull.

2. **Body Plan (Transverse Sections):** A composite of all transverse sections, drawn with forward body on the left side and aft body on the right side of the centreline. Each section is the intersection of the hull with a plane perpendicular to the longitudinal axis at a particular station.

3. **Half-Breadth Plan (Horizontal Sections):** Plan view (top-down) showing each waterline as a horizontal slice of the hull, drawn only on one side of the centreline (hence "half-breadth"). It defines the waterplane shape.

These three views are linked by the **Offsets Table** — a numerical matrix giving, for each station, the half-breadth at each waterline and the height at each buttock.

#### (b) Shell Expansion Plan and Its Role in TMP — 7 marks

The **Shell Expansion Plan** is the developed (flattened) view of the entire outer shell plating. It is constructed by:

1. Plotting each station's intersection with the shell.
2. "Unrolling" the cylindrical/curved surface using the developed length formula (true length = station spacing / cos(shear angle)).
3. Drawing plate seams, butt joints, and frame lines on the developed surface.

**Role in TMP:**

1. **Gauging Point Location** — Every thickness gauging point on the TMP is given a unique ID that references a specific plate number and frame on the shell expansion.
2. **Renewal Zone Identification** — When a plate is found below $t_{\min}$, its boundaries are marked directly on the shell expansion to order the correct insert plate.
3. **Trend Analysis** — Successive dockings' TMPs are overlaid on the same shell expansion to identify corrosion-prone zones.
4. **Documentation for Class** — The class surveyor signs off the shell expansion with measured thicknesses as the official record.

*(Marks distribution: 3 marks for three projections description, 2 marks for offsets table, 2 marks for shell expansion construction, 2 marks for TMP role. Total 7+7 = 14 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in trim-angle derivation — students often compute $h_b$ without sign and get the aft and forward block heights interchanged. Examiner deducts **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing "Lines Plan" with "GA Plan"** — Lines Plan is **hull form geometry**; GA Plan is **general arrangement of compartments**. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks** if used interchangeably.
> 3. **Skipping the offsets table** — many students describe the three projections without mentioning how they are numerically linked. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the trim-angle derivation — both $\tan\theta$ and $\theta$ must carry the correct unit (radians or degrees). Examiner deducts **0.5 mark** if missing.
> 5. **Confusing "Docking Plan" with "Docking Drawing only"** — the Docking Plan is a **complete pack** including block loading schedule, profile, plan view, end views, and clearances, not just one drawing.
> 6. **Wrong substantial-corrosion threshold** — students write "$t_{\min} + 1.0$" instead of "$t_{\min} + 0.5$". Examiner deducts **1 mark** as it is a class-rule fact.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **Planning Chart Pack** is a coordinated bundle: Lines Plan + Shell Expansion + Docking Plan + TMP + Wastage Chart + Appendage Drawing.
- The **Docking Plan** is the single most operationally critical planning chart and must show: profile, plan view, end views, block loading schedule, and critical appendage clearances.
- The **Hull Lines Plan** has three views: **Sheer Plan** (profile), **Body Plan** (transverse sections), **Half-Breadth Plan** (horizontal sections), all linked by the **Offsets Table**.
- The **Shell Expansion** is the unrolled/developed view of the shell plating and is the master reference for plate numbering and TMP overlay.
- **Trim angle:** $\tan\theta = (T_{\text{AFT}} - T_{\text{FWD}}) / L_{\text{BP}}$.
- **Keel block height:** $h_b(x_b) = h_{\text{ref}} - \tan\theta \cdot x_b$.
- **Block reaction:** $R_{b,\max} = k \cdot W_{\text{light}} g / N_b$, with $k = 1.2$ to $1.5$ (up to $2.0$ for slender vessels).
- **Substantial corrosion criterion (IACS):** $t_{\text{meas}} \leq t_{\min} + 0.5\,\text{mm}$ → close-up survey required.
- **Renewal mandatory** when $t_{\text{meas}} < t_{\min}$.
- **Doubling plate** is an acceptable alternative to full renewal in many cases, subject to class approval.
- **Scales to remember:** Lines Plan 1:100, Shell Expansion 1:50, Docking Plan 1:200 (profile) / 1:100 (plan).
- **Standard coordinate system:** Origin at midship CL at keel; X forward +, Y starboard +, Z upward +.
- **Appendages** (rudder, propeller, sea chest, bilge keel) must be clearly marked on the docking plan with their clearance to dock floor.
- **Class Society references:** IACS UR Z7.1 / Z7.2, Z17, Z23 for thickness measurement and corrosion criteria.
- **Most-examined topics:** Trim angle derivation, block height calculation, components of docking plan, three projections of lines plan, role of shell expansion.
- **Common units:** thickness in **mm**, drafts in **m**, block load in **kN** or **tonnes**, angles in **degrees or radians**.
- **Exam tip:** Always state assumptions (e.g., "assuming uniform block load", "assuming waterplane-area-based trim") before starting any numerical derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shipbuilding Contracts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shipbuilding Contracts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Contract** is a legally binding engineering and commercial agreement executed between a **Shipowner (Buyer/Purchaser)** and a **Shipbuilder (Seller/Contractor)** for the design, construction, testing, and delivery of a vessel in accordance with approved specifications, classification society rules, regulatory requirements, and mutually agreed commercial terms.

In the context of **Hull Inspection**, the contract is the primary legal document that defines:
- The **inspection milestones** during fabrication
- The **technical specifications** the hull must satisfy
- The **acceptance criteria** at delivery
- The **warranty period** and defects liability
- The **liquidated damages** for delay or non-conformance

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Shipbuilding Contracts form the contractual backbone of hull inspection practice. Without a clear contract, the inspector has no legal authority to reject non-conforming work, demand re-work, or withhold acceptance certificates.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Custom House Construction" Analogy

Think of a shipbuilding contract exactly like the contract you would sign with a custom home builder:

| Custom Home (Analogy) | Shipbuilding (Actual) |
|---|---|
| Plot of land | Shipyard building berth / dry dock |
| Architect's drawings | General Arrangement (GA), Lines Plan, Structural drawings |
| Local building codes | Classification Society Rules (IRS, LR, DNV, BV, ABS) |
| Municipal inspector visits | Classification Surveyor & Owner's Site Representative |
| Foundation poured | Keel Laying |
| Roof completion | Launching |
| Final finishing & handover | Fitting Out, Sea Trials & Delivery |
| Snag list after possession | Defects Liability Period (usually 12 months) |

Just as a homeowner pays the builder in **stages** (booking, plinth, roof, possession), a shipowner pays the shipbuilder in **contractual milestones** (signing, steel cutting, keel laying, launching, delivery). This staged payment protects both parties — the buyer does not pay everything upfront, and the builder gets working capital to progress.

## 1.3 Key Parties to a Shipbuilding Contract

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Principal Parties:**
> 1. **Shipowner / Buyer** — The party ordering the vessel
> 2. **Shipbuilder / Seller / Yard** — The party constructing the vessel
> 3. **Classification Society** — Independent technical body (e.g., Lloyd's Register, IRS, Bureau Veritas)
> 4. **Owner's Representative / Superintendent** — Inspects on behalf of the buyer
> 5. **Guarantor / Bank** — Issues refund guarantees for buyer's pre-delivery payments

---

## 1.4 Importance in Hull Inspection

The hull inspector's role is **contractually mandated** under three clauses:
- **Specification Clause** — defines inspection standards
- **Classification Clause** — mandates society survey
- **Acceptance / Delivery Clause** — defines the inspection certificate issuance

Without these clauses, no inspection has legal teeth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Shipbuilding Contracts

Shipbuilding contracts are classified based on **how the contract price is determined** and **how risk is allocated** between buyer and builder.

### Type 1 — Lump Sum (Fixed Price) Contract

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The builder agrees to construct the vessel for a **single fixed price**, irrespective of actual cost incurred.

- **Risk Bearer:** Shipbuilder (absorbs cost overruns)
- **Buyer Advantage:** Predictable final cost
- **Builder Advantage:** Higher profit margin if cost controlled
- **Disadvantage:** Builder may cut corners; buyer must rely heavily on inspection
- **Common Use:** Most merchant vessels, standard designs

### Type 2 — Cost Plus Contract

$$C_{total} = C_{actual} + F_{fee}$$

Where:
- $C_{total}$ = Total contract price
- $C_{actual}$ = Actual verified cost of construction
- $F_{fee}$ = Builder's fee (fixed or percentage)

- **Risk Bearer:** Buyer (pays actual cost)
- **Use Case:** Naval vessels, complex prototype ships, ice-class vessels
- **Inspection Implication:** Owner's auditor must verify all costs — very inspection-heavy

### Type 3 — Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF)

$$C_{total} = C_{actual} + F_{fixed}$$

The fee is a pre-agreed lump sum, not a percentage — encouraging the builder to control costs.

### Type 4 — Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF)

$$C_{total} = C_{actual} + F_{target} + I \cdot (C_{target} - C_{actual})$$

Where $I$ is the share ratio (e.g., 70/30 between buyer/builder).

### Type 5 — Unit Price Contract

Price is split into **units** (per tonne of steel, per fitting, etc.). Common in **naval auxiliary** and **repair** contracts.

---

## 2.2 Standard Contract Forms Used Worldwide

| Standard Form | Origin | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **SAJ Form** | Shipbuilders' Association of Japan | Japanese-built merchant vessels |
| **AWES Form** | Association of West European Shipbuilders | European yards (Germany, Netherlands) |
| **BIMCO NEWBUILDCON 2007** | Baltic & International Maritime Council | Universal international use |
| **CMAC Form** | China Maritime Arbitration Commission | Chinese-built vessels |
| **NEWBUILDPRO** | BIMCO revised | Modern LNG / offshore vessels |
| **NATO ATP / MOD Contract** | UK Ministry of Defence | Warships and naval auxiliaries |

---

## 2.3 Payment Milestone Schedule (Standard SAJ Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six-Stage Payment Plan** (most commonly tested KTU concept):

| Stage | Milestone | Typical % of Contract Price |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Contract Signing | **20 %** |
| 2 | Steel Cutting Commenced | **10 %** |
| 3 | Keel Laid | **10 %** |
| 4 | Vessel Launched | **20 %** |
| 5 | Vessel Delivered (after sea trials) | **30 %** |
| 6 | Release of Retention Money | **10 %** |
| | **Total** | **100 %** |

---

## 2.4 Liquidated Damages (LD) Clause

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept:** Pre-agreed sum payable by builder for each day of delay beyond the delivery date, OR payable by buyer for each day of late delivery acceptance.

$$LD_{total} = D_{days} \times R_{per\,day}$$

Where:
- $LD_{total}$ = Total liquidated damages payable
- $D_{days}$ = Number of days of delay
- $R_{per\,day}$ = Daily rate (typically **0.5 % to 1 % of contract price per day**)

**Statutory Cap:** Most contracts cap LD at **5 % to 15 %** of contract price. Beyond this, the parties revert to unliquidated (general) damages under common law.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you need for Module 4 numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lump Sum Payment | $P_{total} = \text{Fixed}$ | $P_{total}$ in USD/INR | — |
| 2 | Cost Plus | $P = C_{a} + F$ | $C_{a}$ actual cost; $F$ fee | $F$ = 5–15 % of $C_{a}$ |
| 3 | Cost Plus Fixed Fee | $P = C_{a} + F_{fixed}$ | $F_{fixed}$ lump sum | Negotiated |
| 4 | Cost Plus Incentive Fee | $P = C_{a} + F_{t} + I(C_{t} - C_{a})$ | $C_{t}$ target cost; $I$ share ratio | $0 < I < 1$ |
| 5 | Milestone Payment | $P_{i} = P_{total} \cdot \alpha_{i}$ | $\alpha_{i}$ = fraction for stage $i$ | $\sum \alpha_{i} = 1$ |
| 6 | Liquidated Damages (Delay) | $LD = n \cdot r$ | $n$ = days late; $r$ = daily rate | $r$ = 0.5–1 % of $P_{total}$ per day |
| 7 | LD Cap | $LD_{max} = P_{total} \cdot \beta$ | $\beta$ = cap fraction | $\beta$ = 5–15 % |
| 8 | Refund Guarantee | $G = \sum P_{pre\text{-}delivery}$ | Pre-delivery instalments | Covered 100 % |
| 9 | Speed Deficiency LD | $LD_{speed} = (V_{contract} - V_{actual}) \cdot K_{speed}$ | $K_{speed}$ in USD per knot | $K_{speed}$ = USD 50,000–500,000/knot |
| 10 | Bunker Consumption LD | $LD_{b} = (F_{actual} - F_{contract}) \cdot P_{fuel} \cdot T$ | $T$ = trial duration; $P_{fuel}$ price/tonne | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas allow the hull inspector's office to compute (a) instalment release at each milestone, (b) anticipated penalty exposure, and (c) warranty retention. Mastery is essential for KTU Part B numericals.

---

## 2.6 Key Contractual Clauses Affecting Hull Inspection

| Clause | Inspection Implication |
|---|---|
| **Specification Clause** | Defines design standards (mild steel HTS, aluminium, grade) |
| **Classification Clause** | Mandates society survey at every milestone |
| **Inspection & Testing Clause** | Grants surveyor right of access |
| **Sea Trials Clause** | Defines speed, fuel, manoeuvring acceptance |
| **Liquidated Damages Clause** | Sets deadline and penalty |
| **Warranty Clause** | Defines defects liability (typically 12–24 months) |
| **Force Majeure Clause** | Excuses delay for extraordinary events |
| **Cancellation / Rescission Clause** | Owner's right to cancel if builder defaults |

---

## 2.7 Stages of Hull Construction (Inspection Reference Map)

> [!NOTE]
> This sequence is the **chronological backbone** of hull inspection.

1. **Steel Stock Inspection** — Mill certificates verification
2. **Cutting & Nesting Inspection** — Plate nesting optimisation check
3. **Fabrication Stage** — Sub-assembly and panel inspection
4. **Block Assembly Inspection** — Joining of sub-blocks into mega-blocks
5. **Erection on Berth** — Joining blocks to form hull
6. **Keel Laying** (formal contractual milestone)
7. **Hull Completion / Pre-Launch Inspection** — Final hull survey before launching
8. **Launching** (formal contractual milestone)
9. **Outfitting at Quay** — Machinery, piping, electrical
10. **Pre-Sea Trial Inspection** — Dockside tests
11. **Sea Trials** — Speed, fuel, manoeuvring
12. **Delivery** (formal contractual milestone)
13. **Defects Liability Period** — 12 months warranty claims

---

## 2.8 Risk and Title Transfer

| Event | Risk Transfers From | To |
|---|---|---|
| Contract Signing | None (risk remains with builder's yard) | — |
| Launching | Builder | Owner (partially) |
| Delivery (post sea trials) | Builder | **Owner (fully)** |
| Casualty before delivery | Builder bears risk | — |
| Casualty after delivery | Owner bears risk | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salvage & General Average:** The **York-Antwerp Rules** apply only **after delivery**. Before delivery, builder alone bears any salvage cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Milestone Payment Calculation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU Style):** A shipowner places an order for a bulk carrier with a **contract price of USD 60 million**. The contract stipulates the standard SAJ payment schedule: 20 % on signing, 10 % on steel cutting, 10 % on keel laying, 20 % on launching, 30 % on delivery, and 10 % as retention released 6 months after delivery. Compute the amount payable at each milestone.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the total contract price.**

$$P_{total} = \text{USD } 60{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Apply the milestone fractions from the contract.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 (Signing)} &: P_1 = 0.20 \times P_{total} = 0.20 \times 60{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Stage 2 (Steel Cutting)} &: P_2 = 0.10 \times P_{total} \\[4pt]
\text{Stage 3 (Keel Laying)} &: P_3 = 0.10 \times P_{total} \\[4pt]
\text{Stage 4 (Launching)} &: P_4 = 0.20 \times P_{total} \\[4pt]
\text{Stage 5 (Delivery)} &: P_5 = 0.30 \times P_{total} \\[4pt]
\text{Stage 6 (Retention Release)} &: P_6 = 0.10 \times P_{total}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 0.20 \times 60{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 12{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
P_2 &= 0.10 \times 60{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 6{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
P_3 &= 0.10 \times 60{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 6{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
P_4 &= 0.20 \times 60{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 12{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
P_5 &= 0.30 \times 60{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 18{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
P_6 &= 0.10 \times 60{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 6{,}000{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**

$$\sum P_i = 12 + 6 + 6 + 12 + 18 + 6 = \text{USD } 60\text{ M} \quad \checkmark$$

| Milestone | Fraction | Amount (USD Million) |
|---|---|---|
| Signing | 20 % | 12.00 |
| Steel Cutting | 10 % | 6.00 |
| Keel Laying | 10 % | 6.00 |
| Launching | 20 % | 12.00 |
| Delivery | 30 % | 18.00 |
| Retention Release | 10 % | 6.00 |
| **Total** | **100 %** | **60.00** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Liquidated Damages Computation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A new-building VLCC (contract price = **USD 110 million**) is contracted for delivery on **30 June**. The vessel is actually delivered on **25 August** of the same year. The contract specifies LD at **0.5 % per day** of contract price, capped at **10 %**. Calculate the LD payable.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute delay in days.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{days} &= \text{Actual Delivery Date} - \text{Contract Delivery Date} \\[4pt]
&= \text{25 Aug} - \text{30 Jun}
\end{aligned}
$$

Breaking down:
- June remaining: 0 days (delivery date is 30 June)
- July: 31 days
- August 1–25: 25 days

$$D_{days} = 31 + 25 = 56 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2 — Compute daily LD rate.**

$$r = 0.005 \times 110{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 550{,}000 \text{ per day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute uncapped LD.**

$$LD_{uncapped} = 56 \times 550{,}000 = \text{USD } 30{,}800{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute the statutory cap.**

$$LD_{cap} = 0.10 \times 110{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 11{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Compare and select the lower (since cap is binding).**

Since $LD_{uncapped} = 30.8\text{ M} > LD_{cap} = 11\text{ M}$, the cap applies.

$$\boxed{LD_{payable} = \text{USD } 11{,}000{,}000}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Beyond the cap, the owner may still pursue **unliquidated damages** under common law for actual loss, but this requires proof and is harder to claim.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cost Plus Incentive Fee Settlement

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A naval auxiliary vessel is being built under a CPIF contract.
> - Target cost $C_t$ = USD 80 M
> - Target fee $F_t$ = USD 8 M
> - Share ratio: 70 % builder / 30 % buyer (when actual cost is below target)
> - Actual verified cost $C_a$ = USD 75 M
> 
> Compute the final price payable.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**

$$P = C_a + F_t + I \cdot (C_t - C_a)$$

**Step 2 — Identify parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_a &= 75\text{ M} \\
F_t &= 8\text{ M} \\
C_t &= 80\text{ M} \\
I &= 0.70 \text{ (builder's share of savings)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the savings.**

$$\text{Savings} = C_t - C_a = 80 - 75 = \text{USD } 5\text{ M}$$

**Step 4 — Compute builder's incentive (70 % of savings).**

$$I \cdot \text{Savings} = 0.70 \times 5 = \text{USD } 3.5\text{ M}$$

**Step 5 — Compute final price.**

$$P = 75 + 8 + 3.5 = \text{USD } 86.5\text{ M}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The builder saved USD 5 M vs. target, and earned USD 3.5 M extra fee. The buyer paid USD 1.5 M less than if the savings had been returned entirely. The CPIF mechanism **incentivises efficiency while protecting the buyer**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Refund Guarantee Coverage

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A shipowner pays in 5 instalments of USD 5 M each (signing, steel cutting, keel laying, launching, delivery). The contract requires a refund guarantee from a bank. Compute the guarantee amount at each stage.

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 \text{ (after signing)} &= 5\text{ M} \\
G_2 \text{ (after steel cutting)} &= 5 + 5 = 10\text{ M} \\
G_3 \text{ (after keel laying)} &= 10 + 5 = 15\text{ M} \\
G_4 \text{ (after launching)} &= 15 + 5 = 20\text{ M} \\
G_5 \text{ (at delivery)} &= 20 + 5 = 25\text{ M (then guarantee is released)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The guarantee peaks at USD 25 M just before delivery and is extinguished upon successful delivery and acceptance.

---

## 3.5 Speed & Fuel LD Combined (Sea Trial Defect)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A sea trial reveals:
> - Contracted service speed: 14.0 knots
> - Actual trial speed: 13.4 knots
> - Speed deficiency LD rate: USD 150,000 per 0.1 knot
> - Contracted daily fuel consumption at service speed: 50 tonnes
> - Actual trial consumption: 56 tonnes
> - Bunker price: USD 600/tonne
> 
> Compute total LD claimable.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Speed deficiency.**

$$\Delta V = 14.0 - 13.4 = 0.6 \text{ knots}$$

In units of 0.1 knot:

$$n_{speed} = \frac{0.6}{0.1} = 6 \text{ units}$$

$$LD_{speed} = 6 \times 150{,}000 = \text{USD } 900{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Fuel excess LD (typically USD per tonne above contract).**

Assuming rate of USD 2,000 per excess tonne:

$$\Delta F = 56 - 50 = 6 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$LD_{fuel} = 6 \times 2{,}000 = \text{USD } 12{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Total claim.**

$$LD_{total} = 900{,}000 + 12{,}000 = \text{USD } 912{,}000}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some contracts allow the builder to **rectify** (e.g., by adjusting propeller pitch or engine tuning) and re-trial before LD is enforced. The contract's "curing right" clause governs this.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Contract Type Selection

| Factor | Lump Sum | Cost Plus | Cost Plus Fixed Fee | Cost Plus Incentive Fee |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Builder Risk | High | None | None | Moderate |
| Buyer Risk | Low | High | High | Moderate |
| Inspection Burden | Moderate | Very High | Very High | High |
| Audit Required | No | Yes (cost audit) | Yes | Yes |
| Use Case | Standard merchant vessels | Naval prototypes | Research ships | Naval auxiliaries |
| Tendency to Cut Corners | Yes (builder motivated) | No (buyer pays) | No | Balanced |

---

## 3.7 Defects Liability Period — Time-Bar Analysis

Most contracts state:

$$\text{Builder's Liability} = \min(T_{delivery}, T_{DLPC})$$

Where:
- $T_{DLPC}$ = Defects Liability Period (typically **12 months** from delivery)
- After this period, latent defects may still be claimable under common law for **6 years** (UK Limitation Act) or as per contract.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Six-Year Rule"** (UK / Common Law jurisdictions): For latent hull defects, the owner may bring a claim up to **6 years from delivery**. Beyond that, the builder's contractual liability is extinguished.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Contract Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Owner Issues Enquiry] --> B[Yard Submits Tender]
    B --> C[Negotiation of Specification and Price]
    C --> D[Contract Signing Stage 1 Payment 20 percent]
    D --> E[Steel Cutting Stage 2 Payment 10 percent]
    E --> F[Keel Laying Stage 3 Payment 10 percent]
    F --> G[Hull Block Erection Class Survey]
    G --> H[Launching Stage 4 Payment 20 percent]
    H --> I[Outfitting and Dockside Trials]
    I --> J[Sea Trials Class and Owner Survey]
    J --> K{All Tests Pass}
    K -->|Yes| L[Delivery Stage 5 Payment 30 percent]
    K -->|No| M[Rectification Notice to Builder]
    M --> I
    L --> N[Defects Liability Period 12 months]
    N --> O[Retention Release Stage 6 Payment 10 percent]
    O --> P[Contract Closed]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Payment Flow with Refund Guarantee

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Owner[Shipowner Bank] -->|Pays Instalment| Builder[Shipbuilder Account]
    Builder -->|Provides Contract| BankG[Guarantee Bank]
    BankG -->|Issues Refund Guarantee| Owner
    Owner -->|Claims if Builder Defaults| BankG
    BankG -->|Refund| Owner
    Builder -->|Progresses Construction| Class[Classification Society Survey]
    Class -->|Issues Certificates| Owner
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Risk Transfer Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Contract Signing Risk with Builder] --> A1[Steel Cutting Risk with Builder]
    A1 --> A2[Keel Laying Risk with Builder]
    A2 --> A3[Launching Partial Risk Transfer to Owner]
    A3 --> A4[Sea Trials Risk Shared]
    A4 --> A5[Delivery Full Risk Transfer to Owner]
    A5 --> A6[Defects Liability Risk with Builder for 12 months]
    A6 --> A7[After DLP Risk with Owner]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Type of Contract Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Contract Type] --> Q1{Is design fully defined?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is cost predictable?}
    Q1 -->|No| CP1[Use Cost Plus]
    Q2 -->|Yes| LS[Lump Sum Recommended]
    Q2 -->|No| CP2[Cost Plus Fixed Fee]
    CP2 --> Q3{Need incentive?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| CPIF[Cost Plus Incentive Fee]
    Q3 -->|No| CPFF[Cost Plus Fixed Fee Final]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IM1[Steel Mill Certificate Verification] --> IM2[Plate Cutting and Nesting Check]
    IM2 --> IM3[Sub-Block Fabrication NDT]
    IM3 --> IM4[Block Joining Ultrasonic Inspection]
    IM4 --> IM5[Grand Block Erection Dimensional Check]
    IM5 --> IM6[Pre-Launch Hull Survey by Class]
    IM6 --> IM7[Launching Structural Soundness]
    IM7 --> IM8[Outfitting Inspection Piping and Machinery]
    IM8 --> IM9[Dockside Tests Tanks and Systems]

    IM11 --> IM12[Annual Class Surveys Post-Delivery]
```
