<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q:** Define a **Lump Sum Shipbuilding Contract** and state **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** to the shipowner.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Lump Sum (Fixed Price) Shipbuilding Contract** is one in which the shipbuilder agrees to construct and deliver the vessel for a **single pre-agreed total price**, irrespective of the actual cost of labour, materials, and overheads incurred during construction.

**Two advantages to the shipowner:**
1. **Cost certainty** — The owner knows the final price in advance and can arrange financing precisely.
2. **No cost audit required** — The owner does not need to verify builder's expenditure; the contract price is binding.

**One disadvantage to the shipowner:**
- The builder, motivated to maximise profit, may **cut corners on quality or materials**, requiring the owner to maintain rigorous independent hull inspection to safeguard against non-conformance.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **Q:** What is a **Refund Guarantee** in a shipbuilding contract? Why is it important to the shipowner?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Refund Guarantee** is a **bank-issued financial instrument** by which a reputable bank (or insurance company) undertakes to refund all pre-delivery instalments paid by the shipowner to the shipbuilder, **if the builder fails to deliver the vessel** as per the contract.

**Importance to the shipowner:**
1. **Protects pre-delivery payments** — The owner has paid 60–70 % of contract price before delivery. Without this guarantee, this entire sum is at risk if the builder becomes bankrupt.
2. **Enables financing** — Banks financing the shipowner require this guarantee before releasing loan tranches.
3. **Risk transfer** — Shifts the credit risk from the builder's solvency to the bank's solvency.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Module 4 Part B carries internal choice. Both options below are independent, full-mark questions.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **standard SAJ six-stage payment schedule** used in merchant shipbuilding contracts. State the typical percentage at each stage. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 60,000 DWT bulk carrier is contracted at **USD 55 million** under the SAJ schedule. The yard delays delivery by **45 days**. The LD clause specifies **0.5 % per day** of contract price, capped at **8 %**. Compute the total LD payable and identify which instalments the owner has already paid at the time of delayed delivery. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **SAJ (Shipbuilders' Association of Japan) six-stage payment schedule** is the most widely adopted payment plan in international shipbuilding contracts. It aligns the builder's cash flow with construction progress and protects the buyer's pre-delivery investment through staged disbursement.

**The Six Stages:**

| Stage | Milestone Event | % of Contract Price |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Contract Signing | 20 % |
| 2 | Commencement of Steel Cutting | 10 % |
| 3 | Keel Laying | 10 % |
| 4 | Launching of the Vessel | 20 % |
| 5 | Delivery of the Vessel (post sea trials) | 30 % |
| 6 | Release of Retention Money | 10 % |
| | **Total** | **100 %** |

> **Key advantages of this staged approach:**
> - Aligns payment with physical progress
> - Buyer holds back **10 % retention** for 6–12 months post-delivery to enforce warranty
> - Builder receives cash to fund materials and labour at each critical stage
> - Disputes can be resolved at the next stage with financial leverage on the defaulting party

**(Full mark allocation: Sketch 3 + Tabulation 2 + Explanation 2 = 7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the total LD cap first.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LD_{cap} &= 0.08 \times 55{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{USD } 4{,}400{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the uncapped LD.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LD_{uncapped} &= 45 \times (0.005 \times 55{,}000{,}000) \\[4pt]
&= 45 \times 275{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{USD } 12{,}375{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the cap.**

Since $LD_{uncapped} = 12.375\text{ M} > LD_{cap} = 4.4\text{ M}$, the cap applies.

$$\boxed{LD_{payable} = \text{USD } 4{,}400{,}000}$$

**Step 4 — Identify instalments already paid by the delayed delivery date.**

By delivery (even if late), all stages 1–4 have been completed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Already Paid} &= (20\% + 10\% + 10\% + 20\%) \times 55{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 60\% \times 55{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{USD } 33{,}000{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

The builder still receives the **30 % delivery instalment (USD 16.5 M)** minus the LD deduction. **Net payment at delivery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{net} &= 16{,}500{,}000 - 4{,}400{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{USD } 12{,}100{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The owner deducts USD 4.4 M from the final delivery instalment and pays the builder USD 12.1 M.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Differentiate between **Lump Sum** and **Cost Plus** shipbuilding contracts. Under what circumstances is each preferred? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A naval research vessel is being built under a **Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF)** contract. Target cost is **USD 90 M**, target fee is **USD 10 M**, and the share ratio is **60 % (builder) / 40 % (buyer)** for any saving below target cost. The actual audited cost turns out to be **USD 84 M**. Compute the final contract price. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Basis | Lump Sum Contract | Cost Plus Contract |
|---|---|---|
| **Price Determination** | Fixed in advance | Actual cost + fee |
| **Cost Overrun Risk** | Borne by builder | Borne by buyer |
| **Inspection Intensity** | Moderate to high (anti-corner-cutting) | Very high (continuous cost audit) |
| **Builder's Incentive** | To reduce cost (and may cut corners) | None for cost; quality-driven |
| **Buyer's Audit Cost** | Low | High — requires auditors |
| **Used For** | Standard merchant vessels, series production | Prototypes, naval vessels, complex offshore |
| **Documentation** | Simple | Complex (all invoices audited) |

**Circumstances where Lump Sum is preferred:**
- Design is fully matured and frozen
- Construction is repeatable / series-built
- Owner wants cost certainty
- E.g., bulk carrier series, container ship series

**Circumstances where Cost Plus is preferred:**
- Design is still evolving
- Vessel is one-off or prototype
- Owner wants finest quality regardless of cost
- E.g., aircraft carrier, research vessel, polar icebreaker

**Hybrid — CPIF:** Used when owner wants cost discipline but also flexibility for design changes.

**(Full marks: Table 4 + Justification 3 = 7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= 90\text{ M} \\
F_t &= 10\text{ M} \\
I &= 0.60 \text{ (builder's share)} \\
C_a &= 84\text{ M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the saving.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Saving} &= C_t - C_a = 90 - 84 = \text{USD } 6\text{ M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the incentive share.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Builder's bonus} &= I \times \text{Saving} = 0.60 \times 6 = \text{USD } 3.6\text{ M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final price.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= C_a + F_t + \text{Builder's bonus} \\[4pt]
&= 84 + 10 + 3.6 \\[4pt]
&= \text{USD } 97.6\text{ M}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The builder beat the target by USD 6 M and earned an extra USD 3.6 M fee. The buyer paid USD 97.6 M total — slightly above target price (100 M) — but obtained the vessel at lower effective cost than the target outcome. The CPIF mechanism therefore **balanced the incentive** between the two parties.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Shipbuilding Contract Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the LD cap** — Many students compute $LD = n \times r$ without checking the statutory cap. Always compute **both** uncapped LD and cap, and apply the **lower** value. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Wrong milestone percentages** — Students often use 30 % instead of 20 % for signing or vice-versa. Memorise the SAJ table exactly: **20-10-10-20-30-10**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up "Cost Plus" types** — Cost Plus, Cost Plus Fixed Fee, and Cost Plus Incentive Fee are three **different** contracts. Formula-wise they differ. Don't substitute one into another.
>
> 4. **Ignoring retention money** — The 10 % retention is **released** after 6–12 months post-delivery. Many students omit this instalment entirely.
>
> 5. **Confusing Refund Guarantee with Performance Bond** — Refund Guarantee covers **buyer's pre-delivery payments**. Performance Bond covers **builder's performance**. They are different instruments.
>
> 6. **Risk transfer timing** — Risk transfers at delivery, **not at launching**. A common misconception.
>
> 7. **Not stating units** — Always write **USD million**, **days**, **knots**, **tonnes/day** explicitly. Units carry marks.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Bullets for KTU Module 4 — Shipbuilding Contracts:**

- A **shipbuilding contract** is a binding agreement defining design, price, milestones, inspection, delivery, and warranty terms.

- **Five types** of contracts: Lump Sum, Cost Plus, Cost Plus Fixed Fee, Cost Plus Incentive Fee, and Unit Price.

- **SAJ payment schedule (must memorise):** **20 + 10 + 10 + 20 + 30 + 10 = 100 %** at six milestones.

- **Liquidated Damages** = Days of Delay × Daily Rate, **capped** at 5–15 % of contract price.

- **Refund Guarantee** is issued by a bank to protect buyer's pre-delivery instalments.

- **Performance Bond** is issued by a bank to guarantee builder's performance.

- **Risk and title** transfer at **delivery**, not at launching.

- **Defects Liability Period** is typically **12 months**; latent defects up to **6 years** under UK common law.

- **Five key parties:** Owner, Builder, Classification Society, Owner's Representative, Guarantor Bank.

- **Standard contract forms:** SAJ (Japanese), AWES (European), BIMCO NEWBUILDCON (universal).

- **Hull inspection** is governed by the Specification, Classification, Inspection & Testing, and Sea Trials clauses.

- **Sea trial LDs** cover speed deficiency and fuel consumption overrun — both are pre-quantified in the contract.

- **York-Antwerp Rules** apply only **after delivery** for general average and salvage.

- **Force Majeure** clauses excuse delay for extraordinary events beyond either party's control.

- **Arbitration clause** is essential — most disputes are resolved by LMAA (London), JCAA (Japan), or SCMA (China).

- **Class certificates** (Hull, Machinery, etc.) issued by classification society are **pre-conditions for delivery** in most contracts.

- **Owner's Superintendent** represents the buyer at the yard throughout construction and during sea trials.

- **Variation orders** (changes to specification mid-build) trigger price and time renegotiation per the contract's variation clause.

- **Cancellation/rescission** is the owner's right to terminate the contract if the builder defaults substantially (misses keel laying by 6+ months, becomes insolvent, etc.).

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Shipbuilding Contracts**
> *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme | Course Code: OENST832 — Hull Inspection*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Costing and Estimation - Difference between Cost and Price
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Costing and Estimation - Difference between Cost and Price

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Hull Inspection** under the broader umbrella of **Ship Repair, Shipbuilding, and Marine Cost Engineering**, the concepts of **Cost** and **Price** represent two fundamentally distinct yet interrelated economic parameters that govern every commercial transaction, contract, and budgeting exercise performed by classification societies, shipyards, marine surveyors, and shipowners.

**Cost** is formally defined as the *aggregate monetary expenditure incurred by a producer or service provider* in acquiring, transforming, or employing the necessary economic resources — comprising raw materials, labour, machinery, energy, and overhead — to deliver a defined engineering output. From the perspective of a hull inspection contractor, cost represents the **outflow of capital** required to mobilize surveyors, deploy Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) equipment, erect scaffolding, mobilize cleaning teams, and prepare technical reports.

**Price**, in contrast, is defined as the *monetary value quoted, negotiated, or charged by the supplier to the buyer* for the delivery of a specified product or service. Price is determined by the interplay of cost, prevailing market dynamics, competitive intensity, desired profit margin, taxation, and risk premium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 4 of OENST832, students must clearly distinguish between the **producer-side economics (Cost)** and the **market-side economics (Price)**. Examination questions frequently test the student's ability to identify the components that convert a *Cost* into a *Price*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hull Inspection Café

Imagine you own a small café located near a shipyard that serves hot tea to shipyard surveyors and hull inspectors during their tea break.

- The **milk, sugar, tea leaves, gas, cup, stirrer, and your time** are your **Cost** — the actual money you spent to make that cup of tea.
- The **₹20 you charge the surveyor** for that cup of tea is your **Price** — it covers your cost (₹8), the shop rent you pay to the landlord (overhead), and a small profit (₹5) so you can keep running the café tomorrow.

In hull inspection terms:
- **Cost** = Surveyor wages + NDT equipment depreciation + Scaffolding hire + Materials + Dry-dock charges.
- **Price** = Cost + Profit margin + Administrative overheads + Taxes + Risk contingency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Rule of Commerce:** *Price is always greater than or equal to Cost*. A price lower than cost implies a loss, while a price equal to cost implies a break-even transaction. No rational commercial entity sustains itself by consistently pricing below cost.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following standard monetary and engineering metrics apply universally to hull inspection costing and pricing exercises:

- **Currency Unit:** All cost figures are conventionally expressed in **United States Dollars (USD)** for international ship repair contracts, or in **Indian Rupees (INR)** for domestic KTU-aligned academic problems.
- **Standard Working Hour:** The marine industry universally adopts **8 hours/day** as a single **man-day (MD)** equivalent.
- **Overhead Multiplier:** Typical hull repair yards apply an overhead recovery factor of **1.15 to 1.45** on direct labour cost.
- **Profit Margin:** Standard shipbuilding and ship-repair pricing includes a profit margin ranging from **8% to 15%** of total cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Cost in Hull Inspection

The **Total Cost** incurred in a hull inspection project can be rigorously decomposed into the following hierarchical structure:

**A. Direct Cost (Prime Cost)**
These are expenditures that can be **directly, unambiguously, and economically traced** to the specific hull inspection job. They include:

- **Direct Material Cost (DMC):** Cost of consumables such as steel coupons for calibration, dye penetrants, magnetic particles, radiography films, ultrasonic couplant, and protective coatings.
- **Direct Labour Cost (DLC):** Wages paid to the surveyors, NDT technicians, welders, and riggers physically engaged on the hull being inspected.
- **Direct Expenses (DE):** Specific costs such as dry-dock hire charges, vessel mobilization fees, third-party subcontracted inspection services, and class society survey fees.

**B. Indirect Cost (Overhead Cost)**
These are expenditures that **cannot be directly traced** to a single inspection job and must be apportioned across multiple projects using a rational basis. They include:

- **Factory/Manufacturing Overhead:** Depreciation of inspection equipment, maintenance of testing laboratories, electricity, and consumables common to multiple jobs.
- **Administrative Overhead:** Salaries of the management accountant, HR personnel, IT infrastructure, office rent, and statutory compliance costs.
- **Selling and Distribution Overhead:** Marketing expenses, quotation preparation costs, post-inspection report dispatch, and client entertainment.
- **Research and Development Overhead:** Cost of developing advanced inspection methodologies.

### 2.2 Decomposition of Price in Hull Inspection

The **Quoted Price** submitted to the shipowner is computed by augmenting the Total Cost with additional commercial layers:

$$\text{Quoted Price} = \text{Total Cost} + \text{Profit Margin} + \text{Commission} + \text{Taxes} - \text{Discount}$$

Each component is defined as follows:

- **Profit Margin:** The financial reward accruing to the inspection firm for assuming entrepreneurial risk and deploying capital.
- **Commission:** The payment made to intermediaries (e.g., shipping agents, brokers) who facilitate the contract.
- **Taxes:** Statutory levies such as **Goods and Services Tax (GST)** in the Indian context, or Value Added Tax (VAT) in international jurisdictions.
- **Discount:** A reduction in price offered to incentivize prompt payment, repeat business, or bulk contract awards.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter | Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Units |
|:-----:|:----------|:------:|:------------------------|:------:|
| 1 | Direct Material Cost | $C_m$ | $\sum (Q_i \times P_i)$ | USD or INR |
| 2 | Direct Labour Cost | $C_l$ | $H \times R \times N$ | USD or INR |
| 3 | Direct Expenses | $C_e$ | $\sum \text{Itemized Direct Costs}$ | USD or INR |
| 4 | Prime Cost | $PC$ | $C_m + C_l + C_e$ | USD or INR |
| 5 | Works/Factory Overhead | $WOH$ | $\%_w \times C_l$ | USD or INR |
| 6 | Administrative Overhead | $AOH$ | $\%_a \times PC$ | USD or INR |
| 7 | Selling & Distribution Overhead | $SOH$ | $\%_s \times PC$ | USD or INR |
| 8 | Total Cost (Cost of Sales) | $TC$ | $PC + WOH + AOH + SOH$ | USD or INR |
| 9 | Profit Margin | $PM$ | $\%_p \times TC$ | USD or INR |
| 10 | Quoted Price (Before Tax) | $P_{pre}$ | $TC + PM$ | USD or INR |
| 11 | Tax (e.g., GST @ 18%) | $T$ | $0.18 \times P_{pre}$ | USD or INR |
| 12 | Final Quoted Price | $P$ | $P_{pre} + T$ | USD or INR |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $H$ denotes total man-hours, $R$ denotes the hourly wage rate, $N$ denotes the number of workers, $Q_i$ denotes the quantity of the $i^{th}$ material, and $P_i$ denotes the unit price of the $i^{th}$ material. All overhead percentage symbols ($\%_w$, $\%_a$, $\%_s$, $\%_p$) are expressed as decimals in computation (e.g., $15\% = 0.15$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This cost-versus-price framework is indispensable in the following professional scenarios:

1. **Tendering and Bidding:** Shipyards preparing competitive bids for hull inspection contracts must accurately compute costs and then strategically add markup to remain profitable yet competitive.
2. **Budgetary Quotation to Shipowners:** Survey firms issue detailed price quotations that segregate direct costs, overheads, and profit margins.
3. **Contract Negotiation:** Class societies (IRS, LR, DNV, BV) negotiate inspection scope and price with owners, often benchmarked against international cost norms.
4. **Loss Analysis:** When an inspection job results in a financial loss, forensic accountants compare actual cost with quoted price to identify pricing errors.
5. **Insurance Claim Settlement:** In hull damage cases, insurance assessors evaluate whether the inspection/repair price reasonably reflects the underlying cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Fundamental Derivation: From Cost to Price

We begin by formally deriving the relationship between **Total Cost** and **Quoted Price** for a hypothetical hull inspection engagement. Let us assume the following engagement parameters, drawn from a typical dry-dock hull inspection scenario:

**Given Data (Hypothetical Hull Inspection Contract):**

- Direct Material Cost ($C_m$) = **₹ 2,50,000**
- Direct Labour: 5 surveyors × 8 hours/day × 10 days × ₹ 800/hour = ?
- Direct Expenses (Dry-dock hire, NDT subcontract) = **₹ 4,00,000**
- Factory Overhead Rate = **25% of Direct Labour Cost**
- Administrative Overhead Rate = **12% of Prime Cost**
- Selling Overhead Rate = **8% of Prime Cost**
- Desired Profit Margin = **10% of Total Cost**
- GST (Tax) Rate = **18% of Pre-Tax Price**

**Step 1: Compute Direct Labour Cost ($C_l$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_l &= N \times D \times H_{day} \times R \\
&= 5 \times 10 \times 8 \times 800 \\
&= 40{,}000 \times 800 \\
&= \text{₹ } 32{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* We multiply the number of surveyors ($N = 5$) by the project duration in days ($D = 10$), then by the working hours per day ($H_{day} = 8$), and finally by the hourly wage rate ($R = \text{₹ } 800$). This yields the aggregate direct labour expenditure.

**Step 2: Compute Prime Cost ($PC$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= C_m + C_l + C_e \\
&= 2{,}50{,}000 + 32{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 38{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The prime cost aggregates all three categories of directly attributable expenditure — materials, labour, and expenses.

**Step 3: Compute Works Overhead ($WOH$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WOH &= 0.25 \times C_l \\
&= 0.25 \times 32{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 8{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The factory/works overhead is applied at 25% on direct labour cost, as it includes equipment depreciation, workshop utilities, and consumables that scale with labour deployment.

**Step 4: Compute Administrative Overhead ($AOH$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOH &= 0.12 \times PC \\
&= 0.12 \times 38{,}50{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 4{,}62{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Administrative overhead is applied on prime cost, capturing the cost of management, HR, and office infrastructure.

**Step 5: Compute Selling and Distribution Overhead ($SOH$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SOH &= 0.08 \times PC \\
&= 0.08 \times 38{,}50{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 3{,}08{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Selling overhead covers quotation preparation, client liaison, and report dispatch expenses.

**Step 6: Compute Total Cost ($TC$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= PC + WOH + AOH + SOH \\
&= 38{,}50{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}62{,}000 + 3{,}08{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 54{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Total Cost (also termed Cost of Sales or Full Absorption Cost) is the complete cost incurred by the inspection firm to deliver the service, including all direct and indirect expenditures.

**Step 7: Compute Profit Margin ($PM$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PM &= 0.10 \times TC \\
&= 0.10 \times 54{,}20{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 5{,}42{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* A 10% return on total cost is added to reward the inspection firm for its entrepreneurial risk and capital deployment.

**Step 8: Compute Pre-Tax Quoted Price ($P_{pre}$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pre} &= TC + PM \\
&= 54{,}20{,}000 + 5{,}42{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 59{,}62{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The pre-tax price is the sum of the full cost and the desired profit margin.

**Step 9: Compute GST ($T$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 0.18 \times P_{pre} \\
&= 0.18 \times 59{,}62{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 10{,}73{,}160
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Goods and Services Tax at 18% is levied on the pre-tax price as per Indian statutory requirements.

**Step 10: Compute Final Quoted Price ($P$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= P_{pre} + T \\
&= 59{,}62{,}000 + 10{,}73{,}160 \\
&= \text{₹ } 70{,}35{,}160
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The final quoted price is the amount that the shipowner is contractually obligated to pay for the complete hull inspection engagement.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cost vs Price

| S.No. | Dimension of Comparison | Cost | Price |
|:-----:|:------------------------|:-----|:------|
| 1 | Definition | Total monetary expenditure incurred | Monetary value charged from the buyer |
| 2 | Perspective | Producer / Service Provider | Buyer / Market |
| 3 | Determinant | Production inputs, resources, technology | Market forces, competition, demand |
| 4 | Variability | Relatively fixed for a given scope | Highly variable based on negotiation |
| 5 | Inclusion of Profit | **No** — cost excludes profit | **Yes** — price includes profit margin |
| 6 | Inclusion of Tax | **No** | **Yes** — statutory taxes are added |
| 7 | Objectivity | **Objective and verifiable** from accounts | **Subjective and negotiable** |
| 8 | Time Reference | Historical (incurred) | Forward-looking (quoted) |
| 9 | Role in Decision-Making | Cost control, budgeting | Revenue forecasting, market positioning |
| 10 | Used in Tendering | Cost estimation sheet (internal) | Commercial quotation (external) |
| 11 | Standardization | Subject to costing standards (CAS, GAAP) | Subject to market dynamics and commercial policy |
| 12 | Accounting Treatment | Debit (Expense) | Credit (Revenue) |
| 13 | Numerical Relationship | Lower magnitude | Higher or equal magnitude |
| 14 | Impact of Discounts | No effect on cost | Reduces the realized price |
| 15 | Hull Inspection Example | ₹ 54,20,000 (full absorption cost) | ₹ 70,35,160 (quoted to shipowner) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cost Composition Tree

The following diagram illustrates the hierarchical decomposition of the **Total Quoted Price** into its constituent components, mapping how each layer is built upon the previous one.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Quoted Price P"]
    nodeB["GST Tax 18 percent"]
    nodeC["Pre Tax Price P pre"]
    nodeD["Profit Margin PM"]
    nodeE["Total Cost TC"]
    nodeF["Prime Cost PC"]
    nodeG["Factory Overhead WOH"]
    nodeH["Admin Overhead AOH"]
    nodeI["Selling Overhead SOH"]
    nodeJ["Direct Material Cm"]
    nodeK["Direct Labour Cl"]
    nodeL["Direct Expenses Ce"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeL
```

*Reading the diagram:* The **Quoted Price** sits at the top. The first deduction is the **GST**, which when removed reveals the **Pre-Tax Price**. The pre-tax price contains the **Profit Margin** and the **Total Cost**. The total cost itself is composed of the **Prime Cost** plus three overhead layers — **Factory, Administrative, and Selling Overheads**. Finally, the prime cost is built from **Direct Material, Direct Labour, and Direct Expenses**, which represent the foundational, traceable expenditures of the hull inspection engagement.

### 4.2 Conceptual Flowchart: Cost-to-Price Conversion Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["Step 1 Identify Direct Costs"]
    stepB["Step 2 Compute Prime Cost PC"]
    stepC["Step 3 Apportion Overheads"]
    stepD["Step 4 Arrive at Total Cost"]
    stepE["Step 5 Add Profit Margin"]
    stepF["Step 6 Apply Statutory Tax"]
    stepG["Step 7 Final Quoted Price"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
```

### 4.3 Cost vs Price Visual Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    leftSide["COST SIDE PRODUCER"]
    rightSide["PRICE SIDE MARKET"]

    costMat["Direct Material"]
    costLab["Direct Labour"]
    costExp["Direct Expenses"]
    costOH["Indirect Overheads"]

    priceCost["Underlying Cost"]
    priceProfit["Profit Margin"]
    priceComm["Commission"]
    priceTax["Taxes GST"]
    priceDisc["Discounts"]

    leftSide --> costMat
    leftSide --> costLab
    leftSide --> costExp
    leftSide --> costOH

    rightSide --> priceCost
    rightSide --> priceProfit
    rightSide --> priceComm
    rightSide --> priceTax
    rightSide --> priceDisc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The two schematic domains represent the **internal economics of the inspection firm** (Cost side — left) and the **external commercial interface with the shipowner** (Price side — right). The transformation from Cost to Price occurs at the commercial boundary where profit motivation, statutory obligations, and market forces are applied.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Cost" as applied to a hull inspection contract. List any three elements of cost.*

**Model Answer:**

Cost, in the context of a hull inspection contract, refers to the total monetary expenditure incurred by the inspection service provider in mobilizing resources, executing the inspection, and delivering the technical report to the shipowner.

The three principal elements of cost are:

1. **Direct Material Cost** — e.g., NDT consumables, calibration coupons, radiographic films.
2. **Direct Labour Cost** — e.g., wages paid to surveyors and NDT technicians.
3. **Indirect Cost (Overheads)** — e.g., equipment depreciation, administrative expenses, and selling overheads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Three elements (2 marks: 0.5 + 0.5 + 1.0).

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between "Cost" and "Price" with two relevant points in the context of marine hull inspection.*

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Cost | Price |
|:-----:|:-----|:------|
| 1 | It is the expenditure actually incurred by the inspection firm to perform the hull survey. | It is the amount charged by the inspection firm to the shipowner for the hull survey. |
| 2 | It excludes profit and is determined internally from accounting records. | It includes profit margin, taxes, and is influenced by market competition. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Two contrasting points (2 marks: 1 + 1) + Brief contextual explanation (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

(a) *Explain with a neat block diagram the various elements that constitute the "Total Cost" of a hull inspection project. (7 marks)*

(b) *A hull inspection firm incurs the following expenditure for a 15-day in-water hull survey of a bulk carrier:*
- Direct Material = ₹ 3,00,000
- Direct Labour = 8 surveyors working 6 hours/day at ₹ 700/hour
- Direct Expenses = ₹ 2,50,000
- Works Overhead = 20% of Direct Labour Cost
- Administrative Overhead = 10% of Prime Cost
- Selling Overhead = 5% of Prime Cost

*Compute the Total Cost of the inspection engagement. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer for Part (a) — Elements of Total Cost:**

The Total Cost of a hull inspection project comprises two major categories:

**I. Direct Cost (Prime Cost):**
1. **Direct Material** — NDT consumables, calibration blocks, dye penetrants.
2. **Direct Labour** — Surveyor and technician wages directly engaged on the hull.
3. **Direct Expenses** — Dry-dock hire, third-party subcontracts, mobilization.

**II. Indirect Cost (Overheads):**
1. **Factory/Works Overhead** — Equipment depreciation, workshop utilities.
2. **Administrative Overhead** — Office salaries, rent, statutory compliance.
3. **Selling and Distribution Overhead** — Quotation preparation, client liaison.

The block-level architecture is identical to that illustrated in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Model Answer for Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1: Compute Direct Labour Cost ($C_l$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_l &= N \times D \times H_{day} \times R \\
&= 8 \times 15 \times 6 \times 700 \\
&= 720 \times 700 \\
&= \text{₹ } 5{,}04{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Prime Cost ($PC$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= C_m + C_l + C_e \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}04{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 10{,}54{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Works Overhead ($WOH$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WOH &= 0.20 \times C_l \\
&= 0.20 \times 5{,}04{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Administrative Overhead ($AOH$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOH &= 0.10 \times PC \\
&= 0.10 \times 10{,}54{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 1{,}05{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Selling Overhead ($SOH$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SOH &= 0.05 \times PC \\
&= 0.05 \times 10{,}54{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 52{,}700
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Total Cost ($TC$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= PC + WOH + AOH + SOH \\
&= 10{,}54{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}800 + 1{,}05{,}400 + 52{,}700 \\
&= \text{₹ } 13{,}12{,}900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The Total Cost of the hull inspection engagement is **₹ 13,12,900**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently make two critical errors in such problems:
> 1. **Misapplication of Overhead Base:** Works Overhead is applied on **Direct Labour Cost**, while Administrative and Selling Overheads are applied on **Prime Cost**. Applying all overheads on Prime Cost (or all on Labour Cost) results in **3 to 4 marks being deducted**.
> 2. **Arithmetic Slip in Direct Labour:** The formula is $N \times D \times H_{day} \times R$. Forgetting the daily hours factor ($H_{day}$) leads to a wrong labour cost and cascades into every subsequent step, **losing up to 7 marks**.

---

#### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

(a) *Explain the difference between "Cost" and "Price" in the context of hull inspection contracts. State the formula relating the two and identify at least five distinct components that transform a Cost into a Price. (7 marks)*

(b) *The following data pertains to a hull inspection contract:*
- Total Cost incurred = ₹ 8,00,000
- Desired Profit Margin = 12% of Total Cost
- Commission payable to broker = 3% of Pre-Tax Price
- GST = 18% of Pre-Tax Price

*Calculate the Final Quoted Price to be billed to the shipowner. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer for Part (a) — Conceptual Distinction and Formula:**

**Difference Between Cost and Price:**

| Aspect | Cost | Price |
|:-------|:-----|:------|
| Nature | Internal, expenditure-based | External, market-based |
| Includes Profit? | No | Yes |
| Includes Tax? | No | Yes |
| Determined by | Engineering inputs and accounting records | Market dynamics, competition, and commercial strategy |

**Master Formula:**

$$
\text{Quoted Price} = \text{Total Cost} + \text{Profit} + \text{Commission} + \text{Taxes} - \text{Discounts}
$$

**Five Distinct Components that Transform Cost into Price:**

1. **Profit Margin** — Entrepreneurial reward added on top of total cost.
2. **Selling Commission** — Payment to intermediaries facilitating the contract.
3. **Statutory Taxes** — GST, VAT, or service tax levied by the government.
4. **Administrative Markup** — Recovery of head-office costs not captured in direct overheads.
5. **Risk Contingency** — Provision for unforeseen technical, weather, or geopolitical risks.

**Model Answer for Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1: Compute Profit Margin ($PM$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PM &= 0.12 \times TC \\
&= 0.12 \times 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 96{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Pre-Tax Price Before Commission ($P_0$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= TC + PM \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000 + 96{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 8{,}96{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Establish the Equation for Pre-Tax Price Including Commission ($P_{pre}$)**

The commission is 3% of $P_{pre}$, and is itself part of the pre-tax price. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pre} &= P_0 + 0.03 \times P_{pre} \\
P_{pre} - 0.03 \times P_{pre} &= 8{,}96{,}000 \\
0.97 \times P_{pre} &= 8{,}96{,}000 \\
P_{pre} &= \frac{8{,}96{,}000}{0.97} \\
P_{pre} &= \text{₹ } 9{,}23{,}711.34
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The commission is calculated on the pre-tax price, which itself includes the commission. Hence, we use the reciprocal $(1 - r)$ to back-solve for the pre-tax price inclusive of commission.

**Step 4: Compute GST ($T$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 0.18 \times P_{pre} \\
&= 0.18 \times 9{,}23{,}711.34 \\
&= \text{₹ } 1{,}66{,}268.04
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Final Quoted Price ($P$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= P_{pre} + T \\
&= 9{,}23{,}711.34 + 1{,}66{,}268.04 \\
&= \text{₹ } 10{,}89{,}979.38
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The Final Quoted Price billed to the shipowner is approximately **₹ 10,89,979**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** The most common error in such "commission on price" problems is to compute the commission as a fixed percentage of $(TC + PM)$ instead of recognizing it as a percentage of the **pre-tax price that includes the commission itself**. This is a classic circular dependency. Students who fail to apply the $(1 - r)$ reciprocal lose **up to 4 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cost** is the actual expenditure incurred by the inspection service provider; **Price** is the amount charged to the client.
- **Price ≥ Cost** in every rational commercial transaction; pricing below cost implies a financial loss.
- The **Prime Cost (PC)** aggregates Direct Material ($C_m$), Direct Labour ($C_l$), and Direct Expenses ($C_e$).
- **Works Overhead** is conventionally applied as a percentage of **Direct Labour Cost**, not Prime Cost.
- **Administrative and Selling Overheads** are applied as a percentage of **Prime Cost**.
- **Total Cost** = Prime Cost + All Overheads (Factory + Administrative + Selling).
- **Profit Margin** is typically 8–15% of Total Cost in the marine sector.
- **GST at 18%** is the prevailing Indian statutory tax for hull inspection services.
- **Commission-on-price problems** require the reciprocal trick: if commission rate is $r$, then $P_{pre} = \frac{\text{Base}}{1 - r}$.
- The **Analytical Matrix** (Section 3.2) is a high-yield revision tool for last-minute KTU preparation.
- In KTU valuation, **clearly label every formula used**, **show all intermediate steps**, and **box the final answer** for maximum marks.
- Marine costing problems often carry a **currency, man-day, or unit** marking check; always mention the units in your final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Top Down and Bottom Top Approaches in Costing
Demarcations and Subdivisions of Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Demarcations and Subdivisions of Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Hull Inspection** and **Ship Surveying Economics**, **Demarcations and Subdivisions of Costs** refers to the systematic classification, segregation, and allocation of the total expenditure incurred during hull construction, in-service inspection, maintenance, and repair activities. This framework segregates the gross cost into mutually exclusive, exhaustive, and functionally homogeneous sub-cost elements so that each component can be independently estimated, controlled, audited, and benchmarked against industry standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *Demarcation of costs is the engineering-economic procedure of partitioning the total financial outlay of a hull project (new-build, repair, or survey) into standardized categories such as material, labor, overhead, supervision, dry-dock, staging, consumables, NDT/equipment, and contractor's margin, to enable transparent cost-control, claim reconciliation, and life-cycle budgeting in line with classification society and insurance protocols.*

The principal cost demarcations universally applied in shipbuilding, ship repair, and hull inspection are governed by the following reference frameworks:

- **ISO 12900** – Terminology for shipbuilding cost elements
- **SAJ (Shipbuilders' Association of Japan) Standard Cost Breakdown** – Hull / Outfit / Machinery split
- **Class Rules (ABS / DNV / LR / BV / IRS)** – Survey fee schedules
- **P&I Club & H&M Insurance Survey cost norms** – Damage rectification cost demarcation
- **MARPOL / SOLAS compliance audit costs** – Regulatory cost demarcation

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Automobile Service Centre**
>
> Imagine you take your car for a comprehensive service. The final bill of **₹15,000** is not just "service cost" – it is a *demarcated* sum. The bill breaks it into:
> - **Spare parts** (oil filter, brake pads) → *Material Cost*
> - **Mechanic's wages** for the time spent → *Direct Labour Cost*
> - **Workshop rent, electricity, supervisor's salary** → *Overhead / Indirect Cost*
> - **Lifting the car on the ramp (hydraulic lift usage)** → *Docking/Staging Equivalent*
> - **GST and service centre's profit** → *Tax & Margin*
>
> Exactly the same logic applies to a **hull inspection project**. The total cost of inspecting, surveying, and certifying a ship hull is **demarcated** into well-defined pockets, and each pocket (subdivision) is estimated, controlled, and reconciled independently. Without this demarcation, an owner cannot determine whether a quoted repair cost is inflated, whether dry-dock time is justified, or whether NDT charges are reasonable.

## 1.3 Why Demarcation Matters in Hull Inspection

| # | Engineering Rationale | Real-World Outcome |
|---|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Transparent tender evaluation** | Owner can compare competing shipyards quote-by-quote |
| 2 | **Insurance claim reconciliation** | H&M underwriter can verify each cost head independently |
| 3 | **Cash-flow scheduling** | Owner can stage payments aligned to milestones |
| 4 | **Audit & legal compliance** | Tax authorities & P&I clubs can validate expenditure |
| 5 | **Bench-marking & cost-control** | Yard management can target high-cost sub-heads |
| 6 | **Variation order management** | Scope changes (e.g., extra plate renewal) are costed cleanly |
| 7 | **Dispute resolution** | Arbitration panels arbitrate on demarcated line items |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea to Remember:** Cost demarcation is *not* an accounting exercise alone — it is a **contractual, technical, and legal instrument** that allows every stakeholder (owner, yard, class, insurer, flag state) to speak the same financial language.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of Cost Demarcation

The total cost of a hull inspection / repair / new-build project is demarcated in **three ascending tiers**:

### Tier 1 – Macro Demarcation (Top-Down)
The total cost $C_{\text{Total}}$ is split into the four universally accepted macro-buckets:

$$C_{\text{Total}} = C_{\text{Material}} + C_{\text{Labour}} + C_{\text{Overhead}} + C_{\text{Margin}}$$

where each term is itself a *composite* of multiple sub-costs.

### Tier 2 – Functional Demarcation (by Engineering Function)
The cost is split by what the money *buys*:

$$C_{\text{Total}} = C_{\text{Hull Steel}} + C_{\text{Outfit}} + C_{\text{Machinery}} + C_{\text{Electrical}} + C_{\text{Engineering/Design}} + C_{\text{Inspection/Survey}}$$

### Tier 3 – Activity-Based Demarcation (for Repair & Survey)
The cost is split by the **activity performed** during the in-service inspection:

$$C_{\text{Survey}} = C_{\text{Preparation}} + C_{\text{Access}} + C_{\text{NDT}} + C_{\text{Testing}} + C_{\text{Consumables}} + C_{\text{Labour}} + C_{\text{Documentation}}$$

## 2.2 Detailed Sub-Cost Categories (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important revision sheet** for Module 4. Every KTU Part-A and Part-B question on this topic maps onto at least one of these rows.

### 2.2.1 Cost Demarcation – New-Build Hull Perspective

| S.No | Demarcation Head | Sub-Items Included | Typical % of New-Build Cost (Indicative) |
|------|------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Material Cost ($C_{\text{Mat}}$)** | Hull steel, plates, sections, welding consumables, paints, cathodic protection anodes | **20 – 25 %** |
| 2 | **Outfit Cost ($C_{\text{Outfit}}$)** | Accommodation, deck fittings, lifesaving, mooring, navigation, piping | **15 – 20 %** |
| 3 | **Machinery Cost ($C_{\text{Mach}}$)** | Main engine, aux. engines, boilers, shafting, propellers, reduction gear | **25 – 30 %** |
| 4 | **Electrical & Control ($C_{\text{El}}$)** | Generators, switchboards, automation, cables, bridge electronics | **8 – 10 %** |
| 5 | **Labour – Direct ($C_{\text{DL}}$)** | Cutter, fitter, welder, painter, electrician, machinist hours | **12 – 18 %** |
| 6 | **Labour – Indirect / Supervision ($C_{\text{IL}}$)** | Foremen, QC, planning, safety, class surveyors | **3 – 5 %** |
| 7 | **Overhead ($C_{\text{OH}}$)** | Yard rent, utilities, depreciation, insurance, admin, R&D amortisation | **6 – 10 %** |
| 8 | **Design & Engineering ($C_{\text{Eng}}$)** | Basic design, detailed design, approval drawings, model testing | **3 – 5 %** |
| 9 | **Profit / Contractor's Margin ($C_{\text{Mgn}}$)** | Negotiated; typically 5–10 % of direct cost | **5 – 10 %** |
| 10 | **Inspection / Survey Fees ($C_{\text{Ins}}$)** | Classification, flag state, owner, P&I, regulatory | **1 – 3 %** |

### 2.2.2 Cost Demarcation – Hull Inspection / Repair (In-Service) Perspective

| S.No | Demarcation Head | Sub-Items Included |
|------|------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Dry-Docking Cost ($C_{\text{DD}}$)** | Dry-dock hire, docking/undocking tugs, dock masters, blocking, keel cooling |
| 2 | **Staging & Access ($C_{\text{Stg}}$)** | Scaffolding, ladders, cherry pickers, rope-access teams, safety nets |
| 3 | **Surface Preparation ($C_{\text{SP}}$)** | Blasting (Sa 2.5), water-jetting (WJ), chipping, needle-gunning, grit |
| 4 | **NDT Cost ($C_{\text{NDT}}$)** | Ultrasonic (UT), radiographic (RT), magnetic particle (MPT), dye-penetrant (DPT), eddy-current (ET), thickness gauging |
| 5 | **Consumables ($C_{\text{Cons}}$)** | Welding electrodes, gas, paint, primer, anode zincs, cleaning chemicals |
| 6 | **Steel Renewal ($C_{\text{Steel}}$)** | Cost of replacement plates, sections, machining, bending, profile cutting |
| 7 | **Direct Repair Labour ($C_{\text{RL}}$)** | Welder hours, fitter hours, painter hours, machinist hours at agreed rate |
| 8 | **Indirect / Workshop Labour ($C_{\text{IWL}}$)** | Shop fabrication, pipe-bending, machine-shop work, electrical workshop |
| 9 | **Testing & Commissioning ($C_{\text{Test}}$)** | Hydrostatic test, leak test, NDT re-test, sea trial fuel, port dues |
| 10 | **Survey & Documentation ($C_{\text{Doc}}$)** | Class survey fee, condition survey fee, report preparation, certificates |
| 11 | **Overhead & Supervision ($C_{\text{OH+Sup}}$)** | Project manager, QC, HSE, yard facilities, utilities |
| 12 | **Profit ($C_{\text{Mgn}}$)** | Yard's mark-up |

## 2.3 The KTU Cost-Demarcation Equation (Master Formula)

The **canonical shipyard quotation formula** that a student must be able to write on demand is:

$$
\boxed{
C_{\text{Quoted}} = \underbrace{(C_{\text{Mat}} + C_{\text{Lab}} + C_{\text{Sub}})}_{\text{Direct Cost } (C_D)} + \underbrace{C_{\text{OH}}}_{\text{Overhead}} + \underbrace{C_{\text{Profit}}}_{\text{Margin}} + \underbrace{C_{\text{Tax}}}_{\text{GST/VAT}}
}
$$

where:

$$
C_{\text{Sub}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{\text{Sub},i} \quad \text{(sum of all sub-contracted items)}
$$

$$
C_{\text{OH}} = k_{\text{OH}} \times C_D \quad \text{where } k_{\text{OH}} \in [0.10,\; 0.25]
$$

$$
C_{\text{Profit}} = k_{\text{P}} \times (C_D + C_{\text{OH}}) \quad \text{where } k_{\text{P}} \in [0.05,\; 0.12]
$$

$$
C_{\text{Tax}} = r_{\text{T}} \times (C_D + C_{\text{OH}} + C_{\text{Profit}}) \quad \text{where } r_{\text{T}} = \text{prevailing GST rate (typically } 18\% \text{)}
$$

## 2.4 The Hull-Cost-Only Demarcation (Class Society Approach)

When a classification society (e.g., **ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS, KR**) prepares a **hull condition survey cost estimate**, they demarcate it specifically as:

$$
C_{\text{Class Survey}} = C_{\text{Pre-Survey}} + C_{\text{In-Water Survey}} + C_{\text{Dry-Dock Survey}} + C_{\text{NDT Package}} + C_{\text{Thickness Gauging}} + C_{\text{Doc Fee}}
$$

with the governing equations:

$$
C_{\text{Thickness Gauging}} = N_{\text{Points}} \times R_{\text{UT,pt}} \times (1 + k_{\text{Access}})
$$

$$
C_{\text{NDT Package}} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( A_{j} \times R_{\text{NDT},j} \right)
$$

**Notation & Units (KTU Examiner's Reference):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $N_{\text{Points}}$ | Number of thickness measurement points | dimensionless |
| $R_{\text{UT,pt}}$ | Rate per UT thickness gauging point | ₹ / USD per point |
| $k_{\text{Access}}$ | Access surcharge factor (scaffolding, staging) | dimensionless (0.1 – 0.4) |
| $A_{j}$ | Area of $j^{\text{th}}$ NDT zone | $\text{m}^2$ |
| $R_{\text{NDT},j}$ | Rate of $j^{\text{th}}$ NDT method | ₹/$\text{m}^2$ or USD/$\text{m}^2$ |
| $k_{\text{OH}}$ | Overhead absorption rate | dimensionless |
| $k_{\text{P}}$ | Profit margin rate | dimensionless |
| $r_{\text{T}}$ | Tax rate | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Cost Demarcation

1. **Pre-purchase evaluation** – buyers estimate repair liability for second-hand tonnage.
2. **Insurance underwriting** – H&M (Hull & Machinery) insurers fix premiums and reserve estimates.
3. **Class survey planning** – surveyors plan work packages and downtime windows.
4. **Yard productivity** – yards benchmark labour-hours per tonne of steel.
5. **Dispute resolution** – arbitration relies on itemised cost-head apportionment.
6. **Budgeting of academic ship-design projects** – KTU student design projects use the same framework to estimate new-build cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 – Cost Demarcation for a Hull Repair Quotation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern (14-Mark Module 4 Type Question):**
> A 30,000 DWT bulk carrier is scheduled for a Special Survey (SS). The shipyard submits a quotation with the following demarcated line items. The owner must verify the total cost, compute the per-day cost in dry-dock, and assess the overhead & profit loading.
>
> Given data:
> - Material cost (steel renewal + consumables) = **₹ 1,20,00,000**
> - Direct labour cost (welding, fitting, blasting, painting) = **₹ 85,00,000**
> - Sub-contracted NDT package = **₹ 32,00,000**
> - Dry-dock hire (15 days × ₹ 4,00,000 / day) = **₹ 60,00,000**
> - Staging & access = **₹ 18,00,000**
> - Design, engineering & documentation = **₹ 9,00,000**
> - Overhead loading rate $k_{\text{OH}} = 12\%$ of Direct Cost
> - Profit margin rate $k_{\text{P}} = 8\%$ of (Direct + Overhead)
> - GST rate $r_{\text{T}} = 18\%$

### Step 1 – Identify the Direct Cost components

$$
C_{\text{Mat}} = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Lab}} = 85{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Sub}} = 32{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

$$
C_{\text{DD}} = 60{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Stg}} = 18{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Eng}} = 9{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Direct cost (CD) is *every cost that can be physically and unambiguously traced to the hull-repair work-package*. Dry-dock hire is included here because it is incurred *only* because the repair is being executed (a directly attributable cost).

$$
C_D = C_{\text{Mat}} + C_{\text{Lab}} + C_{\text{Sub}} + C_{\text{DD}} + C_{\text{Stg}} + C_{\text{Eng}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_D = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 85{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_D = 3{,}24{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Out of the entire repair project, ₹ 3.24 Crore is *direct* expenditure traceable to the actual hull work.

### Step 2 – Compute the Overhead Loading

The overhead is loaded as a *percentage* of the direct cost. The rate $k_{\text{OH}} = 12\% = 0.12$ is a yard-specific absorption factor that recovers administration, utilities, depreciation, and supervision.

$$
C_{\text{OH}} = k_{\text{OH}} \times C_D = 0.12 \times 3{,}24{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{\text{OH}} = 38{,}88{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The yard charges ₹ 38.88 Lakh as overhead to cover its fixed operating expenses, allocated to this specific repair project.

### Step 3 – Compute the Profit Margin

The profit is loaded on the *combined* Direct + Overhead cost base. The rate $k_{\text{P}} = 8\% = 0.08$ is the negotiated contractor's margin.

$$
C_{\text{Profit}} = k_{\text{P}} \times (C_D + C_{\text{OH}}) = 0.08 \times (3{,}24{,}00{,}000 + 38{,}88{,}000)
$$

$$
C_{\text{Profit}} = 0.08 \times 3{,}62{,}88{,}000 = 29{,}03{,}040 \;\text{₹}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The yard's gross profit from this project is ₹ 29.03 Lakh before tax.

### Step 4 – Compute the Sub-Total (Pre-Tax)

$$
C_{\text{Subtotal}} = C_D + C_{\text{OH}} + C_{\text{Profit}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Subtotal}} = 3{,}24{,}00{,}000 + 38{,}88{,}000 + 29{,}03{,}040 = 3{,}91{,}91{,}040 \;\text{₹}
$$

### Step 5 – Compute the GST (Tax) Loading

$$
C_{\text{Tax}} = r_{\text{T}} \times C_{\text{Subtotal}} = 0.18 \times 3{,}91{,}91{,}040
$$

$$
C_{\text{Tax}} = 70{,}54{,}387.20 \;\text{₹}
$$

### Step 6 – Compute the Final Quoted Cost to Owner

$$
C_{\text{Quoted}} = C_{\text{Subtotal}} + C_{\text{Tax}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Quoted}} = 3{,}91{,}91{,}040 + 70{,}54{,}387.20 = 4{,}62{,}45{,}427.20 \;\text{₹}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{Quoted}} \approx ₹ 4.62 \text{ Crore}}
$$

### Step 7 – Compute the Per-Day In-Yard Cost (Analytical Sub-Result)

The ship is in the yard for **15 days** of dry-docking plus an assumed 10 days of preparation & 5 days of commissioning → total **30 days** of project duration.

$$
C_{\text{Per Day}} = \frac{C_{\text{Quoted}}}{\text{Total Project Days}} = \frac{4{,}62{,}45{,}427.20}{30}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{Per Day}} \approx ₹ 15{,}41{,}514 \text{ per day} \;\approx\; ₹ 1.54 \text{ Lakh / day}}
$$

### Step 8 – Tabulate the Demarcation (for the answer sheet)

| Cost Head | Amount (₹) | % of $C_{\text{Quoted}}$ |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------------|
| Material | 1,20,00,000 | 25.95 % |
| Direct Labour | 85,00,000 | 18.38 % |
| NDT Sub-contract | 32,00,000 | 6.92 % |
| Dry-Dock Hire | 60,00,000 | 12.98 % |
| Staging & Access | 18,00,000 | 3.89 % |
| Engineering & Documentation | 9,00,000 | 1.95 % |
| **Direct Cost Sub-Total** | **3,24,00,000** | **70.07 %** |
| Overhead (12 %) | 38,88,000 | 8.41 % |
| Profit (8 %) | 29,03,040 | 6.28 % |
| **Sub-Total (Pre-Tax)** | **3,91,91,040** | **84.75 %** |
| GST @ 18 % | 70,54,387 | 15.25 % |
| **GRAND TOTAL** | **4,62,45,427** | **100 %** |

> **Examiner's Insight:** This kind of *demarcated table* is exactly what earns the full 14 marks. A student who only writes the grand total loses up to 4–5 marks on presentation and sub-head mapping.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 – NDT Cost Demarcation for a Hull Thickness Gauging Survey

> **Problem:** A classification surveyor must plan the NDT cost for a 20-year-old Aframax tanker (98,000 DWT). The hull is to be gauged at 250 points distributed across bottom, side, deck, and transverse bulkheads. The NDT sub-contractor's rate card is as follows:
>
> - UT thickness gauging rate = **₹ 850 per point**
> - Access surcharge factor $k_{\text{Access}} = 0.30$
> - MPI (Magnetic Particle Inspection) on welds = 120 m of weld @ **₹ 1,200 per metre**
> - Report & certification fee (lump-sum) = **₹ 75,000**

### Step 1 – Cost of UT Thickness Gauging

$$
C_{\text{UT}} = N_{\text{Points}} \times R_{\text{UT,pt}} \times (1 + k_{\text{Access}})
$$

$$
C_{\text{UT}} = 250 \times 850 \times (1 + 0.30)
$$

$$
C_{\text{UT}} = 250 \times 850 \times 1.30 = 2{,}76{,}250 \;\text{₹}
$$

### Step 2 – Cost of MPI on Welds

$$
C_{\text{MPI}} = L_{\text{weld}} \times R_{\text{MPI}} = 120 \times 1{,}200 = 1{,}44{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

### Step 3 – Documentation Fee

$$
C_{\text{Doc}} = 75{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

### Step 4 – NDT Sub-Total

$$
C_{\text{NDT,Total}} = C_{\text{UT}} + C_{\text{MPI}} + C_{\text{Doc}} = 2{,}76{,}250 + 1{,}44{,}000 + 75{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{NDT,Total}} = 4{,}95{,}250 \;\text{₹} \;\approx\; ₹ 4.95 \text{ Lakh}}
$$

> **Analytical Observation:** The 30 % access surcharge alone adds ₹ 63,750 to the cost — students often miss this in the KTU answer and lose 2 marks.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 3 – Per-Tonne Hull Steel Cost Demarcation

> **Problem:** A Panamax bulker new-build (75,000 DWT) uses **12,500 tonnes** of hull steel. The steel procurement cost is **₹ 95,000 per tonne**. The yard absorbs:
>
> - Cutting, bending, fabrication labour = **₹ 22,000 / tonne**
> - Welding consumables = **₹ 8,500 / tonne**
> - Painting & cathodic protection = **₹ 11,000 / tonne**
> - Yard overhead = **15 % of direct manufacturing cost**
> - Profit = **7 % of (Direct + Overhead)**

### Step 1 – Direct Manufacturing Cost per Tonne

$$
C_{\text{Direct/tonne}} = C_{\text{Steel}} + C_{\text{Fab}} + C_{\text{Weld}} + C_{\text{Paint}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Direct/tonne}} = 95{,}000 + 22{,}000 + 8{,}500 + 11{,}000 = 1{,}36{,}500 \;\text{₹/tonne}
$$

### Step 2 – Total Direct Manufacturing Cost

$$
C_{\text{Direct,Total}} = 1{,}36{,}500 \times 12{,}500 = 17{,}06{,}25{,}000 \;\text{₹} \;(\approx ₹ 17.06 \text{ Cr})
$$

### Step 3 – Overhead Loading

$$
C_{\text{OH/tonne}} = 0.15 \times 1{,}36{,}500 = 20{,}475 \;\text{₹/tonne}
$$

### Step 4 – Sub-Total per Tonne (Pre-Profit)

$$
C_{\text{Sub/tonne}} = 1{,}36{,}500 + 20{,}475 = 1{,}56{,}975 \;\text{₹/tonne}
$$

### Step 5 – Profit Loading

$$
C_{\text{Profit/tonne}} = 0.07 \times 1{,}56{,}975 = 10{,}988.25 \;\text{₹/tonne}
$$

### Step 6 – Final Cost per Tonne

$$
C_{\text{Final/tonne}} = 1{,}56{,}975 + 10{,}988.25 = 1{,}67{,}963.25 \;\text{₹/tonne}
$$

### Step 7 – Total Hull Fabrication Cost

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{Hull,Total}} = 1{,}67{,}963.25 \times 12{,}500 = ₹ 20{,}99{,}54{,}062.5 \;\approx\; ₹ 21.0 \text{ Crore}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Out of ₹ 1.68 Lakh per tonne of finished hull steel, **56.6 % is raw steel**, **13.1 % is fabrication labour**, **5.1 % is welding consumables**, **12.2 % is overhead** and **6.5 % is profit**. The owner benchmarking this against quoted yard rates can immediately detect any abnormal loading.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> **Define the term "Demarcation of Costs" as applied to a hull inspection project. List any six standard cost heads used in such demarcation.**

**Model Answer:**

Demarcation of costs is the engineering-economic procedure of partitioning the total expenditure of a hull inspection, survey, or repair project into mutually exclusive, exhaustive, and functionally homogeneous categories, so that each cost head can be independently estimated, controlled, audited, and benchmarked. It provides a transparent, contractually binding cost-breakdown framework.

Six standard cost heads used in hull inspection demarcation are:

1. Material cost (steel, consumables, anodes, paint).
2. Direct labour cost (welder, fitter, blaster, painter hours).
3. Sub-contract cost (NDT agency, staging, rope access).
4. Dry-dock and staging cost.
5. Overhead cost (administration, utilities, depreciation, supervision).
6. Profit and tax (contractor's margin and GST).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**
> **Explain the difference between "Direct Cost" and "Overhead Cost" in the context of hull repair. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Direct Cost** is the expenditure that can be physically and unambiguously traced to the specific hull repair or inspection work-package. It varies in direct proportion to the quantum of work executed.

Examples: (i) Cost of steel plates used in plate renewal, (ii) Welder-hours expended on-site for the repair.

**Overhead Cost** is the expenditure that is *not* directly attributable to a single work-package but is incurred to keep the yard operational. It is apportioned to projects using an absorption rate (typically 10 – 25 % of direct cost).

Examples: (i) Salary of the yard's administrative and HR staff, (ii) Depreciation of the berth, cranes, and goliath gantry used by all projects.

> **Note to student:** Always state the *traceability* criterion – it is the KTU examiner's key phrase.

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Module Choice Questions (with Sub-Parts)

> **Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**
> **You must attempt ONE full question of 14 marks.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q8(b)]**

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **hierarchical demarcation of total hull project cost** into direct cost, overhead, profit, and tax. State clearly what is included in each tier. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A bulk carrier hull repair quotation has the following demarcated line items:
>
> | Item | Value (₹) |
> |------|-----------|
> | Steel & consumables | 45,00,000 |
> | Direct labour (welder + fitter) | 32,00,000 |
> | NDT sub-contract | 14,50,000 |
> | Dry-dock hire (10 days) | 38,00,000 |
> | Staging | 7,20,000 |
> | Engineering & documentation | 4,30,000 |
> | Overhead | 12 % of direct cost |
> | Profit | 8 % of (direct + overhead) |
> | GST | 18 % of pre-tax total |
>
> Calculate the **final quoted cost to the owner** and the **percentage share of each major head**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hierarchical Demarcation (7 Marks)**

The total hull project cost $C_{\text{Total}}$ is demarcated as:

$$C_{\text{Total}} = C_D + C_{\text{OH}} + C_{\text{Profit}} + C_{\text{Tax}}$$

- $C_D$ (Direct Cost) = Material + Labour + Sub-contract + Dry-dock + Staging + Engineering
- $C_{\text{OH}}$ (Overhead) = 10 – 25 % of $C_D$ covering admin, utilities, depreciation, insurance
- $C_{\text{Profit}}$ (Margin) = 5 – 12 % of $(C_D + C_{\text{OH}})$
- $C_{\text{Tax}}$ (GST) = 18 % of $(C_D + C_{\text{OH}} + C_{\text{Profit}})$

The student should also sketch the 4-tier block diagram (similar to the one in Section 4.1 of these notes).

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

*Step 1 – Direct Cost:*

$$
C_D = 45{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}50{,}000 + 38{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}20{,}000 + 4{,}30{,}000 = 1{,}41{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 2 – Overhead:*

$$
C_{\text{OH}} = 0.12 \times 1{,}41{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}92{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 3 – Profit:*

$$
C_{\text{Profit}} = 0.08 \times (1{,}41{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}92{,}000) = 0.08 \times 1{,}57{,}92{,}000 = 12{,}63{,}360 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 4 – Pre-Tax Sub-Total:*

$$
C_{\text{Pre}} = 1{,}41{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}92{,}000 + 12{,}63{,}360 = 1{,}70{,}55{,}360 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 5 – GST:*

$$
C_{\text{Tax}} = 0.18 \times 1{,}70{,}55{,}360 = 30{,}69{,}965 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 6 – Final Quoted Cost:*

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{Quoted}} = 1{,}70{,}55{,}360 + 30{,}69{,}965 = ₹ 2{,}01{,}25{,}325 \;\approx\; ₹ 2.01 \text{ Crore}}
$$

*Step 7 – Percentage Share Table:*

| Head | Amount (₹) | % of Total |
|------|-----------|------------|
| Direct Cost | 1,41,00,000 | 70.06 % |
| Overhead | 16,92,000 | 8.41 % |
| Profit | 12,63,360 | 6.28 % |
| GST | 30,69,965 | 15.25 % |
| **Total** | **2,01,25,325** | **100 %** |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) – ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Q9(a)]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **classification-society approach to hull survey cost demarcation**. List and briefly explain the major cost heads a classification surveyor incurs during an SS (Special Survey) of a vessel. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A classification society plans an NDT package for the Special Survey of a 15-year-old Suezmax tanker. The survey comprises:
>
> - 320 thickness gauging points at **₹ 900 per point** (UT) with an access surcharge of **25 %**.
> - 180 metres of weld to be MPI-tested at **₹ 1,350 per metre**.
> - Lump-sum documentation and certification fee of **₹ 1,20,000**.
>
> If the surveyor's overhead is **18 % of direct NDT cost** and the certification body adds a **10 % margin** on (direct + overhead), calculate the **final NDT package cost** the owner must pay. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification-Society Approach (7 Marks)**

The classification society demarcates the hull survey cost into the following major heads:

1. **Pre-Survey Cost** – drawing study, thickness measurement database (TMDP) review, planning.
2. **In-Water Survey Cost** – divers, ROV, propeller/rudder inspection.
3. **Dry-Dock Survey Cost** – hull cleaning, blasting, close-up visual, internal tank inspection.
4. **NDT Package Cost** – UT gauging, MPI, DPT, Eddy current, hydrostatic test.
5. **Thickness Gauging Cost** – automated UT with reporting.
6. **Documentation & Certification Cost** – survey report, class certificate, TMDP update.

The class society further applies a *per-point* rate for UT gauging, a *per-metre* rate for MPI/DPT of welds, and a *lump-sum* rate for documentation. Overheads and margin are loaded on the direct cost.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

*Step 1 – UT Thickness Gauging Cost:*

$$
C_{\text{UT}} = 320 \times 900 \times (1 + 0.25) = 320 \times 900 \times 1.25 = 3{,}60{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 2 – MPI of Welds Cost:*

$$
C_{\text{MPI}} = 180 \times 1{,}350 = 2{,}43{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 3 – Documentation Fee:*

$$
C_{\text{Doc}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 4 – Direct NDT Cost:*

$$
C_{\text{NDT,Direct}} = 3{,}60{,}000 + 2{,}43{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 7{,}23{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 5 – Overhead:*

$$
C_{\text{OH}} = 0.18 \times 7{,}23{,}000 = 1{,}30{,}140 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 6 – Pre-Margin Sub-Total:*

$$
C_{\text{Pre}} = 7{,}23{,}000 + 1{,}30{,}140 = 8{,}53{,}140 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 7 – Margin:*

$$
C_{\text{Margin}} = 0.10 \times 8{,}53{,}140 = 85{,}314 \;\text{₹}
$$

*Step 8 – Final NDT Package Cost:*

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{NDT,Final}} = 8{,}53{,}140 + 85{,}314 = ₹ 9{,}38{,}454 \;\approx\; ₹ 9.38 \text{ Lakh}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Conflating overhead and profit** – KTU expects a *clear demarcation* between the two. Loading a 20 % "composite" mark-up and calling it "overhead + profit" without segregation will cost 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the access surcharge** in NDT costing – a 25–30 % factor is *very frequently* missed; the examiner allocates 1–2 marks specifically to detect this.
> 3. **Wrong tax base** – Students often apply GST on $C_D$ alone instead of $(C_D + C_{\text{OH}} + C_{\text{Profit}})$. This is incorrect and loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not presenting a percentage-share table** in 14-mark questions – the table is a *proof* of demarcation; omitting it forfeits 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Mixing units** – mixing ₹/tonne with $/tonne within the same numerical problem is treated as a calculation error and is penalised.
> 6. **Skipping the per-day or per-tonne derived metric** – KTU 2024 has a strong tendency to ask for a derived "cost per day in yard" or "cost per tonne" as a sub-part. Missing it forfeits 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 – Demarcations and Subdivisions of Costs: One-Page Rapid Recap**

- **Definition:** Demarcation = partition of total hull cost into mutually exclusive, exhaustive, and traceable sub-heads.
- **Four Macro-Heads:** Material, Labour, Overhead, Profit (+ Tax).
- **New-Build vs In-Service:** New-build uses Hull/Outfit/Machinery split; in-service uses Dry-dock/Staging/NDT/Consumable split.
- **Master Formula:**
$$C_{\text{Quoted}} = C_D + C_{\text{OH}} + C_{\text{Profit}} + C_{\text{Tax}}$$
- **Overhead Loading:** $C_{\text{OH}} = k_{\text{OH}} \times C_D$ where $k_{\text{OH}} \in [0.10, 0.25]$.
- **Profit Loading:** $C_{\text{Profit}} = k_P \times (C_D + C_{\text{OH}})$ where $k_P \in [0.05, 0.12]$.
- **Tax Loading:** $C_{\text{Tax}} = r_T \times (C_D + C_{\text{OH}} + C_{\text{Profit}})$ where $r_T = 18\%$ (GST).
- **NDT Costing:** $C_{\text{NDT}} = N_{\text{Points}} \times R_{\text{UT}} \times (1 + k_{\text{Access}})$.
- **Direct Cost Traceability Test:** "Can I *physically point* to the work-package this money bought?" → Yes = Direct.
- **Overhead Traceability Test:** "Does this cost exist even if *no* repair was done this month?" → Yes = Overhead.
- **Indicative % Shares (In-Service Repair):** Material ≈ 25 %, Labour ≈ 20 %, Sub-contract ≈ 10 %, Dry-dock ≈ 15 %, Staging ≈ 5 %, Overhead ≈ 8 %, Profit ≈ 6 %, Tax ≈ 11 %.
- **Always** finish a 14-mark answer with a **percentage-share table** — it is the examiner's visual proof of demarcation.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** GST base, access surcharge, per-day / per-tonne cost derivations, classification society NDT packages, dispute resolution through demarcation.
- **Key Standards to Mention in Answers:** ISO 12900, SAJ standard cost breakdown, Class Rules (ABS/DNV/LR), IACS Unified Requirements.
- **Key Engineering Constants / Defaults to Memorise:**
    - GST in India = **18 %**
    - Overhead absorption typical range = **10 – 25 %**
    - Profit margin typical range = **5 – 12 %**
    - Access surcharge for NDT = **20 – 40 %**
    - UT thickness gauging typical rate = **₹ 800 – 1,000 per point** (India, 2024 indicative)
- **Single Golden Rule:** *If a cost is not demarcated, it cannot be controlled.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST832 - HULL INSPECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structural Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Structural Costs** in the discipline of hull inspection refer to the complete spectrum of financial expenditures incurred over the operational life of a marine vessel that are directly attributable to the **integrity, maintenance, repair, inspection, and possible renewal of the load-bearing hull structure**. According to the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and major classification societies such as **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and Bureau Veritas**, structural costs encompass both **tangible (direct)** and **intangible (indirect)** economic consequences arising from decisions taken during routine and special surveys.

In the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned OENST832 syllabus**, structural costs are classified under the broader umbrella of **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** and **Life-Cycle Cost Analysis (LCCA)** of marine structures, where the goal is to minimize the *Expected Total Cost (ETC)* while maintaining an acceptable level of structural safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Definition:** Structural Costs = *Total expenditure related to ensuring the hull's structural adequacy across design, fabrication, in-service, and disposal phases, evaluated using present-value economic metrics.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you bought a house near the coast. You don't just pay once for it; you keep paying for **paint every 3 years, anti-rust coating every 5 years, structural inspection every 10 years, and major repairs if a crack appears**. Now imagine a ship — which is a *much larger*, *much more complex*, and *much harsher-environment* structure than a house. A ship's hull is constantly battered by waves, salt corrosion, cargo loading stresses, and engine vibrations.

**Structural costs** are essentially the **"lifetime medical bills" of the ship's hull**. Just like a person gets regular health check-ups to avoid expensive surgeries later, a ship undergoes scheduled hull inspections to prevent catastrophic structural failures (which can cost **millions of dollars** and result in total loss of vessel, cargo, and even life).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:**
> - **Hull Structure** = The human body / skeletal frame
> - **Routine Survey** = Annual health check-up
> - **Special Survey** = Major surgery consultation
> - **Corrosion / Fatigue** = Disease progression
> - **Structural Cost** = Lifetime medical expenditure
> - **Inspection Strategy** = Preventive healthcare plan

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discount Rate (real) | $r$ | **3 % – 8 % per annum** | IMO Guidelines |
| Design Life (tanker) | $T_d$ | **20 – 25 years** | IACS S21 |
| Design Life (bulk carrier) | $T_d$ | **25 years** | IACS S21 |
| Survey Interval (special) | $\Delta t$ | **5 years** | SOLAS / IACS |
| DWT cost index | $C_{DWT}$ | **\$800 – \$1,500 / DWT** | Clarksons Research |
| Corrosion rate (mild steel) | $v_c$ | **0.1 – 0.5 mm/year** | IACS UR Z17 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Structural Costs

Structural costs in hull inspection are categorized into a four-tier hierarchy. Mastering this hierarchy is **essential** for KTU Part-B (14-mark) questions.

### Tier 1 — Initial / Acquisition Costs ($C_{init}$)
- Steel procurement, fabrication, outfitting
- One-time capital expenditure (CAPEX) at shipbuilding yard
- Typically **40 % – 55 %** of new-building price

### Tier 2 — Inspection Costs ($C_{insp}$)
- Class survey fees (annual, intermediate, special)
- Ultrasonic thickness gauging (UTG) campaigns
- Close-up survey access arrangements (scaffolding, cleaning)
- **\$50,000 – \$500,000** per special survey depending on vessel size

### Tier 3 — Maintenance & Repair Costs ($C_{m\&r}$)
- Steel renewal (plate replacement)
- Coating maintenance (paint, cathodic protection)
- Welding and rectification of detected defects
- **Largest recurring OPEX** in ship operation

### Tier 4 — Failure / Consequence Costs ($C_{fail}$)
- Total constructive loss (TCL)
- Salvage, wreck removal, pollution fine
- Cargo loss, third-party liability
- Reputational damage, insurance premium escalation
- **Catastrophic tier — can exceed \$100 million**

## 2.2 The Expected Total Cost (ETC) Framework

The fundamental optimization principle in hull inspection economics is:

$$
\text{ETC} = \sum_{t=0}^{T_d} \frac{C_{insp}(t) + C_{m\&r}(t) + P_f(t) \cdot C_{fail}(t)}{(1+r)^t}
$$

Where:
- $P_f(t)$ = Probability of structural failure in year $t$
- $r$ = annual discount rate
- $T_d$ = design life of vessel

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is the **decision-making backbone** for ship owners, class surveyors, and flag-state administrations when deciding whether to *repair, renew, or scrap* an aging hull.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | Present Value of future cost | USD |
| 2 | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Net Present Value of cash flows | USD |
| 3 | $ETC = \sum \dfrac{C_{insp}+C_{m\&r}+P_f C_{fail}}{(1+r)^t}$ | Expected Total Cost (life-cycle) | USD |
| 4 | $P_f = f(\sigma, \ K_{IC}, \ \Delta a)$ | Failure probability (fracture mechanics) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_{rep} = \rho_s \cdot A_{plate} \cdot t_{plate} \cdot c_{steel}$ | Steel renewal cost | USD |
| 6 | $C_{down} = R_{day} \cdot T_{off}$ | Vessel downtime cost | USD |
| 7 | $C_{coat} = A_{surf} \cdot c_{paint}$ | Coating maintenance cost | USD |
| 8 | $BF = \dfrac{C_{avoided}}{C_{insp}}$ | Benefit Factor of inspection | dimensionless |
| 9 | $AISC = \dfrac{1}{T_d}\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Annualized Inspection & Surveillance Cost | USD/year |
| 10 | $C_{total} = C_{init} + \sum(C_{insp}+C_{m\&r}+C_{fail})$ | Total Life-Cycle Structural Cost | USD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall Avoidance:** Never confuse *nominal* (current-dollar) values with *real* (constant-dollar) values when applying the discount factor. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.

## 2.4 The Inspection Optimization Principle

The optimal inspection interval $t^*$ is found by minimizing ETC:

$$
\frac{d(\text{ETC})}{dt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t^* = t \text{ that minimizes } \text{ETC}(t)
$$

A **parabolic ETC curve** typically results:
- **Too frequent inspections** → high $C_{insp}$
- **Too infrequent inspections** → high $P_f \cdot C_{fail}$
- **Optimum at the trough** of the parabola

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Structural cost analysis is applied in:
- **Ship recycling** decisions (when to scrap a vessel)
- **Insurance underwriting** by P&I clubs
- **RBI planning** for FPSOs, naval combatants, LNG carriers
- **CAPEX vs OPEX** trade-offs in fleet renewal
- **Litigation support** in maritime law (e.g., total loss disputes)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Present Value of a Future Structural Cost

**Problem Setup:** A shipowner must pay a **\$1,000,000** steel-renewal bill in **8 years**. The real discount rate is **6 % per annum**. Find the present-day equivalent cost.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the future value, discount rate, and time horizon.**
We are given:
- $FV = \$1{,}000{,}000$
- $r = 0.06$
- $n = 8$ years

**Step 2 — State the fundamental present-value formula.**

$$
PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values into the denominator.**

$$
PV = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{(1+0.06)^8} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{(1.06)^8}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power term explicitly.**

$$
(1.06)^8 = 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06
$$

Performing successive multiplication:

$$
1.06^2 = 1.1236
$$

$$
1.06^4 = (1.1236)^2 = 1.2625
$$

$$
1.06^8 = (1.2625)^2 = 1.5938
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final present value.**

$$
PV = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1.5938} \approx \$627{,}412
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A repair cost of \$1 M in year 8 is economically equivalent to spending only **\$627,412 today** at a 6 % real discount rate. This is the *time value of money* principle applied to hull maintenance economics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annualized Inspection & Surveillance Cost (AISC)

**Problem Setup:** A bulk carrier undergoes surveys at years **5, 10, 15, and 20** of its life, with each survey costing **\$300,000**. Using a 5 % discount rate, find the equivalent uniform annual cost (AISC).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify all future inspection cost events.**

| Survey | Year $t$ | Cost $C_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1st Special | 5 | \$300,000 |
| 2nd Special | 10 | \$300,000 |
