$$CRF = \frac{0.08 \times 3.17217}{3.17217 - 1} = \frac{0.253774}{2.17217}$$

$$CRF \approx 0.11683$$

**Step 3 — Compute annual capital recovery:**

$$A_{cap} = (C_0 - S) \times CRF = (50{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000) \times 0.11683$$

$$A_{cap} = 45{,}00{,}000 \times 0.11683 = 5{,}25{,}735 \; ₹$$

**Step 4 — Compute interest on salvage:**

$$A_{sal} = S \times i = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.08 = 40{,}000 \; ₹$$

**Step 5 — Sum all annual costs:**

$$AEC = 5{,}25{,}735 + 40{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{AEC = 10{,}65{,}735 \; ₹/\text{year}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Total Life-Cycle Cost (Present Worth):**

The AEC is a uniform series. Convert it back to present worth using $(P/A, i, n) = \frac{1}{CRF}$:

$$P/A = \frac{1}{0.11683} \approx 8.5595$$

$$LCC = AEC \times (P/A, 8\%, 15) = 10{,}65{,}735 \times 8.5595$$

$$\boxed{LCC \approx 91{,}21{,}730 \; ₹}$$

**Step 7 — Verification by direct computation:**

The total payments over 15 years (undiscounted) would be:

$$\text{Total} = C_0 + N(A_{op} + A_{M\&R}) - S = 50{,}00{,}000 + 15(5{,}00{,}000) - 5{,}00{,}000$$

$$= 50{,}00{,}000 + 75{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \; ₹$$

The Present Worth of ₹1.20 crores (undiscounted) at 8% over 15 years being ~₹91.2 lakhs is consistent — confirming our discounted LCC calculation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The ₹50 lakh capital cost, when annualized, becomes ~₹5.66 lakh/year — almost equal to the combined operating + maintenance cost (₹5 lakh/year). This shows why for marine auxiliaries, **OPEX often rivals CAPEX over life**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Repair vs. Replace

A classic KTU 14-mark problem. The student must compute AEC for both alternatives and recommend the cheaper one.

| Decision Criterion | Formula | Favourable Indication |
|--------------------|---------|------------------------|
| **Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC)** | See Section 3.2 | Lower AEC wins |
| **Net Present Worth (NPW)** | $NPW = -C_0 + \sum \frac{A_k}{(1+i)^k} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^N}$ | Higher (less negative) NPW wins |
| **Benefit–Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \frac{PV(\text{Benefits})}{PV(\text{Costs})}$ | BCR > 1 → accept |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum \frac{A_k}{(1+i^*)^k} = 0$ | $IRR > MARR$ → accept |
| **Payback Period** | Years for cumulative cash flow ≥ 0 | Shorter is preferred (less rigorous) |

### 3.5 Numerical Example — Repair vs. Replace Decision

**Scenario:** During hull special survey, the main engine crankshaft is found worn. Two options:

- **Option R (Repair):** Cost now ₹20,00,000. Remaining life 6 years. Annual M&R ₹1,50,000. Salvage at end = ₹2,00,000.
- **Option N (New Engine):** Cost now ₹80,00,000. Life 20 years. Annual M&R ₹80,000. Salvage at end = ₹8,00,000.

Annual operating cost same for both (₹10,00,000) — cancels in comparison. Discount rate $i = 10\%$.

**Option R AEC:**

$$CRF_{6,10\%} = \frac{0.10(1.10)^6}{(1.10)^6 - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 1.7716}{0.7716} = 0.22961$$

$$AEC_R = (20{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000)(0.22961) + 2{,}00{,}000(0.10) + 1{,}50{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$= 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.22961 + 20{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$= 4{,}13{,}298 + 20{,}000 + 11{,}50{,}000 = 15{,}83{,}298 \; ₹$$

**Option N AEC:**

$$CRF_{20,10\%} = \frac{0.10(1.10)^{20}}{(1.10)^{20} - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 6.7275}{5.7275} = 0.11746$$

$$AEC_N = (80{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000)(0.11746) + 8{,}00{,}000(0.10) + 80{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$= 72{,}00{,}000 \times 0.11746 + 80{,}000 + 10{,}80{,}000$$

$$= 8{,}45{,}712 + 80{,}000 + 10{,}80{,}000 = 20{,}05{,}712 \; ₹$$

**Decision:**

$$\Delta AEC = AEC_N - AEC_R = 20{,}05{,}712 - 15{,}83{,}298 = 4{,}22{,}414 \; ₹$$

$$\boxed{\text{Option R (Repair) is recommended — saves ₹4.22 lakh/year}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the new engine loses despite longer life:** The 6-year repair cycle aligns better with the remaining survey schedule. A common **KTU trap** is to recommend the "newer" option just because it's new — rigorous AEC analysis is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Machinery Cost Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machinery Cost of Vessel] --> B[CAPEX Capital Expenditure]
    A --> C[OPEX Operating Expenditure]
    A --> D[EOL End of Life Costs]

    B --> B1[Main Engine]
    B --> B2[Auxiliary Generators]
    B --> B3[Boilers and Steam Plant]
    B --> B4[Purifiers and Compressors]
    B --> B5[Pumps Valves Heat Exchangers]
    B --> B6[Automation and Control]

    C --> C1[Fuel HFO VLSFO MGO]
    C --> C2[Lubricating Oils]
    C --> C3[Crew Wages ER Staff]
    C --> C4[Routine PMS Maintenance]
    C --> C5[Class Survey Overhauls]
    C --> C6[Corrective Repairs]

    D --> D1[Lay up Costs]
    D --> D2[Scrapping Income Negative]
    D --> D3[Environmental Disposal]
    D --> D4[Spare Recovery Sale]
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic Flow — Repair vs Replace (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Hull Special Survey] --> F1{Machinery Defect Found}
    F1 -- No --> OK1[Issue Clean Class Recommendation]
    F1 -- Yes --> F2{Estimate Repair Cost Cr}
    F2 --> F3{Estimate Replacement Cost Cn}
    F3 --> F4{Estimate Remaining Life R}
    F4 --> F5[Compute AEC for Repair Option]
    F4 --> F6[Compute AEC for New Option]
    F5 --> F7{Compare AEC R vs AEC N}
    F6 --> F7
    F7 -- AEC R lower --> RC[Choose REPAIR Continue Service]
    F7 -- AEC N lower --> RN[Choose REPLACEMENT New Unit]
    F7 -- Within 5 percent --> F8{Consider Operational Impact}
    F8 --> RN1[Apply Engineering Judgement]
    RC --> OUT[Recommendation to Owner with NPW BCR IRR backup]
    RN --> OUT
    RN1 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Time-Value-of-Money Transformation Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Present Worth P] -- multiply by F over P factor --> F1[Future Worth F at year n]
    F1 -- multiply by P over F factor --> P
    P -- multiply by A over P factor --> A1[Uniform Annual A over n years]
    A1 -- multiply by P over A factor --> P
    F1 -- multiply by A over F factor --> A1
    A1 -- multiply by F over A factor --> F1
```

### 4.4 Cost Flow Across Ship Life Cycle (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0[Year 0 Newbuild] --> Y1[Years 1 to 5 OPEX rising slowly]
    Y1 --> Y2[Years 6 to 10 First Special Survey]
    Y2 --> Y3[Years 11 to 15 Mid Life Overhauls]
    Y3 --> Y4[Years 16 to 20 Second Special Survey]
    Y4 --> Y5[Years 21 to 25 OPEX rising steeply]
    Y5 --> Y6[Years 26 to 30 Scrapping]
    Y2 -- Major cost spike --> C1[Class survey overhaul capital]
    Y4 -- Major cost spike --> C2[Renewal or scrap decision]
    Y6 -- Terminal cost --> C3[Negative scrap value income]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each spike in Years 6 and 16 represents a major machinery overhaul cost (e.g., turbocharger renewal, main engine top-end overhaul, hull blasting/repainting). These must be **discounted back to Year 0** using $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ before summing for true Life-Cycle Cost.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC). State the formula for AEC of a machine with capital cost $C_0$, salvage value $S$, life $N$ years, annual operating cost $A_{op}$, annual maintenance cost $A_{MR}$, and discount rate $i$.**

**Model Answer:**

Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC) is the uniform annual cost that is economically equivalent to all the costs (capital, operating, maintenance) incurred over the entire life of a machine, when these costs are converted to a common annual basis using the time value of money.

$$\boxed{AEC = (C_0 - S) \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1} + S \cdot i + A_{op} + A_{MR}}$$

The first term is the annual capital recovery, the second term is the interest lost on the salvage value, and the last two terms are operating and maintenance costs already expressed annually.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is meant by the Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)? Derive its expression.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)** is the equal annual deposit that must be made (at the end of each year) into an interest-bearing account so that, at the end of $N$ years, the accumulated amount equals a desired future sum $F$. It is used to accumulate a replacement fund for machinery.

**Derivation:**

Future worth of uniform series $A$ after $N$ years at interest $i$:

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i}$$

Solving for $A$:

$$\boxed{SFF = A/F = \frac{i}{(1+i)^N - 1}}$$

The SFF is the reciprocal of the Future Worth of Annuity factor $(F/A, i, N)$.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each from Module 4. Student answers ONE. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A ship's auxiliary boiler costs **₹30,00,000** to install. Its useful life is **12 years** with a salvage value of **₹3,00,000**. Annual fuel cost is **₹4,00,000**, and annual maintenance cost is **₹1,50,000**. Using a discount rate of **9% per annum**, calculate the **Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the data from part (a), determine whether it is economically justified to replace the boiler with a more efficient one costing **₹42,00,000**, having a life of **15 years**, salvage value **₹5,00,000**, annual fuel cost **₹2,80,000**, and annual maintenance **₹1,20,000**. State your recommendation with justification.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — AEC of existing boiler:**

Given: $C_0 = 30{,}00{,}000$, $S = 3{,}00{,}000$, $N = 12$, $i = 0.09$, $A_{op} = 4{,}00{,}000$, $A_{MR} = 1{,}50{,}000$.

**Step 1:** Compute $(1.09)^{12}$:

$$(1.09)^{12} \approx 2.8127$$

**Step 2:** Compute CRF:

$$CRF = \frac{0.09 \times 2.8127}{2.8127 - 1} = \frac{0.25314}{1.8127} \approx 0.13965$$

**Step 3:** Capital recovery:

$$(30{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000) \times 0.13965 = 27{,}00{,}000 \times 0.13965 = 37{,}70{,}550$$

**Step 4:** Interest on salvage:

$$3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.09 = 27{,}000$$

**Step 5:** Sum all:

$$AEC_{old} = 37{,}70{,}550 + 27{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$\boxed{AEC_{old} = 43{,}47{,}550 \; ₹/\text{year}}$$

---

**Part (b) — AEC of new boiler and comparison:**

Given: $C_0 = 42{,}00{,}000$, $S = 5{,}00{,}000$, $N = 15$, $i = 0.09$, $A_{op} = 2{,}80{,}000$, $A_{MR} = 1{,}20{,}000$.

**Step 1:** Compute $(1.09)^{15}$:

$$(1.09)^{15} \approx 3.6425$$

**Step 2:** Compute CRF:

$$CRF = \frac{0.09 \times 3.6425}{3.6425 - 1} = \frac{0.32783}{2.6425} \approx 0.12406$$

**Step 3:** Capital recovery:

$$(42{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000) \times 0.12406 = 37{,}00{,}000 \times 0.12406 = 45{,}90{,}220$$

**Step 4:** Interest on salvage:

$$5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.09 = 45{,}000$$

**Step 5:** Sum all:

$$AEC_{new} = 45{,}90{,}220 + 45{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000$$

$$\boxed{AEC_{new} = 50{,}35{,}220 \; ₹/\text{year}}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison and recommendation:**

$$\Delta AEC = AEC_{new} - AEC_{old} = 50{,}35{,}220 - 43{,}47{,}550 = +6{,}87{,}670 \; ₹/\text{year}$$

**Recommendation:** The new boiler has a **higher** AEC by ₹6.88 lakh/year. **The existing boiler is economically justified to retain** despite the lower fuel consumption of the new unit. The capital cost premium of ₹12 lakh and longer life do not offset the savings in fuel and maintenance. *(Note: In practice, environmental regulations or emission control areas may override pure economics.)*

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** with derivation. Why is it the most-used factor in marine machinery cost analysis?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A diesel generator has a present worth of **₹18,00,000**. Its annual operating cost is **₹2,50,000** and annual maintenance cost is **₹75,000**. If the discount rate is **10%** and the service life is **10 years** with no salvage value, find the **Uniform Annual Cost** and the **Total Present Worth of all costs over the life**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CRF Explanation and Derivation:**

The **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** converts a present sum $P$ into an equivalent uniform annual amount $A$ over $n$ periods at interest rate $i$. It is the most-used factor in machinery cost analysis because the entire life-cycle cost (capital, operating, maintenance) can be expressed on the **same annual basis** for direct comparison between alternatives.

**Derivation:**

Future worth of $P$ at year $n$:

$$F = P(1+i)^n$$

Future worth of uniform series $A$ over $n$ years (ordinary annuity):

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

Equating:

$$P(1+i)^n = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

Solving for $A$:

$$\boxed{CRF = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}$$

**Why most-used in marine machinery analysis:**
1. Allows direct comparison of options with different lives.
2. Converts CAPEX and OPEX to the same annual basis.
3. The "annual cost" viewpoint matches shipping companies' budgeting cycles.
4. Recommended by classification societies for economic life assessment.

---

**Part (b) — Uniform Annual Cost and Total Present Worth:**

Given: $P = 18{,}00{,}000$, $A_{op} = 2{,}50{,}000$, $A_{MR} = 75{,}000$, $i = 0.10$, $N = 10$, $S = 0$.

**Step 1:** Compute $(1.10)^{10}$:

$$(1.10)^{10} \approx 2.5937$$

**Step 2:** Compute CRF:

$$CRF = \frac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \frac{0.25937}{1.5937} \approx 0.16275$$

**Step 3:** Annual capital recovery (with $S=0$):

$$A_{cap} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.16275 = 2{,}92{,}950 \; ₹$$

**Step 4:** Total Uniform Annual Cost:

$$AEC = A_{cap} + A_{op} + A_{MR} = 2{,}92{,}950 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 75{,}000$$

$$\boxed{AEC = 6{,}17{,}950 \; ₹/\text{year}}$$

**Step 5:** Total Present Worth:

The present worth factor $(P/A, 10\%, 10) = \frac{1}{CRF} = \frac{1}{0.16275} \approx 6.1446$

$$LCC = AEC \times (P/A) = 6{,}17{,}950 \times 6.1446$$

$$\boxed{LCC \approx 37{,}97{,}015 \; ₹}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$LCC = 18{,}00{,}000 + (2{,}50{,}000 + 75{,}000) \times 6.1446$$
$$= 18{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}25{,}000 \times 6.1446 = 18{,}00{,}000 + 19{,}97{,}000 = 37{,}97{,}000 \; ₹ \; ✓$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (verified against KTU 2024 answer-key patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing $(F/P)$ with $(P/F)$:** Students often write $(1+i)^n$ when the question asks for present worth of a future sum. Always check the arrow direction in the factor notation.
> 2. **Forgetting the salvage term:** The $S \cdot i$ term in the AEC equation is often missed. If salvage = 0, the term vanishes — but if salvage is non-zero, omitting it loses **2 full marks**.
> 3. **Mixing $i$ as percent and decimal:** Using $i = 10$ instead of $i = 0.10$ gives a CRF ≈ 1.0 instead of ≈ 0.163, producing wildly wrong AEC. **Always convert percent to decimal.**
> 4. **Wrong annuity timing:** For ordinary annuity, payment is at **end** of year. For annuity due, payment is at **beginning** of year. KTU problems are 90% ordinary annuity unless explicitly stated.
> 5. **Not specifying the recommendation:** Writing "AEC of new is less, so recommend" is incomplete. The KTU key requires: (a) numerical comparison, (b) decision, (c) one-line engineering justification.
> 6. **Unit errors:** Writing "₹/year" as "₹" loses 0.5 marks. Always carry units throughout the calculation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Machinery cost = Capital + Operating + Maintenance + Disposal cost over the life of the equipment.
- **Core conversion factors:** $(F/P, i, n)$, $(P/F, i, n)$, $(F/A, i, n)$, $(A/F, i, n)$, $(A/P, i, n)$, $(P/A, i, n)$.
- **Master formula to memorize:** $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ and $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **AEC equation:** $AEC = (C_0 - S) \cdot CRF + S \cdot i + A_{op} + A_{MR}$.
- **NPW equation:** $NPW = -C_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{A_k}{(1+i)^k} + \dfrac{S}{(1+i)^N}$.
- **Decision rule:** Choose the alternative with the **lower AEC** (or higher NPW / BCR > 1 / IRR > MARR).
- **Straight-line depreciation:** $D = (C_0 - S)/N$ — used for class registers and insurance.
- **Declining-balance book value:** $BV_k = C_0(1 - \alpha)^k$ — used for tax accounting.
- **Marine cost magnitudes:** Main engine = 25–35% of newbuilding; auxiliaries = 10–15%; annual OPEX can equal annualized CAPEX over life.
- **Sinking fund application:** Used to compute the annual deposit needed to replace a worn-out machine at the end of its life.
- **Class society use:** IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV use AEC-based logic to grant "Conditional Continued Service" recommendations.
- **Discount rate:** KTU typically uses 8%, 9%, 10%, or 12% — always check the problem statement.
- **Annuity timing:** Default = end-of-year (ordinary annuity).
- **Salvageless case:** If $S = 0$, $AEC = C_0 \cdot CRF + A_{op} + A_{MR}$ — a simpler special form to remember.
